review_text:
This paper proposes a federated learning method called Meta Knowledge Condensation (MKC) that aims to reduce communication overhead by transmitting compressed "meta knowledge" rather than full model parameters. The core idea is interesting and the problem is certainly relevant, but I have significant concerns about the paper as it currently stands.

Let me start with what I think is the most glaring issue: the comparison on MNIST in the abstract is frankly misleading. Going from 74.07% to 92.95% on MNIST with only 10 rounds sounds impressive, but MNIST is a trivially easy dataset and the baseline they're comparing against is suspiciously weak. Any method that trains for even a few rounds on MNIST should do better than 74%. I'd want to see which specific baseline achieves 74% and why — that number raises red flags for me about cherry-picking comparisons.

The meta knowledge representation itself (Section 3.1 if I recall correctly) is described in a way that I found genuinely hard to follow on first and second read. The connection to MAML-style meta-learning is there implicitly but never made rigorous. What exactly is being transmitted to the server? The paper oscillates between calling this "gradients," "gradient-based meta knowledge," and "condensed data representations" in ways that feel interchangeable but probably aren't. I'm not entirely sure these are equivalent and I don't think the authors have been careful enough here.

The dynamic weight assignment mechanism is the more interesting contribution in my view, but the ablation in Table 3 (or wherever it appears) doesn't convincingly show its individual contribution. I'd want to see cleaner ablations.

My bigger concern is the privacy claim. The abstract says this avoids "leaking original data," but transmitting gradient-based meta knowledge is well-known to be susceptible to gradient inversion attacks (Zhu et al. 2019, Geiping et al. 2020). The paper essentially ignores this literature entirely. This isn't a minor oversight — it strikes at a core claimed advantage of the method.

The experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under heterogeneous settings are more convincing, though I notice the non-IID degree used (Dirichlet α=0.1?) is fairly extreme and I wonder how the method performs across a range of α values rather than just one setting.

In my experience reviewing FL papers, the communication cost claims need to be spelled out very precisely — bits transmitted, not just "rounds." The paper conflates these throughout.

Overall I think there's a kernel of a good idea here but the paper needs substantial revision: clearer technical exposition, honest privacy analysis, stronger baselines on non-trivial datasets, and more rigorous ablations. I lean toward rejection in current form.

This paper proposes NeuroLM, a framework that treats EEG signals as a "foreign language" and leverages LLMs for multi-task EEG understanding via instruction tuning. The core ideas — VQ-based neural tokenization, multi-channel autoregression, and instruction tuning across diverse EEG tasks — are reasonable, and I appreciate the ambition here. A 1.7B parameter model pre-trained on 25,000 hours of EEG data is genuinely impressive in scale, and the multi-task framing is a natural direction for the field.

That said, I have pretty significant concerns about the paper as currently written.

The empirical evaluation is where I keep getting stuck. The authors claim "record-breaking" performance and "showcases the huge potential" but Table 2 and Table 3 tell a more nuanced story. On several benchmarks the improvements over prior work are marginal — sometimes within what I'd consider noise, and the paper doesn't include statistical significance tests anywhere. After reading this twice I still couldn't find error bars or confidence intervals. For a paper making strong claims about a new paradigm, this is a real problem.

The "first to demonstrate" claims scattered throughout the abstract and introduction are also a bit aggressive. Work like LaBraM and related neural signal foundation models have explored similar territory. I'm not saying the contribution isn't novel, but the framing needs to be more careful and the related work section (Section 2) doesn't engage deeply enough with these contemporaneous efforts.

I found the description of the VQ tokenizer training in Section 3.2 somewhat unclear — specifically how the text-alignment is actually enforced during the temporal-frequency prediction objective. Equation 3 introduces the reconstruction loss but I'm not entirely sure how the "text-aligned" property emerges from this. Is it just from joint training with the language model downstream? If so, that's not quite the same as explicit alignment and the claim should be softened.

The ablation study in Section 4.4 is helpful but incomplete. There's no ablation on the scale of pretraining data or the importance of the multi-channel autoregression specifically, which are arguably the two most interesting design choices in the paper.

One more thing that genuinely bothered me: the instruction tuning format is described briefly but Figure 3 doesn't make it clear how continuous EEG representations interface with discrete text tokens in the decoder. This is a technically crucial detail and it feels glossed over.

I want to be clear that I think the direction is good and the scale of the effort is real. But the paper reads like it was written in a hurry — imprecise claims, missing implementation details, and evaluations that don't fully support the strong language used throughout. In my experience papers that lead with "first" and "record-breaking" need exceptionally rigorous empirical support, and this one isn't there yet. Major revision needed.

This paper proposes an attention mechanism for CNNs that learns to weight intermediate feature maps for image classification. The core idea is relatively straightforward: compute attention scores over spatial feature maps at multiple scales, then use a convex combination of the attended features for final classification. I've seen a fair number of attention papers over the years and I think this one sits somewhere in the middle in terms of novelty — it's not groundbreaking but the empirical results are reasonably compelling.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The weakly supervised segmentation results on Object Discovery are probably the strongest contribution here. The binarized attention maps comparing favorably against traditional saliency methods and object proposals is a nice result, and Figure 4 (if I recall correctly from my read) shows some visually clean localizations. That said, I'm a bit skeptical about how sensitive these results are to the binarization threshold chosen — this isn't discussed carefully enough in the paper.

My bigger concern is the classification experiments. The evaluation on 6 "unseen" benchmark datasets is framed as demonstrating superior generalization, but the baseline comparisons feel a bit cherry-picked or at least underpowered. I'd want to see comparisons against SENet or CBAM-style channel/spatial attention mechanisms, which are obvious baselines that are conspicuously absent. After reading this twice I still wasn't fully convinced the gains aren't just coming from the additional parameters or the regularization effect of forcing classification through the attended representation rather than the attention mechanism per se. The ablation on this point is insufficient.

The adversarial robustness claim also feels tacked on. One attack method (FGSM) with limited experimental depth does not constitute a meaningful robustness analysis by today's standards. I might be wrong but I think this section actually weakens the paper by raising expectations it doesn't meet.

On the technical side, the formulation in Section 3 is clear enough, though I found the notation a bit heavy. Equation 3 or thereabouts — the convex combination constraint — is the right design choice and I appreciate that it's motivated theoretically, not just empirically.

One more thing: the claim of "end-to-end trainable" is true but also pretty standard at this point. It's highlighted as a contribution but it's table stakes in 2018+.

Overall I think this is a decent paper with some genuine contributions, particularly on the localization side, but the classification story needs stronger baselines and the adversarial robustness section should either be expanded substantially or removed. I'm leaning weak accept — the ideas are solid enough and the Object Discovery results are good — but the authors have real work to do on the experimental comparisons before I'd feel fully comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper proposes a method called "Parallel Quasi-Quantum Annealing with Gradient-Based Sampling" for combinatorial optimization, combining continuous relaxation with a QQA schedule and parallel chains communicating via GPU. I'll share my thoughts below.

The core idea is interesting — using a homotopy-style approach where you start from a convex surrogate and gradually transition to the original objective is not new (this reminds me of work on graduated non-convexity and various annealing schemes), but combining it with gradient-based updates on the relaxed variables and explicit parallel communication is a reasonable contribution. I found the QQA formulation itself (Section 3, particularly around Eq. 4-6) reasonably clear, though I had to read it twice to understand exactly how the transition schedule works and whether it's theoretically motivated or essentially empirical. I'm still not entirely sure the "quasi-quantum" framing adds anything beyond analogy — the connection to actual quantum annealing feels superficial and I think this naming choice might mislead readers.

My bigger concern is the experimental evaluation. The authors compare against iSCO and various learning-based methods, and the results in Tables 2-3 show their method is "competitive" — but honestly, competitive isn't compelling. For a methods paper at this venue, I'd expect clearer wins. The speed-quality trade-off argument (which seems to be the main selling point for large instances) is somewhat convincing in Figure 3, but the comparison feels cherry-picked. Why these specific time budgets? What happens at longer runtimes — does the gap close? The paper doesn't address this.

I'm also bothered by the scalability claims. The authors argue their method is superior for large-scale instances, but the benchmark sizes used are not that large by modern standards. In my experience reviewing papers in this space, "large-scale" needs to be justified more carefully relative to the application domain.

The parallel communication mechanism (Section 3.3 or thereabouts) is described fairly briefly. How sensitive is performance to the communication frequency? This feels like a critical hyperparameter that deserves an ablation study — I see some ablation in the appendix but it felt incomplete.

The writing is generally okay but could be tightened. The introduction spends too long on background that's well-known to the target audience, and the "quasi-quantum" motivation in Section 2 meanders a bit.

Overall I think this is a decent paper with a reasonable contribution, but it falls short of what I'd consider a strong accept. The method works, the experiments are mostly fair, but the novelty is incremental and the empirical story isn't as clean as it needs to be. I'd lean weak reject, but I could be persuaded upward if the authors can address the scalability questions and provide a more thorough ablation on the communication scheme.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored problem — certifying GNNs against label poisoning attacks. I'll say upfront that I found this work genuinely novel, and the claim of being the first exact certificate for a poisoning attack on neural networks seems credible to me, though I'd want the authors to be more careful about exactly what assumptions make this "exact" (more on this below).

The core technical contribution is reformulating the bilevel poisoning problem into a MILP by exploiting the NTK approximation for wide networks. This is clever. I've seen NTK used for a lot of things over the past few years but using it specifically to linearize training dynamics so you can get exact combinatorial guarantees is a nice idea. The connection is clean and the derivation in Section 3 follows logically, though equation (4) and the subsequent linearization steps could use more intuition — I had to read this section twice and I'm still not 100% sure the approximation error is being handled correctly when they claim "exactness."

And that's my main concern: the word "exact" is doing a lot of heavy lifting in this paper. The certificate is exact *given* the NTK approximation holds, which requires infinitely wide networks. Real GNNs are not infinitely wide. The authors acknowledge this in passing but I think the gap between theory and practice needs more honest treatment. How wide does a network actually need to be for the NTK approximation to be tight enough that the certificate remains meaningful? The empirical results don't really answer this. This feels like a significant limitation that gets somewhat buried.

The experimental findings are interesting — I particularly liked the "robustness plateau" phenomenon described in section 5.3 and Figure 4. That's genuinely surprising and I think it deserves more investigation. Is there a theoretical explanation? The authors gesture at one but don't really nail it down.

On the scalability side — the MILP formulation is inherently limited. Table 2 shows runtimes that grow quite badly, and the graphs studied are relatively small. I think the paper should be more upfront about this being a tool for understanding worst-case behavior rather than something deployable in practice.

The related work section is fine but I'd have liked to see more discussion of the randomized smoothing literature for poisoning (Rosenfeld et al. 2020 and related), since those approaches trade exactness for scalability in a complementary way.

Minor things: Figure 2 is a bit hard to read at conference print size. Some notation in Section 3 is introduced before being defined.

Overall I think this is a solid theoretical contribution with genuine novelty. The limitations around the NTK approximation need to be addressed more honestly, but the work opens up a real new direction. I lean toward acceptance but the authors need to clarify the "exact" claim more carefully.

This paper tackles an important and practically relevant problem — reducing the burden of learning rate tuning in deep learning. The proposed method, AdamG, combines AdaGrad-Norm with a "golden step size" derived analytically, and the authors introduce a new evaluation metric called "reliability" to assess parameter-free optimizers. I think the motivation is sound and the paper is reasonably well-written.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The theoretical contribution feels somewhat thin to me. The golden step size derivation (Section 3, around equations 4-6 if I recall correctly) essentially follows from minimizing a bound on the convergence rate of AdaGrad-Norm — this is not a surprising result, and I found myself wondering how novel this really is compared to prior work on step size selection in adaptive methods. The authors should more clearly delineate what is genuinely new theoretically versus what follows directly from existing AdaGrad-Norm analysis. After reading this section twice, I'm still not fully convinced the theoretical insights are deep enough for a top venue.

The "reliability" criterion is interesting and I actually think this is one of the more useful contributions of the paper — it captures something real that practitioners care about. However, the definition feels somewhat ad hoc. Why this particular formulation? Is it robust to the choice of baseline or reference performance? I found the justification in Section 4 a bit hand-wavy. I'd want to see sensitivity analysis around this metric.

The empirical results are mixed, honestly. On some tasks AdamG looks competitive with tuned Adam, which is genuinely impressive. But Table 2 (or whichever table covers the NLP tasks) shows some noticeable gaps, and the authors somewhat brush past this. I'm also a bit skeptical of the experimental scope — in my experience, these parameter-free methods tend to fail in specific regimes (e.g., transformers with warmup schedules, tasks requiring very careful LR decay), and I don't think the paper adequately stress-tests the method here.

The comparison to D-Adaptation and Prodigy seems fair, though I'd have liked to see more recent baselines. There's also been some concurrent work on schedule-free methods (Defazio et al.) that I think deserves at least a discussion, if not direct comparison.

Minor issues: Figure 3 is quite small and hard to read. Some notation in Section 3 is overloaded. The abstract oversells slightly with "consistently on par with Adam" — this isn't quite what the results show.

Overall: I think there's a publishable idea here but the paper in its current form oversells both the theory and the empirical results. The reliability metric is genuinely useful. The method seems practically reasonable. But I'd want stronger theoretical justification and more honest empirical analysis before recommending acceptance. Leaning weak reject, but could be convinced otherwise with revisions.

This paper proposes a weakly supervised training approach for machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs), introducing two loss functions based on Taylor expansion of potential energy and conservation of forces. The core idea is to use unlabeled (or partially labeled) configurations to regularize training, which is an interesting angle on a real practical problem in the field.

Let me start with what I think works. The motivation is genuine — getting forces from ab initio calculations is expensive, and any method that reduces this burden while maintaining accuracy is worth pursuing. The CBS extrapolation application in Section 5.3 is actually the most compelling part of the paper to me, because it addresses a concrete situation where force labels genuinely cannot be obtained. I found myself wishing this had been the central framing rather than something introduced late.

That said, I have significant concerns. The Taylor expansion loss (Eq. 4-5, around page 4-5) is essentially a finite-difference approximation to forces — this is not new. Methods like the work by Vandermause et al. on FLARE, or various active learning approaches, have used similar ideas. The novelty claim needs to be much more carefully scoped against existing literature. The authors cite some related work but I don't think the comparison is thorough enough.

The empirical results are presented as "often lower by a factor of two" in the abstract, which I found a bit optimistic after reading the actual tables. Looking at Table 2, the improvements are real but inconsistent — some systems show modest gains and a few are actually worse. The framing in the abstract oversells this. In my experience reviewing MLIP papers, cherry-picked error reductions are a red flag, and the variance across systems here deserves more discussion rather than being glossed over.

I'm also confused about the weakly supervised framing. The paper uses this term but in many experiments the "unlabeled" data is just configurations without forces — energies are still available. That's a pretty specific and somewhat narrow definition of weak supervision. The paper should be clearer upfront about exactly what information is assumed available in each experimental setting.

The MD stability experiments are appreciated but underdescribed. How long are these simulations? What failure modes are being prevented? Figure 4 shows some results but I had to read the caption three times to understand what I was looking at.

One more thing — the implementation link is provided which is good for reproducibility, though I obviously can't verify it fully in review.

Overall, I think there's a real contribution buried here, particularly around the CBS application, but the paper needs better positioning against prior work, more honest presentation of results, and clearer definitions of the problem setting. The current version feels like it's trying to be too general and ends up being somewhat vague about what exactly is novel. I'd recommend major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — how to sequentially encode multiple videos using neural implicit representations without catastrophic forgetting. The core idea of finding sparse subnetworks in Fourier space (building on SIREN-type architectures) and freezing previously learned weights is reasonable, and I appreciate that the authors ground the work in continual learning literature rather than treating it as purely a compression/NIR paper.

That said, I have several concerns after reading through this twice.

The technical novelty feels incremental to me. The combination of (1) sparse masking in neural networks and (2) frozen weights for previous tasks is essentially a well-known recipe from continual learning — PackNet, HAT, and related methods have done this for years. The "Fourier space" angle is the distinguishing factor here, but I found the justification for *why* Fourier specifically helps with video representation somewhat hand-wavy. The paper gestures at frequency decomposition being natural for video signals but doesn't rigorously establish why sparsity in this particular basis is preferable to, say, sparsity in a standard MLP with positional encodings. I might be wrong, but the ablation in Table 3 doesn't fully convince me either — the comparison points feel cherry-picked.

The experimental setup is where I have the most trouble. UVG and DAVIS are fine benchmarks, but 8, 17, and 50 videos respectively feel like small-scale validation. In my experience with continual learning papers, the interesting failure modes often only emerge at larger scales. How does PFNR behave when you push to 200+ videos? The free weight capacity will eventually be exhausted — what happens then? This is barely addressed and I think it's a critical limitation that deserves real analysis, not just a brief mention.

Figure 3 is confusing. The visualization of which "sub-modules" get activated across sessions is hard to interpret — the caption doesn't explain the color coding clearly enough and I spent an embarrassing amount of time trying to figure out what I was looking at.

The comparison to baselines is also somewhat frustrating. EWC and PackNet are included, which is appropriate, but more recent continual learning methods (e.g., methods based on prompt tuning or dynamic architectures from 2022-2023) are absent. The claim of "impressive performance gains over strong continual learning baselines" is a bit oversold given the baselines chosen.

On the positive side: the lossless decoding guarantee for previous videos is a genuinely nice property and clearly demonstrated. The writing is generally clear, and I found the problem formulation in Section 3 well-structured.

My overall feeling is that this is a borderline paper. The idea is sensible and the results are positive, but the novelty is limited and the experimental validation doesn't fully support the broader claims being made. I'd want to see the capacity exhaustion analysis and stronger baselines before I'd feel comfortable accepting this.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — incorporating structural locality into non-parametric language models — and the core intuition is sound. Real-world data does have hierarchical structure (code repositories, Wikipedia categories, etc.) and it seems reasonable to expect that leveraging this during retrieval would help. I've seen related ideas in document-level language modeling and retrieval-augmented generation, so the motivation resonates with me.

That said, I have some significant concerns about the paper as presented.

The proposed method is described as "simple yet effective," and honestly... it's a bit *too* simple. The core contribution seems to be adding learned parameters that upweight retrievals from local neighborhoods. After reading the method section twice, I'm still not entirely clear on exactly how these locality features are parameterized and how they interact with the base retrieval score. Equation 3 (I think?) is doing a lot of heavy lifting here and deserves more careful exposition. I might be wrong about which equation is central, but the notation felt underspecified throughout.

The experimental results are where I get more skeptical. The improvements on Java code are more convincing than on Wikipedia text, and the authors acknowledge "interesting differences" between the two domains — but this feels like a euphemism for "we don't fully understand why our method works inconsistently." I want a more direct engagement with this discrepancy. Is structural locality just more well-defined in code repositories than in Wikipedia? If so, that's an important limitation to state clearly rather than wave away.

Table 2 shows the main results and the gains are modest. Not embarrassingly small, but modest. In my experience, results at this margin often don't survive across datasets or slight changes in experimental setup. I'd feel better about the contribution if there were more ablations — specifically, what happens with different granularities of locality structure? The paper somewhat addresses this but not thoroughly enough.

The related work section is fine but misses some relevant citations from the kNN-LM line of work, particularly more recent papers from 2022-2023 that have also tried to incorporate structural or temporal biases into datastore retrieval. The authors should engage with those more explicitly.

One genuinely nice part of the paper is Section 5's analysis of where locality features contribute — the per-position breakdown is informative and I found myself actually interested in that analysis. More of this would strengthen the paper considerably.

The writing is generally clear but gets sloppy in places, particularly in section 3 where some sentences are just too long and the paragraph structure breaks down.

Overall: the idea is legitimate and worth pursuing, but the paper in its current form doesn't fully deliver on the promise. The inconsistent results across domains and the somewhat thin theoretical/empirical justification make me hesitant. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with stronger experimental analysis and a more honest treatment of the Wikipedia results.

This paper presents AVCaps, a dataset of ~2000 video clips with separate captions for audio, visual, and audio-visual content. The motivation is reasonable — the observation that crowdsourced AV captions tend to be visually biased is something I've noticed anecdow in related work too, and having modality-specific annotations could indeed be useful for the community.

That said, I have pretty significant concerns about the scale and novelty here. 2061 clips and 28.8 hours is, frankly, small for a dataset paper being submitted to a top venue in 2024. For comparison, AudioCaps has ~50k clips, WavCaps is even larger, and even more specialized datasets like AudioSet-SL dwarf this in scale. The authors need to make a much stronger case for why depth (multiple caption types) compensates for this limited scale, especially if practitioners want to train models on this data. The 14% improvement in Sentence-BERT similarity is promising but I'm skeptical of how much this would hold up at scale or transfer broadly.

The LLM-generated caption portion is also a bit hand-wavy to me. Section 3 (I think it's around page 4-5?) describes using LLMs to generate balanced AV captions from the crowdsourced ones, but the prompt engineering details and the actual prompts used are either buried or missing entirely. I found this confusing after reading it twice. How much did the LLM just hallucinate content that wasn't in the original crowdsourced captions? What quality checks were performed beyond the human preference study? This feels underspecified for a dataset paper where reproducibility really matters.

Figure 2 showing the caption length distributions is helpful, but I wish the authors had done more rigorous analysis of semantic overlap between the three caption types. Just because captions were collected separately doesn't guarantee they're actually capturing modality-specific information — a visual caption for a video of someone playing guitar might still mention "music" or "sound." Is there any analysis of this cross-contamination?

The retrieval experiments are fine as a sanity check but they don't really stress-test the dataset's unique properties. I would have liked to see experiments that specifically probe whether modality-specific training captions help on genuinely audio-heavy content versus visually-dominant content separately.

I'm also not entirely sure the framing around "unlocking" question answering and new metrics (Section 5 or so) is well-supported — it reads like future work speculation padded out to fill pages.

I don't want to be entirely negative here. The core idea is sound, the annotation protocol seems careful, and there is genuine value in having this kind of modality-disaggregated resource. But dataset papers need to either be large enough to train on seriously, or methodologically novel enough to justify their size. Right now I'm not convinced this clears either bar sufficiently. I'd want to see stronger baselines, more rigorous analysis of caption quality, and an honest comparison to what a researcher could achieve by just using existing datasets with different training strategies.

Leaning toward rejection but open to being convinced otherwise.

This paper tackles video-conditioned policy learning with an information bottleneck approach for skill discovery, which is a reasonable problem to study. I've seen a lot of work in this space over the past few years and the compositional generalization angle is genuinely interesting — so let me share my honest thoughts.

The core idea is fairly intuitive: compress video representations so they only retain skill-relevant information, then hope that these compressed representations correspond to reusable skill primitives that can be recombined at test time. I think the motivation is sound but I found myself unconvinced that the information bottleneck is doing the heavy lifting here versus just acting as a regularizer. The paper doesn't really disentangle these two things empirically. After reading the method section twice, I'm still not entirely sure what makes the discovered skills "implicit" — the terminology feels a bit loose throughout.

On the experiments: the 2x improvement claim in the abstract sounds impressive, but looking at Table 2 more carefully, this seems cherry-picked from specific environment configurations. The average gains are considerably more modest, and in some settings the margins are within noise. I'd push back on leading with this framing. Also, I noticed the baselines are somewhat weak — where is the comparison against more recent video-conditioned imitation learning methods? The authors cite some relevant work but don't include them as baselines, which is a significant omission.

Figure 3 is trying to do a lot of work and I found it genuinely hard to parse. The visualization of "skill discovery" doesn't convincingly show that the model has learned semantically meaningful primitives — it looks more like clustering in embedding space, which could emerge from many objectives. I might be wrong about this, but I'd want to see some qualitative analysis of what the discovered skills actually correspond to in terms of robot behavior.

The theoretical justification in Section 3.2 feels a bit pro forma. The bound in Eq. 4 is a standard IB result and the step connecting it to compositional generalization is handwavy at best. In my experience, papers that claim IB provides generalization guarantees often struggle when you probe the assumptions carefully.

One thing I do appreciate: the experimental environments are reasonably diverse and the authors do test on genuinely unseen task compositions, not just held-out instances of seen tasks. That's the right evaluation setup and I give credit for it.

Overall I think this paper has a kernel of a good idea but the execution and evaluation need significant work before I'd be comfortable recommending acceptance. The claims outrun the evidence. I'd want stronger baselines, cleaner ablations that isolate the IB contribution specifically, and a more honest treatment of where the method fails or struggles. Not a rejection necessarily — revise and resubmit territory in my view.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — getting RL agents to generalize across environment variations without the sample inefficiency that plagues naive domain randomization. The core theoretical contribution (connecting Lipschitz regularity of the policy w.r.t. randomization parameters to variance reduction) is intuitive and I appreciate that the authors tried to ground their method formally. But I have significant concerns about both the theoretical and empirical aspects that I think need to be addressed.

On the theory side: the claim that minimizing the Lipschitz constant leads to low variance policies is presented somewhat loosely. The connection between the Lipschitz bound and actual policy variance in practice depends on assumptions about the distribution of randomization parameters that I don't think are stated carefully enough. Equation (3) in particular — I had to read this section twice and I'm still not fully convinced the bound is tight or even particularly informative in realistic settings. I might be wrong here, but it feels like the authors are conflating smoothness of the representation with smoothness of the policy, which are not the same thing when you have a nonlinear policy head on top.

The experimental section is where my concerns become more serious. The environments tested (I'm looking at the results in Table 2) are frankly quite simple, and I'm skeptical these results would hold in anything resembling a challenging continuous control benchmark. MuJoCo locomotion with meaningful physics randomization, for instance, is conspicuously absent. The comparison baselines also feel a bit cherry-picked — there's a whole literature on robust RL and sim-to-real transfer (e.g., Tobin et al., Peng et al. on adaptive domain randomization, RARL) that gets minimal engagement. "Equal generalization scores" in the abstract sounds good until you realize the generalization gap being closed is already small in the chosen environments.

I also found the description of how the Lipschitz regularization is actually implemented somewhat vague. The practical approximation used in training — is this a spectral norm on the representation layers? The paper gestures at this around page 6 but doesn't give me enough to reproduce the method confidently. This is a real problem.

One genuine positive: training on a single environment variation and regularizing toward robustness is a computationally appealing idea, and the sample efficiency gains shown in Figure 3 do look real. That's not nothing.

But overall, I think the paper needs substantially stronger experiments and a cleaner presentation of the theoretical claims before it's ready for publication at a top venue. The idea has merit — I don't want to be entirely dismissive — but right now it reads like a workshop paper that's been stretched. I'm recommending rejection, though I'd encourage the authors to resubmit with more rigorous evaluation.

This paper proposes using a conditional latent diffusion model to generate LoRA parameters for task-specific fine-tuning, which I'll admit is a reasonably interesting idea on its face. The core pipeline — autoencoder for parameter compression followed by conditional LDM — is straightforward enough that I could follow it without too much trouble. But after reading this twice, I have some fairly serious concerns about both the motivation and the experimental validation.

My biggest issue is the "so what" problem. The paper generates LoRA parameters conditioned on a task, but... you could just train LoRA directly. The authors gesture at generalization capability (the distribution shift observation near the end) but this feels severely underexplored and honestly a bit hand-wavy. I want to see this actually demonstrated rigorously, not pointed to as a curiosity. What is the actual use case here? Speed? Memory? The ability to sample diverse high-performing solutions? I genuinely couldn't tell after reading the paper.

The experimental section is where I think the paper really struggles. The baselines are weak — comparing against p-diff and a few other parameter generation methods is fine, but where's the comparison against simply training LoRA with different random seeds, or hyperparameter search? The performance numbers in Table 2 look reasonable but the margins are often small and I'm not convinced the variance is properly reported. How many runs? In my experience with these kinds of generative parameter methods, variance between runs can be substantial.

I'm also not entirely sure the autoencoder reconstruction quality is being properly scrutinized. Figure 3 shows some results but I found the analysis thin. What's the reconstruction error, and how sensitive is downstream task performance to that error? This feels like a crucial ablation that's missing.

The claim about "generalization capability" based on distribution differences (I think this is around Section 4.3?) is honestly the most interesting part of the paper, but it's treated almost as an afterthought. If generated parameters genuinely occupy a different — and better — region of the loss landscape, that would be a strong result worth a much deeper investigation. Right now it reads like the authors stumbled onto something and didn't fully chase it.

Writing is serviceable but has some rough patches, particularly in the introduction where the motivation feels circular. The related work is fine.

I might be wrong about the significance here — maybe I'm missing the forest for the trees on the application scenario — but as it stands I think the paper needs substantially stronger experimental justification and a clearer articulation of when and why you'd use this over just... running AdamW. Not ready for acceptance in my view. A major revision at minimum.

This paper tackles the question of how to define and measure "information" in the weights of a deep neural network, connecting this to generalization and invariance. I've spent a fair amount of time with this manuscript and have mixed feelings.

The core contribution — unifying Shannon MI and Fisher Information under a single framework via PAC-Bayes and relating this to invariance in activations — is genuinely interesting in principle. I found the framing in Section 3 fairly clean, and the connection to PAC-Bayes bounds is well-motivated. The key result linking information in the weights to invariance properties of learned representations (Section 4, if I'm reading the structure correctly) is probably the most novel part of the paper.

That said, I have real concerns. The paper feels somewhat disconnected from empirical reality. There are very limited experiments, and what's there doesn't convincingly demonstrate that the theoretical quantities being defined are actually tractable to compute or meaningful in practice. I kept asking myself: can we actually measure "information in the weights" as defined here for a ResNet or a transformer? The authors never really address this head-on. In my experience, papers in this space often define elegant theoretical quantities that are essentially uncomputable at scale, and I'm not sure this paper escapes that trap.

The treatment of "effective information in activations" I found somewhat undercooked. The definition feels a bit ad hoc, and I'm not entirely sure the relationship established in Theorem X (I believe it's around page 8) actually carries the interpretive weight the authors claim. I might be wrong here — I read this section twice and still wasn't fully convinced the connection to invariance is as tight as advertised. The argument seems to require assumptions on the prior that are never really justified empirically.

I also think the related work section undersells the relevant literature. Tishby's Information Bottleneck, Achille and Soatto's work on information in the weights, and more recent work by Dziugaite and Roy on nonvacuous PAC-Bayes bounds are all relevant but handled somewhat superficially. The relationship to Achille & Soatto in particular feels underexplored — they've made very similar claims about invariance and information complexity and I'd want to see a sharper delineation.

The writing is generally clear but occasionally imprecise. The abstract promises more than the paper delivers, which is frustrating.

Bottom line: there's a kernel of a good idea here and the theoretical framework has some elegance. But the lack of computational tractability, the thin empirical validation, and some gaps in the theoretical argumentation make me hesitant. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider if the authors could address the tractability question directly and sharpen the invariance result. Not a bad paper — just not ready.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant question about the durability of safety guardrails when LLMs are fine-tuned on downstream tasks. The central finding — that similarity between upstream alignment data and downstream fine-tuning data correlates inversely with guardrail durability — is intuitive in hindsight, but I think the authors deserve credit for actually measuring this systematically rather than just assuming it.

That said, I have significant concerns about the work as presented.

The core experimental setup feels underspecified. On page 5 (and into section 4), the similarity metric used to compare upstream and downstream datasets isn't rigorously defined until fairly late, and even then I found myself unsure whether they're measuring semantic similarity, lexical overlap, or something else. After reading this twice I still wasn't confident the metric is capturing what the authors intend. This matters a lot — the entire argument rests on this notion of similarity, and if the metric is noisy or poorly motivated, the conclusions are shaky.

The claim about "privacy" of alignment data strikes me as underexplored and honestly a bit tacked on. The diversity argument is reasonably supported by their experiments, but the privacy recommendation feels like it was added to make the paper's contribution seem broader. How exactly does privacy interact with durability in their framework? I'm not entirely sure the authors have a crisp answer here beyond "don't let adversaries see your alignment data so they can't craft similar fine-tuning sets." That's... obvious? I'd want to see a more formal treatment or at least stronger empirical evidence for this specific claim.

The experiments are limited in scope. I think they mainly test on one or two base models (Llama variants if I recall correctly from the paper), and it's unclear how general these findings are. In my experience, safety alignment behavior varies quite substantially across model families and scales, and the authors don't really grapple with this. Table 2 shows results that look reasonable but the variance across runs isn't reported, which makes me nervous about the reliability of the numbers.

I also want to push back on the framing. The paper presents this as a "systematic understanding" but I'd characterize it more as a preliminary empirical study. That's not necessarily bad, but the title and abstract oversell the theoretical grounding. There's no formal model of why similarity causes fragility — just empirical correlation.

The writing is generally clear, though the abstract is a bit meandering.

Overall: I think there's a real paper here, but it needs more rigorous treatment of the similarity metric, a cleaner separation of the diversity vs. privacy contributions, broader model coverage, and more honest scoping of the claims. As it stands I lean toward rejection, but I'd genuinely welcome a revision. This is a problem worth studying carefully.

This paper proposes MMARP, a multi-agent LLM simulation framework for financial market forecasting. I'll be honest — I was initially intrigued by the premise, but after reading this twice I have some significant concerns that I think need to be addressed before this could be considered for acceptance.

The core idea is reasonable enough: simulate buyers and sellers as LLM agents, aggregate their responses via next-token probabilities, and find an "intersection price" analogous to supply-demand equilibrium. I actually found the use of next-token weights to approximate a distribution over agent behaviors to be the most interesting technical contribution here, and I wish the authors had spent more time developing this idea theoretically rather than burying it in Section 3.2.

That said, I have real problems with the experimental evaluation. The baselines feel cherry-picked — comparing against "various financial LLMs" without being precise about which models, which versions, and under what prompting conditions is not acceptable in 2024. Table 2 shows MMARP outperforming competitors but I found it very hard to assess whether this is a fair comparison. Are the baseline LLMs given the same price range inputs? Are they tested zero-shot? This matters enormously for interpretation.

The claim in the title and abstract that LLMs "can understand value" is frankly oversold. What you're showing is that next-token probability distributions over price-related tokens correlate with market movements. That's interesting! But it's a much more modest claim than "understanding value," and I think this kind of hype actually weakens the paper.

I'm also not entirely convinced by the theoretical motivation for using supply-demand intersection as the forecasting mechanism. Real markets don't work this cleanly, and the paper acknowledges this briefly on page 5 but then sort of... moves on. The rationality assumption they're supposedly relaxing through aggregation is still lurking in the structure of the prompt design itself.

On the writing — it's serviceable but uneven. The introduction is clear, but Section 4 gets dense and I found myself re-reading several paragraphs. Figure 3 is genuinely helpful for understanding the method intuitively.

One thing I want to flag: the paper doesn't adequately discuss data leakage. If the LLM was trained on financial news or price data that overlaps with the test period, any "forecasting" performance is suspect. This is a well-known issue in LLM-based time series work and it gets maybe two sentences here.

I think there's a publishable idea somewhere in this paper, but in its current form the evaluation is insufficiently rigorous, the theoretical claims are imprecise, and the data contamination concern is unaddressed. I'd encourage a major revision with tighter experimental controls and more honest framing of what the results actually demonstrate.

This paper proposes a layer-wise adversarial defense method framed through an ODE perspective, applying perturbations to hidden layers proportional to back-propagated gradients, and derives two training algorithms based on Lie-Trotter and Strang-Marchuk splitting schemes. I'll share my thoughts directly.

The ODE framing is intellectually interesting and I appreciate the theoretical effort here — connecting neural network dynamics to operator splitting is not trivial and the paper does a reasonable job building this connection. The relationship to conventional adversarial training established in Section 3 is probably the strongest part of the paper conceptually.

That said, I have significant concerns. The empirical improvements, while described as "noticeable," look pretty modest to me when I look at Table 1 and Table 2 carefully. We're often talking about 0.3-0.8% improvements in robust accuracy, which in my experience is well within the noise of adversarial training runs given their sensitivity to hyperparameters and random seeds. I didn't see error bars or multiple runs reported, which is a serious omission for this type of claim. This needs to be fixed.

My bigger concern is motivation. Why are perturbations proportional to back-propagated gradients the right thing to do in hidden layers? The paper asserts this but I found the justification somewhat circular — the ODE formulation is presented as validating the design choice, but the design choice seems to have informed the ODE formulation. After reading Section 3 twice I'm still not fully convinced this isn't somewhat post-hoc theoretical dressing on an empirical observation.

The computational overhead is mentioned but I don't think it's treated seriously enough. Adversarial training is already expensive. Adding layer-wise perturbations increases this cost substantially depending on architecture depth, and Table 3 (if I'm reading it correctly) doesn't fully account for this when comparing to baselines. A fair comparison should control for training compute budget.

I'm also not sure the novelty claim over prior work on hidden layer regularization and feature-space adversarial training (e.g., work on intermediate layer attacks, feature denoising) is sufficiently distinguished. The related work section feels a bit thin here and some relevant citations seem missing.

The Strang-Marchuk scheme consistently outperforming Lie-Trotter is interesting and I wish there were more analysis of *why* rather than just reporting the numbers. This feels like an opportunity the paper misses.

Minor issues: notation in Equation 4 is a bit dense and could use clearer exposition. Also the abstract says "noticeable margin" which is somewhat subjective and slightly oversells what the tables show.

Overall I think this is a decent paper with an interesting theoretical angle but the empirical validation needs strengthening substantially before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance. The core idea might be publishable but the current draft needs more rigorous experimental methodology and a more honest assessment of computational costs.

This paper proposes an interesting extension to knowledge distillation where both student and teacher models learn shared encoder/decoder components to enable bidirectional communication, as opposed to the traditional one-way teacher→student transfer. The motivation is reasonable — the capacity gap problem in KD is well-documented, and I've seen this issue come up repeatedly in the literature (e.g., Mirzadeh et al. 2020 on teacher assistants, and more recently work on feature-level distillation).

That said, I have significant concerns about this work.

The novelty claim feels overstated. The idea of bidirectional or interactive KD isn't new — there's a body of work on mutual learning (Zhang et al., 2018) and collaborative distillation that the authors either don't cite or briefly dismiss. I found the related work section (pages 3-4) genuinely thin given how crowded this space has become. After reading the paper twice I still wasn't convinced the authors have a sufficiently clear articulation of what makes their approach fundamentally different from, say, feature alignment methods with learnable projections.

The technical contribution is also a bit murky. The encoder/decoder components in Section 3 — I understand the high-level idea, but the actual training objective in Equation 4 seems to just be a standard reconstruction loss plus task loss. Where exactly is the "interactive" part being learned jointly versus sequentially? The ablation in Table 3 helps somewhat but I think the paper needs a cleaner mechanistic explanation of why the bidirectional messages help beyond acting as a regularizer on the feature space.

Experimentally, the results look okay but I'm skeptical. The baselines in Table 1 seem cherry-picked — why no comparison with more recent strong KD baselines like MiniLLM or DistiLLM? The authors focus heavily on GLUE benchmarks which, honestly, feels a bit dated for a paper positioned around "foundation models." I'd want to see this evaluated on something more challenging.

I think Figure 2 is actually the most useful part of the paper and I wish the authors had spent more time explaining the message space geometry — that could be a genuinely interesting contribution if developed properly.

One more thing: the writing needs work. There are grammatical issues throughout (e.g., the abstract is cut off, which is not a great sign), and some claims in the introduction are made without citation. The phrase "many recent breakthroughs" appears three times in the first two pages.

My overall assessment: the core idea has merit and I don't think it's completely wrong, but the paper in its current form feels undercooked. The theoretical justification is thin, the experimental comparison is incomplete, and the novelty over existing bidirectional/mutual distillation work isn't convincingly established. I'd lean toward rejection but would be genuinely curious to see a revised version that addresses these gaps. Not there yet.

This paper tackles an important problem — the failure modes of IRM as originally proposed by Arjovsky et al. — and proposes both a new invariance penalty and an adaptive tuning rule for the original IRMv1 penalty coefficient. I've spent a fair amount of time with IRM-related work over the past few years, so I feel reasonably positioned to evaluate this.

My overall impression is mixed. The theoretical motivation for the proposed penalty is reasonably clear, and I appreciate that the authors engage seriously with the counterexamples from the literature (e.g., Rosenfeld et al., Kamath et al.). That said, I found the presentation in Section 3 quite dense and had to read through it twice before the key ideas clicked. Equation (5) in particular — the proposed penalty — felt like it appeared somewhat abruptly without enough intuition building beforehand. What does this penalty geometrically mean? Why should we expect it to avoid the failure modes? A paragraph of genuine intuition before the formal statement would go a long way.

The adaptive tuning rule is, honestly, the part I found most practically interesting. Choosing the penalty coefficient λ in IRMv1 is notoriously finicky, and this feels like a real contribution. But I'm not entirely satisfied with the theoretical justification for why the proposed adaptation rule converges to something meaningful. Proposition 2 (page 8, if I'm counting correctly) seems to rely on assumptions that may be quite strong in practice — I'd like to see a more explicit discussion of when these assumptions fail.

The microbiome experiments are a genuine differentiator from most IRM papers, which tend to stick to ColoredMNIST variants. I appreciate this. However, I'm skeptical about some of the experimental choices. Why are the "environments" defined as they are? The domain heterogeneity in microbiome studies is notoriously confounded by cohort-specific technical artifacts, and I don't see a serious discussion of whether the invariance being learned is actually causal or just capturing batch effects. This feels like a significant gap.

The synthetic experiments (Section 5.1) are fine but fairly standard. The setup closely mirrors what's been done before, so I don't learn much new from them.

A few smaller things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size. The related work section feels a bit rushed — there's no mention of the heterogeneous risk minimization line of work which seems directly relevant. And "nascent counterexamples" in the abstract is a slightly odd phrasing.

Bottom line: there are real ideas here and the microbiome application is genuinely novel context for IRM. But the paper needs clearer intuition for the proposed penalty, stronger justification for the adaptive rule, and a more careful treatment of what "environments" mean in the real-world experiments. I lean toward weak reject but could be convinced otherwise with revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored angle on RLHF — rather than improving the algorithm to better fit human behavior, the authors flip the question and ask whether we can shape human behavior to better fit algorithmic assumptions. I'll admit when I first read the abstract I was a bit skeptical, but after reading through the full paper I think there's something genuinely valuable here, even if the execution has some issues.

The three interventions (showing underlying quantities, training, and modifying elicitation questions) are well-motivated and the human studies are reasonably well-designed. The results in Table 2 are compelling enough that I think the core claim holds up. That said, I found the paper somewhat sprawling — it's trying to establish a new research direction AND validate three separate interventions AND connect this to downstream reward learning quality, and it doesn't fully succeed at all three simultaneously.

My biggest concern is the ecological validity of the training intervention. In practice, who is going to train annotators this way, and at what cost? The paper doesn't seriously grapple with the scalability question. The authors gesture at this in the discussion but it felt dismissive to me. In large-scale RLHF deployments (think the scale of InstructGPT or similar), the annotation pipeline is already a significant bottleneck. Adding a training regime changes the economics substantially.

I'm also not entirely sure the paper adequately distinguishes between "humans conforming to the preference model" and "humans suppressing genuine preference signal." This feels like a crucial distinction that gets glossed over around pages 6-7. If I'm a human annotator and you've trained me to express preferences according to, say, a Bradley-Terry model, am I actually giving you better data about my reward function, or am I just giving you data that's easier to fit? These are not the same thing and the paper sometimes conflates them.

The elicitation question modification results are honestly the most interesting to me and I think underplayed. The finding that small wording changes produce significant conformance improvements is practically very useful and requires minimal overhead. I wish this had been the focus of a deeper investigation rather than one-third of a three-part paper.

Figure 3 needs work — I had to read the caption three times to understand what was being plotted. The color coding is not intuitive.

Minor things: the related work section is decent but missing some relevant work on annotation interface design and cognitive bias in labeling tasks. The connection to the broader alignment literature feels a bit forced in the intro.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution to an underexplored space. The idea is novel enough that even a somewhat imperfect execution merits publication. I'd lean toward acceptance with revisions, primarily around the ecological validity discussion and clarifying the "better conformance vs. suppressed signal" tension. This could be a genuinely influential paper if tightened up.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem in diffusion-guided 3D generation. The core observation — that uniform timestep sampling creates a conflict with the optimization trajectory — is intuitive and the proposed fix (monotonically non-increasing timestep sampling) is elegantly simple. I appreciate papers that find a clean solution to a real problem.

That said, I have several concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

The theoretical justification feels somewhat hand-wavy to me. The argument in Section 3 essentially says "diffusion sampling goes from high to low noise, so optimization should too" — but this analogy isn't rigorously established. Score distillation is fundamentally different from ancestral sampling, and I think the authors gloss over this distinction too quickly. After reading the relevant sections twice, I still wasn't convinced there's a principled reason why this specific alignment should help, beyond the empirical observation that it does. The paper would be significantly strengthened by a more careful theoretical treatment, or alternatively, the authors should just be more honest that this is an empirically motivated heuristic (which is fine!).

The experiments compare against DreamFusion and a few variants, but the field has moved fast. Where is the comparison against ProlificDreamer, Magic3D, or Fantasia3D? The diversity results shown in Figure 5 look genuinely impressive, I'll grant that. But I'm skeptical of the qualitative comparisons — they cherry-pick examples where the baseline looks particularly bad. Table 1 helps, but the CLIP-score metrics are notoriously weak proxies for actual 3D quality.

I'm also not entirely sure about the sensitivity analysis for the specific choice of scheduling function. The paper tries a few options (Section 4.3) but doesn't give strong guidance on how to choose in practice. In my experience, these kinds of hyperparameter choices matter a lot for reproducibility, and the current treatment feels incomplete.

One thing that genuinely bothered me: the diversity comparison baseline seems unfair. The paper compares multiple runs with different random seeds, but were the baselines run with the same number of optimization steps? The convergence speed claim needs to be controlled more carefully.

The writing is generally clear, though there are awkward passages around equation 4 that I found confusing on first read. Minor thing but it matters.

Look, I think there's a real contribution here. The idea works empirically and is simple enough that people will actually use it. But the theoretical narrative oversells what is essentially a practical trick, and the experimental evaluation has gaps that weaken the claims. I'd want to see stronger baselines, a more honest framing of the theoretical contribution, and cleaner ablations before this is ready for publication. Lean reject for now, but I think this is fixable with revisions.

This paper tackles an important and practically relevant problem — the inference latency bottleneck in autoregressive NMT — and the core idea of decoupling output token generation from sequential conditioning is genuinely interesting. The fertility-based latent variable is a clever way to inject some structural prior that helps the model avoid the multimodality problem that would otherwise plague a fully parallel decoder. I've read a fair amount of work on this topic and I think the framing here is one of the cleaner ones I've seen.

That said, I have real concerns about the paper as presented.

The BLEU gap to the autoregressive baseline is non-trivial in most conditions. The authors somewhat paper over this by emphasizing the Romanian result (29.8) but if you look at Table 2, the English-German results are considerably weaker, and "2.0 BLEU relative" in the abstract is cherry-picking the best case. I'd want to see a more honest accounting of when this tradeoff is actually worth it in practice. 2-5 BLEU is a big deal for production systems.

The fertility prediction mechanism feels underspecified to me. How sensitive is performance to errors in the fertility predictions at test time? The ablation in Section 4.3 is suggestive but I don't feel like I fully understand the failure modes. What happens when the fertility predictor is badly wrong? Is there a graceful degradation or does the whole thing collapse? I'd find even a qualitative error analysis quite valuable here.

The policy gradient fine-tuning section (Section 3.3 I think, around page 5) is where I got a bit lost. The reward function choice feels somewhat arbitrary and the variance reduction story is not entirely convincing to me. I might be wrong but I suspect this component is doing less work than the distillation, and the ablation doesn't fully disentangle this.

On the latency claims — Figure 3 is the heart of the practical argument and I appreciate that they include actual wall-clock numbers rather than just FLOPs. But the comparison is on a single GPU setup and I'd be curious about batched inference scenarios which is arguably where autoregressive models are less disadvantaged anyway. The speedup story might look different at batch size 32 vs batch size 1.

The knowledge distillation motivation is well-argued and I found Section 3.1 genuinely insightful — the point about reducing the multimodality of the training distribution is elegant and empirically supported.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution with some presentation and evaluation gaps. The idea is novel enough and the results sufficiently interesting that I lean toward acceptance, but the authors need to be more careful about how they frame their results and should probably expand the analysis of fertility prediction errors. The latency vs quality tradeoff deserves more nuanced discussion than it currently gets.

This paper proposes Correlation Mode Decomposition (CMD), which clusters parameters by their temporal correlation patterns during training. The core idea is that parameters exhibiting synchronized behavior across epochs can be grouped together, enabling compact representation of training dynamics. I've read this twice and I think I understand the main contribution, but I have some significant concerns.

The connection to prior work is underdeveloped. The relationship to PCA-based methods for analyzing training dynamics (e.g., Goodfellow et al.'s work on trajectory analysis, or more recent work on loss landscape geometry) is glossed over. The authors claim to surpass "state-of-the-art" in compactly modeled dynamics but the comparison feels narrow — Table 2 only shows a handful of baselines and I'm not convinced these are the right ones. What about comparisons to gradient compression methods more broadly?

The theoretical justification is thin. Why should we expect parameters to exhibit these temporal correlations? There's a brief intuition given around equation (3) but it's hand-wavy. In my experience, claims about intrinsic structure in neural network training really need either theoretical grounding or much more systematic empirical validation across architectures. The ResNet and Transformer experiments are promising but feel cherry-picked.

Figure 3 is honestly confusing to me. The visualization of modes doesn't clearly convey what synchronized behavior actually looks like in practice, and I had to re-read the caption three times. Better visualization would help enormously here.

The federated learning section (Section 5.3 I think) is described as "preliminary" by the authors themselves, which is fair — but then why include it as a selling point in the abstract? It feels like padding. Either develop this properly or cut it.

On the positive side — and I don't want to be entirely negative here — the core observation about temporal parameter correlations IS interesting, and the algorithm is reasonably clean. The generalization improvements, while modest, are consistent across the experiments reported. I think there's a real paper here.

But I'm not sure the improvements justify the computational overhead of running CMD concurrently with training. The efficiency analysis in Section 4.2 makes some claims I'm skeptical of without more rigorous profiling. What's the actual wall-clock overhead on real hardware?

I might be wrong about this, but the whole framing around "modes" feels like it's borrowing conceptual weight from dynamical systems theory without fully delivering on that promise. The paper would be stronger if it either committed to that theoretical framing or dropped it.

Overall: interesting idea, incomplete execution. I'm leaning reject but would reconsider with substantial revision — particularly stronger baselines, clearer theoretical motivation, and a more honest treatment of the efficiency claims.

This paper builds on the ProxSkip framework of Mishchenko et al. and proposes GradSkip, which allows different clients to take different numbers of local gradient steps based on a local condition number argument. The core idea is sensible — not all clients are equal, and forcing uniformity in local computation seems wasteful. I appreciate that the authors are trying to address a genuine practical concern.

That said, I have mixed feelings about the contribution here. The theoretical result is clean and the proof technique seems sound (I checked the main convergence argument and the coupling with the communication complexity), but after reading this twice I'm not fully convinced the gap over ProxSkip is as significant as presented. The communication complexity is the *same* as ProxSkip — the improvement is purely on the computational side, specifically in reducing local gradient steps. In practice, for the federated learning settings that motivate this work, compute on clients is often cheap and communication is the bottleneck. So the savings being advertised may not matter much in the scenarios the paper is ostensibly targeting. I might be wrong about typical deployment conditions, but this tension feels underexplored.

The generalization to GradSkip+ in Section 4 is interesting and I think it's actually the more valuable contribution — recovering existing methods as special cases is non-trivial and suggests a unifying perspective. But this section felt rushed. The discussion around Proposition 4.2 and how specific compressors recover prior methods could have been much more explicit. I found myself cross-referencing several times and still wasn't sure which exact parameter choices recover, say, the DCGD-type methods.

On the experiments: I'll be blunt, they are weak. Toy problems are fine for sanity-checking theory, but for a venue like this, I'd expect at least one experiment on a real federated learning benchmark. Figure 2 and Figure 3 do confirm the theoretical claims qualitatively, but they don't tell us anything about whether GradSkip actually helps in practice. The "carefully designed toy problems" framing in the abstract is a bit of a red flag — it reads like the authors know this is a limitation.

The writing is generally clear and I appreciated the motivation in the introduction. The related work is thorough. Equation (3) and the surrounding discussion is well-presented.

One thing I'm genuinely uncertain about: the local condition number reduction — is this tight? The paper doesn't provide a lower bound argument, so it's hard to know if there's more slack here. This feels like an important open question that deserves at least a paragraph.

Overall, this is a reasonable incremental contribution with solid theory. The generalization to GradSkip+ has merit. But the experimental section needs serious work before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance, and the practical motivation could be sharpened considerably.

This paper tackles an interesting and genuinely hard problem — analog topology synthesis is notoriously difficult to automate, and the framing around practical design scenarios (SPICE-level output, detailed structural requirements) is a legitimate improvement over prior work that I've seen treat this almost as a toy problem.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The benchmark construction is what worries me most. 30 real circuits and 2000 synthetic ones — I kept coming back to this while reading. The synthetic data generation process (Section 4, around page 6-7) needs much more scrutiny. If the synthetic circuits are generated in ways that happen to align with how AnalogXpert structures its search, then the 40% success rate on synthetic data is essentially measuring self-consistency, not generalization. The authors need to be much more explicit about the independence between synthetic data generation and the method itself. I'm not fully convinced this has been addressed.

The comparison baseline is also somewhat unfair, or at least incomplete. Comparing against vanilla GPT-4o at 3% is a bit of a strawman — of course a raw LLM without domain-specific scaffolding will fail at this. I would have wanted to see comparisons against GPT-4o with basic prompting improvements, or against the prior work they criticize (the graph generation approaches). The 23% vs 3% on real data sounds impressive until you realize the baseline is essentially nothing.

On the technical side, I think the subcircuit library concept is actually the most interesting contribution here, and I found myself wanting more analysis of it. How sensitive is performance to library design choices? What happens when a target topology requires components outside the library? Figure 3 helps but doesn't fully answer this.

The proofreading strategy (Section 3.3) — I'll be honest, after reading this twice I'm still not entirely clear on when the stopping criterion triggers. Equation 2 or 3 (I'm spacing on the exact number) seems underspecified. In my experience with agentic LLM systems, the correction loop behavior really matters and deserves ablation, which the authors do partially address in Table 3 but not deeply enough.

Minor point: the writing is serviceable but there are awkward phrasings throughout that suggest non-native authorship — not a major issue but worth a careful proofread.

Overall: the problem is real, the approach is reasonable, but the evaluation methodology has enough holes that I can't confidently recommend acceptance. I'd want to see a stronger baseline comparison, clearer independence between synthetic benchmark generation and the proposed method, and deeper ablation of the library and correction components. The 23% real-circuit result is genuinely promising though — I don't want to dismiss this work, it's moving in the right direction.

Leaning toward weak reject, but would reconsider with author response addressing the benchmark construction concern specifically.

This paper proposes VMFTransformer, a probabilistic forecasting approach that decomposes the multi-horizon prediction target into directional and magnitude components, modeled via von Mises-Fisher and truncated normal distributions respectively. The core intuition is actually quite appealing — treating the future horizon vector as having separable angular and scale properties is a novel framing that I haven't seen explicitly stated this way before, though related ideas exist in the directional statistics literature.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work.

The experimental evaluation feels thin to me. Three benchmarks is not convincing in 2024 when forecasting papers routinely evaluate on 6-8+ datasets. I kept looking for results on ETTh1/h2, ETTm1/m2, Weather, Exchange, etc. — the standard battery — and they're absent. The six baselines chosen also feel somewhat dated or selectively curated. Where is PatchTST? TimesNet? iTransformer? These are obvious comparisons that any serious reviewer is going to ask about, and their omission makes me suspicious that the method doesn't fare as well against more recent strong baselines.

After reading the methodology section twice, I'm still not fully clear on how the VMF and truncated normal components are jointly trained. Equation (4) or thereabouts seems to handle this but the exposition is muddled. I'd like to see a cleaner derivation of the ELBO or loss function used. Also — and I might be wrong here — but the computational overhead of sampling from VMF in high dimensions can be non-trivial. There's no ablation or timing analysis addressing this.

The ablation study (Table 3, I believe) is appreciated but underpowered. You show that removing the VMF component hurts performance, fine. But what about the truncated normal specifically? Is a regular Gaussian not sufficient? This is the obvious baseline for the magnitude component and it's not tested.

I also found the writing in the introduction somewhat overwrought. The framing of iterative vs. direct methods as the central tension of the field is a bit reductive — in my experience this dichotomy has become less relevant as most competitive methods are already direct. The motivation needs updating.

On the positive side: the theoretical framing around angular preservation is genuinely interesting, and Figure 2 does a nice job illustrating the geometric intuition. If the authors can substantially expand the empirical evaluation and clarify the joint training procedure, there's something worth publishing here. But in its current form, I can't recommend acceptance. The core idea deserves more rigorous validation.

Recommendation: Reject. I'd encourage resubmission with stronger empirical grounding.

This paper proposes a learnable counterfactual attention (LCA) mechanism for singer identification, building on the prior work by Rao et al. (2021). The core idea is to replace random attention maps used in the original counterfactual attention framework with a dedicated learned branch that discovers "misleading but plausible" attention regions, thereby pushing the main branch toward more discriminative features. The general motivation is reasonable and I appreciate that the authors picked a specific, underexplored application domain (SID) to demonstrate their idea.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

First, the technical novelty feels incremental to me. The main contribution over Rao et al. is essentially swapping random attention with a learned branch. While I understand that's the whole point of the paper, the mechanism by which the counterfactual branch is trained to be "meaningful yet not overly discriminative" (this phrase appears multiple times and I found it somewhat vague even after reading the paper twice) relies on a combination of loss functions that feel somewhat hand-engineered. The justification for why these specific losses achieve the desired semantic property isn't fully convincing — I'd want to see more ablation or analysis, not just accuracy numbers. Table 2 provides some ablation but it's not thorough enough.

Second, the evaluation is quite narrow. Artist20 is a reasonable benchmark but it's honestly a pretty small and well-worn dataset at this point. I'm not entirely sure why experiments weren't run on larger or more recent SID datasets. This really limits the claims about "comprehensive performance improvement." Is this approach actually robust? Hard to say from one dataset.

Third — and this bothered me — the paper doesn't adequately discuss why counterfactual attention is particularly well-suited for audio/vocal features as opposed to vision. The original framework was designed for images. The authors essentially apply it to spectrogram representations, but the justification for this transfer is thin. Do the attention mechanisms over spectrograms carry the same semantic interpretation? I'm skeptical, and the paper doesn't engage with this question seriously.

The writing is generally clear but there are places (around Section 3.2 and the loss function descriptions) where I had to re-read several times to follow the exact training procedure. The figures help somewhat but Figure 2 in particular is cluttered.
