The fact that the noise can be isotropic and state-dependent (rather than the standard Gaussian, state-independent assumption) does cover cases that matter in practice, and I appreciate that the authors are explicit about the inexactness in the drift/diffusion coefficients. The boosting application in particular is something I haven't seen analyzed this way before, and that's interesting.

That said, I have significant concerns. The paper is quite dense and the writing is honestly rough in places. After reading this twice I still found myself confused about the exact relationship between Theorem 3.1 and the corollaries for specific applications — the constants are buried and it's hard to tell on a first pass whether these rates actually improve on prior work or just match them in a more general setting. The claim in the abstract that results "improve or cover most known estimates" needs to be substantiated much more carefully in the main text, not just in appendix comparisons. Table 1 helps but it's not sufficient.

I'm also not entirely sure the proof technique is novel enough to warrant acceptance at a top venue. The Girsanov-based coupling arguments used here are fairly standard in this literature — see e.g. Dalalyan & Karagulyan (2019), Cheng et al. (2018), and related work. I might be wrong, but I didn't see a clear articulation of where the *technical* novelty lies beyond the generalized noise model. The authors should make this explicit, page 5 onwards is where I got lost.

The condition on the noise (Assumption 2.3, I think) — is it clear this is satisfied for the boosting case? The argument in Section 5 felt hand-wavy to me. I'd want to see this verified more carefully or at least a cleaner reduction.

Minor things: the title says "Ito Chains" but the paper alternates between "Itô" and "Ito" throughout, which is sloppy. Also Figure 1 is barely referenced in the text. Some of the bound expressions in Section 4 have notation that's introduced later — this should be fixed.

Bottom line: the paper has a legitimate contribution but it reads like a first submission to a workshop, not a polished conference paper. The technical content may be there but it's not presented clearly enough for me to confidently assess the claims. I lean toward weak reject, with the hope that the authors significantly revise the exposition and provide cleaner comparisons to prior work.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — offline RL with limited data coverage — and the general direction of incorporating domain knowledge as a regularizer is sensible. I've seen a fair amount of work on offline RL over the past few years, and the core challenge of distribution shift under sparse data is real and worth addressing.

That said, I have significant concerns about this submission.

The abstract is vague to the point of being nearly uninformative. "Adaptively refines the initial domain knowledge" — what does this mean concretely? After reading the paper twice, I still found the actual mechanism somewhat murky. The description of the regularization term in Section 3 (around Eq. 4-6) needs substantially more exposition. I'm not entirely sure whether the "expert insights" are hand-coded rules, demonstrations, or something else — the paper seems to conflate these at different points, which is genuinely confusing.

On the experimental side, the results in Table 2 show improvements on some D4RL benchmarks, but the limited-data regime they construct feels somewhat artificial. They subsample standard datasets, which is fine as a proxy, but I'd want to see evaluation on at least one domain where data scarcity is a genuine real-world constraint rather than artificially induced. The variance across seeds also looks suspiciously low in several entries — I'd want to see more runs reported.

The comparison baselines are a bit thin. CQL and IQL are there, which is good, but given the paper's framing around domain knowledge integration, I'd expect comparisons to prior work on knowledge-informed RL or constrained policy methods. The related work section gestures at some of this but doesn't engage deeply enough with, say, work on reward shaping or safe offline RL that uses similar structural priors.

Figure 3 is honestly hard to read — the color scheme makes it difficult to distinguish curves, and the caption doesn't tell me enough to interpret the ablation without hunting through the text.

I think the core idea has merit. Using domain knowledge to regularize policy learning in low-data regimes is a reasonable and practically motivated approach. But the paper reads like it's not quite finished — the theory section feels bolted on, the writing is rough in places (several grammatical issues in Section 4), and the empirical evaluation doesn't fully support the strength of claims made in the abstract about "substantial average performance increase."

My honest assessment: this is probably a borderline paper that needs another revision cycle. The problem is real, the idea is okay, but the execution and presentation aren't there yet. I'd encourage the authors to tighten the technical description considerably and run stronger baselines before resubmitting.

This paper proposes f-FERM, a stochastic optimization framework for fair ERM using f-divergence measures, and extends it to a distributionally robust setting. I've read through this carefully and have a number of thoughts.

The core idea is reasonable — using f-divergences to unify various fairness constraints under a common framework that admits stochastic optimization is a natural and useful contribution. The connection to DRO is also interesting and I think the paper is trying to do something genuinely worthwhile here. That said, I have real concerns about the execution and presentation.

My biggest issue is the theoretical contribution. The convergence guarantees feel somewhat routine given the existing literature on stochastic optimization for constrained problems. After reading the theorem statements (Theorems 1 and 2, around pages 6-7), I found myself wondering what's truly novel here beyond a careful application of existing SGD/variance-reduction machinery. The authors don't do enough work to explain what makes their problem structurally harder than related work, and therefore why new theory was needed. I might be wrong and there may be genuine technical novelty buried in the appendix, but the main paper doesn't make this case convincingly.

The experiments are where I have the most complaints. The baselines are reasonable but the comparison methodology is sloppy in places. Table 2 in particular — I'm not sure the hyperparameter tuning is fair across methods. Did the authors use the same tuning budget for all baselines? This matters enormously when you're claiming superiority "for almost all batch sizes." The claim in the abstract about outperforming other baselines feels overstated given what I see in the figures. Some of the differences are within what I'd consider noise, and I didn't see error bars or confidence intervals reported consistently.

The distributionally robust extension is interesting but feels somewhat bolted on. The ℓ_p norm uncertainty sets are standard in DRO literature (Sagawa et al., Duchi & Namkoong, etc.) and I didn't come away with a clear sense of why this particular combination with f-FERM is non-trivial. The paper would benefit from a cleaner separation of what's new versus what's inherited from prior work.

Writing quality is generally okay but there are places where the notation gets heavy without sufficient intuition. The paragraph around equation (5) lost me on first reading. Also, "stochastic" is used somewhat loosely throughout — the abstract defines it as working with small mini-batches but later the usage drifts.

In my experience, papers in this space live or die by the clarity of the practical tradeoff story, and I think f-FERM has a genuinely interesting story to tell. But as written, it needs sharper theoretical motivation, cleaner experiments with proper statistical reporting, and a more honest positioning relative to DRO literature.

I'm leaning toward weak reject, with the suggestion that the authors tighten the theoretical narrative and fix the experimental methodology.

This paper proposes using image-guided diffusion to generate a spectrum of synthetic-to-real images and then scheduling training with a curriculum that gradually transitions from easier (lower guidance) to harder (higher guidance) examples. The core idea is interesting and the empirical results on iWildCam and ImageNet-LT look promising on the surface.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The motivation around "image guidance strength = difficulty" feels underspecified to me. The paper asserts (around the introduction and Section 3) that stronger image guidance produces harder samples while weaker guidance produces easier ones, but I found this relationship to be poorly justified theoretically. Why exactly does proximity to real data make something harder to learn? The intuition is hand-wavy at best, and I think the paper would benefit substantially from either an ablation or a more rigorous analysis showing this relationship holds empirically across datasets. Figure 2 provides some visual intuition but honestly it's not convincing.

The comparison to baselines also concerns me. In Table 1 and Table 2, the proposed method is compared against relatively standard augmentation and some LT classification methods, but I don't see comparisons against more recent diffusion-based augmentation work — there's been quite a bit of this in 2023 and it feels like the related work section is selectively ignoring some obvious competitors. I might be wrong about specific papers but this area has been moving fast.

The curriculum scheduling itself (Section 4.2 or so) — I found this confusing after reading it twice. The description of how guidance levels are selected per training stage isn't crisp. Is this a hyperparameter search? Is it adaptive? The algorithm description needs to be much cleaner.

On the positive side, the gains on tail classes in ImageNet-LT are genuinely impressive — going from 4.4% to 23.64% is a striking number and if it holds up under scrutiny this is the paper's strongest result. I'd want to see this broken down more carefully though. Are there classes where performance actually dropped?

The iWildCam results are more modest and I'm less sure the 2.7% OOD gain is really attributable to the curriculum design versus just having more synthetic data in general. The ablation in Table 3 helps somewhat but doesn't fully disentangle these factors.

Writing quality is okay but uneven — the abstract is a bit of a mess honestly, and phrases like "self-evolving AI" feel more like marketing than scientific description. Section 3 needs tightening.

My overall assessment is that this is a decent idea with real empirical promise, particularly for long-tail learning, but the paper needs more rigorous justification of its core assumptions and stronger comparisons. In its current form I lean toward rejection, but I think with significant revisions this could be a solid contribution. Not there yet.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — enforcing hard constraints during graph generation with diffusion models. The plug-and-play framing is appealing, and I appreciate that the authors don't require retraining the base model. That said, I have significant concerns about the technical novelty and the experimental evaluation that I think need to be addressed before this can be accepted.

The core idea (projecting intermediate diffusion samples onto a constrained manifold at each timestep) is conceptually straightforward, maybe too straightforward. This is essentially a constrained sampling approach analogous to what's been done in continuous diffusion for images (think manifold-constrained generation, CCDF, etc.), adapted for the discrete/graph setting. I think the authors underplay how much of the heavy lifting is done by the projection operator itself — and for many of the constraints they consider (e.g., fixing node degree sequences), these projections can be quite problem-specific and non-trivial to implement. The paper could do a better job distinguishing what is genuinely novel vs. what is inherited from prior work in constrained diffusion sampling.

On the experiments: the "up to 100% improvement" framing in the abstract is a bit misleading. After reading the actual tables (Tables 2 and 3), it becomes clear this is cherry-picked from specific constraint types where baselines essentially fail completely. The average-case improvements are more modest, and I'd want to see a more honest summary statistic. Also, I found the baseline comparisons somewhat weak — the main comparison seems to be against unconstrained generation and a few conditional models, but I didn't see a comparison against rejection sampling or other simple constraint-enforcement heuristics. In my experience, rejection sampling with a good base model is a surprisingly strong baseline that authors often conveniently omit.

Figure 3 is helpful but I found the visualization a bit hard to parse on first read. The caption could be clearer about what exactly the projection step looks like in practice for the molecular case.

I'm also not fully convinced by the theoretical framing in Section 3. The formalization of "hard constraints" seems reasonable but the connection to the projection operator and the guarantees on staying close to the data distribution feels hand-wavy. Proposition 1 is stated but the proof in the appendix seems to rely on assumptions that aren't clearly flagged in the main text — I'd want this tightened up.

One thing I genuinely liked: the application to drug-like molecules with valency constraints is well-motivated and the results there are more convincing. This feels like the strongest use case and the paper might benefit from leaning into it harder.

Overall, I think this is a decent paper with a useful contribution, but it needs stronger baselines, more honest reporting, and better theoretical grounding. Currently leaning toward weak reject, but I'd reconsider with a thorough revision.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem in RAG — the mismatch between what a compression model produces and what the target LM actually finds "familiar" (low perplexity). The core idea of combining decoding probabilities from both a compression model and a target model during evidence generation is clean and, I'll admit, somewhat elegant in its simplicity. Training-free is also a genuine selling point.

That said, I have some real concerns.

The technical contribution, while sensible, feels incremental. Equation 3 (the interpolated decoding objective) is essentially a product-of-experts combination that has appeared in various forms in the decoding literature — Contrastive Decoding, Proxy-Tuning, and related work come to mind immediately. The authors do cite some of this but I don't think they adequately distinguish FaviComp from these methods beyond the application context. After reading section 3 twice, I'm still not convinced the "familiarity" framing adds meaningful conceptual novelty over just saying "we do interpolated decoding between two LMs." The motivation is nice but the method is... straightforward.

The experimental results are genuinely strong in places — the 23.91% improvement number is eye-catching, though I notice this seems to be on a specific dataset/model combination (Table 2, if I'm reading correctly) and the average gains are more modest. I'd appreciate a clearer characterization of when the method helps most vs. least. The variance across settings is large enough that I think it warrants more discussion.

I'm also skeptical of the perplexity analysis in Section 4.3. Showing that FaviComp produces lower perplexity outputs and then using perplexity reduction as partial explanation for performance gains feels somewhat circular — you've literally optimized for this. The more interesting question is whether perplexity reduction is causally responsible for accuracy gains, and I don't think the paper establishes this convincingly.

The compression rate experiments are appreciated but I'd want to see more analysis of what actually gets preserved/dropped versus baselines. Qualitative examples in the appendix are helpful but cherry-picked by nature.

One thing I genuinely found confusing: the computational overhead of running inference through the target model during compression isn't discussed until quite late, and even then somewhat briefly. For large target models this could be a real practical concern and deserves more attention upfront.

Minor: the writing is generally fine but "proactively composes" in the abstract is a bit of a nothing phrase.

Overall I'm on the fence. The problem is real, the results are decent, but I think the novelty bar isn't clearly cleared and some of the analysis is circular. I'd want to see a revision that more honestly situates this relative to product-of-experts decoding literature and addresses the computational cost question more directly. Weak reject, but I could be convinced otherwise.

This paper attempts to provide theoretical guarantees for joint edge-model sparse learning in GNNs, specifically analyzing sample complexity and convergence rates. The topic is timely and I appreciate the ambition here — there's genuinely a gap in the theoretical understanding of why sparse training works so well empirically for GNNs.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The main theoretical results (Theorems 1 and 2) are derived under assumptions that I find quite restrictive. The two-layer GNN with structural data constraints is a fairly idealized setting, and while the authors acknowledge this, I'm not convinced the "insights are applicable to more general setups" as claimed in the abstract. This feels like hand-waving. What specifically breaks down with deeper networks? The paper doesn't address this seriously. In my experience reviewing theoretical ML work, this kind of gap between the stated generality and the actual proof scope is a red flag.

The claim that pruning lowest-magnitude neurons *provably* reduces sample complexity is interesting but I found the proof sketch in Section 4 hard to follow — after reading it twice I'm still not entirely sure the argument is tight. The dependency on the sparsity parameter s in the sample complexity bound (Equation 8 if I'm reading correctly) seems to assume a particular data distribution that may not hold in practice. I might be wrong here, but this needs clearer exposition.

The experimental section is underwhelming for a theory paper of this scope. Citation datasets (Cora, Citeseer presumably) are fine as sanity checks, but the synthetic experiments feel designed to confirm the theory rather than stress-test it. I'd want to see what happens when the structural assumptions are violated — even partially. Where does the theory break?

On the positive side, I think the framing of *joint* edge-model sparsification is genuinely novel and the connection between graph topology sampling and neural pruning under a unified theoretical lens is a contribution worth acknowledging. The related work coverage is decent, though the authors should engage more carefully with recent work on lottery ticket hypotheses applied to GNNs.

Minor issues: Figure 2 is nearly unreadable at print size. Some notation in Section 3 is introduced without definition (the ψ function around line 180-something?). The writing overall is serviceable but dense in places.

My bottom line: the theoretical contribution, while real, is narrower than advertised. The experimental validation is insufficient to support the broad claims. I lean toward rejection in current form, but this is genuinely borderline — the core idea has merit and with a more honest scoping of the results and stronger empirical analysis this could be a solid paper. I'd encourage the authors to revise rather than abandon this direction.

This paper proposes a meta-learning approach to automatically discover auxiliary tasks for image classification, framing the problem as learning sub-class label assignments that improve generalization on a principal classification task. The core idea is interesting and I appreciate the motivation — reducing dependence on hand-crafted auxiliary tasks is a real practical concern.

That said, I have significant concerns about the scope and depth of the empirical evaluation. The experiments are restricted entirely to CIFAR variants (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and one other configuration if I'm reading Table 2 correctly). After reading this twice, I kept wondering: why no ImageNet experiments, or at least something like Tiny-ImageNet? The claim that MAXL is "general" feels very hard to support based on three closely related benchmark datasets. In my experience reviewing papers in this space, this would be considered a fairly narrow evaluation for a method presented as a general framework.

The comparison to methods using human-defined sub-class hierarchies is kind of the crux of the paper — the whole point is that MAXL should be competitive without requiring that domain knowledge. The fact that it's only "competitive" (and not clearly better) is a somewhat underwhelming result, though I understand the authors are trying to be honest about this. Still, if the automated method is basically matching the human-defined one on CIFAR, that's actually pretty decent, but the paper doesn't sell this point convincingly enough.

I'm also not entirely satisfied with the description of the meta-learning update in Section 3. The bi-level optimization is described but I found the explanation of exactly how gradients flow through the label assignment process somewhat hand-wavy. Equation 3 or 4 (I'd have to go back and check) — the update rule there needs more justification. How sensitive is this to the meta learning rate? There's no ablation on this that I could find.

One thing I actually liked: the visualization of the learned sub-class groupings is genuinely interesting and gives some interpretability to what the meta learner is discovering. This should be highlighted more prominently.

The self-supervised framing in the title is a bit misleading to me — this isn't self-supervised learning in the contemporary sense (contrastive learning, masked autoencoders, etc.), and I think calling it that will cause confusion with readers coming from that community. Worth reconsidering the terminology.

The computational cost of the approach is mentioned briefly but not quantified carefully. Running a bi-level optimization is expensive. How does wall-clock training time compare to the baselines? This matters for practical adoption.

Overall, I think the idea has merit and this is a reasonable first paper on this direction, but it reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference submission. More rigorous evaluation and deeper analysis of the optimization behavior are needed before I'd be comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — growing neural networks during training without disrupting what's already been learned. The core idea is elegant: when adding new neurons, initialize them such that they're functionally equivalent to zero (so existing computations are preserved) but with maximally large incoming gradients (so they can learn quickly). The SVD-based solution to find this initialization is neat, and I appreciate that the authors framed it as an optimization problem with a closed-form solution.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The experimental evaluation feels thin for a paper making fairly broad claims. The authors demonstrate results on vision tasks (CIFAR, ImageNet from what I can tell), but the comparison baselines are weak. Random initialization with zero-output constraint is really the only serious baseline considered? In my experience, there's a decent body of work on network growing — Net2Net (Chen et al. 2016), progressive training approaches, and more recent NAS-adjacent methods — and the paper's positioning against these feels somewhat superficial. Table 2 in particular shows improvements that are real but... modest. I kept asking myself whether the complexity of the SVD-based initialization is actually buying much over simpler alternatives.

I also found the theoretical motivation a bit hand-wavy in places. The argument around equation (4) for why maximizing gradient magnitude leads to better training dynamics — I'm not entirely convinced this is as principled as presented. Maximizing initial gradients sounds good intuitively but could just as easily lead to instability or poor conditioning. The paper acknowledges this only briefly. I'd want to see ablations on exactly when GradMax helps vs. hurts.

The writing is generally clear, though Section 3.2 required me to read it twice before understanding the precise constraint being enforced. Figure 2 is helpful but the caption undersells what's being shown.

One thing I genuinely liked: the computational overhead analysis. The authors are honest that SVD adds cost and they quantify it. That's refreshing.

My bigger concern is novelty framing. The "don't disrupt existing computation" constraint is essentially the function-preserving transformation idea from Net2Net, and while GradMax extends this with gradient maximization, I'm not sure the delta is as large as the paper implies. The authors should engage more directly with this connection rather than treating it somewhat cursorily.

Overall: interesting idea, reasonable execution, but the paper needs stronger baselines, more rigorous theoretical justification for the gradient maximization objective, and a more honest comparison to prior work. I'm borderline on this one — leaning toward weak reject, but I could be convinced otherwise if the rebuttal addresses the baseline comparison issue seriously.

This paper proposes structuring few-shot examples as multi-turn conversations rather than concatenating them into a single prompt for instruction-tuned chat models. The core idea is simple and honestly kind of obvious in retrospect — if a model is trained on conversational data, give it conversational examples. I'll admit the intuition is reasonable and I was curious to see how well it held up empirically.

Unfortunately, after reading this twice, I have significant concerns about the execution and the claims being made.

The experimental setup feels thin. The benchmarks selected (I'm looking at the results in Table 2 and Table 3) seem cherry-picked or at least not comprehensive enough to support claims about "significantly improves performance... across tasks." What about tasks where this framing might actually hurt? In my experience, methods like this often have failure modes that authors conveniently don't report. The "particularly in low-shot scenarios" claim needs more careful unpacking — the variance across seeds isn't reported consistently, and some of the improvements look marginal to me. Are these differences statistically significant? I don't see any significance testing.

The comparison baselines also trouble me. The paper compares against "traditional few-shot prompting" but I'm not sure the baseline is implemented fairly. There's been substantial work on prompt formatting for chat models — Perez et al., Min et al. on rethinking the role of demonstrations — and the paper doesn't adequately engage with this literature. The related work section feels rushed.

I found the framing around "instruction-following abilities" (around page 4-5) vague. What does this actually mean mechanistically? The paper gestures at alignment with training distribution but doesn't really dig into *why* the conversational format helps. A small ablation or even some attention visualization would go a long way here. Right now it's mostly hand-waving.

The writing is generally clear but there are places where the claims outrun the evidence. "Reducing prompt sensitivity" is stated almost as fact but the evidence for this (Figure 3, I think?) is underwhelming — you're showing variance across maybe 3-4 prompt variants.

I might be wrong about this, but I also wonder whether the gains here are really about the conversational format per se, or just about having more tokens/context between examples, which could affect attention patterns independently of the dialogue structure. This confound isn't addressed.

Look, the idea is simple enough that it could make for a solid short paper or system note. But the current version overclaims, underdelivers on analysis, and doesn't situate itself properly in the existing literature on in-context learning. I'd want to see stronger baselines, significance testing, a genuine ablation of what's driving the gains, and more honest discussion of limitations before this is ready for publication at a top venue.

Leaning toward rejection in current form, but I'd genuinely encourage revision and resubmission — there might be something here worth pursuing properly.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — automating the construction of vision-language benchmarks to reduce human annotation costs and enable on-demand evaluation. The high-level idea is appealing, and I appreciate that the authors actually ran evaluations across seven LVLMs. That said, I have pretty significant concerns about both the technical depth and the validation methodology.

My main issue is essentially circular reasoning that runs through the entire paper. The framework uses LVLMs to generate and validate VQA tasks, and then evaluates LVLMs on those same tasks, with quality assessed by... LVLMs. The authors acknowledge this somewhat but I don't think they adequately grapple with it. If the judge models have systematic biases (and they do — this is well documented), those biases will propagate into both benchmark construction AND evaluation. The reliability analysis in Section 4 feels insufficient to address this. Comparing against human judgments on a small subset would have gone a long way here.

The text-to-image generation component also raises questions I couldn't find answered clearly. When images are generated by models like Stable Diffusion or DALL-E, they carry specific distributional biases and artifacts. How does this affect what's actually being measured? A model might fail on a generated image not because of genuine capability limitations but because the generated image is weird or unnatural in ways real photographs aren't. Figure 3 shows some generated samples but I found myself squinting at them wondering if I'd even answer those questions correctly.

The four observations listed in the abstract (and expanded in the results section) read more like restatements of expected behavior than genuine discoveries. "As task difficulty rises, performance gap widens" — I mean, yes? This is almost tautologically true and doesn't really validate the framework's usefulness.

I also think the comparison to existing benchmarks like MMBench, SEED-Bench, etc. is handled too briefly. The authors claim their approach is complementary but don't really demonstrate scenarios where AutoBench-V provides evaluative signal that existing benchmarks miss. That would be the killer experiment.

On the writing: the abstract has a grammatical issue ("the evaluation benchmark always demands") and the paper reads like it was edited in a hurry in places. Table 2 caption is unclear to me — I had to re-read it three times.

I'm genuinely not sure this is ready. The core idea has merit and I could imagine a stronger version of this paper making a real contribution. But right now the validation is too self-referential, the baselines are weak, and the insights feel shallow. I'd strongly encourage the authors to collect even modest human annotations to ground the reliability claims, and to think harder about what unique evaluative capability this framework actually provides that justifies using generated images over curated ones.

Borderline reject from me, leaning toward rejection. Happy to reconsider if the authors can address the circularity concern substantively.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question — whether LLMs actually "understand" concepts or merely pattern-match to produce correct answers. The motivation is solid and I appreciate that the authors are trying to move beyond simple accuracy metrics. The connection to theory of mind at the end of the abstract is intriguing, though I think it's somewhat oversold given what the paper actually delivers.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical execution and the novelty of the contribution.

The core idea of "conceptual consistency" — checking whether a model's responses to background knowledge queries can predict its response to an anchor query — is reasonable, but after reading sections 3 and 4 twice I'm still not entirely clear on the predictive modeling step. Specifically, how are the background knowledge responses aggregated before feeding into the predictor? The description around equations 2-3 feels underspecified to me. I might be wrong about this, but I think a reader trying to reproduce this would struggle. This needs to be tightened considerably.

The experimental setup using CSQA + ConceptNet is fine, but I worry about circularity here. ConceptNet is widely believed to have been part of the training data for many of the models evaluated. If the models have memorized ConceptNet paths, then "conceptual consistency" might just be measuring memorization coherence rather than anything like genuine understanding. The authors acknowledge this briefly but don't really address it — a sentence or two isn't sufficient given that this is arguably the most serious threat to the entire framework's validity.

The results in Table 2 are interesting — the variation across relation types is genuinely surprising to me — but the analysis feels shallow. Why do certain relations (e.g., "AtLocation") show consistently lower consistency? There's speculation but no real mechanistic insight. I wanted more here.

I'm also not convinced the scale analysis (Section 5.3) adds much. "Bigger models are more consistent" is not a surprising finding at this point in the field.

On the writing: the paper is generally readable but the introduction spends too long on motivation before getting to the actual contribution. The mountain example is cute but we're on page 3 before I understood what the method actually does. Real reviewers at top venues are busy — get to the point faster.

The theory of mind framing in the abstract and conclusion feels like it was added to make the paper sound more ambitious than it is. I'd either commit to that framing with real theoretical grounding or drop it entirely, because as written it reads like hand-waving.

Overall I think there's a kernel of a good idea here but the paper needs substantially more rigorous treatment of the circularity concern, clearer methodology, and deeper analysis of the interesting empirical findings it does uncover. In its current form I don't think it clears the bar for acceptance.

This is a well-written paper that tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — unsupervised style transfer — through a principled probabilistic lens. I'll admit I came into this review somewhat skeptical (there's a lot of hand-wavy "generative model for NLP" work out there), but after reading this twice I think the core contribution is genuinely solid.

The key idea of modeling non-parallel data as a partially observed parallel corpus is elegant. The derivation in Section 3 is mostly clean, and I appreciated the effort in Section 4 to draw explicit connections between the variational objective and existing methods like backtranslation. This kind of unification work is valuable — in my experience, reviewers and readers often undervalue it, but it's intellectually honest and useful for the field. That said, I'm not entirely sure the unification is as tight as the authors claim. The connections to adversarial losses in particular feel a bit hand-wavy to me around equation 6-7, and I'd want to see a more rigorous treatment of exactly under what conditions these equivalences hold.

The experimental results are fairly convincing across the board. Five different tasks is ambitious and I respect that. The sentiment and formality transfer results look strong. However, I have real concerns about the evaluation methodology. Automatic metrics for style transfer are notoriously unreliable — the authors use classifier accuracy and BLEU, which are standard, but the human evaluation feels thin. Table 3 in particular: how many annotators? What was inter-annotator agreement? This matters a lot for a paper making strong empirical claims.

The related language translation experiments are interesting but feel somewhat bolted on. I'm not sure this task belongs in a paper about "style transfer" and it muddies the narrative a bit.

My bigger concern is computational cost. The amortized variational inference approach presumably requires multiple forward passes, and I didn't see any serious discussion of training time or computational overhead compared to baselines. For practitioners this matters enormously. A brief runtime comparison in Table 2 or an appendix would have helped.

Minor things: Figure 2 is a bit hard to read at print size. The discussion of the prior (recurrent LM) choice feels underexplored — I'd be curious whether the results are sensitive to this choice, but there's no ablation.

Overall I think this paper makes a genuine contribution and the probabilistic framing is the right way to think about this class of problems. The writing is clear, the math is mostly rigorous, and the breadth of experiments is commendable. The weaknesses are real but not fatal. I lean toward acceptance with revisions, primarily around tightening the adversarial loss connection and strengthening the human evaluation reporting.

This paper introduces MathGAP, a framework for generating arithmetic word problems with controllable proof tree complexity, and uses it to study OOD generalization in LLMs. The core idea — systematically varying proof depth and width to probe easy-to-hard generalization — is sensible and the contamination concern motivating synthetic generation is legitimate.

That said, I have mixed feelings about the contribution here. The finding that LLMs degrade as problems get harder (deeper/wider proof trees) is... not surprising? I've been reviewing papers in this space for years and this has been shown repeatedly in various forms. The authors need to do more work convincing me that MathGAP reveals something qualitatively new that existing benchmarks like GSM8K, MATH, or even the harder ORCA-style datasets don't already tell us. The sentence ordering sensitivity result (Section 4.3 I believe) is actually the most interesting finding to me, and I'd love to see it developed further rather than treated somewhat briefly.

The framework itself seems technically sound. I appreciate that they generate chain-of-thought traces alongside problems — this is useful. But I found the description of the proof tree construction (around page 5-6) a bit hard to follow on first read. Figure 2 helps but the formal definition of "nonlinear" proof structures could be clearer. After reading it twice I think I understand what they mean, but this shouldn't require two readings.

A few specific concerns: First, the problems generated feel somewhat artificial — the numerical relationships are constrained in ways that real math problems aren't. I'm not entirely sure this limits the conclusions, but it's worth acknowledging more directly. Second, the evaluation is almost entirely on accuracy; I'd be curious whether the models' intermediate reasoning steps in the CoT traces are actually correct even when final answers are wrong. Third, Table 3 shows results across multiple models but the variance/error bars seem absent or hard to read — with generated data you have the luxury of running many trials, so this feels like an oversight.

I think the paper would be strengthened considerably by a more careful ablation of what's actually driving the performance drops. Is it the number of reasoning steps? The need to track multiple intermediate variables? The sentence complexity? Right now "depth and width" is doing a lot of explanatory work but these are correlated with other things.

The writing is generally clear though occasionally verbose. The related work is adequate but I'd push the authors to engage more directly with work on compositional generalization (SCAN, COGS, etc.) since MathGAP is essentially a compositional generalization study by another name.

Overall: decent paper with a useful artifact, but the analysis feels somewhat surface-level given the controlled setup the authors have access to. The framework enables much richer experiments than are presented here. Leaning toward weak accept — the benchmark itself has value for the community — but I'd want to see the authors dig deeper in the revision.

This paper tackles an interesting question at the intersection of memorization/generalization and the role of individual neurons/directions in deep networks. The core claim — that reliance on single directions predicts generalization — is intuitive and I think there's something real here, but I have significant concerns about the execution and framing.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The connection between single-direction reliance and generalization across the corrupted labels experiments (following Zhang et al.) is reasonably compelling. Figure 3 in particular tells a clean story, and I appreciated that the authors tested this across multiple axes (different hyperparameters, over training time, not just across corruption levels). The finding that class selectivity is a poor predictor of task importance also feels important and somewhat underappreciated — this pushes back against a lot of the neural tuning literature in a useful way.

That said, I have real problems with the paper. The central metric — "single direction reliance" — feels underspecified in the early sections. I had to read through to section 3 twice before I felt I understood precisely what was being measured. The notation around equation 2 is particularly sloppy. I'm not entirely sure the authors have ruled out that this metric is just a proxy for something simpler, like weight norm or effective capacity. The paper would be substantially stronger if it included a direct comparison showing that single-direction reliance predicts generalization *beyond* what simpler existing metrics already capture. This feels like a major gap.

The batch normalization analysis is interesting but I think the causal story is being oversold. Saying BN "implicitly discourages single direction reliance" based on these experiments is a stretch — these are correlational observations and the mechanism isn't really established. I've read Ioffe & Szegedy and subsequent mechanistic analyses of BN, and the story is complicated enough that I'd want much stronger evidence before buying this framing.

On the class selectivity experiments: the selectivity-vs-importance dissociation is the most novel contribution in my reading, but it gets somewhat buried. The ablation study in section 5 is suggestive but limited to fairly small networks if I'm reading Table 2 correctly. Does this hold in larger ResNets? The paper feels like it's making broad claims about generalization that are tested on a somewhat narrow set of architectures.

Minor issues: the related work section is thin on engagement with the representation learning and disentanglement literature, which seems directly relevant. Also Figure 5 is nearly unreadable in grayscale.

Overall I think this is a borderline paper. There's a real contribution here but the writing obscures it, the central metric needs better justification, and several claims are stated with more confidence than the evidence warrants. I'd want to see a revision that tightens up the metric definition, adds comparison baselines, and is more careful about causal vs. correlational language. Leaning toward weak reject but I could be persuaded otherwise.

This paper tackles an interesting and genuinely underexplored problem — spatial understanding in MLLM-driven image editing. The motivation is clear enough, and I've seen similar frustrations in my own work when trying to get GPT-4V or similar models to handle spatially-grounded instructions reliably. That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the presentation.

The "theoretical analysis" the authors keep referencing in the abstract (and throughout Section 3, I think around pages 4-5) is... not really theoretical analysis in any rigorous sense. It's more of an informal argument about why spatial information gets diluted in current pipelines. I don't mind informal motivation, but calling it "theoretical" repeatedly feels like overselling. This bothered me more with each read.

On the technical side, the two-stage training approach and the data engine are the core contributions. The data engine idea — enriching instructions with spatial information — makes intuitive sense, but I found the description of how spatial annotations are actually generated to be frustratingly vague. Figure 3 helps somewhat but I'm still unclear on the quality control pipeline. How much manual annotation is involved? What's the error rate on the spatial labels? These details matter a lot for reproducibility.

The benchmark construction is potentially the most valuable contribution here, honestly more than the model itself. But again, the paper undersells or underexplains it. How many images? What spatial relationship types are covered? I found Table 1 hard to parse on first read — the metric definitions needed to be clearer.

The experimental results are a mixed bag. The SOTA claims on "several key metrics" are doing a lot of hedging — which metrics, and why not all of them? I'm genuinely uncertain whether the gains are meaningful or cherry-picked. The qualitative examples in Figure 5 look decent, but I'd want to see failure cases. Real papers show failure cases. The absence here is a red flag.

One thing I'll give credit for: the problem framing is sharp and the examples in the introduction are compelling. "Change the clothes color of the leftmost person to red" is a great concrete illustration of where current systems fail.

But overall I think this paper needs significant revision. The writing quality is uneven — there are grammatical issues throughout ("even using an powerful MLLM model") and some sections read like they were written in a hurry. The related work coverage of spatial reasoning in vision-language models feels thin; there's a whole body of work on referring expression comprehension and spatial VQA that seems relevant but barely gets mentioned.

My bottom line: interesting problem, some promising ideas, but the execution and presentation aren't there yet. I'd encourage resubmission after substantial revision rather than rejection, but in its current form I can't recommend acceptance.

This paper presents UnrealZoo, an extension/collection built on top of UnrealCV that provides photo-realistic 3D environments for training and evaluating embodied AI agents. The core contribution is a set of diverse Unreal Engine environments along with tooling for data collection, RL training, and benchmarking, demonstrated on visual navigation and tracking tasks.

I'll be honest — I've reviewed a lot of simulation environment papers over the years, and this one feels a bit caught between being a systems paper and a research paper, without fully committing to either. The environments themselves look impressive visually (Figures 3-5 are genuinely nice), but the paper struggles to articulate what scientific questions are being answered beyond "more diverse environments are better," which... we kind of already knew?

The experimental section is where my concerns really pile up. The benchmarking on visual navigation and tracking is presented but I found it hard to extract clear takeaways. Table 2 shows performance numbers across environments but the variance is huge and the analysis feels superficial. Why do certain agents fail catastrophically in specific scenes? The paper gestures at "latency in closed-loop control" and "spatial reasoning" as challenges but doesn't really dig into whether these failures are environment-specific or fundamental algorithmic issues. I had to read the results section twice and I'm still not sure what the key message is supposed to be.

The comparison to existing simulators (Table 1) is the kind of feature checklist table I've seen a hundred times, and I'm always skeptical of these. The paper claims advantages in photorealism and diversity, but there's no rigorous perceptual study or quantitative comparison with, say, Habitat or AI2-THOR in terms of actual sim-to-real transfer performance. That would actually be compelling. Right now it's just "our environments look better" which is hard to evaluate objectively.

I'm also not entirely sure about the novelty over the original UnrealCV work. The paper positions itself as significantly advancing beyond UnrealCV but the technical delta isn't completely clear to me. More environments, yes. Better tooling, apparently. But the core infrastructure seems largely inherited.

That said — I don't want to be entirely dismissive. The scale of environments provided (the diversity of scenes, dynamic objects, playable characters) does seem genuinely larger than what's currently available, and there's real value in community resources like this. The tracking benchmark results in Section 5.2 are interesting and suggest the environments expose real algorithmic weaknesses. That's good! More of that analysis would strengthen the paper considerably.

The writing needs work throughout. Several sentences in the abstract and introduction are grammatically awkward and I had to re-read them. "reasoning the accordance of the spatial structure" (abstract, last line) doesn't quite make sense to me.

Overall: I think this could be a useful community contribution but in its current form the paper reads more like a technical report than a conference paper with a clear scientific thesis. Major revisions needed.

This paper tackles an important and underexplored problem — combining missing data handling with causal inference — and I appreciate that the authors are thinking carefully about the interaction between these two issues rather than just slapping an imputation step on top of a standard causal estimator.

That said, I have a number of concerns after reading through this twice.

The framing around "latent confounders" is the part that gave me the most pause. The paper seems to conflate two distinct things: (1) using a VAE to learn a lower-dimensional representation that serves as a deconfounding proxy, and (2) imputing missing covariates via a deep generative model. These are genuinely different operations with different justifications, and I found the theoretical treatment in Section 3 a bit hand-wavy about when and why the learned latent space actually satisfies unconfoundedness. I might be wrong, but I don't think Proposition 1 (or whatever the authors are calling it near page 5) fully closes this gap — the assumptions required feel quite strong and aren't empirically verified in the experiments.

The experimental setup worries me too. The baselines are... fine, but I'd have liked to see comparison against more recent work — GAIN, for instance, or the Miwae-based imputation approaches which seem directly relevant here. The authors compare mostly against older multiple imputation standards, which makes the improvements look more impressive than they might be against a fully modern benchmark. In my experience with causal inference papers, the choice of DGP for synthetic experiments matters enormously, and using only relatively low-dimensional settings with simple missingness mechanisms (mostly MCAR/MAR) undersells the difficulty of the problem the paper claims to solve.

Figure 3 is actually the most convincing result in the paper — the non-linear regime where the proposed method pulls ahead is clearly illustrated and I believe it. But the linear regime performance being comparable to simple baselines is somewhat deflating given the complexity of the proposed model.

I also found the writing uneven. The introduction is strong, but Section 4 gets quite dense and I think a cleaner algorithm box would help readers follow the multiple imputation variant. There are also some grammatical issues throughout that suggest the paper was perhaps rushed to submission.

The core idea is genuinely interesting and I think there's a publishable paper here. But right now it reads more like a technical report than a mature conference submission. The theoretical contributions need sharper statements, the experiments need stronger baselines, and the connection between the VAE latent space and causal identification needs to be made much more explicit. I'd encourage the authors to revise substantially. As it stands, I lean toward rejection, though I acknowledge this is a hard problem and the direction is promising.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored theoretical question in meta-learning: why does the train-validation split (as used in MAML and related methods) help compared to just using all task data for both inner and outer loop optimization? The motivation is clear and I appreciate that the authors are trying to provide rigorous justification for a practice that is widely adopted but poorly understood theoretically.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The main theoretical result (Theorem 1, around page 6-7) establishes that the train-validation split is necessary when label noise is large. I found the setup somewhat restrictive — the analysis assumes specific neural network architectures and gradient descent with particular step sizes, and I'm not entirely sure how much of the insight generalizes beyond these conditions. The authors claim this is "the first work" on this topic, which may be true for this exact framing, but feels slightly overstated given related work on implicit regularization and task-specific overfitting in meta-learning (e.g., the line of work around Baxter 2000 and more recent PAC-Bayes approaches to meta-learning that touch on similar issues).

The key intuition — that train-validation split prevents the prior from memorizing task-specific noise — is actually fairly intuitive to me and I think most practitioners already believe this. So the question becomes whether the formal treatment adds enough beyond confirming the obvious. After reading this twice, I'm not fully convinced the theoretical machinery yields insights that couldn't be stated informally. That's a somewhat harsh judgment, I know, but I think it's fair.

The experiments in Section 5 are decent but feel underpowered for a venue like this. The synthetic experiments confirm the theory (which they should by construction, so that's not very surprising), and the real dataset results on miniImageNet and Omniglot show modest improvements or at least validation of the split. But the experimental comparison feels thin — why not test on more recent few-shot learning benchmarks or compare against more baselines beyond the basic train-train setup?

Figure 3 in particular is a bit hard to read. The caption doesn't fully explain what we're supposed to take away, and the confidence intervals overlap in several conditions, making the conclusions less crisp than the authors suggest in the text.

On writing: the paper is generally readable but there are passages in the introduction and related work that feel repetitive. The notation around equation (3) is dense and could use a clearer walkthrough.

My overall assessment is that this is a worthwhile research direction and the paper has real merit, but in its current form the theoretical contributions feel somewhat narrow and the empirical support is limited. I'd encourage a revision that either broadens the theoretical scope or substantially strengthens the experimental validation. Leaning toward reject, but I'd genuinely reconsider with major revisions.

This paper proposes an extension of synthetic gradients (Jaderberg et al.) by drawing an analogy to TD(λ) in reinforcement learning. The core idea — using eligibility traces to reduce bias in synthetic gradient estimation — is conceptually clean and the RL analogy is well-motivated. I've spent a fair amount of time with both the synthetic gradients literature and the TD learning literature, so I feel reasonably positioned to evaluate this.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The theoretical contribution in Section 3 is the strongest part of the paper. The bias analysis is clear and the parallel to accumulate TD(λ) is genuinely insightful. Equation 7 and the surrounding derivation make the mechanics transparent. I appreciated this. However, I'm not entirely sure the "bias-free" claim in the title is fully justified — the paper shows bias *control* or bias *reduction*, but the title implies something stronger. After reading this twice I still couldn't find a proof that bias goes to zero in the limit, only that λ=1 recovers an unbiased target. That's an important distinction and the title feels a bit oversold.

The empirical evaluation is where things get shaky. The tasks are fairly simple — copy tasks, sequential MNIST — and I'm not convinced these adequately stress-test the method. Synthetic gradients were motivated partly by the desire to unlock computation in large-scale settings, but the experiments here don't demonstrate scalability at all. Table 2 shows improvements but the variance is high enough in several conditions that I'm not confident the differences are meaningful. Were these results averaged over multiple seeds? I couldn't find this clearly stated anywhere in the experimental setup, which is a real problem.

The biological plausibility section at the end feels tacked on honestly. I understand why authors want to make this connection — it's fashionable — but the argument is speculative and doesn't add much to the paper's core contribution. I'd either cut it or develop it much more carefully with concrete mechanistic claims.

A few smaller things: Figure 3 is hard to read, the color scheme doesn't reproduce well in grayscale, and the caption doesn't explain what the shaded regions represent. Also, the related work treatment of RTRL and other online gradient methods feels thin — there's a whole literature on approximating BPTT online (e.g., Tallec et al.'s UORO, Murray & Pearlmutter) that deserves more engagement given that this paper is positioning itself in that space.

I think there's a real idea here and the TD(λ) analogy is the kind of cross-domain insight that can be genuinely useful. But the paper needs stronger empirical validation, more careful claims about bias, and better engagement with the existing online learning literature. In my experience papers in this space live or die on whether they can show the method works at meaningful scale. Right now this reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference submission.

Leaning toward rejection, but I'd encourage a revision.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored problem — whether VLMs can identify "visual data-types" (rotations, noise, weather effects, sketches, etc.) as opposed to semantic content. The motivation is reasonable and I appreciate that the authors have put together what seems like a fairly comprehensive benchmark spanning 27 data-types and 39 models. That's genuinely a lot of work.

That said, I have some significant concerns about both the framing and the conclusions.

The central claim — that this capability "does not emerge from scaling" — feels overstated based on the evidence presented. The paper shows marginal gains for CLIP-style models and a drop for the largest autoregressive models, but I think calling this a definitive conclusion about scaling laws is a stretch. The largest models tested are 80B parameters, which is already not frontier-scale in 2024. GPT-4V, Gemini Ultra, etc. are conspicuously absent. I understand API costs are real, but the title makes a strong claim that the experimental setup doesn't fully support. After reading this twice, I kept coming back to this tension.

The finding about OpenFlamingo degrading at larger scale (Section 4, around the discussion of Figure 3 if I recall correctly) is actually the most interesting result in the paper, and I wish the authors had dug deeper here rather than treating it somewhat in passing. Why does this happen? Is it catastrophic forgetting of low-level features? Is it a training data issue? The caption fine-tuning experiment in Section 5 is suggestive but feels underdeveloped — it's essentially showing that if you tell the model about data-types during training, it gets better at identifying them. Well, yes. That's not surprising. I'd want to see ablations on how much data is needed, whether this hurts semantic understanding, etc.

I'm also not entirely sure the task definition is crisp enough. The boundary between "data-type" and "style" feels fuzzy in places. Cartoons and sketches being in the same taxonomy as JPEG compression artifacts and additive Gaussian noise is a bit of an odd mix. The authors gesture at a 4-category breakdown but I found the categories somewhat arbitrary — why these four? What's the theoretical grounding?

The writing is generally clear, though the abstract is a bit dense. Figure 2 is helpful for getting a sense of the dataset diversity.

On the positive side: the breadth of model evaluation is commendable, the dataset itself seems like a useful contribution to the community, and the core observation that VLMs have a genuine blind spot for low-level perceptual properties is real and worth highlighting. I've seen this informally in my own work with CLIP-based retrieval systems.

My overall feeling is this is interesting work that needs sharper claims and more thorough analysis. The current version reads somewhat like an extended empirical report. I'd lean toward weak reject but would be open to changing my mind if the authors address the scope of the scaling claims and flesh out the mechanistic analysis.

This paper proposes a variational approach to prompt tuning for vision-language models, framing the learned prompts as distributions rather than point estimates. The core idea is intuitive — overfitting in prompt tuning is a real problem, and treating prompts probabilistically is a natural way to address it. I've seen similar ideas applied in other transfer learning contexts (e.g., Bayesian fine-tuning of transformers) so the conceptual territory isn't entirely novel, but the specific application to VLM prompt tuning is reasonably well-motivated.

That said, I have several concerns. The variational objective (Eq. 4-6 if I'm reading the paper correctly) relies on a fairly standard VAE-style ELBO, and I found the justification for the specific choice of prior somewhat hand-wavy. The authors say the prior "captures the concept space of the language model" but it's not entirely clear to me how this prior is actually constructed or calibrated — I went back and re-read section 3.2 twice and I'm still not confident I understand this. This feels like the crux of the method and it deserves much more careful treatment.

The experimental results are honestly the strongest part of the paper. Surpassing CoCoOp by 1.6% on the standard benchmark is meaningful, and the new-class generalization numbers are impressive. Table 2 in particular is compelling. However, I'm a bit suspicious about the comparison setup — are all baselines using the same number of shots? The paper mentions 16-shot in some places but I couldn't find a consistent statement about this across all experiments. This needs to be clarified.

I also think the ablation studies (Table 4?) are somewhat thin. The authors show that removing the KL term hurts, which is expected. But what about sensitivity to the KL weight β? In my experience this hyperparameter is notoriously finicky in VAE-style training and I'd want to see that the method isn't just lucky on the benchmark datasets with a well-tuned β.

One thing I genuinely appreciated: the claim that the method can be integrated seamlessly with conditional prompt learning frameworks (like CoCoOp) is backed up with actual experiments, not just a theoretical claim. That's good practice.

Minor issues — the writing in the introduction is a bit bloated and could be tightened. Figure 1 is not very informative in its current form; I looked at it and couldn't immediately grasp what the variational sampling was doing differently from point-estimate prompts. A better visualization would help.

Overall, I think this is a borderline paper leaning toward acceptance. The idea works empirically and the improvements are real. But the theoretical grounding needs to be tightened considerably, especially around the prior specification. If the authors can address that and clarify the experimental setup, I'd be more enthusiastic. As is, I'm cautiously positive but want to see revisions.

This paper tackles a genuinely interesting problem — how to extend mixup-style augmentation to graphs — and the core idea of using a soft assignment matrix to establish node correspondences before interpolating is reasonable and fairly well-motivated. I've seen a few papers in this space over the last couple of years (ifMixup, G-Mixup, etc.) and this fits into that emerging literature.

That said, I have some real concerns I'd want addressed before feeling comfortable recommending acceptance.

The soft assignment matrix computation is central to the whole approach, but I found Section 3.2 somewhat underspecified. How sensitive is the method to the temperature parameter in the Sinkhorn iterations? The ablation in Table 3 touches on this but feels thin — I'd want to see more systematic sensitivity analysis. Also, the computational cost of computing these assignments for every augmented pair during training seems non-trivial. The paper mentions this briefly but doesn't give actual wall-clock comparisons. In my experience, augmentation methods that are 3x slower than baseline often don't get adopted regardless of accuracy gains.

The experimental results are decent but not overwhelming. The improvements on several benchmarks in Table 2 are often within a percentage point or two, and I'm not always convinced the variance estimates support statistical significance. Are these results averaged over how many runs? I might be wrong but I think I saw 10 runs mentioned somewhere — if so, the standard deviations seem surprisingly tight for some datasets like PROTEINS.

The noisy label experiments (Section 4.3) are actually the most compelling part to me. The gains there are more substantial and the intuition for why soft-alignment mixup would help with label noise is clearer. I wish the authors had leaned into this more rather than presenting it as almost an afterthought.

I found Figure 2 helpful for understanding the overall pipeline, but Figure 3 honestly didn't add much — the visualization of the assignment matrix is nice in principle but hard to interpret without clearer examples showing what "good" vs "bad" alignments look like.

One thing that bothers me: the method requires graphs to be the same size after alignment (or padding), and the paper's handling of graphs with very different sizes feels hand-wavy. What actually happens when you're mixing a graph with 8 nodes with one that has 80 nodes? The padding strategy affects the learned representations in ways that aren't fully analyzed.

The related work is adequate but misses some relevant optimal transport literature that feels pretty directly applicable here — Vayer et al.'s Fused Gromov-Wasserstein work in particular seems like an important connection to make.

Overall: interesting idea, execution is okay but not great, and I think the paper needs more rigorous analysis before publication. I'm on the fence, leaning toward weak reject, but a strong revision addressing the computational cost issue and the sensitivity analysis could change my mind.

This paper tackles conditional/selective classification under agnostic settings, specifically looking at sparse linear classifiers with halfspace selectors under Gaussian distributions. The problem is genuinely interesting and I think there's a real contribution here, but I have some concerns about the presentation and the strength of the results.

Let me start with what I found valuable. The positive result — the PAC-learning algorithm achieving O(sqrt(opt)) error for homogeneous halfspace selectors — is the most interesting piece of work here. After reading section 3 twice I'm reasonably convinced the technical approach is sound, though the proof sketch in the main body is quite compressed and I had to take several lemmas on faith. The hardness results are also appreciated, and I think the reduction from agnostic classification is clever, but honestly it felt somewhat expected given the existing literature on agnostic learning hardness (Daniely et al., Guruswami & Raghavendra, etc.).

My bigger concern is the sqrt(opt) guarantee. In my experience with the agnostic learning literature, this kind of "weak" approximation — where you get sqrt(opt) rather than opt + epsilon — is often a sign that the algorithm isn't doing something fundamentally optimal. The authors don't adequately justify why sqrt(opt) is the right target here. Is this an artifact of their technique or is there a lower bound suggesting you can't do better? This question is left completely open and I think it significantly weakens the contribution. The negative results in section 4 don't directly address this gap.

The related work discussion (pages 3-4) is okay but I think it undersells some relevant work on selective prediction and abstaining classifiers — Geifman & El-Yaniv's line of work comes to mind, and the connection to coverage-constrained learning feels underexplored.

On the technical side, I found Theorem 2 (the main positive result) to be stated somewhat awkwardly. The dependence on the sparsity parameter k and the dimension d isn't made sufficiently explicit in the theorem statement itself, you have to dig into the proof. For a theory paper this is a real presentation problem.

The Gaussian assumption feels both necessary and limiting. I understand why it's there — it's standard in the distribution-specific learning literature — but the paper would benefit from at least a paragraph discussing whether the positive results might extend to other log-concave distributions or whether the Gaussian structure is being used in a fundamental way.

I'm also not entirely sure about the computational complexity claims — specifically whether the reductions preserve the relevant parameters cleanly enough. I didn't find a bug exactly, but I wasn't fully satisfied by the argument in section 4.2.

Overall: interesting problem, the positive result is meaningful, but the paper needs better motivation for the sqrt(opt) bound, cleaner theorem statements, and more honest discussion of limitations. I lean toward weak accept but could be convinced either way. The writing needs polish — there are several places where sentences are just too dense.

This paper proposes CAN, a combination of contrastive learning, masked autoencoding, and diffusion-style noise prediction for self-supervised visual representation learning. The core idea is that these three objectives capture complementary aspects of visual structure — global semantic relationships, low-frequency spatial structure, and high-frequency details respectively. I've been following the SSL literature closely for several years and this kind of "let's combine the best of everything" paper always makes me a bit skeptical, so I read this one carefully.

The results are genuinely strong. The 74.8% linear probe on ImageNet with ViT-B is impressive, and the JFT-300M numbers are competitive. The 41% FLOP reduction over SimCLR is a meaningful practical contribution. I'll give credit where it's due.

That said, I have real concerns. The paper claims the framework is "simple" and "minimal" but combining three distinct objectives with presumably several loss weighting hyperparameters is... not that simple? I found the ablation section (Table 3 if I recall correctly) somewhat unsatisfying — it shows each component helps, but doesn't really illuminate *why* the noise prediction component adds value beyond what MAE already provides. What exactly is the functional difference between reconstructing masked patches and predicting noise on visible patches? The paper gestures at "high-frequency components" but I think this deserves more rigorous analysis. After reading this twice I'm still not fully convinced this isn't just MAE + contrastive loss with some extra noise thrown in.

The comparison to prior work also troubles me slightly. The SimCLR baseline — are they using the same augmentation pipeline? Same training schedule? I might be wrong but some of the gaps seem larger than what I'd expect given the incremental nature of the combination. The experimental setup section needs to be more precise about these details.

I'm also a bit puzzled by the claim of scalability. The paper shows JFT-300M results which is good, but there's no real scaling curve showing how CAN behaves as model size increases versus the baselines. "Scalable" in the abstract feels like a marketing claim more than a demonstrated empirical fact.

The writing is generally clear but the motivation for why these three things are *complementary* in Section 2 feels hand-wavy. The claim about contrastive learning "shaping the embedding space across a batch" while MAE works on "single image samples" — this is true but it's not obvious this complementarity is the *mechanism* driving the gains versus just having more training signal.

Minor thing: the code URL is a placeholder (www.xxx.yyy), which at submission time is fine I suppose, but reproducibility concerns are real here given the number of moving parts.

Overall I lean toward weak accept. The empirical results are solid enough that the community would benefit from this work, but the paper needs to do significantly better at explaining *mechanistically* why this combination works, not just that it does.

This paper tackles early-exit dynamic neural networks, specifically the train-test mismatch problem that arises when gating mechanisms and inference modules are trained independently. The core idea — jointly training these two components — is sensible and the motivation in the introduction is reasonably well-articulated. I've read a fair amount of work in this space (Teerapittayanon et al., Schwartz et al. on "Right Tool for the Right Job", and more recently work on BERT-based early exit) and I think the authors have identified a real problem.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The experimental evaluation feels thin to me. The authors demonstrate improvements on classification benchmarks, but I kept wanting to see stronger baselines. Where is the comparison to CALM (Schuster et al. 2022)? Or the more recent work on learned routing in mixture-of-experts style architectures that has obvious overlap here? The related work section acknowledges some of these but the empirical comparison doesn't. After reading the results tables (Tables 2 and 3) twice, I'm still not convinced the improvements are robust — the gains on some datasets are within what I'd consider noise territory, and there's no statistical significance reported anywhere.

The uncertainty quantification story is interesting but honestly feels bolted on. The authors claim in the abstract that their approach "enables better uncertainty characterization capabilities," but Section 4.3 dedicates maybe two paragraphs to this and the evaluation is weak — comparing ECE scores without really interrogating whether the model produces well-calibrated confidence at different exit points. I think this could be a genuinely compelling contribution if developed properly, but right now it reads like an afterthought.

On the technical side, I found the description of the joint training objective (around Equation 4-6) somewhat hard to follow. The notation gets cluttered and I'm not entirely sure the gradient flow through the gating mechanism is handled correctly. The authors should clarify whether the gating decisions are treated as hard or soft during training — I think I understand what they're doing but I shouldn't have to guess.

One thing I did appreciate: Figure 3 is genuinely informative and the computational savings analysis is honest about the tradeoffs. I also think the framing around the positive confidence bias in standard threshold-based gating (Section 2.2) is the strongest part of the paper — that's a crisp observation.

My overall feeling is that this is a decent workshop paper that needs more work to clear the bar for a top venue. The idea is sound, the writing is mostly clear, but the experiments need to be more comprehensive and the uncertainty story needs to either be cut or substantially expanded. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit after addressing these points. Leaning toward reject in current form.

This paper introduces GitChameleon, a benchmark dataset of 116 Python code completion problems designed to evaluate LLMs' ability to generate version-specific code. The core idea is genuinely useful — I've been frustrated myself watching code generation benchmarks ignore the temporal dimension of software libraries, so I appreciate what the authors are trying to do here.

That said, I have significant concerns about the scale and methodology. 116 problems is... not a lot. I get that manual curation is expensive, but for a benchmark paper to be convincing, I'd want to see something more substantial. After reading this twice, I kept coming back to the question: are 116 problems enough to draw statistically meaningful conclusions about model capabilities? The variance in pass@k estimates with this sample size must be quite large, and I didn't see adequate confidence intervals reported anywhere in the main results (Table 1 or 2, I forget which). This feels like a real oversight.

The evaluation methodology using pass@10 is reasonable but I think the authors undersell the difficulty of interpreting these numbers. GPT-4 at 39.9% — is that actually bad? Compared to what baseline? A random code generator? A model that just copies the old API? The paper needs a clearer lower and upper bound to contextualize performance. What does 100% look like? Is it even achievable? I'm not entirely sure the authors have thought this through carefully enough.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the version coverage. How were the specific library versions chosen? The paper mentions popular libraries but I didn't find a clear methodology for selecting which version transitions to include. This matters enormously — cherry-picking dramatic API changes would inflate difficulty artificially.
