The error feedback experiment showing GPT-4 improving from 39.9% to 43.7% is interesting but honestly underwhelming in terms of the delta. I think the authors could do more with this — maybe multiple rounds of feedback? There's a more interesting paper buried here about iterative refinement.

One thing I genuinely liked: the distinction between syntactic correctness and functional correctness via execution is the right framing, and the unit test design seems thoughtful based on the examples shown in the appendix.

My main recommendation is major revision. The authors need to either expand the dataset significantly or provide much more rigorous statistical analysis justifying why 116 problems is sufficient. They also need stronger baselines and clearer contextualization of what the performance numbers actually mean in practice. The writing is generally clear but the contribution feels thin for a standalone benchmark paper at this venue. I might be wrong about the 116 number being a dealbreaker — maybe the community will find even a small but high-quality benchmark valuable — but as submitted this needs more work.

This paper tackles dynamic data pruning / importance sampling for training efficiency, which is a topic I've spent quite a bit of time thinking about. The core observation — that many existing methods can be framed as weighted Bernoulli sampling — is actually a useful unifying perspective, and I appreciate that the authors try to derive the optimal distribution under this framework rather than just proposing another heuristic.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as it stands.

The theoretical contribution in Section 3 feels incremental. The result that optimal Bernoulli sampling weights proportional to gradient norms is... not surprising? This mirrors what's known for sampling with replacement (which the authors themselves cite), and the extension to the Bernoulli case, while technically correct, doesn't feel like it required substantial new machinery. I might be wrong about the difficulty here, but after reading the derivation twice I wasn't convinced this warranted the emphasis it receives.

The practical contribution — using EMA of historical statistics to avoid the extra forward pass — is reasonable engineering but I'm not sure it's novel enough on its own. Methods like GradMatch, CRAIG, and others have explored proxy signals for gradient importance. The paper's positioning relative to these feels a bit thin, especially in the related work. What specifically does the EMA formulation buy you over, say, just using loss values from the previous epoch? The ablations in Table 2 gesture at this but don't fully convince me.

The experiments are my biggest concern. The results on CIFAR-10/100 with ResNets are fine, but this is a well-worn benchmark at this point. I'd want to see ImageNet results, or at minimum results on something closer to a realistic training scenario. The speedup numbers reported (Table 1, if I'm reading correctly) also seem to conflate wall-clock improvements with theoretical FLOPs reductions — this needs to be clarified. Real training throughput depends heavily on implementation details.

Figure 3 is hard to read. The color scheme makes it genuinely difficult to distinguish the lines at small print sizes. Please fix this.

The probability smoothing piece (Section 4.2) feels somewhat tacked on. It's motivated informally and the ablation showing its benefit is modest. I'd either develop this more carefully or cut it.

One more thing: the writing has some rough patches throughout. "Dynamic data pruning, which also known as data importance sampling" in the abstract is a grammatical error, and there are several similar issues in the body. This isn't a dealbreaker but it does affect readability.

Overall: there's a kernel of a decent paper here but it needs stronger experiments, clearer novelty claims, and more careful positioning against the prior literature. I'm leaning reject at this stage, though I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can demonstrate compelling results beyond CIFAR.

This paper proposes a stochastic optimization method that incorporates forward step model building to adjust both stepsize and search direction, essentially introducing second-order information into SGD variants. The core idea of computing per-layer/per-group steps using a diagonal approximation is interesting and the robustness to initial stepsize is a potentially valuable practical property.

That said, I have significant concerns about this submission.

The theoretical contribution feels incremental. The convergence analysis (Section 4) largely follows standard SGD convergence proofs with minor modifications, and I didn't find the resulting bounds particularly illuminating. The authors claim convergence rates comparable to or better than vanilla SGD, but given the additional computational overhead, it's not clear this is a meaningful improvement. I'd want to see more careful discussion of the per-iteration cost — the paper buries this somewhat and I had to go looking for it. Table 1 shows wall-clock improvements in some cases but not all, and the explanation for why the method underperforms on certain tasks (e.g., the CIFAR-100 results look notably weaker) is hand-wavy at best.

The experimental section is where my frustration really grows. The baselines chosen are somewhat dated — comparing against Adam, SGD with momentum, and a couple of line search methods is fine, but in 2024 there are more competitive adaptive methods worth including. I'm not entirely sure which version of AdamW was used; the hyperparameter tuning procedure for baselines isn't described with enough precision to reproduce. This matters a lot because the claimed robustness advantage could easily evaporate with proper baseline tuning.

I also found Figure 3 confusing on first read — the caption doesn't adequately explain what "model building iterations" refers to in this context, and I had to cross-reference with Section 3.2 to understand what was being plotted. Similarly, Equation (7) is introduced somewhat abruptly without sufficient intuition for why this particular quadratic approximation form was chosen over alternatives like those in L-BFGS or AdaHessian.

The per-parameter-group diagonalization idea (Section 3.3) is honestly the most novel part of this paper, and I wish the authors had leaned into this more heavily. The motivation drawing on the layer structure of deep networks is genuinely interesting. But right now it feels underdeveloped.

Minor things: the writing is generally okay but there are awkward phrasings throughout (e.g., page 6, the sentence beginning "Noting that convergence..."). The related work section misses some relevant work on curvature-aware stochastic methods from the past few years.

Overall, I think there's a kernel of a good idea here but the paper needs more work — stronger baselines, cleaner theory discussion, and a more honest accounting of computational costs. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit with these issues addressed. This isn't a bad paper, it's just not ready.

This paper proposes adding structural objectives to the GAN discriminator to encourage it to learn better representations, combining a coarse-scale Gaussian regularizer with fine-scale local clustering. The core idea is interesting and I think there's genuine value in exploring discriminators as representation learners rather than treating them as throwaway components.

That said, I have a number of concerns.

The motivation for the specific choices made — particularly the Gaussian assumption at the coarse scale — feels underspecified. Why Gaussian? The authors mention "smoothness and diversified representation" but I don't find the theoretical justification convincing. I read Section 3 twice and I still couldn't fully convince myself that the proposed regularizer meaningfully distinguishes itself from existing approaches like InfoGAN or BiGAN beyond the engineering details. The connection to prior work on feature regularization in discriminators (e.g., the line of work from Donahue et al., Chen et al.) deserves more careful treatment.

The experimental results are... fine, but not particularly convincing at the margin. Table 2 shows the proposed method competing with contrastive approaches, but "competing with" is doing a lot of work here — in several settings the gap is non-trivial and the authors' framing of this as roughly equivalent seems optimistic. I'm also concerned about the evaluation protocol. Linear probe accuracy on ImageNet is now a pretty well-understood benchmark, but I'd want to see more downstream tasks to believe the representations are genuinely general-purpose. What about transfer to detection or segmentation? The claim that this method is valuable partly because it avoids data augmentation is interesting, but I think the authors need to be more careful — the GAN training itself implicitly encodes inductive biases about the data distribution that aren't entirely dissimilar from augmentation.

The writing is generally clear but gets dense in Section 4. Figure 3 is helpful but Figure 4 honestly confused me — the caption doesn't explain what the color coding represents until you hunt through the main text.

One thing I genuinely appreciated: the authors are upfront that the generator quality doesn't degrade significantly with these added objectives, which addresses an obvious concern. The ablation in Table 3 is useful.

My bigger worry is about the practical significance of this work. Contrastive methods have moved fast. SimCLR, MoCo v2, BYOL — these are now quite mature and the performance gap has widened since some of the baselines cited here. I'm not sure this paper is benchmarking against the right contemporaries, and I might be wrong about the submission timeline, but it reads like it's chasing a target that has moved.

Overall I think this is a borderline paper. The idea is legitimate and worth exploring, the execution is reasonable, but the empirical story isn't strong enough to be fully convincing and the theoretical grounding needs shoring up. I'd lean toward weak reject but I'm open to being persuaded otherwise.

This paper proposes unfolding the reweighted ISTA (IRL1) into a trainable network called RW-LISTA for recovering cluster-structured sparse signals. The core idea is reasonable and fits within the now fairly crowded LISTA-style literature. I've read a fair number of papers in this space — LISTA, ALISTA, LISTA-CPSS and related work — so I feel reasonably positioned to evaluate this.

My main concern is novelty. The contribution feels incremental to me. Unfolding reweighted ISTA is a fairly natural extension once you've seen LISTA, ALISTA, and the many variants that followed. The "cluster structured sparsity" framing is interesting, but I'm not convinced the paper does enough to distinguish itself from LISTA-CPSS and related structured sparsity networks. The authors need to be much clearer about what specifically is novel here beyond "we use reweighting." After reading this twice I still wasn't entirely sure what the theoretical contribution is versus what's purely empirical.

The global vs. local reweighting distinction (Section 3, if I recall correctly) is actually the most interesting part of the paper, but it's underdeveloped. The intuition behind why local reweighting should capture cluster structure better is gestured at rather than rigorously argued. I found this confusing — there should be either a more formal justification or at least a cleaner ablation that isolates exactly what the reweighting is buying you.

On the experiments: the comparisons seem reasonable but I have questions about fairness. Are all baselines trained with the same number of layers and comparable parameter counts? I couldn't find a clear statement of this. Table 1 (or whichever table shows the main NMSE results) shows improvements, but the margins in some settings look modest. I might be wrong, but some of these differences might not be statistically meaningful — no error bars or variance across trials is reported, which is a real problem.

The writing needs work. There's a typo in the abstract itself ("rewegihting") which honestly doesn't inspire confidence. Several sentences in the related work section are awkward. "Benefit from available training data" is not grammatical. These are fixable but suggest the paper was submitted in a hurry.

I also think the paper undersells the convergence behavior. Do the learned weights preserve the convergence guarantees of IRL1? This seems important and is essentially not discussed.

In my experience papers in this area live or die by their theoretical grounding — ALISTA worked because it had clean theory. This paper is more empirical, which is fine, but then the experiments need to be airtight. Right now they're not quite there.

I'm leaning toward rejection but I'd encourage the authors to revise. The core idea isn't bad, but it needs sharper positioning, stronger experiments with proper statistics, and a cleaner story about what the reweighting mechanism is actually doing theoretically.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — the saturation of single-agent self-improvement loops — and proposes a fairly intuitive solution: instead of one model improving itself, maintain a society of models that specialize via multiagent interaction. The core idea is clean and I think the motivation is solid. Diminishing returns in iterative self-play/self-distillation is a real observed phenomenon, and trying to preserve diversity through multiple specialized agents makes intuitive sense.

That said, I have several concerns that prevent me from being enthusiastic about this work.

First, the experimental setup. After reading this twice, I'm still not fully convinced the baselines are fair. The single-agent self-improvement baseline — is it given the same total compute budget as the multiagent system? If you're running N agents and aggregating their outputs, a single agent with N times the inference budget during data generation might close much of the gap. This feels like the critical comparison and I don't think the paper handles it adequately. Table 2 in particular needs a more careful accounting of this.

Second, the "specialization" claim. The paper argues that agents specialize across different reasoning strategies, which is appealing. But the evidence for genuine specialization feels thin to me. Figure 3 shows some diversity metrics but I'm not entirely sure these capture what we care about — are the models actually learning qualitatively different reasoning chains, or just surface-level stylistic variation? I'd want to see some qualitative analysis or case studies. The paper gestures at this but doesn't deliver.

Third — and this is maybe my biggest gripe — the theoretical grounding is essentially absent. I understand this is an empirical paper, but some intuition about *why* independent data streams prevent collapse would strengthen the contribution considerably. Right now it's presented almost as an empirical observation without much explanatory depth.

On the positive side, the benchmark coverage is reasonably broad (math, commonsense, and a couple other reasoning tasks), and the improvement over rounds of finetuning is the most compelling result. The single-agent approach clearly saturates faster, and that's a real finding. I also appreciate that they test this across multiple rounds rather than just one or two iterations — that's more work than many similar papers do.

I might be wrong, but I also wonder about the sensitivity to the number of agents. Is 3 agents meaningfully different from 5? The ablations on this (Section 4.3 I think) are underdeveloped.

The writing is generally clear, though Section 3.2 gets a bit dense and could use reorganization.

Overall: interesting idea, real empirical signal, but the execution leaves important questions unanswered. I lean toward weak reject, primarily because the compute-controlled baseline issue feels like a significant gap. I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can address this directly — it's not an insurmountable problem, just needs to be confronted head-on.

This paper tackles an interesting extension of the linear representation hypothesis, moving beyond binary contrasts to handle features and categorical/hierarchical concepts. The core theoretical contribution — representing features as vectors rather than just directions, and showing how hierarchical concept structure maps onto geometric relationships between these vectors — is clean and, I think, genuinely useful for the field.

That said, I have some concerns I want to flag before I can feel confident about this work.

The theoretical results in Section 3 feel well-motivated, but I found the jump from Equation (3) to the geometric claims about polytopes somewhat compressed. After reading this twice, I think I follow the argument, but a reader less steeped in this literature might lose the thread. The authors should consider expanding the intuition here — maybe an additional toy example beyond what's in Figure 2? The figure itself is helpful but the caption does a lot of heavy lifting.

On the empirical side: 900+ concepts from WordNet sounds impressive, but I'm a bit skeptical about how cleanly WordNet's hierarchy actually maps onto the kinds of representations LLMs have internalized. WordNet encodes a specific, somewhat arbitrary lexicographic organization. The fact that geometric relationships *mostly* hold isn't necessarily validating the theory so much as showing LLMs correlate with WordNet — which we kind of already knew from embedding literature going back to word2vec days. I might be wrong about this, but I think the authors need to engage more seriously with this confound. Table 2 in particular shows some notable failure cases that are brushed past a little too quickly in the discussion.

The connection to previous work on the geometry of representations (Park et al., Elhage et al., etc.) is handled reasonably well, though I'd have liked to see more direct comparison to the "superposition" framing. Are these views compatible? In tension? The paper sort of gestures at this on page 6 but doesn't commit to a position.

One thing I genuinely liked: the proof of the hierarchical structure theorem (Section 4) is, to my knowledge, novel and the argument is elegant. Even if the empirical validation is imperfect, this theoretical result has value on its own.

Minor stuff: the notation gets a bit heavy around equation (7) and I had to flip back to the definitions multiple times. And honestly the abstract slightly oversells the empirical scope — "validate" is a strong word for what are still fairly indirect measurements.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a real theoretical contribution, but the empirical section needs more critical self-examination. I'm leaning toward acceptance, but I'd want the authors to address the WordNet confound and tighten up the discussion of failure cases. Borderline accept from me, with some revision needed.

This paper presents SPA-VL, a large-scale safety preference alignment dataset for vision-language models. The core contribution is straightforward: a dataset of ~100K quadruples (question, image, chosen/rejected response pairs) covering 6 harm domains and 53 subcategories, constructed using responses from 12 VLMs.

I'll be honest — I have mixed feelings about this paper. The dataset itself seems useful and I appreciate the scale, but I have some fairly significant concerns about the methodology and claims.

My biggest issue is with the automated quality assessment pipeline. The paper claims the dataset is "high-quality" but the validation of this claim feels circular to me. You're using VLMs to evaluate responses generated by... VLMs. The authors acknowledge using GPT-4V as a judge in places, but in my experience with similar work (e.g., papers building on RLHF-V, LLaVA-RLHF), the quality of preference labels is absolutely critical and deserves much more rigorous human validation than what's described here. Table 3 shows some human evaluation numbers but 200 samples feels inadequate for a dataset of 100K entries, especially when you're making strong claims about data quality. I'd want to see inter-annotator agreement statistics and a more systematic audit.

The experimental results in Section 4 are where I think the paper is weakest. The improvements on safety benchmarks look decent, but the evaluation suite is somewhat narrow. The authors test on SafetyBench and a few others, but there's no evaluation on more adversarial settings — jailbreaking, prompt injection, the kinds of attacks that are increasingly relevant in deployed VLMs. After reading this twice, I'm also not fully convinced the "helpfulness" improvements are real rather than artifacts of the specific benchmarks chosen. The MMBench and MME scores barely move, which could mean the alignment is working correctly, or could mean the model is gaming the safety metrics.

The categorization scheme (6 domains, 53 subcategories) is presented somewhat uncritically. How were these categories chosen? The paper references some prior work but I didn't see a clear justification for why *these* 53 subcategories are the right ones vs. alternative taxonomies. This matters because coverage gaps in the taxonomy become coverage gaps in the dataset.

One thing I genuinely found interesting is the cross-model response collection strategy — using 12 different VLMs to generate responses does seem like a reasonable way to get diversity in the rejected/chosen pairs, and Figure 2 illustrating the pipeline is clear.

The writing is generally fine but the abstract oversells things ("significant milestone" is a bold claim for what is essentially a dataset paper, however useful).

I'm leaning toward weak reject. The dataset contribution is real and probably useful to the community, but the paper needs stronger validation methodology and more honest evaluation. I might be wrong about the sufficiency of the human evaluation — perhaps the authors can clarify what additional checks were done — but as presented, the quality claims outrun the evidence.

This paper proposes replacing gradient descent inference in SPENs with a learned "inference network" — a neural network trained to directly approximate argmax inference. The core idea is clean and the empirical results are fairly compelling, particularly the 10-60x speedup on multi-label classification. I've read this twice now and I think the contribution is real, though I have some concerns about framing and experimental rigor.

Let me start with what I think works. The large-margin training objective for jointly training the energy function and inference network (Section 3) is well-motivated, and I appreciate that the authors connect this to prior work on structured SVMs. The label language model augmentation is an interesting idea that I hadn't seen applied in quite this way before, and the POS tagging results in Table 3 do suggest it helps with long-range dependencies. That said, I wish there was more analysis of *why* it helps — the paper just kind of reports the number and moves on.

My main concern is about the baseline comparisons. The speedup claims (10-60x) are prominently advertised but I found the experimental setup in Section 5.1 a bit underspecified — what hardware? What batch sizes? These things matter enormously for wall-clock comparisons and in my experience authors sometimes inadvertently stack the deck here. I'm not accusing anyone of bad faith, but Table 1 needs more detail.

On the sequence labeling experiments: the claim that inference networks perform "comparably to exact inference" in CRFs is interesting but I found it somewhat unsatisfying. Comparably within what margin? The differences in Table 2 look small but I'd want confidence intervals. Also, is this really surprising? If you train a network to mimic Viterbi on a relatively simple model, of course it can do so approximately. The more interesting question — which the paper gestures at but doesn't fully answer — is whether this scales to cases where exact inference is genuinely intractable.

I think the paper undersells its own contribution in places and then overclaims in others. The framing around CRFs as a "demonstration" (Section 5.3) feels like it was added late. It reads like a separate experiment tacked on, and I found myself confused about how it connects to the main thread of the paper.

One more thing: the relationship to amortized inference (e.g., VAEs, the inference compilation work) deserves more than a sentence in related work. This is essentially amortized structured prediction inference and there's a growing literature there that the authors should engage with more seriously.

Despite these complaints, I think this is a solid contribution. The idea is timely, the implementation seems careful, and structured prediction researchers will find it useful. I lean toward acceptance but would want the authors to address the experimental details and the baseline comparisons more carefully in a revision. The writing is generally clear, though Section 4 gets dense and could use another pass.

This paper proposes PerturbGCL, a graph contrastive learning framework that sidesteps data augmentation by instead perturbing the model architecture — specifically through weight pruning (weightPrune) and randomized message propagation steps (randMP). The core motivation is reasonable: data augmentation for graphs is notoriously finicky and domain-sensitive, so exploring model-space perturbations as an alternative is a legitimate research direction.

That said, I have real concerns here.

The motivation in the intro conflates two distinct problems. The authors argue that data augmentation is hard because (1) it's complex to mimic in parameter space and (2) it introduces different per-node disturbances. But then their solution — weightPrune and randMP — doesn't really address (1) at all, and only loosely addresses (2). The framing feels retrofitted onto a solution that was designed independently of this stated motivation. I found this confusing after reading the intro twice.

On weightPrune specifically: pruning weights by magnitude to create a "dynamic perturbed model" is essentially just a form of dropout with a structured criterion. I'm not convinced this is meaningfully different from DropEdge or feature masking at the data level — you're still creating two views with different information bottlenecks, just in a different space. The theoretical justification for why magnitude-based pruning specifically is the right perturbation is quite thin. Why not random weight perturbation? Why not noise injection? The paper doesn't adequately address these alternatives.

The randMP strategy — randomly varying propagation steps — is simpler and I actually think more interesting, because different k-hop neighborhoods genuinely capture different structural information. But the two strategies feel somewhat bolted together without a principled story for why they're complementary. Figure 2 helps a bit but doesn't fully convince me.

Experimentally, I'm troubled by the baselines. The comparison against GraphCL and GRACE is fine, but these are not the strongest current baselines. Where are comparisons against more recent work like GCC, MVGRL with stronger augmentations, or the SimGRACE paper (which is actually very closely related and also does model-level perturbation — the authors cite it but I don't think they adequately differentiate from it)? The gains over SimGRACE in particular need much more careful analysis. On some datasets the improvements are marginal and within noise.

I also want to flag: the abstract is cut off mid-sentence. That's a basic submission quality issue.

In my experience, the "avoid data augmentation" framing is popular right now but often the methods still implicitly encode domain assumptions — they're just hidden in architectural choices. I'm not sure PerturbGCL escapes this.

Overall this is a borderline paper. The idea is worth exploring and there are some interesting pieces, but the current version lacks theoretical depth, has gaps in empirical comparison, and the narrative coherence between motivation and method needs substantial work. I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with major revisions.

This paper proposes combining Johnson-Lindenstrauss sketching with federated learning to simultaneously address communication efficiency and differential privacy. The core idea — iteratively applying random sketches to gradients before transmission — is reasonably intuitive, and I appreciate that the authors attempt a theoretical analysis showing the introduced randomness doesn't inflate overall communication rounds.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty and execution here.

The connection between sketching/JL transforms and federated learning is not new. There's a substantial body of work on compressed/quantized gradient communication (Alistarh et al., Wangni et al., Stich et al.) and the relationship to privacy amplification via subsampling/randomization has been explored in various forms. I think the authors need to do a much more thorough job positioning their contribution against this literature. After reading the related work section twice, I still wasn't clear on what's genuinely new versus a recombination of known ideas. This isn't automatically disqualifying — good synthesis papers exist — but the authors don't frame it that way, and their novelty claims feel overstated.

On the theory: the main convergence result (Theorem 1, I believe, around page 6) — I found the proof sketch unconvincing. Specifically, the argument that the de-sketching step at the server side recovers sufficient gradient information relies on assumptions about the sketch dimension that feel underspecified. What's the actual dependence on the number of clients, the gradient dimension, and the target privacy parameter? The bounds presented seem to hide constants that could make the result practically vacuous. I might be wrong here, but I'd want to see a more careful parameter instantiation with concrete numbers.

The privacy analysis is where I'm most skeptical. The paper claims privacy benefits from "randomly sketched gradients with low-dimensional noises," but the formal DP guarantees are vague. Is this (ε, δ)-DP? Local DP? Appendix B gestures at this but doesn't give clean theorems. In my experience reviewing DP papers, this kind of hand-waving is a red flag.

Experiments are weak. Figure 3 shows some communication savings on what appears to be MNIST or a similarly toy dataset — I'd want to see this on something like CIFAR or a language modeling task where the gradient dimension is large enough that sketching actually matters. The comparison baselines are also limited; no comparison to QSGD or similar quantization schemes.

Minor: the writing needs polish. Several sentences in the introduction are grammatically awkward, and "de-sketch" is used inconsistently as both a verb and noun.

I think there's a kernel of an interesting idea here but the paper in its current form isn't ready. The theory needs sharpening, the privacy claims need formalization, and the empirical evaluation needs to be significantly expanded. I'd encourage the authors to revise substantially rather than submit elsewhere without addressing these issues.

This paper proposes Hi-Patch, a hierarchical patch-based graph neural network for irregular multivariate time series. The core idea is to use intra-patch and inter-patch graph layers to handle variables with different sampling rates, building up representations progressively from local to global scales. I think the motivation is reasonable and the problem setting (IMTS) is genuinely underexplored compared to regular MTS.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work.

The experimental comparisons feel cherry-picked or at least incomplete. Looking at Table 2, the baselines for forecasting include some strong models but I notice the absence of several recent IMTS-specific methods — particularly mTAN and Raindrop, which are fairly standard in this space. Why aren't these included? The authors mention "8 datasets" but the characterization of what makes these datasets truly "irregular" varies a lot. Some of these feel like standard MTS benchmarks that just happen to have missing values, which is not quite the same thing as genuinely irregular sampling across variables. This distinction matters for the claims being made.

The architecture description is honestly a bit hard to follow. After reading Section 3 twice, I'm still unclear on how exactly the patch boundaries are determined when variables have different native sampling rates. Figure 2 helps somewhat but the notation in equations 3-5 is overloaded — the same subscripts are reused with slightly different meanings across the intra and inter-patch sections. I might be wrong but I think there's an inconsistency between the formulation in Eq. 4 and what's described in the paragraph following it on page 5.

The hierarchical aspect is the main selling point but in my experience this kind of progressive aggregation has been explored extensively in spatial-temporal GNNs (STGCN, MTGNN, etc.). The novelty delta over those works when applied to irregular settings is not argued convincingly enough. I'd want to see a more direct discussion of what specifically breaks in those methods when applied to IMTS and how Hi-Patch addresses it.

Ablation in Table 4 is appreciated but I'd like to see sensitivity to the number of inter-patch layers and patch size choices — these seem like critical hyperparameters for the hierarchical design and there's barely any discussion of how to set them.

On the positive side, the classification results look genuinely strong, and I appreciate that the authors test on both forecasting and classification tasks rather than just one. The code being available is good.

Overall though, I think the paper needs clearer exposition of the core mechanism, stronger justification of the novelty claim relative to existing hierarchical GNN literature, and more rigorous baseline comparisons specific to IMTS. In its current state I lean toward rejection, but I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can address the baseline gaps and clarify the architectural formulation.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — automating the next-best-view selection for active 3D reconstruction using a generalizable RL policy. I've reviewed a fair number of NBV papers over the years, and the core motivation here is solid. The limitation of rule-based methods tied to discrete action spaces is real and worth addressing.

That said, I have several concerns after reading through this carefully.

The scene representation combining action, geometric, and semantic embeddings (Section 3.2 if I recall correctly) is described as a key contribution, but honestly I found the ablation in Table 3 somewhat underwhelming. The gains from adding semantic embeddings seem marginal in some settings, and the paper doesn't give me great intuition for *why* semantics help here — the explanation feels hand-wavy. I'd want to see a deeper analysis, maybe some qualitative examples of failure cases with and without the semantic component.

The RL training setup concerns me a bit. Training in Isaac Gym with Houses3K and then evaluating generalization on OmniObject3D is a reasonable test, but the domain gap between architectural structures and general objects is quite large. The 83.61% coverage on OmniObject3D sounds decent but I'm not entirely sure how to contextualize this — what does a random or greedy baseline achieve on these object categories? Table 2 has comparisons but I think the baselines chosen are somewhat weak, and I'd push back on the claim of "outperforming prior solutions" as strongly as the abstract frames it.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the "free-space" framing. After reading this twice, I'm still not 100% clear on how the continuous action space is actually parameterized and constrained during execution. There's some discussion around equation 4-5 but the details feel underspecified. In my experience, this is exactly where these systems can fail in practice and the paper doesn't give enough implementation detail to reproduce or fully evaluate the claim.

The writing is generally okay but gets sloppy in places — there are grammatical issues scattered throughout and the abstract has a dangling clause ("Even with the recent advances... enable"). Minor but it affects readability.

On the positive side, the benchmark itself (with the Isaac Gym simulator integration) seems like a genuine contribution to the community, and I appreciate that the authors are releasing this. The 98.26% coverage on unseen buildings is impressive if it holds up.

Overall I think this is borderline. The idea is good, the execution is partially convincing, but the evaluation needs strengthening and some of the technical claims need tighter justification. I'd lean toward weak reject at this stage, but I could be persuaded if the authors can address the baselines and provide clearer analysis of when and why their representation design choices matter.

This paper proposes "neuron editing," a method for out-of-sample extrapolation by learning transformations in the latent space of an autoencoder and applying them to new domains. The core idea is intuitive — if you can characterize how a transformation shifts neuron activations in a latent space, maybe you can apply that shift to data the model never saw during training. I've seen related ideas in the style transfer and disentanglement literature, and honestly this feels like it's covering ground that's already been well-trodden without fully acknowledging it.

My biggest concern is the positioning. The paper claims to address a problem that's "less well studied," but out-of-sample generalization of transformations is literally the premise of domain adaptation, transfer learning, and a huge chunk of the disentangled representation literature. The authors gesture at some of this in the related work but don't seriously engage with, e.g., StarGAN, UNIT, or the biological batch correction literature (scVI, Harmony, etc. for the bio applications). The comparison baselines in the experiments feel cherry-picked as a result.

The method itself — I had to read Section 3 twice. The editing transformation as described in equations 3-5 is essentially computing a mean shift in latent space conditioned on the transformation class, which is... fine, but not novel. This is essentially what analogy-based methods have been doing since the word2vec days. The "neuron editing" framing makes it sound more mechanistic than it is.

The image experiments (Section 4.1, Figure 3) are qualitatively okay but there's no quantitative evaluation I found convincing. FID scores are reported but on what sample sizes? The drug synergy application (Section 4.3) is the most interesting part of the paper to me — predicting held-out drug combinations is a genuinely hard problem and the results in Table 2 look promising, though I'm not qualified to fully evaluate the biological validity. The batch correction results feel like they're competing against weak baselines.

A few smaller things: Figure 2 is confusing — the caption doesn't explain what the colors represent until you dig into the text. The ablation in Section 4.4 is appreciated but it's thin. I'd want to see what happens with different autoencoder architectures.

I think there's something real here, particularly for the biological applications where the extrapolation framing is most natural and the baselines are more fairly chosen. But as a general method paper, the claims are overclaimed and the experimental validation for the image tasks doesn't support the narrative. I'm leaning toward rejection but would be open to revising that opinion if the authors can seriously engage with the missing baselines and clarify what's actually novel beyond the mean-shift-in-latent-space interpretation.

This paper proposes OML-AD, an online machine learning approach for anomaly detection in non-stationary time series, implemented in the River Python library. I'll be honest — after reading this twice, I'm left with more questions than answers, and I think the paper has some fairly significant issues that need to be addressed before it's ready for publication at a top venue.

My main concern is the lack of methodological novelty. The core idea seems to be: use online learning models from River to predict the next value, compute residuals, flag large residuals as anomalies. I might be wrong about this, but if that's essentially the contribution, it's... thin. The paper doesn't clearly articulate what makes OML-AD fundamentally different from, say, just wrapping any online regressor with a threshold. The abstract promises a "novel approach" but I struggled to identify what exactly is novel beyond the engineering integration with River.

The experimental evaluation also worries me. The baselines chosen feel somewhat cherry-picked — I'd want to see comparisons against more recent deep learning-based approaches like LSTM-AD or Anomaly Transformer, which have become standard comparisons in this area. The datasets used are not described with enough detail in the main text, and I found myself hunting for information that should be front and center. Table 2 (if I'm reading it correctly) shows improvements in some metrics but the margins aren't always convincing, and there's no statistical significance testing anywhere.

The treatment of the threshold selection problem is inadequate. In my experience, this is THE hard problem in anomaly detection — how you set the anomaly threshold dramatically affects all your metrics — and the paper sort of hand-waves through it. Page 6 mentions a quantile-based approach but the sensitivity analysis is minimal.

I also found the writing in places to be quite rough. Some sentences in Section 3 are genuinely hard to parse on first read. The related work section is okay but misses some important recent literature on concept drift detection, which is closely related to what they're doing.

To be fair, there's something practical here. Online anomaly detection for non-stationary streams is a real and important problem, and having a clean implementation in River is useful for practitioners. I don't want to dismiss that entirely. But "we implemented existing techniques in a popular library" is closer to a software paper than a research contribution.

I'm recommending rejection in the current form, but I think with substantially stronger novelty claims, a more rigorous experimental section with better baselines, and a proper treatment of the threshold selection problem, this could become a solid paper somewhere. The authors clearly understand the problem space — they just need to make a stronger scientific case.

This paper proposes viewing RL algorithms as "hand-written information-state policies" in the BAMDP framework, and derives several theoretical results from this perspective. I read this twice because I wanted to make sure I wasn't missing something, and I think the core idea is genuinely interesting — but I have real concerns about the depth of the contributions.

Let me start with what I found compelling. The connection between BAMDP optimality and the observation that optimal algorithms need not converge to optimal MDP policies (Section 3) is a clean point, and I appreciate that it directly challenges a somewhat sacred assumption in the RL theory literature. The decomposition of BAMDP Q-values into exploration and exploitation components is also nice, and feels like it could be useful for practitioners trying to reason about intrinsic motivation bonuses. The reward shaping analysis in Section 5 is probably the most technically substantive part of the paper.

That said, I found myself increasingly frustrated as I read on. The paper reads more like a conceptual reframing than a genuine technical advance. Most of the individual results are either (a) known in some form already, (b) fairly direct consequences of applying existing theory to the BAMDP setting, or (c) stated at a level of generality that makes them hard to evaluate for practical usefulness. The potential-based shaping results in particular — I've been familiar with Ng et al. (1999) and its extensions for a long time, and I'm not entirely sure what's being added beyond restating those results in the BAMDP language. The authors need to be much more explicit about what is genuinely new.

The paper also suffers from a kind of conceptual ambiguity that bothered me throughout. When the authors say RL algorithms are "information-state policies," they're making an analogy, not a technical equivalence in all cases. I found this confusing in Section 4 in particular — the mapping between an arbitrary RL algorithm and a well-defined policy in the BAMDP requires assumptions that aren't always made explicit. What class of algorithms does this framework actually cover? I might be wrong but I don't think this is fully addressed.

The experiments, if you can call them that, are minimal. I understand this is primarily a theory paper, but even a small illustrative example showing the reward shaping bound (Theorem 5 or wherever it lands) producing meaningful predictions about algorithm behavior would substantially strengthen the case.

I also think the writing could be tightened significantly. There's a lot of "we will show" and "this suggests" that doesn't fully pay off. The abstract is actually more compelling than what the paper delivers.

In summary: the framing is interesting, some results are useful, but the contribution feels thin for a top venue. I'd want to see clearer delineation of novel results versus reframings of known results, and some empirical grounding. Borderline reject leaning toward rejection in current form, but I could be convinced otherwise with major revisions.

This paper proposes DiffAug, which combines a diffusion model with contrastive learning to generate positive pairs for unsupervised representation learning. The core idea is appealing — using a conditional diffusion model to synthesize semantically consistent augmentations rather than relying on hand-crafted transforms. I've seen a lot of papers in this space over the past few years, and the motivation is reasonable, but I have significant concerns about the execution and the claims being made.

My biggest issue is with the experimental setup. The results on Table 2 and Table 3 show improvements, but the baselines feel cherry-picked or undertuned. SimCLR and MoCo numbers reported here are notably below what I'd expect on CIFAR-10/100, and the authors don't convincingly explain why. Are these reproduced from scratch? What augmentation pipeline was used for the baselines? This matters enormously for a paper whose central claim is about augmentation quality. I found myself going back to the experimental details section multiple times and still not getting a clear answer.

The iterative training procedure described in Section 3.3 is also underspecified. How sensitive is this to the initialization? What happens if the semantic encoder is poorly initialized early on — does the diffusion model just learn to generate garbage? There's no ablation on training stability, which I think is a pretty serious omission for a method that depends on this bootstrapping dynamic.

I'm also not entirely convinced the generated augmentations are actually preserving semantic content in the way the authors claim. Figure 4 shows some qualitative examples, and honestly some of those look like they've drifted meaningfully from the original. The paper would benefit enormously from a more rigorous analysis here — maybe nearest neighbor retrieval in the learned embedding space, or a user study, or at minimum some quantitative measure of semantic consistency.

The biological dataset results are interesting and I'll admit I found that section more compelling than the vision experiments. But the description of the biological data preprocessing is thin — I'm not sure I could reproduce those experiments from what's written.

One more thing: the name "DiffAug" is already used in a fairly well-known paper (Zhao et al., differentiable augmentation for GANs). The authors should be aware of this collision and address it, or rename their method.

The writing is generally clear but occasionally awkward, and there are a handful of grammatical errors throughout (e.g., "diffusion mode-based" in the abstract should presumably be "diffusion model-based"). Minor but worth cleaning up.

Overall, I think there's a legitimate idea here and the direction is worth pursuing. But in its current form the paper doesn't meet the bar for acceptance. The experimental validation needs to be substantially strengthened, and the method description needs more rigor. I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit.

This paper tackles the problem of constructing coresets for robust clustering (k-median/k-means with outliers) and claims an ε-coreset of size O(m + poly(k/ε)), which is a genuine improvement over the prior state of the art. I've spent a fair amount of time with this paper and I think the core contribution is solid, though I have some concerns worth raising.

The main technical result (Theorem 1.1 or thereabouts) is the headline contribution, and the authors are right that the separation between m and the poly(k, 1/ε) factors is meaningful — especially in regimes where m is large. The lower bound argument showing near-optimality in m is a nice touch and I appreciated that they actually bothered to prove it. In my experience, lower bounds for coreset sizes are often skipped in these papers.

The adaptation of the Braverman et al. FOCS'22 framework to the outlier setting is technically interesting. The core challenge — that the set of "participating" points changes with the center set C, making the cost function non-oblivious — is real and nontrivial. I think the authors handle it reasonably well, though I found some of the arguments in Section 4 a bit dense and had to re-read them a couple of times. Page 12 specifically, the case analysis around the outlier exclusion felt like it was glossing over some details that matter.

My bigger concern is with the experiments. The empirical evaluation feels somewhat rushed and the baselines are weak — uniform sampling and sensitivity sampling are fine, but where is the comparison to the Huang et al. FOCS'18 bi-criteria coreset in practice? I know it has worse theoretical guarantees but practitioners might still use it. Also the datasets chosen (Table 2, if I'm reading correctly) are fairly small and low-dimensional, which somewhat limits the story about "large number of outliers." I'd want to see this on something messier.

I'm also not entirely sure about the near-linear construction time claim — is this truly near-linear in n, or does it hide dependence on d in ways that become painful in high dimensions? The paper is a bit coy about this.

Minor gripes: the notation is occasionally inconsistent between sections (the use of Δ shifts meaning slightly around page 9), and the related work discussion undersells some relevant work on robust statistics and coreset constructions from the ML community outside the TCS bubble.

Overall though, I think this is a good paper. The problem is important, the result is clearly an advance, and the lower bound gives it some weight. I'd lean toward acceptance. The experiments could be stronger but they don't undermine the theoretical contribution. I'd ask the authors to clarify the high-dimensional runtime behavior and beef up the experimental section in a revision.

This paper tackles a genuinely important problem — temporal consistency in video diffusion — and the core idea of integral noise is creative and technically interesting. I've seen a lot of papers in this space try to address flickering through attention manipulation or latent blending, so the noise-level approach feels refreshingly principled. That said, I have significant concerns about how the work is presented and validated.

The ∫-noise formulation (Section 3) is the clear highlight. Reinterpreting pixel values as integrals over a continuous noise field is elegant, and the connection to area sampling in rendering feels well-motivated. Equation 4 in particular is clean and I appreciate that the authors actually derive the covariance structure rather than just asserting it. I had to read this section twice to fully follow the derivation, but I think it's correct. My concern is that the practical advantage over simply using correlated noise via optical flow warping (which people have been doing since at least Texler et al. and the work on layered neural atlases) isn't made crystal clear until quite late in the paper.

The transport method described in Section 4 is where I start losing confidence. The handling of occlusion boundaries feels underspecified. What actually happens at disocclusion regions? The paper mentions "noise inpainting" almost in passing around line 340 (if I'm reading the right section) but this seems like it could be a major failure mode and deserves more careful treatment. Figure 5 shows some nice results but the examples are somewhat cherry-picked looking — I'd want to see failure cases.

The experiments, honestly, are the weakest part. Table 1 shows improvements in CLIP consistency and warp error, fine, but the margins are sometimes quite small and I'm not convinced the baselines are implemented fairly. Were the optical flow-based noise warping baselines (e.g., the approach from Gen-1 or similar) tuned properly? The paper doesn't say. Also, the video restoration experiments feel a bit disconnected from the surrogate rendering ones — I'm not sure the paper has a coherent story about what its primary application is.

Visually the results look good to me — the supplement videos are convincing, especially the surrogate rendering examples which I found genuinely impressive. But I think the paper oversells the theoretical generality a bit. The method still depends on accurate optical flow, and in practice that's a hard dependency that limits applicability to scenes where flow estimation works well.

Minor things: the title is trying too hard to be cute (I know this is a style choice but "How I Warped Your Noise" made me cringe a little). The related work misses some relevant concurrent work on noise initialization in video diffusion.

Overall: interesting contribution, technically solid core, but the evaluation needs strengthening and the paper needs to be clearer about its actual limitations. Borderline accept for me right now, leaning toward weak accept if authors address the occlusion handling and baseline fairness questions in the rebuttal.

This paper tackles the bank loan problem (BLP), which is a genuinely important and underexplored area in the online learning literature. The core idea — using adversarial domain adaptation to debias the training set, then combining this with PLOT for optimism — is reasonable and the empirical results look strong. That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The motivation is clear enough and I appreciate that the authors situate their work relative to PLOT and related optimism-based approaches. However, after reading the technical sections twice, I found the presentation of AdOpt somewhat muddled. The explanation around equations 3-5 in particular felt rushed — the connection between the adversarial domain adaptation objective and the specific distributional shift being targeted (accepted vs. all seen data) could be made much more precise. I'm not entirely sure the authors have justified *why* this particular choice of source/target domains is the right one theoretically, versus, say, other ways one might define the bias correction problem. A brief theoretical analysis — even just a bound on how much bias is reduced under idealized conditions — would substantially strengthen the paper.

The empirical results in Table 2 are impressive and I don't doubt the numbers. But I think the experimental setup deserves more scrutiny. The benchmark datasets used — are these standard BLP benchmarks from prior work, or did the authors construct them? I found this confusing. If the authors constructed the evaluation environments themselves, that's a significant methodological concern given that AdOpt was presumably developed with some knowledge of these settings. More detail on the train/test protocol and how hyperparameters were selected is needed.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the ablation study. The comparison between AdOpt with and without the PLOT component is useful, but I'd really want to see results for adversarial debiasing alone, without any optimism mechanism, to understand the independent contribution of the domain adaptation piece. Right now it's hard to disentangle what's doing the work.

One thing that bothered me — the paper claims in the abstract that AdOpt "significantly exceeds state-of-the-art performance" but the framing of statistical significance is weak. Are those differences actually significant? In my experience with these kinds of online learning benchmarks, variance across runs can be substantial.

The related work section is decent but misses some relevant literature on partial label learning and covariate shift correction that seems directly applicable here.

Overall: I think there's a solid paper here, maybe. The idea is good and the empirical gains are real. But the theoretical grounding is thin and the experimental methodology needs tightening. I'd lean toward weak reject currently, with the expectation that a revised version addressing the theoretical justification and experimental clarity could be publishable.

This paper proposes PRO-SCALE, a method for reducing computational cost in the Mask2Former transformer encoder by progressively reducing token length across encoder layers. The core observation — that ~50% of compute in Mask2Former sits in the encoder due to full-length multi-scale token representations — is valid and worth addressing. I've seen similar observations made in other contexts (e.g., token pruning in ViTs, hierarchical attention mechanisms), so the motivation isn't entirely novel, but the specific application to universal segmentation encoders is reasonable.

My main concern is the technical depth. The method essentially drops tokens progressively across layers, which is... fine, but I found the justification for *why* this works surprisingly thin. There's some hand-waving about later encoder layers requiring less spatial detail, but after reading section 3 twice I still wasn't convinced there was a principled argument here rather than an empirical observation that it doesn't hurt much. What's the theoretical grounding? How are tokens selected for removal — the paper mentions spatial downsampling but the exact mechanism described around equation 2 felt underspecified to me.

The 52% GFLOPs reduction with "no drop in performance" claim on COCO is the headline result, and Table 2 does support this, but I'm skeptical of how this generalizes. The authors test on panoptic, instance, and semantic segmentation which is good. However, the backbone choices feel limited — mostly ResNet variants. How does this behave with stronger backbones like Swin-L or recent ViT-based encoders? This matters a lot for the "universal" claim in the title.

I also think the comparison to related work is incomplete. Methods like Eff-DETR, Lite-DETR, and various token merging approaches (ToMe comes to mind) are doing very similar things. The related work section acknowledges some of these but the experimental comparison is weak — Table 3 has only a handful of baselines and I'm not sure the selected comparisons are the strongest available ones. This is where the paper needs the most work.

The writing is generally clear, though Figure 3 is a bit cluttered and took me a while to parse. The ablation in Table 4 is helpful and actually one of the stronger parts of the paper — showing that the progressive nature of the scaling matters versus uniform reduction is a nice result.

Honestly, my overall take: this is a solid engineering contribution with a clean implementation and reasonable results, but it reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference submission. The novelty is incremental, the theoretical motivation is thin, and the experimental scope needs broadening. I'm not opposed to acceptance if the authors can significantly strengthen the comparison to related token reduction methods and provide more analysis on *why* early layers benefit from full resolution while later layers don't. That story needs to be told more convincingly.

Borderline reject leaning toward weak reject in current form.

This paper tackles an interesting and somewhat niche problem — understanding how positional information gets encoded in CNNs through padding, and proposing better metrics to measure this so-called PPP (Position-information Pattern from Padding). I've spent a fair amount of time with this work and have mixed feelings.

Let me start with what I think works. The core observation that existing metrics (particularly those based on PosENet and F-Conv) give unreliable or misleading results is a legitimate concern, and the authors do a reasonable job motivating why new metrics are needed. The visualization approach is intriguing, and Figure 3 in particular helped me understand what they're actually measuring. The finding that PPP is primarily a learning artifact rather than a direct consequence of padding scheme choice is genuinely interesting if true — it would have real implications for how people think about designing architectures.

That said, I have significant concerns. The paper is, frankly, quite hard to follow in places. The formal definition of PPP (around Section 3) is introduced with mathematical notation that I found confusing on first and second read. I'm not sure if this is a clarity issue or a substance issue — possibly both. The connection between the formal definition and the actual metrics proposed isn't as clean as it should be.

The experiments feel somewhat thin. Table 2 shows comparisons against baseline metrics but the experimental setup isn't described with enough detail to fully evaluate the reliability claims being made. What datasets? What architectures exactly? I kept flipping back looking for these details. Also, the claim that PPP is "primarily a learning artifact" is a strong claim that I don't think is sufficiently backed by the evidence presented. A few ablations don't really clinch this argument.

I'm also not entirely convinced the problem framing is sharp enough. The authors say existing metrics "frequently lead to erroneous results" but I wanted more concrete, quantitative examples of where this goes wrong in practice and why it matters for downstream tasks. The abstract promises to address negative effects on model performance, but the paper kind of drifts away from this and focuses more on measurement per se.

The related work section is okay but misses some recent work on translation equivariance and positional encoding in vision transformers that seems relevant — the problem of unwanted or uncontrolled position information is certainly not unique to padding in CNNs anymore.

Minor thing: the title is a bit overwrought. "Through the Mist of Image Features" reads like creative writing, not a paper title.

Overall, I think there's a real paper buried here but in its current form it's not ready. The core ideas need sharpening, the experiments need strengthening, and the writing needs significant revision. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a strong revision.

This paper tackles inverse rendering for glossy objects, which is indeed a challenging and important problem. The core idea — integrating volume rendering with physically-based rendering through what they call PBVR to reduce material-lighting ambiguity — is reasonable and I can see the motivation. That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the experimental validation.

Let me start with what I think is the central issue: the "unified representation" described in Section 3 feels underspecified. After reading this twice, I'm still not entirely clear on how the mutual constraints are actually enforced during optimization. The authors describe PBVR loosely but the connection between the volume rendering branch and the PBR branch — particularly how gradients flow and how conflicts are resolved — is never made rigorous. Equation 4 and 5 look plausible on the surface but I'd want to see ablations specifically isolating this coupling mechanism, which the paper doesn't really provide in a satisfying way.

The experimental section is where things get frustrating. Table 1 comparisons against NeRFactor, Ref-NeRF, and InvRender are fine, but notably absent is any comparison with more recent work like GaussianShader or recent SDF-based methods (e.g., NMF, EG3D-based approaches). The field has moved fast and benchmarking against methods from 2021-2022 feels cherry-picked. I'm not saying the results are fabricated, but the comparisons need to be more competitive. Also, the DiLiGenT-MV results in Table 2 show improvements that are... modest? The PSNR gains are sometimes within what I'd consider noise given the variance in these benchmarks.

Figures 4 and 5 look visually compelling for the glossy objects, I'll grant that. The qualitative results on the car and the helmet are genuinely nice. But qualitative results for inverse rendering are notoriously easy to cherry-pick, and I'd want to see more failure cases. Where does this break down?

The writing has some rough patches — the abstract contains a grammatical error ("decompose the an image") which suggests this wasn't carefully proofread, and there are several awkward constructions throughout Section 4. Minor thing but it affects my confidence in the overall polish.

My broader concern is novelty. Combining volume rendering with PBR is not new — VolSDF, NeuS, and PhySG-adjacent work have explored this territory. The authors need to much more clearly articulate what is specifically novel here beyond the combination. In my experience, "unified framework" papers often end up being engineering contributions rather than conceptual advances, which is fine, but then the bar for experimental validation becomes higher.

I lean toward rejection in the current form. The idea has merit and I'd encourage the authors to revise with stronger baselines, cleaner theoretical exposition of the constraint mechanism, and more honest discussion of limitations.

This paper tackles an important and timely problem — how to dynamically adjust data mixture ratios during pretraining without the overhead of proxy models or expensive ablation runs. The core idea of using per-domain scaling laws to estimate "learning potential" and adjust sampling weights online is genuinely interesting, and I think the framing around scaling laws as a diagnostic tool (not just a predictive one) is somewhat novel.

That said, I have significant concerns about the empirical evaluation. The experiments feel thin for a paper making claims about "scalability across computation scales." Table 2 shows comparisons at fairly small scales, and I'm not convinced the gains would hold at, say, 10B+ parameter models. The authors gesture at scalability in the abstract but the actual evidence is limited. I've seen similar claims in data mixing papers that didn't pan out at larger scales — this is a real pattern in the literature.

The per-domain scaling law fitting procedure (Section 3.2, around equations 4-6) also deserves more scrutiny. Fitting Chinchilla-style power laws online with limited data points per domain seems like it could be quite noisy, especially early in training. How sensitive is ADO to the quality of these fits? I didn't see a proper ablation on this. What happens when a domain has very little data and the fit is poor? The authors should address this — it feels like a potentially serious failure mode that's kind of glossed over.

I found Figure 3 genuinely informative, but the visualization of domain weight trajectories in Figure 4 raises questions. Some domains seem to oscillate quite a bit. Is this instability or is it "working as intended"? The paper doesn't really explain this and I found it confusing on first and second reading.

The related work section handles DoReMi and other dynamic mixing baselines reasonably well, but I think the comparison to Skill-It and data curriculum methods is a bit superficial. There's also recent work on data mixing (some from late 2023/early 2024) that I'm not sure is fully accounted for.

One more thing — the claim that ADO "does not require external knowledge" is slightly misleading. You still need to know your domain taxonomy. That's not nothing.

Minor issues: the notation in Section 3 gets a bit overloaded around equation 7 and I had to re-read it several times. Also "concurrent with model training" in the abstract is a selling point but I'd like to see a proper wall-clock time comparison, not just FLOPs estimates.

Overall: this is a solid idea with a clean motivation, but the evaluation needs to be stronger and several methodological questions need clearer answers before I'd feel confident recommending acceptance. I'm leaning weak reject currently, but I could be convinced otherwise with better empirical support and ablations addressing the scaling law fitting robustness question.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — joint audio-visual generation from a static face image conditioned on text prompts — which is genuinely underexplored compared to either talking head generation or TTS in isolation. The motivation is reasonable and I can see potential value here.

That said, I have significant concerns about this submission that I don't think are minor.

The writing is, frankly, quite poor throughout. The abstract alone contains grammatical issues ("speaking and taking faces" — I assume this means "talking faces"?) and the description of the architecture is nearly incomprehensible. I read the methodology section twice and I'm still not entirely clear on what "multi-entangled latent space" actually means technically. This phrase is used repeatedly as if it's self-explanatory, but equation 3 and the surrounding text don't adequately formalize what entanglement means in this context or why it's preferable to simpler cross-attention mechanisms. In my experience, when a paper coins a term and then leans on it heavily without rigorous definition, it's usually a red flag.

The experimental section is where my concerns become most serious. The baselines chosen are weak and in some cases quite dated. There's no comparison against recent strong baselines like DiffTalk or SadTalker for the video component, and the audio quality metrics feel cherry-picked. Table 2 shows improvements on MOS scores but the confidence intervals are suspiciously tight — was statistical significance actually tested? I couldn't find this discussed anywhere.

I'm also troubled by the dataset contribution. The paper claims a new dataset but provides essentially no detail about collection methodology, consent procedures, demographic diversity, or potential biases. For a dataset involving face and voice data this is not a minor omission, it's a serious ethical gap that should disqualify the contribution in its current form.

The figures (particularly Figure 3 and 4) are low resolution and hard to interpret. The qualitative comparisons look somewhat favorable but I'd want to see a proper user study with more participants — 15 evaluators is really not sufficient for claims about perceptual quality.

I might be wrong about this, but the connection to the text prompt conditioning feels underdeveloped. Most of the ablations don't isolate the contribution of the text conditioning specifically, which is supposedly the core novelty here.

The GitHub link in the abstract leads to what appears to be an empty or incomplete repository, which undermines the reproducibility claims.

I want to be fair: the core idea has merit and some of the qualitative results look promising. But in its current state this paper needs substantial revision — clearer technical exposition, stronger baselines, a proper ethical statement about the dataset, and more rigorous experiments. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with major revisions.

This paper proposes BT-Cell, a recursive neural network approach that uses beam search over easy-first parsing to induce tree structures automatically. The core idea is reasonably clear and the empirical results on ListOps are impressive — near-perfect performance is a genuine achievement that warrants attention.

That said, I have some significant concerns I want to work through.

The framing around "simple yet overlooked backpropagation-friendly framework" bothered me throughout. After reading the related work section twice, I'm still not fully convinced this is as overlooked as the authors claim. The connection to prior work on differentiable parsing (Maillard et al., various CYK-based approaches) felt somewhat glossed over. I think the authors need to be more precise about exactly what the novelty is relative to, say, the ordered neurons work or the work on straight-through estimators for discrete structure induction.

On the technical side — equation 3 and the surrounding description of the beam search procedure is where I got confused. The notation gets dense and I found myself wishing for a cleaner algorithmic presentation, maybe a pseudocode block. Figure 2 helps but doesn't fully compensate. I'm not entirely sure I understand how gradients flow through the hard top-k step in the basic version — this feels underexplained for something that is literally the central technical contribution.

The ListOps results in Table 2 are genuinely strong. The out-of-distribution generalization experiments are a nice addition and I appreciate that the authors identified the failure case with unseen argument counts — that's actually a useful empirical finding that I think deserves more discussion than it gets (it's kind of buried after page 7). In my experience, these kinds of failure mode analyses are often more valuable than the main benchmark numbers.

The realistic data results are... fine? "Comparable to other RvNN-based models" is doing a lot of work in the abstract. On SST and some of the other tasks the improvements over baselines are modest and I wonder whether the complexity of the approach is justified for those settings.

I also think the ablations in Section 5 are incomplete. The comparison between the hard and soft relaxation variants is interesting but the variance across seeds is large enough in some conditions that I'm not confident in the conclusions. More runs or clearer confidence intervals would help.

One more thing: the paper is a bit long and could be tightened. Section 3.2 in particular feels like it could be condensed.

Overall I'm on the fence. The ListOps result is real and the failure case discovery is genuinely interesting. But the paper would benefit from clearer exposition of the core technical mechanism and a more honest accounting of what's novel versus prior art. Leaning weak accept but would need revisions.

This paper proposes NANSY++, a unified framework for voice synthesis built on top of what appears to be a prior NANSY system, extended here to handle voice conversion, TTS, singing voice synthesis, and voice designing under a single self-supervised backbone. The core idea — learn disentangled analysis features without annotations, then train lightweight task-specific modules on top — is appealing and I think the motivation is genuine. That said, I have some significant concerns about the execution and the claims being made.

First, the "unified" framing feels somewhat oversold. After reading this twice, what I see is really a shared feature extractor with four separate downstream modules trained somewhat independently. That's useful engineering, but calling it a "unified framework" implies tighter integration than what's actually described. The authors should be more precise about what is and isn't shared at training time.

On the experimental side, Table 2 and Table 3 present MOS scores for TTS and VC respectively, but the baseline comparisons are a bit cherry-picked in my opinion. Several strong recent baselines (YourTTS, NaturalSpeech) are absent without explanation. I'm not entirely sure if this is because of timing issues with concurrent work, but it weakens the comparative evaluation considerably. The singing voice synthesis evaluation (Section 4.3) is especially thin — basically just a small listening test with limited conditions. In my experience, SVS is tricky to evaluate and this section needs substantially more rigor.

The self-supervised training approach is interesting and I appreciate that they don't require paired text-audio data. The feature decomposition into linguistic content, speaker, and periodicity features (around page 4-5) is the technical heart of the paper, but I found the description of how these are kept disentangled somewhat hand-wavy. Equation 3 and the surrounding text describe the information bottleneck approach but I'm not fully convinced the ablation in Table 5 really isolates what's doing the heavy lifting here.

Figure 4 is actually quite informative and I wish more analysis like this appeared in the main paper rather than just the appendix.

The data efficiency claims (Section 4.4) are one of the stronger parts of the paper — showing competitive performance with limited labeled data is practically valuable and reasonably well-supported.

Writing quality is decent but there are places where the English is awkward and could use another pass. "Partially modeling the analysis features" in the abstract is unclear on first read.

Overall: I think this is solid work with a good core idea and some genuinely useful properties, but the paper oversells the unification aspect and the evaluations have notable gaps. I'd lean toward weak accept if the authors can address the baseline comparisons and sharpen the SVS evaluation, but right now it sits at the borderline for me.

This paper proposes combining POET with unsupervised RL (specifically mutual information-based skill discovery) to learn diverse skills without hand-crafted reward functions. The core idea is reasonably intuitive — if you're already generating a curriculum of environments via POET, why not also learn reward-free skills in those environments? I can see the appeal.

That said, I have significant concerns about the execution and presentation.

The writing quality is honestly quite rough in places. The abstract alone has grammatical issues ("an agents pre-defined reward function", "an agents ability") and the framing is imprecise. After reading the paper twice I'm still not entirely clear on the exact algorithmic details of how skill discovery is integrated into the POET loop.
