The multi-future-frame prediction objective is presented as a novel contribution (Section 3.2 if I recall correctly), but honestly this feels like a fairly natural extension of existing predictive coding ideas. The ablation in Table 3 does show it helps, which is good, but I'd push back on how strongly this is framed as a core contribution.

The baselines comparison is reasonable but I notice the absence of some recent relevant work — OCVT and SlotFormer come to mind, though I might be wrong about whether their publication timing overlaps. The authors should double-check this.

One thing I genuinely liked: the compositional imagination experiments where attributes from different objects are recombined are visually compelling and feel like a real qualitative advance over prior work. That's the exciting part of this paper. I wish it were the focus rather than the DCI scores.

Smaller issues: the abstract oversells the cognitive science angle a bit much, the related work section is dense but not always illuminating, and some figures (particularly Figure 5) are too small to read comfortably.

Overall, I think there's a real contribution buried here but the paper needs clearer exposition of the architecture, more convincing evaluation beyond DCI metrics, and a tighter story about what's truly novel. The current version reads like the authors are still figuring out which contribution to lead with. Borderline for me — leaning weak accept if the authors can address the evaluation concerns, but I could be convinced either way.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — understanding how aggressive sparsification and quantization affect spectral clustering performance. The theoretical framework using random matrix theory is the clear selling point here, and I think the authors have done reasonable work connecting the abstract machinery to concrete downstream implications.

That said, I have several concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

The main theoretical results (particularly around Theorem 3.2 and the phase transition characterization) are technically sound as far as I can tell, but the presentation is quite dense. After reading section 3 twice, I'm still not entirely sure I understand the precise conditions under which the "informative eigenstructure" is preserved. The authors gesture at this informally in the abstract but the formal statement has enough conditions that a reader needs to work hard to understand what regime they're actually operating in. More intuition here would help enormously.

The experiments are underwhelming. Figure 3 shows clustering accuracy as a function of sparsification rate, which is fine, but I found it hard to connect back to the theory. Specifically — the phase transition predicted by the theory, is it actually visible in Figure 3? It looks vague to me. I'd want to see a cleaner experiment that directly validates the phase transition result, not just "accuracy stays high until it doesn't." Also, the datasets used are fairly small and synthetic. In my experience, real-world graph data often has structural properties (power-law degree distributions, etc.) that violate the assumptions underlying these RMT results pretty badly, and the paper doesn't really grapple with this.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the novelty claim relative to existing work. The connection between nonlinear transformations of random matrices and spectral preservation has been studied — Chung et al. and some of the work coming out of the RMT+ML community (I'm thinking of papers around 2019-2021 on implicit regularization) overlap with the core technical ideas here. The authors cite some of this but I think the discussion of what's actually new versus what's a straightforward application of existing machinery could be significantly sharper. To be fair, I might be wrong about the extent of the overlap — but the burden is on the authors to clarify this.

One more thing: the "spurious eigenvectors" result mentioned in the abstract is actually the most interesting part to me, and it feels buried. Section 4.3 or wherever this appears deserves more prominence. This has real practical implications for people using quantized matrices in spectral methods and I don't think the authors fully appreciate how interesting this finding is.

Overall: interesting topic, solid technical foundation, but the paper needs clearer presentation, stronger experiments, and a more careful positioning relative to prior work. I'm leaning weak reject but would genuinely reconsider with a strong rebuttal addressing the experimental concerns.

This paper proposes combining a VAE framework with NMF constraints to get probabilistic non-negative matrix factorization. The core idea — constraining decoder weights to be non-negative and using a Weibull distribution for the latent space — is reasonable enough, and I can see why someone would want this kind of model. NMF has obvious interpretability advantages and adding a generative component is a natural extension. So I'm not dismissing the paper out of hand.

That said, I have pretty serious concerns about the execution and the claims being made.

The experimental evaluation is weak. Three datasets is fine in principle, but the comparisons are shallow. On the image experiments, I kept asking myself: compared to what? The baselines are not rigorously established, and in my experience with generative models on image data, you really need to be systematic about what you're comparing against. The financial time series application feels particularly underdeveloped — it's almost an afterthought. I'd have appreciated much more careful analysis there, given that non-negativity constraints on financial data have some non-obvious interpretations that the authors never really grapple with.

The choice of Weibull distribution for the latent space is, I think, the most technically interesting part of the paper, but it's also the least justified. Why Weibull specifically? The authors gesture at non-negativity as the motivation but there are other non-negative distributions (log-normal, gamma) that would be equally natural. This needed a proper ablation or at least a theoretical argument. As presented it reads like an arbitrary choice.

I found the writing in Section 3 genuinely confusing — after reading it twice I was still unclear on exactly how the reparameterization trick is being applied for the Weibull case. The equations are there but the exposition around them is poor. This is fixable but it's a real problem for reproducibility.

The related work section is thin. There's a body of work on probabilistic NMF (Cemgil 2009, and the broader Bayesian NMF literature) that deserves more engagement. The paper positions itself somewhat in a vacuum, which makes it hard to assess the actual contribution delta.

I want to be fair: the combination of these specific pieces (VAE + NMF weight constraints + Weibull latent) is novel as a package, and the genomics application is genuinely interesting — NMF is already widely used in mutational signature analysis so a probabilistic extension has real potential value there. But the paper doesn't develop this enough to make a convincing case.

My overall feeling is that this is a workshop paper that got submitted to a main track. The idea has merit but the experiments, justification, and writing all need substantial work. I'd encourage a major revision with stronger baselines, ablations on the distributional choice, and a clearer story about what you gain over existing probabilistic NMF approaches.

This paper proposes MPPN, a multi-resolution periodic pattern network for long-term time series forecasting. The core ideas involve constructing multi-resolution semantic units, mining periodic patterns, and a channel adaptive module for handling multivariate series. There's also an entropy-based predictability bound thrown in. I've read this twice now and have fairly mixed feelings.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The entropy-based upper bound idea (Section 4.3 I believe) is actually the most novel contribution here, and frankly it feels underexplored and almost tacked on. In my experience, providing theoretical grounding for forecast difficulty is rare and valuable — but the connection between this bound and the actual architecture design feels loose. Why does knowing the entropy bound influence how you build the network? The paper never really makes this connection tight.

The multi-resolution periodic pattern mining is reasonable but not particularly surprising given the existing literature. TimesNet, FEDformer, and related work have all played in this space. I'm not entirely sure the authors have sufficiently differentiated their contribution from TimesNet in particular — the decomposition into period-based 2D representations feels similar in spirit. Table 2 shows improvements but some of these margins are thin, and I'd want to see statistical significance testing. The claim of "significantly outperforms" in the abstract is doing a lot of work for what are sometimes sub-1% improvements.

The channel adaptive module is described in Section 3.2 but I found the explanation genuinely confusing on first read. The motivation is clear — individual variates have different characteristics — but the actual mechanism isn't explained cleanly enough. Figure 3 helps somewhat but the caption is inadequate.

Nine benchmarks is solid coverage. But I notice the comparison set doesn't include some more recent strong baselines from 2023-2024. Given how fast this field moves, that's a real concern. PatchTST and iTransformer are there, good, but where is TimeMixer or some of the more recent MLP-based methods that have been quite competitive?

The writing needs work. There are several places where the English is awkward and the logic jumps without adequate transition. The related work section feels perfunctory — it lists methods without really engaging with how MPPN relates to them conceptually.

Ablation study in Table 4 is appreciated but I'd like to see more granular ablations. Removing the whole channel adaptive module as one piece tells me less than I'd like about which design choices actually matter.

Overall: the paper has interesting ideas but the execution is uneven. The contribution feels incremental over existing periodic/multi-scale decomposition methods, and the theoretical contribution (entropy bound) isn't integrated tightly enough with the rest of the work. I lean toward rejection in the current form, but with genuine revisions — particularly around the entropy bound connection and stronger baseline comparisons — this could be a reasonable paper.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question about shortcut learning — specifically, *which* shortcuts DNNs gravitate toward when multiple equally-valid shortcuts are available. I appreciate the framing through a multi-cue experimental setup (WCST-ML), and the Kolmogorov complexity argument is intellectually appealing. That said, I have significant concerns about the rigor and execution.

Let me start with what I think works. The core experimental setup is clever. Giving models genuinely equivalent discriminative power across multiple cues and then observing preference is a cleaner way to study shortcut bias than most prior work I've seen, which typically compares shortcuts of unequal predictive value. The connection to flat minima (Figures 3-4) is interesting, though honestly not surprising given prior work on loss landscape geometry (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, Keskar et al., etc.). I'm not sure this adds much beyond confirming existing intuitions.

My main complaint is with the Kolmogorov complexity argument. I found this section (around Section 4) frustratingly vague. Kolmogorov complexity is uncomputable — so how exactly are the authors operationalizing it? They seem to be gesturing at an intuitive notion of "simplicity" rather than making a formal argument. After reading this section twice, I still couldn't find a rigorous connection between the theoretical claim and the empirical measurements. The abundance argument in parameter space is suggestive, but the leap from "more solutions exist" to "Kolmogorov complexity explains this" feels hand-wavy. I might be wrong, but I think the paper would be stronger if it either committed fully to the theoretical framing or dropped it in favor of a cleaner empirical story.

The UTKFace results are concerning for a different reason. The authors observe that "ethnicity" is a preferred shortcut cue. I think the societal implications section is too brief given how loaded this finding is. This needs careful treatment — both methodologically (is ethnicity annotation reliable? are we confident this is truly "equal opportunity" with other cues?) and ethically.

Minor issues: Table 2 is hard to parse. The caption doesn't give enough context. Also, I found the DSprites experiments more convincing than the face experiments, possibly because the synthetic setting gives cleaner control, and I'd have liked to see more ablations there.

In my experience reviewing shortcut/spurious correlation papers, the best ones either give us new theory OR new empirical insights — this paper attempts both and partially succeeds at neither. The ideas are genuinely interesting and I think there's a publishable paper here, but the Kolmogorov framing needs either formalization or significant toning down. As it stands the gap between the theoretical claims and the actual evidence is too wide.

Borderline reject, leaning toward major revision.

This paper proposes a fairly straightforward generalization of transformer attention to handle edge features in graphs — essentially updating both node and edge representations in each layer rather than just node representations. The core idea (equations 3-5 if I'm counting right) is clean enough, though I'd argue it's not quite as novel as the authors seem to think. Related work like Dwivedi & Bresson (2021) on graph transformers and even some earlier work on relation networks already operates in this general vicinity. The authors do cite some of this but I think they undersell how incremental the architectural contribution actually is.

That said — and this is important — the empirical results on CLRS are genuinely impressive. The gains over baseline GNNs are large enough that I don't think they're noise, and I appreciated that the authors ran enough ablations to make a credible case that edge updating specifically is driving the improvements rather than just having more parameters. Figure 3 in particular is useful here. I found myself more convinced by the CLRS results than I expected to be going in.

My main concern is the framing. The paper claims to "generalize transformers for graph-structured tasks" but the actual scope of experiments is somewhat narrow — CLRS plus a few smaller benchmarks. I'd want to see this on, say, molecular property prediction or something from OGB to really believe the generality claim. CLRS is a specific benchmark with specific characteristics (it rewards certain kinds of structured reasoning) and I'm not sure results there transfer cleanly to other graph domains. The authors gesture at this briefly but don't really grapple with it.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the analysis section. The argument that gains come from "greater expressivity of graphs over sets" is intuitive but the empirical support for this specific claim feels thin to me. After reading this section twice I'm still not totally sure what they're measuring and whether it establishes what they think it establishes. This needs tightening considerably.

Minor things: the notation in Section 3 gets cluttered fast, especially around equation 6. I had to read it several times. Also I think there's a subtle issue with how they handle missing edges versus zero-weighted edges that deserves more explicit treatment — I might be wrong about this being a problem in practice but it felt glossed over.

Overall: the architecture is simple (not necessarily a bad thing), the CLRS results are strong, but the novelty claim is overstated and the generalization story needs more empirical support. I lean toward weak accept — the community should probably see this work, but I'd push the authors hard to revise the framing and add at least one more domain of experiments. The paper is trying to do too much with too narrow a base of evidence.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — extending 3D-aware GANs to the video domain for portrait synthesis. I'll admit I was initially excited reading the abstract, and after going through the full paper twice, my enthusiasm is... mixed.

The core idea is reasonable: take something like EG3D and push it into the spatio-temporal domain. The motion generator using modulated convolutions (Section 3.2) is sensible, and the camera conditioning strategy to disambiguate camera vs. head motion is probably the most practically important contribution here. Without it, I imagine training would be a complete mess. That said, I'm not entirely sure the camera conditioning is as novel as the authors seem to believe — there's related work in video generation and talking head synthesis that handles similar issues, and I felt the related work section undersells these connections.

My bigger concern is the evaluation. Table 2 shows FID, FVD, and identity consistency scores, but the baselines feel cherry-picked or unfairly compared. Some of the competing methods aren't really designed for this exact task, so beating them isn't a strong signal. I also found the geometry evaluation (Figure 5) qualitatively convincing but I'd want to see quantitative 3D consistency metrics — the paper is claiming multi-view consistency as a core contribution but the quantitative evidence for this specifically is thin. The depth maps look reasonable but "looks reasonable" isn't what we need here.

The motion editing application in Section 5.2 is cool and I think it's genuinely the most compelling demonstration of why 3D-awareness matters for video generation. I wish the authors had spent more time here instead of the somewhat tedious ablation setup.

On the writing — it's generally clear but there are awkward phrasings throughout, particularly around equations 3-5 where the notation gets cluttered. I had to re-read the discriminator design section (3.4) several times and I'm still not 100% sure I understand how the temporal discriminator patches are sampled.

The video results on the project page are honestly pretty good. I'll give credit where it's due. But in my experience, papers that look great in cherry-picked video results often have training instabilities or mode collapse issues that don't surface in the write-up. The paper says nothing about training stability or failure modes, which feels like an omission.

I'm leaning toward acceptance but I think this needs revisions. Specifically: (1) stronger quantitative multi-view consistency evaluation, (2) clearer comparison to talking head / video generation literature on the camera conditioning idea, (3) clarification of the temporal discriminator sampling procedure. The contribution is real but the paper needs to make a stronger case for itself. Not a rejection, but not a slam dunk either.

This paper proposes a bias-variance decomposition for deep neural networks and uses it to derive a regularization term (GEM) that is added to the standard training objective. The empirical results look reasonable on the surface, but I have significant concerns about both the theoretical and experimental contributions.

On the theory side: the bias-variance decomposition presented is interesting, but I'm not convinced the "analytical proxy" for generalization error is actually tractable or well-motivated in the way the authors claim. Specifically, around equations 4-7, the derivation makes several assumptions that I think paper over some real difficulties. The connection between the derived bound and what's actually being minimized during training feels hand-wavy to me. I've read similar decomposition arguments in Geman et al. and more recent work like Yang & Yu (2020), and this paper doesn't clearly situate itself against those. After reading this section twice I still couldn't convince myself the proxy is tight enough to be meaningful. The authors should be more explicit about when this approximation breaks down.

The experimental results are where I have the most trouble. A 13.19% improvement on ImageNet under distribution shift is an extraordinary claim and the experimental setup in Section 5 does not justify it adequately. Which distribution shift benchmark exactly? ImageNet-C? ImageNet-R? The paper is frustratingly vague here. Table 3 shows some of these results but the baselines feel cherry-picked — where's the comparison against standard data augmentation approaches like AugMix or DeepAugment, which are specifically designed for distribution shift robustness? Including those seems absolutely necessary for the claims being made.

I also found Figure 2 confusing. The caption doesn't explain what the shaded regions represent and I genuinely couldn't tell if this was showing variance across seeds or something else entirely. Small thing but it matters for reproducibility.

The in-distribution results in Table 1 are actually less impressive than I'd expect given the motivation — improvements are modest and within what you might get from careful hyperparameter tuning. I'm not sure the method is doing what the theory says it's doing, or whether it's just acting as a generic regularizer. Ablations showing what happens when you remove specific components of the GEM term would help here. Right now I can't distinguish "this works because of the theory" from "this works because adding any reasonable penalty helps."

My overall read is that the paper has a potentially interesting idea buried inside it, but the current presentation oversells the theoretical contribution and undersells the need for rigorous experimental comparison. The writing is also somewhat dense and could be improved — Section 3 in particular reads like it was compressed significantly.

I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I'd encourage a revision that tightens the theory claims, adds proper baselines for the distribution shift experiments, and provides cleaner ablations. Not a bad paper, just not ready yet.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically motivated problem — reducing the computational burden of multiview systems by intelligently selecting which camera views to process. The core idea of using reinforcement learning to learn a sequential view selection policy (MVSelect) is reasonable, and I appreciate that the authors evaluate on both classification and detection tasks.

That said, I have fairly significant concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication at a top venue.

The comparison baselines feel weak to me. On page 6-7, the authors compare against random selection and a few handcrafted heuristics, but I would really want to see comparisons against learned attention-based methods or other policy gradient approaches for view/sensor selection. There's a decent body of work on active perception and next-best-view prediction that seems underrepresented in the related work. I found myself wondering whether a simpler learned scoring function (without the full RL apparatus) would achieve comparable results — this ablation seems missing or at least not clearly presented.

The RL training details also concern me. In my experience, RL-based selection modules can be notoriously unstable to train, and I didn't see sufficient discussion of training stability, sensitivity to reward shaping, or how many seeds were run. Table 2 shows results but variance/error bars are absent. This is a non-trivial omission given the stochastic nature of the approach.

I found the framing around "few glances" somewhat oversold. The method selects 2-3 views from N, but if N is small (like 4-6), the computational savings are modest and the problem is arguably easier. The paper would benefit from clearer experiments with larger N to demonstrate the approach scales meaningfully. Figure 3 is helpful for intuition but I'm not sure the visualization tells us as much as the authors claim — the "camera layout optimization" insight in the abstract feels like a stretch based on what's actually demonstrated.

One thing I genuinely liked: the joint training with the task network is a nice contribution and the ablation in Table 3 (if I'm reading it correctly) does suggest this matters. The integration seems cleaner than I expected.

The writing is generally clear though a bit dense in Section 3. After reading the methodology twice I still wasn't completely sure how the reward function handles partial observability across the sequential selection steps — equation 2 needs more explanation.

I'm not going to recommend rejection outright because the core problem is real and the RL-based joint training angle has some novelty. But I think the paper as submitted doesn't sufficiently justify the design choices over simpler alternatives, and the experimental validation has gaps. Minor revision at minimum, possibly major. I'd want the authors to seriously address the baseline comparisons and the training stability question.

This paper proposes "Mockingbird," a platform that wraps LLMs as runtime "mock functions" for general ML tasks. The core idea — defining functions purely by signature and docstring, then having an LLM execute them at inference time rather than generating code statically — is actually kind of interesting as a software abstraction. I'll give the authors that.

But I have serious concerns about this submission that I don't think are minor.

The empirical evaluation is weak, frankly. The authors test on "several Kaggle datasets" but I found the experimental section genuinely difficult to parse. Which datasets exactly? What are the baselines? Table 2 (if I'm reading it right) compares against what appears to be fairly naive baselines, not state-of-the-art methods for the respective tasks. Saying results are "highly competitive" without rigorous comparison to established ML pipelines on standardized benchmarks is not convincing. I've seen this kind of vague competitive claim before and it usually doesn't survive scrutiny.

The "reinforcement learning" framing in the abstract is also, I think, a stretch. What's described is essentially chain-of-thought prompting with feedback — calling this RL feels like marketing language rather than a technical claim. I'd push back hard on this characterization. Real RL involves policy gradients, reward signals, exploration-exploitation tradeoffs... none of that is actually present here. The authors should either properly formalize what they mean or drop the terminology entirely.

I'm also not sure the novelty claim holds up. After reading this twice, I think what's being described is fairly close to existing work on LLM agents, tool use, and structured prompting frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, etc. — even if those are more engineering-focused). The paper doesn't do a thorough job distinguishing Mockingbird from these. The related work section needs substantial expansion.

The writing quality is uneven. Some sections are clear, but there are grammatical issues throughout and the abstract contains redundancies ("defined solely by method signature" appears to be restated multiple times in slightly different forms). This is fixable but suggests the manuscript was rushed.

I might be wrong about this, but I also worry the "zero-shot" advantage claimed in contribution (a) is somewhat trivially true for any LLM-based system — this isn't specific to the mock function paradigm and shouldn't be listed as a distinctive advantage of this particular architecture.

There's a seed of a good idea here — the runtime mock function abstraction is genuinely a nice software engineering concept for integrating LLMs into pipelines. But as currently written, this reads more like a technical report or workshop paper than a full conference submission. The theoretical grounding is thin, evaluation is insufficient, and the novelty positioning relative to existing work is unclear. I can't recommend acceptance in its current form. Major revisions needed at minimum.

This paper tackles an interesting and somewhat underappreciated point in the ML community — that we often reach for heavy-duty deep learning solutions when simpler, more interpretable approaches would work just as well or nearly as well. I'm sympathetic to this message in principle. In my experience reviewing papers over the years, there's a real publication bias toward complex models, and papers like this serve a useful corrective function.

That said, I have serious concerns about the execution here.

The banana ripeness classification task is... fine as a demonstration, but it's almost *too* simple to be convincing. The whole argument of the paper is "stop overkilling simple tasks," but choosing such an obviously simple domain feels like cherry-picking. I'd be much more impressed if the authors demonstrated this on tasks where practitioners actually face the temptation to use black-box models — something with higher stakes or less obvious structure. The banana example kind of proves the point by being trivially easy, which paradoxically weakens the argument.

The user study (Section 4, if I'm reading the structure correctly) is where I found the most problems. After reading it twice, I'm still not clear on the methodology. How were participants recruited? What was the sample size? The evaluation of "perceived explainability" is notoriously tricky — there's a whole literature on this going back to Lipton's 2018 "Mythos of Model Interpretability" piece that I don't see adequately engaged with here. The user study feels like it was designed to confirm the authors' priors rather than rigorously test them.

The accuracy comparison between the transparent and opaque models also needs more careful treatment. "Minimal loss of accuracy" is doing a lot of work in the abstract, and the actual numbers in Table 2 (I think?) show a gap that some practitioners would not consider trivial. The authors should be more upfront about this tradeoff rather than waving it away.

I think the title is honestly a bit embarrassing for a venue like this. It reads like a blog post headline, not a scientific contribution. Strong opinions can be expressed professionally.

The related work section is thin. There's a rich body of work on inherently interpretable models — Rudin's group at Duke has produced very relevant work here, and I don't see it cited. The discussion of XAI methods also feels surface-level.

What I do appreciate: the framing around planning explainability *during* design rather than bolting it on afterward is genuinely valuable and underemphasized. That's a good contribution. The color histogram approach is sensible and the pipeline is clearly described.

My honest assessment is that this paper has a real idea buried in it, but needs significant restructuring, a stronger empirical evaluation with harder tasks, and a properly rigorous user study before it's ready for publication. I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit rather than reject outright, but this version isn't there yet.

This paper proposes ProFS, a tuning-free model editing approach for toxicity reduction, and attempts to draw connections between their method and DPO. I've read through this twice and have mixed feelings about it.

Let me start with what I think is genuinely interesting here: the theoretical framing connecting model editing via subspace projection to a single step of DPO is novel and I found myself engaged by Section 3. The factor analysis grounding gives the method a principled feel that a lot of ad-hoc editing papers lack. And the sample efficiency story is compelling — if you can get comparable toxicity reduction with far fewer preference pairs, that matters practically.

That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

First, the empirical evaluation feels thin. The toxicity benchmarks used (I'm looking at Table 2) are somewhat narrow, and I'm skeptical that performance on RealToxicityPrompts tells us much about whether the model remains useful for general tasks. What's the helpfulness degradation? There's a brief mention of perplexity but in my experience this is a weak proxy for downstream task quality. I'd want to see MMLU or at least some generation quality metrics before trusting the claim that ProFS preserves model utility.

Second — and this is my bigger concern — the claim that ProFS is "more robust to noisy data" than DPO is interesting but the noise model used in the experiments feels constructed to favor the proposed method. Injecting random label flips (if I'm reading Section 4.2 correctly) is a pretty artificial noise regime. Real preference data noise is much more systematic and correlated. I might be wrong about how representative these experiments are, but it reads to me like the evaluation is set up to showcase the method's strengths rather than probe its weaknesses.

The connection to DPO in Section 3.3 is the most interesting part of the paper but also the most hand-wavy. The "single DPO step" equivalence relies on approximations that aren't fully justified — equation 7 involves an assumption about the structure of the weight update that I'd like to see motivated more carefully. Is this tight? Under what conditions does it break down?

I also found the writing in the introduction somewhat overselling. Phrases like "inhibiting widespread use" applied to DPO seem odd given how widely DPO has actually been adopted in practice.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size, and there are a handful of grammatical issues throughout (e.g., around line 240).

Overall: interesting idea, theory section has promise, but the empirical story needs strengthening and some of the theoretical claims need more careful treatment. I'm leaning toward reject but would genuinely reconsider with stronger experiments and a more honest discussion of limitations.

This paper tackles imbalanced classification by applying Loss Conditional Training (LCT) — originally proposed for rate-distortion tradeoffs in compression — to the domain of class imbalance. The core idea is straightforward: instead of training with a single cost-sensitive loss hyperparameter, you train over a distribution of them, conditioning the model on the hyperparameter value at each step. I'll admit the idea is simple enough that I'm a little surprised it hasn't been more explicitly studied in this context, which is either a good sign or a red flag depending on how you look at it.

The experiments cover CIFAR imbalance benchmarks and two medical imaging tasks (melanoma and diabetic retinopathy), which I appreciate — medical imaging is exactly where this kind of flexibility matters in practice. The results in Table 2 and Table 3 are generally convincing that LCT matches or beats individual models trained at fixed hyperparameter settings. Fine. But here's my main issue: the paper never really explains *why* LCT improves over the single best fixed-hyperparameter model. The authors note this improvement somewhat triumphantly but offer almost no mechanistic insight. Is it a regularization effect? Something about the gradient diversity during training? I found this frustrating after reading the paper twice. The empirical gains are real but feel like a black box even to the authors.

I'm also not entirely satisfied with the baselines. The comparison is primarily against fixed-hyperparameter variants of the same loss functions, which is fair as far as it goes, but there's no comparison against ensemble methods or multi-objective approaches that also aim to give practitioners flexibility across the precision-recall tradeoff. In my experience, a simple ensemble of 3-4 models trained at different operating points is a natural baseline that's conspicuously absent here.

The efficiency argument (Section 4-ish, around page 6) is probably the strongest practical contribution, and I think it's undersold. The ability to do post-hoc operating point selection without retraining is genuinely useful in clinical deployment contexts. This should be front and center, not buried.

Figure 3 is a bit hard to read — the curves are close together and the color scheme doesn't help. Minor point, but presentation matters.

One thing that bugs me: the method essentially requires you to decide on a *distribution* over hyperparameters before training. How sensitive is performance to this choice? There's a brief ablation but I don't think it's thorough enough. Uniform vs. log-uniform over what range? This feels like it just pushes the hyperparameter problem one level up.

Overall, I think this is a reasonable paper with a practical contribution, but it reads more like an empirical report than a research paper with genuine insight. The "why does this work better" question needs at least an attempt at an answer. Borderline accept for me — I could be convinced either way with a more thorough revision.

This paper attempts to unify contrastive and predictive self-supervised learning methods under an information-theoretic framework using a multi-view perspective. The core idea — that SSL representations should capture task-relevant information while discarding task-irrelevant information — is sensible and the theoretical framing around mutual information is fairly clean. I've seen related ideas in Tian et al. and in the CMC line of work, so this isn't coming out of nowhere.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty and execution here.

The theoretical results, while presented formally, feel somewhat incremental. After reading this twice, I couldn't identify a theorem or lemma that genuinely surprised me or that I couldn't have derived myself from first principles given the setup. The bound in Section 3 (around Eq. 4-6 if I recall correctly) essentially says that a good representation should be sufficient for the task while being minimal — this is basically a restatement of the information bottleneck principle applied to SSL. The authors should be more upfront about this connection and clearly articulate what their work adds beyond Tishby et al.'s IB framework and its more recent applications to representation learning.

The composite objective proposed in Section 4 is where the paper gets more interesting to me, but I think the empirical evaluation is too weak to support the claims being made. The experiments seem to be conducted on relatively small-scale datasets and controlled settings. I appreciate the ablation study, but in 2021+ the community expects results on ImageNet-scale benchmarks with standard protocols (linear evaluation, few-shot transfer, etc.). Table 2 shows improvements but the baselines feel cherry-picked — why not compare against MoCo v2 or SimCLR with the same backbone?

I'm also confused by Section 5 where the authors try to extend beyond the multi-view perspective. This section feels underdeveloped — almost like an afterthought. The empirical generalization they claim is supported by thin evidence, and I'm not entirely convinced the framework actually extends meaningfully to settings where cross-view redundancy doesn't hold. This might be a paper-within-a-paper situation where they should've just cut this section.

The writing is generally okay but there are places where the notation gets cluttered. Figure 2 is helpful but Figure 3 I found confusing — the caption doesn't adequately explain what we're supposed to take away from it.

My honest assessment: the theoretical contribution is modest and the empirical validation is insufficient for acceptance. The paper identifies a genuinely useful unifying perspective, but it doesn't push far enough in either direction — it's neither a strong theory paper nor a strong empirical one. I think there's a real paper here with more work, but in its current form it reads like a workshop paper that got expanded. Leaning toward rejection, though I acknowledge I might be wrong about the novelty if the authors can better situate their specific contributions against the IB literature.

This paper proposes Magnetic Preference Optimization (MPO) for LLM alignment, building on Magnetic Mirror Descent to achieve last-iterate convergence to the Nash equilibrium of the original (unregularized) preference game. The theoretical contribution is reasonably clear and addresses a genuine gap — most existing self-play methods like SPPO either give average-iterate guarantees or converge to a regularized NE, which is indeed a meaningful limitation.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

On the theory side: the linear convergence result (Theorem 3.2, I believe) relies on assumptions that I'm not fully convinced hold in the LLM fine-tuning setting. The authors sort of acknowledge this in the practical implementation section, but then the gap between theory and practice feels uncomfortably wide. The "magnetic" pull toward a reference policy — great, it's a nice mechanism — but in practice this reference policy is being updated iteratively (Section 4.2 if I'm reading this right), which seems to undermine the convergence guarantees? I read this section twice and I'm still not entirely sure how the authors justify this. This needs to be addressed much more explicitly.

The empirical evaluation is okay but not particularly convincing. The AlpacaEval 2.0 and MT-Bench results in Table 1 show improvements over SPPO and some baselines, but the margins aren't always dramatic, and I notice the variance/confidence intervals are absent for several key comparisons. In my experience, these benchmarks can be quite noisy, and without proper statistical treatment I can't tell if some of these improvements are meaningful. Also — and maybe I'm being unfair here — the baselines feel a bit dated. Where's a comparison against more recent iterative DPO variants or some of the stronger online methods?

I found the writing in Section 3 pretty dense. The transition from the abstract game-theoretic setup to the practical RLHF objective could be smoother. Figure 1 is helpful conceptually but the caption undersells what's being shown.

One thing I genuinely liked: the framing of the problem is crisp, and the motivation for why average-iterate convergence is practically problematic (storage costs, inference overhead) is well-articulated in the introduction. This is a real issue that practitioners care about.

My bigger worry is novelty. Magnetic Mirror Descent is not new, and applying it to the two-player game formulation of RLHF is a fairly direct extension. The authors need to more carefully delineate what's technically novel versus what's application of existing machinery. Right now it reads a bit like the hard work was done by MMD and the paper is connecting dots.

Overall: interesting direction, theory has some gaps I'd want addressed, empirics need strengthening. I'm leaning weak reject but could be convinced otherwise if the authors clarify the theory-practice gap and add more robust experimental comparisons.

This paper proposes a three-stage learning framework that essentially inverts the information bottleneck principle — rather than compressing representations, the authors argue that retaining "supplemental" (redundant/superfluous) features can help in OOD and low-resource settings. The core intuition is reasonable and I've seen related ideas in the domain generalization literature, but I have significant concerns about both the framing and execution.

The theoretical motivation feels somewhat hand-wavy to me. The argument that superfluous features become useful under distribution shift is not new — this connects directly to work on spurious correlations, invariant risk minimization, and the debates around IRM (Arjovsky et al. 2019) and follow-ups. The paper doesn't adequately position itself against this body of work, and after reading the related work section twice I'm still not sure what the authors think distinguishes their contribution from, say, ensemble-based or multi-view approaches that also try to capture complementary feature sets. The connection to information bottleneck theory specifically feels more like framing than substance.

The three-stage framework described in Section 3 — I found the description genuinely hard to follow on first pass. Figure 2 helps somewhat but the notation in equations 4-6 is inconsistent with the text description. What exactly gets frozen between stage 1 and stage 2? This needs to be made much clearer. In my experience, training stability in these sequential setups is a real practical concern and the paper barely addresses it.

On the experiments: Table 2 shows improvements in low-resource settings and I'll grant that the numbers look reasonable. But the baselines are weak. Where is the comparison against more recent domain generalization methods? The paper compares against standard ERM and a few IB variants, but given the claims being made, I'd want to see comparisons against CORAL, DomainBed benchmarks, or similar. The "out-of-domain" evaluation setups described in Section 4.2 are also somewhat narrow — two datasets is not extensive, whatever the abstract claims.

I'm also skeptical of the framing around "relieving supplemental features from suppression." This reads more like a story told post-hoc around an architectural choice rather than a principled insight. Maybe I'm wrong about that, but the ablations in Table 3 don't definitively isolate what's doing the work here.

The writing needs work. There are several awkward phrasings throughout — "mainline features" is used constantly but never formally defined until quite late. Minor thing but it bothered me.

Look, the paper isn't without merit. The core idea that we shouldn't always compress representations for robust generalization is worth exploring, and the low-resource results are interesting. But in its current form this feels like a workshop paper that needs more theoretical grounding, stronger baselines, and clearer exposition before it's ready for the main track. I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with major revisions.

This paper proposes a benchmark (USMB) for evaluating text similarity metrics beyond cosine similarity, and demonstrates that task-specific ensembles of metrics can outperform cosine similarity by a substantial margin. The motivation is reasonable — I agree that the field has perhaps over-indexed on cosine similarity without questioning whether it's actually optimal — but I have significant concerns about the execution.

My biggest issue is with the 40.3% improvement claim. After reading the abstract and then hunting through the paper for where this number comes from, I found it's based on an ensembled model that is trained/tuned on the benchmark datasets themselves. This raises serious circularity concerns. If the ensemble is optimized on the benchmark, of course it's going to do well on the benchmark. What's the generalization story here? I think this needs to be addressed much more carefully, and honestly the headline claim feels misleading as currently presented.

The benchmark construction itself — 5 tasks, 30+ datasets — seems reasonable on the surface, but I found myself wanting more justification for why *these* tasks and *these* datasets. The paper doesn't convincingly argue that this particular collection represents a principled or comprehensive coverage of semantic similarity needs. Why not STS-B variants? What about tasks like semantic textual similarity in multilingual settings? The scope feels a bit arbitrary.

I'm also not entirely sure what the actual novel contribution is beyond aggregating existing datasets and running a bunch of metrics on them. The metrics evaluated (cosine, dot product, euclidean, various others) are all well-known. The ensemble approach is not novel. So the contribution is really the benchmark itself, and I think that needs to be more compelling. Benchmark papers at top venues need to make a strong case for why the community *needs* this benchmark — MTEB (Muennighoff et al.) already covers a lot of this ground, and the authors don't adequately distinguish USMB from it.

On the writing: Table 2 is hard to read and I had to squint at it for a while to understand what I was looking at. The presentation could be much cleaner. Also, the term "Unified semantic Similarity Metric Benchmark" — the capitalization of "semantic" in the title is inconsistent and just looks sloppy.

I think there's a genuinely interesting question buried here about metric selection for embedding spaces, and some of the empirical findings are mildly interesting. But in its current form this reads more like a workshop paper or a technical report than a full conference contribution. The claims need to be more carefully scoped, the benchmark needs stronger justification, and the comparison to existing work (especially MTEB) needs to be much more thorough. I'd encourage the authors to revise significantly before resubmission.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about the expressivity of GNNs with different activation functions, specifically asking whether the celebrated uniform expressivity result of Barceló et al. (2020) for ReLU networks extends to other common activations like sigmoid and tanh. The answer is no, and the paper also provides what I think is the more practically interesting result: that you can still get efficient (logarithmic or log-log) parameter dependence on the degree of input graphs.

Let me start with what I found genuinely impressive. The negative result — that Pfaffian activations cannot achieve uniform expressivity for GC2 — is a clean, well-motivated theorem, and the connection to Pfaffian function theory is elegant. I had to read Section 3 twice to fully follow the argument, but once I did, I was fairly convinced. The key insight about the number of zeros of Pfaffian functions being bounded is the right tool here and I haven't seen it applied in this context before.

That said, I have real concerns about the paper's presentation. The gap between the theoretical machinery and what the experiments actually demonstrate is pretty wide. The experimental section (Section 5, roughly pages 9-10) feels tacked on — showing that parameter counts scale logarithmically in practice is fine but doesn't really stress-test the theory in any interesting way. I'd want to see harder cases, failure modes, something that could actually falsify a claim.

I'm also not entirely satisfied with the discussion of what "efficiently expressive" means in practice. A log dependency on max degree sounds nice until you realize that for graphs with degree in the millions (social networks, web graphs), even log(degree) can be substantial. The paper acknowledges this briefly but I think it deserves more honest treatment. The log-log result for specific activation functions is intriguing but the activation function required seems somewhat artificial — I'd like to understand whether anything resembling it appears in practical architectures.

The related work section is okay but I think the relationship to work on circuit complexity and GNNs (e.g., some of Grohe's other work, and the line connecting to descriptive complexity more broadly) could be handled more carefully. The paper does cite Grohe 2021 as motivation but I felt the positioning relative to the broader descriptive complexity literature was a bit rushed.

Minor complaints: some notation in Section 2 is introduced without sufficient motivation, and Figure 1 is honestly not very informative. The writing is generally clear but there are several places where grammar suggests non-native English and a careful proofread would help.

Overall, I think this is solid theoretical work that answers a meaningful open question. The positive results on efficient expressivity are probably the more impactful contribution. My hesitation about acceptance is mainly around the somewhat weak experimental validation and the question of whether the practical implications are as significant as claimed. I'm leaning toward accept but would want the authors to substantially revise the experiments and be more candid about the practical limitations of the log-degree bound.

This paper proposes MIDR, a multi-interest disentangled representation learning approach for multimodal recommendation. The core idea of separating modal-shared and modal-specific interest representations is reasonable, and I can see the motivation for combining this with contrastive learning. However, I have significant concerns about both the technical presentation and the experimental evaluation.

Let me start with the writing, because honestly it's quite rough. After reading this twice I'm still unclear on several key components. The description of "expected maximum" in the abstract and introduction is vague — equation 3 (if I'm reading the right one) introduces this concept but the connection to the actual optimization objective is not well explained. I found myself flipping back and forth trying to understand what exactly is being maximized and why this formulation is preferable to simpler alternatives. The notation is inconsistent across sections, which makes following the math frustrating.

On the technical side, the disentanglement approach feels underspecified. How exactly is the separation between modal-shared and modal-specific representations enforced? I see some mention of orthogonality constraints but the implementation details are thin. In my experience reviewing papers on disentangled representations (there's a substantial literature here going back to beta-VAE and beyond), you really need to be precise about what "disentangled" means in your specific setting and provide some verification that the representations are actually disentangled in a meaningful sense. The paper doesn't do this.

The experiments are on three datasets but I'm concerned about the baselines. Several recent strong multimodal recommendation baselines from 2023 are missing — I won't name them all but the comparison feels dated. Also, the improvements on some metrics look quite marginal (Table 2, a few of those numbers are within what I'd consider noise range), and there's no statistical significance testing reported anywhere. That's a real problem.

The ablation study (Table 3 I think) does support the value of individual components, which is the one clearly positive element here. At least that part tells a coherent story.

I might be wrong, but the multi-interest contrastive module seems conceptually similar to several existing works on contrastive learning for recommendation — the novelty here relative to, say, work on self-supervised graph collaborative filtering or similar lines feels incremental. The paper needs a much sharper articulation of what is genuinely new.

Minor things: Figure 2 is hard to read, the caption doesn't explain enough, and there are grammatical errors throughout that suggest this needed more careful proofreading before submission.

Overall, I think there's a kernel of an interesting idea here but the paper is not ready. The writing needs substantial revision, the technical details need to be made rigorous, and the experiments need stronger baselines and significance testing. I'm recommending rejection but would encourage the authors to resubmit after a thorough revision.

This paper proposes Speculative Streaming, a method to accelerate LLM inference by integrating draft token generation directly into the target model via multi-stream attention, rather than relying on a separate draft model. The core idea is interesting and the parameter efficiency claim (~10000x fewer extra parameters than alternatives like Medusa) is compelling on its face.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work that I think need to be addressed.

The experimental evaluation feels thin to me. The speedup numbers (1.9-3x) are reported across "summarization, structured queries, and meaning representation" but I found the baseline comparisons somewhat cherry-picked. Where is the comparison against standard speculative decoding with a properly tuned small draft model? The authors somewhat hand-wave this away by arguing that maintaining draft models is complex in multi-task settings, which is fair, but if the goal is to demonstrate superiority, you need the comparison. The 2.9-3.2x speedup in the lossless chatbot setting is interesting but I'd want to see this on more standard benchmarks — MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, something the community actually uses.

I found the multi-stream attention description in Section 3 a bit hard to follow on first read. After going through it twice, I think I understand the mechanism, but Figure 2 could be clearer about how the streams interact during training vs. inference. The masking scheme particularly needs better exposition. Also, equation 3 — I'm not entirely sure the formulation correctly captures the dependency structure being claimed. I might be wrong here but it felt underspecified.

The claim about "interdependence among speculated tokens" being a key advantage over Medusa is reasonable and I believe it, but I don't think this is rigorously demonstrated. The ablation in Table 3 helps somewhat but doesn't fully isolate this factor.

One thing I genuinely liked: the framing around parameter efficiency and the practical deployment story for resource-constrained devices is compelling and honestly undersold in the abstract. For edge deployment scenarios this could matter a lot. I'd encourage the authors to lean into this more with concrete memory/compute profiling.

The related work section is adequate but misses some recent concurrent work on self-speculative decoding that seems quite relevant. The authors should engage with this.

Minor: some of the writing in the intro is a bit breathless ("novel," "first," "prominent") — dial it back. The actual contribution is solid enough that it doesn't need marketing language.

Bottom line: interesting idea with real practical value, but the evaluation needs strengthening and some technical exposition needs clarification. I'm leaning weak reject right now, primarily because of the baseline comparison gap, but I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can provide stronger comparisons and address the technical clarity issues in a revision.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — the fact that recourse recommendations can become invalidated when the underlying model changes due to distribution shift. The motivation is sound and I've seen this issue come up repeatedly in fairness and explainability discussions, so I appreciate that the authors are taking it seriously.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the experimental evaluation.

On the technical side, the distributionally robust formulation (centering around a mixture of model shifts) feels like a fairly straightforward application of existing DRO machinery to the recourse setting. After reading this twice, I couldn't identify a technically novel insight that distinguishes this from, say, just plugging recourse objectives into standard robust optimization frameworks. The authors claim the projected gradient descent algorithm is efficient, but I found the convergence guarantees in Section 4 to be quite weak — the assumptions feel restrictive and aren't well justified for the practical settings described. I might be wrong about this, but equation (6) in particular seems to assume something very close to convexity of the decision boundary, which is a strong ask when you're dealing with neural network classifiers.

The experiments are where I became genuinely frustrated. The real-world datasets used (German Credit, COMPAS, etc.) are fine, but I've seen these exact benchmarks in probably thirty recourse papers at this point. The baselines feel cherry-picked — where is the comparison against Upadhyay et al.'s robust recourse work or the invalidation-aware methods from Pawelczyk et al.? These feel like direct competitors that are conspicuously absent. Table 2 shows improvements in validity rates under shift, but the margins are sometimes small (e.g., ~2-3%) and I'd want to see statistical significance testing here. The synthetic experiments honestly don't tell me much.

I also found the "mixture of model shifts" framing a bit underspecified. How is this mixture chosen in practice? The paper hand-waves this somewhat in Section 3.2 and I think a practitioner reading this would be stuck. This connects to a broader concern: the framework requires specifying an uncertainty set over models, and the paper doesn't give much guidance on how to do this sensibly.

The writing is generally clear but there are patches of dense notation that could be cleaned up — Figure 3 in particular is hard to parse without reading the caption three times.

Look, I think the problem is real and worth solving. But in its current form, this paper reads like an incremental application of DRO to recourse rather than a substantive contribution. I'd want to see a more thorough comparison to existing robust recourse methods, stronger theoretical results, and a clearer story about practical deployment. I'm leaning toward rejection but would reconsider if the authors can meaningfully address the missing baselines and clarify the convergence analysis.

This paper proposes DDAD, a defense method that combines statistical adversarial data detection (specifically MMD-based) with denoising to handle both clean and adversarial examples at inference time. The core idea is sensible enough — rather than discarding detected adversarial inputs entirely, use a denoiser on them — and I appreciate that the authors try to ground this in some theoretical justification.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The theoretical contribution (Section 3, the claim that minimizing distributional discrepancy reduces expected loss on AEs) feels thin to me. After reading the relevant propositions twice, I'm not convinced the bound is tight enough to be practically meaningful. The gap between "minimizing MMD helps in theory" and "this justifies our specific training procedure" is larger than the authors acknowledge. I'd want to see this fleshed out considerably, or honestly, toned down in the paper's framing.

The experimental setup raises red flags. The comparisons against "SOTA" methods — I'd want to know exactly which attack strengths and budgets are being used, because in my experience, papers in this space sometimes cherry-pick epsilon values or iteration counts that favor their method. Table 2 shows impressive numbers but I couldn't find sufficient detail about the adaptive attack construction in the main text. Adaptive attacks against detection-based defenses are notoriously tricky to set up correctly, and the brief treatment in what appears to be Section 5.3 doesn't fully convince me the adversary has been given a fair shot at breaking the detection component. Has the attacker been given full knowledge of the MMD-OPT kernel? This needs to be crystal clear.

The two-pronged inference idea is intuitive, but the denoiser training via MMD-OPT is not obviously better than just training a standard denoiser with L2 loss. The ablation in Table 3 helps somewhat, but I found myself wanting a cleaner comparison where everything else is held fixed. Also, the potential for misclassification errors — clean examples routed to the denoiser, adversarial examples passed through directly — seems underexplored. What's the sensitivity to the detection threshold? Figure 3 touches on this but I felt it needed more discussion.

On the writing: it's generally clear, though there are places where the notation gets cluttered (around equations 4-6 especially). The related work is adequate but doesn't engage deeply with diffusion-based purification methods, which feel like a natural comparison point.

I'm not entirely sure DDAD is as strong as claimed here. The results look good on paper but the adaptive attack evaluation needs to be substantially more rigorous. My recommendation is major revision. The core idea has merit, the theoretical framing needs honest recalibration, and the experimental validation — particularly adaptive attacks — needs to be more thorough before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper tackles an important and timely problem — generalization in deep RL — and the core contribution of automatically selecting data augmentations is well-motivated. I've read a fair amount in this space (UCB-DrAC, RAD, PLR, etc.) and this work fits naturally into that lineage while adding something genuinely new.

The theoretical motivation for the regularization terms is probably the strongest part of the paper. I appreciated that the authors didn't just throw augmentation at an actor-critic method and call it a day — actually deriving why naive augmentation can be problematic for policy and value function updates is useful and I think underappreciated in prior work. Equations 4 and 5 (if I'm remembering the numbering correctly) make a reasonable case, though I'd want someone to push harder on the assumptions baked into Proposition 1. The bound feels a bit loose and I'm not fully convinced it's tight enough to be practically meaningful, versus just providing post-hoc justification for something that empirically works anyway.

The empirical results on Procgen are strong. A 40% relative improvement over the PPO baseline is notable, and the comparisons against CURL, RAD, and UCB-DrAC look favorable. That said, I'm somewhat skeptical of how the baselines are tuned. Were all methods given equal hyperparameter search budget? In my experience this is where these comparisons often quietly break down, and the paper is a bit thin on this in the experimental setup section.

The three automatic augmentation selection strategies (I'll call them the UCB approach, the online approach, and the adversarial approach roughly) are interesting but honestly feel a bit loosely connected to each other. It reads like three ideas that each sort of work rather than a unified framework. The ablations in Table 3 help somewhat but I still came away uncertain which of the three is actually doing the heavy lifting and why. Is the augmentation selection itself critical, or is most of the gain coming from the regularization terms? This feels like the key question and I don't think the paper fully answers it.

The background robustness experiments are a nice addition — Figure 5 or thereabouts is compelling visually — though this section feels somewhat tacked on compared to the main Procgen story.

Minor things: the writing is generally clear but the related work section is a bit rushed and misses some relevant citations around curriculum learning and domain randomization that I'd expect to see. Also the notation gets a bit heavy around section 3.2 and could be cleaned up.

Overall I think this is a solid paper. The problem matters, the results are good, and there's real technical content here. My main hesitation is the somewhat scattered feeling of the method section and the unanswered question about what's actually driving performance. I'd lean toward acceptance but I'd want the authors to address the baseline tuning question and sharpen the narrative around which component matters most.

This paper tackles an interesting and arguably important question in the hyperbolic learning literature — namely, whether HNNs actually achieve the theoretical embeddings that motivate their use in the first place. I'll admit I came in somewhat skeptical, because the framing felt like it might be a "negative result" paper dressed up as something more constructive, but after reading the full paper I think there's genuine value here, even if the execution has some issues.

The core empirical finding — that HNNs fail to achieve theoretical optimal embeddings and that this gap is heavily influenced by optimization objectives rather than architecture — is actually a meaningful contribution. In my experience, this is something practitioners have suspected but nobody has tried to systematically quantify. The proposed HRC benchmark (Section 3) seems reasonable, though I found the formalization somewhat hard to follow on first read. Specifically, around equations 4-6, I think the paper could do a better job distinguishing between the metric they're proposing and existing tree-distortion metrics from the embedding literature. The connection to Gromov delta-hyperbolicity is mentioned but feels underdeveloped.

My bigger concern is with the pre-training strategies proposed in Section 5. I found this section rushed. The strategies themselves are not particularly novel — they feel like fairly standard self-supervised objectives adapted to hyperbolic space — and the ablations in Table 3 don't fully convince me that the improvements are coming from the HRC enhancement specifically rather than just the additional pre-training signal in general. This is a pretty serious confound that I don't think the authors adequately address. Did you try the same pre-training objectives in Euclidean space? That baseline seems obviously necessary.

The experimental setup is large-scale, which I appreciate, but I'm not entirely sure the datasets chosen in Section 6 are the right ones to stress-test the hierarchy-sensitivity of the analysis. A lot of the datasets have fairly shallow hierarchical structure, which might explain why some of the HRC numbers cluster in a narrow range in Figure 4.

I might be wrong about this, but the claim in the abstract that "much research is built on flawed motivations" feels a bit overstated given the results. The paper shows a gap exists, not that the motivation is *wrong* per se — hyperbolic space may still provide useful geometric inductive biases even if optimal embeddings aren't achieved. The framing should be more careful here.

Writing quality is okay but could be tightened. Some sentences on pages 6-7 are genuinely hard to parse.

Overall I think this is a borderline paper. The research question is good, the empirical work is mostly solid, but the theoretical analysis is shallow and the pre-training contribution feels tacked on. I'd want to see the Euclidean pre-training baseline and a cleaner treatment of the benchmark formalization before recommending acceptance.

I found the core idea here pretty intuitive, and honestly a bit overdue. If you are going to use early-layer [CLS] attention as the signal for token pruning, then yes, giving the early layers some actual task pressure makes sense. In that sense the paper’s main claim landed for me quickly. The plots in Fig. 1 and later the ablations around early exits support the story reasonably well. I also appreciated that the method is conceptually simple rather than yet another complicated token scoring gadget.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates “multi-exit helps representation learning” from “multi-exit specifically improves token scoring reliability.” The argument on p. 3–4 is plausible, but the evidence feels somewhat indirect. Eq. (6) and the surrounding text describe the auxiliary loss and self-distillation, but I wanted a more direct analysis of whether the [CLS]-attention ranking actually aligns better with leave-one-out token importance or some perturbation-based metric. Right now the paper mostly shows end-task accuracy/FLOPs improvements, which is necessary but not sufficient for the mechanistic claim.

I also found some details underexplained. On p. 5, around the implementation of exits, it seems the exits are used only in training and removed at inference, but the wording is a bit slippery. If so, good, but then the exact training overhead should be reported more explicitly. Table 1 focuses on inference efficiency, which is fair, but in practice training cost matters here because you are adding multiple heads plus distillation. Maybe I missed it.

The empirical results are solid overall. Table 2 and Table 3 look competitive, especially at more aggressive reduction ratios, and that is exactly where these methods often fall apart. I liked that. Still, I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are perfectly apples-to-apples. Some baselines seem adapted from prior codebases with different schedules/augmentations, and the appendix needs to be very explicit there. In my experience, token pruning papers are extremely sensitive to recipe details.

A smaller issue: the writing is occasionally a bit too cute (“simple romance,” “newfound chemistry”). I get the intent, but for a conference paper it sometimes undercuts an otherwise technically serious contribution.

Overall I think this is a meaningful and practically useful paper, though not a huge conceptual leap. My leaning is positive, but I would want the camera-ready (or rebuttal) to clarify the causal evidence for the token-scoring story and the true training-time cost.

I had to read this twice, partly because the core claim is interesting and partly because the presentation is pretty hard to follow. The idea of using TRUST-TECH to systematically traverse to multiple local optima during one training run is, at least on paper, novel for deep nets. I’ve seen a lot of “find diverse minima / cheap ensembles” papers over the years, and this one is trying to do something more deterministic than the usual cyclic LR / snapshot / mode connectivity story. I appreciate that.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper really delivers on its strongest claims. The biggest red flag is the experimental framing around “20% improvement” on CIFAR-10 and “15%” on CIFAR-100 in the abstract. Looking at Table 2 (and I think also Table 3), this seems to be relative error reduction rather than absolute accuracy gain, which is a very different thing. As written, it reads much more dramatic than it is. I find that a bit slippery.

Technically, the DSP component is also under-explained. The discussion around the dynamic search paths on p. 4–5 is intuitive, but I never felt I got a clean algorithmic understanding of what is actually guaranteed versus heuristic. Eq. (7) and the surrounding text are especially dense, and I’m not entirely sure how the method avoids just becoming a complicated perturb-and-retrain procedure. The claim that the computation graph remains constant with only minor GPU overhead is nice, but where is the actual accounting? I wanted a wall-clock and memory comparison against standard baselines, not just conceptual statements.

I also struggled with the notion of “high-quality local optimal solutions.” Are these truly distinct basins, or just nearby points found along a path induced by the same training trajectory? Figure 3 suggests some diversity, but not enough to support the stronger narrative. In my experience, ensemble gains often come from fairly mundane diversity effects, and the paper does not convincingly isolate what TRUST-TECH specifically contributes beyond generating several checkpoints with some structured perturbation.

The paper is promising, and I do think there is something here. But right now it feels oversold and under-validated. I would have liked stronger comparisons to snapshot ensembles, SWA/SWAG-style basins, and modern cheap ensemble methods, plus clearer ablations on DSP itself. My leaning is weak reject: interesting idea, but the evidence and exposition are not at top-conference level yet.

I thought this was a strong paper overall, and honestly one of the more interesting “multilingual self-supervised speech” submissions I’ve read in a while. The core idea is quite simple—take wav2vec 2.0 and pretrain it jointly on raw speech from multiple languages—but the empirical results are good enough that I don’t think simplicity is a criticism here. In fact, Table 1 and Table 3 are pretty convincing. The CommonVoice gains are large, and the low-resource transfer story seems real rather than cherry-picked.

What I particularly liked is that the paper does not just report downstream ASR numbers and stop there. The analysis of shared quantized units across languages (I think this is around Figure 4 / the later analysis section) is actually useful. A lot of multilingual papers hand-wave about “shared representations,” but here there is at least some attempt to inspect whether the codebook usage aligns with language relatedness. That said, I wanted more. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t fully sure whether the observed sharing is genuinely linguistic or partly an artifact of acoustic/domain overlap in CommonVoice. I might be wrong, but the paper seems a bit too quick to interpret this as language-structure sharing.

I also found some of the comparisons slightly slippery. The “best known results” claim in the abstract is flashy, maybe too flashy. On page 5/6 (where the CommonVoice results are discussed), I wanted a cleaner accounting of supervision, model size, and data balance across baselines. Some baselines are monolingual, some multilingual, some use different decoding setups; this is all normal, but then the framing should be more careful. Otherwise it reads a little like marketing.

The methodology itself is sound. Equation (1)-style wav2vec objectives are standard by now, so the novelty is mostly in the multilingual setup and shared quantization, but I think that is enough for a conference paper. Figure 1 is clear. The BABEL results also matter because they suggest this is not only a CommonVoice effect.

One thing I was missing: more ablations on language sampling and imbalance. In my experience, multilingual training lives or dies on this. How sensitive is XLSR to high-resource languages dominating the codebook? The paper hints at this but doesn’t really answer it. Also, the single multilingual model being “competitive” with individual models is true, but from Table 4 it is not uniformly better, so I’d tone that down a bit.

Still, I came away thinking this will likely have impact. It’s practical, timely, and the gains are substantial. I would lean accept.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of meta-RL submissions I’ve seen lately. The core idea—use offline reward-labeled data for meta-training, then collect unlabeled online interaction to reduce the adaptation-time distribution shift—is sensible, and in retrospect kind of obvious in a good way. The problem setup on p.2–3 is well articulated, especially the distinction between the adaptation data distribution induced by the learned exploration policy versus the static offline buffer. That is a real issue; I’ve run into related failure modes in offline RL and this paper is one of the few that states it clearly.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the method is as principled as the presentation suggests. The objective around Eq. (4) / Eq. (5) (assuming I read it correctly) seems to splice together offline reward-conditioned meta-training with an online self-supervised representation or dynamics-learning term, but I had to read that section twice. Figure 2 helps, but the algorithmic description is still a bit hand-wavy about what exactly is updated during the unlabeled online phase and what is frozen.
