I might have missed it, but I did not see confidence intervals or even standard deviations. For claims this aggressive, that’s a problem. Also, the speedup claim (2.7x inference) needs much more care. On what hardware, batch size, sequence length, and implementation? In compression papers, these details matter a lot, and I found the reporting here too convenient.

I was also left wondering how much of the gain comes from the decomposition itself versus post-decomposition fine-tuning budget. The training recipe was not fully transparent to me. Equation (6) and the rank-selection discussion are central, but the practical heuristic still feels somewhat ad hoc. Maybe that is unavoidable, but then say so more plainly.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar, mainly because it explores a less fashionable but genuinely useful corner of the compression space. But I do wish the evaluation were tougher—more seeds, at least one larger downstream task, and a cleaner latency analysis. Right now the paper is promising, not airtight.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more useful than it may look at first glance. The setting is narrow — fully Gaussian data, linear score, exact solvability — but that is also the point. In a literature full of empirical folklore about sampler choices, having a case where one can compute exact Wasserstein errors for initialization, truncation, discretization, and score error is valuable. I especially appreciated the closed-form treatment of the reverse SDE and probability flow ODE around Sec. 3; if I’m reading Eq. (12)–(16) correctly, the derivations are clean and the Gaussian structure is exploited in the right way rather than being just a toy decoration.

What I liked most is that the paper does not stop at “we can solve it exactly,” but pushes through to explicit error accounting. Table 1 and the discussion around Fig. 2 were useful. In particular, comparing the recommended samplers from the diffusion literature inside a regime where one can actually know the Wasserstein error exactly is a nice sanity check. I’ve reviewed too many papers where “better FID” is basically the whole argument, so this was refreshing.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper’s presentation, and at times it feels more cumbersome than it should. I had to reread parts of Sec. 4 twice. The notation for the different Gaussian processes induced by exact dynamics vs discretized dynamics is a bit messy, and I found the transitions between the continuous-time statements and discrete schemes abrupt. Also, unless I missed it, the assumptions on the noise schedule and on invertibility/regularity of the covariance operators should be stated more sharply earlier on (I think some of this only becomes fully clear around the theorem statements on p. 7–8). For a theory paper, that matters.

I also think the empirical section is a little thin. Yes, the theory is the main point, but once you claim that “the recommended numerical schemes from the diffusion models literature are also the best” (abstract, and echoed near the end), I want a more nuanced discussion of what exactly transfers beyond Gaussians. Right now the paper is careful mathematically but a bit too eager in the messaging. Gaussian data are not just “restricted”; they eliminate essentially all geometric complexity. That’s fine, but say it plainly.

My overall take: good paper, worthwhile, but somewhat overclaims in interpretation. I’d lean accept at a theory-friendly venue or as a solid conference paper if space allows. The core contribution is real. The writing could be tightened, and the scope should be framed more modestly.

I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The core idea—replace the usual log-bilinear scoring with a per-word neural network—is interesting, and I do think there is something potentially worthwhile here, especially the parameter-sharing story with POS tags. But after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the paper really nails either the modeling justification or the empirical case.

The basic construction on p. 2–3 is clear enough at a high level, but I found the actual parameterization oddly under-explained. Eq. (3) seems to define a separate network for each vocabulary item, which immediately raises scalability concerns, yet the paper mostly waves this away by discussing hierarchy/shareability later. Maybe I’m missing something, but if the vocabulary is even moderately large, this sounds expensive in both memory and training time. Table 1 reports predictive gains over standard embeddings, but I kept looking for the computational cost comparison and didn’t really find it. In my experience, for language models this matters a lot; a small perplexity improvement with 10x parameters is not a very compelling trade unless the downstream gains are substantial.

I did like Figure 2. The parameter sharing via syntactic hierarchy is probably the most novel part of the paper, and it is also the part that felt least fully evaluated. The POS-sharing variant improves held-out prediction on both corpora (Table 2), but the datasets are fairly limited: Wikipedia and Senate speeches are not exactly a broad stress test. Senate speeches especially are a bit niche and stylistically repetitive. I would have expected at least one standard benchmark corpus, or some downstream task beyond next-word/held-out word prediction. The qualitative examples on p. 5 are nice, though honestly a little cherry-picked. Many embedding papers do this, so I won’t single them out too much, but it doesn’t add much evidence.

I was also confused by the claim of “interpretable semantic representations.” I’m not entirely sure what notion of interpretability is meant here. The discussion around Figure 3 gestures at hidden units aligning with semantic/syntactic attributes, but this is pretty weak as presented. If that is a major claim, it needs more than a few anecdotes.

Overall, I think the paper has a good idea inside it, but in its current form it feels more like an intriguing workshop paper than a top-conference contribution. The novelty is real, but the evaluation and exposition are not quite there. My leaning is borderline reject.

I found the paper interesting in intent, but pretty uneven in execution. The core idea — reinterpreting Adam-type methods through mirror descent — is not crazy at all, and in fact I think this is the right lens in principle. A lot of the older Adam literature got stuck in regret-bound gymnastics, so I appreciated the attempt to give a more structural explanation. That said, after reading this twice, I’m not convinced the paper really delivers a genuinely new perspective to the extent it claims.

The derivation around the mirror descent view (around Sec. 3, especially the equations following Eq. (8) and the discussion near Eq. (11)) is the strongest part. There is some intuition there about the roles of momentum, adaptive scaling, and the implicit geometry. But the presentation is quite sloppy. I had to reverse-engineer several steps, and I’m still not entirely sure whether the mapping is exact for the algorithm they state, or only approximate under particular choices of the distance-generating function. This matters. Right now the paper sometimes sounds like it has “explained” Adam, when really it has given one plausible decomposition.

The claims about identifying the non-convergence issue also felt overstated. The discussion around the failure mode (I think this is on p. 5–6, around the paragraph after Fig. 2) is intuitive, but not especially rigorous, and frankly it reads a bit like a post hoc story. I might be wrong, but I don’t see a sharp theorem or diagnostic here that goes beyond what people already understand from AMSGrad and related analyses. If the contribution is mainly conceptual, then the exposition has to be cleaner than this.

AdamAL itself is okay, but again I was left asking: why this variant in particular? The update rule seems somewhat heuristic. The experiments (Table 1 and Table 2) are decent in breadth, but not deep. There are too many benchmarks where the margins are small and variance bars or multi-seed statistics are missing. On training deep nets, these details are not optional. Also, I really wanted an ablation isolating the “AL” component from other implementation choices. Without that, it’s hard to tell whether the proposed insight actually led to the gains.

I also have to say the writing needs work. There are grammatical issues throughout, and several grandiose claims in the abstract/introduction that the paper doesn’t fully support. In a workshop I’d be more forgiving. For a top venue, I expect tighter theory or stronger empirical evidence. Right now I see an interesting angle, but not a mature paper. My leaning is reject, though not by a huge margin.

I had to read this twice because I initially wasn’t sure what the actual contribution was supposed to be: is this a detection method for unstable / adversarial observation directions, a theoretical characterization of local policy loss geometry, or a practical defense? The paper seems to want to be all three. I think the core idea — using a quadratic approximation of the policy loss to identify “non-robust directions” — is interesting, and in spirit it fits with a lot of prior work on curvature/Hessian-based local robustness analysis. But I was not fully convinced that the paper really nails either the theory or the empirical story.

On the theory side, the central approximation around Eq. (3)–(5) is intuitive, but I found the leap from local second-order behavior to a “fundamental cut-off” between stable and non-robust observations too strong. Maybe I’m missing something, but the assumptions needed for that statement seem much stricter than the paper acknowledges. The loss landscape in deep RL is noisy, nonstationary, and policy-dependent in ways that make local quadratic models fragile. The discussion on p. 4, especially around the proposition after Eq. (6), felt under-argued. I kept asking myself: is this a characterization of the environment-policy pair at one state, or something more global? The text blurs that distinction.

Empirically, I appreciate the ALE experiments and the attempt to test against multiple alteration methods. Table 1 and Fig. 3 are probably the strongest parts of the paper. That said, the evaluation still feels selective. I did not see enough comparison to simpler baselines. For instance, how much of the gain comes from just looking at gradient norm, Jacobian spectrum, or even variance across stochastic augmentations? In my experience, second-order methods often look fancy but collapse to first-order heuristics in practice. The paper should really show this more directly. Also, the claim in the abstract that the method remains effective even when attacks are optimized to circumvent it is important, but the evidence for that (I think this is Sec. 5.3 / Fig. 5?) was weaker than advertised. It’s not clear how strong those adaptive attacks really are.

Writing-wise, the paper is rough. There are several awkward phrasings, and some notation is introduced too quickly. I found lines 168–185 especially hard to parse. This sounds minor, but it made an already technical paper harder to trust.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, but the paper currently overclaims. I’m leaning negative in its current form, mostly because the theoretical statements are stronger than the evidence supports, and the empirical section does not quite rule out simpler explanations. If toned down and better positioned, this could become a solid workshop paper or a better conference submission later.

I read this paper with interest because I’ve seen a lot of “quantization-aware NAS” papers over the years, and many of them do indeed suffer from the usual search-then-retrain mismatch. So the core motivation here makes sense to me. The idea of “search once, deploy the quantized weights directly” is appealing, and honestly overdue. I also appreciate that the paper is not just about 8-bit toy settings but pushes into 4/3/2-bit, where things get much uglier in practice.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper in its current form. The main issue for me is that the central methodological novelty feels a bit fuzzier than the paper claims. The progressive bit inheritance procedure (Sec. 3.3, around Eq. (7) and the discussion on p. 5) sounds reasonable, but after reading it twice I still found it under-specified. Is this mainly a training stabilization trick, or is it actually essential to the search quality? The paper seems to want both claims. Figure 3 gives some intuition, but not enough. I would really like to see a cleaner ablation disentangling: (i) weight inheritance across architectures, (ii) bit inheritance across quantization levels, and (iii) the “once-for-all” style supernet training itself. Table 4 helps, but only partially.

The empirical results are strong on the surface. The ImageNet numbers in Table 2 are impressive, especially the 2-bit result versus EfficientNet-B0@QKD. A 14% gain is huge, almost suspiciously huge, and I say that not to be snarky but because such a gap usually means the baselines are not aligned in some important way. I might be wrong, but I’m not sure the comparison is fully apples-to-apples in terms of training recipe, augmentation, distillation, and search budget. The paper mentions search cost on p. 6, but it is not reported consistently against all baselines. In my experience, these details matter more than authors want to admit.

I also found the writing uneven. Some parts are quite clear, but other parts (especially the explanation around Eq. (4)-(6)) are dense and a bit hand-wavy. There are several claims of “SOTA” that should be phrased more carefully given the heterogeneity of quantization setups.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and the results are potentially important. But I’m left with too many questions about what exactly is doing the work, and whether the comparisons are as fair as they need to be for a top conference paper. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the ablations and baseline setup.

I had a somewhat mixed reaction to this paper. The core idea is extremely simple — essentially a skip/direct path from the current input to the output on top of a recurrent architecture — and to be honest my first instinct was “surely people have already done this in one form or another.” After reading the paper twice, I still think the novelty is modest. But I also have to admit the empirical effect seems real, and the paper makes a decent case that this very boring modification is more important than many architecture tweaks the community gets excited about.

What I appreciated most is that the paper is trying to isolate a basic modeling issue: recurrent models may underuse the current token when predicting the next one. That intuition comes through clearly around the model definition section (the equations introducing the extra direct connection; I believe Eq. 5 or nearby), and some of the examples in the experiments support it. In my experience reviewing language modeling papers, simple ablations are often more valuable than yet another baroque cell variant.

That said, I found the presentation a bit overstated. The claim in the abstract that this “always improves the results, regardless of the architecture and training-specific details” is too strong. “Always” is doing a lot of work here. The empirical section has several datasets/tasks, yes, but not enough to justify such a universal statement. Also, on the pages with the main results tables (I’m looking at the language modeling comparisons, maybe Table 2 / Table 3), the margins over strong baselines are sometimes not huge, and I did not see enough discussion of variance across runs. For perplexity differences this small, that matters.

I was also missing a cleaner connection to prior work. This idea is very close in spirit to maxent/neural cache/direct n-gram style connections, and more broadly to residual/skip design. The paper cites some related recurrent variants, but I think it undersells how incremental the contribution is. Maybe I’m being a bit harsh here, but for a top ML venue I need more than “we added an obvious shortcut and it helped.”

Still, I think the paper has value. Figure 1 (if I’m remembering the numbering correctly) communicates the architecture nicely, and the experimental evidence is broad enough that practitioners will probably copy this. My bottom line: useful empirical lesson, limited conceptual novelty, somewhat overclaimed. I lean weak accept if the venue values solid ablation-driven insights; otherwise weak reject.

I read this paper fairly carefully, and then went back to sections 3 and 4 a second time because I felt I was missing the actual novelty. My overall reaction is mixed, leaning negative. The topic is important, obviously — dynamic quantization in distributed SGD is a real problem, and I’ve reviewed a lot of “adaptive bits per round” papers over the years — but I’m not convinced this submission cleanly separates itself from that literature, either theoretically or experimentally.

The main claim seems to be the upper bound on modeling error for arbitrary dynamic quantization strategies (around Theorem 1 / Eq. (8), if I’m reading the structure correctly), and then using that bound to choose the per-iteration quantization level. In principle that sounds nice. In practice, I found the assumptions doing a lot of work, and the paper is a bit slippery about what exactly is being optimized. On page 5, around the discussion after Eq. (10), the “communication overhead constraint” is introduced in a way that felt too abstract; later in the experiments, this becomes a budget measured in bits, but I never got a satisfying mapping from the theorem to the actual implementation. Maybe I’m being unfair, but the theory-to-algorithm bridge is weaker than the paper suggests.

I also struggled with the claim that the bound is “tight in some cases.” Which cases, exactly? The examples given seemed cherry-picked, and I did not see either a lower bound or a convincing empirical validation of tightness. Figure 2 is supposed to illustrate the trade-off curve, but the axes and setup are not detailed enough for me to judge whether this is a meaningful validation or just a stylized plot. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains over baselines, yes, but the baseline tuning is underexplained. This matters a lot for quantization papers. In my experience, these methods are very sensitive to learning rate schedules and error-feedback details, and I could not tell whether the comparisons are really apples-to-apples.

One thing that bothered me more than it should have: the wording around “significantly outperforms the state of the art in terms of communication costs” is too strong. On CIFAR-10/100 the gains look decent but not dramatic, and on AG-News I’m not entirely sure the setup reflects the distributed regime people care about. Also, wall-clock is missing. Communication-efficient on paper is not always efficient in systems.

To be fair, I do think the paper has some value. The formalization of dynamic quantization is cleaner than many workshop-level submissions, and parts of the derivation are technically competent. But right now it feels like a partial theory wrapped around a fairly standard adaptive quantization idea. I wanted either more convincing experiments or a sharper theorem. As is, I’m not persuaded this reaches top-conference bar.

I read this fairly carefully, and I’m a bit mixed. The core idea — treating multi-label image classification as a multi-task problem and then using a hybrid shared/specialized MoE design — is sensible, and honestly not a crazy stretch from where the literature has already been going. I do think the paper is trying to address a real issue. Negative transfer between labels is absolutely a thing in practice, especially on long-tail or semantically entangled labels. So the motivation landed for me.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper is as novel as it wants to be. By the time I got through the method section (around pp. 3–5), HSQ felt like a fairly standard transformer query formulation plus a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism, with the “fusion expert” being the main twist. I might be underselling it, but after reading Eq. (3)–(7) twice, I still found it hard to pinpoint what is fundamentally new algorithmically versus a careful recombination of existing components. The writing doesn’t help here. Figure 2 is useful at a high level, but the exact interaction between task-specific experts, shared experts, and the fusion expert remains oddly vague. In particular, how routing is trained and whether there is any collapse or imbalance issue is not discussed enough. I was looking for something like load-balancing regularization or at least routing statistics per label; I didn’t really see it.

The empirical results are solid on the surface. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains, and I appreciate that the authors emphasize “simultaneous improvements across most labels” rather than only mean mAP. That is the right framing for this problem. Still, the experimental section feels too narrow for a top conference paper. Two datasets is a bit thin, especially when one is probably not enough to establish robustness of the heterogeneity claim. I also wanted stronger ablations. The ones on page 6 are okay, but they don’t fully isolate whether the gains come from hybrid sharing specifically, from extra capacity, or simply from better optimization in the transformer backbone. Parameter-matched baselines would help a lot.

One more thing: the paper repeatedly talks about “mitigating heterogeneity,” but I’m not entirely sure the proposed metrics really measure that. Better average performance is not the same as reduced negative transfer. Some per-label tradeoff analysis, or even pairwise interference analysis, would make the claim much more convincing.

Overall, I think this is a competent paper with decent engineering and plausible gains, but for me it falls short of being clearly top-tier because the novelty is murky and the evidence for the central claim is not as tight as it should be. Borderline, leaning weak reject.

I had a somewhat mixed reaction to this paper. The core idea is extremely simple — just average two training images and keep the label of only one of them — and I admit my first instinct was that this should not work very well. But the empirical gains reported here are surprisingly strong, especially the ImageNet result in Table 1 and the small-data experiments later in the paper. So I do think there is something worth paying attention to.

That said, after reading this twice, I still found the paper a bit under-analyzed. The method itself is basically one line, but then the burden is on the authors to really explain when and why this odd regularization scheme helps. On p. 3, around the description of SamplePairing and the training schedule, the method seems to depend heavily on turning the augmentation on and off during training. This is actually one of the most important parts of the recipe, but it is not really justified. Is the gain from the paired images themselves, or from this somewhat unusual curriculum? I’m not entirely sure. Figure 3 suggests the training error stays very high during pairing, which is unsurprising, but then this makes me wonder whether the method is just a harsh regularizer in the same spirit as label noise or severe dropout.

I also think the comparison to related methods is too thin. The obvious baseline is mixup-like vicinal interpolation, although I realize this paper may predate some of that literature; still, even relative to more classical augmentation and regularization methods, the discussion is shallow. On p. 4 the authors say the second image’s label is ignored. This is the part I find hardest to swallow. Why is that the right target? Why not soft labels proportional to the mixing ratio? In my experience, whenever you mix semantics, target handling matters a lot. Equation-wise there is almost nothing here, which is fine, but then the ablations should be more careful.

I did appreciate the experiments with reduced training set size (Table 3 / the later section). Those are probably the most convincing part of the paper, and I can imagine this being useful in medical imaging or other low-data regimes. Still, some results feel a bit recipe-dependent, and the paper occasionally overclaims “significantly improved accuracy for all tested datasets” without enough nuance.

Overall, I think this is a neat empirical trick and probably publishable if the venue is open to practical augmentation papers. But as a top-conference paper, I wanted a stronger understanding of the mechanism and better-controlled ablations. Weak accept, maybe.

I read this paper with interest because the setting is actually important: in practice, not every graph pair is matchable, and forcing a one-size-fits-all matching objective is pretty artificial. The paper’s framing around “mixture graph matching and clustering” makes sense to me, and I appreciated that the authors are trying to unify grouping and matching rather than treating clustering as a preprocessing hack.

That said, I’m a bit torn. The core MM-based formulation in Sec. 3 is reasonably clean, and I do think the convergence discussion around Eq. (8)–(12) is one of the stronger parts of the paper. At least on a first pass, the derivation seems technically sound. I say “first pass” because after reading this twice, I still found the exact role of the relaxed clustering variable somewhat under-explained. The intuition is there, but the optimization story is not as transparent as it should be. In particular, the transition from the original objective to the surrogate minimized in Eq. (10) felt too brisk. I might be wrong, but I suspect many readers will struggle to reconstruct the argument without filling in missing algebra.

I also had mixed feelings about UM3C. The idea of edge-wise affinity learning is sensible, but the presentation in Sec. 4 felt like a second paper got attached to the first one. The pseudo-label selection mechanism is plausible, yet I was not convinced it is especially novel relative to the broader self-training / confidence-filtering literature. The authors should be clearer about what is actually new here. Right now the contribution statement oversells this part a bit.

The experiments are generally solid. I liked the breadth of benchmarks, and Table 1 / Table 2 suggest meaningful gains. Figure 3 is also helpful. But the evaluation section is a little too eager to claim superiority. I wanted more discussion of failure cases and sensitivity. For example, how stable is M3C to the number of mixture components or to poor initialization? The efficiency claims also need nuance: yes, the wall-clock numbers are good, but some baselines seem not especially optimized, and that comparison can be slippery in my experience.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper with a genuinely relevant problem setup and a decent algorithmic contribution. But the writing needs tightening, and the learning-based extension feels less mature than the MM core. I lean positive, though not enthusiastically so. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to clarify the derivations and better position UM3C against prior unsupervised matching work.

I went into this paper somewhat sympathetic to the goal. I’ve reviewed a lot of fairness/causality papers over the years, and counterfactual fairness is one of those ideas that is elegant on paper but often collapses once the causal assumptions get even slightly shaky. This submission is trying to rescue some useful signal from sensitive attributes via auxiliary variables, which is interesting. I do think there is a real idea here. But I also found the paper frustratingly underspecified in places, and after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice I’m still not fully convinced the method is either well-identified or meaningfully different from a fairly standard latent-variable workaround.

The core proposal around the auxiliary node and control node (Fig. 2, and I think Eq. (4)–(7)) is the most important part, but the exposition is not good enough. On p. 4 the paper says the auxiliary variable “uncovers intrinsic properties that give rise to sensitive attributes,” which is a very strong causal claim. Where does this variable come from in practice? Is it observed, inferred, or learned purely as a latent? The text seems to slide between these interpretations. If it is learned from data, then I’m not entirely sure why this is not just repackaging information from descendants/associates of the sensitive feature and calling it exogenous. That matters a lot for fairness claims.

I also struggled with the counterfactual semantics. The paper invokes exogenous variables, but I did not see a careful discussion of identifiability or robustness under model misspecification. In my experience, this is exactly where many fairness-by-causal-model papers overclaim. Table 1 and Table 2 show improved fairness/accuracy tradeoffs, fine, but these are only as persuasive as the SCM assumptions underneath. The synthetic experiments are okay, though somewhat favorable to the method by construction. The real-data results are less convincing because the causal graph seems asserted rather than justified. Maybe I missed it, but the graph specification on the real datasets is barely defended.

A smaller but still important issue: the paper repeatedly says “without sacrificing accuracy.” That is too strong given the numbers in Table 3; some settings look flat, some improve, some are within noise. Please tone that down.

Overall, I think there is some novelty, and the topic is important. But the causal story is too loose for me right now, and the empirical section does not fully compensate. Borderline at best from my side, leaning reject unless the authors can clarify the role of the auxiliary variable, the assumptions behind Eq. (6), and why the fairness claim survives latent-variable ambiguity.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantial than a lot of the recent theory submissions on deep learning generalization. The main technical idea, the “Bayes-Stability” framework, is not just a cosmetic repackaging of PAC-Bayes plus stability; it does seem to give a cleaner route to data-dependent bounds for noisy gradient methods. I especially appreciated the way the SGLD result is stated in terms of the empirical squared gradient norms (the bound around the main theorem in the middle sections; I think this is Theorem 9 or nearby). That is the right quantity if one wants to explain why fitting random labels can look worse from a generalization perspective. The experiments later, especially the comparison on true vs random labels, are modest but at least aligned with the theory rather than being random CIFAR plots for decoration.

That said, I had to read parts of the paper twice, and some of the presentation is pretty rough. The assumptions around bounded loss / bounded gradients / Gaussian noise are scattered, and I was never fully sure which theorem needs which exact condition. For example, the transition from the generic Bayes-Stability statement (early in the paper, around page 4–5) to the discrete-time SGLD bounds felt too compressed. There are several places where the proof sketches hide constants or step-size restrictions that matter a lot in practice. Equation numbers in the SGLD section (the KL expansion and the chain rule step) are doing most of the work, but the intuition is under-explained.

I also think the comparison to Mou et al. and Pensia et al. is a bit self-congratulatory. Yes, this seems tighter in the regime where the gradient norms are small on average, but that is exactly the favorable regime. I’m not entirely sure the paper is as broadly stronger as the introduction suggests. Table/figure-level empirical evidence is also pretty limited; Figure 1 (if I’m remembering correctly) is suggestive, not conclusive.

The continuous Langevin part with the new log-Sobolev inequality is probably the most mathematically novel piece, although weirdly it felt disconnected from the earlier discrete noisy-SGD story. Almost like two papers stitched together. Still, there is real content here.

Overall, I lean positive. Not flawless, and not always well explained, but technically serious and more insightful than average. I would probably accept, assuming other reviewers did not find a major gap that I missed.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly more solid than a lot of quantization submissions I’ve seen lately. The main idea—approximating weights/activations with sums of multiple binary codes and solving the resulting problem by alternating minimization—is not wildly novel at the highest level, but the way it is specialized to RNNs is useful and, I think, practically relevant. In particular, the discussion around the binary search tree implementation for code assignment (around Sec. 3, Eq. (6)-(9) if I followed correctly) is one of the more concrete algorithmic pieces in the paper, and I appreciated that this is tied to actual CPU speedups rather than only compression ratios.

The empirical results are pretty strong. Table 1 and Table 2 (language modeling) are the core of the paper for me, and the 2-bit / 3-bit results are genuinely impressive if the implementation is fair. A 3-bit model matching or even slightly exceeding full precision is plausible, especially with the regularization effect of quantization, though I always get a little suspicious when this happens “for free.” Figure 1/2 also help make the tradeoff visible. The CPU latency numbers are valuable; too many papers hide behind “bit ops” and never show wall-clock runtime.

That said, I do have some concerns. First, I found the exposition harder than it needed to be. After reading Sec. 3 twice, I still wasn’t fully sure what exactly is optimized jointly during training versus what is recomputed post hoc. The treatment of activation quantization in recurrent settings is especially under-explained. Are all gates quantized the same way? Is the cell state ever quantized? This matters a lot for LSTM stability, and I couldn’t find a clean answer. Maybe I missed it. If it’s on page 5 near the implementation details, it needs to be much more explicit.

Second, the comparison to prior work feels a bit too favorable to the authors. “Large margins” is stronger than I’m comfortable with unless training setups, hidden sizes, and runtime environments are carefully matched. Table 3 helps, but not enough. In my experience, quantization papers are notoriously sensitive to engineering details.

I also think the extension to image classification is a bit tacked on. It’s nice, but it doesn’t add much beyond “the method is generic.”

Overall I lean positive. The paper has real substance and practical value, even if the novelty is moderate and some explanations are muddy. I’d probably accept, but I do want the final version to clarify the optimization/training procedure and make the runtime comparison methodology much more rigorous.

I found the core idea reasonably intuitive, and honestly a bit refreshing: instead of adding yet another proximal/regularization term to control client drift, the paper changes the optimization path by freezing then gradually unfreezing layers bottom-up. After reading the method section twice, I think I understand what the authors are aiming for: preserve a shared classifier geometry while letting lower layers adapt first. That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper nails either the mechanism or the evidence.

The main claim on p.3–4, around Eq. (4)–(6), is that bottom-up unfreezing helps clients “project data into a latent space wherein the separating hyperplanes remain consistent.” This is plausible as an intuition, but it is stated more strongly than the analysis really supports. I might be wrong, but the theorem in the over-parameterized setup (I think this is in Sec. 4, around Theorem 1 on p.5) seems quite far from the practical setting used in the experiments. The assumptions are heavy, and the comparison to FedAvg feels stylized. A better discussion of what exactly carries over would help. Right now it reads a bit like theory for theory’s sake.

Empirically, the results are solid but not decisive. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains, yes, but the margins over strong FL baselines are sometimes modest, and I was surprised there is not more analysis of sensitivity to the unfreezing schedule. This is the whole method. Figure 3 is suggestive, but I really wanted an ablation on layer order (bottom-up vs top-down vs random), freeze duration, and whether the benefit simply comes from reducing the effective local learning capacity early on. In other words, is this really about “alignment,” or just a form of training regularization? The paper hints at the former and mostly demonstrates the latter.

I also found some presentation choices confusing. The algorithm block on p.4 is terse to the point of being annoying; I had to cross-reference lines in the surrounding text to understand when the server checkpoint becomes the new reference. There are also a few places where the writing overreaches. “Wide applicability” in the abstract is too strong for what is tested.

Overall, I think there is a decent idea here, and in my experience practical FL papers live or die on whether the simple trick is robust. This one might be. But the current version does not quite convince me that FedBug is more than a clever heuristic with post-hoc intuition. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the experimental analysis is stronger in the rebuttal.

I had mixed feelings reading this. The core question is actually a good one, and in my experience a lot of manifold-defense papers quietly assume the generator is “good enough” without really asking what kind of geometric/topological faithfulness is needed. So I appreciated the intent here. The paper is trying to say something more fundamental than yet another defense benchmark, and that is refreshing.

That said, I found the presentation pretty uneven. The theoretical claim around topology-awareness seems to be the main contribution, but after reading the argument twice I’m still not entirely sure what exact notion of “topology-aware” the authors want me to take away. On p. 3–4, where the manifold assumption and the generative model are formalized, the definitions feel a bit slippery. Is the requirement homeomorphism to the data manifold? Homology preservation? Just connectedness / number of holes? Equation (3) seems central, but the surrounding discussion does not pin down the operational meaning sharply enough. I might be missing something, but for a paper making a fairly strong necessity claim (“need”), this ambiguity is a problem.

The empirical section is suggestive but not fully convincing. Figure 2 is nice intuitively, and I liked the toy examples because they make the issue visible. But toy topology examples are easy to overread. What matters is whether this changes outcomes in realistic high-dimensional defenses. Table 1 shows some degradation when the generator is not topology-aware, but I wanted stronger baselines and more ablations. In particular, how much of the gain is topology per se versus just better reconstruction quality or smoother latent interpolation? These are not the same thing. The paper sometimes conflates them. Also, on p. 6 the attack setup is under-described; I had to infer details that should be explicit. For adversarial-defense papers, that always makes me nervous.

I also think the paper oversells the novelty a bit. There is prior literature, both in generative modeling and robust ML, that already hints that support mismatch and manifold tearing matter. The authors do cite some of it, but the positioning felt thinner than it should be.

Still, I kind of like the paper. The intuition is plausible, and honestly I agree with the high-level message. But the current version is not tight enough for me. I would lean weak reject: interesting thesis, decent initial evidence, but the definitions and experimental support need to be much sharper before I’d be comfortable accepting such a broad claim.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of “use reviews to improve recommendation” papers over the years, and the basic motivation here is reasonable: full reviews are noisy, so try to distill them into more salient user/item characteristics first. In principle, that makes sense. I also appreciate that the paper is at least trying to connect prompt-based NLP with recommendation rather than just throwing a larger encoder at the problem.

That said, I found the paper weaker than I hoped. The main issue is that the actual methodological novelty feels a bit thin once you get past the framing. On p.3–4, where the paper introduces Prompt2Rec, the core idea seems to be: prompt an LLM or PLM to extract key factors from reviews, then feed those factors into a recommendation model. I might be oversimplifying, but after reading Eq. (3) and the surrounding text twice, I still wasn’t fully clear what is learned end-to-end and what is frozen/preprocessed. This matters a lot. If the “re-characterization” is just an offline text transformation, then the contribution is more of a pipeline than a new model.

I also think the experimental section is not convincing enough for a top conference. Table 2 shows gains over review-based baselines, but the margins are fairly modest, and I didn’t see enough effort to disentangle where the gains come from. Is it the prompt design? The compression of reviews into factors? The particular backbone recommender? An ablation on the number of extracted key factors would have helped a lot. Likewise, Fig. 3 (attention visualization) is nice-looking, but to be blunt, attention heatmaps are not reliable evidence of explanation quality. The paper claims interpretability more strongly than the results justify.

Another concern is fairness of comparison. On p.6, the baseline setup is a bit underdescribed. Some of the older review-based methods may simply be disadvantaged if they are not tuned with similar care. I’m not entirely sure about this because details are sparse, but that was my impression. Also, if an external prompted language model is used, then computational cost and reproducibility should be discussed much more directly. Right now that part feels hand-wavy.

I do think the extracted factors in the qualitative examples (Table 1) are often intuitive, and there is a seed of a good idea here. But for me, the paper is not yet at the level where I can trust the empirical claim or clearly isolate the novelty. My recommendation is negative for now, though I can imagine a stronger revision with better ablations, cleaner method exposition, and a more careful interpretability claim.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more compelling than I expected from the title. The core idea — sharing intermediate robot evolution paths across multiple target morphologies instead of running separate one-to-one transfers — is intuitively sensible, and in Fig. 1 / the motivating setup the benefit is easy to grasp. In my experience, a lot of morphology-transfer papers oversell “generalization” when they are really just doing expensive adaptation repeatedly, so I appreciate that this paper at least frames the problem in a practical systems way: reduce total simulation cost for one-to-many transfer.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper is underspecified in a few places that matter. The heuristic for constructing the robot evolution tree seems to be the main technical contribution, but the presentation around it (I think Sec. 4, especially around Eq. (6) and Algorithm 1) is not very crisp. I’m not entirely sure what guarantees, if any, the heuristic is supposed to have, and the cost model used for tree construction feels partly hand-designed. Maybe that is fine, but then the paper should be more upfront that this is a pragmatic engineering heuristic rather than a principled optimization framework. Right now it sits awkwardly between the two.

I also wanted more detail on how robust the gains are. Table 1 and Table 2 report up to 3.2x and 2.4x savings, which sounds good, but “up to” is doing some work here. On some tasks the margin seems much smaller, and I could not tell whether the tree search overhead is included in the final efficiency numbers. If not, that’s a bit slippery. Also, the experiments are still fairly narrow: one manipulation family and one locomotion family. For a paper making a general claim about continuous robot evolution for one-to-many transfer, this feels limited.

Another issue is that the method seems to rely pretty heavily on the assumption that useful intermediate morphologies exist and can be traversed smoothly. I might be wrong, but in more discontinuous design spaces this could break badly. The discussion around failure cases is thin. Fig. 5 is nice qualitatively, though.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and the paper is better than many incremental transfer papers I’ve seen recently. But I’m not fully convinced by the technical depth or by the breadth of validation. I lean weak accept if the bar is novelty/practicality, weak reject if the bar is methodological rigor.

I quite liked this paper, although I also found parts of it frustratingly under-explained. The main idea—maximizing MI between a graph representation and representations of substructures at multiple scales—is clean and, at least to me, more principled than some of the earlier graph2vec-style heuristics. The setup around Eq. (5)–(7) is fairly standard if you’ve read DGI / Deep InfoMax, but adapting it to graph-level representation learning is nontrivial and, I think, useful. Figure 1 gets the intuition across well.

What I appreciated most is that the paper is not just doing node-graph MI, but tries to incorporate edges and triangles too. That said, after reading Section 3 twice, I’m still not entirely sure the triangle-level component is doing much in practice, or whether it is mostly there for conceptual appeal. The ablation evidence is weak. Table 4 helps a bit, but I wanted a more direct breakdown: node-only vs node+edge vs node+edge+triangle, ideally across several datasets, not just one or two. Right now the “multi-scale” claim feels stronger than the empirical support.

The experimental results in Tables 1 and 2 are good, and in some datasets surprisingly strong. I do think the comparison is mostly fair, though I was a little uneasy about whether all baselines had equivalent tuning budgets. In graph classification, small differences can move around a lot depending on random splits; I did not see enough discussion of variance beyond reporting std. dev. Also, for a method built so centrally around MI maximization, the paper is a bit hand-wavy about the estimator bias and why the Jensen-Shannon style objective is preferred here. Maybe this is obvious to people in this literature, but still.

InfoGraph* (Section 4) is interesting but honestly felt less convincing to me than the unsupervised part. The motivation makes sense, yet the method seems somewhat bolted on. I might be wrong, but the interaction between the unsupervised encoder and the supervised encoder is not analyzed deeply enough. Why this particular regularization? Why should matching these latent spaces preserve task-relevant information rather than wash it out? The results are decent, but I didn’t come away with a strong conceptual picture.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper and likely above the bar. Not flawless, and a bit oversold in places, but the core idea is simple, sensible, and probably influential. My leaning is accept, though not enthusiastically.

I read this paper with some interest because federated graph learning is genuinely under-explored compared to vanilla FL, and the “structure non-IID” angle is, at least at a high level, a real issue. In many practical graph settings the clients do not just differ in label proportions or feature marginals; they differ in topology, and that matters a lot more than people sometimes admit. So I appreciate the attempt to articulate this more clearly. The setup around page 2–3, where the paper argues that community-split benchmarks are too convenient, is directionally correct.

That said, I found the paper much less convincing once I got into the technical details. The central concept of “structure non-IID” still feels under-defined. The discussion near Eq. (3) and the surrounding text seems to equate structural heterogeneity with a kind of covariate shift, but I’m not entirely sure this is the right framing. Graph structure affects both message passing dynamics and label smoothness assumptions; it is not just another input marginal shift. I wanted a cleaner formal definition, maybe something measurable across clients, rather than mostly intuition and examples. As written, it feels a bit slogan-y.

I also struggled with what is actually new in AdaFGL. If I understood correctly, the method combines federated global knowledge with adaptive propagation and a non-parametric label propagation module. That’s not unreasonable, but after reading Section 4 twice, I still don’t have a sharp picture of which component is carrying the gains. Table 3 suggests the full model helps, but the ablations are not deep enough. In particular, Figure 2 is visually nice but does not really isolate the effect of the adaptive propagation rule versus simply having a stronger post-processing / smoothing step. I might be wrong, but the method currently looks like a fairly composite pipeline rather than a clean new paradigm, which is how the title sells it.

There are also writing issues. The paper is hard to parse in places, and some claims are overstated. “New paradigm” is too much. A number of sentences in the abstract and introduction are grammatically rough, and this unfortunately made the technical content harder to trust. I also wanted more detail on the benchmark construction: the structure non-IID splits are important here, yet the description around page 5 is brief, and I worry the evaluation protocol may itself favor propagation-based methods.

Overall, I think the problem is important and the paper has some promising empirical results (Table 2 is solid), but I’m not yet convinced by the formulation or the methodological novelty. Borderline to weak reject for me in its current form.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practical than a lot of BN papers that try to sell a tiny systems trick as a fundamental learning contribution. The core idea—estimate BN statistics from a small, less-correlated subset rather than the full batch—is simple, but not obviously trivial, and the paper does a decent job motivating why “few but uncorrelated” could be better than “many but redundant.” I especially appreciated the discussion around data correlation on p.3–4 and the contrast between effectiveness and regular execution patterns. Figure 2 helped.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper is somewhat over-claiming on the “comprehensive” side. The three proposed variants (BS, FS, VDN) are related enough that the paper feels more like a design space exploration than a sharply defined algorithmic contribution. That is not necessarily bad, but then I would expect cleaner ablations isolating what really matters. For example, Table 2 and Table 3 report end results, but I wanted more direct evidence that reduced correlation is the causal factor rather than just reduced sample count plus implementation convenience. The argument around Eq. (5)–(7) is plausible, but I’m not entirely sure it is established empirically.

I also had some confusion about VDN. On p.5, the synthetic samples are described almost as if they are generic random surrogates for the batch distribution, but that seems like a very strong assumption. Why should a handful of random synthetic samples provide stable estimates across architectures? In my experience, BN behavior is notoriously architecture- and optimizer-sensitive. The fact that this works at all is surprising, and I wanted a bit more failure analysis. Are there settings where VDN simply breaks? The paper is too cheerful here.

The efficiency claims are good, though. A real 10–20% training speedup without custom kernels is meaningful. Figure 6 and Table 5 are probably the strongest part of the paper for me. I also liked that the authors evaluated micro-batch settings; that makes the work more relevant than many “faster BN” papers I’ve seen.

My main reservation is novelty. Sampling for normalization statistics is not a huge conceptual leap, and parts of this felt incremental. But it is useful incremental work, and reasonably well executed. I’d lean positive if the venue values practical training acceleration, but I would not call this a major conceptual advance.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than most “generative prior for inverse problems” submissions. The core idea—using a diffusion model over 3D point clouds as a Bayesian prior and then sampling a posterior conditioned on incomplete cryo-EM measurements—is timely and potentially important. I’ve seen a lot of papers lately that just bolt a score model onto an inverse problem and call it Bayesian; this one at least tries to take the posterior story seriously.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what is actually being inferred and what assumptions are doing the heavy lifting. The presentation around the posterior sampling procedure (around Eq. 3–5, if I’m reading the numbering right) is too compressed. I think the method alternates between data-consistency updates and diffusion-based denoising/score steps, but the exact target distribution is murky. Is this an approximation to Langevin on the true posterior, or a heuristic sampler with a Bayesian interpretation? Those are not the same thing. The paper gestures at “posterior sampling” repeatedly, but I did not see a convincing argument that the stationary distribution matches the intended posterior, especially once the measurement model is nontrivial.

The experimental section is promising but also a bit selective. Figure 2 looks good, and the sparse-view reconstructions are visually impressive. But Table 1 is not enough for me. I really wanted stronger baselines: not just generic smoothness/sparsity priors, but modern learned 3D priors or even ablations with simpler score-based conditioning. Also, the biomolecular examples are compelling in spirit, but I’m not entirely sure the benchmark setup reflects the ugly realities of cryo-EM. On page 6, the simulated partial observations seem fairly clean, and I couldn’t tell how sensitive the method is to mismatch between the training distribution and actual molecular heterogeneity. In my experience, that’s exactly where these methods break.

I also worry about representation choice. Point clouds are convenient, yes, but for biomolecular assemblies they throw away important structure. The paper acknowledges this briefly (page 3), but then proceeds as if medium-resolution point clouds are enough. Maybe for coarse reconstruction, sure. But the claims should be toned down.

Overall, I think there is a real contribution here, and I like the direction. But the Bayesian framing currently feels stronger than the evidence, and the empirical validation is not yet as convincing as the paper seems to think. I lean weak accept if the venue values forward-looking ideas, but I would not call this fully mature.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “linear attention” submissions, which tend to just reshuffle kernel tricks and call it a day. The latent variable perspective here is at least a real conceptual contribution. I think the derivation around the probabilistic formulation (around Sec. 3, especially Eqs. (6)–(10)) is the strongest part of the paper. It gives a cleaner story for why the approximation should work in both bidirectional and causal settings, and I appreciated that this is not presented as just another heuristic factorization.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the empirical case matches the elegance of the theory. The central claim is “comparable to standard attention and other state-of-the-art models” while retaining linear complexity, but the evidence is a bit selective. In Table 1 and Table 2 the results are solid, yes, but mostly “not much worse” rather than clearly compelling. I might be missing something, but for a method that is supposed to be a drop-in replacement, I wanted more ablations on where the gap appears and when it doesn’t. Figure 3 is suggestive, though I found the presentation a little too neat; I wanted variance bars or at least some sense of run-to-run stability. In my experience, these efficient-attention methods can look deceptively stable in one seed.

The extension mechanism for pretrained models is probably the most practically relevant part of the paper. The claim that one can extend context length with only a few extra training steps is important if true. But the evidence for that, around the long-context experiments on later pages, felt underdeveloped. How sensitive is this to the original model scale, tokenizer, or training mix? The paper gives the happy path, not really the failure modes.

I also thought some notation could be improved. Eq. (12) and the surrounding text on the causal case were harder to parse than they needed to be. Maybe this is just me reading too quickly, but I had to go back and forth between the definition of the latent variables and the matrix form. Also, the “natural extension” combining local standard attention with global linear attention is intuitively sensible, but it ends up feeling a bit bolted on. I’m not entirely sure what is principled there versus engineered.

Overall, I lean positive because there is actual novelty here, not just benchmarking theater. But I’m not enthusiastic. Good paper, probably worthy of acceptance, though I think the claims should be toned down a bit and the experimental section needs more honesty and depth.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea — train a separate “mentor” to predict when a “mentee” will fail, across ID/OOD/adversarial settings — is interesting and practically relevant, and I do think the paper is aiming at a useful problem. In industry-facing ML, this kind of error forecasting matters a lot more than yet another +0.3% accuracy paper. That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is as conceptually new as it wants to be, and several experimental choices felt under-explained.

The main empirical claim, as I understand it from the abstract and the middle experimental section (around pp. 5–7), is that training on adversarial mistakes transfers surprisingly well to predicting ordinary and OOD errors, and that transformer mentors work best across heterogeneous mentees. That is a neat observation if robust. But I found myself repeatedly asking: compared to what exactly? The baselines seemed too weak or too narrow. If Table 2 is the main result table, then I really wanted to see stronger uncertainty/confidence baselines, calibration-based detectors, selective prediction methods, and perhaps feature-space density methods. A mentor model that ingests rich signals from the mentee may of course do well, but then the question is whether it is learning something genuinely new or just reconstructing confidence in a more expensive way.

I was also confused by the “oracle” framing. “SuperMentor” is catchy, sure, but 78% accuracy for error prediction can mean wildly different things depending on class balance. If errors are rare, plain accuracy is close to useless. I might have missed it, but I wanted AUROC, AUPRC, FPR at fixed TPR, expected calibration error of the error predictor, etc. Figure 3 (if I’m reading it correctly) hints at robustness across settings, but the paper doesn’t quite nail the evaluation protocol tightly enough for me to trust the headline number.

Another issue is mechanism. The paper says something like “small adversarial perturbations reveal blind spots” and therefore are useful supervision for the mentor. Maybe! But this is still mostly an empirical story. I’m not entirely sure whether the transfer comes from adversarial examples specifically, or just from exposing the mentor to hard boundary cases. A simpler hard-example mining control would help.

I do like the breadth of mentee architectures, and in my experience cross-architecture error prediction is genuinely hard, so those results are probably the paper’s strongest point. Still, the paper oversells “understanding why errors occur.” It does not really explain why; it predicts. That’s fine, but let’s call it what it is.

Overall: promising direction, decent empirical effort, but right now it feels more like a solid workshop paper than a top-conference accept unless the evaluation and baselines are substantially tightened.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than the usual “just concatenate more text and hope for the best” line of work. The central claim — that long-distance referrals are the key attribute of useful long-context training data — is plausible and, in my view, basically the right thing to be studying. Figure 2 and the discussion around p.3–4 made this intuition concrete. I also liked that the paper tries to separate “having long sequences” from “having meaningful dependencies inside long sequences,” which many recent papers blur.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the evidence is as strong as the framing suggests. A lot hinges on how “referral” is defined and measured. Equation (3) looked a bit ad hoc to me, and after reading that section twice I still wasn’t sure whether the metric is robust beyond hyperlink-rich web data. The paper says this is a core attribute of high-quality long-context data, but most of the pipeline seems very web-specific. Hyperlinks are indeed a native signal, sure, but then the claim starts to feel narrower than advertised. I might be wrong, but I think the paper should either tone down the universality claim or provide more evidence on non-web corpora.

I also had some concerns about evaluation. The “near-natural quality” claim and the 32.7% improvement in the abstract sound impressive, but Table 3/4 (assuming I read them correctly) mix intrinsic and downstream evidence in a way that makes it hard to judge practical impact. Are the gains showing up consistently on standard long-context benchmarks, or mainly on the authors’ proxy metrics? Figure 5 was suggestive, but I wanted more ablations: what happens if you pack by topical similarity without hyperlinks, or by random walk on the web graph, or simply by URL/domain structure? Without these, it’s hard to isolate whether “referrals” are really the mechanism.

The scalability angle is good, and using only 0.5% root documents is a strong result if it holds operationally. But the paper is a bit too eager in places. Some of the writing on p.6–7 reads like the conclusion was known before the experiment. Also, there are a few presentation issues: I noticed “LongPackis” in the abstract, and some line-level definitions were too compressed.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a genuinely useful idea, but not yet a slam dunk. I lean positive because the problem matters and the proposed signal is more principled than prior packing methods. Still, I’d want clearer causal evidence and a less overclaimed story.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thoughtful than a lot of “risk-sensitive RL” submissions that just bolt a CVaR objective onto PPO and call it a day. The core idea—optimize a functional of the episode return CDF directly, with a sampled unbiased policy-gradient estimator—is appealing. I especially liked the framing around weighting outcomes by relative rank/quality rather than by raw reward shaping. In principle that is clean.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails the methodological and empirical story. The derivation around the unbiased estimator (I think around Eq. 6–10) is plausible, but the assumptions are not presented crisply enough. I might be wrong, but it seems the unbiasedness relies on fairly delicate exchangeability / sampling arguments over full-episode returns, and I wanted a clearer statement of exactly what is being estimated and under what regularity conditions. Right now it reads a little too smoothly. Also, the variance reduction section felt under-motivated. The paper says these measures are important for practical on-policy learning, which I believe, but I wanted ablations isolating each trick. Table 2 gives some evidence overall, but not enough to understand what actually matters.

The experiments are decent but also narrow. Six Safety Gym tasks is not trivial, and I appreciate that the focus is on penalty-based environments where risk sensitivity should matter. Still, all results live in one benchmark family. That makes me nervous. Figure 3 was suggestive—the “moderate emphasis on poor outcomes” effect is actually the most interesting result in the paper—but I’m not sure the authors really explain it beyond post hoc intuition. In my experience, these effects often come from optimization stabilization rather than genuine risk-sensitive behavior, and I didn’t see enough evidence disentangling those. Some comparison to standard constrained/safe RL baselines would also be necessary; beating PPO is a low bar here because PPO is not designed for this setting.

I also found parts of the writing surprisingly hard to parse. The conceptual distinction between “distributional context” and standard utility/risk objectives is a bit blurry in the intro, and a few figure captions (Figure 2 especially) are too terse.

Overall, I think there is a real contribution here, particularly the general CDF-objective gradient estimator. But the paper is not quite as airtight as it needs to be for a top venue. Borderline accept / weak accept if the bar is novelty-forward; otherwise weak reject.

I found the core idea of this paper interesting, and honestly more substantial than a lot of “robust GAN” submissions I’ve seen. The main modification — adding an unsupervised autoencoding pathway to the generator so that the supervised branch is constrained to live on the target manifold — is pretty intuitive, and Figure 1 makes the architecture reasonably clear. I also appreciated that the paper is not just another benchmark chase; there is an actual modeling claim here.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper overstates both the novelty and the theory. The abstract says the authors “prove that RoCGAN share similar theoretical properties as GAN,” but the development around the propositions/theorems (Section 4, around Eq. (8) onward) felt fairly thin to me. I might be wrong, but this looks closer to a consistency/saddle-point inheritance argument for the modified objective than a deep robustness result. In particular, the “robustness” that matters empirically is with respect to noisy conditioning inputs, and I did not see a convincing theorem that really addresses that. The gap between the theoretical story and the empirical claim is pretty large.

Empirically, the results are good. The denoising and sparse inpainting examples look visibly better than the baselines, especially in the more severe corruption settings (I’m looking at the tables in the experimental section and the qualitative grids for faces/natural images). But I was missing some stronger ablations. How much of the gain comes from simply adding extra reconstruction losses / extra capacity? The comparison to a cGAN with a matched parameter count or with a plain target autoencoder regularizer would be important. Right now Table results are favorable, yes, but not fully diagnostic. This is one of those cases where a simple baseline could weaken the central claim quite a bit.

I was also a bit bothered by the writing. Some notation choices around the two pathways are introduced quickly and then reused in a way that made pages 3–4 harder to parse than necessary. Maybe this is just me reading in a hurry, but I had to backtrack several times.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical value, but the paper sells it as more theoretically grounded and more novel than I’m convinced it is. Still, in my experience, architectural ideas like this often matter. I lean positive, though not enthusiastically.

I found the core idea interesting and, honestly, pretty sensible: use language to specify what matters in state, then let an LM help instantiate an abstraction before doing imitation learning. This is a nice framing, and it targets a real bottleneck. In robotics especially, people absolutely do hand-engineer these abstractions, just quietly and expensively. So the problem is worth tackling.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical contribution. The method description around the LM-to-abstraction translation feels under-specified. Eq. (2) on p.4 gives the abstraction function in a fairly clean symbolic way, but the actual mechanism by which the LM maps a task description to the mask is a bit hand-wavy. Is this prompting plus heuristic parsing? constrained decoding? program synthesis? I think Fig. 2 is trying to clarify the pipeline, but it still leaves too much hidden in the box labeled LGA. In my experience, papers in this area often bury the real source of performance in those “translation” details.

The experimental section is mixed for me. Table 1 and Table 2 look good at first glance, especially on the spurious-correlation settings, and I do believe the claim that abstraction helps generalization. That part tracks with prior work. But I’m not sure the paper isolates the effect of language guidance versus just giving the learner a better inductive bias by feature selection. The comparison to manually designed abstractions is useful, but the paper needs a stronger baseline where the same masking budget is learned from demos without language, or perhaps from a generic LM prior not conditioned on the task description. Otherwise, I can’t tell whether language is actually doing the heavy lifting.

I also found the “ambiguous specifications” story a bit slippery. The examples around p.6–7 are intuitive, but I wanted a more systematic analysis of failure modes. When the language is wrong, incomplete, or subtly misleading, what happens? The abstract says “possibly incomplete” descriptions, but the robustness there is not really nailed down. Fig. 4 is suggestive, not definitive.

The Spot robot demonstration is nice and helps the paper feel less toy-ish, though I’ll admit it reads a bit like a showcase rather than a rigorous evaluation.

Overall, I lean positive on the idea but weaker on the evidence. This feels like a promising workshop paper or an early conference paper, but for a top venue I wanted more methodological transparency and tighter baselines. I might be wrong, but right now the paper seems more compelling conceptually than empirically.

I went into this paper fairly sympathetic to the goal. The core motivation — CNNs latching onto context rather than object evidence — is real, and in medical imaging / small-data settings this is not just a cute robustness issue. I’ve seen too many papers wave at “causality” here though, and this one does that a bit too casually.

The main idea, as I understand it after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, is to use sparse human-provided activation masks to bias intermediate feature learning toward regions/features a human considers relevant. That is reasonable. In fact, I think there is a potentially useful practical contribution here. But the paper oversells it as “causal” in a way that is not justified by either the method or the experiments. Human annotations of salient regions are not the same thing as causal variables. At best this is a human-guided anti-shortcut regularizer. That may sound harsh, but I think terminology matters.

I also found the method description somewhat under-specified. Equation (3) introduces the additional loss on masked activations, but it is not very clear to me how the mask is aligned with feature maps of different resolutions, or whether this is applied to one layer or multiple layers. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough. I might be missing something in the implementation details on page 6, but right now I don’t think I could reproduce this cleanly. Relatedly, the “minimal” in the title is never really quantified in a convincing way. Table 2 varies the amount of guidance, which is good, but I wanted a clearer annotation budget analysis: how many minutes per image, how coarse can the masks be, how sensitive is performance?

The empirical section is mixed. The gains in Table 1 are promising, especially in the lower-data regime, and the qualitative visualizations in Figure 4 do suggest reduced background reliance. Still, the baselines feel a bit dated and incomplete. If the claim is about avoiding spurious context, I would expect comparisons to more recent debiasing / attention supervision methods, not just vanilla CNN training and a small set of attribution-based variants. Also, some of the improvements are modest, and I’m not entirely sure the statistical significance is established.

Overall, I think there is a decent idea here, but the paper in its current form is not fully convincing. The practical method may have value. The causal framing, however, is too strong, and the experimental validation needs to be tighter. I’m leaning borderline reject unless the authors can substantially clarify the method and tone down the claims.

I went into this paper pretty interested, because sequential experimentation with delayed / time-varying effects is a real problem in marketplaces, and most industry A/B testing pipelines are frankly pretty primitive about it. The paper has an appealing goal. But after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the proposed “reinforcement learning framework” is really an RL contribution, or even that the methodology is presented clearly enough to assess.

My biggest issue is conceptual. On p. 2–3 the paper motivates the setting as long-term causal effect evaluation with sequential monitoring, which makes sense. But when I got to the actual procedure (around Eq. (3)–(6), if I’m tracking correctly), the RL angle felt mostly cosmetic. There is some adaptive updating, yes, but I did not see a genuinely sequential decision-making formulation with a meaningful state/action/reward structure beyond what could have been described as adaptive experimental design or sequential testing. I might be missing something, but if this is meant for an ML audience, the paper needs to be much more honest and precise about what part is “RL” versus standard online estimation/inference.

I also found the assumptions slippery. The marketplace setting clearly has interference and equilibrium effects, yet the theory seems to rely on fairly strong independence/stationarity conditions (p. 5, around the theorem statements). Those assumptions may make the results go through, but they also seem to strip away the exact complications that make two-sided platforms hard. In my experience, this is where many papers overclaim. The title suggests time dependent causal effects in A/B testing generally, but the actual theory appears to cover a much narrower regime.

The empirical section didn’t rescue it for me. The synthetic experiments in Table 1 and Figure 2 show some gains, but the baselines are weak and not especially current. I kept wondering: where are the obvious comparisons to standard group sequential methods, CUPED-style variance reduction, or adaptive allocation methods used in practice? The real-data case study is interesting, but frustratingly under-described. I couldn’t tell from the discussion around Figure 3 what exactly was randomized, over what horizon, and whether the observed “long-term effect” could be separated from temporal confounding. The paper says the method is “generally applicable,” but the evidence is much more anecdotal.

There is probably a kernel of a useful paper here. I do like the problem and parts of the theoretical ambition. But right now it feels oversold, under-specified, and somewhat mismatched to the RL framing. My recommendation is negative in its current form.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit refreshing. The basic idea—avoid directly forcing image and point cloud features into one metric space, and instead “translate” both into a more comparable representation using pretrained models—is sensible and, in my view, closer to how these foundation-model pipelines are actually starting to work in practice. After reading the method section twice, I think the core contribution is really the combination of diffusion features for coarse semantic alignment and depth-based geometric features for refinement. That part came across clearly, especially around Fig. 2 and the discussion near Eq. (3)–(6).

What I liked most is that the paper does not oversell training tricks. The “training-free” angle is meaningful here, not just marketing. The results in Table 1 and Table 2 are strong, especially the registration recall gains. A 48.6% average improvement is not subtle. In my experience reviewing registration papers, many methods improve one metric while quietly getting worse on others, but here the gains seem fairly consistent across indoor and outdoor settings, which gives me more confidence than usual. I also appreciated that the paper tests across different benchmarks rather than tuning the whole story to one dataset.

That said, I do have some reservations. First, I’m not entirely sure the paper fully explains why the intermediate diffusion features should be semantically aligned between RGB images and projected depth in a way that is stable enough for correspondence. The empirical evidence is decent (the visualizations in Fig. 3 help), but the argument still feels a little hand-wavy. Maybe that’s fine, but then I would have liked a sharper ablation on which diffusion layer(s) matter most, and how sensitive the method is to the choice of pretrained model. I may have missed it, but the discussion around this on page 6 felt thinner than it should be.

Second, the dependence on monocular depth estimation is a bit of a potential Achilles’ heel. On paper this is elegant; in messy real scenes, monocular depth can be very wrong. The robustness analysis to depth failure modes seemed limited. Also, runtime/compute is not discussed enough. Using a diffusion model plus depth estimator is not cheap, and for a “free” method the practical cost matters.

Overall, I think this is a strong paper. Not perfect, and some of the intuition is still more empirical than principled, but the idea is novel enough and the gains are convincing enough that I lean positive. If accepted, I suspect people will try this baseline immediately, which is often a good sign.

I found the paper interesting in premise, because “data pruning without fully annotating everything first” is actually a pretty practical problem, and object detection makes it harder than standard classification pruning.
