I might be missing some subtle distinction, but the paper needs a sharper explanation of what is genuinely new beyond packaging. Right now the conceptual contribution feels a bit inflated.

I also struggled with clarity in the method section. Figure 2 helps, but the interaction between the communication action, message aggregation, and task action policy is not written as cleanly as it should be. In particular, on p. 5 around the description following Eq. (7), I was unsure whether communication decisions are trained purely through task reward or whether there is any auxiliary signal / regularization beyond the budget term. If it is only task reward, then the credit assignment problem seems nasty, and the paper doesn’t really discuss this difficulty. Also, the wording around “far apart” communication in the experiments is hand-wavy. Are agents actually routing information over multiple hops, or just selecting nonlocal recipients under a simulator-defined topology? Those are not the same thing.

The experiments are decent but not fully persuasive. Table 1 shows gains, yes, but I wanted stronger ablations. For example: what happens if you keep adaptive “when to communicate” but remove adaptive “who to communicate with”? Or vice versa? That would tell us where the benefit actually comes from. Figure 4 is suggestive, but mostly qualitative. In my experience, these MARL communication papers live or die on ablations, and this one is a bit thin there.

Overall, I think there is a solid idea here and some promising empirical results, but the paper oversells novelty and underspecifies key implementation/training details. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can better situate the contribution and tighten the experimental evidence.

I have somewhat mixed feelings about this paper. The core observation is interesting and, honestly, a bit uncomfortable for the recent group-robustness literature: a lot of the reported gains can apparently be recovered by a post-hoc class-specific scaling of logits, without retraining. If that claim really holds as broadly as suggested, it is worth airing. I appreciated the simplicity of the idea, and Table 1 / Table 2 make the headline point fairly clearly. In particular, the fact that ERM + scaling can match or beat several specialized methods is the kind of result this community should pay attention to.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the framing and some of the evaluation choices. The “class-specific scaling is all you need” title feels overstated. It may be enough for the benchmarks used here, under the specific average-vs-robust tradeoff the paper emphasizes, but “all you need” is a big claim. In my experience, methods that look dominant under one scalarized tradeoff often fall apart once the deployment constraint changes slightly.

The unified metric introduced around p.5 (I think Eq. 7?) is where I became skeptical. Compressing average accuracy and worst-group/unbiased accuracy into a single scalar is convenient, sure, but this also bakes in a value judgment. I’m not entirely sure the chosen form is justified beyond convenience. The paper argues it gives a “comprehensive” comparison, but I found that too strong. Figure 3 is nice visually, and I actually think the Pareto frontier plots are more informative than the scalar summary. I would have preferred the paper to lean on those more.

I also wanted more clarity on the tuning protocol for the scaling parameters. This is crucial. If the post-hoc method gets to tune against group-labeled validation data, then the practical story changes a lot. I may have missed it, but the details around pages 4–6 felt slippery. Relatedly, the instance-wise adaptive scaling sounds more substantial than the paper lets on; at that point this is no longer just a simple correction, and the “without additional training” narrative becomes muddled.

Still, I think the empirical message is important. The paper is provocative in a useful way. I just wish it were a bit more careful and less slogan-y. Tentative recommendation: weak accept if the claims are toned down and the model-selection protocol is clarified; otherwise borderline.

I liked this paper more than I expected to. The core idea is actually pretty simple — retrieve a small candidate set, then let a reader jointly align spans to retrieved entities/relations in one pass — but in this space, simple and well-executed matters. After reading the method section twice, I think the main contribution is really the input/output formulation around the Reader, more than some radically new architecture. That’s fine, and in fact probably a virtue.

The paper is generally clear, especially around the overall pipeline in Fig. 1 and the cIE setup later on. The efficiency claims are also compelling. The “single forward pass instead of one per candidate” point is not just a minor engineering trick; it changes the practical usability of these systems. Table 2/Table 3 (assuming I’m reading the right ones; the numbering got a bit blurred in my notes) do suggest a pretty strong speed/accuracy tradeoff, and the “academic budget” framing felt refreshingly concrete rather than marketing fluff.

That said, I do have a few reservations. First, I’m not entirely sure the retrieval stage is analyzed deeply enough. The whole approach depends on high recall there, but the discussion of failure modes is thin. On p. 5–6, when you describe the retriever setup, I wanted a clearer ablation on candidate set size and recall ceilings. Some of that is hinted at in the appendix, I think, but it should be more central. If retrieval misses the gold item, the reader is dead on arrival; this is obvious, but the paper sometimes writes as if the reader is doing all the heavy lifting.

I also found the comparison to prior retriever-reader EL systems a little selective. The speedups are impressive, yes, but apples-to-apples details matter a lot here: hardware, batch size, candidate pool size, whether preprocessing/index lookup time is included, etc. I might have missed it, but around the efficiency section I wanted a much stricter accounting. “Up to 40x” always makes me suspicious.

On novelty: I think this is good work, but not conceptually groundbreaking. It is more of a smart reformulation plus careful engineering. In my experience, these papers can have high impact anyway if the empirical case is strong enough, and here it mostly is. The cIE result is probably the part I found most exciting, though I wanted more analysis of when joint training helps versus hurts.

Overall I lean positive. Not flawless, and a bit under-analyzed in places, but useful, practical, and honestly more convincing than many overclaimed IE papers I’ve read recently.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “personalized federated LLM” submissions that are mostly just packaging. The core idea—splitting experts into globally aggregated generalists and local specialists—is sensible, and the bi-level training of the router is probably the most technically meaningful part of the paper. In particular, the discussion around the validation split for router updates on p.4–5 (around Eq. 6–8, if I tracked it correctly) gets at a real issue in MoE personalization that many papers hand-wave away.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the “first to effectively address both” claim in the abstract. That is a very strong statement. There is a lot of prior work on personalized FL, clustered FL, local adaptation, and modular federated architectures, and while this paper combines these ideas in a neat way, I would be careful about novelty wording. I might be wrong, but I think the paper oversells this point.

I also had some trouble pinning down the exact training protocol. After reading Section 3 twice, I was still unsure how the validation set used for router optimization is constructed in the realistic on-device setting. Is this split always available locally? Is it fixed across rounds? The text near Algorithm 1 and the paragraph below it was a bit too tidy; in practice these design choices matter a lot, especially when local data are scarce, which is precisely the setting the paper motivates.

The experimental section is decent, and Table 1 / Table 2 do suggest gains under higher heterogeneity. Figure 3 was helpful too. But I wanted more ablations. For example, how sensitive is performance to the number of generalist experts versus specialists? The paper repeatedly claims robustness to overfitting due to the generalists’ regularization effect, but I did not see a really clean experiment isolating that mechanism. Also, communication/computation costs are under-discussed. Saying the method “accommodates users’ varying computational resources” is nice, but where is the systems evidence? Even a small table with FLOPs, memory, or per-round cost would help.

I think the paper has merit, especially because the method is modular and the empirical results are mostly solid. Still, some claims are broader than the evidence. My overall feeling is positive but not enthusiastic: a good paper, not quite a slam dunk. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to clarify the local validation protocol, tighten the novelty claims, and add at least one stronger ablation on expert allocation.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than the average “let’s ensemble some LLMs and report gains” submission. The central idea — that there is a nontrivial trade-off between diversity and consistency, and that this trade-off should be managed dynamically rather than fixed a priori — is sensible and mostly well motivated. I especially appreciated the attempt to disentangle semantic-level consistency from model-level diversity, which comes through around the discussion of the consistency score and the refinement procedure (I think this is around Sec. 3, with the main mechanism formalized near Eq. 2 / Eq. 3). In my experience, many papers in this area hand-wave this distinction.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as clean as it wants to be. After reading the method section twice, I still found the gating mechanism a bit underspecified operationally. The consistency score is intuitive, but I wasn’t always sure how robust it is to prompt phrasing, answer verbosity, or cases where semantically equivalent outputs are phrased differently. Figure 2 helps, but only partially. If the semantic comparison itself depends on another LLM judge or embedding model, then a lot hinges on that choice, and I don’t think the paper really confronts this enough. Maybe I missed it, but I wanted a stronger ablation here.

The empirical results are good, and the BBH mixed evaluation result is impressive on paper. Table 1/Table 2 suggest real gains, not just noise. Still, BBH is a somewhat crowded benchmark for inference-time tricks, and I would have liked to see a broader testbed or at least stronger evidence that DMoA is not over-tuned to reasoning-style tasks. The claim in the abstract that different reasoning tasks require distinct capabilities is plausible, but the support for that claim felt more observational than causal. Some of the analysis around “cross-validation bias can enhance performance” (I think discussed later in the experiments section) is intriguing but also slippery; I’m not entirely sure the paper pins down when this is beneficial versus just acting as a lucky heuristic.

I also think the novelty is moderate rather than high. Dynamic routing/gating over LLM outputs is not new, and mixtures of agents plus self-consistency are both established baselines. The contribution here is more in the synthesis and analysis than in a fundamentally new algorithm. That’s not fatal, but the paper occasionally oversells the conceptual leap.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper is careful, timely, and empirically solid, even if parts of the method feel more engineered than principled. I’d probably support acceptance, but not as a clear standout.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a lot of “compact neural field” papers over the last few years, and frankly many of them are just repackaging standard tensor decompositions with a neural rendering wrapper. This one is a bit better than that, but I’m still not fully convinced by the presentation or the empirical case.

The core idea — parameterizing a neural field on a dense regular grid using a tensor-train decomposition and then learning it end-to-end — is reasonable and, I think, technically sound. The sampling procedure described around Eq. (3)–(5) is the part I found most useful. Figure 2 also helps; without it, I think the method section would have been much harder to parse. That said, after reading pages 3–5 twice, I still found the exposition more cumbersome than it needed to be. The paper keeps saying “neural field,” but in the proposed formulation this is really a compressed discrete field with interpolation/sampling machinery, not a coordinate MLP in the usual sense. Maybe that is intentional, but I think the terminology blurs what is actually novel here.

I did like the denoising sandbox experiment. Table 1 gives a clean comparison against SVD-style baselines, and it is one of the few places where the paper actually isolates the effect of the low-rank parameterization. In my experience, these controlled tests matter a lot more than another NeRF benchmark with many moving parts. But then the paper pivots to NeRF, and the evidence becomes less sharp. I might be wrong, but the gains in the rendering setup (Table 2?) did not look substantial enough to justify the fairly strong claims in the abstract about “discovering” the best low-rank structure through learning. There is a lot of tuning hidden in that sentence.

I was also missing stronger baselines. Why no comparison to CP / Tucker variants implemented with the same training budget? And on page 6, around the discussion of rank selection, the paper seems to imply TT is especially well-suited, but I did not see convincing ablations showing this beyond parameter count arguments. Figure 4 is suggestive, not decisive.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it’s not just hype. But the paper oversells the generality a bit, and the empirical section feels narrower than the framing. I’d lean weak accept for a workshop, borderline for a main conference. If accepted, I’d want the authors to tighten the claims and clarify what exactly is “neural” versus simply tensorized grid optimization.

I read this paper with some interest because blind restoration with generative priors is an area I’ve followed pretty closely, and the basic idea here is actually pretty appealing. The “instant reference” mechanism — repeatedly decoding a compact image representation into a generative reference during diffusion — is a nice twist on using frozen priors. In particular, the intuition around adapting the conditioning online rather than fixing it once at the beginning makes sense to me, and qualitatively the results in Fig. 1 and Fig. 5 look strong. Some of the face and low-light examples are honestly impressive.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as clean as it wants to be. The core method description around Eq. (3)–(6) is more opaque than it should be. I found the interaction between the “current diffusion latent,” the instantiated generative prior, and the re-encoded degraded image hard to pin down precisely. On p. 4, the text seems to suggest a kind of alternating decode/re-encode loop, but the exact information flow is not very explicit. Maybe this is obvious to others, but I had to infer a lot from Fig. 2, and even there the diagram is a bit too high-level.

The adaptive sampling idea based on reference variance is interesting, but also where I got skeptical. The claim that variance tracks degradation intensity is plausible, and Fig. 4 seems to support it, but the evidence feels somewhat anecdotal. I would have liked a more systematic analysis across degradations and datasets, maybe even a calibration plot. Right now it reads a little like “we observed this and it worked,” which is not nothing, but not entirely satisfying either.

The experimental section is pretty strong overall. Table 1 and Table 2 show solid gains, and the visual comparisons are better than a lot of recent restoration papers that oversharpen or hallucinate nonsense. Still, I wish there were more discussion of failure cases. For a method explicitly leaning into generative priors, the risk of semantic drift is real. The “creative restoration” angle on the last pages is fun, but it also weakens the restoration framing a bit; I’m not entirely sure where the boundary is between faithful reconstruction and guided image editing.

My feeling is positive but not unreserved. The paper has a real idea, and in a crowded space that matters. But the presentation is looser than it should be, and some of the central empirical claims need more careful backing. I’d lean weak accept if the conference is looking for impactful systems papers, though I can also imagine more theory-minded reviewers being harsher.

I like the general motivation here, and honestly I’m sympathetic to the paper. The field does need more standardized benchmarks for sequence modeling beyond the usual one-off genomics datasets with custom splits and somewhat slippery evaluation. BANANA is a catchy name too, for better or worse. After reading the paper twice, I think the benchmark itself is probably useful, but I’m less convinced the paper really nails down what we learn scientifically from the experiments.

The benchmark construction is the main contribution, and in that sense the paper is fairly practical. I appreciated the effort to define fixed splits and metrics, and Table 1 gives a decent overview of the six tasks. The diversity across DNA/RNA settings is nice. That said, I found the justification for the task selection a bit thin. On p. 3–4, the authors present the four “languages” and derived tasks, but I never got a strong sense that these six tasks are representative enough to support broad claims about “neural architectures for nucleic acids.” It feels more like a first collection than a benchmark with the scope implied by the title.

I also had some trouble with the experimental section. The claim in the abstract that self-supervised pretraining “is beneficial in every task” is a little too neat. Looking at the results tables (I assume Tables 2 and 3; the organization there was a bit hard to follow), the gains are sometimes pretty modest, and I’m not entirely sure they are robust given the apparent lack of variance estimates. Where are the multiple seeds? In my experience with biological sequence classification, seed sensitivity and split idiosyncrasies can absolutely dominate small differences. Without confidence intervals or significance testing, some of these conclusions feel overstated.

Another issue: the benchmark seems focused entirely on whole-sequence classification. That’s fine, but then the paper should be clearer that this is a narrow slice of genomics ML. No token-level labeling, no structure-aware tasks, no generative evaluation, no cross-species transfer. I might be wrong, but this limits the benchmark’s longevity. Figure 1 is helpful conceptually, though.

I also wanted more detail on leakage prevention and sequence similarity control in dataset construction. For nucleic acids this is not a minor implementation detail; it is the detail. The discussion around splits on p. 5 is too brief for me. If homologous sequences appear across train/test, results can look much better than they really are.

Overall: useful resource, somewhat underpowered paper. I can see this being valuable to the community, but I’d want a more careful experimental analysis and stronger dataset methodology before being fully enthusiastic.

I went into this paper pretty interested, because multimodal transfer from structured clinical variables to images is a genuinely useful problem, and in my experience a lot of papers in this area either hand-wave the alignment or just concatenate things and call it “knowledge transfer.” This paper is at least trying to address the real issue: not every tabular field should be injected into the image model, and categorical/numerical variables are not interchangeable. I appreciate that.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the core technical story. The main idea—aligning channel groups with tabular attributes via mutual information plus optimal transport—is intuitive, but the presentation around what exactly is being optimized felt murky. I especially struggled around Eq. (4)–(7): I think the authors are trying to learn a soft assignment between channel groups and tabular columns while simultaneously maximizing informativeness, but the distinction between “semantic capture” and plain regularized correlation is not very clear. Maybe I’m missing something, but the MI term seems more motivational than rigorously justified. In papers I’ve seen on cross-modal alignment, this is usually where things fall apart.

I did like the high-level interpretation of the transport plan as selecting “which subset to reuse.” Figure 2 helps. But then the empirical evidence for actual subset selection is weaker than the claims. Table 2 shows performance gains, yes, but it doesn’t really tell me whether the method identified meaningful columns or just used the easiest predictive shortcuts. The interpretability discussion felt a bit optimistic. If there is a case study with selected variables on, say, page 6 / Figure 4, it needs much more scrutiny. Are these stable across seeds? Across folds? I would really want that.

Another issue is baselines. The comparisons look somewhat incomplete. If you are claiming a principled transfer mechanism from tabular expert knowledge to image models, I expect stronger baselines than simple fusion and ablations. Where are the more recent multimodal distillation or cross-attention style methods? Maybe there is a reason they were omitted, but as is, the improvement margins in Table 1 are hard to interpret.

I’m also a bit uneasy about generality. The method seems fairly tailored to settings where tabular variables have moderate semantic overlap with visual features. That is a reasonable setting, but then the paper should say so more clearly instead of sounding broad.

Overall, I think the problem is important and the paper has some originality, but the current version feels under-validated and somewhat over-claimed. Borderline for me, leaning reject unless the authors can clarify the objective and provide stronger evidence that the learned alignments are meaningful rather than merely useful.

I went through the paper fairly carefully, and I have mixed feelings. The central idea—casting attribution as a submodular subset selection problem and explicitly favoring fewer, more interpretable regions—is interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing compared to the endless stream of saliency variants that mostly differ in implementation details. I do think the paper is trying to solve a real problem: many attribution maps are noisy, fragmented, and not useful for wrong predictions. That part resonated with me.

That said, I found the paper harder to parse than it should be. The method section around the submodular objective (I think this is around p. 4–5, especially Eq. (6) and the definitions following it) is dense and not very cleanly motivated. The four scores—confidence, effectiveness, consistency, and collaboration—sound reasonable, but after reading this twice I still don’t have a sharp intuition for why these exact terms are the right decomposition, versus a somewhat ad hoc engineering recipe. The theoretical claim that the proposed function is submodular is important, but the proof sketch felt a bit too convenient. I might be wrong here, but some assumptions seem to be tucked away in the construction of the set function rather than discussed openly.

I did like the emphasis on incorrect predictions. That is a real gap in a lot of attribution work. The results in Table 1 / Table 2 are promising, especially the gains on highest confidence and insertion for misclassified samples. Still, I am not entirely convinced the comparisons are fully fair. HSIC-Attribution is a strong baseline, yes, but the experimental section (around p. 6–7) does not really discuss whether all methods are matched in terms of region granularity, mask budget, and post-processing. For attribution papers, these details matter a lot, and Figure 3 in particular seems visually favorable to the proposed approach partly because it produces fewer regions by design.

Another issue: the writing needs work. There are several awkward phrases in the abstract and method description (“fewer interpretable region” is already grammatically off in the title), and this unfortunately makes the contribution feel less mature than it probably is. Also, I would have liked at least one stronger sanity check or human-centered evaluation. Deletion/insertion are standard, but they are not the whole story, and in my experience they can be gamed.

Overall, I think there is a potentially good idea here, but the paper is not yet fully convincing. My leaning is borderline reject for a top conference, mainly because the formulation feels under-justified and the evaluation, while decent, is not airtight. If revised carefully, this could become a solid paper.

I had a somewhat hard time with this paper. The core idea, as I understand it, is to detect adversarial examples by comparing “manifold distances,” and then to improve that detector with a service orchestration layer that chooses a preprocessing transform before detection. That is a reasonable engineering direction, and I do appreciate that the method does not require retraining the protected classifier. In practice that matters. But after reading this twice, I still found the paper frustratingly vague in places where it really needs to be precise.

The biggest issue is conceptual clarity. The term “manifold distance” is used throughout, but I never felt I got a clean mathematical object. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text (I think around p. 4) seem to define a distance in feature space after some transformation, but that is not the same thing as a data manifold distance in any meaningful geometric sense. Maybe I’m being too literal, but if you are going to build the whole paper around this term, it needs a much tighter definition and some justification for why adversarial examples should systematically move differently than benign samples. Right now it reads more like a heuristic detector with a grand name.

I also worry about the threat model. The abstract claims effectiveness in white-box, gray-box, and black-box settings, but from Table 2 / Table 3 (assuming I’m reading them correctly), the white-box attacker does not seem fully adaptive to the detector-orchestration pipeline. This is a classic problem in adversarial defense papers, and frankly the community has learned this lesson many times. If the attacker knows the preprocessing selection rule and the detector score, can they optimize through it? The paper does not convincingly answer that. Figure 5 looks encouraging at first glance, but without a truly adaptive evaluation I don’t put much weight on it.

There are also presentation issues. The “service orchestration” framing feels over-sold. In simpler terms, this is selecting among image processing operations before a detector. That’s fine, but the systems language makes it sound more novel than it is. Some parts on p. 6–7 read almost like architecture documentation rather than an ML paper. Also, the comparison to Feature Squeezing is too narrow. That baseline is old. Why not compare against more recent detection approaches, even simple Mahalanobis-style or feature-density detectors?

I don’t want to be entirely negative: the empirical effort seems nontrivial, and I can imagine this being useful in a constrained deployment setting. But as a conference paper, I think the evaluation and technical grounding are not yet at the level I’d want. My leaning is reject.

I found the paper fairly easy to follow overall, and the problem is definitely relevant. In imbalanced classification, the usual marginal coverage story is often pretty misleading in practice, especially for rare classes, so I appreciate that the paper is directly targeting class-conditional guarantees rather than hand-waving around them. The main idea in Sec. 3 — restricting to the classifier’s top-\(k\) labels and then doing class-conditional calibration there — is simple, maybe even a bit obvious in hindsight, but that is not a bad thing. In my experience, these are often the most useful conformal variants.

That said, I’m a bit torn on the novelty. After reading the method twice, I think the theoretical statement on p. 4–5 (around Theorem 2, if I’m tracking correctly) is the real contribution, not the algorithmic idea itself. The top-\(k\) truncation feels like a natural heuristic that many people would try anyway, so the value of the paper really depends on whether the guarantee is both correct and nontrivial. I believe it is mostly correct, but I found the setup around Eq. (3) and the exact role of ties / score discreteness somewhat under-explained. Maybe I missed it. Also, the claim that \(k\)-CCP “produces smaller prediction sets over CCP” needs to be stated carefully: yes, by construction in a set-inclusion sense, but the practical question is whether this comes at the cost of empty sets or unstable behavior for minority classes when the true label is often not in the top-\(k\). The coverage theorem addresses this in principle, but the intuition is not presented very clearly.

The empirical section is okay but not fully convincing. Table 1 and Table 2 show size reductions, which is nice, but I wanted more detail on how \(k\) is chosen. Fixed globally? Tuned on validation? Class-dependent? This matters a lot. Figure 2 was helpful, though I wish there were per-class results for the rarest classes rather than just aggregate summaries. For a paper about imbalance, averaging can hide the main issue. Also, I think the baselines are a little thin. Why not compare to APS/RAPS-style set construction adapted as much as possible to the class-conditional setting, even if they do not have the same guarantee?

So: good problem, solid theoretical angle, decent paper. But I’m not fully sold that the empirical evidence is strong enough yet, and the method is a bit incremental. Borderline accept for me, leaning weak accept if the theorem details are sound.

I found this paper interesting, and a bit surprising in a good way. The main claim — that standard gradient clipping is not actually robust to label noise “in general,” while a slight variant is — goes against a lot of the informal intuition people repeat. I’ve definitely heard (and probably said) versions of that intuition myself. So I appreciated the attempt to separate optimization folklore from what can actually be proved.

The theoretical part is the strongest component for me. The negative result around standard clipping is, I think, the real contribution here, more than the proposed variant. If I understood Theorem 3.1 correctly, the issue is that clipping the full sample gradient still leaves the wrong dependence on the corrupted label, whereas the modified rule effectively induces a different loss with bounded influence. That is a clean story. Eq. (4) and the discussion right after it made this click for me. I also liked the connection to a modified cross-entropy-style objective; this makes the method feel less like a trick.

That said, I struggled with the presentation in places. Page 4, around the setup of the noise model, is more compressed than it should be. I had to reread the paragraph before Proposition 2.2 because it was not obvious what is assumed about the hypothesis class versus what is a property of the loss. Maybe this is just me reading too quickly, but the paper is not helping the reader there. Figure 1 is intuitive, but I wish it were tied more explicitly to the theorem statements rather than left as a kind of motivational picture.

On the empirical side, I’m less convinced. Table 1 shows gains, yes, but the baselines feel a bit narrow. If the claim is robustness to label noise, I really wanted to see comparisons to more established noisy-label methods, not just standard CE plus clipping variants. Otherwise it risks being a somewhat strawman experimental section. Also, the sensitivity to the clipping threshold/shape parameter is not explored enough; in my experience these methods can look great on one corruption level and then become finicky.

Still, I think the core idea is solid and the theory is nontrivial. I’m not entirely sure the empirical section is strong enough for a top conference by itself, but the main result is novel and useful, and I suspect people will cite the negative result. Lean accept.

I found this paper interesting and a bit frustrating in equal measure. The core claim — identifiability of content/style without knowing the latent dimensions in advance — is genuinely important, and I agree with the authors that this is a longstanding sore spot in unsupervised representation learning. In that sense the paper is aiming high, which I appreciate. The latent distribution matching idea is also not just a cosmetic rebranding; at least on first read it seems to give a cleaner way to talk about cross-domain constraints than some of the older disentanglement papers I’ve reviewed.

That said, after reading the theory sections twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper’s “substantially more relaxed conditions” are as relaxed as advertised. The assumptions around sparsity and the coupled latent parameterization seem to be doing a lot of work, maybe too much. Around the main identifiability result (I think this is Theorem 1/2 in the middle pages, around pp. 4–6), the statement is appealing, but the practical meaning of the sparsity condition is murky. Is this an identifiable model class, or a theorem about a fairly special parameterization? I might be wrong, but I came away feeling the latter.

I also found the equivalence claim between LDM and the GAN objective a little slippery. The discussion near the reformulation section (roughly p. 6, around the equations defining the regularized GAN loss) says the conditions are “mild,” but they did not feel mild to me. In my experience, these equivalence arguments often hide topological or optimization assumptions that are harmless on paper and quite nontrivial in finite-sample training. The paper should be more upfront here. Figure 2 helps with intuition, but I wanted a more explicit map from the theorem assumptions to what is actually enforced in training.

The experiments are okay, not great. Table 1 suggests the proposed method is robust to latent dimension misspecification, which is the right thing to test, but I wanted stronger baselines. In particular, comparisons to more recent nonlinear ICA / weakly supervised disentanglement papers would help. Also, if the central claim is unknown latent dimension, then ablations varying overcomplete latent spaces should be front and center, not somewhat buried.

Overall, I think there is a real theorem here and probably a worthwhile paper, but the presentation overclaims a bit and the empirical section undersells the theory. I’m leaning weak accept if the venue values theory, but I’m not enthusiastic.

I like the basic intuition here. The paper is trying to revive a pretty old idea—bag-of-visual-words / topic structure over images—but with a more modern framing around segmentation-derived “visual vocabularies.” I think that’s a reasonable direction, and the paper is readable overall. Figure 1 helps a lot. The examples of recurring segments becoming visual words are probably the most persuasive part of the submission.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is doing enough beyond rebranding a familiar pipeline. Segment images, cluster segments, treat clusters as words, run a topic model. That is basically the method as I understood it from Sec. 3, especially around Eq. (1) and the paragraph right after it on p. 4. Maybe I’m being a bit unfair, but the novelty feels thin unless the authors can articulate much more clearly what is fundamentally different from prior visual codebook methods and older image-topic models. Right now the related work section (p. 2-3) gestures at this, but not in a serious enough way.

I also found the evaluation somewhat shaky. Using standard topic metrics is fine in principle, but these metrics were designed for text, and I’m not entirely sure they transfer cleanly to visual words constructed from segments. The paper says this is justified because the representation is “word-like,” but that felt hand-wavy. Table 1 reports coherence/perplexity-style numbers, but I wanted much more interpretation. Better than what baseline, and why should I care if the underlying vocabulary itself may be unstable? I kept wondering how sensitive the results are to the segmentation algorithm and the number of clusters. This seems central, yet the ablation is very limited. If Table 2 is supposed to address this, it doesn’t go nearly far enough.

The human study is a good idea, but again I had questions. On p. 6, the protocol description is too brief. Were annotators shown top-k segments per topic, whole images, or both? Figure 3 suggests one thing, the text another. Also, sample sizes look modest. I might be wrong, but the claims about “confirming coherency” seem stronger than the evidence supports.

So: interesting direction, decent presentation, but I came away feeling this is more of a workshop paper than a top-conference one. The core idea is intuitive, yet the technical contribution and empirical validation both feel undercooked. My inclination is weak reject.

I found this paper interesting in intent, but honestly pretty frustrating to evaluate in its current form. The basic idea — automatically exploring Bayesian graphical model “wiring diagrams” for nuisance-robust inference — sounds appealing, and I can imagine this being useful in practice, especially for subject-transfer settings. But after reading the paper twice, I still don’t have a crisp sense of what the actual scientific contribution is beyond “we try a bunch of combinations of encoder/decoder/classifier/adversary/estimator blocks and pick what works.”

The presentation is a big part of the problem. Around pp. 3–4, where the framework is introduced, the space of candidate graphs is described too abstractly. Figure 1 looks nice, but it never really pinned down for me what is being searched over, what constraints define valid graphs, and what the search objective is exactly. Is this architecture search? Model selection over probabilistic graphical structures? A recipe book over known nuisance-invariance losses? I think it is somewhere in between, which is maybe why it feels blurry. Eq. (3) and the surrounding discussion (I think on p. 5) also left me confused about whether the method is genuinely Bayesian or just variational/deep latent-variable flavored. The title says “Bayesian graph exploration,” but the procedure feels much more heuristic than principled Bayesian model comparison.

I also worry the empirical section overclaims. Table 1 and Table 2 show improvements, yes, but there are many moving pieces: variational modeling, adversarial training, disentanglement choices, and then ensembles over explored graphs. At that point, if the ensemble wins, what exactly did AutoBayes discover? In my experience, ensembling many reasonable models almost always helps, so the gains in the last results table are not that surprising. I wanted stronger ablations isolating the value of the graph exploration itself. For example, how does it compare against a careful manual selection by an expert, or even random search over the same design space? I may have missed it, but I did not see a convincing baseline of that sort.

Another issue: the subject-transfer story is potentially important, but the nuisance assumptions are underspecified. On p. 6 the paper seems to treat subject identity as nuisance, but sometimes subject may carry task-relevant signal. That makes the invariance objective tricky, and I didn’t see enough discussion of failure modes.

So, mixed feelings. There is something here, and I do appreciate the attempt to systematize a messy design space. But the paper currently reads more like a framework/demo paper than a solid methodological advance. I’d lean weak reject unless the authors can clarify the search procedure, tighten the Bayesian claims, and provide much sharper ablations.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea — using an iterated learning style procedure to bias emergent communication toward compositional structure — is sensible, and honestly I’m surprised more papers have not pushed this exact angle in a clean neural setup. The experimental story is generally coherent: later generations produce languages that look more structured and generalize better. Figure 1 makes the setup easy to grasp, and Table 1/Table 2 (assuming I’m reading them correctly) do show a fairly consistent gain over the non-iterated baseline.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the theoretical claim that compositional languages obtain “larger posterior probability” and are therefore amplified over generations. The argument around this, especially near Eq. (3)–(5), felt more hand-wavy than the abstract suggests. I might be wrong, but it seems the posterior preference is doing a lot of work, while the actual neural optimization dynamics are quite messy and not obviously captured by the derivation. In my experience, these evolutionary/iterated-learning intuitions often break once you add finite-capacity neural agents and SGD. So I wanted a more careful bridge between the idealized analysis and the actual training setup.

I also found the evaluation of compositionality a little too convenient. The paper leans heavily on topographic or disentanglement-style metrics (I’m referring to the section around page 5 and the results in Figure 3), but those measures can be gamed by fairly brittle protocols. Better zero-shot tests help, yes, and I do appreciate that the authors include generalization experiments, but I wanted harder held-out splits and maybe some qualitative examples of messages mapped to attribute combinations. Right now the paper says “compositional” a lot more confidently than I would.

There are also some clarity issues. On page 4, the exact data passed from one generation to the next was not immediately clear to me — is it the full supervised dataset of meanings/messages, or samples from the teacher’s production distribution? That matters. Likewise, the role of the listener in transmission versus only in within-generation optimization is a bit buried.

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The paper is better than many emergent communication submissions because it asks a nontrivial question and gives a plausible mechanism rather than just another benchmark bump. But I’m not at the level of enthusiasm the paper seems to want from me. Weak accept, maybe. If the claims were tightened and the compositionality evidence made less self-serving, I’d feel much better.

I read this with some interest because approximate vanishing ideals are one of those niche-but-important topics that keep resurfacing, and OAVI in particular has always been held back by runtime. The paper’s main pitch — that the original complexity understanding was too pessimistic, and that OAVI can actually be made practical at larger scale — is potentially meaningful. I also appreciate that the authors are not proposing yet another heuristic basis construction, but are staying within the only framework (to my knowledge as well) that comes with learning guarantees.

That said, I had a somewhat mixed reaction. The complexity claim is the centerpiece, but I found the presentation around it harder to trust than I wanted. The argument around the linear dependence on the number of samples m (I think this is around the theorem in the middle of the paper, maybe p. 4–5) is plausible, but the wording is occasionally too sweeping. “Not superlinear” is a strong rhetorical correction to prior understanding, and I’m not entirely sure the assumptions under which this holds are emphasized enough. In particular, some constants seem to hide nontrivial dependence on degree growth / active set sizes, and in practice that matters a lot more than the asymptotic statement. I would have liked a more careful separation between worst-case complexity and the empirical runtime story.

The second contribution, swapping pairwise conditional gradients for blended pairwise conditional gradients, is sensible. Honestly this part felt less novel algorithmically, more like a very reasonable engineering/theory update. The “exponential speed-up in n” claim made me raise an eyebrow. Maybe I’m being too cautious, but whenever I see “exponential speed-up” in an optimization subroutine result, I immediately check whether this is mostly inherited from previous convergence-rate machinery rather than something genuinely specific to the AVI setting. If so, the paper should say that more plainly.

The inverse Hessian boosting idea is probably the most interesting practical piece. The experimental gains in the tables/figures (I’m thinking of the runtime plots near the end, maybe Fig. 2/3 and Table 1) are large enough that I believe there is something real here. But the ablations are thinner than I wanted. After reading this twice, I still could not tell when this boosting helps and when it might be unstable or unnecessary. Some numerical robustness discussion is missing.

Overall I think this is a solid paper, but not a completely clean one. Useful, likely impactful for the small community working on algebraic methods in learning, but the theoretical claims should be toned down and clarified. I lean positive, though not enthusiastically so.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more compelling than a lot of the recent “speech LLM” submissions that mostly just scale up an obvious recipe. The central idea—distilling from a text-only LLM using transcripts, without instruction-response speech data—is simple but good. In my experience, those are often the papers that end up mattering. The framing around avoiding capability forgetting also resonates; I’ve seen this in multimodal adaptation papers repeatedly, and the discussion around that on p. 2 is sensible.

What I liked most is that the method seems practical rather than baroque. Figure 1 makes the setup very clear, and I appreciated that the paper does not pretend to have magical supervision when in fact it is just using transcript-conditioned teacher outputs. Table 1 / Table 2 (if I’m reading correctly) give a decent spread of tasks—QA, classification, translation—and the claim that this works across all three without task-specific instruction tuning is notable. The 72% win rate against Qwen2-Audio is eye-catching, although I’d be careful here: preference wins can be very sensitive to prompt setup and evaluator protocol. I had to reread the human evaluation section because the exact comparison conditions felt under-specified. Were both systems constrained to the same output style/length? That matters a lot.

I do have some concerns. First, the novelty is somewhat incremental. This is basically response distillation from ASR transcripts into a speech-conditioned model. Useful, yes. Radical, no. The paper sells it a bit too hard. Second, I’m not entirely sure the evidence for “forgetting” is as strong as the motivation suggests. The intro leans on that claim, but later experiments don’t really isolate forgetting versus just poor multimodal alignment. A more direct comparison to standard SFT on the same base model, with matched compute/data, would help a lot. Right now the compute comparison (“>100x less”) on p. 6 feels favorable but maybe not apples-to-apples.

I also found Eq. (3) oddly under-explained. Maybe I’m missing something, but after reading it twice I still wasn’t sure what exactly is optimized when the teacher response is noisy or ambiguous. Relatedly, I wanted more ablations on transcript quality. If the whole pipeline depends on transcripts, how robust is this to ASR errors? This seems central, not peripheral.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a clean idea, decent empirical coverage, and real practical relevance. Not flawless, and I wish the evaluation were tighter, but I’d lean positive. Probably not an award paper, but likely worth accepting.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The main idea — a pruning-based graph clustering method with claimed \(O(m \log m)\) time and \(O(m)\) memory — is potentially quite valuable, especially for sparse large graphs where the usual spectral-style methods are just too expensive. I’ve reviewed a lot of graph clustering papers over the years, and I do appreciate when something is clearly motivated by actual runtime/memory pain rather than yet another objective with no implementation story.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails the theoretical or empirical case. The core “ignorance” condition is intuitively appealing, but I found the presentation of it a bit slippery. Around the main algorithmic development (I think this is on pp. 4–6, especially near Eq. (3) / the pruning rule), the authors seem to move back and forth between a heuristic argument and a correctness-style claim. I might be wrong, but I could not tell whether the pruning is guaranteed not to eliminate edges critical for the final cut under ncut/rcut, or whether the guarantee is only about a relaxed search space. That distinction matters a lot.

The multi-view extension is also underspecified. The abstract says “a small number of views” adds no runtime increase, which sounds great, almost too great. But in the method section the cost seems to depend at least linearly on the number of views unless one assumes some preprocessing or shared ordering. If that assumption is there, it should be stated much more explicitly. Right now it reads a little like marketing.

On the experiments: mixed feelings. The runtime tables (Table 1 or 2 — sorry, I may be mixing them up) are strong and honestly the most convincing part of the paper. The quality evaluation is weaker. The claim that “linfcut” produces clusters that “make more sense” to a human observer is plausible, but the evidence is pretty anecdotal. Figure 3 looked suggestive, not definitive. In my experience, once authors start appealing to visual intuition, they need either a user study or at least a more systematic proxy. Otherwise it feels subjective.

I do think the paper has merit. It is refreshingly practical, and there may be a real contribution here if the pruning mechanism is as robust as claimed. But the writeup needs tightening, especially around guarantees, what exactly is optimized, and the multi-view complexity claim. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the conference values scalable graph methods, but I wouldn’t call this an easy accept.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than most theory submissions in geometric deep learning. The main claim — essentially that a broad family of invariant geometric models are complete under fully-connected conditions — is important if true, because people have been hand-waving about the “limits” of invariant models for years. I’ve reviewed a lot of GDL papers, and this is one of the few that tries to cleanly separate what is impossible from what is merely an artifact of architecture design.

The core technical development around DisGNN and then GeoNGNN is, I think, the strongest part. The discussion around the exceptional cases for distance-based message passing (around the theorem in the middle of the paper; I’m referring to the part that characterizes unidentifiable point clouds as highly symmetric ones) is quite nice. I also liked the idea of using GeoNGNN as a proof device to transfer completeness results to other architectures. That said, I had to read the proof sketch twice. Some of the notation is pretty dense, and the paper sometimes moves too quickly from geometric intuition to algebraic conclusion. In particular, the assumptions bundled into the “fully-connected” setting felt under-motivated. This is doing a lot of work. In practice, many of these models are not run on fully connected graphs because of cost, inductive bias, or both, so the practical significance is a bit narrower than the abstract suggests.

I was also not entirely convinced by how “seamlessly extended” the graph subgraph GNN results are to geometric settings. Maybe I’m being picky, but whenever a paper says “seamlessly,” alarms go off. There are geometric degeneracies and continuous symmetries here that do not have a clean graph analogue. If this is discussed in the appendix, it should be surfaced more clearly in the main text.

The claims about DimeNet, GemNet, and SphereNet are potentially high impact, but the presentation there felt compressed. I would have appreciated a more explicit mapping from each model’s actual update equations to the abstract completeness framework, rather than a theorem statement and abbreviated argument. As written, I believe the result, but I don’t feel the paper makes it easy to verify.

Overall, I think this is a strong theory paper, though not an easy read. My main hesitation is that the headline message is a bit broader than what is actually established. Still, the results seem meaningful and likely to influence how people think about invariant vs equivariant design. I lean accept, probably weak accept.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing, because there has been a lot of sloppy language in the interpretability literature around “detectors,” “grandmother cells,” etc. The central point here — that many common selectivity measures can make units look much more object-selective than they really are from a signal-detection perspective — is worth making. I especially appreciated the side-by-side comparison across localist selectivity, precision, CCMAS, network dissection, and human judgments of AM images. Figure 1 and Table 1 (assuming I’m reading the layout correctly) make the mismatch pretty concrete.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper in its current form. My main issue is that the argument sometimes overreaches relative to the experiments. The title asks whether there are any “object detectors” in hidden layers of CNNs, which is a broad claim, but most of the detailed analysis is on AlexNet, and then only “a few units” from VGG-16 / GoogLeNet, as stated in the abstract and later around the discussion of those case studies (I think this was around p. 12-13). That generalization feels thin. In my experience, claims this broad need either broader sampling or a narrower title.

I also found the operationalization of “object detector” a little slippery. The paper criticizes precision and CCMAS because they can be high even when hit rate is low or false alarm rate is high, and I agree with that in spirit. But then the paper seems to move toward a fairly strict detector notion tied to signal detection metrics. That is reasonable, but it is still a choice. Some units may be useful feature templates without being stand-alone detectors. The discussion around Eq. (if I remember correctly, the d-prime / hit-false-alarm section on p. 6 or so) should acknowledge this more explicitly.

There are also places where I got confused by the dataset/class accounting. After reading this twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure how negatives were constructed for units associated with visually broad concepts, and whether class imbalance affected the reported precision numbers. This matters a lot.

The paper is strongest when it is empirical and specific, and weaker when it becomes a sweeping corrective to prior work. I do think the critique of human interpretation of activation maximization images is fair — maybe overdue, frankly. But the comparisons to “grandmother cell” findings in RNNs felt a bit rhetorical.

Overall: useful paper, probably worth acceptance if the venue values careful negative/clarifying results, but I’d want the claims tightened and some methodological details clarified. Right now I’m positive but not wildly enthusiastic.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of recent “alignment/eval” submissions that just rediscover annotator noise and call it a day. The central point — that disagreement in preference data is often substantive rather than random noise — is important, and I think the taxonomy is the most valuable part of the paper. The examples around style and refusals especially rang true to me given what I’ve seen in instruction-following data. Figure 2 and the discussion on p.4–5 were probably the clearest part of the paper.

That said, I’m less convinced by the empirical depth than by the framing. After reading this twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure how stable the 10-category taxonomy is. On p.3 the categories are presented as fairly crisp, but in practice several seem entangled. “Task underspecification” vs “response style” is not always separable, and some of the refusal cases looked to me like underspecification plus value differences. I really wanted inter-annotator agreement for the taxonomy labels themselves, or at least a stronger discussion of adjudication. Otherwise there is a risk that the paper is building a lot on somewhat subjective coding.

The reward modeling result is intuitive, maybe too intuitive. The claim around Bradley-Terry on p.6 and Eq. (2) is basically that a scalar preference model cannot represent multimodal human values. Yes, of course. I don’t mean that sarcastically — it still matters to show it carefully — but the experimental setup needs to go further to demonstrate novelty. Table 3 was underwhelming to me because I kept asking: compared to what stronger baseline? A mixture model, user-conditional RM, or even simple entropy-aware abstention baseline would make the argument much sharper. As written, the paper mostly shows standard methods fail in the way one would expect them to fail.

I did like the LM-as-a-Judge analysis. Figure 4 was one of the more compelling results, especially the tendency to force a winner under disagreement. But again, I wanted calibration analysis, not just win rates. Do judges know they are uncertain? The title asks “do models know?”, and I don’t think the paper fully answers that.

Overall I think the paper is asking the right question, and I appreciate the authors resisting the lazy “aggregate harder” mindset. But the current version feels a bit halfway between a qualitative dataset paper and a modeling paper. If accepted, it would be for the problem formulation and taxonomy more than for the technical contribution. I’m positive but not fully sold.

I found the core idea interesting, and honestly more interesting than the paper’s current presentation. The notion of “fractal nodes” as auxiliary summarization tokens that coexist with original nodes is not crazy at all; in fact it feels adjacent to graph coarsening / hierarchical pooling / virtual node ideas, but with a different motivation. I can see why the authors think this helps with long-range communication. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the method is as novel or as well-justified as the framing suggests.

A lot hinges on the “fractal” terminology, and I think the paper oversells that. On p.2–3 the discussion of renormalization and self-similarity is evocative, but I did not see a real mathematical connection beyond a design metaphor. Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) define the fractal node updates in a fairly standard message-passing form; I was expecting something more principled about scale invariance or recursive construction. Instead, this reads more like adaptive supernodes. That is not necessarily bad, but then the claims should be toned down. Right now the paper sounds deeper than it is.

I was also confused by the construction details. In Fig. 2, the fractal nodes seem to summarize subgraphs at multiple scales, but the text around lines 180–220 (I may be off by a few lines) does not make it fully clear how these subgraphs are selected, whether the assignment is learned jointly, and what prevents collapse to trivial partitions. I think Table 1 shows good results, but without a stronger ablation the gains are hard to attribute. For example: what happens with plain virtual nodes, hierarchical pooling, or even random shortcut nodes at the same parameter budget? The over-squashing claim especially feels under-supported. The paper says fractal nodes “alleviate” over-squashing, but where is the direct evidence beyond downstream accuracy? I wanted curvature-based or path-based diagnostics, maybe like what people now routinely include in this literature.

The empirical section is decent but not fully persuasive. Table 2 is fine, and I appreciate the efficiency discussion, though the comparison to graph Transformers is a bit cherry-picked. Some of the baselines are not the strongest available, at least in my experience reviewing recent graph papers. Also, Fig. 4 looks nice, but the visualization is more illustrative than probative.

So overall: promising idea, weak conceptual grounding, and not enough experimental disambiguation. I’m leaning borderline reject. If the authors reposition this more honestly as a hierarchical message-passing variant with learned summary nodes, and really tighten the ablations, I think it could become a solid paper. Right now it feels one iteration short.

I found this paper interesting, but also pretty uneven. The core idea — giving a theory for diffusion transformers on sequential Gaussian-process data and explicitly tying approximation/statistical guarantees to spatial-temporal covariance decay — is timely, and honestly more relevant than yet another generic “transformers are universal approximators” result. I appreciate that the authors are at least trying to say something specific about DiT-style models rather than treating the architecture as a black box. The “transformer as unrolling an algorithm” angle is also nice in spirit. It reminded me a bit of the recent line of theory papers that reinterpret attention as iterative kernel smoothing / message passing, though here the connection is more tailored to diffusion-score learning.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper lands the main claim as cleanly as it should. The assumptions around the Gaussian process seem to be doing a lot of work. On p. 3–4, where the covariance classes with different decay patterns are introduced, the setup is mathematically tidy, but also narrow enough that I’m not sure how much this tells us about realistic video data beyond a stylized surrogate. Maybe that is fine for a first step, but then the title/abstract feel a bit too broad. “Captures spatial-temporal dependencies” is a strong statement. What is really shown is something closer to: under a GP model with structured covariance, there exists a DiT parameterization achieving certain score/distribution approximation rates.

I also struggled with the presentation of the main theorem(s). The result around score approximation (I think Theorem 4.1?) is potentially the centerpiece, but the dependence on sequence length, dimension, and covariance decay is not explained intuitively enough. Equation (7) and the discussion immediately after it on p. 6 felt especially compressed. I kept wanting one paragraph of plain-English interpretation. Figure 1 helps a bit, but not enough. Table 1 is useful, though I wish the baselines there were contextualized better: are these comparisons actually apples-to-apples in approximation complexity?

The empirical section is okay, not great. The attention visualizations in Fig. 2/3 are suggestive, but to be blunt, this kind of evidence is very easy to overread. I’m not entirely sure the experiments really validate the theory beyond showing behavior consistent with it. In my experience, for theory papers that make architectural claims, a sharper synthetic ablation is usually needed.

Overall, I think there is a real contribution here, and I’d rather see papers like this than vague scaling-law theory. But the scope should be stated more carefully, and the exposition needs work. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values theory on modern generative architectures, otherwise weak reject.

I found this paper interesting, though I’m a bit torn on it. The core idea — treating restoration as a controlled dynamical system with a learned stopping time / moving endpoint — is actually pretty neat, and it feels more principled than yet another “adaptive depth” story dressed up as novelty. The formulation around the moving endpoint control on p.3–4, especially Eq. (3)–(6), gives the paper a cleaner conceptual backbone than many restoration papers I’ve read recently. I also liked the intuition that different degradation levels should correspond to different effective unfolding lengths. That part makes sense.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I still think the technical novelty is a little slippery. The actual instantiated model seems quite close to an RNN restorer plus a policy network that decides when to stop. Maybe I’m underselling it, but I kept asking myself whether this is fundamentally different from adaptive computation / early exiting methods, beyond the control-theoretic language. The paper gestures at this distinction, but not convincingly enough for me. In particular, the transition from the control formulation to the final trainable architecture felt too quick. I would have liked a more explicit discussion around Eq. (8) and the training objective on p.5: what exactly is gained by the “moving endpoint control” framing, beyond motivation?

The experiments are solid overall. Table 1 and Table 2 look strong, and the generalization to unseen higher noise levels is genuinely impressive if it holds up. Figure 1 is also helpful. But I was less convinced by the empirical analysis than by the headline numbers. The policy network behavior is underexplored. How often does it choose different unfolding lengths in practice? Is it monotone with corruption severity? Figure 4 hints at this, but I wanted a much more detailed diagnostic. Otherwise the method risks feeling like a black box with an adaptive loop count.

I also found some missing comparisons odd. Given the paper’s main claim, I expected stronger baselines against other adaptive-depth or recurrent restoration models, not just standard feed-forward restoration networks. Maybe I missed one, but this is important. And the computational story is a bit fuzzy: if the policy often selects long horizons, what is the actual efficiency gain?

Overall, I think this is a good paper, maybe borderline very good, but not an easy accept for me in its current form. The idea is elegant, the results are competitive, but the paper oversells conceptual novelty and undersells analysis. My leaning is weak accept if the bar is impact/empirical performance, closer to borderline if the bar is methodological clarity.

I read this fairly carefully, and I’m still a bit unconvinced. The core idea—casting PU learning in an adversarial framework—is interesting, and I can see why the authors thought GAN-style training might be a natural fit. In spirit it makes sense. But after reading the objective on p.3 and then again around Eq. (4)–(7), I found the paper harder to trust than to appreciate.

The biggest issue for me is that the method feels under-justified theoretically. The paper says a “direct adaptation of GAN” does not work well and then introduces PAN with a KL-based objective, but the transition is pretty hand-wavy. Eq. (6) seems to be the key step, yet I’m not entirely sure what exact distribution the predictor is matching, or why this particular divergence should lead to a better PU classifier rather than simply a more stable adversarial game. There is a lot of intuition, not enough derivation. Maybe I’m missing something, but in PU learning the class prior and the risk estimator usually matter a lot, and here that whole connection is surprisingly fuzzy.

I also had trouble interpreting the empirical section. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains, yes, but the baselines are not especially strong by current PU standards. I would have expected comparison to more modern non-negative PU methods and perhaps stronger deep PU baselines. If those are there, they are not discussed clearly enough. The image and text experiments are a nice touch, though Figure 2 is not very informative beyond showing training curves that look GAN-like and noisy. In my experience, adversarial methods can look impressive while being annoyingly brittle, and I wanted much more evidence on stability across runs. Means without variances are not enough here.

A smaller but real concern: the writing is often confusing. On p.2 the analogy to GANs is oversold. GANs generate samples; this method is selecting/reweighting unlabeled examples. That is related, but not the same thing. I think the paper would be stronger if it were less eager to claim “natural” equivalence and more precise about what is actually adversarial.

Overall, I like the ambition, and there may be something useful here, but as submitted this feels premature. Interesting idea, insufficiently nailed down. My inclination is weak reject.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than I expected from the title alone. The idea of pushing learning-to-learn into the zeroth-order setting is quite natural in hindsight, but I have not seen many papers really do it in a convincing way. The two-level design — one RNN for updates and another RNN for sampling/query directions — is clever. The black-box attack experiments are probably the most compelling part for me, since that is a setting where hand-designed ZO methods are actually used and not just toy benchmarks.

That said, I have fairly mixed feelings after reading it twice. My main issue is that the paper sometimes oversells “learning” where there is still a lot of classical ZO machinery doing the heavy lifting. In Eq. (3) / around the gradient estimator description on p.3, the optimizer is still fundamentally relying on a standard Gaussian smoothing estimator, and then the learned component sits on top of that. That is fine, but then the claims should be calibrated a bit more carefully. Right now the framing makes it sound like the method discovers a new optimization principle, while in practice it seems closer to “learned control on top of noisy ZO estimates.”

I was also not entirely convinced by the analysis section. The discussion around the variance reduction effect of the sampling RNN (I think this is around p.5–6, and maybe Fig. 3?) is suggestive but not really a theory result. Maybe I’m being too picky, but calling the experiments “extensive analytical experiments” in the abstract felt like a stretch. They are ablations. Useful ones, yes, but not analysis in the stronger sense.

Another concern is generalization. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains, sometimes substantial, but I kept wondering how brittle the learned optimizer is to changes in dimension, query budget, or objective class. There are some cross-task evaluations, but I wanted more on out-of-distribution behavior. In my experience with learned optimizers, this is exactly where things fall apart. The paper acknowledges this a little, but not enough.

The writing is decent overall, though a few implementation details are annoyingly buried. For example, I had to hunt for how the hidden state is initialized and how training truncation is handled. That should not be hard to find in a paper centered on RNN optimizers.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real empirical value, especially for black-box attacks, but I am less convinced by the broader methodological claim. I lean positive, though not enthusiastically so.

I found the paper interesting and reasonably ambitious. The central idea — conditioning a Transformer on a structured representation of the PDE itself rather than training one model per equation family — is the right direction, and honestly overdue. A lot of recent “foundation model for physics” work gestures at this but ends up being just scale + heterogeneous data. Here, at least, there is an attempt to formalize what the conditioning variables should be. I appreciated the discussion around PDE components on p.3–4, especially the split between domain-wise and point-wise conditions. That part is probably the clearest contribution.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the “universal” claim in the title. It feels oversized relative to what is actually demonstrated. Looking at the experiments (Tables 1–3), the benchmarks are broad by neural PDE standards, yes, but still mostly within the regime of supervised operator learning with fairly structured coefficient/boundary variations. In my experience, “universal” would require much stronger evidence on out-of-support PDE forms, more pathological boundary conditions, irregular geometries, or at least some serious symbolic compositional generalization. Figure 1 gives the impression of a solver for arbitrary PDEs, but the actual evaluation seems narrower.

I also found the theory section a bit slippery. The argument around the solution being “under the control” of PDE components is intuitively fine, but the formalism did not really cash out into a theorem that justifies the architecture in a strong sense. Equation (2) and the surrounding text (around p.5, if I’m tracking correctly) read more like a representation proposal than a theoretical result. That’s okay, but then I would tone down the framing. Right now it oversells.

The empirical results are strong overall, though I had to read the ablations twice. The component ablation in Table 4 is useful, but I really wanted a clearer sense of which conditioning types matter most under distribution shift, not just average in-distribution error. Also, I might have missed it, but I did not see enough discussion of compute. For a method positioned as a practical surrogate for numerical solvers, training cost and inference scaling matter a lot.

So, mixed but positive. I think this is a good paper, maybe even very good, but the claims need tightening. If accepted, I’d strongly encourage the authors to rename/reframe away from “universal PDE solver,” which is catchy but, frankly, not earned yet.

I found this paper interesting but also pretty frustrating to read. The core idea — replacing discrete image tokens with a continuous diffusion-based image token sampler on top of an LLM, with the goal of “lossless” joint image-text modeling — is ambitious and, honestly, worth exploring. I’ve seen several recent attempts in this direction, and I do think the community is converging on the view that VQ-style discretization is a real bottleneck. So in spirit I’m sympathetic.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper quite delivers on its main claims. The word “lossless” in the title and throughout the abstract feels overstated. The theoretical discussion around this is on p.3–4, especially around Eq. (4)–(7), but I don’t think the authors really prove losslessness in any meaningful practical sense; they show a factorization and then instantiate it with a learned continuous probabilistic model. That is not the same thing. Maybe I’m being too literal, but this distinction matters, and I found the rhetoric too strong.

I also had trouble following the parameterization argument for numerical stability in low precision. The derivation around Eq. (11) and the associated discussion on p.5 is one of those sections I had to read twice. I think the intended point is that a particular diffusion target reduces numerical error under bf16/fp16, which is plausible, but the presentation is rushed and the assumptions are not very transparent. In my experience, if a method claim hinges on “theoretically optimal” parameterization, the empirical ablation should be extremely clean. Table 3 helps, but not enough. I wanted to see more direct evidence that this is actually the reason training works.

The empirical section is mixed. The scale-up trend is nice, and Figure 4 is probably the strongest part of the paper. Also, getting close to CLIP-based MLLMs on 18 benchmarks is nontrivial. But “approaches comparable performance” is doing a lot of work here. On several benchmarks in Table 2, the gap is still noticeable. I’m also not entirely sure the comparison is fair without more discussion of data mixture, pretraining compute, and image resolution. Those details are scattered.

One more thing: the reliance on a very large CFG scale at inference (p.6, lines around the training/inference recipe) feels a bit like a hack. Maybe it works, fine, but then I want a clearer explanation of what distribution is actually being sampled and whether this hurts the clean probabilistic story.

Overall, I think this is a promising paper with real ideas, but it currently oversells the theory and under-explains some crucial engineering choices. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the rebuttal clears up the “lossless” claim and the training stability argument.

I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The high-level motivation is actually interesting: instead of yet another conservative offline RL objective, the paper asks what kind of data one should collect in the first place, especially in a task-agnostic setting. That is a real question, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to connect data generation and downstream offline RL performance rather than treating the dataset as fixed forever.

That said, I found the theory-to-method jump pretty shaky. On p. 3–4, the central claim seems to be that the performance gap is controlled by the distance between the behavior-induced state-action distribution and the optimal-policy distribution (I think this is around Theorem 1 / Eq. (6)?), but the assumptions are doing a lot of work and are only called “mild.” I’m not convinced they are mild. In model-based offline RL, even small model bias can dominate these bounds, and here the argument seems to hide that behind concentration or boundedness assumptions. I might be wrong, but after reading this twice I still don’t see what is genuinely new versus a rephrasing of standard distribution shift arguments in offline RL.

The unsupervised RL part is also a bit under-motivated. The claim that “a series of policies trained by unsupervised RL can minimize the worst-case regret” sounds nice, but I struggled to understand exactly what policy class and task distribution this statement ranges over. The discussion around the task-agnostic setting on p. 5 felt hand-wavy. If the guarantee is only worst-case over some restricted reward family, that needs to be much clearer. Right now it reads broader than it probably is.

Empirically, the results are decent but not fully convincing. Table 1 and Figure 3 suggest UDG beats the baselines on locomotion tasks, but the benchmark is narrow.
