These are all MuJoCo-style tasks where unsupervised exploration often looks better than it really is. I would have liked at least one non-locomotion domain, or some stronger ablation on the “select proper data” part of UDG, which frankly seems to be doing a lot of the heavy lifting. Is the gain from unsupervised generation, or from dataset filtering/selection? That distinction matters. I also couldn’t tell from the experimental section whether the supervised generation baseline was tuned comparably.

The writing needs work. There are many grammatical issues, and some notation changes abruptly (I noticed this around Eq. (8)–(10)). Figure 2 helped, but overall the presentation made the paper harder to evaluate than it needed to be.

My bottom line: interesting direction, but I’m not yet convinced by either the theoretical novelty or the empirical depth. Borderline to weak reject for me.

I read this paper fairly carefully, and I’m a bit torn. The main idea — adding lightweight task-specific projection vectors on top of a shared attention module to reduce interference in class-incremental learning — is sensible, and I do think the paper is aiming at a real problem. The motivation in the introduction is fine, and the distinction between “almost zero forgetting” in task-IL vs the much harder class-IL setting is accurate. In my experience reviewing CL papers, a lot of work hand-waves this point, so I appreciated that.

That said, I found the technical presentation somewhat slippery. The method description around Eq. (3)–(5) is not as clear as it needs to be. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure what exactly is task-conditioned at train time versus inference time, and whether the task projection vectors require task identity explicitly or are inferred implicitly from the input. This matters a lot for class-IL. If task labels are used in any way at test time, the setting changes substantially. The wording on p. 4, around the paragraph introducing the “shared task-attention module,” is especially ambiguous.

The empirical section is stronger than the method section. Table 1 and Table 2 show fairly consistent gains over rehearsal baselines, and the calibration / recency-bias discussion is interesting. I liked that the authors did not only report average accuracy. Figure 3 is also useful, although I wish the visualization had been tied more directly to a quantitative claim about interference reduction rather than serving as a qualitative sanity check. I might be wrong, but some of the gains look modest relative to stronger modern replay methods, and I was missing comparisons to a couple of obvious baselines from the last 2–3 years. The paper claims “significantly outperforms rehearsal-based approaches,” but from the tables this feels a bit overstated.

I also worry about scalability claims. “Minimal overhead” is asserted several times (abstract, and again near the end of p. 6), but I wanted an actual parameter/FLOP breakdown, ideally as the number of tasks grows. Without that, the claim is a little cheap.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it may well be useful. But the paper needs sharper clarification of the class-IL protocol, stronger baseline coverage, and a more honest accounting of overhead. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject in its current form.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of offline-to-online RL papers I’ve read recently. The core idea is quite simple: keep the pessimistic learning objective fixed across offline pretraining and online fine-tuning, but inject optimism only at action selection time. In principle this is appealing, because it avoids the awkward “objective switch” that many prior methods make. I liked that framing, and Figure 1 (if I understood it correctly) does a decent job motivating the mismatch between pessimistic offline training and exploratory online behavior.

That said, I’m a bit torn on how much of this is actually a conceptual advance versus a fairly sensible engineering choice. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, my impression is that the method mostly amounts to sampling/selecting actions with higher predicted Q under the learned critic while leaving training unchanged. That is not nothing, but the paper occasionally sells this as more surprising than it is. Eq. (4) is basically the whole method, and I wish the authors were more upfront about how sensitive this is to Q overestimation and critic miscalibration. In offline RL, those are not side issues.

The empirical results are pretty solid overall. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, and I appreciated that the paper includes locomotion, navigation, and manipulation rather than just D4RL MuJoCo again and again. The reported “56% more returns on average” from the abstract seems supported directionally, though averages across tasks in RL can hide a lot. On some tasks (I think the manipulation setting in Figure 4?), the gains looked modest or noisy. I would have liked confidence intervals to be more prominent; some plots are hard to parse.

One thing I found confusing is the exact comparison to baselines that also alter exploration during fine-tuning. Around page 6, the distinction between your method and simply increasing actor entropy / action noise is explained, but not totally convincingly. In my experience, these details matter a lot, and I’m not entirely sure the ablations isolate the claimed mechanism. The paper needs a stronger discussion of when optimistic action selection helps versus when it just exploits critic errors.

Still, I think the paper has value. It is clean, easy to implement, and likely useful. My overall feeling is positive but not enthusiastic: good idea, decent evidence, slightly overstated novelty. I’d lean weak accept if the conference has room for practical RL papers, though I do think the authors should tone down some claims and clarify robustness.

I had a somewhat frustrating time with this paper. The general motivation is reasonable — icon recognition on tiny, low-texture images is a real problem, and I’ve seen similar issues in UI understanding and document parsing work — but the paper, at least in its current form, reads more like a bundle of fashionable techniques than a coherent contribution.

The biggest issue is that I could not pin down what is actually novel here. From the abstract and the method section (especially around pp. 3–5, assuming I’m reading the intended core correctly), the paper combines a Siamese backbone, attention, multi-scale features, contrastive learning, adversarial training, dynamic margins, self-supervised pretraining, and NAS. That is a lot. Too much, honestly. When a paper throws in this many ingredients, I expect extremely careful ablations, and I did not see convincing evidence that each component matters or even that the final recipe is stable. Table 2 and Table 3 seem intended to address this, but after reading them twice I still found the comparisons under-specified. Are these cumulative ablations? Are hyperparameters retuned after each addition? It’s not clear.

I was also confused by the task definition. The title says “detection,” but the abstract and, from what I gather, much of the setup sound more like pairwise matching or recognition/retrieval. Figure 1 gives a high-level pipeline, but it does not resolve whether the model localizes icons in larger images or simply classifies cropped icons. That distinction matters a lot. If this is really detection, where are the detection metrics and baselines? If it is verification/metric learning, then the framing is misleading.

Another concern is evaluation realism. The paper claims “significant improvements,” but I could not judge how meaningful the gains are without stronger baselines. Why not compare against straightforward modern small-image classifiers or metric-learning methods specialized for low-res inputs? The face-recognition analogy is plausible, but icon data are much more synthetic and structured; I’m not entirely sure the transfer of ideas is as deep as the paper suggests.

There are also writing problems. Several claims are broad and hand-wavy, especially near the end of the introduction and around the discussion of dynamic margins. I kept wanting a precise equation or implementation detail and instead got another conceptual paragraph. Equation references (if Eq. 3 is the margin formulation I think it is) need more explanation.

Overall, I think the problem is interesting, but the paper feels undercooked and overstuffed. My leaning is reject in its current form. A tighter paper with one or two real ideas, a cleaner task definition, and much stronger ablations would be much easier to support.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of diffusion-for-X papers I’ve seen lately. The core idea — separating anomaly-specific tuning from normal-image tuning while sharing the U-Net — makes intuitive sense for industrial data, where the object identity is stable and the defects are sparse and weird. I liked the observation in the abstract and later discussion (I think around p. 3) that defects vary much more than product structure; that part felt grounded in actual factory imagery rather than just benchmark-chasing.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as clearly justified as the paper suggests. The UA prompt design (product token + multiple anomaly tokens) is plausible, but the mechanism by which different anomaly tokens reliably disentangle shape/texture/appearance is a bit hand-wavy. Eq. (6) and Eq. (7) for the DA/NA losses are presented cleanly, but I had trouble mapping them to the claimed compositional behavior. Figure 2 helps somewhat, but I wanted a more explicit ablation: what exactly breaks if you use one anomaly token, or if you do joint rather than separated fine-tuning? Table 3 only partially answers this.

The downstream gains are impressive on paper, especially the +11.17% and +15.49% IoU numbers, but I’m a little cautious here. In my experience, augmentation papers often look strongest when the baseline segmentation setup is under-tuned. The paper compares on “three common segmentation methods” (Table 5/6), but I’m not sure the training budgets are fully matched. Also, some of the generated masks in Figure 5 look very sharp, maybe too sharp, compared to real defect boundaries. I might be wrong, but this makes me wonder whether the annotation generation pipeline is benefiting from artifacts of the generative model rather than true defect localization.

Another issue is evaluation. IS and IC-LPIPS are okay, but for industrial anomaly generation they are not enough. FID is also imperfect, sure, but some realism metric or human study would help. The paper repeatedly claims “never-seen anomalies,” which is a strong claim. I did not see convincing evidence beyond visual samples.

Still, I think there is a solid paper in here. The practical setting is important, and the method is more thoughtful than average. My overall feeling is positive but not enthusiastic: good idea, useful results, but the empirical validation and explanation need tightening. I’d lean weak accept if the conference has room for application-driven generative work; otherwise borderline.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea — turning a neural network into an abstract simplicial complex and then comparing networks through persistent homology signatures — is interesting, and honestly kind of refreshing compared to the usual representation-similarity papers that just recycle CKA or SVCCA. I’ve seen a fair bit of TDA-for-ML work over the years, and this at least feels like it is trying to say something structural rather than purely functional. That said, after reading the paper twice, I still found the formulation and empirical story underdeveloped.

The main construction on p.3–4 is the heart of the paper, but it is not explained as cleanly as it needs to be. In Eq. (2), if I understand correctly, the filtration is induced by edge weights (or some transformed version of them), but the choice seems fairly arbitrary. I might be wrong, but I don’t see a convincing argument that this filtration is canonical or even stable under the kinds of reparameterizations we know neural networks admit. For instance, simple neuron permutations or layerwise rescalings can leave the function unchanged while altering the simplicial complex substantially. The paper gestures at “structural similarity,” but then what exactly is being measured? Architecture? Trained function? Optimization history? These are not the same thing.

I also struggled with the experiments. Figure 2 and Table 1 show separation between architectures, but this is not yet enough to establish that the descriptor is useful. In some sense, if one encodes layer connectivity, of course different architectures will differ topologically. The more interesting question is whether the method captures meaningful variation within an architecture family, across random seeds, training stages, or dataset shifts. The results on p.6 are suggestive, but too small-scale. I kept wanting a comparison against embarrassingly simple baselines: parameter histograms, spectral summaries of weight matrices, graph kernels, even just distance between degree distributions. Without that, it is hard to know whether PH is doing real work here or just adding mathematical ceremony.

There are also some presentation issues. Figure 1 is intuitive, but Figures 3 and 4 are pretty hard to parse, at least for me. The discussion around line 168–185 is vague, and the claims of “effectiveness” in the abstract feel stronger than what the evidence supports.

Still, I don’t want to dismiss it outright. I think there is a real seed of an idea here, and I appreciate the attempt to bring TDA into network comparison in a concrete way. But in its current form, the paper feels more like a promising workshop paper than a top-conference submission. My leaning is reject, mostly due to insufficient validation and lack of clarity about invariances and what notion of similarity is actually being captured.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, kind of provocative in a good way. The core idea—detecting whether a model is lying by asking a fixed bank of unrelated yes/no follow-ups—is unusual enough that I was initially skeptical, but the empirical results are stronger than I expected. Figure 1 communicates the setup clearly, and Table 1 / Table 2 (especially the cross-model generalization) are the main reason I take the paper seriously. If those numbers hold up, this is a pretty striking result.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some uncertainty about what exactly is being detected. The paper repeatedly uses “lie” in the sense of “false statement produced when incentivized despite knowing the truth,” but in the actual experiments the operationalization seems much closer to “the model is in a prompted deceptive mode.” Those are not the same thing. I might be wrong, but a lot hinges on this distinction. In particular, the detector may be picking up a conversational/distributional artifact induced by the lying prompt rather than anything like deception per se. The discussion around this on p. 3–4 felt a bit too quick.

Relatedly, I wanted a stronger control: if you prompt the model to answer randomly, role-play, be sarcastic, or deliberately hedge, does the detector still fire? The sycophancy and sales settings help, yes, but they don’t fully rule out that the classifier is learning a generic “abnormal dialogue state.” In my experience, black-box behavioral probes often look more general than they are until you add these annoying controls.

I also found the methodological details a bit underexplained in places. Around the classifier construction (I think this is Sec. 2 / around Eq. 1), I wanted more about feature stability, question bank selection, and sensitivity to wording. How predefined is “predefined”? Was there tuning on the evaluation settings, even informally? The simplicity is appealing, but it also makes me suspicious. Logistic regression on yes/no answers should not work this well unless there is a fairly strong artifact somewhere.

Still, I don’t want to be too negative. The cross-architecture transfer is genuinely impressive, and the black-box setting matters a lot. This is much more practically relevant than the activation-based lie-detection papers people have been excited about lately. I also appreciated that the paper does not oversell mechanistic understanding.

Overall, I lean positive. The paper has a clever idea, strong empirical signal, and clear potential impact. But I really think the authors need to tighten the claim language and add stronger baselines/controls before I’d fully buy “lie detection” rather than “deception-prompt detection.”

I think this is a useful paper, and honestly probably more useful than it is methodologically novel. The main contribution is the resource, not some new algorithm, and I’m mostly fine with that. After reading it twice, my overall take is that MetaShift could become a genuinely practical benchmark for robustness work, especially because it makes shifts more interpretable than the usual train/test split games people play.

What I liked most is the construction idea itself. Using Visual Genome relations/attributes to define subsets like “cat with sink” versus “cat on sofa” is intuitive, and Figure 2 (if I’m reading it right) conveys this well. The scale is also impressive: 12,868 sets over 410 classes is not trivial, and it addresses a real limitation of prior OOD benchmarks that basically hard-code a handful of shifts and call it a day. I also appreciated the attempt to define pairwise shift magnitude via metadata-derived distance; that’s the kind of thing people have wanted for a while.

That said, I found some parts underspecified. The dataset construction section around pages 4–5 is a bit too hand-wavy for something that will likely be used by many others. Exactly how noisy are the Visual Genome annotations after filtering? How often do contexts collapse into near-duplicates? I wasn’t fully convinced by the discussion around the subset-merging/pruning rules (I think around Eq. 1 / the graph construction), and I worry the benchmark may partially reflect annotation artifacts more than “natural” contextual shifts. Maybe that’s unavoidable, but the paper should be more candid.

The benchmarking section is interesting but also a little underwhelming. Table 1 and the later robustness comparisons mostly show ERM is hard to beat under moderate shift, and nobody wins consistently under large shift. That result is believable, maybe even important, but the experimental narrative feels somewhat diffuse. I kept wanting a sharper claim: is MetaShift exposing failures of robustness methods, or just confirming what many of us already suspect? In my experience reviewing OOD papers, benchmark design can dominate conclusions, and I’m not entirely sure the paper disentangles that.

I also wasn’t convinced by the “training conflicts” analysis. The visualization is nice, but the causal story is weak. Are these true optimization conflicts, or just semantically different subsets with low transfer? There’s a difference.

Still, I lean positive. The paper fills a real benchmark gap, and I can imagine many follow-up papers using it. I just think the authors should tighten the construction details and temper some of the broader interpretive claims.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea—making the search procedure itself differentiable/learned rather than hand-specifying selection / expansion / backup—is interesting, and honestly it is the kind of direction many of us have expected since the first wave of “neuralized” planning papers. I liked the ambition. The architecture in Fig. 1 is suggestive, and the high-level decomposition into “where / what / how to search” is clean. But after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper really delivers on the promise in a way that is either methodologically sharp or empirically persuasive.

My main issue is that the connection to actual MCTS feels loose. On p. 2–3, and especially around Eq. (1)–(4), the paper describes tree expansion, evaluation, and backup over latent embeddings, but this is not really Monte Carlo tree search in the usual sense. There is no meaningful Monte Carlo estimation happening, at least not in the standard rollout/statistical sense, and the “tree” seems heavily amortized into learned modules. That may be fine, but then I think the paper should be more upfront that this is a learned recurrent search-like architecture inspired by MCTS, rather than something that would teach us how to replace MCTS itself. The name MCTSnet is catchy, maybe too catchy.

I also found the experimental section underwhelming. Sokoban is a reasonable domain, but the setup on p. 5 and Table 1 is quite narrow. The claim that the learned search “significantly outperformed MCTS baselines” needs much more context. How much tuning did the baselines get? This matters a lot. In my experience, weakly tuned MCTS in Sokoban can look surprisingly bad. I could not tell whether the comparison is against a strong implementation with sensible rollout/value heuristics, or a strawman with a small search budget. Figure 3 shows improvement with more simulations, but I wanted variance bars, more seeds, and frankly stronger non-neural search baselines.

There are also some clarity problems. The backup operator description around p. 4 was hard to parse; I’m not entirely sure what is shared across simulation steps and what is depth-specific. The training signal is also a bit slippery—supervised on action labels? value targets? end-to-end returns? I might be wrong, but the paper seems to slide between these.

Overall, I think the paper has an intriguing idea, and I appreciate the attempt to learn search computation itself. But the current version feels more like an interesting prototype than a solid conference paper. I’d lean weak reject unless the authors can better justify the MCTS framing and substantially strengthen the empirical case.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit overdue for the field. A lot of verification work has stayed in the ReLU comfort zone for too long, so I appreciate the attempt to build a BaB framework that really handles general nonlinearities instead of forcing everything through piecewise-linear approximations. The core idea—general branching points with potentially more than two branches—makes sense, and the paper does a decent job motivating why the usual ReLU-style split heuristics break down once you move to sigmoid/tanh/multiplication. The examples around multi-dimensional nonlinearities were especially useful. I think Fig. 1 and the discussion around Eq. (6)-(8) made the intended abstraction fairly clear.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the presentation is rough in places. The main framework is conceptually simple, but the paper sometimes hides it under too much notation. I found the transition around p.5–6 (where the generic branching formulation gets instantiated for specific nonlinearities) harder to follow than it should be. Maybe this is just me, but I kept wanting one concrete running example all the way through. The BBPS heuristic is probably the most practically important contribution, yet its intuition felt under-explained. On p.7, the scoring rule seems plausible, but I’m not entirely sure why it should generalize well across such different operators. It looks partly engineered from empirical behavior rather than derived from a strong principle. That’s not fatal, but the paper should be more candid about it.

The empirical section is broad, which I liked. Verifying Sigmoid, Tanh, sine, GeLU, LSTMs, ViTs, and even ACOPF is ambitious. Table 2 and Table 3 are impressive at first glance, though I wish there were clearer ablations isolating “general branching” versus “better heuristic” versus just “more compute”. Some of the wins seem substantial, but the cost side is not discussed enough. In my experience, BaB papers can look great until you ask how node counts and wall-clock scale with slightly larger models. Fig. 4 hints at this, but not convincingly.

I also worry a bit about novelty at the algorithmic level. The framework is useful, yes, but parts of it feel like a natural extension of existing BaB + bound propagation ideas rather than a fundamentally new verification paradigm. Still, useful matters. And this is more useful than many papers I’ve seen in this area.

Overall I lean positive. Not a flawless paper, and the writing needs tightening, but I think the contribution is real and the scope is broader than most verification submissions.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of recent zero-shot VAD submissions. The core idea — separating “typical” anomalies from scene-specific “unique” ones — makes intuitive sense, and the language-guided semantic projection is a reasonably fresh angle for skeleton-based ZS-VAD. I also appreciate that the paper is not just doing another reconstruction/prediction variant with “zero-shot” loosely claimed. The motivation on page 2 is clear enough, and Figure 1 helps.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical novelty and, more importantly, by the evidence. The typicality module feels like a CLIP-style semantic alignment adapted to skeleton snippets, which is fine, but the paper sometimes overstates how principled this is. Around Eq. (3)–(6), I had trouble understanding what exactly is learned beyond a supervised alignment to text prompts for normal/abnormal action categories. If that’s the main contribution, then the burden is on the experiments to show that the semantic space really transfers better than a strong skeleton encoder with metric learning. I did not see that ablation in Table 3 (unless I missed it). The comparisons are mostly against prior ZS-VAD systems, but I wanted at least one very strong non-language skeleton baseline adapted to the same setup.

The uniqueness analysis module is also a bit fuzzy in presentation. On page 5, the “test-time context uniqueness” idea sounds appealing, but the formulation around Eq. (8) and the associated description is surprisingly hard to parse. I’m not entirely sure whether this is essentially a nearest-neighbor style scoring in a contextualized feature space, or something more novel. Figure 3 could have been much clearer. In my experience, when a method needs this much prose to explain a test-time scoring rule, it usually means the formulation is less clean than it should be.

The empirical results are good, yes. Table 1 is strong. But UCF-Crime especially makes me uneasy because skeleton extraction quality there is highly variable, and the paper does not really discuss failure modes. Also, page 7 claims robustness to domain shift, but I wanted qualitative examples where “typical” fails and “uniqueness” rescues the decision. The current visualizations are too selective.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with practical value, but not a slam dunk. My leaning is weak accept if the bar is application-driven, weak reject if the bar is methodological clarity/novelty. Right now I’m in between.

I read this paper with interest because I’ve seen a lot of constrained RL submissions over the years, and this one is at least trying to change the object being constrained rather than repackage CMDPs again. The core idea—putting constraints on the state density / occupancy measure directly—is, in spirit, quite natural. In fact my first reaction was “why don’t more papers formulate it this way?” So I do think there is a real conceptual contribution here.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as “pioneering” as claimed in the abstract. Occupancy-measure views of RL and constrained control are old, and some of the duality story on pp. 3–5 felt more like a repackaging of known LP duality than a fundamentally new theorem. Equation (6) and the derivation around Theorem 1 are probably the technical heart, but I found the presentation too compressed. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure what assumptions are actually needed for the duality to hold beyond the finite/state-action setting implicitly suggested by the notation. If this is limited to tabular MDPs, that should be stated much more plainly early on. If not, the function-approximation story is underdeveloped.

I also struggled with the algorithmic novelty. The primal-dual update in Algorithm 1 looks fairly standard, and the practical distinctions from existing Lagrangian CRL methods are not sharp enough in the paper. The authors argue that density constraints are easier to specify than reward/cost shaping, which I agree with in some domains, but in others specifying a feasible density set is actually harder. Figure 2 makes the intuition nice, but the paper oversells universality here.

Empirically, the results are promising but a bit thin for the strength of the claims. Table 1 and Table 2 show improvements, but I wanted stronger evidence that the gains come from the density formulation itself rather than implementation details or hyperparameter tuning. Baselines in constrained RL are notoriously sensitive. I could not find enough detail on tuning budgets (maybe I missed it in the appendix). Also, the benchmark suite is okay, not amazing. For a paper making broad claims about safety/resource constraints, I expected at least one more realistic continuous-control setting.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with an interesting angle, but not yet a fully convincing top-tier one. I lean positive on originality of framing, but I have reservations about technical clarity and the empirical case. My score would be around borderline accept / weak accept.

I found the paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea — compressing the growing KV cache into a learned compact memory for online interaction — is timely and practically relevant. A lot of recent long-context work still quietly assumes either recomputation or just bigger memory budgets, so I appreciate that this paper is actually targeting the inference-time systems bottleneck. In particular, the conditional LoRA-based compression mechanism (introduced around Sec. 3, especially Eq. (4)–(7)) is a nice design choice: lightweight, easy to slot into an existing model, and more realistic than methods that require full-model adaptation.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates what is genuinely new from what is a fairly straightforward combination of known ingredients. “Compress KV into memory + train a small module to preserve downstream behavior” is intuitively sensible, but the novelty over prior memory-token / recurrent-memory / KV-eviction style papers felt thinner than the paper claims. The related work section on p. 2–3 is a bit too quick here. I kept wanting a more explicit comparison to prior recurrent memory transformers and recent cache compression methods, not just conceptually but operationally.

The empirical results are solid but also somewhat selective. Table 1 and Table 2 look good, especially the claim of matching full-context performance with 5x smaller memory, but I would have liked more stress-testing on harder retrieval-heavy settings. The current conversation/personalization/multi-task setup is reasonable, but these are also domains where semantic summarization can work unusually well. I’m not entirely sure the method would hold up when exact token-level details matter. Figure 3 suggests graceful degradation as memory shrinks, which is encouraging, though the axes/normalization were oddly hard to parse.

One thing I did like a lot is the “single parallelized forward computation” trick for training the recursive compressor (Sec. 3.3). That is clever and probably the most technically valuable part of the paper in my opinion. But the paper undersells the computational tradeoffs: what is the actual wall-clock overhead from inserting conditional LoRA during inference? The memory savings are clear; the latency story is less so. I looked for this in Table 3 / appendix and didn’t find enough.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper with practical value, but it feels a little under-argued and slightly over-claimed. Borderline accept for me, mostly because the problem matters and the method seems usable in practice.

I went into this somewhat skeptical, because “can we just use the LLaMA decoder for images too?” is the kind of idea that sounds cleaner architecturally than it often works in practice. After reading the paper twice, I think the paper is interesting and the empirical story is stronger than I expected, but I’m still not fully convinced by some of the central claims.

The most useful part for me is actually the step-by-step “LLaMAfication” of ViT around pp. 3–5. The ablations that isolate FFN, norm, causal attention, and positional embedding changes are helpful, and the discussion of attention collapse is probably the main technical contribution here. I appreciated Fig. 3 and the rank analysis later in the paper (I think around Fig. 6?), because they at least try to explain why naïvely replacing bidirectional attention with causal attention fails. The post-sequence class token is a neat trick. In hindsight it feels obvious, which is usually a good sign.

That said, I found the soft mask idea under-explained. Eq. (8) looks simple enough, but the schedule and why it works are not really justified beyond “optimization becomes easier.” Maybe that is the truth, honestly, but then say so more plainly. I also wasn’t entirely sure whether the gains come from the architectural consistency with LLaMA or just from introducing a curriculum on the attention mask. Table 2/3 suggest both matter, but the causal narrative in the paper is a bit too tidy for the evidence.

The efficiency claim also needs more care. Yes, causal attention reduces computation in principle, but for image classification with a class token at the end, I’m not sure the practical wall-clock story is demonstrated convincingly. I saw complexity discussion, but not enough real throughput/latency measurements under comparable kernels. In my experience, these details matter a lot and papers often oversell them.

On the experimental side, ImageNet results are solid, especially 75.1% at 5.7M params and 86.0% with scaling/pretraining. The extra analyses on calibration, quantization, and shape-texture bias are nice, though they felt a little like “and here are some other things we checked” rather than a coherent argument. ADE20K transfer is appreciated, but I would have liked stronger dense prediction evidence, since causal masking can easily hurt spatial tasks.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, maybe not a slam dunk. The idea is simple but nontrivial, the results are credible, and the paper has some real insight. But parts of the explanation feel post hoc, and the standardization/unification motivation is a bit grandiose. I lean positive.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of “efficient training” submissions I’ve seen lately. The focus on emerging NVM constraints is real, and I appreciate that the authors are not just doing generic low-rank fine-tuning and rebranding it for hardware. The four constraints listed in the abstract do show up throughout the paper, and on p. 2–3 the discussion of write cost/endurance is a useful framing. In my experience, many papers in this area hand-wave the device model; this one at least tries to connect algorithmic choices to memory behavior.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the technical novelty. The core idea—restricting updates to a low-rank form—felt pretty close to existing low-rank adaptation / factorized-update methods, even if the hardware angle is different. After reading the method section twice, I still wasn’t sure what is fundamentally new beyond applying a known parameterization to online/edge training with quantization constraints. Eq. (3) and Eq. (5) seem to be the key update rules, but the paper moves a bit too quickly from formulation to claims about update sparsity/density. Low-rank does not automatically mean “hardware friendly” in the way the authors sometimes imply. Depending on how the factors are materialized, one could still induce fairly broad writes. Figure 2 gestures at this, but I wanted a more explicit accounting.

The experimental section is solid but also a bit narrow. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains over SGD in both accuracy and number of weight updates, which is the main empirical claim, and that’s good. But the baselines are weak. Why only standard SGD? If the paper is really about constrained edge adaptation, I would expect comparisons to sparse updates, sign-based methods, quantization-aware fine-tuning, maybe even LoRA-style baselines adapted to CNNs. Without that, the “out-performs standard SGD” result is less impressive than the authors seem to think. Also, on p. 6 the online learning setup is not described carefully enough for me to judge whether hyperparameters were equally tuned across methods. This matters a lot.

I did like Figure 4; it makes the endurance/accuracy tradeoff tangible. But I’m missing ablations on rank choice and auxiliary memory budget. The paper repeatedly claims low auxiliary memory, yet I couldn’t find a clean table breaking down optimizer state, factor storage, and temporary buffers. Maybe I missed it.

Overall, I think this is promising and relevant, but the paper currently overclaims novelty and under-explains the hardware realism. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values practical systems-oriented ML, otherwise weak reject.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea—augmenting OT with a mutual information term so the coupling respects cluster/coherence structure—is intuitively appealing, and honestly this is the kind of thing people have wanted from vanilla OT for a while. Plain OT is notoriously brittle when geometry is misleading, and the outlier issue the authors mention is very real in practice. So the motivation landed for me.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as clean as it wants to be. The objective around Eq. (4) is the conceptual center of the paper, but the exact role of the MI term versus the transport cost felt under-explained. I might be wrong, but it seems the method depends heavily on the balance hyperparameter, and I did not see enough discussion of sensitivity. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains, yes, but without a more systematic ablation on that tradeoff, it’s hard to know whether InfoOT is robust or just carefully tuned. Figure 2 is suggestive, not decisive.

I did like the generalized OT framing. That part is clever. The projected gradient scheme also seems reasonable, though I wish the authors were more transparent about computational overhead. On p. 5 they claim efficiency, but compared to what exactly? Sinkhorn OT? GW? Some runtimes or scaling curves would help a lot. In my experience, these “still efficient” claims often hide nontrivial constants, and here the repeated MI-related updates do not look cheap.

The projection/generalization-to-unseen-samples angle is potentially quite useful, especially for retrieval and domain adaptation. But this part also felt underspecified. The mechanism on p. 6 (around lines 210–235) reads more like a recipe than a well-justified estimator. Why should this particular projection preserve the desirable properties of the learned coupling? I wanted either a theorem or at least a stronger empirical stress test.

Empirically, the single-cell results are probably the strongest part of the paper. The retrieval/domain adaptation experiments are okay, though some baselines felt a bit dated. I was surprised not to see stronger modern contrastive or OT-regularized baselines in the main table. Maybe they are in the appendix, but if so, that weakens the main claim.

Overall, I think there is a good idea here, and parts of it are novel. But the paper currently overclaims a bit. I’m leaning positive but only weakly: accept if the bar is novelty + promising results, not if the expectation is a fully nailed-down method.

I went into this paper a bit skeptical because “yet another linear attention with better approximation” is a pretty crowded space, but I do think the control variates angle is more interesting than the usual kernel-trick repackaging. The main idea, as I understand it from Sec. 3, is that the random-feature attention estimator can be rewritten as a sum of per-token control variate estimators, and this gives a clean way to reason about the bias/variance gap to exact softmax. Equation (7) is the conceptual center of the paper for me. After reading that part twice, I think the decomposition is elegant, and the statement around exact recovery by “manipulating each control variate” is at least intellectually appealing.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as clear as it should be. The exposition around pages 4–5 gets pretty dense, and I found the jump from the decomposition to the proposed estimator somewhat abrupt. In particular, the assumptions needed for the variance reduction argument are not stated crisply enough. I might be wrong here, but some of the claims around unbiasedness vs. approximation quality seem to blur together. Equation (11) and the discussion immediately after it needed more care. Also, Fig. 2 is visually helpful, but it abstracts away exactly the part I wanted spelled out: what is being estimated, what is cached, and where the linear complexity is preserved without hidden constants exploding.

Empirically, the results are solid, maybe the strongest part of the paper. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains over prior efficient attention baselines, and I appreciate that the authors included both vision and language rather than overfitting the story to one domain. But the comparisons are not quite as airtight as I’d like. Some baselines seem under-tuned, and the compute budget discussion on page 8 is thin. In my experience reviewing efficient attention papers, wall-clock and memory numbers often tell a less flattering story than asymptotics, and here those measurements feel a bit selective. I also wanted a more direct ablation on the number/form of control variates; Table 3 only partially answers that.

Overall, I think this is a meaningful paper with a real theoretical insight, not just benchmark chasing. But it needs sharper writing and a more careful empirical accounting before I’d feel fully enthusiastic. Tentatively positive, though not by a huge margin.

I thought this was a solid paper overall, and in some sense overdue. A lot of graph work treats DAGs as just another graph class and then throws generic message passing at them, so I do appreciate the attempt to build the partial order directly into the architecture rather than hoping the network figures it out. The idea is intuitive, and the paper is generally readable. I had little trouble following the main construction on first pass, which is honestly not always true for GNN papers.

What I liked most is that the paper is not just “run a topological order and call it a day.” The discussion around the design components of DAGNN, especially the separation between forward/backward flow and the role of aggregation/update choices, felt more thoughtful than many architecture papers. Figure 1 does a decent job motivating the setting, and I found the formulation around Eq. (3)–(5) fairly clean. The claim that prior tree/recurrent-on-graph models fall out as special cases is plausible, though I think the paper oversells this a bit. After reading that section twice, I’m still not entirely sure the unification is as deep as advertised versus mostly a broad template.

The experiments are pretty comprehensive by conference standards. I liked the diversity of domains. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, and the ablations were useful rather than decorative. In particular, the comparison to generic GNNs on DAG inputs is important and, in my experience, often omitted in these papers because the result is inconvenient. Here it’s front and center, which I respect.

That said, I do have reservations. The novelty is moderate. Once you decide to exploit topological order, some form of directional dynamic programming/message passing is the obvious thing to try. So the burden is on the authors to really isolate what is essential. I’m not sure they fully do that. Several components seem bundled together, and the paper sometimes shifts between “framework” and “specific architecture” when convenient. Also, the computational discussion felt too light to me; for a method tied to DAG ordering, I wanted a clearer treatment of parallelism/efficiency tradeoffs, maybe around the section introducing the layer computation (I think this was around p. 4–5). The practical cost relative to standard MPNNs is not very transparent.

I also wonder how robust the advantage is when the DAG is noisy or when edge directions are only partially meaningful. Maybe that’s outside scope, but it matters for real applications.

Still, I think this is a worthwhile paper. Not a huge conceptual leap, but a careful one, and the empirical case is pretty convincing. I’d lean accept, though not enthusiastically.

I came away pretty unconvinced by this paper, though I do think the problem is important and under-served. Children’s speech recognition/screening is genuinely difficult, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to build something broader than yet another ASR benchmark model. In principle, a multi-task system plus an alignment pipeline could be useful. But after reading this twice, I still had trouble pinning down exactly what the core technical contribution is versus a combination of dataset curation, standard fine-tuning, and broad claims.

A lot hinges on FASA, yet the presentation is oddly vague. The claim in the abstract that alignment quality is improved “13.6× compared to human annotations” is, frankly, hard to believe as written. On p. 4–5 (around the method description), I could not find a clean operational definition of this metric. Better than human annotation by a factor of 13.6? That needs extraordinary evidence, and I don’t think Table 2 or the qualitative examples in Fig. 3 get you there. Maybe I missed something, but the evaluation protocol seems partly circular if the same alignment assumptions are used in downstream filtering.

I also found the model side under-specified. The “speech-enhanced Foundation Model” language feels oversold. From Eq. (2) and the training description on p. 6, this looks more like a two-stage encoder + task heads setup with phonetic supervision, which is perfectly reasonable, but not obviously a foundation model in the sense the paper suggests. The four tasks are somewhat heterogeneous, and the average 87% accuracy in Table 3 is not very informative when the label spaces and class balances differ so much. I would have preferred per-task comparisons against strong baselines, with significance or at least variance across seeds. Some tables report gains that are modest, and in a couple of cases the baseline choice seems weak/outdated.

I did like the motivation around pathological and early developmental speech, and I think the CHILDES-based data processing is potentially useful to the community. But the paper mixes screening, ASR, generative modeling, and therapy-oriented claims in a way that feels too ambitious for the actual evidence. The clinical framing especially made me uneasy; on p. 2 and again in the conclusion, the language drifts toward deployment implications without enough discussion of failure modes, demographic coverage, or risk.

So, interesting direction, but for a top ML venue I think this needs much sharper methodology, much more credible validation of FASA, and a more restrained framing. Right now it reads bigger than it is.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than a lot of “real-world RL” papers that are mostly polished demos. The framing is a bit systems-heavy, but I think that is actually the point here: the contribution is not some fancy new algorithm, it is the recipe. In my experience, that kind of paper can be more valuable than another minor policy optimization tweak, assuming the evidence is solid enough.

What I liked most is that the paper is refreshingly concrete about the annoying practical issues people usually sweep under the rug. The discussion around non-episodic data collection and resets (around Sec. 3 / 4, especially the part tied to reset-free operation) felt grounded. Same for the treatment of reward learning without heavy instrumentation. Figure 1 gives a decent overview of the full system, and I appreciated that the authors seem to have actually built the thing rather than just gestured at it. Table 1 and the ablations later in the paper were helpful; the comparisons between different “ingredients” make the paper much stronger than if it were just a single end-to-end success number.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the main novelty is somewhat diffuse. The title promises “ingredients,” and that is indeed what the paper delivers, but this also makes it hard to isolate what is fundamentally new versus what is a competent integration of known ideas. Some of the methods struck me as more like engineering choices than research contributions. I don’t mean that dismissively — engineering matters a lot in robotics — but for a top ML venue I wanted a crisper statement of what is learned here beyond “these pragmatic choices work together.” The paper occasionally slips into that mode.

I was also not fully convinced by the simulation-to-real narrative. The real-world results are encouraging, but the scale still seems limited relative to the broad claims in the abstract. I might be wrong, but I think the paper overstates “continual and autonomous improvement” a bit; from the experiments, I saw a promising prototype of that, not yet a fully convincing demonstration. Some failure cases are mentioned, but I wanted more. Figure 4/5 (assuming I’m remembering correctly) show the success trends, but I would have liked variance across longer deployments, not just headline performance.

One smaller issue: parts of the writing are more high-level than they should be. I occasionally had to hunt for exact implementation details, especially around reward construction and state estimation. Those details matter a lot here.

Overall, I lean positive. This is one of those papers where the ambition and realism count for a lot, and I do think the community needs more papers like this. But I also think the paper should be judged as a strong systems/empirical contribution, not as a major conceptual advance in RL.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more careful than I expected from the title alone. The core claim — that learned local divisive normalization over feature channels improves AlexNet performance, usually by ~1–2% on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet — is plausible and mostly supported by the experiments. I liked that the authors didn’t stop at top-1 accuracy and tried to connect the behavior to manifold capacity, sparsity, and first-layer RF structure. That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper is somewhat narrow and the presentation is messier than it should be.

The main issue for me is novelty. Divisive normalization is of course a very old idea in computational neuroscience and also not new in deep nets. The specific choice here — arranging features in a line topology with exponential distance-dependent interactions — is reasonable, but I’m not convinced the paper makes a strong case for why this particular parameterization is the right one, beyond convenience. I might be wrong, but Eq. (presumably the normalization equation in the methods section, around p. 3) seemed under-motivated. Why a 1D feature ordering at all? Channels in AlexNet are not naturally topographic unless you enforce that somehow. This felt a bit arbitrary.

I also think the empirical scope is limited. Everything is AlexNet. In 2025 that is a tough sell. If the effect is real and robust, I would expect at least one modern architecture, even a small ResNet or ConvNeXt-style baseline. Without that, it’s hard to know whether this is saying something general about normalization or something idiosyncratic about old convolutional pipelines. Table 1 and Table 2 (I assume these are the CIFAR/ImageNet results) are encouraging, but the gains are not huge, and I wanted error bars or multiple seeds everywhere, not just point estimates. A 1% gain on ImageNet can evaporate quickly.

Some of the analysis sections were intriguing but also a little “kitchen sink.” The manifold capacity discussion on the later pages (around Fig. 4?) was hard to interpret causally. Same for the sparsity results: the paper notes divisive normalization increases sparsity while canonical normalization decreases it, yet final-layer sparsity trends don’t line up simply with performance. That’s fine scientifically — reality is messy — but then the mechanistic story should be toned down.

I did like the RF analysis (Fig. 6 maybe), especially the Fourier power shift. That felt concrete. Still, I wanted actual visual examples at higher resolution and maybe a comparison to known Gabor-like structure from unnormalized AlexNet.

Overall, I think this is a solid but not especially exciting paper. Useful empirical result, decent analysis, but limited modern relevance and a somewhat over-claimed mechanistic interpretation. I’d lean weak accept in a niche venue, but for a top ML conference I am closer to borderline / weak reject unless the authors can better justify novelty and generality.

I went into this paper fairly positively because large-scale MIC prediction for Salmonella is a useful problem, and 5,278 genomes across 14 antibiotics is not a toy dataset. Also, I’ve seen a lot of AMR prediction papers recently that just do binary resistant/susceptible classification, so targeting MIC directly is more interesting. That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the methodological contribution is as strong as the authors claim.

The main issue for me is novelty. A “lightweight Multitask Learning Transformer” sounds nice, but on p. 4–5 the actual model description feels pretty standard: embedding heterogeneous genomic features, shared encoder, task-specific heads. Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) are basically the usual multitask objective. I might be missing something, but I did not see a clear technical idea here beyond applying an existing architecture to a relevant biological dataset. The paper leans heavily on performance gains, so then the experimental design has to be airtight, and I’m not sure it is.

In particular, the split strategy is underexplained. If genomes from closely related lineages appear in both train and test, these models can look much better than they really are. This is a recurring problem in AMR genomics, and in my experience it matters a lot more than people want to admit. I was looking for phylogeny-aware or temporal holdout evaluation and did not find it. The results in Table 2 are therefore hard to interpret as evidence of real generalization. The reported >10% raw accuracy gain is impressive on paper, but without a harder split it may just reflect memorization of lineage structure.

I also found the interpretability claims overstated. Figure 4 is suggestive, but highlighting genomic regions or ARG features with high attention/importance is not, by itself, mechanistic understanding. We as a field should really stop equating attention with explanation. Some of the identified signals are biologically plausible, yes, but the validation is weak.

There are also clarity issues. Figure 1 is too compressed and the feature construction pipeline on p. 3 is harder to follow than it should be. I was confused about whether pan-genome presence/absence, SNPs, and ARG profiles are all jointly tokenized or concatenated at different stages. The text around lines 188–205 should be rewritten.

Overall, useful application paper, probably publishable somewhere in bioinformatics, but for a top ML venue I think this is short on methodological novelty and not rigorous enough in evaluation. My score is below the acceptance bar.

I read this paper with some interest because robustness via biologically inspired front-ends is the kind of idea that periodically comes back, and sometimes there is something real there. Here, I’m not fully convinced. The core proposal — an STDP-trained spiking feature extractor prepended to a standard DNN — is intuitive enough, and Fig. 1 gives the high-level picture clearly. But once I got into the details, especially around Sec. 3 and Eq. (4) on p. 5, I found the method description frustratingly under-specified. I still do not have a crisp sense of what exactly is passed from the SNN to the downstream DNN: spike counts? filtered membrane traces? learned convolutional kernels derived from STDP? Different parts of the text seem to suggest different things.

The empirical claim is robustness to “stochastic perturbation of inputs,” but the evaluation feels narrow. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains under the chosen noise models, yes, but I kept asking whether this is really robustness or just a front-end denoiser effect tuned to local corruption. I might be wrong, but the perturbations seem mostly low-level and stationary. There is no serious comparison to stronger and more modern robustness baselines beyond standard DNN variants. In 2025, I would expect at least some connection to adversarial training, randomized smoothing, corruption benchmarks, or even just stronger augmentation-based baselines. As it stands, the comparisons feel a bit dated.

I also found the “without sacrificing accuracy on clean images” claim too strong. The clean accuracy differences in Table 1 are small, but some are within what I’d consider normal run-to-run variation, and I did not see confidence intervals or multiple seeds. That matters here because the reported robustness gains are also not huge in several settings. After reading p. 6 twice, I’m still unsure whether the SNN front-end is frozen after unsupervised training or jointly fine-tuned in any form. That design choice is important and under-discussed.

There is something appealing in the idea of using STDP to induce locality-sensitive features that ignore pixel noise. I’ve seen related neuromorphic papers make similar arguments, though usually with cleaner ablations than what is presented here. The ablation in Fig. 4 is a start, but I really wanted to see sensitivity to spike thresholds, STDP parameters, and front-end depth. Also, computational cost is mostly brushed aside; if this adds substantial training complexity, the practical case weakens considerably.

Overall, I think the paper has a potentially interesting intuition but the current version is not solid enough. Too many implementation/details questions, and the experimental story is not rigorous enough for a top conference. My leaning is reject.

I found the paper interesting and pretty ambitious, but also frustrating in a few important ways. The central claim — that cortical learning can infer the dimensionality of color space from optic nerve fluctuations alone — is a big one, and if true it would be genuinely exciting. I like the overall direction. In particular, the idea of coupling an eye simulation with a self-supervised cortical learner is, in spirit, much closer to the actual developmental problem than the usual “assume the latent color space is known” setup. Figure 1 helped orient me, and I thought the natural eye motion argument was intuitively appealing.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper establishes what it says it establishes. A lot depends on exactly what information is available to the learner and what inductive biases are built in. Around Eq. (3)–(5), the learning objective seems to already privilege temporal stability/invariance structure that may effectively bake in the answer. I might be wrong, but the model appears to recover the dimensionality because the simulation environment was designed so that this factorization is available and clean. That is not the same as showing that cortex “naturally” infers it in any robust sense.

I also struggled with the biological plausibility claims. The text around p. 5, lines 180–210 (if I tracked correctly) says the rule is bio-plausible, but the implementation still looks like a fairly engineered representation learner. In my experience, papers making strong neuroscience claims need to be much more careful here. “Inspired by” would be fine; “bio-plausible” feels overstated.

The evaluation is mixed. Table 1 and the colorimetry analysis are useful, and I appreciated that the authors did not stop at qualitative visualizations. But the “formal verification” language is too strong. The 4D color vision extension, especially the gene-therapy/squirrel monkey discussion near the end, is exactly where I wanted more caution. This part reads a bit like science-fiction-by-simulation. Interesting, yes. Demonstrated, no.

A practical concern: how sensitive are the results to the natural image distribution, eye motion statistics, receptor spectral overlap, and noise model? Figure 4 suggests some robustness, but not enough for the scope of the claims. I would also have liked ablations where the retinal statistics are perturbed in clearly non-biological ways.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I actually enjoyed parts of the paper. But the claims are too broad relative to the evidence. I’d lean weak reject unless the authors substantially tone down the neuroscience conclusions and sharpen what is actually proved in the computational model.

I found this paper interesting and, at points, quite elegant, but also harder to evaluate than it should be. The main idea — replacing the usual focus on KRR by a general spectral profile \(h(\lambda)\) and treating GD/KRR in one framework — is definitely worthwhile. In my experience, a lot of recent theory papers motivate themselves via neural networks and then only analyze ridge, so I appreciate the attempt to close that gap.

The strongest part for me is the asymptotic characterization of the test error as a functional of \(h\). The statements around the power-law regimes are, at least qualitatively, compelling. I especially liked the “localization on spectral scales” discussion (around the middle of the paper; I think this is tied to the theorem after the main asymptotic formula, maybe around pp. 12–14 depending on the version). That gives a more concrete lens on saturation than the usual abstract bias-variance story. Figure 2 was also helpful, though I wish it did more than confirm the theory visually.

That said, I struggled with presentation. After reading this twice, I still found the setup overly dense. The notation around the profile \(h\), the effective resolvent-type quantities, and the two separate data models is introduced too quickly. Equation (3) and the surrounding assumptions are doing a lot of work, and I’m not convinced the paper makes sufficiently clear which conclusions are genuinely universal and which are artifacts of the Gaussian / translation-invariant settings. The universality conjecture is plausible, but right now it feels a bit aspirational. The noisy-vs-noiseless distinction is actually one of the most interesting claims here, yet the intuition for why universality should break in the noiseless case did not fully land for me.

I also think the neural-network motivation is somewhat stronger than what the results really justify. Yes, GD is included as a spectral algorithm, but only in the kernel regime. That’s fine, but the introduction occasionally sounds broader than the technical content.

Technically, the paper seems solid, though I did not check every derivation in the appendices. I might be wrong, but I would have liked a more explicit comparison to prior precise-risk formulas for KRR, ideally in a table, to clarify exactly what is new beyond replacing the ridge profile by general \(h\). As it stands, the contribution is meaningful but a bit diffuse.

Overall, I lean positive because the framework is nontrivial and potentially useful, but I do think the paper needs clearer exposition and slightly more restraint in its claims.

I found this paper interesting, and in a way more ambitious than most of the recent “neural counting” papers I’ve seen. The main idea — canonical partitioning of the target graph into neighborhood graphs, then predicting a canonical count objective locally and aggregating — is, at least to me, the most compelling part of the submission. If I understood Sec. 3 correctly, especially around Eq. (4)–(6), the partition is doing a lot of the heavy lifting: it is what turns a global counting problem into something learnable without obvious double counting. That said, I had to read that section twice. The exposition is not great. Figure 2 helps, but the exact conditions under which a subgraph instance is assigned uniquely to one neighborhood were still a bit slippery to me.

I do think the paper overclaims in a few places. The abstract says “on any given target graph,” and the text around p. 6 seems to suggest fairly broad generalization, but the experiments are still in a relatively controlled regime. In my experience, subgraph counting methods can look excellent on synthetic or semi-synthetic benchmarks and then become very brittle once graph statistics shift even modestly. I would have liked a clearer OOD test: train on one graph family, test on another, or at least vary degree distributions more aggressively. Table 2 is strong, yes, but it mostly shows that the method beats baselines on the authors’ chosen setup.

The heterogeneous GNN piece is plausible but less novel to me than the paper seems to think. The subgraph-based message passing is reasonable, though I’m not entirely sure how much of the gain comes from that versus the canonical partition itself plus the gossip correction. The ablation in Table 4 goes some way toward answering this, but not fully. I wanted either a sharper disentanglement or a simpler baseline with the same partition and no fancy architecture. As written, it feels a bit “throw every idea in.”

Also, the claim of polynomial runtime needs to be handled more carefully. Yes, the learned inference stage may be polynomial, but there is preprocessing, neighborhood extraction, and dependence on query size that is easy to sweep under the rug. Page 8 handwaves this a little.

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The count distribution prediction angle is nice, and the empirical gains are large enough that I can’t dismiss them. My overall reaction is positive but cautious: good paper, somewhat overstated, not always clearly written, but definitely above the bar if the camera-ready tightens the theory/exposition. I’d lean weak accept.

I found the paper interesting in intent, but I’m not convinced the current version really delivers on the “Theory of Mind” claim in the title. After reading it twice, my main reaction is that this is closer to pragmatic reranking with an auxiliary listener than anything I would comfortably call ToM. That may sound nitpicky, but for a paper explicitly framed around child language acquisition, I think the distinction matters.

The core setup—a speaker with an internal listener used to rerank utterances—is clear enough, and the experiments on the referential game are generally sensible. Figure 1 helped, although I wish the training dynamics were spelled out more explicitly there or around p. 3–4. I had to jump back and forth between the model description and the experimental section to understand whether the listener is modeling a specific partner, an average listener, or just serving as an extra training signal. Eq. (2) and the weighting of the ToM term are central, but the paper does not really justify the choice beyond empirical tuning. In my experience, if the effect is highly dependent on a large coefficient on the auxiliary listener, that weakens the cognitive story.

The empirical results are promising but a bit thin. Table 1 shows gains, yes, but I’m not entirely sure they are large enough, or robust enough, to support the broader claims. I would have liked variance across seeds everywhere, not just in some settings (unless I missed it). Also, the “more fluent and precise utterances” claim feels under-evidenced. The examples in the qualitative analysis section are suggestive, but not strong. If this is based on human judgments or some automatic metric, the paper should be much more explicit. Right now it reads a little like the authors wanted that conclusion to be true.

Another issue: the task remains very toy. That’s not fatal, but then the child language acquisition framing on p. 1 feels overextended. Children infer beliefs, intentions, common ground, etc. This model seems to infer “what utterance would maximize listener success.” Useful? Sure. ToM? Maybe in a very weak operational sense. I might be wrong, but I think the paper would be stronger if it toned down the developmental claims and presented itself more honestly as a pragmatic language game model inspired by ToM.

Overall, I think there is a real nugget here, and I like the direction. But the conceptual framing overshoots the evidence, and the experimental support is not yet deep enough for a top conference acceptance from me. Borderline leaning reject.

I found the paper interesting and honestly a bit frustrating. The core idea — that a learner can improve its own objective by changing the future input distribution in undesirable ways — is important, and I do think the “auto-induced distributional shift” framing is useful. People in recommender systems and performative prediction have been circling this issue for a while, but the paper does make it concrete in a way that could be valuable. The abstract claim about meta-learning “revealing” hidden incentives is plausible, and Figure 1 (if I understood it correctly) does a decent job motivating the setup.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully clear on what is genuinely new beyond repackaging known feedback-loop / performative effects under a new term. The distinction between ADS and HI-ADS seems central, but the paper’s definitions felt slippery. In particular, around the formal setup (I think this was around Eq. 2–4), I wanted a much sharper statement of when an incentive counts as “hidden” versus simply not exploited by a weaker optimizer. Right now it reads a little like: if a stronger learner finds a bad strategy, then the incentive was hidden. Maybe that is the point, but then the contribution is more diagnostic than conceptual.

I also struggled with the empirical section. The toy recommendation environment is fine as a first pass, but it is very toy. Table 1 and the corresponding discussion make fairly strong claims from a narrow synthetic setup with hand-designed user dynamics. In my experience, these dynamics determine everything. Small modeling choices can flip whether “driving users away” is instrumentally useful. So I’m not convinced by the broader implications. The unit tests are a nice idea, but they also feel somewhat bespoke; I’m not entirely sure they would generalize outside the exact environment class considered here.

The mitigation strategy is probably the most practically interesting part, but the presentation was too quick. I wanted ablations. What exactly is sacrificed when the mitigation is turned on? The paper says the methods “pass” the tests, but at what cost in reward, adaptation speed, robustness, etc.? Without that, it feels a bit like teaching to the test.

Overall, I think there is a real paper here, and I like the direction. But for a top ML venue I wanted either stronger theory (cleaner definitions, clearer relation to performative prediction / strategic classification / feedback loops) or more convincing experiments. Right now it lands in an in-between zone for me. Weak reject.

I like the motivation here, and honestly I’m glad someone is finally trying to evaluate travel planning in a setting that looks more like what actual users ask for, rather than toy itinerary generation. The focus on Chinese travel is also valuable in its own right; too many benchmarks are basically English/US-centric and then people overclaim generality. The paper is easy enough to read, and I found the three challenge categories—constraint recognition, concept openness, customized preference—fairly sensible. Figure 1 and the examples around the benchmark construction helped.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates “benchmark contribution” from “evaluation pipeline engineering.” A lot seems to hinge on the 46 metrics and the claim that the process is both efficient and faithful, but I wanted more evidence that these metrics are reliable and not just numerous. On p. 4–5 (the benchmark/evaluation setup section), I kept looking for stronger annotation quality checks, inter-annotator agreement, or at least some calibration study showing that the automatic/assisted evaluation agrees with human judgments at a high rate. Maybe I missed it, but if this is there, it should be much more prominent. In my experience, travel planning evaluation gets messy very quickly because “reasonable” plans can differ a lot.

I also have concerns about the empirical framing. The headline result in the abstract—51.3% constraint validation—is striking, but it’s hard to interpret without a stronger baseline ladder. Table 2/Table 3 (if I’m mapping them correctly) compare several agents, but I wanted simpler baselines too: a strong prompting-only LLM, a retrieval-heavy pipeline, maybe even human-written plans scored by the same metrics as a sanity ceiling. Otherwise the number feels a bit floating in space.

Another issue is scope. The paper sells “real-world deployment,” but the benchmark still seems largely offline. Human-derived requirements are not the same thing as interactive human-agent planning. That distinction matters. I’m not saying the authors claim full interaction, but the abstract edges close to that.

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The dataset/problem formulation seems useful, and I can imagine people building on it. My recommendation is borderline positive, but not enthusiastic. For me the key question is whether the evaluation methodology is solid enough to become a community benchmark. Right now, I’m not entirely sure.

I read this paper with a fair amount of interest because diffusion for raw audio is a pretty natural idea in hindsight, but getting it to actually work well is not trivial. Overall, I think this is a strong paper and probably a clear accept, though I do have a few reservations about how some of the claims are framed.

The main thing the paper gets right is that it is very simple, almost annoyingly simple, compared to a lot of the overengineered neural vocoder literature. The formulation around the forward/reverse process is standard DDPM material, but adapting it to waveform synthesis and showing it works for both conditional vocoding and unconditional generation is, in my view, enough of a contribution. I liked the presentation of the objective around Eq. (8) / Eq. (9); after reading that section twice, I think the training recipe is actually cleaner than many comparable audio papers. Figure 1 also does a good job conveying the iterative denoising process.

The empirical results are impressive. Table 1 is the headline: MOS 4.44 vs 4.43 against WaveNet is basically parity, and the speedup is substantial. If those numbers hold up, people will use this. I also found the unconditional generation results more interesting scientifically than the vocoder story. In my experience, unconditional waveform modeling is where many models collapse into either noisy mush or low-diversity samples, and the samples here (at least from the paper’s description and evaluation) seem noticeably better. The comparisons in Table 2 / Table 3 to GAN and autoregressive baselines are pretty convincing.

That said, I do think the paper occasionally oversells “constant number of steps at synthesis.” Constant, yes, but still not exactly small. A diffusion sampler with dozens of steps is a very different runtime story from a truly one-shot generator, and I wish the latency discussion on the experimental pages were a bit more honest about that tradeoff. I’m also not entirely sure the MOS setup is described in enough detail; I may have missed it, but I wanted more clarity on raters, confidence intervals, and whether the differences are statistically meaningful. A 0.01 MOS gap is noise.

Another minor issue: the architecture section felt a bit underexplained. The residual block design is intuitive if you already know WaveNet-style stacks, but for reproducibility I would have liked more explicit detail about conditioning injection, noise-level embedding, and whether any tuning was task-specific. This is the kind of paper where lots of labs will try to reimplement it quickly.

Still, I think the core result is real. The paper is impactful, technically sound, and likely to age well. My recommendation is accept.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit frustrating. The core idea—optimize an unsupervised objective at prediction time, and train the model so that this adaptation helps the supervised task—is neat and feels like a natural synthesis of test-time adaptation, transductive learning, and bilevel/meta-learning. In particular, the framing on p.2–3 around auxiliary losses “suffering the same generalization gap” as the task loss is provocative. I’m not fully convinced by that argument as stated, but it does motivate the method well.

What I liked most is that the paper is trying to make a fairly general conceptual point rather than selling yet another narrow trick. The examples are diverse. The physics/conservation-law setup was the most convincing to me; if I’m reading Table 1 correctly, the gains there are substantial and conceptually aligned with the method. The adversarial robustness section was also interesting, though I’m less persuaded that this really says much beyond “test-time optimization with a smoothness prior can help on attacks.” Figure 3 helped, but I wanted a cleaner comparison to stronger test-time adaptation baselines.

My main issue is novelty, or maybe the paper’s positioning. After reading this twice, I still think the relationship to prior work on test-time training/adaptation is underspecified. The distinction seems to be: (i) per-input adaptation before prediction, and (ii) explicit meta-training through the adaptation process. But both ingredients have pretty clear precedents. Eq. (4) is basically a standard nested objective; the paper should be more candid about what is actually new here. Is it the general “tailoring” lens, the theoretical claims, or a specific algorithmic recipe? Right now it’s a bit slippery.
