I also found the theory section weaker than the empirical story. The discussion around why tailoring avoids the generalization gap (around p.4, lines roughly 120–150) felt too hand-wavy. You are still choosing an unsupervised objective and an adaptation procedure based on training tasks/distributions, so some version of the same issue comes back in through the side door. I might be wrong, but the claims seem overstated.

There are some practical concerns too: test-time compute is nontrivial, and the paper doesn’t really confront latency-sensitive settings. Also, several experiments seem custom-tailored (sorry) to the method. I would have liked a more standard benchmark where prediction-time adaptation is not obviously blessed by the setup.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a strong idea and uneven execution. I’m positive on it, but not enthusiastically so. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to sharpen the relation to test-time training/meta-learning and tone down some theoretical claims.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core message is actually interesting, and honestly it resonates with what many of us have suspected after watching DomainBed-style results stagnate for years: if you only have a handful of source domains, maybe “domain generalization” is just underdetermined. I think that intuition is right, and the paper’s framing is timely. The lower-bound perspective is also a reasonable contribution for this area, which has had a lot of algorithmic optimism and not much theory that says “maybe this is impossible.”

That said, after reading the theorem statements a couple of times, I found the actual scope of the result narrower than the abstract makes it sound. The abstract says “any learning algorithm,” but the fine print in the setup (I’m looking at the assumptions around the domain family and test-domain generation, I think around Sec. 3 / the main theorem) matters enormously. I might be wrong, but the hardness seems to come from constructing a family where identifying the right invariant structure is impossible without many domains, not from a universal statement about realistic DG problems. That is still valid, but it should be stated more carefully. Right now the paper oversells a bit.

I also wasn’t fully convinced by the jump from the lower bound to the empirical claim on DomainBed. The plot showing monotonic improvement with more training domains (Figure 1? maybe later, depending on the version) is directionally sensible, but this is almost tautological unless one controls for total sample size, domain diversity, and architecture/model selection effects. If adding domains also increases total data or coverage, then of course accuracy may rise. The paper says the number of samples per domain is large, but the experimental section didn’t fully isolate the variable I care about. Table results are suggestive, not decisive.

Another issue: the notion of “lost domain generalization” is catchy but vague. I found the writing around this slogan a little loose. In some places the paper seems to argue impossibility of nontrivial OOD generalization; elsewhere it argues only polynomial domain complexity in 1/epsilon. Those are not the same thing.

Still, I think there is something real here. In my experience, papers in this area often propose yet another penalty term and get +0.7% on PACS; this is more interesting than that. I just wish the paper were more honest about what exactly is proven, and more careful in tying the theorem to practical benchmarks.

Leaning weak accept if the theory is cleaned up in the final version; otherwise borderline.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more useful than flashy. The core premise — comparing post-hoc alignment and joint multilingual training directly, then proposing a hybrid — is sensible and overdue. People in this area often argue almost ideologically for one camp or the other, so I appreciated the attempt to disentangle where each actually helps. The comparisons in Table 1 and Table 2 are, in my view, the most valuable part of the paper, maybe even more than the new method itself.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as clean as it wants to be. On p.3–4, where the two paradigms are formalized, the setup still leaves some room for apples-to-oranges effects. I might be wrong, but it seems the monolingual corpora, model capacity, and optimization budgets are not matched tightly enough to make the “alignment vs joint training” comparison fully controlled. This matters because some of the conclusions later (especially around task dependence) could partly reflect training recipe differences rather than paradigm differences. I had to reread Eq. (3) and the paragraph below it to understand what exactly is frozen and what is updated in the unified framework.

The proposed framework itself is simple, which I mean as a compliment. Figure 1 communicates the idea well. Still, the novelty is moderate. Conceptually this is “joint training with an alignment-shaped inductive bias” (or vice versa), and while the exact formulation may be new, it did not strike me as a major conceptual leap. The empirical gains are what make it publishable. On the MUSE BLI benchmark (Table 3), the improvements are solid. On CoNLL NER, the mb-BERT extension is probably the strongest result in the paper, although I wish the authors had been more careful in explaining why the gains are larger for some language pairs than others. Right now the discussion is a bit hand-wavy.

I also found the writing uneven. Some parts are crisp, but on p.6 the analysis becomes too qualitative. Figure 2 is suggestive, not conclusive. Also, the claim in the abstract that alignment and joint training were “previously mutually-exclusive” feels overstated. In my experience, people have been informally combining these ideas for a while, even if not in exactly this form.

Overall, I lean positive. The paper is not theoretically deep, and the experimental control could be tighter, but it asks the right question and gives a practical answer backed by fairly extensive experiments. I’d probably accept this at a conference if there is room, though not as a standout paper.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea — building networks out of “\(\ell_\infty\)-dist neurons” so that the whole model is automatically 1-Lipschitz in \(\ell_\infty\) — is clean, and in some sense elegant. I appreciate papers that try to bake the certificate into the architecture rather than bolt it on afterward. The argument around the basic neuron being 1-Lipschitz (around Eq. (2)–(4), if I followed correctly) is straightforward and believable, and the resulting margin-based certificate is standard but useful. In that sense, the paper is principled.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper really delivers on the “towards certifying” claim in a practically meaningful way. The expressiveness theorem for approximating any 1-Lipschitz function is nice on paper, but I think the gap between universality and useful finite architectures is enormous here. The construction in the proof section (I think this is around page 5–6) feels highly non-constructive / potentially very inefficient. I might be wrong, but this is exactly the sort of theorem that can be technically true and almost irrelevant for modern-scale learning.

I also struggled with the empirical section. The experiments are labeled “preliminary,” which is honest, but also a warning sign. Table 1 and the discussion around it are too thin for a paper making robustness claims. I wanted comparisons to stronger certified baselines, not just standard networks or weak attack-based evaluations. If the model is inherently certified, then please compare to the usual certified robustness literature more carefully. Figure 2 gave some intuition for the neuron geometry, but I didn’t see enough evidence that this architecture scales beyond toy or near-toy settings. In my experience, many Lipschitz-constrained models look promising until accuracy collapses on anything moderately hard.

There are also some presentation issues. Several definitions and assumptions are introduced too quickly. The generalization bound later in the paper (page 7?) reads like a fairly generic large-margin statement specialized to this architecture, and I’m not sure what is genuinely new there versus what follows automatically from 1-Lipschitzness. The paper could be clearer about that. Right now it slightly oversells.

Overall, I think the idea has merit, and I do like the architectural direction. But for a top ML conference, this feels underdeveloped experimentally and somewhat overstated theoretically. My leaning is weak reject, though I’d be interested in a stronger version of this work later.

I read this paper fairly carefully, and honestly I’m not convinced. The core idea — stacking attention into “phases” with fusion layers in between — is not unreasonable, and in fact parts of it sound like a sensible engineering extension of question-passages attention plus self-matching attention. But the paper oversells this as a conceptual advance. After reading Section 3 twice, I still found the distinction between the “question-passage attention phase” and the “self-attention phase” more incremental than novel. The terminology “phase conductor” also feels a bit grandiose for what is, in practice, a multi-layer attention stack with gating/fusion.

A lot of the technical presentation is harder to follow than it should be. On p. 3–4, around Eq. (8)–(14), the notation becomes quite dense, and I had to keep flipping back to figure out what exactly is being fused at each stage. Figure 2 helps a little, but not enough; I think the architecture diagram is too abstract relative to the equations. I’m also not entirely sure the “improved dot-product attention” is really improved in a principled sense, versus just using more embedding views and hoping the model learns to combine them. That can work, of course, but then the contribution is mostly empirical.

The experimental section is decent but not fully convincing. Table 2 shows gains on SQuAD, but the margins over strong baselines are not huge, and I really wanted cleaner ablations. The paper does include some variant comparisons, but they don’t isolate the effects well enough: is the gain from multi-layer attention itself, from the fusion design, or from the multi-view embeddings in the attention function? My impression is that several knobs are being turned at once. In my experience reviewing reading comprehension papers, this usually means the final system is fragile and the story becomes difficult to trust.

I did like some of the qualitative analysis. The visualization discussion around the later figures (I think Figures 4 and 5) is actually one of the more interesting parts of the paper, because it at least gives some intuition for why repeated attention might refine representations. Still, visualizations are easy to over-interpret.

Overall, I think this is a competent systems paper with some empirical value, but it falls short of the novelty and clarity bar I’d expect for a top conference. If this were framed more modestly as a careful architecture study, I’d be more sympathetic. As written, I lean negative.

I found the paper interesting, and in spirit I’m quite sympathetic to it. The core idea—using an LLM-like “System-2” to decompose a visual reasoning problem into atomic visual queries handled by a separate “System-1”—is intuitive and, honestly, closer to how many of us already think these systems should work than the usual monolithic VLM story. The framing on p.2 is clear, and Fig. 1 does a decent job conveying the loop between decomposition, extraction, and reasoning. I also appreciated that the paper does not just claim better interpretability in the abstract; the intermediate sub-questions are at least inspectable.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails down what is genuinely new versus a careful orchestration of existing components. The decomposition-execution loop is sensible, but the actual mechanism felt under-specified in places. Eq. (2) and the surrounding text on p.4 describe the iterative prompting process, but I wanted more precision about termination, failure recovery, and how often the System-2 plan is revised based on noisy extractions. Maybe I missed it, but this matters a lot in practice. Right now it reads a bit too cleanly.

The empirical results are promising, especially the gains on the human-authored benchmark in Table 2. Beating a fully supervised end-to-end model by 5.7% is not trivial. Still, I’m uneasy about the comparison setup. The paper emphasizes the small amount of fine-tuning data for System-2, but the compute and model scale here are huge (LLaMA-2 70B, plus a visual extraction module). So the “data efficiency” claim needs to be stated more carefully. In my experience, these kinds of modular systems can look great on benchmarks and then be quite brittle when the visual parser makes one small mistake. The qualitative examples in Fig. 4 are helpful, but they are cherry-picked. I really wanted a more systematic error analysis—e.g., what fraction of failures come from decomposition versus OCR/value extraction versus arithmetic.

I also found Table 3 a little confusing. The ablations suggest decomposition matters, but I’m not entirely sure the paper cleanly separates the benefit of stepwise reasoning from simply giving the model more chances to query the image. Those are related, but not the same thing.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical value, but I’m stopping short of being enthusiastic. The idea is good, the results are strong, but the novelty is somewhat incremental and the analysis is thinner than it should be for a top conference paper. Weak accept / borderline accept from me.

I had mixed feelings about this paper. The high-level idea is sensible and, frankly, overdue: if the lower-level skills are frozen at transfer time, of course you can end up with a pretty bad solution. I’ve seen this issue in a lot of hierarchical RL papers over the years, even when they try to sell “transfer” as a core benefit. So I appreciate that the paper tackles the adaptation problem head-on instead of pretending fixed skills are always enough.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the technical novelty relative to the amount of machinery introduced. The derivation around the hierarchical policy gradient and the latent-dependent baseline (Sec. 4, especially around Eq. (7)–(10), if I’m tracking the numbering correctly) reads cleanly, but after reading it twice I still wasn’t sure what the truly new ingredient is beyond a careful factorization plus PPO-style clipping at multiple levels. Maybe I’m being unfair, but HiPPO felt more like a well-engineered synthesis than a fundamentally new algorithmic principle. That is not necessarily bad! But the paper occasionally oversells it.

I did like the time-abstraction adaptation idea more than I expected. The discussion around variable durations on p. 5–6 is one of the more interesting parts, and Fig. 2 helps. In my experience, temporal commitment is exactly where many HRL methods become brittle when transferred to slightly shifted dynamics. So this part feels practically important. But the exposition is not always great. I found the notation a bit slippery when switching between latent, duration, and action policies. Also, the unbiasedness claim for the baseline is central, yet the assumptions under which it holds are not emphasized enough. I might be wrong, but I think some readers will come away with a stronger guarantee than is actually justified.

The experiments are decent but not totally conclusive. Table 1 and Fig. 3 show gains, yes, though mostly against a somewhat predictable set of baselines. I really wanted a stronger comparison to prior hierarchical adaptation methods, not just fixed-skill HRL and flat PPO. Also, it would help to isolate whether the gains come mainly from jointly updating sub-policies, from the duration mechanism, or just from on-policy stability. The ablations on p. 7 go partway, but not far enough.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real empirical value, but I’m less excited about the theoretical contribution than the authors seem to be. Borderline accept for me, leaning weak accept if the conference values practical HRL progress.

I found this paper interesting, and a bit frustrating. The core idea — replacing the finite random-walk counting in RWGNN with a geometric/random-walk kernel over effectively infinite walks, then training through an implicit layer — is actually pretty neat. In particular, the connection between the fixed-point computation and implicit differentiation is technically natural here, and I appreciated that the model still keeps the “hidden graph” interpretation from prior RWGNN work. That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is as clear or as solid experimentally as it should be.

The main technical claim seems to hinge on the fixed-point operator used to compute the geometric random walk kernel (around Eq. (6)–(9), if I followed correctly). I think the paper really needs a cleaner discussion of existence/uniqueness/convergence. The text gestures at contraction-like behavior, but I did not see a satisfying statement of conditions under which the iteration converges for the learned hidden graphs, especially once parameters are being updated during training. Maybe I missed it, but this feels essential, not optional. If the answer is “the geometric factor ensures it,” then please say that explicitly and quantify it. Right now it reads a bit hand-wavy.

I also found Fig. 1 conceptually helpful, but the implementation details around the implicit backward pass are too compressed. The constant-memory claim is plausible and standard in implicit-layer papers, but the actual wall-clock tradeoff is what matters in practice. Table 2 reports accuracy wins on several benchmarks, but I really wanted runtime and iteration counts, maybe even variance across runs. On these small graph classification datasets, tiny gains are easy to overinterpret. In Table 1 / Table 2 (page 7?), some of the improvements over baselines look marginal, and I’m not entirely sure whether the comparisons are apples-to-apples in terms of model size and tuning budget.

One more thing: the paper leans heavily on “transparency” and interpretability of hidden graphs. I like that story, but I don’t think it is really demonstrated. Where are the learned hidden graphs, or even a qualitative analysis? Without that, it feels like marketing language.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it is more interesting than another generic message-passing variant. But the presentation is not yet mature, and the empirical section is thinner than I’d expect for a top venue. My leaning is borderline negative unless the authors can clarify the convergence assumptions and strengthen the experimental evidence.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of “predict omics from H&E” papers over the years, and most of them are incremental or overclaim badly. This one is a bit better than average, mainly because it is at least trying to build in distributional assumptions that make biological sense, rather than just throwing a ViT at image patches and regressing counts. The use of a ZINB prior in the flow matching objective (Eq. 6, if I followed correctly) is a reasonable idea, and the spatial coupling across spots is, in principle, more faithful to actual tissue organization than spot-wise independent prediction. Figure 2 helped; Figure 1 was more of a branding figure than a clarifying one.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper as written. My main issue is that the central methodological claim—“biologically informed flow matching”—is not explained clearly enough to let me separate what is genuinely new from what is just a repackaging of standard flow matching plus a hand-chosen endpoint distribution. I had to reread Section 3.2 twice. On page 5, around the equations defining the conditional path, it was not obvious to me whether the ZINB prior is only used at initialization, or whether it meaningfully shapes the full trajectory during training. Those are very different things. If it is mostly an initialization trick, then the biological motivation is weaker than advertised.

I also found the experimental framing a little too eager. The abstract says “substantially outperforms all baselines,” and Table 1 does show strong gains on HEST-1k, but the comparison is not entirely clean. Some of the pathology foundation model baselines seem adapted in a relatively shallow way, and I’m not sure they were given the best possible decoding heads or tuning budget. Maybe that’s unfair, but I’ve seen this movie before. Also, the claim about modeling “gene regulatory effects” through cross-spot interactions feels overstated. Spatial correlation, yes. Regulatory effects, no—not from these experiments.

A few specifics: I wanted more ablations. The ZINB choice versus NB or Gaussian should be isolated more carefully (Table 3 only partially does this). The effect of the number of flow steps / solver discretization is missing. Figure 4 looks nice, but qualitative heatmaps are always seductive. I cared more about whether improvements are concentrated in highly expressed genes or in sparse markers; page 7 hints at this but doesn’t really analyze it.

Overall, I think there is a solid paper in here, and the results are promising. But the presentation currently oversells the biological interpretation and underspecifies the method. I lean positive because the problem matters and the empirical gains seem real, but only weakly.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more careful than a lot of the “information bottleneck in deep nets” literature, which has a bad habit of over-claiming from fragile estimators. The core contribution seems to be the adaptive mutual information estimators for hidden activations, and on that front I think the paper is addressing a real problem. The discussion around why fixed binning / naive estimators fail for unbounded activations was useful, especially around the estimator construction in the middle of the paper (I’m referring to the section with the two adaptive methods; I think around pp. 4–5, though the exposition there is denser than it needs to be). Figure 2 and Figure 3 were the most convincing parts for me: they make it pretty clear that whether one “sees” compression depends a lot on how one measures it.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by all of the interpretation. The paper moves from “our estimator detects compression in ReLU-like networks” to a stronger narrative about the information bottleneck theory itself. I think that leap is too fast. Compression here is still being operationalized through fairly particular discretization/noise choices, and I’m not entirely sure the quantities being measured correspond cleanly to the theoretical IB object. Equation (3) / the estimator definition is doing a lot of work, and I would have liked more sanity checks on synthetic data where the true MI is known or at least tightly controlled. Maybe I missed it, but I didn’t see enough of that.

I also had some trouble with the claims about generalization. The statement that only the final layer’s compression correlates positively with generalization is intriguing, but the evidence felt thin. Table 1 (if I’m remembering the numbering right) reports correlations, but the number of runs / seeds and the variability across initializations seem substantial. In fact one of the more believable findings in the paper is that initialization matters a lot. Once that is true, I want a more rigorous statistical treatment before buying the last-layer claim. Right now it feels suggestive, not established.

There are also some writing issues. A few places are sloppy (“Secondary” in the abstract should be “Secondly,” and there are several awkward sentences). More importantly, the paper sometimes blurs estimator sensitivity with scientific discovery. Those are not the same thing.

Still, I think the paper has value. In my experience, negative/contradictory results in this area often come from measurement artifacts, and this work pushes that conversation forward. I’m leaning positive, but not strongly. The method is probably the real contribution; the broad theoretical claims should be toned down.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a lot of neuroimaging work over the years where people default to linear SVMs mainly because they can at least say something about the weights, even if that story is often oversimplified. So the motivation here is real. The idea of importing the “pattern” view into kernel space for RBF-SVMs is, at least to me, a sensible direction, and I appreciate that the paper is trying to solve an actually-used problem rather than invent yet another generic attribution method.

That said, I’m a bit torn on the paper. The core claim is “fast” and “less affected by noise variables,” but I found both claims only partially substantiated. The derivation around the kernel-space pattern (Eq. 6 to Eq. 9, if I followed it correctly) is interesting, but honestly a little too compressed. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t fully sure what assumptions are needed for the resulting explanation to be stable, especially when the support vector set is large and highly redundant. I might be wrong, but the method seems to inherit some fairly delicate dependence on the training distribution that the paper doesn’t really discuss. In neuroimaging that matters a lot.

I did like the simulated experiments. Figure 2 and Table 1 make the intended point fairly clearly: the proposed explanation is less distracted by nuisance features than naive importance scores. But the baselines feel undercooked. On page 5, the comparisons seem to be mostly against standard feature-importance style methods; I was expecting something stronger or at least more discussion of why SHAP/LIME-style methods are not really the right competitors here. Also, if the contribution is specifically “fast,” then runtime reporting in Table 2 should be much more detailed. Right now it reads almost like an afterthought.

The real MEG/EEG results are promising but also where I became more skeptical. Figure 3 looks plausible, yes, but plausibility is not validation. In my experience with these datasets, nice-looking spatial patterns are cheap. I wanted either a stronger sanity check or some perturbation-based verification that the highlighted variables really matter for the classifier.

Overall I think there is a worthwhile idea here, and it is more interesting than many incremental XAI papers I’ve read recently. But the paper currently feels a bit too eager to claim robustness and speed without enough careful analysis. I’d lean borderline positive if the venue values practical neuroimaging interpretability, but scientifically I think it still needs tightening.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because “suppress texture to improve transfer” has been floating around in different forms since the Stylized-ImageNet / shape-bias discussion, and a lot of papers in this space overclaim. But after reading the paper twice, I think there is a real empirical result here, even if I’m less convinced by the depth of the analysis than the authors seem to be.

The core idea—using anisotropic diffusion as an augmentation to suppress texture while preserving edges—is refreshingly simple. I actually like that. On p. 3, around the method description and Eq. (2), the paper is easy enough to follow, and Fig. 2 makes the preprocessing effect visually clear. It is also nice that the method is tested in both supervised and self-supervised settings rather than only in one cherry-picked regime. The gains on transfer benchmarks are nontrivial, especially on Sketch-ImageNet and DTD (Table 3 / Table 4, if I’m reading the numbering right). Those are exactly the kinds of datasets where I would expect texture reliance to hurt, so the story is at least internally coherent.

That said, I found the paper a bit too eager to attribute everything to “higher-level representation learning.” I’m not entirely sure the evidence supports that strong a claim. The saliency maps on p. 6 are suggestive, but saliency figures can basically be made to support almost any narrative. I would have liked a more direct probe of shape-vs-texture bias, or even a controlled corruption study. Right now the mechanism feels more asserted than demonstrated. Also, the comparison set is weaker than it should be. Why not compare against stronger augmentation baselines that also destroy local texture statistics (blur, bilateral filtering, stylization-lite, frequency filtering)? If the gains mostly come from “make texture less reliable,” then anisotropic diffusion may not be special.

A smaller issue: the method section is a little under-specified. The diffusion hyperparameters and sampling policy seemed scattered; I had to hunt for them. Maybe I missed it, but reproducibility would benefit from a clearer training recipe. And some of the “state-of-the-art” language feels too broad given that the baselines are not always the latest or fully matched.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a practical idea and decent experimental breadth. I’m positive on it, though not wildly enthusiastic. The empirical results are good enough that I’d lean accept, but I do wish the causal story were tighter and the baselines a bit less convenient.

This paper tackles an interesting and, frankly, pretty hard problem: CVaR optimization in episodic RL when the MDP is low-rank but the representation is unknown. I’ve read a fair amount of the recent low-rank / latent linear structure literature, and I do think the problem formulation here is meaningful. The headline claim — first provably efficient CVaR RL result in low-rank MDPs — may well be true, or at least I don’t immediately know a counterexample from the literature.

That said, I came away somewhat unconvinced by the presentation, and only moderately convinced by the technical contribution after reading it twice. The main theorem (I think this is Theorem 4.1 on p. 7?) gives a sample complexity of roughly \tilde O(H^7 A^2 d^4 / (\tau^2 \epsilon^2)). This is polynomial, yes, but the dependence is pretty ugly, especially in H and d. Maybe this is unavoidable for CVaR plus representation learning, but the paper doesn’t really help me calibrate whether these factors are an artifact of the proof. Table 1 would have been much more useful if it included a sharper discussion of what is actually new relative to tabular CVaR RL and standard low-rank RL separately, instead of mostly a checklist comparison.

I also found the planning oracle section a bit hard to parse. The discretized LSVI construction around the CVaR objective (Algorithm 2, and the surrounding text on p. 5–6) is probably the most novel part, but the exposition is dense. Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) introduce the Bellman-style quantities in a way that felt under-motivated; I had to reverse-engineer what was being optimized. Maybe this is just me, but the role of discretization error versus estimation error is not cleanly surfaced until much later in the proofs. Figure 1 didn’t help much.

On the positive side, I think the authors are trying to do something nontrivial rather than polishing an incremental extension. The interplay between exploration and representation learning under a risk-sensitive criterion is genuinely subtle. The bonus design seems thoughtful, and the use of MLE oracles is standard but appropriate here.

My biggest issue is that the paper is theorem-heavy and intuition-light. For a top conference, I wanted at least one concrete example showing why CVaR in low-rank MDPs is qualitatively harder than expected-value RL. Also, the assumptions need more discussion. I’m not entirely sure how benign the realizability / oracle assumptions are in practice.

Overall: technically interesting, probably correct, but not especially well communicated, and the quantitative result is weaker than I hoped. I lean weak accept if the bar is novelty/theory, but I wouldn’t fight hard for it.

I read this with some interest because whitening-based normalization has come up repeatedly over the years, and every few cycles someone tries to make it practical for deep nets, usually with mixed success. This paper is actually better than I expected on first pass. The main idea — replacing BN/cBN with a whitening + coloring transform and then using the coloring parameters for conditioning — is pretty clean, and in the GAN setting I can believe it helps because BN’s implicit assumption of feature-wise independence is especially questionable there. Figure 2 makes the motivation reasonably intuitive, and the formulation around Eq. (4)–(7) is not hard to follow.

That said, I also found parts of the paper annoyingly under-explained. The discussion of the computational overhead of full-feature whitening is too light. On p. 4, around the implementation details of the whitening matrix estimation, the authors say it is efficient/stable, but I wanted actual wall-clock numbers or at least a comparison of training cost versus standard BN and versus group-wise alternatives. In practice this matters a lot. “Works better” is nice; “works better at 1.8x cost” is a different story. I kept looking for this in Table 2 or the appendix and didn’t really find it.

The empirical results are strong, though. The CIFAR-10 conditional results in Table 1 are impressive, and I do think the gains over cBN are meaningful rather than noise. I also liked that the method was tested across more than one GAN setup; too many papers overfit to a single training recipe. The qualitative samples (Figure 4, if I’m reading correctly) also seem genuinely better, not just cherry-picked sharper images. Still, I’m not entirely sure the paper fully disentangles where the gains come from: whitening itself, the coloring parameterization, or simply extra capacity in the conditional transform. The ablation around this, especially on p. 5, felt incomplete. I would have liked a stricter parameter-matched baseline.

One more concern: the claim that “full-feature whitening is important” is stronger than the evidence supports. Maybe. But I suspect some of the gain could come from regularization effects or optimization details specific to GANs. The paper hints at this but doesn’t really dig in.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper and probably above the bar. It is not conceptually revolutionary, but it is a thoughtful adaptation of old ideas to a hard setting, and the results are better than I expected. I’d lean accept, though not enthusiastically.

I read this paper with fairly high interest because parameter-efficient tuning has become a bit crowded, and a lot of papers in this space are basically “LoRA but with one more heuristic.” This one is better than that. The main idea—adaptive rank allocation rather than uniform rank everywhere—is intuitive, and honestly I’m a little surprised it took the community this long to package it cleanly. The SVD-style parameterization and the pruning of singular values are a sensible way to operationalize the idea.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as conceptually deep as it sometimes presents itself. After reading Sections 3.1 and 3.2 twice, I think the core contribution is mostly an importance scoring / budget scheduling mechanism on top of low-rank adapters, rather than a fundamentally new PEFT formulation. That is not a dealbreaker, but the framing occasionally oversells it.

The empirical section is solid overall. I liked the breadth: GLUE-style NLU, question answering, and generation tasks. Table 1 and Table 2 are the key evidence, and the gains in low-budget regimes do look real. In particular, the low-rank settings are where this method actually matters; if the budget is generous, the advantage seems smaller, which is exactly what I would expect. Figure 2 (if I’m reading it correctly) was also useful in showing how rank gets redistributed across layers instead of staying flat. This is one of the more convincing parts of the paper.

Still, I had some concerns. First, I found the importance score under-motivated. The paper gives the formula (Eq. 7, I think), but I wanted more intuition for why this specific criterion should correlate with downstream utility, beyond “it worked.” Maybe I’m missing something, but the justification felt thin. Second, the computational overhead is discussed a bit too casually. The abstract says this circumvents intensive exact SVD computations, which is fine, but there is still machinery here for dynamic allocation/pruning. On pages 5–6, the efficiency story could be much clearer. What is the wall-clock overhead versus vanilla LoRA during training? Not just parameter count.

I also would have liked stronger ablations. For example: how sensitive is the method to the initial rank, pruning interval, and target budget schedule? Some of this may be buried in the appendix, but in my experience these adaptive methods can be annoyingly brittle.

Overall, I think this is a good paper and likely worthwhile for acceptance. It is practical, reasonably novel, and empirically stronger than most incremental PEFT submissions I’ve seen. I’m positive, but not wildly enthusiastic. The idea is useful more than profound.

I went through this paper fairly carefully, and I have mixed feelings. The core idea — using matched historical segments to supplement training for diffusion-based imputation under very high missingness — is sensible, and honestly a bit more practical than a lot of recent “yet another diffusion imputer” papers. In particular, the historical data supplement module described around p.4–5 is the part I found most interesting. The motivation is believable: when the observed sequence is too sparse, you borrow similar historical patterns. People do variants of this implicitly all the time in forecasting/imputation systems, so I appreciate seeing it formalized.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper is as technically solid as the empirical claims suggest. After reading Sec. 3 twice, I still found the supplement-matching mechanism under-specified. Equation (3) and the discussion around lines 210–235 (if I’m tracking the PDF correctly) do not make it clear how similarity is computed when the current sample itself is heavily missing. Is the matching done only on observed dimensions? Is there masking in the distance? This matters a lot. Otherwise there is a risk that the “historical supplement” is effectively leaking easy patterns from the dataset rather than robustly handling true sparse observations.

I also had questions about fairness of the comparisons. Table 2 and Table 3 show strong gains, especially at 90% missing, and those numbers are impressive, maybe a little too impressive. I might be wrong, but I did not see enough detail on hyperparameter tuning budgets for the baselines. Some diffusion and transformer baselines are notoriously sensitive. If HDDI got more tuning, the comparison is less meaningful. Also, the long-term gap setting in Fig. 4 needs better explanation: how are gap lengths sampled, and are they identical across methods/seeds?

Another issue is novelty. The diffusion module itself feels fairly standard; the contribution is mostly the retrieval/supplement idea. That’s okay, but then the ablation should really isolate this much more sharply. Table 4 helps, but I wanted a stronger analysis of when historical supplementation hurts. In my experience, on nonstationary datasets, retrieving old segments can easily bias the model.

Writing is okay but uneven. Some claims are overstated (“achieve high-accuracy imputation” is too broad), and there are a few awkward explanations around Fig. 2.

Overall, I think this is promising but not fully convincing yet. I lean borderline reject unless the authors can clarify the matching procedure, rule out leakage concerns, and tighten the experimental protocol.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating in the way many synthesis papers are frustrating. The core claim — that training on randomly generated IO examples induces a brittle distributional bias, and that adversarially constructed synthetic datasets help — is believable and, frankly, overdue. People in neural program synthesis have been getting away with “randomly sampled training data” for too long. So I’m sympathetic to the motivation.

The empirical section does most of the work here. I thought the results in Table 2 and Table 3 were the most convincing part of the paper: the degradation under shifted test distributions is pretty stark, and the proposed adversarial data generation seems to recover a nontrivial amount of performance. Figure 3 also helped me understand the setup, although I had to read the caption twice. On the other hand, I found the method section somewhat under-explained. Around Eq. (4)–(6), I think the paper is trying to define a bilevel or minimax data-generation procedure, but the exact optimization target is murky. Is the adversary selecting programs, input distributions, or full IO datasets? These are not equivalent, and on p. 5 I wasn’t fully convinced the distinction is handled carefully.

A related concern is whether the contribution is really algorithmic or mostly a data curriculum / hard-example mining result in disguise. I don’t mean that dismissively — hard data generation matters — but the paper occasionally overstates the novelty. After reading this twice, I still wasn’t sure how different this is from prior adversarial example generation ideas adapted to synthesis, except that the object being perturbed is a synthetic dataset rather than an input. That is a real twist, but the paper should position it more precisely.

I also wanted more detail about computational cost. The adversarial data construction sounds expensive, and I didn’t see a clean accounting on p. 6–7. If training becomes 5x slower, that matters. Maybe I missed it. Similarly, I’m not entirely sure the baselines are as strong as they could be; for example, the comparison to bias-control methods in Table 1 feels a bit narrow.

Overall, though, I think the paper has a solid message and one the community should hear. The experiments suggest a genuine problem and a plausible remedy. I’m positive but not wildly so. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to clarify the objective, tighten the relation to prior adversarial training/data generation work, and be more honest about cost and scope.

I found this paper interesting in intent but ultimately pretty underdeveloped as a conference submission. The basic idea—casting fluid prediction as an image-to-image translation problem—is not crazy, and in fact feels fairly natural given how many simulation papers now operate on grid fields with CNNs. But after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced there is enough novelty here, either on the ML side or on the simulation side.

The central framing in the abstract (“there is no mathematical way to decrease this correctness...”) already rubbed me the wrong way. That is just not true in any literal sense; reduced-order models, coarser solvers, approximate schemes, and learned surrogates all exist precisely to trade accuracy for speed. So the motivation feels overstated from the start. I know this may sound picky, but these things matter because they signal whether the authors really know the literature.

Technically, the method seems to be a fairly standard supervised image-to-image predictor operating on simulation frames. From Eq. (1) / the training objective section (I think this is around p. 3), I did not see anything beyond a reconstruction loss with a standard convolutional architecture. Maybe I missed something, but there is no real physics prior, no conservation constraint, no divergence-free enforcement, no discussion of boundary conditions beyond a brief mention. For fluid simulation, that is a huge omission. If you roll out for “tens to hundreds of time steps” as claimed in the abstract, error accumulation and instability are exactly the core issue. Figure 3 looks visually plausible, yes, but visual plausibility is a very low bar here.

This brings me to evaluation, which I think is the weakest part. The paper leans heavily on “nearly indistinguishable for a human observer,” but this is not an adequate metric. Table 1 reports runtime and some pixelwise error, but nothing about physical correctness. No conservation statistics, no vorticity-spectrum comparison, no long-horizon drift analysis, no generalization to different initial conditions in a convincing way. In my experience reviewing learned physics papers, this is where many nice-looking methods fall apart. I suspect that may be happening here too.

I also found the paper hard to place relative to prior work. There have been multiple papers on CNN-based fluid prediction, surrogate PDE solvers, and latent-space rollouts. The related work section (p. 2-ish) is thin and reads more like a project report than a mature conference paper.

So, overall: decent idea, probably a nice student project, but not enough technical depth or experimental rigor for a top ML venue. I might be wrong if the full version has substantially more details than what I inferred, but based on the current manuscript I would lean reject.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “yet another explanation for robust overfitting” submissions. The central claim — that robust overfitting is driven by fitting hard adversarial instances rather than being just a generic optimization artifact — is plausible, and the empirical story is mostly coherent. I especially liked the attempt to define a model-agnostic difficulty metric early in the paper (if I’m reading correctly, around Sec. 3 / Eq. (4)?), because many papers hand-wave “hard examples” without ever operationalizing it.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the way the argument is stitched together. After reading the theory section twice, I still think the jump from the linear case to the “general nonlinear models” result is a bit too slick. The statement around the main theorem on p. 6 (I think Theorem 2) feels qualified enough that the practical conclusion is weaker than the prose suggests. In other words, the experimental claims are stronger than what is actually proven. This is not fatal, but it should be toned down.

The empirical part is solid but uneven. Figures 2 and 3 were useful: the separation between easy and hard instances, and the way the hard subset correlates with later robust overfitting, is probably the most convincing part of the paper. Table 1 also looked good. But I was confused by the adaptive data usage results later on (p. 8–9, maybe Table 3/4). Are these gains coming from genuinely better robustness, or from a kind of curriculum/data-filtering effect that would also help standard training? The clean-training baseline comparisons here felt too thin. I might be wrong, but some of the improvements seem modest enough that implementation details could matter a lot.

Another issue: the “model-agnostic” language is overstated. The metric may not depend on architecture in the narrow sense, but it still depends on attack setup, loss geometry, and training dynamics. In my experience reviewing robustness papers, these details are exactly where conclusions break. I would have liked to see stronger sensitivity analysis: different threat models, different perturbation budgets, maybe at least one larger-scale setting beyond the usual CIFAR-ish regime.

Still, I think the paper has a real idea. It is not just repackaging known robust overfitting observations. The instance-level perspective is useful, and some of the practical recommendations seem actionable. I’m positive overall, though not wildly so. If accepted, I would want the authors to clarify the theorem-to-practice connection and be more careful with the “model-agnostic” framing.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more convincing than a lot of the recent “human-AI coordination” submissions I’ve seen. The core idea—using regularization toward human data not just in search (à la piKL) but also during learning, and then again at test time—is quite sensible. It’s one of those papers where the high-level story hangs together. After reading the method section twice, I think the three-stage pipeline is coherent: improve the human model first, train a regularized best response, then add search at deployment. That said, the presentation is sometimes murky, and I had to do more work than I wanted to reconstruct what exactly is optimized in each stage.

In particular, around the learning objective (I think Eq. 4/5 in the middle of the methods section), I was not entirely sure how the regularization coefficient is selected and whether it is fixed across human skill groups. This matters a lot for the claim that the model “captures diverse skill levels.” The discussion around the human model on page 4 felt a bit hand-wavy to me. Are these genuinely distinct latent conventions, or is this just smoothing over BC errors with search? Figure 2 suggests meaningful separation, but I wanted a more quantitative analysis there.

The human evaluation is the main selling point, and I do think it is stronger than usual. The ad-hoc team experiment is compelling, especially the comparison against experts (Table 1, if I’m remembering correctly). Still, I’m uneasy about the exact fairness of that comparison. Human experts are not optimized for playing with anonymous partners under possibly weird conventions, whereas the AI is explicitly trained for that setting. So “outperforms experts” is catchy, but maybe a little overstated. The repeated-play study is actually more informative to me, because it gets at adaptation over time. I liked that result.

A bigger concern: the paper leans heavily on Hanabi again. Yes, it’s the standard benchmark for this topic, but in 2026 I think the burden is higher. I’m not asking for a whole new domain, but some evidence that the regularized-learning idea is not deeply tied to Hanabi conventions would help. Right now, I believe the empirical claim in Hanabi; I’m less sure about the broader methodological claim.

Overall, I lean positive. The contribution feels real, and the human study gives this paper weight. But the exposition needs tightening, and some claims should be toned down. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to clarify the objectives and ablations, especially the contribution of each regularization stage versus just better human modeling.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than most of the recent “training data extraction” papers. The core idea — optimize a dummy dataset by simulating the training trajectory from initialization so that it lands at the observed final weights — is intuitive, and I do think it is a meaningful step beyond the linearized / NTK-style assumptions that a lot of prior work hides behind. In that sense, the paper is trying to solve a real limitation rather than just squeeze another benchmark point.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the central claim that this really captures “non-linear training dynamics” in a substantive way, as opposed to introducing enough flexibility in the optimization to fit the endpoint. The discussion around the dummy parameters (Eq. 6–8, if I’m following correctly) is the key technical ingredient, but I found the justification somewhat hand-wavy. The method seems to add degrees of freedom that make matching the final model easier, but then the burden is to show this does not just make the inverse problem underconstrained. I might be wrong, but I did not see a clean identifiability discussion anywhere.

The empirical section is strong in parts. The ResNet reconstruction result is definitely the headline, and Table 2 / Figure 4 are impressive at face value. But I wanted much more detail on sensitivity. How dependent is this on knowing the exact optimizer state, learning rate schedule, augmentation, batch order, etc.? Page 6 briefly mentions the training setup assumptions, but in practice these assumptions are a huge deal. In my experience, these attacks tend to be brittle, and the paper does not really quantify that brittleness. A small mismatch experiment would have helped a lot.

I also thought the comparison to prior work was a bit too favorable to the authors. Some baselines appear evaluated in settings that are almost designed to expose their weaknesses. If the claim is “significantly outperforms previous approaches when handling non-linear dynamics,” then okay, but I would like to see either stronger reimplementations or at least a more careful caveat.

One more thing: the computational cost seems pretty brutal. Simulating training dynamics while optimizing data is expensive, and the paper kind of shrugs at this. For a systems/ML audience, that matters.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably above the bar, mainly because the problem is important and the ResNet result is nontrivial. But I’m not as sold as the paper wants me to be on the theoretical story, and the practical threat model needs sharper articulation.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more novel than I expected from the title alone. Applying lottery ticket ideas to inverse problems / image priors is not something I’ve seen explored carefully before, and I do think the question is worth asking. In particular, the contrast between the untrained DIP setting and the pretrained GAN prior setting is a sensible way to structure the empirical story. Figure 1 gives the basic intuition reasonably well, and Tables 1–3 do suggest there is a real phenomenon here rather than pure cherry-picking.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper is much stronger empirically than conceptually. The central claim is “there exists a sparse subnetwork that can be trained in isolation to match the original prior,” but in practice the whole paper seems to hinge on IMP plus surrogate tasks, and I’m not entirely sure what is being learned about image priors specifically versus just reusing known LTH machinery in a new application domain. The wording around “validate the prevailing existence” felt too strong to me. Existence is not really established in a principled sense; rather, the authors show that IMP often finds decent subnetworks under their setups.

I also found some parts confusing. Around the methodology section (I think p.4–5), the definition of LIP is informal, and the distinction between “matching the original DNN’s performance” and “remaining useful as an inverse-problem prior” is blurry. Eq. (3) and the surrounding discussion should be clearer about what exactly is optimized during the surrogate task and why success there should transfer to the downstream reconstruction objective. This transfer is empirically claimed, but the justification is hand-wavy. In my experience, these details matter a lot in inverse problems because tiny changes in optimization setup can dominate the final PSNR.

The experimental results are promising, especially the relatively high sparsity in the DIP case. But the GAN case seems much less impressive (only 5–36% sparsity, if I read Table 2 correctly), and I wish the paper were more candid about that. Also, I wanted stronger baselines: random pruning, one-shot pruning, maybe even smaller dense architectures matched for parameter count. Right now it is hard to separate “winning ticket” from “some subnetwork after enough pruning iterations.”

Overall I lean positive because the paper opens a new angle and the experiments seem competently done, but I think the claims should be toned down and the methodological exposition tightened. Borderline accept for me.

I found this paper interesting, and in a slightly surprising way: I went in expecting another “neural guidance for symbolic search” paper, but the core technical claim is actually stronger than that. The authors propose a continuous semantics for SMT formulas and a neural architecture that supposedly learns explicit formulas, not just embeddings. If this really works as advertised, it is quite a nice idea.

The main technical section around the semantic mapping (Eq. 3–8, if I tracked the numbering correctly) is the heart of the paper. I liked that the authors at least try to make the “continuous truth value” story precise rather than hand-wavy, and the soundness/completeness statement is ambitious. That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not entirely sure what is doing the real work: the CLN architecture itself, or the very constrained structure of the invariants in Code2Inv. The benchmark is notorious for being limited, and the paper leans very heavily on the fact that all 124 “theoretically solvable” problems are solved. Good result, yes, but I wanted more evidence that this is not partly benchmark overfitting in disguise.

The empirical section is strong on headline numbers but weaker on diagnosis. Table 1 and Table 2 give the speed/solve-rate story clearly, and Figure 4 is useful, but I was missing ablations. For example, what happens if you replace the proposed semantic mapping with a simpler relaxed logic? What is the contribution of the verifier feedback vs. the CLN learner? Also, the claim in the abstract about learning 12 more complex invariants is intriguing, but in the main text this part felt almost anecdotal. I wanted concrete examples earlier, maybe side-by-side with generated formulas and failure cases. Figure 2 helps a bit, though I found the notation dense.

A smaller but important issue: the writing is uneven. Page 5 is quite clear, page 6 less so. I got confused around the training objective and how exactly candidate formulas are extracted from the network outputs. Maybe this is obvious to people closer to neuro-symbolic methods, but I work on verification-adjacent ML and still had to reconstruct parts of it.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar. The results are impressive and the idea is nontrivial. But I do have some skepticism about generality, and I wish the paper were more rigorous in showing where the gains actually come from, not just that the final system wins. My inclination is a weak accept.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more original than a lot of the recent weak supervision submissions I’ve seen. The core idea—using supervision sources with mismatched label spaces rather than forcing everything into the task label space—feels important and practically motivated. In industry settings this comes up all the time, and most WS papers sort of dodge it. So I appreciate the problem formulation.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I still think the presentation is rough in a few places. The setup around the label relations was not fully crisp for me. On p.3–4, where the paper introduces the relation between indirect source labels and task labels, it seems like a lot is pushed into user-provided structure, but the burden of specifying that structure is under-discussed. I’m not entirely sure what the realistic annotation cost is here, or how robust PLRM is when these relations are incomplete or slightly wrong. This matters a lot. In my experience, “lightweight” schema engineering often turns out not to be lightweight.

The probabilistic model itself is sensible, and Eq. (3) / Eq. (4) make the intended factorization reasonably clear, although I found the notation a bit overloaded. Figure 2 helped, but I wish there were a concrete toy example worked through end-to-end earlier in the paper. The distinguishability test for unseen labels is a nice touch theoretically, but I’m less convinced by its practical role. The theorem on the generalization side reads fine, but like many such results, it seems somewhat detached from the actual empirical regime. I might be wrong, but I don’t see this theory changing how I would use the method.

The experiments are decent, especially because they span text, images, and the ad application. Table 2 and Table 3 show consistent gains, which I appreciate. Still, the baselines feel a bit selective. Given the framing, I really wanted a stronger comparison to more recent data programming / label model variants, not just straightforward reductions or ablations. Also, the 2–9% gains are good but not overwhelming, and I would have liked error bars or significance testing in at least some tables.

Overall, I lean positive because the problem is real and the paper does push beyond standard weak supervision assumptions. But the paper needs a clearer account of what exactly the user provides, how brittle that is, and whether the method remains useful when those relations are noisy. Right now it feels promising, but not fully nailed down.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit overdue: bringing monotonic attention into a Transformer-style multi-head setting is a pretty natural idea, and I’m somewhat surprised it had not been worked out cleanly before. The core proposal around Eq. (7)–(11) is sensible, and Figure 2 helped. After reading that section twice, I think I understand how the authors combine per-head monotonic decisions with the multi-head context, but the exposition is not as clear as it should be. In particular, the distinction between the training-time expected attention and the test-time hard monotonic policy is buried a bit; page 4 was where I had to slow down.

The main empirical claim — better latency/quality tradeoff than MILk — seems mostly supported by Table 1 and Table 2. The gains are not gigantic, but they are real, and in simultaneous MT that matters. I also appreciated that the paper does not just report BLEU in isolation but tries to control latency explicitly. The two latency-control methods are probably one of the more useful contributions in practice. That said, I’m not entirely sure the comparison is fully fair. MILk is built on a recurrent architecture, while this paper benefits from a stronger Transformer backbone. The authors acknowledge this, but a lot rides on whether the gain comes from “MMA” versus “Transformer + decent latency heuristic.” I really wanted one stronger ablation here. For example, on page 6/Table 3, the ablation is helpful, but it still doesn’t isolate the architectural advantage cleanly enough for me.

I also found the writing rough in several places. The abstract has grammatical issues, and some notation on page 3 is introduced too quickly. Line-by-line, it feels like a paper that knows what it wants to say but didn’t quite get enough polishing. Not fatal, but noticeable.

One thing I liked: the method is interpretable in a way many simultaneous translation papers are not. Figure 3 is nice, and in my experience these visualizations do matter when diagnosing latency behavior. Still, I wish there were more failure cases. When does MMA break? Does head disagreement cause instability? I might be wrong, but the paper seems to assume the multi-head monotonic decisions play nicely together.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real technical merit, though not a completely airtight empirical case. I lean positive because the idea is useful, the method is plausible, and the results are good enough to matter. But I do think the authors should be more careful in separating “new attention mechanism” from “better base model.”

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of molecule generation / synthesis-planning papers over the years, and this one is at least trying to do something a bit more conceptual than “yet another graph generator + synthetic accessibility score.” The program-synthesis framing is clever. I especially liked the decoupling between a “syntactic skeleton” and the semantic instantiation of the synthesis tree; that idea comes through fairly clearly by Fig. 1 and the discussion around Eq. (2)–(4). In principle, this is a nice way to structure a nasty combinatorial search problem.

That said, I found the paper harder to trust than I wanted to. A lot of the pitch is elegant, but the empirical section feels selectively convincing. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains, yes, but I kept asking myself whether the baselines are really competitive for the exact problem being posed. Some of the comparisons seem against methods that are not naturally operating under the same synthesis constraints, which makes the “synthesizable analog generation” claim a little slippery. I might be wrong here, but after reading the experimental setup twice, I still wasn’t fully clear whether all methods had access to the same reaction inventory / building blocks / budget constraints. That matters a lot.

I also think the paper undersells how much is packed into the “black-box oracle” assumption. On p. 5–6, the joint optimization over syntax and descriptors is presented almost as a general recipe, but in practice these oracles dominate behavior. If the oracle is simple or noisy, evolutionary search can look better than it really is. If it is strong, almost anything can be made to work. I wanted more ablation here: what happens if you freeze the skeleton and only optimize semantics, or vice versa? The amortization claim around the fixed-horizon MDP is interesting, but the evidence for that specific mechanism is weaker than the prose suggests. Fig. 3 hints at it, but not enough.

There are also some writing issues. The paper is ambitious and occasionally too abstract for its own good. Around the MCMC refinement over skeletons (p. 4, lines roughly 150–180), I found the proposal distribution and acceptance criteria underexplained. Maybe this is in the appendix, but if so, it should be in the main paper. Also, terminology oscillates between “syntactic template,” “skeleton,” and “tree shape”; I eventually inferred the distinctions, but it’s not clean.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it’s more original than many papers in this area. But I’m not yet convinced the experiments fully validate the central claims, especially the bilevel decomposition as the main source of improvement rather than just a well-engineered constrained search procedure. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the missing clarifications are already in the supplement in a more convincing form.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more practically useful than a lot of systems-for-GNN papers that mostly reshuffle well-known ideas. The core point — that independently sampled minibatches across PEs artificially worsen overlap-related redundancy, and that one should cooperate rather than pretend each PE is isolated — is intuitive in hindsight, but I don’t think I’ve seen it framed this cleanly. The concavity argument for sampled subgraph size versus batch size (around Sec. 3, especially the discussion near Eq. 4/5) is the conceptual heart of the paper, and I think it is the right lens.

That said, I had to read parts of the paper twice because the presentation is not always as crisp as the idea. In particular, the distinction between “cooperative minibatching” across PEs and the “dependent consecutive minibatches” idea for serial execution felt blurred at first. Figure 3 helped, but I think the paper waits too long to give a really concrete toy example. A tiny 2-GPU example on page 4 or 5 would have saved me some effort. Also, I’m not entirely sure the term “concave” is being used in the strongest possible way here; empirically it seems true, but the assumptions under which it holds were a bit slippery to me.

The experimental results are solid overall. Table 2 and Figure 7 are the most convincing pieces for me: the bandwidth reduction is substantial, and the end-to-end speedups are not just microbenchmark gains. I also appreciated that the authors address convergence, since this kind of dependence across minibatches can absolutely bite you. Still, I wanted a bit more stress-testing on model quality. “No harm to convergence” is a strong statement. On which datasets is this truly robust? The plots on page 9 suggest it’s mostly fine, but some variance bars or multiple seeds would help. Right now I’m taking this somewhat on faith.

I was also skeptical of the “load balancing is not an issue” claim in the abstract. That felt too sweeping. The evidence in Sec. 6 is decent, but only for the evaluated settings. In my experience, lock-step behavior on irregular graphs can become ugly fast, especially with skewed degree distributions.

Overall, I lean positive. The idea is simple, useful, and backed by meaningful systems results. The paper could be clearer and slightly less absolute in its claims, but I think there is enough novelty and practical value here for acceptance.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more convincing than I expected from the title. The central claim — that one fairly simple procedure can cover SSL, UDA, and SSDA with the same hyperparameters — is ambitious, and in a lot of papers that kind of “unified” framing turns out to be mostly branding. Here, I think there is actually something real. The method description around Eq. (2)–(7) is reasonably clear, especially the way relative confidence thresholding is used to avoid brittle fixed thresholds across domains. Figure 2 helped; without it I think the algorithm section would have felt more opaque.

The empirical section is the strongest part of the paper. Table 1 and Table 2 are pretty striking, especially on DomainNet. If those numbers hold up, this is a meaningful result, not a marginal one. I also appreciated that the authors compare against methods from all three settings rather than cherry-picking only one literature. In my experience, domain adaptation papers often hide behind one benchmark or one backbone, and this paper at least tries to be broad. The claim on page 6 that the same hyperparameters are used across datasets/tasks is unusually strong; I would have liked a small appendix table listing the exact values just to make this airtight.

That said, I do have some reservations. First, I’m not entirely sure the “unified” story is as principled as the paper suggests. After reading the method twice, it still feels like a careful combination of existing ingredients: weak/strong augmentation, consistency training, pseudo-labeling, distribution alignment. That is not necessarily bad — good engineering matters — but the novelty is more in the composition than in any fundamentally new idea. The paper should probably be more upfront about that. Right now the tone oversells the conceptual contribution a bit.

Second, some ablations felt too light. The role of the random logit interpolation / source-target mixing (around page 4, near Eq. (5)) is not fully disentangled from the distribution alignment component. Table 4 helps, but I wanted a cleaner understanding of what is actually carrying performance on the hard shifts. Also, I might be wrong, but some baselines seem a little dated for UDA; I was expecting at least a discussion of stronger recent transformer or pretraining-heavy baselines.

Still, I think this is a solid paper. Not perfect, and a bit too eager in its claims, but the results are strong enough that I would lean accept. The method is simple, practical, and likely to be used. That matters.

I went into this paper somewhat skeptical because “SVRG for deep nets finally works” is a claim that has come around a few times, and usually the gains disappear once you look closely at the cost model. After reading this twice, I think the paper does have an interesting empirical point, but the presentation overstates how far it goes.

The main idea is very simple: introduce a coefficient on the variance reduction term and decay it over training. Honestly, that part is almost too simple, but simple is not bad if it works. I found Figure 1 and the surrounding discussion helpful; the observation that deeper networks seem to prefer a weaker correction term is plausible and matches intuition from optimization practice. In my experience, these control-variate style methods are often extremely brittle once you move beyond convex-ish settings, so damping the correction is not crazy at all. The method itself, if I understood Eq. (4) correctly, is basically SVRG with a scalar attenuation. Fine. But then the paper needs to be very clear about whether the contribution is “we found a practical heuristic” or “we made SVRG effective” in some stronger sense. Right now the title is more sweeping than the evidence.

A bigger issue is the experimental framing. The paper repeatedly says “better optimizes models” and emphasizes lower training loss (e.g., Table 1 / the main result tables), but for deep learning I really care about wall-clock, memory overhead, number of full-gradient snapshots, and final test accuracy. I might have missed it, but I did not see a convincing accounting of the extra cost relative to SGD/Adam-style baselines. If alpha-SVRG lowers training loss but requires expensive snapshot gradients, that is a very different story. This is exactly the historical problem with SVRG in deep learning, and the paper doesn’t fully confront it.

I also found the schedule choice under-motivated. Why linear decay specifically? Why not cosine, exponential, or adaptive based on gradient disagreement? The paper gives an empirical rationale, but it still feels like a tuned heuristic. Relatedly, I’m not entirely sure the baselines are as strong as they should be. Standard SVRG is the obvious baseline, yes, but modern optimizers matter if the claim is “real-world neural networks.”

Overall, I think there is a real empirical nugget here, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to rehabilitate a method many people (including me, maybe unfairly) had written off. But the current version feels more like a workshop paper or a solid empirical note than a top-conference contribution. I’d lean weak reject unless the camera-ready version can substantially tighten the evaluation and temper the claims.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing, because it tries to revisit a theoretical trope in FedAvg analysis that has bothered people for a while: the standard bounded gradient divergence assumption is often clearly not the right lens for explaining why local steps work surprisingly well in practice. The proposed “heterogeneity-driven Lipschitz” assumption is the main contribution, and at a high level I think the paper is asking the right question.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced the new assumption is as natural or as clearly weaker as the paper claims. The discussion around this on pp. 3–4 is one of the more important parts of the paper, but it felt a little too eager to dismiss prior assumptions without pinning down exactly when the new one is substantially different in practice. The comparison theorem is useful, but I would have liked more concrete examples or even a toy construction showing a regime where bounded gradient divergence fails badly while the proposed assumption remains benign. Right now the paper asserts this repeatedly, but I didn’t feel it in my bones, so to speak.

The technical development seems competent. I checked the logic around the main convergence statement (I think Theorem 1 and the corresponding bound near Eq. (10)?), and the replacement of the local Lipschitz constant by the global one plus the heterogeneity-driven constant is potentially meaningful. If correct, this is cleaner than a lot of earlier FedAvg analyses I’ve seen. The partial participation result is also nice since many papers dodge that case. Still, some derivation steps were a bit too compressed. Around the proof sketch after Eq. (12), I found myself filling in gaps that really should not be left to the reader in a theory paper.

I also think the “more local updates can improve convergence under high heterogeneity” message is a little oversold. It may be mathematically implied under the stated conditions, yes, but the relevant region seems quite specific. The discussion around the outperforming-mini-batch-SGD regime (toward the end, maybe Sec. 5 / Fig. 1 if I’m remembering right) is intriguing, but also a bit slippery. I’m not entirely sure how robust that conclusion is outside the asymptotic/theoretical setup.

Overall: solid theory paper, better motivated than many, but I wanted sharper intuition and less rhetorical overclaiming. I lean positive if the proofs are fully correct, though not enthusiastically so.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than most theory submissions in this area. The central question—how much more complex the encoder must be than the decoder/generative map in a VAE-style setup—is a real one, and I appreciated that the paper tries to say something beyond the usual “assume everything is invertible layer by layer” story. The distinction they make around “strong invertibility” is, at least to me, the main contribution, and the negative result for non-invertible maps is the part that stuck with me after reading.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper lands as cleanly as it wants to. I had to read the formal setup twice. The definition of strong invertibility (I think around p. 3–4, near Def. 3.1, though I may be off by a page) is doing a lot of work, and I’m not entirely sure it captures a property that is broad enough to be meaningful for the architectures people actually use. The authors emphasize that they do not require layerwise invertibility, which is good, but then the actual sufficient condition still feels fairly stringent. I kept asking myself: beyond the theorem, what are the concrete examples where this applies and is nontrivial? Figure 1 gives intuition, but I wanted more than cartoons.

The lower bound result is probably the strongest technical part. If I understood Theorem 4.2 correctly, the exponential separation for non-invertible generators relies on standard complexity assumptions, which is reasonable, but the discussion around what class of encoders is being lower bounded felt too compressed. This is one of those places where page limits hurt, but still: if the whole point is “encoders may need to be exponentially larger,” I need the model class and notion of size to be extremely explicit. Equation (6) and the paragraph after it were especially dense.

I also think the practical claims are a little overstated. The abstract’s last sentence about low-dimensional manifolds is plausible and maybe even true, but the bridge from the formal results to that empirical wisdom is not airtight. In my experience, manifold structure alone is not the whole story; optimization and amortization gaps matter a lot too.

Overall, I think this is a serious theory paper with a worthwhile idea, but the exposition needs work and the scope of the assumptions should be clarified much more honestly. Tentatively positive, though not a slam dunk.

I thought this was a timely and mostly well-executed benchmark paper. The basic contribution is clear: a cybersecurity-agent eval with realistic CTF tasks, runnable environments, and subtask annotations for partial credit. In a space where people often wave their hands about “cyber capabilities” without a serious measurement setup, I appreciate the attempt to make this concrete.
