That said, I’m a bit torn on how much is actually new here versus careful dataset/engineering work. After reading the paper twice, I think the benchmark construction is the main value, not the agent experiments. The agent side felt somewhat conventional, and in places undercontrolled. For example, the comparison across scaffolds in the later experimental section is interesting, but I found it hard to tell whether differences are due to scaffold design or prompt/interface details that are not fully standardized. I might be wrong, but the pseudoterminal setting especially seems like it could be quite sensitive to small implementation choices.

The paper is strongest when it discusses task design and realism. The 40 tasks from 4 CTFs seem thoughtfully selected, and I liked that the environments include starter files and execution access rather than just static QA-style prompts. Figure 1 helps convey the setup, although I wish the taxonomy of tasks were a bit more explicit earlier in the paper. Table 1/Table 2 (assuming I’m tracking the numbering correctly) give a decent overview of category coverage and model performance, but I wanted more breakdown by task type—pwn, web, crypto, reverse, etc.—because “cybersecurity capability” is too broad otherwise.

I also had some concerns about contamination and benchmark longevity. These are recent CTF tasks, yes, but many challenge writeups and solution fragments inevitably end up online. The paper acknowledges this, though I don’t think it really resolves it. Web search as a scaffold muddies the waters further. If an agent retrieves a near-solution, is that measuring autonomous cyber capability or search-and-apply? In practice maybe both matter, but the paper slides between these notions.

The subtask idea is good, maybe the best idea in the paper. Realistically many full tasks are too hard, and subtasks give signal. But I wanted more annotation methodology. Who wrote the subtasks? How consistent are they across challenge styles? On p. 6-ish (I don’t have line numbers in front of me), the description felt too brief for something so central.

Overall, I lean positive. I think the benchmark will be useful and probably adopted. But the experimental claims should be framed a bit more cautiously, especially any implication that these results cleanly quantify real-world cyber risk. They don’t. They quantify performance on a curated, partially internet-exposed CTF suite with a particular agent harness. Still valuable, just narrower than some of the framing suggests.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing, because “risk-sensitive IRL” is a thing people gesture at all the time without really pinning down what exactly is identifiable from demonstrations. The paper’s core move—switching from reward inference to utility inference in MDPs—is conceptually clean, and I think there is a real question here. The partial identifiability discussion was probably the part I appreciated most. If I’m reading Theorem 1 / the surrounding discussion correctly (around pp. 3–4), the authors are quite careful about what can and cannot be recovered, and that is good. Too many papers in this area quietly pretend full recovery is possible.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the overall contribution yet. After reading this twice, I still felt the setup was a little slippery. On p. 2 the paper motivates “risk attitude” via stochastic outcomes, but the exact behavioral model only became clear to me around the formal definition of UL (I think Def. 2). Even then, I’m not entirely sure whether the utility is meant to capture all deviations from risk-neutrality, or whether some of what is being inferred is effectively absorbing model mismatch. This matters, because in practice demonstrations are noisy for many reasons, not just because the expert has a nonlinear utility.

I also had trouble judging novelty relative to prior risk-sensitive control / IRL literature. The paper cites some of it, but the distinction from existing preference-learning or coherent-risk formulations felt underexplained. Maybe I missed it, but the introduction and related work should do more to say: here is what existing methods can infer, here is what they fundamentally cannot, and here is the precise gap we close. Right now it reads a bit like a reframing, albeit a useful one.

The algorithms section is solid technically, though somewhat “theorem-forward.” The sample complexity results are nice on paper, but I wonder how meaningful the assumptions are. For example, the regularity assumptions around the utility class (around Eq. (6) and the proposition after it) seem to do a lot of work. I might be wrong, but the finite-data guarantees seem tied to a fairly controlled setting.

The experiments are okay but definitely proof-of-concept, as the authors say. Figure 2 and Table 1 show the expected trends, but they don’t really stress-test the method. I wanted at least one harder environment, or some ablation showing failure modes when the utility family is misspecified.

Overall: promising idea, decent theory, but the paper currently feels more preliminary than top-conference ready. I lean weak reject unless the camera-ready-level clarifications are substantial.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of privacy-for-generative-models submissions I’ve seen. The core idea—bringing PATE into GAN training—is sensible, and the paper does a decent job arguing why this should behave better than directly privatizing SGD on the discriminator/generator. In particular, the discussion around the teacher discriminators and the noisy aggregation mechanism on p.3–4 is clear enough that I could follow the intended privacy story without having to reverse-engineer the method. I also liked the shift in evaluation toward preserving relative algorithm ranking on synthetic data (the discussion around Table 1 / p.6). That’s a genuinely useful perspective, and in my experience it is often more meaningful than raw distributional similarity metrics that look nice but don’t help downstream users.

That said, I have some reservations, and they are not minor. First, the technical novelty feels a bit incremental. PATE itself is not new, GANs are not new, and this paper is mostly an adaptation. That is fine if the empirical case is really strong, but I’m not sure it is. The experimental section on p.5–7 is somewhat thin. The datasets are not especially challenging, and I wanted more evidence on higher-dimensional or more realistic data. Maybe I missed it, but Figure 2 did not convince me that mode coverage is acceptable; the visual examples are limited and, frankly, GANs can look okay while still failing badly.

I was also confused by the privacy accounting details. The claim of “tight differential privacy guarantees” in the abstract felt stronger than what is actually substantiated. After reading the privacy analysis twice, I’m still not entirely sure how tight the bound is in practice relative to the baseline, especially since a lot depends on query patterns and the moments accountant-style reasoning. If this is around Eq. (4) or the discussion immediately after, it needs to be spelled out better. Right now it reads a bit hand-wavy.

Another issue: the “relative performance” metric is nice in spirit, but I think the paper oversells it. Preserving ranking between two models is a weak condition. It can hold even when absolute utility is poor. Table 2 should have been discussed more carefully in that regard.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, maybe even impactful, but not a slam dunk. I lean positive because the problem matters and the method is plausible, yet I do think the paper needs a more careful privacy exposition and stronger empirical validation.

I went into this paper fairly positively because offline-to-online RL is a space I care about a lot, and the premise here is appealing: add a simple evolving regularizer so the policy does not drift too aggressively early in finetuning, then relax it over time. In spirit this is sensible, and honestly not that far from what many of us would try first in practice. I also appreciate the “few lines of code” angle. If true, that matters.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m less convinced by the novelty than the authors seem to be. The core idea, as written around Eq. (6)–(9), looks like a fairly standard trust-region / behavior-regularized continuation scheme, just made iterative with a moving reference policy. I might be missing some subtlety, but the paper repeatedly markets this as a general new framework, whereas to me it reads more like a clean heuristic packaging of existing intuitions. That is not necessarily bad, but the claim level should be toned down.

I also found the exposition oddly slippery in places. The transition from the offline pretrained policy to the “iteratively evolving regularization term” on p.4 was harder to follow than it should have been. Figure 1 gives the high-level idea, but I still wanted a more explicit algorithm box earlier. The update rule for the regularization coefficient/schedule is especially under-explained. Is there theory motivating the annealing beyond “stability first, performance later”? Maybe there is, but I did not find it clearly supported. The trust-region-style language also feels stronger than what is actually guaranteed.

Empirically, the results are good, maybe very good, but I have some reservations. Table 1 and Figure 3 show consistent gains, yet I’m not fully persuaded the comparisons are as fair as they should be. Some baselines seem under-tuned, especially in the online phase. In this literature, small implementation choices massively matter. I would have liked more detail on hyperparameter selection and compute budget parity. Also, the efficiency claim is plausible but weakly substantiated; wall-clock numbers or per-update overhead on p.7/8 would help a lot.

One thing I did like: the adaptability across different offline pretraining methods. If those integrations are really as lightweight as claimed, that has practical value. Still, the paper overstates conceptual novelty and understates experimental sensitivity.

Overall, I’m somewhere in the middle. Useful paper, probably. Big leap forward? I’m not there.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because “use 3DGS as a prior for compression” is exactly the kind of idea that sounds better in the abstract than it works in practice. But after reading the paper twice, I think there is a real contribution here. The core intuition—using 3D Gaussian geometric structure to stabilize correspondence estimation under wide baselines—makes sense, and the results seem meaningfully better than standard disparity-driven multi-view codecs in the settings that actually matter. I appreciated Fig. 1 and Fig. 3; they communicate the motivation pretty clearly. The issue with 2D-only matching under large viewpoint change is real, and the examples are convincing.

That said, I found the paper uneven. The method section around the 3D prior construction and how exactly it feeds into disparity estimation is harder to parse than it should be. Eq. (4)–(7) are doing a lot of work, but the text around them is pretty compressed. I’m not entirely sure I understand what information is assumed available at encode/decode time versus what is transmitted as side information. This matters a lot. On p. 5, around the depth-map compression description, the paper says the geometric redundancy is reduced across views, but I wanted a cleaner accounting of the actual bit budget. Is the 3DGS-derived prior free? Obviously not. If it is amortized somehow, say so more explicitly. Table 1 gives the final RD numbers, but I kept looking for a breakdown table of image latents vs depth bits vs any geometry-related overhead and didn’t find one.

I also think the sequence ordering idea is a bit undersold or maybe underjustified. It helps, sure, but from the presentation it feels bolted on. If that component contributes materially, I’d like to see a stronger ablation in Table 3 (or wherever the ablations are—sorry, I may be mixing up table numbers). Right now the main novelty is clearly the 3D prior, and the ordering method reads like “also we did this.”

The empirical section is overall solid. The gains over both traditional and learned baselines are good, especially in wide-baseline cases. Speed claims are nice, but again, I wanted more detail: hardware, whether 3DGS preprocessing is included, etc. In my experience, these pipeline papers often hide expensive preprocessing and then claim fast decoding, which is technically true but a bit slippery.

So, overall: interesting paper, probably above the bar for me, but the exposition needs cleanup and the accounting of geometry-related cost needs to be much more transparent. If the authors clarify that, I’d be considerably more confident.

I found this paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating to read. The basic idea—using textual semantics from an LM to improve temporal KG extrapolation—is sensible, and honestly overdue. A lot of TKG work still treats entities/relations as IDs with timestamps attached, which is a bit artificial. So I’m sympathetic to the motivation from the start.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly isolates what is actually new here. The overall pipeline on pp. 4–6 feels like a bundle of reasonable tricks: history pruning, LM-based prompt construction, then layer-wise fusion between LM and GNN features. Each component is plausible, but the novelty claim seems broader than what is really delivered. The “sampling prompt construction” in particular sounded more substantial in the abstract than it appears in the paper. Around Eq. (6)–(8), the scoring/ranking of historical tuples mostly looks like heuristic retrieval with a time-decay term. That may work, but it is not especially conceptually deep.

I did like the multi-layer fusion idea more than I expected. Fig. 3 helped. If I understood correctly, the bidirectional exchange between the graph encoder and LM encoder is more than simple late fusion, and that is probably the paper’s strongest technical point. Still, I wish the authors had been much clearer about computational cost. On p. 7 the discussion of efficiency is very light, and for a method that invokes an LM over sampled history this matters a lot. Table 2 reports accuracy gains, yes, but where is the runtime / memory comparison? In my experience, these methods often look great until you try to scale them.

The experimental section is solid but not airtight. The gains on five datasets are nice, and Table 3/4 suggest the modules all contribute. But I’m not entirely sure the comparisons to “LLM and LM+KG models” are fully fair. Were those baselines tuned to the same extent? Also, the pruning strategy itself may already do a lot of the heavy lifting by denoising history. I wanted a stronger analysis of failure cases: when does text help, and when is topology enough? Fig. 4 hints at this, but only vaguely.

Writing quality is mixed. Some sentences are awkward, and there are places where I had to infer the intended meaning (e.g., the abstract and lines around the method overview). Not fatal, just distracting.

Overall, I think this is a borderline accept for me. There is real value here, especially in bringing semantic information into TKG extrapolation in a more integrated way, but the paper overclaims a bit and underserves efficiency/clarity concerns.

I read this paper with interest because catastrophic overfitting in single-step AT is a real problem, and honestly a lot of papers in this area repackage known tricks with new terminology. This one is somewhat better than that, but I’m still not fully convinced.

The main idea — identifying “abnormal adversarial examples” whose loss decreases after the inner maximization step — is interesting. I think the discussion around this on p.3–4 is the most compelling part of the paper. Figure 2 (if I’m reading it correctly) does show a fairly sharp correlation between the rise in these abnormal examples and the onset of CO. That said, correlation is not causation, and the paper sometimes slides between the two a bit too casually. After reading the derivation around Eq. (6) and Eq. (7) twice, I still found the actual regularizer somewhat heuristic. Why exactly these two terms (count and logits variation), and why should they be sufficient beyond the empirical observation? I might be missing something, but the theoretical grounding feels thin.

I also found some of the experimental presentation frustrating. Table 1 and Table 2 report improved robustness under strong attacks, which is good, but I wanted a much more careful sanity check that this is not just another instance of gradient masking. The paper says “iterative-step adversarial attacks” and includes AutoAttack (I think on p.6), which helps, but the attack setup details are a bit scattered. In this subfield, details matter a lot. Step size, restarts, whether evaluation uses CW or only CE, etc. need to be crystal clear. I’ve seen too many papers overclaim here.

Another issue is novelty relative to the broader literature on preventing CO in FGSM-based training. The paper positions AAER as explicit regularization of abnormal examples, but in spirit it still feels related to mechanisms that stabilize training by controlling local linearity / loss landscape distortion. The distinction is there, but narrower than the writing suggests.

Still, I do think there is something useful here. The abnormal-example statistic itself seems like a nice diagnostic, and in my experience diagnostics that track CO are valuable even when the method built on top is not the final answer. If the authors can tighten the explanation, clean up the experimental protocol, and better situate the method against prior anti-CO techniques, this could be a solid contribution. Right now I’m leaning borderline positive, but not enthusiastically so.

I had a somewhat hard time figuring out what the actual technical contribution is here. After reading the abstract and then skimming the method sections again, my impression is that this is part new agent, part survey/discussion of DRL techniques, and part empirical study in grid worlds. That can work, but in the current form it feels diffuse. The title promises “favorable inductive biases,” which is interesting, but I never felt those biases were crisply formalized. What exactly is the bias, beyond using a causally-informed intrinsic reward? Is it architectural, algorithmic, representational, or just a reward shaping choice? I think the paper needs to be much sharper on that.

The paper’s ambition is good, and I do appreciate the focus on data efficiency and sequential/causal dependencies in simple environments. In my experience, grid worlds are actually a fine place to test these ideas if the claims are scoped correctly. But here the framing drifts into broad language about “causal knowledge” and “robust” learning that the experiments do not seem to fully substantiate. If the evaluation is mostly on hand-designed grid worlds, then the causal claims need to be modest and very carefully supported. Otherwise it reads a bit like over-interpretation of exploration bonuses.

I also found the empirical section underwhelming. The comparisons are described as systematic, but I wanted much more detail on baselines, tuning fairness, and variance across seeds. If Table 1 / the main performance table is meant to carry the paper, then I need confidence that PPO/DQN/etc. were not just under-tuned relative to the proposed agent. Right now I’m not entirely sure. The same goes for any ablations: if the causal intrinsic reward is the key novelty, then I want to see a very explicit ablation removing only that component, ideally with matched capacity and training budget. Without that, it is hard to know what is doing the work.

The writing is also pretty vague in places. There are several sentences that sound important but don’t say much concretely. I found myself asking “what is actually computed?” especially around the intrinsic reward mechanism. If there is an equation defining it, that needs to be front and center and tied directly to implementation details.

Overall, I think there is a kernel of a decent workshop paper here, maybe even a solid empirical study if tightened, but for a top ML venue I don’t think the novelty and evidence are yet strong enough. My leaning is reject.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea — enriching graph nodes with what you call “schema encoders” / 2-D node attributes so that each node carries some global vocabulary-aware representation — is interesting, and honestly a bit unusual compared to the standard document graph constructions I’ve seen in text-GNN papers. I can believe this helps on small clinical datasets where local co-occurrence alone is too weak. Figure 1 helped a little, although I still found the exact construction under-specified even after reading the method section twice.

My main issue is that the paper repeatedly claims novelty and “substantial” gains, but the experimental story is not yet solid enough for that level of confidence. On the DAIC-WOZ benchmark (Table 2, if I’m reading correctly), the comparison set seems selective. Some of the stronger recent language-model-based baselines are missing, and I’m not convinced the multimodal comparisons are apples-to-apples anyway because preprocessing, splits, and transcript availability differ a lot across papers. In this literature, these details matter enormously. I’ve reviewed enough depression detection papers to know tiny evaluation differences can produce surprisingly large swings.

I was also confused by the formulation around the node features. Equation (3) and the discussion around p.4–5 seem to imply every node receives an embedding over the entire vocabulary, which sounds very expensive and potentially brittle. How large is the vocabulary after preprocessing? What is the memory/computation scaling? The paper gestures at efficiency but never really quantifies it. Maybe I missed it, but I did not see a proper ablation isolating whether the gain comes from the schema encoding itself versus simply increasing feature dimensionality or adding more global context. Table 3 is in the right direction, but not enough.

The Twitter sentiment result feels a bit bolted on. It shows some transferability, sure, but sentiment is not depression severity, and the task is much easier / cleaner. I’m not entirely sure this adds much beyond “the model also works elsewhere.”

I did appreciate that the paper tackles severity estimation rather than just binary diagnosis. That is clinically more meaningful. But then I wanted more discussion of error characteristics: where does the model fail? Is it just picking up obvious lexical markers? Are there fairness concerns by gender/interviewer style on DAIC-WOZ? This is especially important in a medical framing and is mostly absent.

Overall, I think there is a promising modeling idea here, but the paper currently overclaims and underspecifies. I’m leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the method, strengthen the baselines, and provide cleaner ablations.

I found the paper interesting, but also somewhat frustrating. The core claim — that “intraclass clustering” is an implicit regularization mechanism and predicts generalization — is plausible, and honestly not crazy at all given what we know from representation learning. I’ve seen related phenomena in papers on class manifolds, neural collapse, and intermediate feature geometry, so the general direction makes sense to me. But after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper really establishes the mechanism it wants to establish.

The main empirical result seems to be that the four proposed clustering measures correlate with test performance across hyperparameter sweeps. If I’m reading Table 1 and the discussion around p. 5–6 correctly, this is reasonably consistent across LR, batch size, optimizer, etc. That’s nice. But correlation here is doing a lot of work. I think the paper overstates the conclusion when it moves from “predictive descriptor of learned representations” to “implicit regularization mechanism.” Those are not the same thing. A thermometer predicts fever; it does not cause it. I know that’s a bit of a cheap analogy, but the paper really needs some intervention or ablation that manipulates clustering independently of the usual suspects.

I was also confused by the exact definition of the measures. Around Eq. (2)–(4), and especially the neuron-level vs layer-level distinction, the motivation felt underdeveloped. Why these four measures and not simpler within-class scatter / between-class scatter quantities? Maybe I missed it, but I don’t think the invariance properties are discussed carefully. Some of these metrics may be quite sensitive to scaling, width, or representation dimensionality, which matters a lot if one of the experimental axes is network width/depth. Figure 2 looks suggestive, but I wanted error bars or at least some sense of variance across seeds. Without that, some of the trends are hard to trust.

Another issue: the paper seems to ignore a lot of adjacent literature. Neural collapse in particular comes to mind immediately, and also work on supervised contrastive geometry and class-wise compactness. The novelty claim would be stronger if the authors positioned this as a broader, earlier-layer, or training-wide phenomenon rather than sounding like they discovered class clustering from scratch.

That said, I do think there is signal here. The breadth of hyperparameter variation is a real plus, and I appreciate that the paper is trying to get at a messy but important question instead of just proposing another optimizer tweak. My overall take is: interesting empirical observation, but the causal interpretation is not there yet, and the framing is more ambitious than the evidence supports. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can tighten the claims substantially.

I’m somewhat skeptical of this paper, though I do think the problem is interesting and timely. The premise — training transformer models over heterogeneous, unreliable internet-connected GPUs rather than tightly coupled clusters — is attractive, and honestly a lot more relevant than yet another paper assuming ideal NVLink/IB everywhere. But after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper really delivers on the central claim of “effective” decentralized training.

The main issue for me is that the paper seems to stitch together several known ideas — parameter servers, pipeline parallelism, task pools, graph clustering — and present the combination as a novel framework, but the novelty feels fuzzy. The resource allocation part is probably the most substantial technical piece, especially around the time-cost model (I’m referring to Eq. (3) through Eq. (7), assuming I’m reading the notation correctly), but I found the assumptions there too clean relative to the actual setting the paper cares about. On p. 5, the model appears to treat bandwidth and device availability in a fairly averaged way, whereas the whole challenge of public-internet training is burstiness, tail latency, churn, and weird correlated failures. I might be wrong, but the analysis seems to optimize for the wrong regime.

I also struggled with the experimental section. Figure 4 and Table 2 suggest the system can train an LLM on gaming GPUs under real-world internet conditions, which sounds impressive, but the scale is still modest compared to the rhetoric in the abstract (“GPT-4 and Llama3” are invoked a bit too freely, in my opinion). If the trained model is relatively small, then this is more of a systems prototype than evidence that the approach generalizes to frontier-scale training. That’s okay! But the paper oversells it. Also, I wanted much more detail on failure cases. How often did workers disconnect? What happened to convergence when stragglers were severe? The discussion around line 312–340 is too qualitative.

Another thing: the baselines don’t feel especially convincing. Comparing against Swarm and Dapple is reasonable, but I’m not sure the implementations/configurations are competitive in this exact setting. And there’s not enough ablation. For example, how much of the gain comes from the task pool alone vs. the clustering-based allocation? Figure 6 hints at this, but it’s not cleanly isolated.

I do like that the paper tackles a messy real-world problem. In my experience, these papers are hard to execute and easy to underestimate. Still, the current version feels more like an ambitious engineering demo with partial analysis than a solid, principled advance. Borderline reject from me, unless the camera-ready could substantially tighten the claims and provide stronger evidence on robustness and convergence.

I found the paper reasonably interesting, mostly because PTQ for learned image compression is actually a practical problem and not just another generic quantization paper with a different application label. In deployment, decoder consistency matters a lot, and the authors are right that retraining-based quantization is often a non-starter. So I appreciate the motivation.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m not fully convinced by the novelty/technical depth relative to what is claimed. The central point — that minimizing layerwise MSE during PTQ is not aligned with end-to-end rate-distortion behavior — is intuitively true, almost obvious if you’ve worked on compression. The “theoretical proof” around this (I think around Sec. 3, especially the discussion near Eq. (7)–(10)) felt more like a formalization of known intuition than a deep new result. I might be underselling it, but that was my reaction.

The practical method is clearer. Optimizing quantization parameters directly on an RD objective using a few calibration images is sensible, and Table 1 / Table 2 do show consistent gains over MSE-based PTQ baselines. Figure 1 also communicates the idea well. I liked that the method applies to weights, biases, and activations together; many papers quietly avoid the full activation quantization problem. Also, using only ~10 images is nice if it really holds broadly.

But the empirical section still left me with questions. First, I wanted more detail on calibration sensitivity. The abstract makes a big deal about “few images (e.g., 10),” but on page 6 the evidence for robustness to image selection felt limited. Are these 10 sampled from the training distribution, Kodak-like natural images, or what? In my experience PTQ can look stable until you change the calibration set slightly. Second, the comparisons are a bit too convenient. I did not see a strong retraining-based quantization baseline, which is exactly the thing the paper argues against as impractical. Even one such baseline would help anchor the tradeoff.

I was also confused by the scope of “interoperability.” INT8 fixed-point inference does help determinism, yes, but actual cross-platform bit-exact decoding depends on implementation details beyond just quantization. The wording on page 2 is a bit too sweeping.

Overall, I think this is a useful systems-oriented contribution with decent experimental evidence, but I’m not convinced it rises to a top-conference level without stronger baselines and a more convincing analysis of generalization/stability. My leaning is weak reject, though I can imagine others being more positive if they value practicality more than I do.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly more thoughtful than a lot of recent “hybrid architecture” submissions, but I’m still not fully convinced by the way the claims are framed. The core idea — putting attention heads and SSM heads in parallel within the same layer — is sensible, and Figure 2 makes the architecture reasonably clear. I also liked the motivation around complementary roles: attention for precise retrieval, SSM for compressed context tracking. That part tracks with my own intuitions from reading Mamba-style and hybrid Transformer papers over the past year.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I think the empirical story is doing a lot of work to cover for some conceptual fuzziness. The meta token idea, in particular, felt under-explained. On p.4 (around the paragraph introducing them), the paper says they “store critical meta information” and reduce the forced-to-attend burden, but I never really got a mechanistic account of why learned prepended tokens should be better than just more standard prefix/prompt tokens or latent memory tokens from prior work. Maybe Eq. (3) is supposed to formalize this, but it reads more like implementation than explanation. I wanted at least one ablation isolating meta tokens more cleanly — Table 3 helps, but not enough.

The efficiency claims are impressive, especially the KV cache reduction. If the numbers in Table 1 and Table 4 hold under comparable decoding settings, then this is genuinely useful. But I’m a bit wary here: the comparisons to Llama-3.2-3B and other baselines need more care. Are tokenization, batching, and kernel implementations aligned? In my experience, throughput tables in LM papers are notoriously slippery. I might have missed it, but I didn’t see enough detail in the main text or appendix to fully trust the 3.49x number.

I also think the paper undersells how much of the gain may come from engineering choices rather than the “hybrid-head” principle itself. Cross-layer KV sharing, local/global attention mix, and meta tokens are all bundled together. Nice system, yes. Clean scientific claim? Less so.

Still, the results are strong, and this is more than a gimmick. I lean positive, though not enthusiastically. For a top conference, I would want clearer ablations and a more honest disentangling of what actually matters. Right now it feels like three papers stuffed into one, and the narrative is slightly too neat.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The basic question—how latent dimensionality affects a GAN’s ability to “fit” or even memorize the training distribution—is interesting, and honestly I think it is a more worthwhile question than yet another incremental architecture paper. I also appreciated the attempt to connect this to compression / autoencoder lower bounds. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced the empirical study is tight enough, and some of the claims feel broader than what is actually shown.

A lot of the paper seems to hinge on the notion that a good generative model should “include the sample based dataset as part of its induced probability distribution” (already in the abstract, and then again early in the intro, around p. 2). I understand the intuition, but this is a slippery premise. In continuous spaces, exact inclusion of training samples is not really the right object; near-reconstruction or support coverage would be more meaningful. I found this conceptual setup a bit muddled, and it bleeds into the experiments. The reconstruction procedure for the GAN—optimizing over latent codes post hoc, I assume from Eq. (3)?—is doing a lot of work, and I’m not sure the paper cleanly separates “generator expressivity” from “ease of inversion.” Those are not the same thing, in my experience.

The figures are suggestive but not fully convincing. Figure 2 and Figure 3 (if I’m reading them correctly) show the expected trend that larger latent spaces reduce distortion. Fine. But the comparison to AEs is not apples-to-apples unless capacity, training budget, and decoder architecture are carefully matched. Table 1 gives some quantitative numbers, but I wanted error bars or multiple seeds. GAN inversion results can vary a lot. Also, the claim that latent size is “paramount” feels too strong; architecture, regularization, and discriminator strength matter a ton here. I might be wrong, but the paper seems to over-attribute everything to latent dimension.

I did like the discussion around the perception-distortion tradeoff on p. 5. That part felt more grounded, and it aligns with what many of us have seen empirically for years. But then the paper stops short of giving a real theory, so the compression-theory framing ends up feeling a little aspirational.

Overall: interesting question, some nice empirical intuition, but the methodology and framing need sharpening. I’m leaning borderline reject. If the authors tightened the definitions, controlled the AE/GAN comparison more carefully, and toned down the claims, I’d be much more positive.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “just apply diffusion to science data” submissions. The core idea — first map the coarse input into a latent/base distribution with an encoder, then use flow matching to generate the unresolved stochastic fine scales — makes intuitive sense for this setting. In geophysical downscaling, the mismatch between coarse and fine trajectories is real, and I appreciated that the paper actually acknowledges that instead of pretending this is just image SR with different pixels. Figure 1 and the discussion around p.2–3 help motivate this well.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the method section is not as clear as it needs to be. The adaptive noise scaling is presumably important, but I found the description around Eq. (6)–(8) pretty murky. I think the authors are estimating uncertainty from the encoder via an MLE objective and then using that to perturb the latent before flow matching, but the exact training procedure is underspecified. Is this joint optimization, alternating, or staged pretraining? Lines around the algorithm box on p.4/p.5 should be much more explicit. Right now it feels like a clever trick hidden behind notation.

The empirical results are promising. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, and I do think evaluating on both the CWA dataset and the Kolmogorov PDE case helps. The weather application is particularly compelling; 25 km to 2 km over Taiwan is not a toy setup. Also, I liked that the authors compare against both conditional diffusion and conditional flow baselines rather than cherry-picking weaker alternatives. Still, I’m not entirely convinced the comparisons are fully fair. I might be wrong, but some baselines seem under-tuned, especially if they use the same capacity/training budget despite very different optimization behavior. The paper should say more about hyperparameter search and compute parity.

My other concern is whether the gains come mainly from the encoder doing the heavy lifting. The decomposition into deterministic large scales plus stochastic residuals is plausible, but I wanted a stronger ablation isolating: encoder only, encoder + fixed noise, encoder + learned adaptive noise, and full SFM. Some of this is in Table 3, but not enough to really settle the question.

Overall, I lean positive. The problem is important, the framing is better than average, and the results look meaningful. But the paper needs clearer exposition and somewhat tougher ablations before I’d feel fully confident.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more ambitious than most of the recent “adaptive coordination” submissions in this area. The core idea — separating rule-dependent structure from partner-/convention-dependent structure — is intuitively right, and I appreciated that the paper is trying to formalize something people in HRI / cooperative MARL often hand-wave. The theorem around the rule representation being a sufficient statistic for the distribution of best responses (I think this is Theorem 1 on p. 4, around Eq. 3–5) is probably the most valuable part of the paper. At least on a first read, it gives the work a cleaner conceptual backbone than many empirical-only papers.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper delivers on the full scope of its claims. A lot hangs on the assumptions, and they felt pretty strong to me. After reading p. 4 twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure how realistic the factorization assumption is in settings like Hanabi, where “rules” and “conventions” are deeply entangled in practice. The paper acknowledges this a bit, but then the experimental framing still reads as if the method has broader applicability than what is really justified. In my experience, this is exactly where these disentanglement-style stories start to wobble.

The experiments are decent, but mixed. The contextual bandit and block placing tasks are fine sanity checks, though not especially demanding. Hanabi is the one that matters. Figure 4 / Table 2 (assuming I’m reading the numbering right) suggest meaningful gains in adaptation and zero-shot coordination on new tasks with old partners, which is nice, but I wanted stronger baselines. Why not compare more directly to prior partner-modeling or latent-strategy methods that do not explicitly disentangle conventions? The current comparisons felt a little curated. Also, some implementation details are too sparse; e.g., on p. 6–7 I couldn’t quite reconstruct how the convention representation is trained and updated at test time.

One smaller thing: the writing is generally good, but occasionally too polished in a way that hides the hard parts. The transition from the theory section to the practical learning objective (around Eq. 6?) is abrupt. I think the authors know what they mean, but I’m not sure the reader always will.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a real idea in it, not just benchmark chasing. But I also think the claims should be tightened, and the empirical story needs a bit more stress-testing before I’d be totally comfortable. Lean weak accept, maybe borderline depending on the bar this year.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea — using both “local” perturbation information and a more “global” latent distribution matching signal through a discriminator — is intuitively appealing, and I do think there is something nontrivial here. In particular, the framing in Eq. (6)–(8) (if I understood it correctly) of the classifier/discriminator game is a reasonable way to push adversarial examples back toward the natural latent distribution instead of just chasing the decision boundary. This is a real issue in adversarial training, and the paper is trying to address it in a more principled way than standard PGD-style augmentation.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not entirely sure what is genuinely novel relative to prior adversarial-distribution matching / feature-level alignment ideas. The paper repeatedly contrasts itself with Feature Scattering, but that comparison feels a bit narrow. The discriminator over latent representations on page 3 is very GAN-like, and the “local + global” terminology is somewhat vague. I kept looking for a cleaner statement of what exactly is optimized locally versus globally, and how this differs conceptually from existing feature regularization methods. Figure 2 helps a little, but honestly not enough.

The empirical results are strong on paper. Table 1 and Table 2 show large gains, especially on SVHN, which caught my eye because the margins are surprisingly big. Maybe too big? I might be wrong, but whenever I see 17–18% improvements over a strong baseline under PGD20/CW20, I immediately want more attack details. The evaluation section needs to be much more careful. Are these white-box adaptive attacks against the full defense, including the discriminator and any stochasticity? Was AutoAttack tried? I did not see convincing evidence ruling out gradient masking. In my experience, methods that alter latent distributions can accidentally make optimization harder without truly improving robustness.

I also think the writing needs work. There are many places where the claims are overstated (“substantially outperforms” etc.), and the explanation around the unsupervised generation of adversarial examples is confusing, since the classifier is still trained with labels. Some notation around the latent distributions on page 4 is also sloppy.

Overall, I think the idea has potential, and I like the direction. But for a top conference, I need a more rigorous robustness evaluation and a clearer novelty story. Right now I lean weak reject.

I found the paper interesting in premise, but ultimately I’m not convinced the contribution is as solid as the authors seem to think. The central idea—controlling model inversion vulnerability by manipulating representation separability—is intuitively reasonable, and in fairness I do think there is something here. The discussion around the trade-off between utility and recoverability is timely, and Figure 1 gives a decent high-level picture of the claimed “sweet spot.” But after reading the paper twice, I still had trouble pinning down what is actually new beyond “add a regularizer that makes features less separable, then inversion gets harder.”

A lot hinges on the theory section, and I thought that part was overstated. The argument around Eq. (3)–(5) on page 4 seems more like motivation than a real derivation of privacy guarantees. Maybe that is intended, but then the wording should be much more careful. Right now the paper sometimes slides from “harder to invert in our experiments” to something that sounds almost principled. I’m not entirely sure about the assumptions in Proposition 1 either; they seem fairly strong, and I did not see much discussion of whether realistic deep nets satisfy them. In my experience, these separability-style analyses are very brittle once you move beyond toy settings.

The empirical section is a mixed bag. Table 2 and Table 3 show the expected trade-off, but the baselines feel incomplete. Why not compare more directly to existing privacy-preserving training methods, especially ones designed for inversion resistance rather than generic regularization? As written, MixCon is mostly compared to fairly standard objectives, which makes the gains less compelling. Also, the attack setup in Section 5 felt narrow. If the defense is tuned against one inversion pipeline, I worry it may simply induce attack mismatch. Figure 4 is visually persuasive, yes, but visual reconstructions are notoriously easy to cherry-pick. I would have liked stronger quantitative attack diversity.

There were also some clarity issues. The definition of separability around lines 168–182 is a bit slippery; at first I thought you meant class margin, then distribution overlap, then something representation-geometry-based. These are not the same. The name “MixCon” also suggests some connection to mixup or contrastive learning, but the method is not really either, which is mildly confusing.

So: promising direction, but the paper currently feels under-validated and somewhat over-claimed. I like the intuition. I don’t yet buy the evidence. My leaning is weak reject unless the authors can better justify the novelty and broaden the attack/baseline comparisons.

I found the paper interesting and, at least on the main theorem, potentially significant. The claim that for compact-domain \(L^p\) approximation the exact minimum width is \(\max\{d_x,d_y,2\}\) for ReLU-like activations is clean and, if correct, worth knowing. In particular, the contrast with the \(\mathbb R^{d_x}\) result \(w_{\min}=\max\{d_x+1,d_y\}\) is conceptually nice. I’ve seen a lot of “minimal width” papers over the years, and many are technically fussy without really changing the picture; this one does seem to sharpen the picture.

That said, I had to work pretty hard to extract the precise scope. After reading Theorem 1 and then going back to the setup section, I was still not entirely sure what class of “ReLU-like” activations is actually needed for the upper bound. The abstract lists GELU and Softplus, which is a bit surprising since the constructions in this area often exploit piecewise linearity quite heavily. If the proof only uses asymptotic linearity on two sides, that should be stated very explicitly early on. As written, the assumptions felt scattered. I might be wrong, but I think the paper would benefit from a very crisp proposition near the start saying exactly which activation properties are used where.

I also found the lower bound for uniform approximation less satisfying than the \(L^p\) result. The \(d_y+1\) lower bound when \(d_x<d_y\le 2d_x\) is interesting, but it feels partial, and the “dichotomy” language in the abstract is a bit stronger than what is really proved. It’s a real phenomenon, yes, but the boundary of the phenomenon is not fully mapped out. Figure 1 (if I understood it correctly) helps, but the discussion around it is too breezy.

On presentation: several proofs are dense to the point of being unfriendly. The construction around the main upper bound (I’m referring to the section with the encoding/transport argument, around pp. 6–8) is clever, but the notation balloons. I think an explicit toy example for \(d_x=2, d_y=1\) would help a lot. Table 1 is useful, though.

My overall take: technically strong, likely correct, and probably accept-worthy, but not especially well written. I lean positive because the main result is sharp and nontrivial. Still, I’d want the camera-ready to clean up the assumptions and tone down a couple of claims.

I read this paper with interest because imitation learning in linear MDPs is a pretty active niche, and the claimed improvement from \(\epsilon^{-5}\) to \(\epsilon^{-4}\) without exploration assumptions is, on paper, meaningful. The paper is technically ambitious and I do think there is a real idea here, especially the reduction/connection to online learning with adversarial losses in discounted linear MDPs. That part, around the main theorem in Section 4, is probably the most interesting contribution.

That said, I found the presentation harder than it needed to be. After reading Theorem 1 and then going back to the assumptions on page 3, I was still not fully sure what is actually being removed relative to prior IL results. The abstract says “remove exploration assumptions,” but the way feature coverage / realizability / boundedness enters later (I’m looking at the setup around Eq. (2)–(4)) made me wonder whether the assumption is really gone or just shifted into the linear structure and occupancy regularity. I might be wrong here, but the distinction should be stated much more cleanly.

The online-learning reduction is clever, but the paper leans a bit too hard on that machinery without enough intuition. Figure 1 helped somewhat, though I honestly think the role of the adversarial loss construction is still under-explained. In my experience, if a result is “first for infinite-horizon linear MDPs” as claimed, the authors need to spend more effort making the proof roadmap legible. Right now several lemmas seem to arrive abruptly. I had particular trouble following the transition from the regret guarantee to the final imitation bound; the constants and dependence on \(1-\gamma\) look important, but Table 1 emphasizes \(\epsilon\)-rates and kind of hides the rest.

The finite-horizon strengthening to \(\mathcal{O}(\epsilon^{-2})\) is nice, but it also made me ask whether the infinite-horizon result is actually the main story or just a stepping stone. The experiments are okay but not especially convincing. The environments are small, and the baselines are “commonly used” but not necessarily the strongest theoretically aligned competitors. Also, the empirical section does not really isolate the claimed no-exploration-assumption advantage.

Overall, I think this is a solid theory paper with a nontrivial contribution, but the exposition is rough and some claims feel a bit over-marketed relative to what is formally shown. I’m leaning positive, though not enthusiastically so. If accepted, I would strongly encourage a revision that clarifies assumptions and makes the proof architecture less opaque.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more original than the average “let’s add interpretability to X” submission. The core idea — introducing concepts as an intermediate abstraction for OPE, not just for explanation but specifically for variance reduction — is novel to me, and I’ve read a fair amount of both the OPE and CBM literature. The high-level motivation in the first few pages is compelling. The medical example helps, even if it is a little overused.

That said, after reading the theory section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the practical story matches the theorem story. The main unbiasedness / variance reduction claims around the concept-based estimators (I think this is around Sec. 4, especially the proposition following Eq. (6), maybe page 5?) seem to rely on concepts being “known and predefined” in a way that feels very strong. In the actual learned-concept setting, you no longer have this clean separation, and the paper moves fairly quickly over the gap. I might be wrong, but the learned concepts seem optimized partly using the same data on which OPE is later assessed, and I wanted a more careful discussion of overfitting / data-snooping effects. Right now the end-to-end algorithm feels empirically motivated but theoretically under-justified.

I also found Figure 2 helpful conceptually, but the experimental section is a bit uneven. Table 1 looks good for synthetic data, where of course the setup favors the method because the latent conceptual structure is more likely to exist by construction. On the real-world results (Table 2?), the gains seem smaller and I had trouble judging statistical significance. Please report confidence intervals consistently. Some of the claims in the text are stronger than what the numbers support.

One thing I did like a lot is the intervention angle. That part felt less like window dressing than in many interpretability papers. If you can really intervene on a learned concept and improve estimation quality, that’s useful. But then I wanted more sanity checks: are the concepts stable across runs? Are they actually interpretable to humans, or just sparse latent factors with nice names attached after the fact? Figure 4 was suggestive, not definitive.

Overall, I think this is a promising paper with a genuinely fresh idea, but it currently sits in an awkward place: the theory is for a simplified setting, and the practical method is ahead of the justification. I’m leaning weak accept / borderline, mostly on novelty, though I would not fight hard either way.

I found this paper interesting and also a bit frustrating. The overall idea — split the problem into planning, long-context HTML understanding, and grounded action via code synthesis — is sensible, and honestly more realistic than the end-to-end agent papers that sort of hand-wave the interface issue. In particular, the use of program synthesis for actions feels like a good inductive bias for real websites, and I can believe the gains reported in Table 2 / Table 3 (assuming I’m reading the setup correctly). The paper is clearly aiming at the right bottlenecks.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper tries to claim too much at once. It is simultaneously a new agent architecture, a new HTML language model (HTML-T5), a new training recipe, and an empirical benchmark paper. Each of these pieces is large. As written, the contribution boundaries are muddy. For example, the abstract and results sections strongly emphasize “real-world websites,” but a lot of the convincing quantitative evidence seems to come from MiniWoB and Mind2Web, which are useful but not the same thing. I might be wrong, but the jump from offline planning evals to robust real-world deployment feels under-argued.

I did like the modularity discussion around planning and summarization. The canonical sub-instruction decomposition (I think this is around p. 4–5) is intuitive. But I wanted much more detail on failure cases. Figure 3 gives the pipeline, but where exactly does it break most often? Planner? summarizer? code generator? Without that, the “50% improvement” claim is hard to interpret. Fifty percent over what baseline, under what exact website distribution, and with how much variance? Some of the tables report averages without enough texture.

The HTML-T5 part is promising, though I’m not entirely sure the novelty is as high as the paper implies. Local/global attention plus denoising over long structured documents is a fairly natural extension of existing long-context pretraining ideas. The paper should be more precise about what is genuinely new here versus adapted. Equation details and pretraining objectives (around the model section) felt rushed relative to the importance of the claim.

One other concern: the system seems quite expensive and brittle in a practical sense. Multiple models, summarization, planning, then code generation/execution. In my experience reviewing web-agent papers, these pipelines often look great until one changes the site layout slightly. I wish there were stronger robustness tests.

Overall, I think there is a real contribution here, especially on the agent design side, but the paper needs sharper attribution of gains and a more honest accounting of limitations. Borderline positive from me, but not an easy accept.

I found the paper interesting and timely. The core intuition — that chunking is a bad fit for procedural “how-to” QA because it breaks logical dependencies across steps — is, frankly, pretty obvious once stated, but that does not make it unimportant. In fact I think this is one of those papers where the value is in taking a common complaint about RAG systems and turning it into a concrete data organization pipeline. The “logic unit” idea on p.3–4 is sensible, and Figure 2 helped. I also appreciated that the paper does not stay purely academic and includes an industrial setting; too many RAG papers dodge that.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not entirely sure the paper cleanly separates “better organization” from “better LLM preprocessing.” A lot seems to depend on using an LLM to transform raw documents into logic units, but then the comparison against chunk-based baselines feels a bit unfair unless those baselines get similarly strong restructuring or summarization. Table 2 looks impressive, yes, but I wanted a stronger ablation isolating: (i) granularity change, (ii) explicit links between units, and (iii) the quality/cost of the logic-unit construction model. Right now the gains may partly come from expensive offline distillation by an LLM, not the paradigm itself. Maybe I missed it, but Eq. (3) and the surrounding text on retrieval/ranking were also a bit under-explained.

I also had some concerns about evaluation. The reported “success rate” improvements of 21–33% are large, almost suspiciously large, and I wanted more detail on annotation protocol and variance. The open-domain benchmark setup on p.6 is a little thin. How many questions are genuinely multi-step procedural questions rather than just definitional questions with a “how” prefix? In my experience reviewing QA papers, this distinction matters a lot and is often hand-waved. Figure 4 is nice qualitatively, but a few cherry-picked examples do not convince me.

Another issue: the claim that Thread “drastically” reduces candidate quantity and required information to one-fourth (Abstract, Table 4) sounds good, but I wanted actual latency/cost numbers, not just storage-ish proxies. If the indexing pipeline requires substantial LLM calls, there is a real systems tradeoff here.

Overall, I think there is a solid idea here, and the paper is more practical than many RAG submissions. But the empirical story is not yet airtight, and some of the claims are a bit too sweeping for the evidence provided. I lean positive on novelty/usefulness, but only weakly.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The basic idea in Infoprop—separating aleatoric from epistemic uncertainty during model rollouts and then explicitly tracking accumulated error to decide when to terminate—makes intuitive sense, and frankly it is a direction the MBRL community has needed for a while. A lot of prior Dyna-style work handwaves the “model error compounds” issue and just tunes rollout length. Here the authors are at least trying to make that knob principled.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced I understand what is genuinely new versus a careful recombination of existing uncertainty-aware ensembles and rollout filtering. Eq. (3)–(5) on p. 4 are central, but the exposition is pretty compressed. In particular, the way the information propagation quantity is estimated across rollout steps felt under-motivated. I might be wrong, but the transition from per-step uncertainty decomposition to a trajectory-level corruption criterion seems to rely on assumptions that are never really stated clearly. The discussion around lines 178–205 is especially hand-wavy. Figure 2 helps a little, but not enough.

The empirical results are strong on the surface. Table 1 and Table 2 show clear gains over prior Dyna baselines, and I do appreciate that the paper reports longer rollout lengths rather than quietly keeping them short. That is actually meaningful. But I have some concerns about fairness. I’m not entirely sure the baseline tuning is equally serious, especially for methods that are known to be very sensitive to model architecture and rollout schedules. The appendix may address some of this, but in the main paper it reads a bit too cleanly. Also, “state-of-the-art” is doing a lot of work here. On MuJoCo, these claims age badly unless comparisons are exhaustive and very carefully normalized.

One thing I did like: the termination criterion is practical. In my experience, papers on model uncertainty often stop at diagnostics, whereas this one plugs the signal directly into the algorithmic loop. That part feels useful. On the other hand, the paper could do a much better job disentangling whether gains come from the uncertainty decomposition itself or simply from being more conservative about bad synthetic data. The ablation in Fig. 4 is a start, but it’s not enough. I wanted a cleaner “decomposition only / stopping only / both” analysis.

Overall, I lean positive on novelty-impact, but weakly. Good idea, decent results, somewhat murky technical presentation, and I’m not yet fully sold on the claimed mechanism.

I went into this paper a bit skeptical because “use Fisher information to detect poisoned parameters” is the kind of idea that sounds cleaner than it usually works in decentralized FL. But I have to say, the paper is more interesting than I expected. The empirical observation in Fig. 2 / Fig. 3 (the diagonal FI pattern difference between benign and adversarial updates) is probably the most novel part of the submission, and I do think there is a real signal there. The setting also matters: most backdoor defense papers still hide behind centralized aggregation, so I appreciate that this is actually about gossip-based DFL, where things are messier and, frankly, more realistic.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the way the core claim is presented. The paper repeatedly suggests the FI pattern is “invariant” (around p. 4 and again near the method intro on p. 5), but the evidence for invariance seems narrower than the wording implies. I saw experiments across several attacks/settings, yes, but not enough to justify that language. In my experience, these kinds of statistics are very architecture- and optimizer-dependent. I might be wrong, but I would want to see whether the same pattern survives stronger adaptive attackers who know you are pruning based on Eq. (6)/(7), or simply under different local epoch counts and non-IID severity.

The method itself is a little hard to parse on first read. I had to reread the two-stage pipeline on p. 6–7 to understand what is happening locally versus globally. The surrogate sparse training idea is sensible, but the interaction between the local pruning/reclamation step and the final consensus filtering is not explained as clearly as it should be. Algorithm 1 helps, but only somewhat. Also, “dummy parameters” in the second stage is vague terminology.

The experimental section is solid but a bit too favorable. Table 2 and Table 3 look strong, especially the ASR reductions, but I wanted more accounting of computation and communication overhead. In DFL that is not a side issue. If each client is estimating FI diagonals and doing dynamic pruning/reclamation, what is the actual runtime cost? The paper kind of waves at this. Also, I found the baselines slightly dated; there are stronger robust aggregation / anomaly filtering ideas that should at least be discussed, even if not directly portable.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a genuinely novel angle, but it overstates robustness a bit and underspecifies costs. Borderline accept for me, leaning positive. The main reason is that the paper has a real idea, not just benchmark engineering.

I found the paper interesting, but also somewhat frustrating. The core claim — that the empirical gains of categorical distributional RL can be understood as coming from an implicit distribution-matching regularizer that induces uncertainty-aware exploration — is a nice angle, and honestly more original than yet another “distributional RL works well” paper. I’ve read a lot of the older distributional RL theory papers, and this one is at least trying to say something different. That said, after reading the derivation section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper has nailed the causal story it wants to tell.

The main technical development around the return density decomposition (I think this is in Sec. 3, around Eq. (6)–(10)) is intriguing, but the presentation is pretty dense and occasionally slippery. In particular, the jump from the categorical projection/objective to the policy-level regularized optimization interpretation felt under-justified. Eq. (9) seems to be doing a lot of work, and I would have liked a cleaner statement of assumptions there. Are these exact identities, approximations, or “up to parameterization”? I might be wrong, but I suspect some of the interpretation depends quite a bit on the categorical support being fixed and the specific decomposition chosen. If so, the claim in the abstract is a bit too broad.

I also found the comparison to MaxEnt RL a little too convenient. The paper says the proposed regularization “aligns the learned policy with environmental uncertainty” rather than explicitly encouraging action diversity. Fine, but this distinction is a bit rhetorical unless the experiments really isolate it. Table 1 and the ablations later on (I think page 8) help, but not enough. I wanted to see stronger evidence that the gains are not just due to another form of smoothing or optimism. Figure 3 is suggestive, not definitive.

Empirically, the results are solid enough, and I appreciated that the authors did more than a toy bandit. Still, the experimental section feels geared toward confirmation rather than stress-testing the hypothesis. For example, what happens in low-uncertainty environments where this regularizer should presumably matter less? I didn’t see a convincing negative case.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I’d rather see papers like this than purely empirical benchmark chasers. But the theory-to-algorithm connection needs tightening, and some of the claims should be toned down. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values fresh conceptual perspectives, weak reject if the bar is “theory fully supports the story.”

I found this paper interesting, but also oddly frustrating to read. The core idea — train a model on both prefix and suffix and force it to maintain a compact “belief state” that supports predicting in both directions — is genuinely appealing, and I do think there is something here. In particular, the framing around difficult search / planning-like text generation problems is better motivated than in a lot of recent “bidirectional generation” papers. Figure 1 helps. So does the story-writing setup later on. But after reading this twice, I still don’t feel I have a crisp understanding of what is really new algorithmically versus what is mostly a training objective plus architectural bottleneck.

The paper leans heavily on the “belief state” language, but I’m not entirely sure the authors have earned that terminology. Eq. (2) and the surrounding discussion (I think page 3) describe a latent representation that is useful for conditional prediction, yes, but that by itself does not make it a belief state in the stronger sequential decision-making sense. Maybe this is just naming, but the paper’s claims are a bit grander than the evidence. I also found the exposition around the compactness constraint vague. Is compactness enforced architecturally, information-theoretically, or just by hidden dimension? This mattered to me because several claims in the abstract and around Table 2 depend on this being the key mechanism.

Empirically, the results are promising but narrow. The story-writing experiments are the strongest part of the paper, and the comparison to FIM is appropriate, but the scale is small and the tasks are somewhat bespoke. I would have liked at least one less curated benchmark. The gains in Table 1 / Table 3 look real, though I wish the authors had reported variance more consistently. Some of the ablations are useful, but a few are too “obvious baseline removed, performance drops” to be very diagnostic. Also, unless I missed it, there is not enough discussion of compute and decoding cost. If the claim is “more efficient goal-conditioned decoding,” I need more than qualitative statements.

One thing that bothered me: the paper occasionally implies improved performance even when goals are unknown, which is a strong claim. I might be wrong, but the evidence for that seemed indirect and somewhat underexplained, especially around the page 6 discussion and the corresponding figure. Please make that much tighter.

Overall, I think this is a good idea wrapped in an overclaimed paper. I’m positive on the direction, but only moderately positive on the current submission. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to clarify the actual novelty, tone down some of the belief-state rhetoric, and be more honest about the experimental scope.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea—aligning sensor streams with language so an LLM can do HAR—is timely, and I do think there is a real question here about whether LLMs can become useful “sensor learners” rather than just text-conditioned classifiers. The two-stage design in Fig. 2 is sensible, and the automatic trend-to-text generation is probably the most novel part of the paper. In particular, the claim on p. 4 that this avoids human annotation while still injecting semantics is appealing. I also appreciated that the authors froze the LLM during task-aware tuning; that makes the story cleaner and avoids the usual “just fine-tune a giant model until it works” issue.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the evidence. A lot of the paper’s framing is stronger than what the experiments actually show. The abstract says “par with or surpassing SOTA,” but when I looked at the main results table (I assume Table 2 / 3 depending on version), the margins over specialized HAR models seemed fairly modest and dataset-dependent. In my experience with wearable sensing, small gains on UCI HAR / WISDM / PAMAP can disappear with slightly different windowing or subject splits, so I really wanted more detail on preprocessing and split protocol. Some of this is probably in the appendix, but it should be much clearer in the main paper.

I was also confused by the alignment objective around Eq. (5)–(7). Maybe I’m missing something, but it seems the model is trained to associate generated textual trends with sensor-tokenized sequences, and then later evaluated on classification. If the trend text generator is itself heuristic, how much of the final performance is coming from those handcrafted priors? This matters a lot. Right now the paper sometimes reads as if the LLM learned sensor semantics from scratch, which I don’t think is really true.

The reasoning claims are also a bit oversold. The qualitative examples around Fig. 4 are nice, but “reasoning” is a strong word for what may just be decent latent alignment plus classification. I’m not saying it’s useless—far from it—but I would tone this down. Also, computational efficiency is oddly under-discussed given that one motivation is practical sensor applications.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a good idea and decent empirical results, but the presentation overclaims and some methodological details are too fuzzy for me. Borderline accept, maybe weak accept if the experimental protocol is made more airtight.

I found the paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea—learning the penalty parameter for MPALM in the multi-block setting—is sensible, and honestly overdue. A lot of L2O work has fixated on 2-block ADMM-style updates or on fully unrolled architectures, while practical structured problems are often naturally multi-block. So I do think the paper is targeting a real gap. The motivation in the first 2–3 pages is fairly convincing.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical novelty. The method seems to amount to supervised prediction of a good penalty schedule/parameter for a convergent baseline, rather than a fundamentally new optimization scheme. That is not necessarily bad, but then the paper should be much sharper about what exactly is being learned and what remains guaranteed. Around the discussion of MPALM convergence (I think near Eq. (7)–(10)), the exposition gets slippery: are the convergence guarantees preserved for every output of the learned module, or only when the predicted parameter lies in some admissible range? If the latter, how is this enforced in training/inference? I might have missed it, but this point matters a lot.

The experiments are decent but not yet strong enough for a top conference paper. The Lasso and OT examples are reasonable toy-to-midscale testbeds, but they also feel a bit safe. Table 1 looks good for iteration count, but I wanted actual wall-clock and maybe sensitivity to distribution shift in the test instances. Figure 3 is visually nice, though I found it hard to parse what exactly is fixed across methods. Also, if the learned policy is trained supervisedly, what are the labels? Best hindsight penalty from a grid search? If so, that can be expensive, and the paper downplays this cost. In my experience, these details often make or break L2O claims.

I also think the comparison set is a little curated. “Popular alternatives” is doing some work here. Why not compare to tuned over-relaxed ADMM variants, recent residual-balancing heuristics, or even simple adaptive penalty rules? If these are in the appendix, they should be in the main paper.

Overall: promising direction, some empirical value, but the contribution feels incremental and the evaluation somewhat narrow. I lean borderline reject for a top venue, though I’d be happy to see a revised version with clearer guarantees and tougher baselines.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly the problem is real. In industry-scale deep learning, people absolutely do compare single runs and pretend tiny deltas are meaningful. So I’m sympathetic to the motivation from the first page and the discussion around metric variance. In that sense, the paper is aimed at something practical rather than yet another marginal architecture tweak, which I appreciate.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced the proposed “Calibrated Loss” is as broadly useful as the paper claims. The core idea, as introduced around Eq. (3)–(5), seems to be a kind of control-variate-style adjustment using a calibration signal. That is not a bad idea at all, but the novelty feels thinner than the framing suggests. I might be wrong here, but the theoretical section around page 4 reads more like a variance decomposition plus sufficient conditions than a genuinely new framework. The assumptions used for the variance reduction claim also felt a bit idealized. In particular, the dependence structure between the calibration term and the target metric is doing a lot of work, and I don’t think the paper is candid enough about when this correlation may be weak or unstable.

I also struggled with the experimental presentation. Figure 2 and Table 1 show lower variance, yes, but what I wanted was a more direct decision-theoretic evaluation: how often does this change the ranking of two methods correctly under a fixed compute budget? The abstract claims “higher accuracy to detect effective modeling improvement,” but the empirical evidence for that exact claim felt indirect. Some of the plots on page 6 look nice, but they mostly show smoother behavior, not necessarily better model selection. Those are not the same thing.

The CTR experiments are probably the strongest part, maybe because the setting naturally has enough repeated trials and enough noise for the method to matter. The image classification section was less persuasive to me. The gains in Table 3 looked modest, and I was left wondering whether for standard vision benchmarks the practical benefit is too small to justify the extra machinery. Also, the paper should be much clearer about computational overhead. “Cheaper than repeated training” is true, but that’s a low bar.

Small thing, but the writing around lines 210–240 was surprisingly hard to parse. I had to reread the definition of the vanilla counterpart metric and how exactly calibration is applied.

Overall, I like the problem and I think there is something here, but in its current form the paper oversells generality and underspecifies failure modes. Weak accept / borderline for me.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core question is good: role-playing agents should know when a user request conflicts with the adopted persona/context and should refuse rather than hallucinate through the contradiction. That is a real issue in deployed systems, and I appreciated that the authors try to separate contextual conflicts from parametric-knowledge conflicts in the benchmark. Table 1 and the examples around p.3 helped motivate that distinction.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the representation story is as solid as the authors claim. The main analysis around the “rejection region” and “direct response region” (I think this is introduced around Sec. 4, with the visualization in Fig. 3/4) feels suggestive rather than definitive. In my experience, 2D projections of hidden states are very easy to over-interpret. The paper seems to rely heavily on clustering / separability in projected space, but I did not see enough evidence that these regions are stable across prompts, roles, or random seeds. I might be wrong, but Eq. (6) and the editing procedure that follows read more like a useful steering heuristic than a genuine mechanistic explanation.

I also had trouble with the benchmark construction. The abstract says there are three categories, but the exact annotation protocol and quality control were not described with enough rigor for me.
