On p.5, the distinction between “hard conflicting” and “non-conflicting but unusual” queries seemed especially fuzzy. This matters because over-refusal is a major failure mode, and if the benchmark is not clean, the reported gains in Table 3 are harder to trust. I would really want inter-annotator agreement or at least some human validation statistics here.

The method itself is lightweight and practical, which I like. The gains on refusal accuracy appear nontrivial, and importantly the general role-playing ability does not collapse completely (Table 4). That is a plus. But the baselines felt a bit thin. Why not compare against simple refusal fine-tuning, system-prompt interventions, or activation steering baselines from prior work? As written, the paper risks overstating novelty.

There are also a bunch of writing issues. The abstract has grammatical problems (“behave significant performance gaps”), and some claims are too strong for the evidence. Fig. 5 was hard to parse. Lines around the discussion of layer selection were confusing; I could not tell whether the edit is robust across layers or tuned per model.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I like the practical angle, but the causal/representation claims are oversold and the evaluation needs tightening. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can better substantiate the analysis and benchmark quality.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The main idea — that “optimal” hyperdimensional representations should depend on whether the downstream goal is learning/generalization versus retrieval/decoding — is actually a good one, and in my experience a lot of HDC papers blur this distinction. So I appreciate the motivation. The empirical jump claimed in the abstract (65% to 95% for classification, 85% to 100% for decoding) is eye-catching, maybe too eye-catching, and made me read the experimental section pretty carefully.

My main issue is that the paper overclaims novelty relative to what is, at core, a kernelized/structured encoding design study for HDC. On p. 3–4, where the encoder is introduced (around Eq. 3 and Eq. 4), the formulation is sensible, but I’m not convinced it rises to the level of a new conceptual framework. It felt more like: choose similarity structure appropriate to the task, then tune the hypervectors accordingly. That is not wrong, but “optimal” in the title is doing a lot of work here. I might be missing something, but I did not see a real optimality result, either theoretical or even under a constrained objective.

The most useful part of the paper for me was actually the task differentiation discussion. Figure 2 and the surrounding text (I think p. 5) make the point reasonably clearly: correlated encodings help classification, separable encodings help decoding/factorization. That said, the evidence is narrower than the paper suggests. The image experiments seem fairly limited, and Table 1/Table 2 do not really convince me this generalizes beyond the chosen setup. I also wanted stronger baselines. Comparing only to “standard HDC encoding” is weak in 2025; there are many supervised or data-aware encoders in the recent literature.

I was also confused by the factorization section. The claim that exclusivity is necessary for convergence makes intuitive sense, but the argument on p. 7 felt hand-wavy. After reading it twice, I still wasn’t fully sure what is empirically observed versus what is theoretically justified. Some notation there also gets a bit slippery.

Overall, I think there is a kernel of a good paper here, especially as a practical guide for representation design in HDC. But as a top-conference submission, I found the experimental scope too small and the novelty somewhat overstated. Borderline at best for me, leaning reject unless the authors can better justify the “optimal” claim and broaden the evaluation.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a few RGB+NIR restoration papers over the years, and in principle I do think this is a useful direction. Real low-light denoising is exactly where auxiliary sensing should help. The paper’s main contributions are a selective fusion block and a new real-world dataset. Of the two, honestly, I think the dataset is the more meaningful contribution.

The motivation is clear enough from p.1–2, and Fig. 1 does a decent job showing why naive fusion is risky when NIR and RGB content do not align semantically. That said, after reading the method section twice, I still found the actual novelty of the Selective Fusion Module a bit underwhelming. The global/local modulation idea in Sec. 3.2 and Eq. (4)–(7) feels like a fairly standard feature gating/reweighting design dressed up in NIR-specific language. I might be missing some subtlety, but I did not come away convinced this is a fundamentally new fusion mechanism rather than a competent engineering variant. The “plug-and-play” claim on p.4 is also a little too easy; in practice these modules are rarely architecture-agnostic in any meaningful sense.

The paper is strongest when it discusses the dataset. Table 1 and the description around p.5–6 suggest the authors actually put effort into capturing varied scenes/noise levels, which I appreciate. Real paired NIR/RGB denoising data is annoying to collect, and the community does need this. But the dataset section still leaves gaps. I wanted more precise details on alignment, sensor characteristics, exposure settings, and failure cases. Fig. 4 looks promising, but it’s curated. How bad is misregistration in the hard cases? Are there moving objects? The paper sort of glides past this.

On experiments, the numerical gains in Table 2/3 are solid, though not huge. I’m not entirely sure the baselines are fully convincing. Some are generic denoisers adapted to extra channels, but the comparison to stronger multimodal fusion baselines feels thin. Also, the ablation in Table 4 is okay, but I wanted more evidence that the selective part is what matters, not just additional parameters and NIR input. A runtime/parameter comparison would help too, especially since “efficient” is claimed repeatedly.

Overall I’m mildly positive, mostly because the dataset seems valuable and the method is sensible, but I’m not excited by the algorithmic novelty. If accepted, I suspect the benchmark will matter more than the module.

I found this paper fairly interesting, and honestly more useful than flashy. The main contribution, to me, is not just AdaLead itself but the FLEXS benchmark framework and the attempt to compare methods under one roof. In this area, papers often make apples-to-oranges claims, so I appreciate the effort. Table 1 and the benchmark results later in the paper make a reasonably strong case that a simple adaptive greedy/evolutionary baseline can beat more complicated methods. That said, I’m not fully convinced the empirical story is as clean as the paper suggests.

AdaLead itself is simple in a good way. After reading the algorithm description twice (I think around p. 4–5, Algorithm 1), I do understand the intuition: maintain a set of promising “leaders,” expand locally, adaptively tune search breadth, and rely on the oracle to filter candidates. Fine. But the paper sometimes oversells this as “robust” without really pinning down what robustness means. Is it robustness to oracle noise? to landscape ruggedness? to alphabet size? to model misspecification? Figure 2 seems intended to address consistency/variance across tasks, but I wanted a more direct stress test. In my experience with protein design benchmarks, methods that look robust under one surrogate setup can fall apart when the oracle is even slightly miscalibrated.

I also had some trouble with the fairness of the comparisons. The discussion around the baselines on p. 6–7 is a bit thin. How much hyperparameter tuning did each method get? AdaLead is simple enough that it may be easier to tune well, which is a legitimate advantage, but then say that clearly. I might be wrong, but the stronger generative/RL baselines looked under-optimized here. Table 2 reports aggregate performance, but I wanted per-task sensitivity or at least confidence intervals everywhere, not only in some plots. Some of the gains also look modest.

A more conceptual issue: the paper leans heavily on oracle-based optimization, but biological sequence design usually lives or dies by the gap between the model and wet-lab reality. The introduction acknowledges this, but the experimental setup still feels a bit sandboxed. FLEXS is useful, yes, but I’m not sure this gets us much closer to actual deployment unless there is some evidence that the benchmark correlates with real experimental success.

Still, I think there is value here. Simple baselines matter, and this community badly needs them. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to clarify the tuning budget, define “robustness” more carefully, and be less triumphant in the claims. My overall take is mildly positive, though not enthusiastic.

I liked this paper more than I expected to, although I also came away with a few pretty serious reservations. The basic idea—train a seq2seq symbolic music model from automatically extracted “descriptions” back to the full sequence—is clean, and in my opinion more compelling than a lot of recent controllable-generation work that basically just renames metadata conditioning. Here the controls are richer and more musically meaningful. I especially appreciated the combination of expert features and learned latent features; Table 2 and Table 4 make a decent case that the hybrid setup is better than either alone.

What worked for me is that the paper is mostly concrete. The description vocabulary on p. 3–4 is understandable, Fig. 1 communicates the pipeline well, and the style transfer examples are intuitively appealing. I also think the claim that the system can handle out-of-distribution descriptions is interesting, though I’m not entirely sure the evidence is as strong as the text suggests. The examples are suggestive, yes, but this is one of those places where cherry-picking is hard to rule out. I would have liked a more systematic stress test.

My main issue is evaluation. The paper repeatedly uses terms like “state-of-the-art” and “closely adhere” (abstract, and later around Sec. 4), but the metrics are a bit shaky. Some of the automatic measures seem too aligned with the extracted controls themselves, so there is a risk of rewarding the model for learning the extractor’s biases rather than actual musical quality or controllability. Maybe that is unavoidable to some degree, but I wanted more discussion of this. The listening study helps, but after reading that section twice I still found the setup under-specified: how many raters per sample, what musical background, and were pairwise comparisons randomized? I might have missed it.

I was also confused by the learned-feature component. Eq. (if I’m reading correctly, the latent conditioning objective in Sec. 3.3) is not explained in enough detail for reproduction. Is the latent token sequence fixed-length? How is discretization handled exactly? This matters, because the novelty claim partly hinges on combining interpretable and learned controls rather than just using one big conditioning stream.

That said, I think this is a strong paper. In my experience reviewing symbolic music papers, it is rare to see something that is both technically reasonable and musically motivated. The results in Fig. 3 / Table 5 are genuinely promising. I’m leaning positive, though not enthusiastically so, because the empirical story is better than the methodological clarity. If accepted, I’d strongly encourage the authors to tighten the evaluation claims and add implementation details.

I read this paper with some interest because the basic empirical claim is familiar: standard VAEs often sample badly once the latent dimension gets moderately large, and a lot of that does seem tied to geometry / concentration effects. So the paper is asking a reasonable question. I also appreciated that the authors are trying to attack the issue directly rather than just stacking more expressive priors/posteriors on top of the usual Gaussian story.

That said, I’m not convinced by the paper in its current form. The central premise in the abstract and around the early motivation section (I think pp. 2–3) is already a bit shaky. “The latent of a standard VAE is by definition close to a uniform distribution on a hypersphere” is not really true in the way stated. A standard isotropic Gaussian in high dimension has concentrated norm, yes, but that is not the same as saying the latent is uniformly distributed on a sphere in the sense needed for the later argument. The paper leans hard on this geometric intuition, and after reading it twice I still found the spin-glass analogy more decorative than explanatory. It’s colorful, sure, but I’m not entirely sure it gives a principled derivation of the method.

The hyperspherical-coordinate parameterization itself is interesting. If I understood Eq. (4)–(7) correctly, the model effectively biases mass toward a localized “island” on the sphere. Fine. But then what exactly is the prior, and how is the KL handled? I found the treatment around the objective somewhat under-specified. On p. 4 the text seems to suggest only a modest change to the ELBO, but the practical implications for optimization and regularization are not discussed carefully enough. Figure 2 looks promising qualitatively, but honestly qualitative samples are cheap. I wanted a more serious quantitative evaluation.

The experimental section felt thin. Table 1 reports generation improvements, but against what baselines? A vanilla VAE is not enough here. In 2025, if you claim improved generative ability from a prior/latent geometry change, I expect comparisons to at least VampPrior / flows / hyperspherical VAE variants / diffusion-regularized baselines, depending on the setup. Also, the paper seems to conflate sample quality with “meaningfulness” of random decodes. Those are related, not identical.

I do think there is a kernel of a nice workshop paper here. The idea is not crazy. But for a top conference, the theory is too hand-wavy and the experiments are not strong enough to support the broad claims. My leaning is reject.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, pretty timely. The basic pitch—using an LLM to “sandbox” naturally occurring code into executable benchmark instances—makes sense, and I do think the community needs more evaluation data that is not just another lightly rewrapped HumanEval/MBPP variant. The dataset scale is not huge, but 1,931 examples across 293 libraries and 367 repositories is enough to be nontrivial, and the fact that this comes from CodeSearchNet repos gives it a somewhat more realistic flavor than the usual toy problems.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails the hardest part, which is benchmark validity. The core method is only as good as the LLM’s ability to produce correct sandboxes/tests, and while the human study helps, it doesn’t completely resolve my concern. On p. 4–5 (around the framework description; I think Fig. 2), the pipeline sounds plausible, but I wanted a much sharper accounting of failure modes: how often are generated tests underspecified, overfit to a reference implementation, or simply wrong? The paper says humans solve 81.3% of examples, with 61% requiring effort, which is encouraging, but this is not the same as saying the benchmark is reliably measuring code generation ability rather than test idiosyncrasies. I might have missed it, but I didn’t see enough manual auditing of test correctness.

I also thought some of the experimental section was thinner than it should be. The model comparison tables (I think Table 2/3) are useful, but the analysis remains fairly surface-level: proprietary models do better, open models struggle, harder problems separate them more. Sure. But what do we actually learn about execution-based benchmark construction? I wanted stronger ablations on the prompting / filtering / sandbox generation steps, and some estimate of contamination risk given the GitHub provenance. That issue is especially important here.

Eq. 1 / the formalization (if I’m remembering the numbering right) didn’t add much for me. It felt like notation around a practical pipeline rather than something conceptually clarifying.

Overall, I lean positive because the problem is important and the artifact seems useful. In my experience, benchmark papers often get judged too harshly for not being algorithmic enough. Still, I think this one overclaims a bit on “scalable” and “diverse” without fully proving robustness. If accepted, I’d want the authors to tighten the validation story and be more candid about benchmark noise.

I found the paper interesting and somewhat ambitious, maybe a bit too ambitious for one submission. The core idea — that recommendation interventions change not only exposure/outcomes but also the target domain itself, and that this should be framed as “domain transportation” rather than standard causal adjustment — is, in my opinion, the most novel part of the paper. The discussion around overlap violations on p.2–3 is directionally correct and frankly overdue in recommender/IR papers, where people often invoke causal language a bit casually. I liked Fig. 1 because it makes the conceptual shift fairly intuitive.

That said, after reading the theory sections twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates “intervention changes the domain” from more standard distribution shift / policy-induced confounding stories. The proposed transportation-constrained objective (around Eq. (6)–(9), if I tracked correctly) seems mathematically reasonable, but I struggled to see exactly what is fundamentally new versus a robust/domain adaptation flavored minimax ERM with causal terminology layered on top. Maybe I’m being unfair here, but the paper really needs a sharper statement of novelty relative to domain adaptation and off-policy learning. Right now the relationship is scattered across the middle of p.5 and the theorem discussion later.

I also found some assumptions slippery. The overlap violation argument is strong rhetorically, but then the theory replaces it with transportation constraints whose practical identifiability is not entirely clear. Assumption 3 on p.6 (or near there) felt especially strong. I might be wrong, but in realistic recommendation systems the transport set may be badly misspecified, and then I’m not sure the consistency result still means much. Theorem 2 and the excess risk bound are nice on paper, but the constants and dependence on the discrepancy term are not very interpretable.

Empirically, Table 2 and Table 3 look solid, and I appreciate the online test — that definitely raises the paper above many theory-heavy submissions. But the experimental section is also where I got annoyed: the baselines are not always the strongest possible causal recommender baselines, and the semi-synthetic setup seems a little too aligned with the paper’s assumptions. The ablation around the transportation coefficient (Fig. 4) is useful, though I wanted more failure cases.

Overall I think this is a good paper with a real idea in it, but it currently overclaims. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to be much clearer about what is genuinely beyond existing robust/off-policy/domain-generalization formulations. My leaning is weak accept / borderline, mostly because the perspective is fresh and the online result matters.

I read this with interest because the core idea is actually kind of refreshing: instead of yet another estimator for global properties, the paper tries to make exact/tractable integration part of the model class itself by composing a bijection with a separable network. In spirit, I like it. It reminds me a bit of the old line of work on normalizing flows plus structured function classes, though here the emphasis is less on density modeling and more on integrating arbitrary learned quantities over regions of input space. That part felt new enough to me.

That said, I had a pretty mixed reaction after reading it twice. The main construction is easy to summarize, but I found the actual scope of the contribution less clear than the paper seems to think. Around the main derivation (Eq. 3–6, if I tracked it correctly), the authors rely heavily on separability after the learned bijection, and that is doing almost all of the work. So the expressive power question becomes central, but I don’t think the paper really answers it. Yes, a flexible bijection can warp the space, but in practice these are not arbitrary diffeomorphisms, and the separable head is still a strong inductive bias. I kept wondering: when does this fail badly? Figure 2 gives an intuition picture, but it’s too friendly.

I also found the “practical integrals” section a bit underdeveloped. Some of the objectives introduced on p. 5–6 seem reasonable, but the motivation feels opportunistic rather than principled. Why these integrals and not others? Table 1 shows gains, but the baselines are not very convincing. In particular, comparing against pointwise training plus Monte Carlo estimation is kind of a straw man unless the MC budget is tuned aggressively. I might be wrong, but I don’t think the compute accounting is fully fair there.

There are also some presentation issues. The notation gets slippery around the change-of-variables term (lines near Eq. 7), and I had to check whether the integral is over the original domain or transformed coordinates. Page 4 was especially dense. On the experimental side, I wanted at least one higher-dimensional, less toy-ish demonstration where exactness actually matters. The examples are clean, maybe too clean. Figure 4 looks nice but doesn’t convince me this will matter in modern ML pipelines.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I’d be happy to see it developed further. But in its current form, I’m not fully sold. The paper overclaims practicality a bit, and the empirical case is thinner than it needs to be for a top venue. My leaning is weak reject.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantive than a lot of the recent grokking literature, which often feels post-hoc. The “competing circuits with different fixed/per-example costs” story is intuitive, and I think the authors do a reasonably good job making it concrete rather than just metaphorical. In particular, the discussion around the scaling of memorization vs generalization circuits on pp. 3–5, and the way Eq. (2) / Eq. (3) are used to motivate the phase transition, gave me something I could actually reason about. I also appreciated that the paper goes beyond standard grokking and predicts ungrokking and semi-grokking; Figure 4 is probably the most compelling part of the paper for me.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the theory is as “precise” as the abstract claims. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still think there is some slippage between a conceptual efficiency argument and a mechanistic account of SGD dynamics. The core claim is that one circuit “out-competes” another, but what exactly is being optimized here—parameter norm under weight decay, description length, effective rank, something else? On p. 6 near the derivation around Eq. (7), I found the assumptions a bit too clean relative to the actual networks used in the experiments. Maybe I’m missing it, but I wanted a sharper statement of when the theory should fail.

I also had trouble with the evidence for circuits as actual identifiable subnetworks rather than an explanatory lens. Figure 2 gestures in this direction, but not very strongly. In my experience, once papers start talking about “emergent circuits,” one has to be careful not to smuggle in the conclusion. Are these circuits localized? linearly separable in feature space? recoverable by ablation? The paper hints at this but doesn’t really settle it.

The experimental section is decent, though somewhat narrow. The modular arithmetic setting is standard, and yes, that’s where grokking was born, but if the claim is general, I wanted at least one less toy domain. Table 1 and the semi-grokking result are neat, though. Ungrokking is a fun prediction; I genuinely didn’t expect that.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a real idea in it, but I’m not at the point of fully buying the explanatory claim. I’d lean weak accept if the bar is “interesting and likely to spark follow-up,” but not if the bar is “theory nailed down.”

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core intuition — that parameters move in correlated ways during training and that one can exploit this low-dimensional structure — is plausible, and honestly not that surprising if you’ve spent time looking at optimizer trajectories. What matters here is whether CMD is actually a clean new formulation and whether the empirical story is strong enough. I’m not fully convinced on either point.

The presentation is part of the problem. I had to read the method section twice. The definition of the modes around Eq. (3) and the optimization that follows in Eq. (5) are not explained at the right level. I think the authors assume the decomposition is more intuitive than it is. In particular, I was confused about whether the clustering is over individual scalar parameters, tensors, or layer-level blocks. Figure 2 helps a little, but not enough. If I understand correctly, the “synchronized behavior” is measured across epochs using a correlation matrix of parameter updates, but then the online variant on p. 5 seems to use a running estimate that may be quite noisy early in training. I might be wrong, but this seems like exactly where the method should be brittle.

The empirical results are mixed for me. Table 1 shows compact modeling gains over the baseline, but the baseline itself feels narrow. There is a lot of prior work on low-rank dynamics, trajectory subspaces, gradient compression, and mode connectivity-ish phenomena, and the paper doesn’t really position itself carefully against those literatures. The claim of “state-of-the-art” in the abstract felt overstated. Also, the generalization improvement claims in Table 2 are small and inconsistent. Some of those deltas are within what I would expect from seed noise unless the authors ran many trials; I could not find enough detail on variance, except maybe in the appendix. This is a recurring issue in ML papers and I’m a bit tired of it.

I did like the federated learning angle, though right now it reads as preliminary indeed. The communication savings are promising, but the setup on p. 7 is toy-ish. I would not hang too much on that.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and in my experience these training-dynamics papers can age well if the abstraction is right. But this version feels under-theorized and somewhat overclaimed. I lean weak reject unless the authors can clarify the method substantially and tighten the empirical evidence.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more compelling than a lot of decentralized FL submissions I’ve seen recently. The core idea — decoupling the model into shared and personalized parts and only aggregating the shared parameters over the peer-to-peer graph — is pretty natural, but in this space “natural” is not a bad thing. In fact, I think this is one of those cases where the design choice matches the problem well. Figure 1 helps sell that intuition. The motivation around avoiding full-model aggregation in decentralized personalized FL makes sense, and the SAM variant is also a reasonable extension rather than a gimmick.

That said, I do have a few reservations. First, I had to read the theory section twice. The convergence statements are broad, but it’s not entirely clear to me how much is really new versus a careful combination of existing decentralized nonconvex analysis with partial personalization. The dependence on heterogeneity and topology through \(\delta^2\) and \(1-\lambda\) is expected; I was hoping for a sharper insight about when decoupling helps beyond “less inconsistency in shared parameters.” Around the assumptions before the main theorem (I think this is on pages 5–6), the smoothness constants \(L_u, L_v, L_{uv}, L_{vu}\) make sense, but the practical interpretation is a bit murky. I might be wrong, but the theory feels more confirmatory than illuminating.

On the experimental side, the results are solid overall. The comparisons in the main accuracy table (Table 2?) look strong, especially under heterogeneous partitions. But I am not fully convinced the baselines are always tuned fairly. In my experience, methods like Ditto / FedPer-style partial personalization can be surprisingly sensitive to local epochs and personalization depth. The paper argues partial personalization is “more suitable” for decentralized FL, which may be true, but then I would like a more systematic ablation on which layers are shared/personalized. Right now, that part feels underexplored. Also, the SAM story is plausible, but the gain seems somewhat dataset-dependent; Figure 4 suggests improvement, though not uniformly dramatic.

A smaller issue: the writing is occasionally rough. There are a number of grammatical problems in the abstract and method section, and some claims are overstated (“SOTA” is used a bit too freely). Still, I think the paper has a real contribution. It is not radically novel, but it is well aligned with an important setting and, unlike some decentralized FL papers, it actually seems implementable.

Overall I lean positive. Weak accept.

I found this paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating to read. The core idea — replacing the usual single embedding vector with a weighted set of vectors and defining similarity over these sets — is not crazy at all, and in fact I think it is one of the more plausible directions for going beyond vanilla CLIP-style representations. The motivation in the abstract about a single point being too restrictive is reasonable, and the paper does make a serious attempt at giving theory rather than just proposing another architectural tweak.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper cleanly connects the theory to the actual method. The derivation around symmetric InfoNCE and the claim that the optimal similarity is PMI is probably the most important part of the paper (I think this is around Sec. 3, with the main theorem statements near Eq. (6)–(9)?), but the exposition is dense and occasionally slippery. I might be wrong, but some steps seem to rely on fairly strong assumptions about the data distribution and function class that are easy to miss on a first pass. In particular, the “optimal similarity” language feels stronger than what is really shown. Is it optimal within a very broad class, or under a specific normalization/realizability setup? The paper should be much more explicit here.

I also had trouble understanding the practical parameterization of the weighted point sets. Figure 1 gives the intuition, but the actual implementation details later on were oddly under-emphasized. How many points per instance? Are weights constrained to be positive and sum to one at all times? How sensitive are results to this cardinality? This matters a lot. Otherwise it starts to feel like a fancy mixture embedding with an expensive similarity function.

On the empirical side, the results are promising but not overwhelming. The benchmark tables (I think Tables 1 and 2) show gains, but they are not huge, and I did not see enough ablations to isolate why the method works. Is the benefit coming from the set representation itself, from increased capacity, or just from more favorable optimization? In my experience with multimodal contrastive learning papers, this distinction is usually where things fall apart. I really wanted a stronger comparison to baselines with matched parameter count and compute.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and the theory is more substantial than average for this area. But the presentation is muddy, and the empirical story is not yet airtight. I lean mildly positive on novelty, but as a conference paper I’m on the fence because the paper asks the reader to trust too many unstated details.

I found the paper interesting, and in some sense quite ahead of a lot of “NAS for compression” work that just throws RL or evolutionary search at the problem and hopes for the best. The central idea — learning an embedding over architectures and then running BO in that space — is sensible, and on page 3 the motivation for why a hand-designed kernel over architectures is awkward is, I think, basically correct. I also liked Figure 2; it is one of the few places where I felt the method became concrete rather than aspirational.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what exactly is being learned and when. Eq. (3) / the surrounding text on page 4 suggests the embedding is updated incrementally as more architectures are evaluated, but the description of the BO loop is a bit slippery. Is the kernel effectively nonstationary over time because the representation itself changes? If so, standard BO assumptions are being bent quite a bit, and the paper does not really discuss this. Maybe I’m overthinking it, but in my experience these details matter a lot for whether BO is actually doing something principled versus just being a heuristic search controller.

The empirical results are promising but not fully convincing. Table 1 shows gains over random search and the RL baseline from Ashok et al., but I worry the comparison is not apples-to-apples. The search budget and wall-clock cost are not discussed as carefully as they should be. If your method needs repeated embedding updates plus BO fitting, the claim of “efficient” compression needs more than just fewer architecture evaluations. Also, comparing to ShuffleNet is nice for intuition, but it is not really a search baseline. It’s a manually designed family with different inductive biases. I would have preferred stronger NAS/compression baselines, or at least an ablation on the embedding itself: what happens with a fixed encoding, or even a simple edit-distance kernel?

I did find the transfer claim on page 6 intriguing. The idea that the learned embedding generalizes across teacher families without retraining is cool, maybe the coolest part of the paper honestly, but the evidence is thin. A couple more settings would help. Figure 4 looks good, but I’m not entirely sure the effect size is large enough to support the broad claim made in the abstract.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper has a real idea in it, not just benchmark chasing. But the presentation is a bit too hand-wavy in places, and some of the empirical claims are stronger than what the evidence currently supports. I’d probably place this around weak accept / borderline.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, quite timely. The basic problem is real: for sparse kernels on new accelerators, you simply do not have the luxury of collecting huge supervised datasets, especially when everything runs through a slow simulator. In that sense the premise of DASH makes immediate sense, and I appreciated that the paper is not trying to oversell some giant foundation-model-style transfer story. It is a fairly practical idea: pretrain on cheap CPU data, then adapt with a tiny amount of target-platform data.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced I understand what is genuinely new in the method beyond a careful transfer learning recipe plus feature handling. The discussion around “homogeneity” and “heterogeneity” of features (around p.4–5) felt a bit hand-wavy to me. I kept looking for something sharper—either a theorem, or at least a more explicit design justification for why the proposed representation should transfer between CPU and accelerator better than prior baselines. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text are supposed to formalize this, I think, but I found the exposition muddy.

The experimental section is solid in some ways. Using 715 sparsity patterns is better than what I often see in this area, and I liked that both SpMM and SDDMM are included rather than just one kernel. Figure 6 and Table 2 are probably the most convincing parts of the paper for me: the low-data regime trends do support the claim that DASH is meaningfully more data-efficient. The “5% of data” headline seems plausible from those plots. Still, I wanted more detail on variance. Some of the gains look modest in the average case, and without clearer error bars / confidence intervals (unless I missed them), it is hard to judge robustness.

I also have a nagging concern about the baselines. The paper says it outperforms existing transfer methods by 28.44%, but I’m not entirely sure the baseline tuning is equally careful. Page 7, lines around the fine-tuning protocol, made me wonder whether all methods get the same search budget and same access to source/target features. If not, the comparison is not clean. Also, using only one emerging accelerator makes the “platforms” claim broader than the evidence.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, but not a slam dunk. Useful idea, credible results, somewhat under-explained method. I lean positive because the problem matters and the empirical story is mostly there, but I would strongly encourage the authors to tighten the technical narrative and be more precise about what exactly is transferred and why.

I have somewhat mixed feelings about this paper, but I do think it raises a real issue that the community has been too willing to ignore. The core empirical observation — that a trivial “recently popular nodes” baseline beats many temporal GNN-style methods on TGB dynamic link prediction — is important if it holds up cleanly. Looking at Table 1 and Table 2, the result is honestly a bit embarrassing for the field, and I mean that in a constructive way. I’ve reviewed a lot of temporal graph papers over the years, and many of them do seem to assume that local temporal patterns are the whole game. This paper argues otherwise, and I found that message convincing.

The strongest part for me is not even the baseline itself, but the diagnosis around negative sampling. The discussion around model degeneration / saturated predictions (I think this is around Sec. 4, with the plots in Fig. 3 or 4) felt very plausible and consistent with failure modes I’ve seen in practice. The point that standard negative sampling can become misaligned with datasets exhibiting strong global temporal drift is well taken. I also appreciated the attempt to formalize “short-term” and “long-term” dynamics via Wasserstein-based measures (Eq. 6/7, if I tracked correctly). That said, I’m not entirely sure the measures are as principled as the authors suggest. They are sensible summary statistics, yes, but the paper sometimes slides from “useful diagnostic” to “capturing global temporal dynamics” in a stronger sense than is justified.

I did find parts of the exposition frustrating. On a first read, I struggled to disentangle what is a critique of the benchmark, what is a critique of the evaluation protocol, and what is a critique of model architectures. These are related but not identical claims. The title is also very broad, arguably too broad. “Temporal graph models fail…” is a sweeping statement, whereas the evidence here is really about current methods on TGB-style dynamic link prediction under certain sampling/evaluation choices. That is still a worthwhile contribution, but the framing overshoots.

A few specifics: I wanted more ablations on the “recently popular” baseline design choices (window size, decay, node cold-start handling). The paper mentions these, but not with enough detail for me. Also, on p. 6–7 the argument about impossibility of ranking under saturation is intuitive, but I wanted either a cleaner theoretical statement or a more controlled synthetic experiment. Right now it’s suggestive rather than airtight.

Overall, I lean positive because this is the kind of paper the area needs: annoying, a bit deflationary, but probably correct in the main takeaway. I’m just less convinced by the breadth of the claims than by the empirical warning sign.

I went into this paper pretty interested, because in principle “intrinsic, multi-scale comparison of data distributions” is exactly the kind of thing people keep claiming to do, and usually it turns out to be some dressed-up mean/covariance comparison. Here the authors are at least aiming at something more ambitious. The core idea — using a lower bound to a spectral Gromov-Wasserstein distance to compare manifolds via intrinsic geometry — is interesting, and I do think the motivation on p. 1–2 is solid. The emphasis on unaligned data and differing ambient dimensions is also a real plus. That part felt meaningful rather than cosmetic.

That said, I found the paper harder to trust than I expected. The main technical object is introduced cleanly enough, but I had to reread the derivation around Eq. (3)–(6) a couple of times, and I’m still not entirely sure what is genuinely new versus a repackaging of known spectral GW relaxations. The phrase “first-of-its-kind” in the abstract is too strong for my taste. There is a lot of prior work around intrinsic/spectral comparisons of metric measure spaces, and the paper does not do enough to separate itself conceptually. Maybe I’m missing something, but the novelty claim feels overstated.

I also thought the empirical section was a bit selective. Figure 2 is nice qualitatively, and Table 1 shows the method can distinguish some synthetic manifold perturbations better than FID-like baselines, but the experiments are mostly set up in regimes favorable to intrinsic geometry. Fine, but then I wanted more stress tests: sample complexity, sensitivity to graph construction / neighborhood size, robustness to noise, and actual runtime scaling. The method sounds expensive, and the discussion around complexity near p. 6 is pretty hand-wavy. In my experience, these spectral manifold methods become brittle surprisingly fast once the graph Laplacian is even slightly misestimated.

The generative model evaluation part (Table 2 / Figure 4) is promising but not fully convincing. I liked the idea, but the comparison to standard metrics felt underdeveloped. If the claim is that this captures “all data moments,” that is a very big statement, and the experiments don’t really substantiate it.

So overall: interesting paper, definitely not trivial, and there is something here. But I think the presentation oversells the theory and the experimental validation is not yet at the level I’d want for a top venue. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the novelty and shore up the empirical story.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more compelling than I expected from the title. The basic question—can we recover most of AdamW’s optimization behavior under a much tighter memory budget—is important, and this is one of those practical systems/optimization papers that people will probably try immediately if they train LLMs on limited hardware.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the way the paper motivates its main claim. On p.2–3, the argument that “adaptive learning rate is more critical than momentum” is based on empirical ablations, but I think this is a bit too strong given the evidence shown. If I’m reading Table 1 correctly, the gap between variants depends a lot on the task/setup, and I didn’t see enough runs to rule out optimizer-tuning effects. In my experience, these conclusions are annoyingly fragile. I would tone that down.

The core method is reasonably clear, though I had to reread the description around Eq. (6)–(9) a couple of times. The use of non-negative matrix factorization for the second-moment state is clever, but the paper is somewhat slippery about approximation error. Figure 2 gives the intuition, but I wanted a more explicit discussion of when this factorization hurts, especially for layers whose gradient statistics are not well captured by the assumed structure. Maybe I missed it, but I did not see a serious failure case analysis. Grouped update normalization also feels a little bolted on. It seems to help in Table 3, sure, but then the method is not just “Adam-style adaptivity at low memory”; it is adaptivity plus an extra stabilization trick. That’s fine, but the presentation undersells this.

On the experimental side, I liked that the paper includes both instruction tuning and continued pretraining. That makes the claims broader. The memory savings are meaningful, and the comparison to LOMO/AdamW is the right one. Still, I was frustrated by the limited reporting of variance and compute cost. On p.6, the throughput/runtime overhead is mentioned only briefly. For a method targeting resource-constrained training, wall-clock and implementation complexity matter a lot. Also, several results are “on par” in a loose sense, but some deltas look nontrivial depending on the benchmark.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical value, but not quite as airtight scientifically as it wants to be. I lean positive because the idea is useful and the results are mostly convincing, but I would want the camera-ready to clarify the claims, add ablations around the factorization rank/grouping choices, and be more upfront about where AdaLomo does not match AdamW.

I found the paper timely and, honestly, a bit unsettling in a way that usually means the authors are pointing at a real problem. The core idea—treating LLM-based retrieval/selection systems as targets for a kind of adversarial SEO—is intuitive in hindsight, but I had not seen it framed this cleanly before. The examples around product recommendations and plugin selection are especially convincing. Figure 1 makes the threat model legible very quickly, and the prisoner's-dilemma framing later in the paper is not just rhetoric; I think it actually helps explain why this could get bad fast in deployment.

That said, I had a mixed reaction to the empirical section. The attacks on Bing and Perplexity are attention-grabbing, sure, but after reading the setup twice I still wasn’t fully clear on how controlled these evaluations are. On p. 5-6, the distinction between manipulating the model’s summarization versus its retrieval/ranking pipeline feels blurry. If the attack works because the model is directly reading attacker-controlled text and then parroting its preferences, that is important, but it is slightly different from “search engine optimization” in the classic sense. Maybe that is the point, but the terminology risks overstating what component is being attacked. I might be wrong here, but I wanted a cleaner decomposition.

I also wanted more quantitative rigor. Table 1 is useful, but the sample sizes and prompt distributions felt a bit thin for the breadth of the claims. Some of the examples are compelling anecdotes rather than robust measurement. In my experience reviewing security/ML systems papers, once you claim “production systems are vulnerable,” readers will immediately ask about reproducibility and variance across prompts, time, and model updates. The paper gestures at this, but not enough. The plugin experiments are actually stronger than the search-engine ones, I think, because the mechanism is more direct.

The game-theoretic discussion (around p. 8, if I’m remembering right) is interesting but underdeveloped. It reads more like a useful intuition than a real model. That’s okay, but then I would present it more modestly.

Overall I lean positive because the paper identifies a real and underexplored threat surface, and the attacks are memorable enough that I suspect people will cite this. But I do think the evaluation is a little too eager and the conceptual boundaries are occasionally fuzzy. Still, this feels like a conference paper, not a blog post. I’d probably support acceptance, with some hesitation.

I had to read this twice, and I’m still not fully convinced I understand what the actual technical novelty is supposed to be. The paper proposes to combine a nonlinear feature transformation based on CPD with manifold embedding for unsupervised metric learning, with the end goal of improving clustering. That is a reasonable direction, and I do think the intuition is fine: deform the space so clusters become more separable, then embed. But the presentation is pretty murky.

The biggest issue for me is that the method description feels under-specified. Around p. 3–4, where the objective is introduced (I assume Eq. (4) and the following derivation are the core), it is not clear what exactly is being optimized jointly and what is alternating. The paper says metric learning and embedding are “integrated and conducted simultaneously,” but the algorithm box later reads more like a staged procedure. I might be wrong, but I could not reconstruct the optimization from the text alone. That is a serious problem for a methods paper.

I also found the role of CPD confusing. CPD is a registration/deformation model; using it as a metric-learning mechanism is interesting, but the paper does not really justify why this particular deformation prior is appropriate for generic clustering data. On p. 5, the discussion around smoothness/coherence sounds plausible, but it stays at the level of intuition. In my experience, these deformation-based methods can easily overfit local geometry and create “nice-looking” separations that do not generalize beyond the dataset at hand. I wanted a stronger argument here, maybe even a failure case.

The experiments are okay but not especially persuasive. Table 1 and Table 2 show improvements, but the baselines seem a bit selective. I was surprised not to see stronger deep clustering or even more classical manifold+spectral baselines, depending on the datasets. Figure 2 looks nice visually, sure, but these 2D scatter plots are always the easiest part. I care more about robustness: sensitivity to hyperparameters, runtime, and whether performance holds when cluster structure is weak. I didn’t see enough of that. Also, on the benchmark datasets, I’m not entirely sure whether the gains are statistically meaningful; variance reporting is either missing or too limited.

There is probably something here, and I do like the general idea more than I like the current execution. But right now the paper feels like a somewhat ad hoc pipeline wrapped in ambitious language. I lean negative. If the authors can clarify the optimization, better motivate CPD specifically, and strengthen the experimental section, this could become much more convincing.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a lot of “latent dynamics + invariance” papers over the last few years, and frankly many of them oversell fairly standard architectural choices. This one is better than that, but I still came away somewhat unconvinced by parts of the story.

The main idea — combining a VAE-style latent representation with a temporal/spatiotemporal transformer and then baking in scientifically motivated invariances — is sensible, and on p. 3–4 the framing is actually pretty clear. I appreciated that the authors are not forcing everything through a neural ODE, which in practice is often more trouble than it’s worth. The continuous-time claim is interesting, and the efficiency/scalability angle is believable, at least relative to ODE-based baselines. Figure 1 gives a decent overview, though I had to go back to it after reading Section 3 because the distinction between the invariance mechanism and the temporal interpolation mechanism was not immediately obvious to me.

Where I struggled is that the paper often gestures at theory without really delivering much. The “simple theoretical analyses” (I assume this is around the proposition/equation development in Section 4) felt fairly lightweight. Maybe I’m being harsh, but the results read more like sanity-check observations than real guarantees. Equation (7) in particular seems to encode the core inductive bias, yet I did not find a precise discussion of when enforcing that constraint could actually hurt identifiability or expressivity. In dynamical systems this matters a lot. If I’m wrong and this is addressed later, it needs to be surfaced much more clearly.

The empirical section is the strongest part. I liked the intervention generalization experiments, and Table 2 / Table 3 suggest there is something real here beyond just fitting trajectories. But the comparison set felt a bit selective. If you want to argue this is a general framework for latent dynamics, I would expect stronger baselines from the recent sequence-modeling literature, not just the usual neural ODE family and a few autoregressive models. Also, in Figure 4 the gains look uneven across regimes, which is fine, but the text glosses over that. Some settings seem only marginally improved.

One thing I found confusing after reading this twice: what exactly is being made invariant, and at what level? Observation space? latent transition? decoder outputs? The wording around this on p. 5, especially near the model objective, is too slippery. In my experience, papers in this area often blur equivariance, invariance, and robustness, and I think this one occasionally does too.

Overall, I think there is a promising method here, and the paper is probably above the bar technically, but it needs sharper exposition and a more honest treatment of limitations. I’m leaning weak accept if the experimental details are solid and reproducibility is good; otherwise weak reject.

I found the core idea here pretty intuitive, and honestly more sensible than a lot of recent ILfO papers that just keep piling machinery onto occupancy matching. The paper argues that in “progress-dependent” tasks, standard proxy-reward ILfO methods fail because rewards from later parts of the trajectory interfere with learning earlier behaviors. I think this is basically right, and Figure 1 makes the point clearly. The proposed fix—automatically scheduling the RL discount factor so the agent first focuses on near-term progress, then later parts of the task—sounds almost too simple, but in this case that is a compliment.

That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper pins down what is genuinely new versus what is a rebranding of curriculum learning through discount manipulation. Eq. (6) is the heart of the method, but the justification for that specific scheduling rule felt a bit heuristic. Maybe that’s okay, but then I would have liked a stronger ablation isolating whether ADS is really doing something beyond “start with low gamma, increase over time.” Table 1 shows strong gains, yes, but I wanted one dumb baseline like a hand-designed monotonic gamma schedule. Without that, the “automatic” part is a little under-substantiated.

I also think the “progress dependency property” is underdefined. The discussion around p. 3–4 is suggestive, but not mathematically crisp. In my experience, if you introduce a task class/property as the motivation for the whole paper, you should either characterize it more formally or be more modest and call it an intuition. Right now it sits awkwardly in between.

The experiments are overall solid. Nine Meta-World tasks is a decent spread, and the results in Table 2/Table 3 are strong enough that I do believe the method is useful. I especially appreciated that some tasks are basically unsolved by prior methods. But I was bothered by the limited diversity of domains. Everything is still Meta-World-style robotic manipulation. Since the pitch is “Internet video” and action-free imitation broadly, the empirical scope feels narrower than the framing.

One smaller thing: the explanation of the proxy reward construction on p. 5 was too compressed. I might be wrong, but I had to infer some implementation details from the appendix.

Overall, I lean positive. The idea is simple, practical, and apparently effective. I don’t think this is a deeply principled paper, and parts of the framing oversell the conceptual novelty, but I can easily see people using this. For me this is around borderline accept to weak accept.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more ambitious than most ML-for-docking submissions. The main idea—doing score-based diffusion directly over rigid-body docking while using MaSIF-style surface representations—is sensible, and in places quite elegant. I especially liked the two-stage setup on p.3–4: first localizing likely interface regions and then restricting the generative docking model to those patches. In practice this kind of pruning matters a lot, and the authors are right that it buys them the budget for a more expressive equivariant model. Figure 1 communicates the pipeline well.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the empirical story is as clean as the paper suggests. The new dataset split meant to reduce interface leakage is probably valuable, but the exact construction on p.5 (and I think Appendix B) needs more transparency. How sensitive are the results to the structural similarity threshold? I might be wrong, but I worry the proposed split is doing a lot of the work in making baselines look worse, especially methods that rely more directly on template-like geometric memorization. If that’s the point, fine, but then I want a more systematic ablation.

I also found the diffusion formulation under-explained. Eq. (4)–(6) gives the score objective over rigid transformations, but the practical parameterization of SO(3) noise and the reverse process is described a bit too briskly. Maybe this is obvious to people working on SE(3) diffusion every day, but for a docking paper it needs to be clearer. Figure 2 helps, but not enough. Similarly, Table 2 is promising, yet I wanted variance over multiple runs. These models can move around a lot depending on training seed.

The comparison to classical docking tools is a little slippery. The claim in the abstract about “considerably fewer numbers of generated decoys” is appealing, but on p.6/Table 3 the accounting is not apples-to-apples enough for me. Traditional methods often generate many decoys because scoring is cheap and broad search is the point. If the argument is efficiency, wall-clock or compute budget should be reported more directly.

Still, I think there is real substance here. The surface-centric representation is a good fit for transient interfaces, and the qualitative examples in Fig. 4 are compelling. I’m moderately positive. Not a slam dunk, but better than the average generative biology paper I’ve seen recently, many of which are much more hand-wavy. My recommendation would depend on rebuttal, especially around the split construction and evaluation protocol.

I read this with interest because I’ve seen a lot of “simpler end-to-end ASR” papers over the years, and this one is actually more grounded than many of them. The basic idea — a letter-based ConvNet acoustic model with GLUs and no phonetic lexicon — is appealing, and on LibriSpeech the results are undeniably strong. Table 2 and Table 3 (if I’m reading the setup correctly) show the system is competitive with much more complicated pipelines, especially given the relatively plain MFSC frontend. I also appreciated that the paper does not overclaim novelty at the full-system level; it is more of a careful engineering paper with a few smart choices.

That said, I found the paper a bit frustrating in places. The central technical claim seems to be that Gated Linear Units plus aggressive dropout are key, but the evidence for this is thinner than I wanted. The ablation around the acoustic model architecture (around p.4–5) is suggestive, but not really definitive. I would have liked a cleaner comparison between plain conv blocks, ReLU-based variants, and GLU variants under matched parameter count and training budget. As written, I’m not entirely sure whether the gain is from the gating itself, the optimization behavior, or just more careful regularization. Equation (1) is standard enough, but the paper leans on it heavily without really unpacking why it should be especially well suited to frame/letter prediction.

I also found the decoder description a bit too terse. The claim that no lexicon is needed is important, but then the search still depends heavily on the word list and LM, and the exact interaction between acoustic scores and beam search is not explained as clearly as it should be. Figure 2 helped, but after reading that section twice I still had questions about whether this is truly a “simpler” inference stack or just a different one.

On the empirical side, the LibriSpeech focus is fine, but also a little narrow. In my experience, letter-based systems often look good on English read speech and become much less compelling in noisier or morphologically richer settings. I might be wrong, but the paper would be stronger with at least some evidence beyond this one benchmark.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar. Not a huge conceptual leap, but useful, credible, and likely to influence practice. My score is positive, though not enthusiastic.

I read this paper with fairly high expectations because long-term VOS with similar-looking instances is genuinely hard, and the abstract makes a pretty strong claim. My overall reaction is positive, but I’m not completely convinced by all of the evidence yet.

The main idea — combining a spatial-semantic block with a masked cross-attention mechanism for query propagation — is sensible, and honestly this is the kind of design that tends to matter in VOS more than yet another huge backbone. I liked the intuition around associating global semantics with local spatial dependencies. Figure 2 helped, although I had to go back and forth between that and the method description on p.4-5 a couple of times because the notation is a bit dense. Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) in particular are not explained as clearly as they should be; I think I understand what is being masked and where the discriminative object query comes from, but the text around it felt compressed.

The empirical results are strong. Table 1 and Table 2 are impressive, especially if the reported DAVIS2017 and YouTubeVOS numbers are under the same protocol as prior work. I assume they are, but this needs to be stated very explicitly. In my experience, VOS papers can look “SOTA” while quietly changing backbone, input resolution, memory setting, or test-time tricks. Table 3’s ablation is useful, and it does suggest both the spatial-semantic block and masked cross-attention contribute. Still, I wanted more diagnosis. For example, does the gain mainly come from better handling occlusion, similar instances, or long-range drift? The paper keeps claiming robustness to all three, but I did not see a convincing per-scenario breakdown. Some qualitative examples in Fig. 4 look good, yes, but they are cherry-picked, as usual.

I also found the novelty a bit incremental. I don’t mean that as a dismissal — incremental ideas can still be valuable if they really work — but the paper is very much in the current transformer/query-propagation trend. The semantic embedding + spatial modeling decomposition is reasonable, though not obviously conceptually new. What saves the paper for me is that the engineering seems thoughtful and the benchmark coverage is broad, including MOSE and LVOS.

One thing that bothered me: efficiency is under-discussed. On p.7 (I think around the implementation details), there is not enough transparency on training cost and inference speed. For a method targeting long videos, this matters a lot.

So: good paper, likely accept from me, but not an enthusiastic one. I think the results are real and the method is useful, but the presentation could be clearer and the claims should be toned down a bit unless the authors provide sharper analysis.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than many “new calibration method” papers that mostly just add another post-hoc trick. The core message — that calibration numbers by themselves can be deeply misleading, and that one should report them together with predictive/generalization quantities like NLL — is important and, in my view, overdue. I’ve had the same reaction reading recent calibration papers where ECE goes down dramatically while the predictor is obviously being made less informative. So I’m sympathetic to the motivation here.

The examples early in the paper (I’m referring especially to the discussion around the trivial recalibration constructions on pp. 2–4) make the point clearly. I also liked the visualization idea extending reliability diagrams; Figure 1 and the later examples are intuitive, and the Bregman decomposition gives it more substance than just “yet another plot.” In particular, the connection between calibration error and excess divergence is neat, and I think the paper does a good job tying together several threads from the literature.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the theoretical contribution is as sharp as the paper sometimes suggests. The results relating full calibration error and confidence calibration error are interesting, but after reading Theorem 3 / the surrounding discussion twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure what the practical takeaway is beyond “confidence calibration can hide pathology,” which we mostly knew already. Maybe that is unfair — perhaps the novelty is in the exact Bregman setting — but the paper could do more to spell out where this changes what practitioners should actually compute. Likewise, the kernel regression consistency result is technically nice, but it felt somewhat detached from the empirical story. I wanted more discussion of bandwidth sensitivity; right now the estimator seems a bit idealized.

I also thought some of the empirical section was thinner than it should be for a paper making a strong claim about evaluation practice. Table 1 is suggestive, but I would have liked broader coverage of modern settings (foundation-model style calibration, distribution shift, class imbalance). Maybe that’s outside scope, but then the paper should be a little less sweeping. Also, a few places are too quick: the paragraph around Eq. (6) on p. 6 could be clearer, and I found Figure 3 harder to parse than the authors probably expect.

Overall, though, I think this is a solid paper. Not flashy, but thoughtful, and in my experience these “reassessment” papers often age well if they are careful. I’d lean accept, mainly because the main warning is correct and the proposed visualization seems genuinely useful.

I have mixed feelings about this paper. The core idea is ambitious and, honestly, pretty appealing: a single system that can take in and emit text/image/video/audio in arbitrary combinations. If it worked as cleanly as the paper suggests, this would be a meaningful step beyond the now-standard “multimodal in, text out” setup. I’ve read a lot of recent MM-LLM papers, and this one is at least aiming at the right problem.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I still found the actual technical novelty somewhat thinner than the framing implies. The architecture in Fig. 2 is basically an LLM plus modality-specific encoders/decoders connected through projection layers, with diffusion models doing the heavy lifting on the generation side. I don’t mean that dismissively—systems work matters—but I’m not convinced the paper clearly separates what is genuinely new here from careful engineering of existing components. The claim around tuning only ~1% parameters is nice, but also not that surprising given how much pretrained machinery is being reused.

I was also confused by the training/data story. The MosIT dataset and “modality-switching instruction tuning” are presented as central contributions, but the description felt under-specified. On p. 5–6 (around the dataset construction discussion), I wanted much more detail about data composition, annotation protocol, failure cases, and especially coverage of cross-modal instruction types. How many examples actually involve nontrivial any-to-any transfer, rather than text acting as the hidden pivot? I might be wrong, but a lot of the examples read like “multimodal in -> language reasoning -> modality-specific decoder out,” which is useful but weaker than the title suggests.

The evaluation is another issue. Table 1 / Table 2 report a broad collection of results, but the comparisons felt uneven and, in places, not entirely fair. For generation quality, there is a strong dependence on the external decoder backbone, so it’s hard to attribute gains to NExT-GPT itself. The human evaluation helps, but it is not described rigorously enough for me to fully trust it. I also would have liked more ablations: what happens without MosIT, with fewer modalities, or with frozen vs partially tuned projection modules? Eq. (3) and the surrounding objective discussion are quite light relative to the breadth of the claims.

Still, I don’t want to be overly negative. The demos/examples are compelling, and the paper is directionally important. In my experience, these unified multimodal systems are messy to build, and the authors clearly put in substantial effort. But as a conference paper, I think it currently overclaims relative to the evidence. I’d lean borderline: interesting system paper, but the empirical validation and methodological clarity need to be stronger.

I found the paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea — learning a shared continuous action latent across multiple environments and grounding it in Lie structure — is genuinely appealing. I’ve seen a lot of recent world model papers that discretize everything and then quietly lose the compositional/control story, so I appreciate what this paper is trying to do. The pitch in the abstract is strong, and Figures 1 and 2 do a good job conveying the intuition. In particular, the notion of “inter-environment” transfer via a learned control interface feels more ambitious than the usual single-domain latent dynamics setup.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as clean or as general as the paper claims. A lot seems to hinge on the specific inductive bias from the Lie action parameterization, but the actual mechanism is not explained as clearly as it should be. Around Eq. (3)–(6), I had to reconstruct for myself what is learned jointly and what is assumed fixed. The object-centric autoencoder is also under-motivated. Is object factorization actually necessary for the cross-environment action alignment, or is it just helping on the chosen datasets? I think the paper never really disentangles those two contributions. The ablations in Table 2 help a bit, but not enough.

I also worry that the empirical section is overselling “minimal or no action labels.” On page 6 (if I’m reading the setup correctly), some environments still seem to require partial supervision or environment-specific adaptation machinery. That’s fine, but then the framing should be more careful. “No action labels” and “quickly adapt” mean different things to different readers, and in my experience these claims often hide a lot of tuning. Figure 4 looks promising, but I wanted more quantitative evidence of transfer under genuinely novel action compositions, not just visually plausible rollouts.

Another issue is baselines. The comparisons feel a bit selective. If the point is continuous/compositional control across environments, then I would expect stronger representation-learning and multi-task dynamics baselines, possibly even non-Lie ones with matched capacity. Right now, it’s hard to know whether the gains come from the mathematical structure or just from a better latent bottleneck.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I like the direction. But the paper currently feels more like an intriguing prototype than a fully convincing conference paper. I’m leaning borderline. If the authors can clarify the training objective, tighten the claims, and strengthen the transfer evaluation, I’d be much more positive.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core motivation is reasonable, and honestly I agree with the premise more than I agree with the execution: most video captioning work does flatten events into bags of actions, and the causal/temporal linkage is often missing. So at a high level, yes, this is a real problem. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the proposed benchmark and model really establish progress on “causal-temporal narrative” in a robust way.

The biggest issue for me is the dataset construction. On p.3–4, the CTN captions are generated by an LLM with few-shot prompting. That immediately raises the question: are we evaluating causal reasoning, or just alignment to the style and priors of the generator model? The examples in Fig. 2 look plausible, but plausibility is not the same as faithfulness to the video. I kept looking for a stronger validation of the generated captions beyond spot examples. Table 1 gives statistics, but not enough evidence that the cause-effect relations are actually grounded in the visual content rather than inferred from commonsense stereotypes. In my experience, this is exactly where synthetic annotation pipelines quietly fail.

I also found the model story somewhat underdeveloped. The Cause-Effect Network sounds more novel than it felt in the method section. Eq. (3)–(6) basically instantiate separate streams and then fuse them; I might be missing something, but this seems closer to a structured inductive bias than a genuinely new learning formulation. That is not necessarily bad, but then the claims should be toned down. The wording in the abstract and on p.2 (“understands… intricate causal-temporal narrative structures”) is too strong for what is shown.

The experimental gains are decent, especially in Table 3 and Table 4, but they rely heavily on CIDEr/BLEU-style metrics, which are a weak fit here. If the whole point is causality and temporal unfolding, I would expect a targeted human evaluation or at least a relation-level metric. The qualitative examples in Fig. 4 help a bit, though some outputs still read like polished paraphrases rather than real narrative understanding.

One more thing: the cross-dataset generalization claim is interesting, but I’m not entirely sure the setup is clean enough to support that conclusion, given both datasets are transformed by the same caption-generation recipe. That could induce shared artifacts.

Overall, I like the direction, and I think this paper may inspire useful follow-up work. But right now it feels a bit too benchmark-driven and LLM-bootstrapped for me to fully trust the conclusions. Weak accept / borderline if the bar for novelty is lower; otherwise I lean reject.

I found the paper interesting, and the basic intuition makes sense: historical descriptive text and future-facing predictive text are not the same signal, so treating them separately could help. That part felt well motivated to me, especially in the discussion around the two text roles on p.2–3. In my experience, a lot of multimodal forecasting papers kind of mush all text together and hope attention sorts it out, so I appreciate the attempt to structure the problem more explicitly.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper as it stands. The main issue is that the empirical story feels a bit thinner than the architectural complexity would suggest. The proposed method has at least three moving parts — the contrastive alignment loss (Eq. 4), the history-oriented interaction module, and the future-oriented interaction module — but when I got to the ablations in Table 3, I still wasn’t sure which component is truly carrying the gains. The drops are there, yes, but they are not always large or consistent across datasets. I read that section twice and still came away unsure whether the “dual” design is essential or just a reasonable implementation choice.

I also had some concerns about the predictive text setup. On p.5 (around the dataset/task construction), it seems the model is given future textual insights that are assumed to be available at inference time. This may be fine for some domains, but it is a pretty strong assumption, and I think the paper undersells how restrictive it is. In some applications this is realistic; in many others it absolutely is not. The paper should be much clearer about what exact forecasting setting is being considered. Right now the framing feels a little too broad.

Figure 2 helped, but the method description is honestly pretty dense. The distinction between the two modality interaction modules is conceptually simple, yet the notation around Eq. 6–9 made it harder than necessary to follow. I might be wrong, but I also wonder whether there is some leakage risk depending on how the predictive text is aligned temporally with the target horizon. The paper should address that explicitly, not just implicitly.

On the positive side, the results in Table 2 are generally good, and I liked that the authors included both synthetic and public datasets. Still, I would have liked stronger baselines, especially more recent LLM-assisted or foundation-model-style multimodal forecasters. The claim of “advanced multimodal comprehension capability” felt overstated to me; this is a specialized architecture, not really evidence of broad multimodal understanding.

Overall, I think this is a promising paper, but not yet a fully convincing one. My current leaning is weak reject: good idea, some nice engineering, but the evaluation and problem formulation need to be sharper.

I like the basic idea here, and I think the paper is aiming at a real bottleneck in current LLM safety/evals work: we keep saying “rule-following” matters, but then measure it with expensive, ambiguous human annotations. A benchmark with programmatic checking is genuinely useful. The setup is also refreshingly concrete. The examples around simple games / security-style constraints made the paper easy to parse, and Figure 1 (if I understood it correctly) does a decent job motivating the interaction pattern.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the claim that BIND meaningfully captures “rule-following behavior” rather than a somewhat narrow form of prompt-robustness under handcrafted adversarial conversational templates. Maybe that is enough, maybe even valuable on its own, but the framing feels broader than the evidence. On p.3–4, where the authors describe the 15 scenarios, I kept wanting a clearer argument for why these scenarios are representative beyond being programmatically evaluable. In my experience, once you optimize for evaluator simplicity, you often end up studying artifacts of the evaluator.

The qualitative taxonomy of 6 attack categories is interesting, but also a bit under-theorized. It reads more like a useful empirical coding scheme than something principled. I’m not sure that’s a fatal issue, but the paper sometimes leans on it as if it were exhaustive. I doubt it is. Also, 862 handwritten test cases sounds impressive, but I found myself asking how much hidden leakage there is across cases within a scenario.
