Table 2 gives aggregate pass rates, but I would have liked more variance estimates or at least scenario-level breakdowns in the main text rather than pushing too much into appendices.

The adversarial optimization result driving Llama2/Vicuna to 0% is striking, though honestly not shocking. I’m a little skeptical about how much to infer from that beyond “these models are brittle under white-box-ish search.” The paper should be more careful there. Also, because the tasks are “trivially solvable by corresponding programs” (abstract; reiterated later), there is a weird mismatch between benchmarking general-purpose LLMs and benchmarking systems that could in principle call tools. That’s not necessarily bad, but it should be discussed more directly.

Overall I think this is a useful benchmark paper, and probably publishable, but I’m not as excited as the authors seem to be. Good engineering, relevant problem, decent empirical effort. The conceptual claims should be tightened. My leaning is weak accept.

I found this paper interesting, mostly because it goes after something the community has been oddly willing to treat as “just preprocessing,” namely oracle construction. In my experience, these choices matter a lot more than papers usually admit. The central point here—that greedy ROUGE-based labeling is both suboptimal and artificially deterministic—is basically correct, and I appreciated that the authors actually formalize the downstream training target instead of only proposing yet another heuristic.

The paper is generally clear, but I had to read the method section twice to convince myself what is genuinely new versus a repackaging of marginalizing over oracle sets. The discussion around the expectation-based labels and the equivalence result (I think this is around Eq. 5–8 on p. 4) is probably the technical core, and it is reasonably elegant. That said, the “proof” felt a bit too lightweight. I’m not entirely sure all assumptions are made explicit, especially about the oracle distribution and whether it is induced by exact search or an approximation. If the expectation is computed over a restricted candidate set, then the interpretation changes a bit, no?

The empirical results are solid. Table 2 and Table 3 show pretty consistent gains, and I do like that the method is tested across languages rather than only CNN/DM again. Figure 2 was also helpful. Still, I was left wanting more ablations. How much of the gain comes from using multiple oracles at all, versus the specific expectation objective? And how sensitive is this to the quality/size of the oracle pool? I may have missed it, but I didn’t see a convincing runtime discussion either. If this is much more expensive than greedy labeling, that matters in practice, especially for larger corpora.

One thing that bothered me: the paper sometimes oversells the novelty. People have been aware for years that greedy oracle extraction is imperfect; the stronger claim here is really that a soft target derived from multiple oracle summaries is a better supervision signal. That is useful, yes, but not revolutionary. Also, some of the zero-shot claims on p. 6 felt a little hand-wavy. Better transfer is nice, but I would be cautious about attributing that specifically to “expectation” rather than simple regularization from softer labels.

Overall, I think this is a good paper. Not flashy, but thoughtful and practically relevant. I’d lean accept, though not enthusiastically. The idea is simple in a good way, and the results look real, but the paper could be more precise about computational cost and the exact source of the gains.

I read this paper with some interest because dynamic ANN benchmarking is genuinely underdeveloped, and in practice people still choose libraries mostly by folklore. So I like the basic motivation a lot. The paper is trying to do something useful rather than proposing yet another index with a tiny gain on one dataset, and I appreciate that.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as comprehensive or as careful as it claims. The central contribution is basically a benchmarking framework plus a study over dynamic workloads, but after reading it twice I still found the workload design a bit under-justified. The scenarios on p. 4–5 are said to be “modelled after common real world applications,” but the mapping from actual applications to the insert/delete/query schedules feels fairly loose. I might be wrong, but several of the update processes looked synthetic in a way that could strongly favor certain structures. Figure 2 is helpful, though; at least it makes the benchmark pipeline concrete.

I also wanted a more precise accounting of what is being measured. On p. 6 around Eq. (3), the notion of “runtime” seems to bundle together different costs depending on the method, but I was not sure whether rebuild/amortization costs are always included consistently. For dynamic ANN that detail is everything. If one method lazily accumulates updates and another eagerly maintains the structure, query-time comparisons can become a little misleading. Table 1 lists supported operations, but it would help to explicitly mark which methods need periodic full rebuilds and how that cost is charged.

The empirical section is useful but somewhat narrow. I was surprised not to see stronger discussion of memory footprint and hardware sensitivity. In my experience with HNSW-like methods, memory overhead and cache behavior dominate many deployment decisions, especially at “big” scale. Figure 4 and Table 3 focus mostly on recall/runtime tradeoffs, which is standard, but for this paper memory really should be first-class. Also, the claim in the abstract that “in all cases” a suitable tradeoff is found is a bit too self-congratulatory; beating exhaustive search is a low bar, frankly.

I do think the paper has value as infrastructure. If released and maintained, this could be a useful benchmark suite. But as a conference paper, I wanted either more rigor in the benchmark design/analysis or sharper insights that go beyond “different methods win in different dynamic regimes.” Right now it feels solidly useful, but not especially deep. My leaning is weak reject, though I’m on the fence because the community probably does need this.

I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The core idea — storing old knowledge as a small set of quantized reference vectors in a learned embedding space, then expanding this set for new few-shot tasks — is actually pretty reasonable, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to address both classification and regression in one framework. That said, I found the paper harder to evaluate than it should be, partly because several important design choices are only vaguely justified.

The main method becomes clear around p. 3–4, especially Eq. (3)–(6), but I had to read that section twice. I think the paper is basically proposing a prototype/LVQ-style memory in deep feature space with some regularization and calibration tricks. That is not necessarily a bad thing, but the novelty felt incremental to me. In particular, the “less forgetting” regularization and the reference vector calibration on p. 5 read more like practical add-ons than a substantially new algorithmic contribution. I might be missing something, but I did not come away convinced that vector quantization itself is the key reason the method works, as opposed to just having a decent embedding plus nearest-neighbor style prediction.

I also worry about the experimental framing. The claims are broad (“outperforms SOTA methods in incremental learning”), but from the tables (Table 1 and 2 especially), it is not always obvious that the comparisons are apples-to-apples. Some baselines seem designed for class-incremental settings with stronger data availability assumptions, which of course makes the proposed few-shot setup look distinct, but then the paper should be more careful in how it positions the comparison. I would have liked stronger ablations: number of reference vectors per class/task, sensitivity to the quantization budget, effect of the calibration step, and whether Eq. (8) for regression really adds anything beyond a standard kernel smoother on frozen features. Figure 2 gives some intuition, but honestly it is a bit schematic and does not reveal failure modes.

Another issue is clarity. The discussion of intra-class variation on p. 4 is hand-wavy, and I was not entirely sure how the method behaves when class distributions overlap strongly in the embedding space. In my experience, nearest-neighbor methods can look great in low-shot benchmarks and then degrade badly once the representation shifts under sequential updates. The paper says catastrophic forgetting is mitigated, but I wanted more direct evidence than aggregate accuracy curves.

Overall, I think there is a useful idea here, and I do like the attempt to handle regression, which many incremental learning papers ignore. But the paper oversells the novelty and underspecifies key methodological details. My leaning is borderline reject unless the authors can better disentangle what is genuinely new and provide more convincing ablations.

I found the paper reasonably clear, but also a bit narrower than it initially sounds. The core idea—replace accuracy-reject curves with precision-reject and recall-reject curves when accuracy is a poor metric, especially under imbalance—is sensible and, honestly, kind of obvious in hindsight. That is not necessarily a criticism; simple evaluation ideas can still be useful. But the paper needs to work harder to convince me that this rises to the level of a substantial contribution rather than a straightforward adaptation of an existing plotting protocol.

After reading it twice, I think the main value is practical rather than methodological. The definitions of the recall-reject and precision-reject curves are easy to follow, and I appreciated that the authors ground things first on artificial data before moving to imbalanced and medical settings. The discussion around imbalanced data is directionally correct. In my experience, people overuse accuracy in exactly these settings, so I sympathize with the motivation.

That said, I had several issues. First, the scope feels oddly tied to prototype-based classifiers / LVQ. I’m not entirely sure why this paper needs to be so centered on that family. The proposed curves are classifier-agnostic evaluation tools, so limiting most experiments to LVQ variants makes the empirical case weaker than it should be. If anything, I would want to see standard probabilistic baselines, maybe even a badly calibrated neural net and a calibrated one, to show how these curves behave in more familiar settings. As it stands, the paper risks reading like “here is a plotting variant demonstrated on our preferred models.”

Second, I found some of the interpretation claims a bit overstated. The abstract says the proposed curves “yield more accurate insights” than accuracy reject curves. Maybe, but “more aligned with the target metric” would be a safer and more precise statement. Precision and recall each discard information in their own way, and precision in particular is prevalence-sensitive. I kept waiting for a more nuanced discussion of when each reject curve can be misleading. If that is somewhere in the later pages, it should be emphasized more.

I also wanted more formalism around edge cases. For high rejection rates, precision/recall can behave strangely depending on class counts. The paper should be explicit about undefined or unstable regions, confidence intervals, and whether comparisons in Figures / Tables are statistically meaningful. I might be wrong, but I did not see enough on variance.

Overall, I think this is a useful evaluation note, but not a particularly deep one. I’m leaning borderline. With broader experiments and a more careful discussion of limitations, I’d be much more positive.

I found the paper interesting, and in some sense timely, because there really is a gap between the elegant equal-risk pricing literature and anything that scales beyond toy dynamic programming setups. The main idea here — adapting DDPG to a risk-averse MDP with dynamic expectile risk — is not crazy at all, and I do think there is some novelty in combining these ingredients. The motivation on p. 2–3 is pretty clear, and I appreciated that the authors are at least trying to move beyond “single asset, known model, finite tree” examples that dominate this line of work.

That said, after reading this twice, I still found the technical contribution somewhat hard to pin down. The algorithmic novelty of ACRL, as described around Algorithm 1 and the discussion near Eq. (10)–(13), feels modest. It seems to be largely DDPG with a modified target induced by the nested expectile recursion. Maybe I’m underselling it, but the paper needs to be much more explicit about what is genuinely new algorithmically versus what is a fairly direct transplantation of existing actor-critic machinery to a new Bellman operator. Right now it reads a bit like “we replace expectation by expectile and then train.” If that is the contribution, the burden is on the experiments/theory to really convince me.

I also struggled with the treatment of time consistency. This is central to the paper’s pitch, but the exposition around the dynamic expectile construction (I think around Sec. 3, especially the equations defining the recursive risk measure) is too compressed. I’m not entirely sure the average ML reader will understand why the static baseline is unfair under re-evaluation at intermediate times, even though that is exactly the paper’s selling point. Figure 2 and Table 2 help, but not enough. I wanted a cleaner diagnostic showing the hedges/prices at time 0 and then the risk reassessment at time t under both methods, maybe pathwise or conditional.

The experiments are okay but a bit thin. One vanilla option and one basket option is not much, and the basket case still seems fairly low-dimensional from what I could tell. If the practical claim is “realistic settings: many assets or historical trajectories only,” then I expected either a genuinely higher-dimensional test or at least an ablation on scaling. Also, the comparison set is narrow. Why no comparison to simpler policy gradient or distributional RL baselines? Even a strong supervised hedging baseline would help. Table 1 reports accuracy, but I wanted variances/confidence intervals; RL results can be noisy, and here the gains do not look huge.

Overall, I think the paper has a real idea in it, and I’m sympathetic to the direction. But in its current form I’m left with “promising but not fully convincing.” If accepted, it should be because the community wants more work at the intersection of dynamic risk measures and RL, not because the empirical evidence is already overwhelming.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than a lot of recent “protein foundation model” submissions that are really just sequence models with a structure head bolted on. The main idea here—extending DPLM to jointly model sequence and structure via discrete structure tokens—is sensible, and I do think the paper makes a credible case that a single multimodal diffusion LM can do unconditional and conditional generation in one framework. Figure 1 and the discussion around the different joint/marginal/conditional generation modes are clear. I also liked the practical angle of the warm-up strategy; on p. 5–6 (around the training description), the way they leverage a pretrained sequence PLM before fully multimodal training feels like the kind of engineering choice that actually matters in this space.

That said, I came away with a somewhat mixed impression. The biggest issue for me is that the paper sometimes oversells “joint modeling” without fully convincing me that the gains come from genuinely better multimodal reasoning rather than just scaling plus decent discretization of structure. The structure tokenizer is central, but I found its description a bit too compressed. Eq. (3)–(5) and the tokenizer explanation on p. 4 are not as transparent as they should be. I had to read that section twice. In particular, I’m not entirely sure how much geometric fidelity is being lost and whether the lookup-free quantization is actually the right tradeoff versus continuous latent structure modeling. Table 1 gives some reconstruction-ish evidence, but I wanted more failure analysis.

The empirical section is solid but uneven. The unconditional joint generation results are probably the most exciting part, and Table 2/3 suggest the model is competitive. Still, there are too many benchmarks where the baselines are heterogeneous enough that direct comparison feels slippery. On inverse folding and folding, the paper claims “competitive” performance, which is fair, but not clearly state of the art. So the novelty is more in unification than raw numbers. That is okay, but then I wanted a deeper ablation on what specifically enables the unification: tokenizer choice, synthetic structure data, warm-up, diffusion objective, etc. The ablations in the later tables help, but not enough.

I also think the paper could be more candid about limitations. For example, what happens on larger proteins or flexible/disordered regions? Figure 4 looks nice, but cherry-picked qualitative examples are cheap in this literature, frankly.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably above the bar, mainly because it pushes toward a genuinely multimodal generative protein model rather than another pipeline of separate modules. But I’m not fully sold on all the claims, and the paper would benefit from clearer exposition and stronger ablations. My leaning is weak accept.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea — doing the operator learning in a complex fractional Fourier domain and keeping the network genuinely complex-valued rather than collapsing back to reals — is, in my view, a legitimate contribution. There is a lot of work in neural operators that is basically “yet another spectral parameterization,” and this is not quite that. The use of the fractional Fourier transform seems more than cosmetic here, at least based on the formulation around Eq. (6)–(9). I also appreciated that the paper goes beyond the usual headline benchmark table and includes noisy settings, OOD tests, and zero-shot super-resolution. In my experience, many operator papers claim “physics generalization” and then only show in-distribution test error.

That said, I had to read parts of Sections 3 and 4 twice. The motivation for why the complex domain should help is stated very confidently, but the actual mechanism is still somewhat hand-wavy. On p. 4, around the discussion after Eq. (10), the text implies that complex algebraic structure and phase information are crucial for PDE solution operators, but I’m not entirely sure this is demonstrated rather than assumed. Figure 2 is suggestive, not conclusive. I would have liked a much sharper ablation on what matters: is it the fractional transform, the complex-valued parameterization, or the aliasing-free activation? Table 3 helps, but not enough. Right now several ingredients are bundled together and the gains are hard to attribute.

I also worry about fairness of comparison. The paper claims “comparable or superior” performance everywhere, but in Table 1 and Table 2 some margins are tiny, and I could not tell whether all baselines were equally tuned for width/parameter count/training budget. Maybe I missed it, but the appendix details did not fully reassure me. If CoNO is larger or more expensive because of complex arithmetic, that needs to be stated plainly. Runtime and memory are oddly under-emphasized for a method that changes the whole representation. This bothered me.

Another issue: the writing occasionally oversells. “Effectively captures the underlying PDE with a single complex fractional Fourier transform” is a strong statement. I think the experiments show improved approximation quality, not that the PDE structure is somehow uniquely captured. Those are different claims.

Overall, I think this is above the bar for novelty and likely relevant to the community, but the paper needs a more careful causal story and cleaner experimental accounting. Tentative accept, though not a strong one.

I thought this was an interesting paper, and the core idea is fairly clear: use an approximate high-order feature interaction layer (PMT) to make point-level matching work in the very low-overlap regime that comes up in shape assembly. That setup makes sense, and in my experience a lot of standard registration machinery really does fall apart once the shared surface becomes tiny, so I appreciate that the paper is targeting a real failure mode rather than just repackaging generic point cloud matching.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical novelty. The PMT layer, as introduced around Sec. 3 and Eq. (4)/(5), feels like a fairly standard proxy/low-rank approximation idea applied to pairwise or higher-order matching. That’s not necessarily bad, but the paper sometimes sells it as more conceptually new than it is. I think the contribution is more “careful engineering for this problem” than a fundamentally new matching principle. The authors should probably be more honest about that.

I did like the empirical section overall. Table 1 and Table 2 show pretty consistent gains, and the efficiency claim seems mostly supported. Figure 1 also communicates the problem nicely. But I was frustrated by how incomplete some of the comparisons are. On page 6, the runtime/memory discussion is too hand-wavy unless all methods are evaluated under the same point count, hardware, and batch size. Maybe this is in the appendix, but it needs to be front-and-center if efficiency is a headline claim. Also, I’m not entirely sure the baselines are all tuned comparably for the low-overlap setting. Some methods are very sensitive to sampling density and correspondence filtering.

Another issue is clarity. I found the transition from the proxy construction to the final rigid transform estimation a bit abrupt; Fig. 3 helps, but the text around lines discussing the mating-surface correspondence extraction is dense and honestly harder than it should be. There’s also a question of robustness: how does PMT behave when fragments are geometrically ambiguous or partially damaged? Shape assembly datasets are often cleaner than reality. I kept waiting for a failure-case analysis, maybe in Fig. 5 or a dedicated table, and it never really came.

So overall: solid paper, probably above the bar for a specialized 3D vision audience, but not a slam dunk. I think the results are good enough that I lean positive, but the novelty is somewhat overstated and the evaluation should be more rigorous about efficiency/fairness.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to pin down. The core idea — learning a meta-level “correction” to the outputs of a base zero-shot learner — is intuitively appealing, and I do think there is something here. In particular, the episodic construction where the correction module sees the task module’s training set representation and learns to compensate for its biases/errors is a nice twist on fairly standard meta-learning. It’s not wildly revolutionary, but it is clever.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper isolates what is actually driving the gains. The method description around the correction network is reasonably clear at a high level, but the exact interface between task module and correction module felt under-specified in places. Around Eq. (3)/(4) I was expecting a more concrete explanation of what form the “training data representation” takes, how sensitive results are to that choice, and whether the correction is effectively just learning a residual calibration layer. Maybe I missed it, but this seems central. Figure 2 helped a bit, though I wish the caption were more informative.

The empirical results are strong on CUB and NAB, and Table 1/Table 2 look good. Still, these are also the standard datasets where incremental improvements can come from many small design choices. I would have liked a tougher set of ablations. For example: how much do you gain from episodic training alone, versus from conditioning on the support/training-set representation, versus simply increasing capacity of the base learner? Right now the paper mostly argues by final accuracy. In my experience reviewing zero-shot papers, that is not enough because these systems are often surprisingly brittle.

I also had some concern about fairness of comparison. The paper claims state of the art, but the baseline setup on page 6 (assuming I interpreted it correctly) seems to rely on natural language descriptions in a specific way that may not match all prior methods exactly. I might be wrong here, but I think this needs to be discussed much more carefully.

The writing is decent, but some claims are oversold. “Learns to perform zero-shot classification” is too broad; really it learns to improve a particular pre-trained learner under a specific episodic scheme. That’s narrower. Still useful, just narrower.

Overall, I lean positive because the idea is novel enough and the results are promising, but I’m not enthusiastic. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to clarify the mechanism and add stronger ablations.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent model-merging submissions I’ve seen. The core idea—turning merging into an alternating procedure between per-task tuning and merge steps—is quite natural in hindsight, but still nontrivial. I especially liked the motivation in the early theory section around the equivalence between task vectors and multi-task gradients in the one-epoch case (I think this is around Eq. 3–5, maybe page 3). That connection is intuitive, but the paper does a decent job making it explicit rather than hand-wavy.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the theoretical story is as clean as the paper wants it to be. The “first epoch dominates” argument is plausible and probably often true in practice, but it also felt a bit selective. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t sure what exact assumptions are really needed for the approximation to remain meaningful after multiple epochs. The upper-bound result later on also reads somewhat like the usual reassuring theory that explains a method after the fact rather than predicting when it should fail. Maybe I’m being unfair, but I wanted a sharper statement of scope. As written, the theory is supportive, not decisive.

Empirically, though, the paper is strong. The gains claimed in the abstract are large, and from the tables (I’m thinking Table 1/2 and maybe the NLP results later, around page 6–7), ATM seems consistently better than standard task arithmetic and other merging baselines. I appreciated that the authors tested both vision and NLP; too many papers stay in one sandbox. Figure 3 (if I recall correctly, the orthogonality analysis) was also useful, though I would have liked to see variance/error bars or at least some sense of run-to-run stability. These methods can be annoyingly seed-sensitive.

One thing I found confusing is the compute story. The abstract says “same data and computing requirements,” but ATM is iterative, so I’m not entirely sure in what sense compute is really the same. Same asymptotic order? Same number of examples touched? In practice, wall-clock and tuning complexity matter, and the paper felt a bit slippery there. This should be clarified, because otherwise the comparison sounds stronger than it is.

Overall, I think this is a good paper. Not flawless, and I’m a little skeptical of how general the theory is, but the method is simple, the empirical results look real, and the paper pushes model merging in a useful direction. I would lean accept.

I read this paper fairly carefully, and I’m still a bit torn. The core idea — using patch-level prototype distributions plus a refinement step to reduce negative transfer in UniNCD — is reasonable, and honestly this is one of the few submissions in this area that is at least trying to grapple with the actual failure mode of prototype methods rather than just reporting another incremental clustering trick. I appreciated the motivation on p.2, especially the discussion around private-known categories causing misalignment for novel classes. That part matches what I’ve seen in prior NCD / generalized category discovery work.

That said, I found the paper harder to trust than I wanted to. The Prototype Refinement module is described as a key contribution, but after reading Sec. 3.2 twice, I’m not entirely sure what is algorithmically new versus a confidence-based filtering of prototypes from unlabeled assignments. Eq. (6)–(8) gives the mechanics, but the actual criterion for “decoupling” common/private-known/novel categories still feels heuristic. Figure 2 gives the pipeline, but it is too high-level to resolve this confusion. If the main claim is that PR avoids negative transfer, I really wanted a sharper analysis or at least an error breakdown showing how often novel samples are incorrectly attracted to private-known prototypes before/after PR.

I also have mixed feelings about the patch semantics story. The patch-level prototypical distribution is intuitively appealing, yes, but there is a lot of language in Sec. 3.3 about “enriched semantics” that reads stronger than the evidence. In Table 3, the gains from adding the patch branch and the PEB term are there, but they are not huge, and I wonder whether this is mostly a regularization effect rather than semantic enhancement per se. Maybe that sounds nitpicky, but the paper really leans on that interpretation. Also, Eq. (11) for the entropy balance objective is plausible, though I found the sparse/uniform dual goal a bit internally contradictory until I got to the implementation details near the end of p.6.

The empirical results are solid overall. Table 1 is good, and the margin over prior work is nontrivial. Still, I would have liked more variance reporting; some of these benchmarks are noisy. The qualitative plots in Fig. 4 look nice but are, frankly, easy to cherry-pick.

My bottom line: interesting paper, likely above the average submission in this niche, but the method is somewhat over-claimed and under-explained. I lean weak accept if the bar is novelty + decent empirical improvement, but weak reject if the venue expects tighter technical clarity.

I found the paper reasonably motivated, and the problem is definitely practical. People do this informally all the time — train on 5%, 10%, 20% and extrapolate — so I appreciate a paper that tries to systematize it. The framing in the abstract is a bit broader than what the paper actually delivers, though. After reading it twice, I think the real contribution is not “learning curve estimation” in general, but a fairly specific empirical comparison of subset-sampling strategies for CNN image classification under a power-law assumption.

The paper is generally readable, but I had trouble pinning down what is actually novel. The framework section (around pp. 3–4) felt more like a setup than a methodological advance. If Eq. (2) is the core power-law model, then a lot hinges on when that model is appropriate, and I don’t think the paper interrogates that hard enough. Figure 1 makes the usual case that curves are approximately linear in log-log space, but “approximately” is doing a lot of work here. In my experience, especially with modern CNN training pipelines, augmentation, regularization, and optimizer schedules can make low-data behavior pretty non-power-law. I might be wrong, but the paper seems to treat deviations as noise rather than as a failure mode.

I did like the attempt to compare strategies under both simulated and real settings. Table 1 / Table 2 (assuming I’m reading them correctly) are useful. But the experimental section left me unconvinced in two ways. First, the image-classification benchmark suite seems a bit standard and limited; it’s not obvious the conclusions would transfer beyond the chosen CNNs and datasets. Second, the evaluation metric for a “good” strategy is somewhat underspecified. Is the goal ranking models correctly? estimating asymptotic accuracy? minimizing regret under a compute budget? These are not the same. Around the discussion near p. 6, I kept wanting a cleaner statement of the decision problem.

Another issue: the compute story is surprisingly thin for a paper whose entire premise is saving training time. I wanted a more explicit accounting, maybe in a figure, of wall-clock overhead of sampling multiple subset sizes versus just training fewer candidates longer. Right now the benefit feels plausible but not fully demonstrated.

Overall, I think this is a solid workshop paper, maybe, but for a top ML conference I don’t see enough conceptual depth. Useful idea, decent experiments, but the novelty is modest and the assumptions are stronger than the paper admits. My leaning is weak reject.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thought-provoking than the usual “add an intrinsic reward and get better coordination” submission. The core idea—rewarding an agent for being identifiable from its observation via a learned classifier—is simple, and I can see why it might induce role specialization. In that sense the paper has a nice intuition. Figure 1 helped, and the feedback loop between classifier confidence and behavioral differentiation is easy to grasp.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not entirely convinced the paper nails down what it means by “individuality” beyond separability in observation space. The classifier objective (around Eq. 3/4, if I’m reading correctly) seems to reward agents for occupying distinct observational regimes, but that is not obviously the same as useful division of labor. In some tasks, this could just encourage arbitrary symmetry breaking or even brittle conventions. The paper gestures at this distinction in the discussion, but I wanted a much sharper argument. Right now, “individuality” feels a little over-claimed.

I also had concerns about confounding. In several of the environments, agents already have asymmetric local observations or positions, so a classifier may become accurate for trivial reasons. If so, the intrinsic reward is partly free. The ablations in Table 2 help somewhat, but I really wanted a control where observations are symmetrized or agent IDs/starting positions are randomized more aggressively. Without that, it is hard to know whether EOI is producing emergent specialization or just amplifying existing asymmetry. This is a pretty central issue.

The empirical section is solid but a bit too favorable to the method. I may have missed it, but I did not see enough discussion of failure cases. Also, the dependence on the classifier capacity and reward weight seems important; Figure 4 suggests some sensitivity, yet the text moves on quickly. In my experience, these intrinsic-reward methods can be annoyingly fragile, and I don’t think the paper fully addresses that.

On the positive side, the method is lightweight and seems easy to plug into MAPPO/QMIX-style baselines, which I appreciate. The results are generally strong, and the regularizers are sensible, even if I’m less convinced they are principled than just useful tricks.

Overall, I lean positive on novelty and empirical value, but I’m not fully sold on the conceptual framing or the causal story behind the gains. Borderline accept for me, though not a clear one.

I found the core idea genuinely interesting. Instead of yet another defense on the classifier side, the paper asks whether the object itself can be redesigned to be more robust. That is a pretty refreshing angle, and honestly more practical than a lot of “certified defense” work that never survives contact with the real world. The physical experiments especially helped; Figure 1 and the examples later in the paper make the premise easy to grasp, and Table 1 suggests the gains are not just cosmetic.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what exactly is being optimized and against which threat model. The abstract says “attacker-agnostic,” but the method is still clearly tailored to patch-style attacks. So this is not attacker-agnostic in the broad sense, only patch-family-agnostic. I think the paper should be much more careful here because otherwise it overclaims. In my experience, wording like this tends to age badly.

I also struggled with the methodology section around the learning objective (I believe Eq. 3 / Eq. 4). The tradeoff between preserving sign recognizability for humans and improving machine robustness seems central, but the manuscript treats it a bit too casually. Is the human usability constraint hard or soft? Who decides when a redesigned sign is still acceptable? If this is only approximated by a perceptual similarity penalty, that is really not enough. For something as safety-critical as road signs, “looks similar” is not the same as “legally and cognitively valid.” This issue comes up again in the physical section where the signs are printed and photographed, but I did not see any human-subject evaluation or even a structured readability analysis.

The empirical results are promising, but I’m not fully convinced the baselines are strong enough. Comparing mainly to existing robust-sign techniques is fine, but I wanted to see whether simple adversarial patch training of the downstream classifier closes some of the gap. Maybe I missed it. Also, the physical setup seems somewhat limited: one camera pipeline, constrained viewpoints, and fairly controlled lighting. For a paper making a real-world robustness claim, that felt thin.

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The paper is novel, and I appreciate that it goes beyond purely digital evaluation. I’m leaning positive, but only weakly. The main reason is that the idea is better than the current experimental support. If accepted, I would strongly encourage the authors to tone down the claims and clarify the design constraints much more explicitly.

I found the core idea here interesting, but the paper left me with pretty mixed feelings. The “backtracking correction” framing is intuitively appealing: train from the final decision backward so the model learns to repair mistakes in a staged way. In spirit this makes sense for multi-step RAG, and I can believe it helps with noisy retrieval. Figure 2 (if I’m reading it correctly) does a decent job illustrating the trajectory of states/actions, although I had to go back to Eq. (3) and Eq. (5) a couple of times to understand what exactly is sampled versus supervised.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the method is as novel as the paper suggests. The combination of error sampling, self-correction, and curriculum-like progression (“easy-to-hard” as described around p.4) feels pretty close to a mix of DAgger-style aggregation, reverse-horizon training, and recent self-correction finetuning recipes for reasoning models. The paper gestures at these connections, but I think it undersells how much this is a recombination rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. That’s not fatal, but then the empirical case needs to be very solid.

And here I struggled. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains, yes, but they are not huge, and I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are clean. On p.6, the claim that competing stepwise RAG methods require “extensive and diverse annotations” feels overstated unless you compare annotation budgets directly. I didn’t see a real accounting of supervision cost. Also, the experimental setup seems narrowly tailored to the paper’s formulation of multi-step assessment. How does this behave when retrieval quality is truly bad, not just mildly noisy? Figure 4 suggests some robustness trend, but the noise construction looked synthetic to me. Maybe I missed it, but I wanted more realism there.

Another issue is clarity. The paper is more complicated than it needs to be. The state definition and the transition/backtracking process (around lines 180–230) are written in a very compressed way. After reading this twice, I still wasn’t sure whether correction targets are generated online or precomputed. That matters.

I do think there is something useful here, especially for training smaller RAG models to be less brittle. But right now I see a promising training heuristic with decent results, not yet a clearly established advance. I’m leaning borderline reject unless the authors can better disentangle novelty, annotation efficiency, and robustness under genuinely hard retrieval noise.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea — learning neuron presence / connectivity jointly with weights by gradient descent rather than treating architecture as an outer-loop search problem — is interesting, and I do think there is a real question here. In particular, the claim around replacing a stack of dense layers with a learned sparse-ish “Farfalle” block could matter in practice if it is actually stable and generally useful. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is mature enough for a top conference.

A lot hinges on the precise parameterization, and I found the presentation frustratingly unclear in places. Around the model definition (I think this is Sec. 3, around Eq. (3)–(6)), it seems the method introduces trainable gating / structural variables that determine both neuron counts and wiring. Fine. But then what exactly is being optimized continuously, and how is discreteness recovered at test time? The text suggests a gradient-based relaxation, but I could not tell whether the final architecture is truly discrete or just a pruned soft network. That distinction matters. If I’m reading Eq. (5) correctly, the effective layer is still dense before thresholding, which weakens the “structure learning” claim quite a bit.

The experimental section also feels thin. Table 1 reports fewer parameters and better accuracy, but the baselines are not especially strong. Comparing against plain dense stacks is not enough in 2026, honestly. Where are the obvious baselines: magnitude pruning, L0/L1 structured sparsification, DARTS-style continuous architecture parameters, even simple width search? I might be wrong, but several of the gains could just be due to regularization from learned sparsity rather than any novel architectural mechanism. Figure 2 is visually nice, but I wanted actual ablations: effect of the regularization coefficient, sensitivity to initialization, how much structure changes during training, variance across seeds. These are missing.

I also worry about optimization stability. The paper claims the architecture is “automatically learned during training,” but on p. 6 the training details are quite sparse, and I suspect the method may be delicate. In my experience, these jointly learned structural masks often look elegant but become brittle outside the narrow setup used in the paper.

There is something promising here, and I don’t want to be dismissive. But as written, it reads more like an interesting workshop paper than a solid conference submission. I would lean reject, mainly due to insufficient experimental validation and lack of clarity about what is actually being learned.

I found the core idea pretty reasonable, and honestly a bit overdue. In molecular latent-variable models, we all kind of assume that “similar molecules should live nearby,” but in practice this often fails badly. So the motivation landed for me. The use of matched molecular pairs as a regularizer is also a sensible choice; compared to generic augmentations, it is at least chemically meaningful. Figure 1 makes the problem intuitive, and I liked the qualitative latent-space visualizations later in the paper (I think around Fig. 4/5), even if they are a bit too polished for my taste.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is as strong as it wants to be. My biggest issue is that the method itself feels very incremental. The regularizer in Eq. (6) is simple—arguably almost obvious once the problem is stated—and the paper does not really develop much theory for when this alignment should help versus hurt. On page 5, the discussion around “more robust similarity information” from MMPs felt hand-wavy. MMPs encode one notion of local chemical change, yes, but they are not universally a better proxy for medicinally relevant similarity than fingerprints or graph-based metrics. I might be wrong, but the authors seem to oversell this.

I also found the experimental section uneven. The optimization improvements in Table 2 are encouraging, but the gains are modest and not always consistent across tasks. In my experience, when a regularizer is claimed to “clearly reflect similarity relationships in latent space,” I want a much more careful quantitative evaluation of neighborhood preservation, rank correlation, retrieval metrics, etc., not mainly t-SNE/UMAP-style plots. The paper has some of this, but not enough. Also, I was looking for stronger ablations: how sensitive is this to the number/quality of MMP pairs, to the similarity threshold, and to the weight of the regularization term? The ablation around lambda (page 7?) was too shallow.

Another concern is novelty relative to metric-learning / contrastive regularization ideas. The authors should be more candid here. This is basically a chemistry-aware latent consistency loss. Useful, yes. Major conceptual leap, not really.

Overall, I think the paper is competent and potentially useful to practitioners, but I’m not fully sold that it clears the bar for a top ML venue in its current form. The idea is clean, the writing is mostly understandable despite some grammar issues, but the empirical case needs to be tougher and the novelty claim toned down. My leaning is weak reject.

I found the paper interesting, but also somewhat frustrating to pin down. The core idea — condense a large table, decompose it, and then reason over a tree structure instead of the raw table — is intuitively sensible, and honestly this is the kind of thing many of us have expected would be necessary if LLMs are ever going to do serious table reasoning at scale. Figure 2 makes the pipeline easy to follow, and I appreciated that the authors evaluate on a fairly diverse set of benchmarks (WikiTQ, TableFact, FeTaQA, BIRD) rather than only on one QA dataset and calling it a day.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not entirely sure what is actually algorithmically new here versus careful prompt engineering plus hierarchical retrieval. The “Table Condensation and Decomposition” part on p.4–5 feels under-specified. Equation (3) looks like a scoring/ranking objective, but the paper never really explains how sensitive the system is to that choice, or whether this is learned, heuristic, or LLM-generated. Same issue for the Table-Tree construction. Is this a deterministic procedure? Is there any search? What happens when the decomposition is wrong early on? In my experience, these hierarchical pipelines often look great when the intermediate structure is clean, and then fall apart due to cascading errors. I was expecting a more serious error analysis, especially given the strong claims in the abstract.

The experimental results are good, maybe very good, but I have some reservations. Table 2 and Table 3 report gains over baselines, yet I could not tell whether the prompting budget / token budget is truly matched. This matters a lot here. If Tree-of-Table gets to summarize, prune, and then query multiple times, while the baselines are just run in a naive single-pass setting, the comparison is not fully fair. The efficiency claims on p.7 also felt a bit selective; wall-clock latency and API calls are not discussed clearly enough.

I did like the qualitative examples in Figure 4, although they are a bit cherry-picked. Also, BIRD is a strange inclusion unless the authors are very explicit about how SQL-oriented reasoning is being mapped into this framework. I might be wrong, but that part felt hand-wavy.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and the paper is more substantial than a lot of recent “agentic reasoning over tables” submissions. But the current version oversells novelty and underspecifies the method. I’m leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the construction/execution details and make the evaluation much more controlled.

I went into this paper somewhat sympathetic to the premise. SAM does have very real brittleness under distribution shift, and the prompting mismatch issue is also something people gloss over too often. So I wanted to like this. After reading the paper twice, I think the idea is interesting, but the paper itself is not yet convincing enough for me.

The core proposal—adding object-centric representations via slot-style feature reconstruction on top of the encoder—is plausible, and on p.3–4 the motivation is explained reasonably well. I especially liked the intuition that decomposing scenes into components can stabilize perception in unfamiliar domains. That said, once I got to the method details, I found the presentation murky. Equation (3) and the surrounding text (around p.5, left column) seem to define the reconstruction objective, but I had to infer several implementation choices that should have been explicit. Are the slots learned jointly with the SAM adaptation parameters throughout, or pre-trained and then frozen? I might be wrong, but Fig. 2 makes it look one way while the training description suggests another. This matters.

I also was not fully convinced by the “bootstrap” framing. It sounds stronger than what is actually done. From what I can tell, this is parameter-efficient fine-tuning with an auxiliary self-supervised object-centric loss. That is not a bad idea, but the paper oversells it a bit. The writing in the abstract and intro is heavy on “human intelligence” analogies, which honestly did not help me understand the technical contribution.

The empirical section is mixed. The gains in Table 1 / Table 2 on shifted domains are encouraging, especially for camouflage and medical images, but I kept looking for stronger ablations. For example, how much comes from the slot module itself versus simply adding another reconstruction regularizer? Where is the comparison to a non-slot latent bottleneck with matched parameter count? Also, if the paper’s claim is partly about prompt inconsistency between train/test, the dedicated evaluation of that issue felt too narrow. I expected a clearer protocol and maybe results broken down by prompt type in one place rather than scattered.

One more thing: the method seems to add complexity to a model people like because it is simple to prompt and deploy. Runtime/memory overhead is barely discussed (unless I missed it on p.7). In practice that matters.

Overall, I think there is a kernel of a good paper here, and I do believe the object-centric angle is worth exploring. But in its current form, the technical novelty feels incremental, and the evidence is not tight enough for a top conference acceptance.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because there is no shortage of “comprehensive” bias benchmarks for LLMs, and many of them end up being just a bundle of prior datasets with a taxonomy pasted on top. After reading the paper twice, I think this submission is a bit better than that, but I’m still not fully convinced it rises to the level of a top conference paper.

The main idea — organizing fairness evaluation compositionally across bias type, social group, and task — is sensible, and honestly overdue. I appreciated the framing on p.2–3 where the authors explain why isolated dataset-specific scores are hard to compare. Figure 1 does a decent job of motivating the 3D taxonomy. In my experience reviewing fairness papers, this kind of structuring is actually useful, because otherwise people cherry-pick one benchmark and declare victory. So the paper is directionally valuable.

That said, I found the benchmark construction under-specified in several places. The description around the taxonomy and dataset mapping (I think this is around Sec. 3, p.4–5) reads cleaner than it actually is. How exactly were ambiguous cases handled, where a dataset spans multiple bias types or multiple tasks? The paper implies a neat compositional grid, but real bias datasets are messy. I kept looking for a more explicit annotation protocol or inter-annotator agreement discussion and didn’t really see enough. Maybe I missed it, but if this benchmark is supposed to standardize evaluation, the standardization procedure itself needs to be much more rigorous.

I also wasn’t persuaded by the experimental section. Table 2 / Table 3 (if I’m tracking correctly) mostly confirms the unsurprising result that bias varies by model, by group, and by task. Sure, but what did we learn beyond that? The authors claim this provides “guidance” for mitigation methods, but the evidence is fairly indirect. I would have liked at least one concrete case study: e.g., a mitigation method that helps on toxicity-style generation but hurts stereotype classification, or vice versa. Without that, the benchmark feels descriptive rather than scientifically incisive.

Another issue is evaluation comparability. The paper criticizes inconsistent metrics in prior work, but I’m not sure Eq. (1) and the aggregation choices really solve that problem. They may just repackage heterogenous signals into one framework. I might be wrong here, but some of the normalization choices looked arbitrary.

Overall, I think this is a useful resource paper with a reasonable taxonomy, but I’m less convinced by the methodological novelty and by the strength of the empirical conclusions. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the camera-ready version substantially clarifies benchmark construction and tightens the claims.

I found this paper interesting in intent, but pretty uneven in execution. The problem setting—OOD detection for multimodal, multi-round dialogue—is absolutely relevant, and honestly underexplored. Most prior work I know either does image-text retrieval style mismatch detection or single-turn OOD in dialogue, so the focus on long dialogue is a good instinct. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the contribution is as clear or as novel as the abstract suggests.

The main issue for me is that DIAEF feels more like a scoring recipe on top of an existing VLM than a genuinely new framework. Equation (3) on p.4 and the surrounding discussion make this look like a weighted combination of alignment/confidence-style scores, but the paper sometimes markets it as something broader. I think the authors should be much more explicit about what is algorithmically new here. Right now it reads a bit inflated. Also, the name “No Matters” is catchy, sure, but slightly gimmicky.

I did appreciate the separation between the two OOD settings: mismatched image-dialogue pairs and unseen-label inputs. That distinction is sensible, and Table 2 is probably the most useful part of the paper because it shows the multimodal signal helping especially in the unseen-label case. But I was confused by the benchmark construction. On p.5–6, the protocol for generating mismatched pairs seems too synthetic. If the negatives are created by random pairing, then of course alignment-based scores will look good. In real systems, the hard cases are semantically nearby but wrong images, or dialogues that drift only late in the conversation. Figure 3 hints at robustness over dialogue length, which is nice, but I wanted much more analysis of failure modes. Where does it break?

Another concern is the baselines. I might be wrong, but the comparison set felt a little weak/outdated. If you are building on modern VLMs, then stronger calibration and energy-based OOD baselines should be there, and ideally something retrieval-based too. Table 1 is okay, but I don’t think it settles the question.

Writing-wise, the paper is readable but occasionally sloppy. Some claims around “establishes baselines for future studies” are too grand. Also, I found the discussion around line 214–230 vague; it gestures at “enhancing” the dialogue signal without really unpacking what information is added versus just reweighted.

Overall, I like the task and think there is a kernel of a solid workshop paper here, maybe even more with stronger experiments. But for a top ML venue, I’m not there yet. The empirical setup needs to feel less convenient, and the method needs a sharper identity.

I think this paper has an interesting core idea, and I say that as someone who has read far too many neural SDF papers over the last few years. The main claim — learning a shape encoder-decoder that only needs the zero level set and enforces the Eikonal equation, avoiding the auto-decoder latent optimization loop — is genuinely appealing. In spirit it sits somewhere between DeepSDF-style representations and the more recent encoder-based implicit models, but the boundary-valued Eikonal framing is a nice angle. Figure 1 sells the motivation well, and I appreciated the attempt to handle non-watertight / non-manifold inputs.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the empirical story. A lot hinges on whether the proposed supervision is actually sufficient to recover a good signed field rather than just “some” function with roughly correct zero crossing and unit gradient norm. The paper says this on p. 3–4 around the discussion of the Eikonal objective, but I wanted a more careful treatment of ambiguity. Equation (6) and the surrounding text seem to imply that the boundary condition plus Eikonal regularization should be enough in practice, but that is not really the same as identifiability. I might be wrong, but the method could still produce incorrect sign structure in hard cases, especially with thin structures or multiple nearby sheets. The examples in Fig. 4 and Fig. 6 look decent, but they are not stress tests.

I also found the architecture description a bit messy. The hybrid graph/voxel encoder is plausible, but the contribution there feels incremental. By the time I got to the decoder and loss details (around p. 5, Eq. 8 onward), I was unsure what was essential versus engineering. Table 2 reports improvements, but it’s hard to tell whether they come from the zero-level-set training itself, the multi-scale encoder, or simply more favorable sampling. The ablations are not strong enough. In particular, I wanted a direct comparison to a strong encoder-based SDF baseline trained with full SDF supervision and the same backbone.

The “normals not well defined” variant is useful, and honestly maybe more practically important than the paper admits. But again, the evidence is thin. Some qualitative examples, yes, but not enough quantitative validation on truly messy geometry.

Overall: promising idea, somewhat under-argued paper. I like the direction, but for a top venue I would want cleaner experimental isolation and a more rigorous discussion of when the boundary-only formulation succeeds or fails. Right now it feels like a solid workshop paper or borderline conference paper rather than a clear accept.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantive than a lot of recent “robust + privacy” submissions that mostly just stack two buzzwords together. The core observation — that in the local model the attacker can poison the reported randomized response directly, which is strictly worse than only manipulating the underlying data — is important and, to my knowledge, not stated this cleanly before. The discussion around this early on (around p. 2–3) is one of the strongest parts of the paper. I also liked the use of graph redundancy (each edge being seen by two users) to recover robustness; that feels like the right structural insight rather than a generic robust statistics patch.

That said, I had to read the technical sections twice, and I’m still not entirely sure the presentation is doing the results any favors. In particular, the model of the adversary and what exactly counts as “poisoning their response” versus “poisoning their local data” gets a bit slippery around the formal definitions on p. 4. Maybe I’m missing something, but the distinction between the two threat models should be made much more explicit, perhaps with a toy example before the theorem statements. As written, I think many readers will get lost before reaching the actual contribution.

The main upper/lower bound story seems strong. I appreciated that the authors do not stop at proposing a protocol, but also characterize optimality information-theoretically. The theorem around the robust degree estimator (I think this is Theorem 4.3? apologies if I have the number wrong) is probably the technical centerpiece. Still, some proof intuition is buried. Eq. (3) and the argument immediately after it are quite dense, and I found myself wanting one paragraph of plain English. Figure 1 helped, Figure 2 less so.

On the empirical side, Table 1 and the poisoning curves are useful, but the experiments are a bit narrow. Degree estimation only, a small set of datasets, and attack models that seem somewhat stylized. Maybe that is fine given the theory focus, but then I would tone down the practical claims in the abstract. “Demonstrate their efficacy in practice” feels a little oversold.

Overall, I lean positive. I think the paper has a real idea, and in my experience that matters more than polish. But the writing is uneven, and the threat model/assumption clarity needs work. If accepted, I would strongly encourage the authors to revise for readability because right now the technical contribution is better than the exposition.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a lot of “automatic agent design” submissions lately, and most of them are either prompt search with extra branding or else require a surprising amount of hidden human scaffolding. This one is better than that, at least partly. The core idea—casting workflow generation as code-space search and using MCTS to explore edits—is sensible, and on first pass it sounds more principled than the usual evolutionary “mutate prompts until benchmark goes up” recipe. I also appreciated that the paper is fairly concrete about the representation of workflows as executable code graphs; Fig. 2 and the example around Eq. (3) made the setup much clearer than many recent agent papers.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not entirely convinced by the central claim of “fully automated” workflow generation. The paper downplays how much is baked into the search operators and the workflow abstraction itself. On p. 4–5, the node types and legal modifications seem quite curated, and I suspect a lot of the eventual success comes from this design choice rather than from MCTS per se. I might be wrong, but the method feels less like discovering workflows from scratch and more like navigating a carefully fenced garden. That’s not necessarily bad! But the framing oversells it.

The empirical section is decent but also a bit frustrating. Table 1 shows average gains over baselines, but I wanted more variance information and more clarity on per-task sensitivity. Some tasks seem to benefit a lot, others barely move. It’s hard to tell whether AFLOW is broadly reliable or just wins on a subset where the search space aligns nicely with the benchmark. Also, the cost claims in the abstract and later near Table 3 need more careful normalization. “4.55% of GPT-4o cost” sounds impressive, but are we accounting for the search-time cost amortized over deployment, or just final inference? Those are very different stories.

I also found the comparison set a little selective. There are relevant papers on automatic prompt/program optimization and multi-agent synthesis that should have been discussed more seriously; the related work section on p. 2 feels rushed. In my experience, if you want to claim a meaningful step beyond prior workflow optimization methods, you need stronger ablations. For example: replace MCTS with simpler search, vary the edit operators, ablate execution feedback, and report failure modes. Fig. 4 hints at search dynamics, but not enough.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a real idea in it, not hype-only, which already puts it above many submissions in this space. But I’m hesitant because the paper sometimes claims more than it demonstrates. Borderline accept for me, leaning weak accept if the experimental claims can be tightened.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practical than a lot of pruning/recovery papers I’ve seen lately. The core idea — don’t blindly re-tune on all instruction data after pruning, but instead select data based on where the model actually got worse — makes intuitive sense, and in my experience these “obvious in hindsight” ideas are often the ones that matter. I especially liked the framing around uneven capability degradation. Figure 1 makes that point clearly, and the cluster-based allocation on p.4–5 is probably the most novel part of the paper.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the method is as principled as the paper sometimes claims. The manifold learning + spectral clustering pipeline (Sec. 3.2, around Eq. 3 and the discussion on p.5) feels a bit elaborate relative to the payoff, and I kept wondering whether simpler baselines would do nearly as well. For example, where is the straightforward comparison to embedding-based k-means, or even random stratified sampling by task/source? Table 2 shows gains over several baselines, yes, but the baseline set still feels curated in PASER’s favor. After reading this twice, I’m still unsure whether the gains come from the adaptive budget allocation, the within-cluster sample prioritization, or the filtering of “conflicting” data. The ablation in Table 4 helps, but not enough; the interactions are muddy.

I also found the “negative transfer” story underdeveloped. The filtering criterion on p.6 (lines around the scoring function; I think Eq. 7?) is presented as if it detects irrelevant or conflicting data, but I’m not sure that interpretation really follows. It may simply be identifying hard examples or distributional outliers. Those are not the same thing. This matters, because the paper makes fairly strong causal language claims.

On the experimental side, the results are good. Recovering performance with only 4–20% of the data is meaningful, and the compute savings are not trivial. Table 3 was the most persuasive to me. But I wanted more evidence of robustness: different pruning methods, different sparsity regimes, and maybe one stronger base model. Right now the scope feels a little narrow for the breadth of the claims (“general capabilities” is too broad, frankly).

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical value, but I’m hesitating on the novelty and the mechanistic justification. I lean positive, though not enthusiastically. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to tone down some claims and add clearer ablations/explanations.

I read this with some interest because multi-subject face NeRFs are something people have been circling around for a while, and in principle the paper is aiming at a real bottleneck: per-identity training is a pain. The idea of a single model across identities, with later personalization, is sensible. I also appreciate that the paper is not trying to oversell itself as “foundation model for faces” or whatever. That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the technical novelty is strong enough, and some of the empirical claims feel softer than the writing suggests.

The main mechanism seems to be the multiplicative interaction between identity and transient/non-identity factors (Sec. 3, especially around Eq. (4)-(6)). I think this is the core contribution, but honestly the presentation is a bit muddy. I found Eq. (5) under-motivated: why this specific multiplicative form rather than a more standard FiLM-style modulation or hypernetwork parameterization? Maybe these are equivalent in some regime, maybe not, but the paper never really clarifies this. Figure 2 gives the high-level architecture, but I had to jump back and forth between that and p. 4 to understand what is actually shared and what is identity-conditioned. In my experience, if the model decomposition is the point, the paper should be much crisper here.

The experimental section is mixed. Table 1 and Table 2 show training-time savings and some reconstruction/generation metrics, which is expected, but I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are fair. If MI-NeRF is trained jointly over many identities, then comparing its total cost to a collection of separately trained NeRFs is obvious but slightly stacked in its favor; what I really wanted was a stronger analysis of per-identity quality vs. compute tradeoff at fixed budget. Also, the personalized finetuning story (p. 6, lines around the target adaptation discussion) is interesting, but then the method is not really eliminating per-subject optimization, just reducing it. That’s fine, but the abstract leans a bit too hard on the simplification narrative.

Qualitatively, some results in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 look good, especially coarse expression transfer, but the mouth interiors and teeth are still pretty unstable. Maybe unavoidable, but then I would like more candid discussion. I might be wrong, but some of the gains may simply come from pooling more data rather than from the multiplicative design specifically. The ablation table (I assume Table 3) helps, but it is not deep enough to isolate that.

Overall, I think this is a decent paper with practical relevance, but for a top ML conference I wanted either a cleaner conceptual contribution or more rigorous evidence that the proposed factorization is the reason it works. Right now it feels somewhat incremental. My leaning is borderline reject.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than a lot of the recent protein design diffusion submissions. The core idea — learning a distribution over full 3D backbones and then doing conditional generation for motif scaffolding — is timely, and I do think the use of an E(3)-equivariant network is the right bias here. The SMC-based conditioning is also, at least on paper, a real contribution rather than just another heuristic “guidance” trick. I appreciated the claim around theoretical correctness in the large-compute limit, though I’m not entirely sure how much that matters in the actual regime they run.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper overclaims in a few places. The abstract and intro make it sound like this is close to a general solution, but the evaluation is pretty narrow. A lot hangs on AlphaFold2 consistency, and in this area that is useful but not enough. If I understood Table 1 and the discussion around Fig. 4 correctly, the main evidence is backbone recovery / motif preservation under AF2-predicted structures, plus some diversity metrics. Fine, but this is still proxy-on-proxy. In my experience reviewing protein design papers, this can look much better than it really is. I really wanted either stronger baselines or at least some physics-based filtering / energy analysis.

I also found parts of the method section hard to parse. The diffusion model itself is relatively standard, but the conditioning algorithm (SMCDiff) around Eq. (6)–(9) felt under-explained. Maybe I missed it, but the practical gap between the “theoretical guarantee” and the finite-particle implementation is not clearly characterized. How sensitive are results to particle count, resampling schedule, and proposal quality? This matters a lot. On page 5 (around the algorithm box), the presentation becomes fairly dense and then the experiments don’t really unpack which piece is doing the work.

The length claim is nice — up to 80 residues is definitely beyond some earlier methods — but 80 is still not especially large in protein design. So I’m not fully convinced by the framing that prior methods are limited to “unrealistically small” scaffolds while this solves the issue. It’s progress, yes. A breakthrough, maybe not.

Overall I lean positive because there is real technical novelty here, and the paper is more serious than many generative design papers I’ve seen. But I’d want clearer ablations, better explanation of SMCDiff in practice, and less reliance on AF2 as the main validator. If accepted, this would be for methodological novelty more than for decisive biological validation.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core question—whether DenseNet/ResNet-style connectivity has an implicit regularization or generalization benefit beyond just optimization—is actually interesting, and in my experience it is under-discussed relative to the flood of architecture papers. So I was initially sympathetic. But after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced the evidence really supports the claims as strongly as the abstract suggests.

The main issue is that the paper keeps sliding between “generalization,” “robustness to reduced data,” and “benefit of low-level feature reuse,” which are related but not the same thing. On p. 2–3 (around the discussion of skip connections and implicit regularization), the framing is broad, but the experiments seem much narrower: mostly showing that architectures with access to earlier representations degrade less when training data quantity/quality is reduced. That is a useful empirical observation, yes, but I think the paper overclaims. It does not really isolate “implicit regularization” in a clean way.

I also found the experimental design a bit under-specified. From Table 1 / Table 2 (assuming I’m reading them correctly), there are accuracy gaps in the expected direction, but I could not tell whether parameter count and optimization budget are controlled tightly enough. DenseNet-like models often differ in effective capacity and gradient flow, so better performance under limited data is not automatically evidence of regularization. It could just be an easier-to-optimize inductive bias. That distinction matters here. Figure 3 was suggestive, though, and I liked the attempt to compare several skip-connection variants rather than just DenseNet vs baseline.

Another thing: the paper needs stronger statistical hygiene. I did not see confidence intervals or variance across seeds in the key plots. Maybe I missed it. If the effects are small, this is a problem; if they are large, showing variance would only strengthen the paper. Right now it feels a bit anecdotal. Also, the “quality and quantity of training data is decreased” setup (abstract, and later in the experimental section) needs more detail. How exactly is quality degraded? Label noise? blur? subsampling? These choices can interact a lot with architecture.

I don’t want to be overly negative. I think the paper is asking a worthwhile question, and some of the observations are plausible and probably true. But for a top ML venue, I wanted either a cleaner causal story or much more rigorous empirical control. As written, it feels like an interesting workshop paper more than a fully convincing conference paper. My leaning is reject, though not by a huge margin.

I went into this paper somewhat sympathetic to the premise, because geometry distortion is a real annoyance in unpaired I2I and a lot of papers kind of hand-wave it away with nicer examples. The core idea here — adding a “minimal geometry-distortion constraint” via a mutual-information-style objective — is interesting, and Figure 1 makes the motivation intuitive. I also appreciated that the authors are not just proposing yet another architecture tweak. In that sense the paper feels more principled than many GAN-ish translation submissions I’ve seen over the years.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by either the formulation or the empirical evidence. The paper keeps saying geometry is preserved by “reducing the randomness of color transformation,” but that connection felt underspecified to me. On p.3 around Eq. (4)–(6), the jump from geometry consistency to maximizing this relative SMI surrogate is pretty fast. I might be wrong, but it seems more like a heuristic than a derivation. The term “minimal geometry-distortion” sounds stronger than what is actually guaranteed. I was looking for some clearer argument that the statistic being optimized is actually sensitive to geometric deformation rather than just low-level correspondence.

I also found the presentation rough in places. The definition of rSMI near Eq. (8) is dense, and the analytic estimation claim is a bit slippery; the computational cost and stability are not discussed enough. On p.5, the training description is too abbreviated for reproducibility. What are the hyperparameter sensitivities for the MGC weight? In my experience these extra constraints often help only in a narrow regime.

The experiments are decent but not quite enough for a top venue. The visual results in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 do suggest fewer distortions, especially on structured objects, but the evaluation metrics in Table 1 and Table 2 are not very geometry-specific. If geometry preservation is the main claim, I really wanted landmark consistency, keypoint transfer, segmentation overlap, something of that sort. FID/LPIPS-style improvements are nice but do not settle the issue. Also, comparisons to more recent baselines felt a bit selective.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and parts of it are promising. But the paper overclaims relative to what it demonstrates, and the technical justification is not yet sharp enough. My leaning is borderline reject, though I’d be open to being persuaded if the authors can clarify the theory and add more targeted evidence.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea—using an LLM as a prompt optimizer for a downstream VLM, and then nudging the LLM’s hidden states with a “positive-minus-negative” offset—is genuinely novel at least in the way it is packaged here. I’ve seen a lot of prompt search / prompt evolution / LLM-as-optimizer papers over the last couple of years, and this one does have a distinct twist. The hidden-state steering mechanism is the part that made me keep reading.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as principled as it wants to sound. The description around the “purity measure” and fitness function (I think around Sec. 3, maybe p. 4?) is too vague. Is purity just validation accuracy, or some transformed score over candidate prompts? Eq. (3) and the surrounding text should have been much clearer. Same issue with the offset intervention: where exactly is this applied, to which layer(s), with what scaling coefficient, and how sensitive is performance to that choice? This is the sort of thing that can make or break the method, and the paper kind of glides over it.

The empirical section is reasonably broad. Sixteen datasets is not nothing, and I appreciate that the authors tested both CLIP-style dual encoders and LLaVA-like encoder-decoder VLMs. Table 1 / Table 2 (if I’m tracking correctly) are impressive on first pass, especially the larger gains for the generative models.
