But I’m not entirely sure these comparisons are fully fair. In particular, I wanted stronger baselines against simpler search methods: random prompt search with equal budget, evolutionary search without hidden-state steering, and maybe a pure in-context optimization loop with no offset vector at all. Some of this is in the ablations, but not enough. Figure 3 suggests the method improves over iterations, which is nice, though I suspect a lot of the gain may just come from repeated guided search rather than the proposed steering itself.

I also worry about cost. The method seems computationally heavy, yet I couldn’t find a very transparent accounting of the number of LLM queries per dataset/task (maybe I missed it). For a paper pitching a fairly practical optimization framework, that matters.

Overall, I think there is a good idea here, and the results are better than I expected. But the presentation is muddy, and the causal evidence for the main technical ingredient is weaker than it should be. Borderline accept for me if the bar is novelty-focused; borderline reject if the bar is rigor.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core message — that prior claims about ViT backdoor attacks being unusually robust are partly an artifact of badly adapted defenses, and that a small perturbation on the trigger makes attacks more persistent again — is actually interesting. In fact I think this is probably the most useful part of the paper: not the new attack per se, but the re-evaluation of the threat/defense landscape on ViTs. There has been a lot of sloppy transfer of CNN-era assumptions into transformer papers, so I’m sympathetic to the motivation.

That said, I found the presentation frustrating in places. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still wasn’t fully sure what is fundamentally new algorithmically beyond “perturb the trigger a bit” and tune the defense adaptation more carefully. Eq. (6) on p.5 looks almost too simple for the amount of emphasis the method gets later, and I kept wondering whether the gains in Table 2 and Table 3 are mostly from extra optimization freedom rather than a qualitatively new attack mechanism. Maybe that is fine, but then the paper should be more honest about it.

The empirical study is fairly extensive, and I appreciated the side-by-side treatment of attacks and defenses. Figure 3 was useful. Table 1 is, in my view, the most important table in the paper because it directly supports the “over-optimistic prior results” claim. If that table is solid, then the paper has value. But I wish the authors were much more careful about statistical variation. I didn’t see enough discussion of run-to-run variance, especially since ViT training can be annoyingly unstable in backdoor settings. Maybe I missed it. If the numbers are single runs, that is not acceptable.

I was also confused by the defense adaptation details around p.6–7. Some choices seem sensible, but others felt a bit hand-tuned. In my experience, defense papers live or die on these implementation details. If you argue previous work was unfair, you need to be almost painfully transparent.

Overall, I think the paper has a worthwhile corrective message and a decent empirical contribution, but the novelty of the proposed attack feels limited, and the experimental methodology needs to be tightened. Borderline accept / weak reject from me; I’m leaning slightly negative, though I could be convinced if the camera-ready clarified the protocol and variance issues.

I went into this paper somewhat sympathetic because I’ve seen a few action-repeat papers recently, and I do think the area is underexplored in continuous control. The core idea here — letting each action dimension decide independently whether to repeat or refresh — is intuitively appealing. In robotics this makes sense almost immediately: some joints should persist while others need quick correction. So at a high level, I buy the motivation.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m not fully convinced the paper nails either the novelty or the empirical case. The method itself, as introduced around Sec. 3 (especially the selection mechanism near Eq. (4)–(6)), feels like a fairly direct factorization of the repeat decision across dimensions. I might be missing something, but this seems more like an architectural tweak to prior action repetition frameworks than a genuinely new framework. The authors repeatedly emphasize “closed-loop act-or-repeat selection,” but I found the exact control logic a bit murky. On p. 4, the description of how the selector interacts with the policy output and previous action is not as crisp as it should be. Figure 2 helps, but even there I had to infer some details that really ought to be explicit in the main text.

I also have concerns about the comparisons. The paper claims improved sample efficiency and reduced fluctuation, but Table 1 and the learning curves in Fig. 3/4 don’t always make this as clear as the text suggests. In a few tasks the gains look modest, and I’m not sure whether the baselines were tuned equally carefully. This is one of those papers where “works on many benchmarks” is doing a lot of rhetorical work. I would have liked stronger ablations: what happens if repetition is grouped by body part rather than fully decoupled? how sensitive is performance to action dimensionality? does this help mostly on high-DoF tasks? Those are pretty central questions here.

Another issue: the paper sells reduced action fluctuation as a virtue, but smoother actions are not automatically better. Sometimes they are just less responsive. I didn’t see enough analysis of the agility/persistence tradeoff beyond aggregate return. Some per-dimension repeat statistics or temporal spectra would have been informative.

Overall, I think the idea is reasonable and probably useful, but the paper feels a bit overstated. If this were a workshop paper I’d be positive. For a top conference, I’m on the fence and leaning slightly negative, mostly because the technical contribution seems incremental and the empirical evidence, while decent, doesn’t quite close the case.

I’m somewhat mixed on this paper. The core idea — using diffusion for closed-loop planning with “flexible guidance” rather than a hard-coded rule fallback — is interesting, and I do buy the motivation in the abstract. In autonomous driving, the brittleness of IL planners plus a giant safety wrapper is a real issue. So I was inclined to like this. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the technical contribution is as clear or as substantial as the authors seem to claim.

The strongest part, in my view, is the joint prediction/planning setup around the transformer diffusion model (Figure 2, and the architecture description on p.4–5). It makes intuitive sense that modeling other agents and ego trajectories in one generative process could help with cooperative behavior. I also liked the qualitative examples in Figure 5; some of the merges/unprotected turns do look more natural than the baselines. That said, “human-like” gets thrown around a bit loosely here. The paper shows benchmark gains, yes, but not much actual analysis of behavioral realism beyond success/collision style metrics.

My bigger issue is with the guidance mechanism. Equation (7) and the discussion around it (p.6, around lines 220–250) are under-explained. I think the method is doing a form of classifier guidance / score modification with hand-designed costs, but it’s not entirely clear how this differs materially from prior guided diffusion work except in the planning context. Maybe I’m missing something, but the novelty felt a bit incremental there. Also, the claim that the method avoids rule-based refinement is slightly slippery: if the guidance includes explicit safety/objective gradients, that is still injecting structure, just in a softer way. That’s fine! But the framing overstates the departure from prior systems.

The experiments are decent, especially nuPlan closed-loop results in Table 1 and the transfer story on the 200-hour delivery dataset (Table 3). Still, I really wanted more ablations. For example: what happens without joint prediction? without guidance? with standard trajectory optimization as post-processing? Also runtime is too lightly treated for a planning paper. On p.7 they mention inference efficiency, but I couldn’t find a serious latency breakdown. For deployment, this matters a lot.

Overall, I think this is a promising paper, but not yet a slam dunk for a top venue. Good empirical results, some real practical value, but the method section is fuzzier than it should be and the novelty over existing guided diffusion formulations is, frankly, arguable. I’d lean weak reject / borderline, though I could be persuaded if the rebuttal clarifies the guidance formulation and gives stronger ablations.

I found the core idea interesting and, honestly, a bit refreshing. There is something appealing about asking whether all the projection/adaptation machinery in prior zero-shot crossmodal work is actually necessary, instead of just taking it for granted. The paper’s main claim — that a frozen PLM can be “activated” for crossmodal tasks purely by injecting detected concepts, both at the input level and into intermediate FFN features — is provocative in a good way.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the framing and some of the empirical interpretation. The method is simple, maybe deceptively simple, but the paper sometimes oversells what is really happening. On p.2–3, the wording suggests the model can “perceive” visual input without learning; in reality, it is heavily dependent on an external visual concept extractor. So the burden is shifted, not removed. I don’t object to that setup per se, but I think the paper should be much clearer that the quality and ontology of those extracted concepts are doing a lot of the work here. In my experience, these systems often look stronger than they are because the detector vocabulary overlaps suspiciously well with benchmark answers.

The technical description is mostly understandable, though Eq. (4) and the discussion around the FFN augmentation mechanism were harder to parse than they needed to be. I think Fig. 2 helps, but I still wasn’t entirely sure whether the injected intermediate representation is layer-specific or reused identically across layers; p.4 implies one thing, the implementation details later suggest another. Maybe I missed it. Also, the term “adaptation layers” is a bit misleading here since these are not learned in the usual sense.

The experiments are decent and the results in Tables 1 and 2 are better than I expected. Competitive with trained baselines is noteworthy. But the comparisons are not fully satisfying. If the method depends on textualized concepts, then stronger prompting baselines and concept-only ablations matter a lot. The ablation in Table 3 goes in this direction, but I wanted more: what happens with noisy concepts, fewer concepts, shuffled concepts, or generic object tags only? Without that, it’s hard to know whether “deep injection” is really the key ingredient or just giving the LM a reasonably good textual summary of the scene.

Overall, I like the paper more than I trust it. The idea is clever and potentially impactful, but the evidence is a bit narrower than the claims. I’d lean weak accept if the conference values simple, thought-provoking negative/contrarian results; otherwise borderline.

I found the paper interesting, mostly because I’ve seen several recent attempts to use radiance-field-style representations for 3D detection and many of them feel a bit overcomplicated for the actual gain. This one is refreshingly direct. The core idea—use 3DGS instead of NeRF, then bias the reconstruction toward object boundaries and object regions—is not conceptually deep, but it makes intuitive sense, and in Figure 1 the qualitative difference is indeed noticeable. I also appreciate that the authors are not overselling some giant architectural novelty.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what exactly is being learned where. The “2D Boundary Guidance” sounds almost too simple: detect edges in 2D, overlay them on the training images, then train 3DGS. If that is really all, then I want a much more careful discussion of why this does not simply inject artificial high-frequency cues that happen to help the downstream detector, rather than actually improving 3D geometry. The text around the method description (I think around Sec. 3, and the equation block defining the modified rendering/training objective) did not fully convince me. I might be wrong, but this seems closer to a data preprocessing trick than a principled geometric constraint.

Similarly, Box-Focused Sampling is reasonable, but also a little dangerous. If 2D boxes are used to define “object probability spaces,” how sensitive is the method to box noise and truncation? In my experience with multi-view detection pipelines, these heuristic sampling schemes can look great on one benchmark and then fall apart when the boxes are loose or the views are sparse. I did not see enough robustness analysis in the tables. Table 1 shows gains over the baseline, yes, but I really wanted ablations on boundary detector choice, box quality, and sampling temperature/ratio. Without that, the method feels somewhat tuned.

The empirical gains in the abstract (5.6 mAP@0.25 and 3.7 mAP@0.5 over baseline) are solid, though I’d like to know how weak that baseline is. “First to introduce 3DGS into 3DOD” is plausible, but novelty by chronology only goes so far. The paper is most convincing as an engineering paper, less so as a conceptual one.

Overall, I lean mildly positive because the idea is practical and the results seem real, but I’m not entirely sure the paper has pinned down why it works, and some parts read as a bit hand-wavy. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to be clearer about the mechanism and robustness.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea is intuitive and honestly not crazy at all: use early stopping to avoid the memorization phase, then continue training on a “safe” subset. I buy the high-level motivation. In fact, this is roughly consistent with a lot of empirical lore around noisy labels. But the paper overclaims, and that hurt my confidence.

The biggest issue is the claim in the abstract (and repeated around p. 2) that Prestopping “realizes noise-free training under any type of label noise for practical use.” That is a very strong statement, much stronger than what the experiments actually support. Four image benchmarks and a handful of baselines do not justify “any type.” I found this phrasing pretty misleading. If the method works well under the specific synthetic and real-noise settings tested, just say that.

Technically, I found the “maximal safe set” construction a bit underexplained. I had to reread the description around Eq. (3)–(5) and the algorithm box to understand what is actually updated each epoch and what guarantees, if any, the set remains “safe.” Maybe I’m missing something, but this feels heuristic rather than principled. That’s not fatal, but then the paper should be more candid. Right now it sort of gestures at theory without really delivering it. Figure 2 is suggestive, yes, but not enough.

I did like some of the empirical trends. The plots showing the test accuracy peaking before full memorization (I assume Fig. 1 or 3; the notation was a bit hard to track) are consistent with prior observations, and the comparisons in the main result table are reasonably strong. Still, I wanted more ablations. How sensitive is performance to the early stopping point? This seems central. Table 2 gives some results, but not enough for me to understand whether this is robust or requires careful tuning. Also, if the stopping point is selected using clean validation labels, then the practical story gets weaker very quickly.

Another concern: the baselines felt a bit dated even for the framing of the problem. In my experience, noisy-label papers live or die by careful comparisons, and I’m not convinced this paper clears that bar. I’m also not entirely sure the gains of 0.4 points in some settings are meaningful absent variance/error bars.

So overall: interesting idea, decent empirical intuition, but too much hype and not enough rigor. I’m leaning weak reject. If the claims were toned down and the mechanism/ablations clarified, I’d feel more positive.

I found this paper interesting but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — doing federated representation learning with MCR2 instead of the usual cross-entropy objective — is genuinely appealing, and honestly a nice change from the endless stream of “FedAvg + some regularizer” papers. I’ve followed the MCR2 line of work for a while, and in principle it makes sense here: if you want a shared low-dimensional representation under data decentralization, coding-rate reduction is a more natural target than local classifier fitting. So the paper has some novelty.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the actual federated algorithm is as clean or as practical as the paper suggests. The optimization description around Eq. (6)–(9) is fairly dense, and I think the notation hides important implementation assumptions. In particular, on p.4 the transition from the centralized MCR2 objective to the client-level updates feels too quick. I might be missing something, but it seems the server still needs fairly structured summary statistics from clients, and I wanted a much clearer discussion of communication/computation cost. Right now FLOW is presented as decentralized-ish, but the extent to which it really scales in realistic FL settings is murky.

The theory also felt thinner than the paper wants me to believe. Yes, there is a first-order stationary point guarantee, but this is a pretty standard type of result in nonconvex distributed optimization. The assumptions in the theorem (I think around p.5, near Theorem 1) looked strong, and I did not see much insight connecting them specifically to MCR2 geometry. So the theory is not bad, just not especially illuminating.

On experiments: mixed feelings. The results in Table 1 and Table 2 are promising, especially where representation quality is evaluated rather than only downstream accuracy. I appreciated that. Figure 2 is also helpful. But the empirical section is too small for a paper making a broad methodological claim. I wanted stronger federated baselines, more heterogeneity settings, and some ablation on representation dimension / client participation / label skew. In my experience, these methods often break exactly there. Also, if the contribution is “learned low-dimensional representations,” then the paper should show more visualization or transfer results, not just accuracy summaries.

Overall, I think the paper has a good idea and some technical merit, but it currently reads more like an interesting workshop paper than a top-conference acceptance. I’m leaning borderline reject unless the authors can better justify practicality and significantly strengthen the experiments.

I found the paper interesting, and I do think conditional graph generation for social networks is a worthwhile problem. In particular, the focus on dual conditions is more realistic than the usual single-label setup. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by either the technical novelty or the empirical evidence.

The main idea—conditioning a diffusion model on two correlated node/user attributes and modeling their “co-evolution dependency” during denoising—sounds reasonable, but I had trouble pinning down what is actually new versus what is essentially conditional guidance plus an auxiliary classifier. Eq. (6) and Eq. (7) on p. 5 seem to be the key, but the exposition is pretty muddy. I might be wrong, but the “mutual dependency” term looks more like a design choice than something derived from a principled probabilistic formulation. The classifier loss introduced around lines 210–235 also feels under-motivated. Why this exact form? Why should it improve fidelity to both conditions rather than just sharpen one at the expense of the other? The paper gestures at this, but doesn’t really prove it.

I also struggled with the social-homophily/social-contagion story. On p. 4 the paper presents these as if they are incorporated in a meaningful social-scientific way, but in implementation they seem closer to regularization biases on connectivity patterns. That’s fine, honestly, but then say that more plainly. Right now it reads a bit like sociology-washing.

The experiments are decent but not fully satisfying. Table 2 and Table 3 show better dual-conditional validity, yes, but I would have liked stronger ablations. The ablation in Fig. 4 is too narrow: where is the version with only one of the two conditions modeled jointly? where is a simpler conditional diffusion baseline without the co-evolution mechanism? Comparing against SPECTRE/EDGE/DiGress is useful, but some of these are not really designed for the same conditional setting, so the comparison is not entirely fair. In my experience reviewing graph generation papers, this is a common issue, but it still matters.

Another concern is scalability. The datasets in Table 1 are not large, and I didn’t see convincing runtime or memory analysis. For social graph synthesis, that’s a big omission.

Overall, I think the paper has a promising direction and some empirical gains, but the current version feels oversold and under-explained. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the method and strengthen the ablation/positioning.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The problem is important, absolutely. Single-domain generalization for rare events in medical imaging is underexplored, and the seizure onset zone use case is clinically meaningful. I also appreciate that the authors are not just throwing another ViT at the problem and calling it a day. The idea in the abstract — using a pretrained large vision model to rank classes by similarity to the rare class, then injecting domain-invariant expert knowledge — is potentially interesting.

That said, after reading this twice, I still found the method description frustratingly vague. The core claim seems to hinge on “integrates domain-invariant knowledge on rare event with DL,” but I could not pin down exactly what this means mathematically. If there is an equation formalizing the regularizer or the knowledge integration term, it needs to be much clearer and better motivated. Right now it reads a bit like a grab bag: pretrained model for similarity ranking, expert prior, deep learning classifier, somehow improved generalization. I’m not against hybrid systems — in fact I’ve reviewed/handled quite a few medical imaging papers where priors help a lot — but the paper needs to be sharper about what is actually novel versus engineering.

I was also not convinced by the evaluation as presented. The abstract reports average F1 of 90.2% and overall F1 of 85.0% across multi-center datasets, which sounds strong, but for a rare-event setup I really want to see prevalence, per-center breakdown, confidence intervals, and preferably AUCPR. Plain F1 can look great while hiding instability. If Table 2 or 3 contains this, it should be emphasized much more. Likewise, if Figure 1 is the pipeline diagram, it should explicitly show where the “single-domain” assumption applies during training and what exactly is transferred to unseen centers.

Another concern: using a large vision model to rank class similarity sounds suspiciously close to importing external domain knowledge through the back door. I might be wrong, but if that model was pretrained on broad data and then used to structure the label space, the SDG framing becomes a bit muddy. Not invalid, but it should be discussed honestly.

Overall, I think there is a real paper in here, but in its current form it feels under-specified and somewhat over-claimed. Promising application, weak methodological clarity, and not enough careful analysis for such a difficult setting. I lean borderline reject unless the full version is much clearer than the abstract suggests.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantial than a lot of recent “foundation model for X” submissions. Training on 6,700 hours of fMRI is nontrivial, and the paper does a decent job motivating why masked prediction over brain dynamics might produce generally useful representations. I was especially interested in the zero-shot results around intrinsic functional networks (Fig. 4, if I’m reading correctly), because that is the kind of claim people often make very loosely, and here there seems to be at least some empirical grounding.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have real concerns about how much of the performance is due to “foundation model” properties versus careful preprocessing, dataset scale, and benchmark selection. The paper leans hard on generalization to external cohorts, but I wanted much more detail on cohort shift. On p. 5–6 the description of the train/test separation across datasets felt too high level. Were scanners, sites, and preprocessing pipelines genuinely disjoint? In fMRI, tiny leakage or harmonization effects can completely change the story. I might have missed it, but Table 2 should really report site/scanner-stratified performance, not just aggregate metrics.

I also found the interpretability claims overstated. The language around the latent space being a “lens” for cognition is a bit marketing-ish. The visualizations in Fig. 5 are suggestive, sure, but suggestive is not the same as interpretable. In my experience, latent traversals and clustering plots in neuroimaging are dangerously easy to overread. Relatedly, Eq. (3) / the masked objective is standard enough that the novelty is less in method and more in scale + evaluation, which is fine, but then the paper should be more careful not to imply a conceptual leap.

There are also some missing baselines. Why not compare more directly to strong sequence models trained per task, or to simpler pretraining objectives? The forecasting results (Table 3) looked promising, but I could not tell whether the gains are practically meaningful or just statistically significant on a huge dataset.

Overall I think this is a strong paper, but not a clean one. The main contribution is probably the dataset scale and demonstration that large self-supervised models for fMRI are viable. That is valuable. I’m just less convinced by the broader claims about interpretability and zero-shot cognitive insight. I would lean weak accept if the conference values ambitious large-scale empirical work, but I do think the authors need to tighten the claims and clarify the evaluation protocol.

I had mixed feelings about this paper. The topic is important, and I’ve reviewed a lot of “information-theoretic generalization of SGD” papers over the years, so I was initially quite interested. The connection to Neu et al. (2021) is clear, and I appreciate that the authors are not overselling a radically new theory. Still, after reading this twice, I’m not convinced the paper really delivers enough beyond incremental extensions.

The main technical contribution seems to be the new upper bounds in Sec. 3, especially around Theorem 2 on p. 5 and the discussion following Eq. (7). I think the derivation is mostly reasonable, but the presentation is harder to parse than it should be. In particular, the dependence on the step sizes and gradient noise is spread across several lemmas, and by the time I got to Eq. (11) on p. 6, I had to flip back repeatedly to check what exactly was assumed about boundedness/sub-Gaussianity. Maybe this is my fault, but I found the assumptions a bit slippery. The paper claims these are “mild,” but for modern deep nets they often are not.

I was also not entirely sure what to make of the linear and two-layer ReLU analyses in Sec. 4. The linear case is clean but somewhat unsurprising. The two-layer ReLU case is more interesting, but here the result feels quite stylized. Figure 2 is supposed to illustrate the scaling predicted by the bound, but visually the gap between theory and observed test error is still very large. That’s not fatal—these bounds are almost always loose—but then the practical implications should be stated more modestly.

The regularization idea introduced later (Table 2, p. 9) is probably the most interesting part of the paper, oddly enough. But this also made me uneasy: it starts to feel like two papers stitched together, one on theory and one on a heuristic inspired by the theory. The experimental evidence is decent, though not especially deep. I would have liked stronger baselines and more detail on hyperparameter tuning; “comparable to SOTA” is too vague as written.

Overall, I think there is some value here, and the paper is certainly competent. But for a top conference, I wanted either sharper theory, or more convincing empirical validation, or ideally a tighter connection between the two. Right now it feels like a respectable follow-up paper, not a standout one. My inclination is weak reject.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more compelling than I expected from the title. The idea of using an LLM+RL loop to generate mapper code for a parallel runtime is not crazy at all, and the DSL abstraction is probably the right way to make this tractable. In particular, the examples around matrix multiplication and the claim that the generated mappers can beat expert-written ones are attention-grabbing. Figure 1 does a decent job motivating the workflow, and Table 2/Table 3 suggest there is some real performance signal here, not just syntactic code generation.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have a fairly basic concern: it is not clear to me how much of the contribution is “LLMs are useful here” versus “we designed a good DSL/search space and then ran an expensive optimization loop over it.” The paper leans heavily on the former, but a lot of the empirical story seems to support the latter. The DSL appears central (I think this is around Sec. 3, maybe p. 4–5), yet I did not see a convincing ablation where the same search space is explored by simpler methods: evolutionary search, MCTS, even a strong enumerative or bandit baseline. Without that, the RL-guided LLM framing feels somewhat under-justified. I might be wrong, but this is the main thing holding me back.

I was also confused by the evaluation protocol. On p. 6/7, the throughput metric is introduced, but I wanted more detail on variance, number of trials, and whether these gains are robust across different hardware loads. Some of the reported speedups are modest except for the headline best cases, and I could not tell from Figure 4 whether the method consistently improves or just occasionally finds a better mapper after enough rollouts. Relatedly, development time “minutes not days” is a nice line, but it ignores the cost of RL exploration and repeated execution. That comparison felt a little hand-wavy.

Equation (3) also went by too quickly. The reward design seems crucial, but there is not much analysis of reward sparsity, invalid programs, or training stability. In my experience, these systems are often brittle in ways papers gloss over.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I like that the paper targets an important systems problem rather than another toy codegen benchmark. But I am not yet fully convinced by the methodology or the attribution of gains. Borderline for me; probably weak reject unless the authors can better isolate the role of the LLM and strengthen the baselines.

I went into this reasonably sympathetic because test-time adaptation is a crowded area and using neighborhood structure instead of raw self-confidence is, at least on paper, a sensible direction. After reading the paper twice, I think the core idea is intuitive but the paper overstates novelty a bit, and the empirical story is decent rather than fully convincing.

The main mechanism—nearest-neighbor-informed pseudo-labels plus a small ensemble of adaptation heads on top of a frozen backbone—is described around Sec. 3, especially Eq. (3)–(6). I found Eq. (4) and the surrounding text a little harder to parse than it should be. Maybe I’m missing something, but it took me a while to understand whether the nearest neighbors are computed only within the current test batch, over a memory queue, or over all observed test samples so far. This matters a lot in online TTA. The paper hints at online accumulation, but the exact protocol is fuzzy. Relatedly, the prototype-based class distribution term felt under-motivated; it reads a bit like “and we also regularize with prototypes because it helps.” I would have liked a cleaner justification or ablation there.

The experimental results in Table 1 and Table 2 are promising, especially on PACS and CIFAR-C, and Figure 3 does suggest some robustness under stronger shift. Still, I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are fully fair. TAST uses multiple randomly initialized adaptation modules and averages them at inference; that is extra capacity and some implicit ensembling. In my experience, these details can buy a surprising amount. The runtime/memory cost is barely discussed (I noticed only a brief comment near the end of p. 7), which is not great for a method intended for test-time deployment. Also, nearest-neighbor methods can be brittle in high dimensions unless the representation is already very good; the paper should say more about when this fails. TerraIncognita results, for instance, are not uniformly exciting.

I also think the claim in the abstract that existing pseudo-label TTA methods “often encounter performance degradation” is too sweeping. Some do, sure, but the literature is more nuanced than that.

Overall, I think this is a solid workshop-level paper and maybe borderline for a main conference depending on the bar. The idea is reasonable and the results are mostly good, but the technical novelty feels incremental, and the paper needs more clarity on the online setting, computational overhead, and ablations (number of heads, neighborhood size, prototype loss, batch order sensitivity). I lean weak accept if the venue values practical empirical gains; otherwise weak reject.

I went into this paper reasonably interested because the problem is actually important, and I’ve seen a lot of fairly hand-wavy “reverse engineering the attack” papers lately. This one is more ambitious on the theory side, and I appreciate that. The central idea—model the clean signal as lying in the range of a GAN and the attack as block-sparse, then jointly invert/recover—makes sense at a high level. The claimed deterministic linear convergence guarantee is also, if correct, nontrivial.

That said, after reading this twice, I still found the paper harder to trust than I wanted to. The theory is presented as the main contribution, but the assumptions seem pretty strong and in places somewhat hidden behind notation. In the main theorem around Eq. (7) / Eq. (8) (I may be off by one equation number), the conditions on the generator and the measurement/operator model look restrictive enough that I’m not sure how much of the practical setup they actually cover. The paper says this goes beyond union-of-subspaces assumptions, which is true in one sense, but replacing that with a GAN range plus regularity conditions is not exactly “mild.” I would have liked a more candid discussion of when these assumptions are expected to hold for modern GANs, especially on the datasets used later.

I also found the optimization description a bit slippery. The algorithm on page 5 reads like an alternating procedure with a thresholding-type update for the attack variable, but the convergence statement seems to rely on quantities that are not obviously computable or tunable in practice. Maybe I missed it, but I could not connect the theorem cleanly to the implementation details in Algorithm 1. Figure 2 helps intuitively, but the paper really needs a sharper theorem-to-code bridge.

On the empirical side, the results are decent but not overwhelming. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains over the baselines, but the comparisons do not feel completely airtight. Are the baselines also given comparable representation power and hyperparameter tuning? In my experience, GAN-inversion methods can be surprisingly sensitive here. I also would have liked an ablation on generator mismatch, since the whole method depends on the clean signal being well modeled by the pretrained GAN. Without that, the practical story feels incomplete.

So overall: interesting paper, serious attempt at theory, but I’m not fully convinced by the realism of the assumptions or the experimental validation. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the camera-ready could substantially clarify the scope of the guarantees.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more novel than a lot of meta-RL submissions I’ve seen recently. The setup—few-shot RL where the task is governed by an unknown subtask graph—is a good one, and it feels grounded rather than contrived. The central idea in MSGI, namely separating “infer the latent subtask dependency structure” from “act given the inferred structure,” makes intuitive sense to me. I liked Figure 2; it conveys the adaptation/test split and the role of graph inference fairly clearly. The StarCraft experiments also help, because otherwise this could have looked like yet another gridworld paper.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper undersells some assumptions and oversells some conclusions. The biggest issue for me is around what exactly is being inferred and how rich the task family really is. On p.3–4, the latent parameterization of the subtask graph seems fairly structured, and Equation (3) plus the update around the posterior/inference model (I may be paraphrasing; the notation got a bit dense there) suggest the agent is benefiting from a pretty strong inductive bias. That is not necessarily bad! But then the claims about “autonomous inference” feel too broad. In my experience, these methods work well when the latent family is carefully aligned with the environment generator, and much less well otherwise. I wanted at least one experiment with misspecified graph structure.

I was also a bit unconvinced by the intrinsic reward design. The UCB-style bonus is sensible, but the paper doesn’t really disentangle whether the gains come from the graph inference machinery or just better exploration. Table 1 and Table 2 show decent margins, but there should really be a stronger ablation: no-bonus MSGI, random bonus, count-based bonus, maybe even oracle graph + no bonus. Some of this is there, but not enough for me to isolate the contribution. Figure 4 looks good, though; the adaptation curves are one of the stronger pieces of evidence.

Another thing: comparisons. The baselines are okay, but I’m not entirely sure they are the right ones. This problem is halfway between meta-RL and structured hierarchical planning, and I missed stronger task-inference baselines or model-based RL baselines. The paper repeatedly says “existing meta RL and hierarchical RL methods,” but the benchmark set felt selective.

Overall, I lean positive because the problem formulation is interesting and the method is coherent. But I do think the empirical story is a little too neat, and the paper needs to be more honest about the structural assumptions behind the latent graph family. Borderline accept for me.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of robustness papers in this space. The core idea—reusing training dynamics from a cheaper reference model and then shortening the main-model fine-tuning—is simple, and that simplicity is a plus here. After reading the abstract and then going through the method section twice, I think the paper’s main claim is really two claims: transferability of data-map style training dynamics across models, and the observation that DM-selected data lets the main model converge faster. If both hold, FTFT is a pretty natural next step.

What I liked most is that the paper is not just “here is another robust fine-tuning trick,” but actually tries to cut the cost of an existing robustification pipeline. In my experience, that matters a lot more than yet another fractional robustness gain. The empirical story around transfer across model sizes/pre-training methods is the most compelling part. I thought the figures showing agreement/correlation of example importance across reference/main models (I believe around Fig. 2 or 3; sorry if I misremember) were persuasive at a high level.

That said, I do have several concerns. First, I’m not fully convinced the method is as generally justified as the paper suggests. The transferability result seems highly benchmark/model-family dependent. On p.4–5, where the authors motivate cross-model transfer, I wanted a clearer failure analysis: when does the reference model give a bad data map? Is there a threshold in capacity gap beyond which the selected subset degrades? Right now it reads a bit too cleanly.

Second, the novelty is somewhat incremental. This is basically “DM but with a cheaper proxy model and fewer steps,” which is sensible engineering, but I’m not sure it rises to the level of a strong methodological contribution unless the empirical evidence is very broad. I might be wrong, but several of the gains in the tables looked modest, and I had to work to disentangle robustness gains from compute reallocation. Table 2/Table 3 should really report wall-clock or FLOPs more consistently; “less than half the training cost” in the abstract is a strong claim and needs to be airtight.

I was also confused by the exact selection rule and step budget. Around the equations defining the transferred dynamics score (Eq. 3?) and the shortened fine-tuning schedule, the paper moves a bit too fast. Why this fraction of selected examples? Why this reduced number of steps? Some ablation is there, but it felt incomplete.

Overall, I think the paper is solid and useful, but not a slam dunk. My leaning is weak accept if the venue values practical efficiency/robustness work, otherwise borderline. The idea is good; I’m just not sure the paper fully nails the generality and novelty claims.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core claim — that in private full fine-tuning the pretrained backbone gets distorted because of misalignment with a random head, and that LP-then-FFT helps — is plausible and, honestly, matches a lot of anecdotal behavior people in DP fine-tuning have seen. So at a high level I buy the motivation. The paper is also aiming at something valuable: not just another empirical recipe, but an explanation of why these methods behave differently under DP noise.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the theory lands cleanly. The approximation scheme introduced for the 2-layer ReLU model (around the main theorem section; I think this is on pp. 4–6) feels fairly strong, and I had trouble judging whether the resulting upper/lower loss bounds are really telling us something robust about realistic fine-tuning, versus mostly formalizing the intuition in a very restricted setting. Eq. (6) and the assumptions around it are doing a lot of work. Maybe I missed something, but the paper sometimes slides from “in this canonical model” to broader claims about pretrained transformers/CNNs a little too quickly.

I did like the discussion of the backbone-head misalignment mechanism. Figure 1 is helpful, and the empirical trends in the later tables are generally consistent with the story. Table 1 / Table 2 (assuming I tracked the numbering right) show the expected pattern that DP-FFT hurts more and DP-LP-FFT is more stable, especially at tighter privacy budgets. That part is useful. But the experiments are not as exhaustive as I wanted. In particular, the privacy-budget allocation trade-off for multi-phase training is one of the more interesting claims in the abstract, yet the empirical treatment of that seemed pretty light. I wanted a more systematic sweep, not just a couple of points.

I also think the paper could be clearer about what is genuinely new relative to prior DP transfer learning work. There have been several papers arguing that freezing most parameters, or staged training, helps under DP. The novelty here is the training-dynamics view and the loss bounds, which is fine, but the related work section undersells how close some of the practical conclusions are to what people already believe.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, maybe borderline depending on the bar. The intuition is good, the problem matters, and there is some real insight here. But I’m not entirely sure the theoretical contribution is as strong as the paper presents it, and the empirical validation, while decent, doesn’t fully close that gap. My leaning is weak accept if the venue values theory-guided understanding of DP fine-tuning, otherwise maybe weak reject.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because “experience sharing across tasks” for LLM agents is a pretty crowded space now, and a lot of papers oversell retrieval plus a thin layer of theory. After reading this twice, I think this paper is better than that, but I still have some reservations.

The core idea is sensible: instead of naively reusing prior trajectories/reflections, CoPS tries to select experiences from previous tasks with a pessimism-based criterion that is supposed to protect against distribution shift. I liked the framing on p.2–3 contrasting reflection-only methods with external experience methods. In my experience reviewing agent papers, this is exactly where many methods break: they retrieve “similar” examples in a way that looks plausible but is basically heuristic. Here the authors at least try to make the selection principle explicit. The theorem around Eq. (4) / Eq. (5) (assuming I parsed it correctly) gives some justification for why matched experience should help and mismatched experience can hurt. That said, I’m not entirely sure the theory really captures the actual deployed system. The bound seems to depend on quantities that are not observable in practice, and the jump from the stylized trial distribution analysis to the concrete prompting/retrieval pipeline felt a bit hand-wavy. This is a common issue, but it matters because the paper leans hard on “provable.”

Empirically, the results are solid. Table 1 and Table 2 show fairly consistent gains on Alfworld, Webshop, and HotPotQA, which is not trivial given how different those benchmarks are. I especially appreciated the sample-efficiency angle; the low-data curves in Fig. 3 are probably the most convincing part of the paper to me. CoPS seems to help early, not just asymptotically. Still, I found the ablations weaker than I wanted. The experience selection module is the whole point, but the comparison to simpler retrieval baselines on p.6 is a bit thin. Why not stronger nearest-neighbor or reranking baselines? Also, I could not tell whether gains come from “cross-task” sharing specifically or just from having more total demonstrations in the prompt/memory.

Some writing was confusing. The method section around Algorithm 1 is denser than necessary, and I had to reread the notation for the distributions. Fig. 2 helped, but honestly the paper could be more direct about what is stored, what is retrieved, and what the LLM actually sees at inference.

Overall, I think this is a meaningful paper with real empirical value and a better-than-average attempt at theory, but I’m not fully convinced the “provable” claim matches the practical contribution. Lean accept, though not enthusiastically.

I found the paper reasonably interesting, but also a bit undercooked. The core idea—compare paired embedding spaces through the similarity of their induced k-NN graphs—is intuitive, and honestly more intuitive than yet another kernel alignment variant. I liked the multi-scale angle in particular. The discussion around varying neighborhood size to expose local vs more global structure (around p. 3–4, where the metric is introduced) makes sense, and I do think this is something CKA does not give you in a very interpretable way.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not entirely sure the paper has nailed down what is actually new versus what is a reasonable but fairly direct graph-overlap statistic. The formal definition around the main metric (I think Eq. 2 or Eq. 3—depending on how the final numbering lands) is straightforward, maybe too straightforward, and the paper doesn’t really situate it against prior work on neighborhood preservation / rank-based embedding comparison. There is a lot of literature here beyond CKA. I was surprised not to see stronger discussion of trustworthiness/continuity style measures, graph matching ideas, or representation similarity work that is explicitly local.

The empirical section is mixed for me. The GloVe analogy case study is fine, but not especially convincing. Correlation between your similarity score and analogy accuracy is suggestive, yes, but there are many confounds. Same for the CLIP/BLIP-2 zero-shot result. I kept wanting a stronger controlled experiment: take spaces with known transformations or known degradations, then show the metric behaves predictably. Figure 2 (if I’m recalling correctly—the one sweeping neighborhood size) is probably the most useful figure in the paper, but the later case-study plots feel more like illustrations than evidence. Table 1 also needs uncertainty estimates; several differences looked small.

A smaller but important issue: the method depends heavily on k, distance choice, and probably hubness in high dimensions. The paper says this can be “configured,” but that is not the same as being robust. I would have liked a much clearer sensitivity analysis, maybe even on synthetic data. Also, computational cost is not discussed enough. Building nearest-neighbor graphs for large embedding sets is not free.

Overall, I think there is a decent idea here, and I can imagine people using it. But in its current form it feels more like a workshop paper or a solid diagnostic note than a fully convincing conference paper. My leaning is weak reject, though I’m a bit on the fence because the basic intuition is good.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more surprising than I expected from the title. The central claim — that self-supervised pretraining helps DP learning essentially across the whole spectrum from “one public image” to large public datasets — is provocative, and in parts the empirical evidence does support it. I especially appreciated the attempt to study the tiny-public-data regime, which most papers conveniently ignore. The “single image” setup is weird, but in a good way: it forces the authors to confront what is really being transferred.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some trouble with how broad the conclusions are relative to what is actually shown. The abstract says “regardless of the size of available public datasets,” which is a very strong statement. But the experiments (from what I can tell around Tables 2–4 and the discussion near Fig. 3) are still confined to image classification, and mostly standard vision benchmarks. In my experience, these claims often break once the private task distribution shifts more seriously, or once the public data are semantically mismatched. I might be wrong, but the paper seems to conflate “small public data” with “small but still visually useful public data.”

I also found the comparison to supervised pretraining a bit underexplained. On p. 6, around Eq. (4) and the surrounding text, the private classifier is trained on frozen features, but I wasn’t fully clear whether the supervised baselines get exactly the same architecture, augmentations, and optimization budget as the SSP model. This matters a lot. Self-supervised methods are often just stronger pretraining pipelines now, independent of labels. If the supervised baseline is weaker or less tuned, the conclusion becomes less interesting.

The single-image result is memorable, but also where I am most skeptical. What exactly is the model learning there beyond invariances induced by augmentation? Maybe that’s enough, fair, but then the paper should say this more directly. The handcrafted-feature baseline in that section felt like a straw man. A stronger non-learned or classical representation baseline would help.

I did like the comparison of multiple DP training frameworks; that part felt practical and useful. Still, privacy accounting details need to be easier to audit. I had to hunt a bit for whether all methods use matched epsilon/delta and clipping choices. Some of this may be in the appendix, but it should be clearer in the main paper.

Overall, I think this is a solid empirical paper with a genuinely interesting message, but the framing overshoots the evidence. I lean positive because the phenomenon seems real, yet I would want tighter claims and cleaner baselines before being fully convinced.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more interesting than I expected from the title. The connection between distilled/synthetic data and IMP is not something I’ve seen explored carefully before, and the linear mode connectivity angle gives the paper a real hook beyond just “we can prune with fewer examples.” The claim in the abstract about matching traditional IMP on ResNet-18/CIFAR-10 with 150x fewer training points is pretty striking, and Table 1 does seem to support that at least on the small-scale setting. I also liked the qualitative loss landscape figures (I assume Fig. 3/4; the numbering was a bit hard to track on first read) because they make the story intuitively plausible: the subnetworks found with distilled data do appear to sit in broader basins.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper has nailed down causality rather than correlation. A lot of the narrative, especially around pages 5–7, is “distilled data removes sharpness-inducing parameters,” but the evidence is mostly observational: LMC between seeds/shuffles, some Hessian-based sharpness proxies, and visualizations. Useful, yes, but still pretty circumstantial. Equation (3) / the Hessian approximation discussion (around page 6, if I’m reading correctly) felt under-explained. I had to reread that section twice and I’m still not entirely sure how stable those estimates are across runs. In my experience, sharpness measurements can be maddeningly sensitive to implementation details.

The ImageNet-10 / ResNet-10 result is probably the most novel part for me. The claim that synthetic-data-derived subnetworks are stable to SGD noise at initialization when dense or standard IMP subnetworks are not is cool, and Fig. 5 makes it look real. But I really wanted more repetitions and error bars there. Some of these connectivity phenomena can be fickle. Also, I think the paper slightly overstates “architectural validation” as a conclusion. This is still a fairly narrow empirical study, not yet a general principle.

Another issue: the method section is a bit too compressed. Details about the distilled data generation procedure, budget, and whether hyperparameters were tuned equally for standard IMP vs synthetic-data IMP need to be crystal clear, because otherwise the comparison is hard to trust. I might have missed it, but I also wanted a sanity check using random compressed data or coreset baselines beyond distilled data, just to isolate whether this is really about “syntheticness” versus simply reduced variance / reduced support.

Overall, I lean positive because the phenomenon seems real and the paper is asking a good question. But I’m not completely sold on the mechanistic claims yet, and the experimental section needs a little more rigor to match the ambition of the conclusions.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because “fix Janus by changing the pose prior” is the kind of idea that sounds cleaner on paper than it usually works in practice. But after reading the paper twice, I think there is a real idea here. The central move—rectifying the diffusion prior so that the pose marginal becomes uniform, then plugging this into SDS—makes intuitive sense, and the way it is framed around reconstructing a density under marginal constraints is more principled than a lot of recent text-to-3D papers that are basically heuristic soup.

That said, I found the presentation harder than it needed to be. The discussion around the auxiliary function on p. 3–4 and especially Eq. (6)–(9) is pretty dense, and I’m still not entirely sure the derivation is as clean as the authors think. I might be wrong, but it feels like a lot hinges on assumptions about the estimated posterior over poses that are only approximately true once noise is added. The noisy-state approximation discussed around lines 210–250 is important, but a bit hand-wavy. Figure 2 helps, though Figure 3 is actually more convincing to me because it shows the practical effect rather than the abstract pipeline.

The training-free classifier is clever, and honestly probably the most useful engineering contribution here. But this also raises a concern: how robust is the whole method to pose misclassification? Table 2 shows gains over baselines, yes, but I wanted at least one ablation where the classifier is deliberately weakened or replaced with a poorer estimator. In my experience, these plug-and-play modules are often where methods quietly fail. Also, the paper keeps saying “uniform” as if that is obviously the right target distribution. I’m not convinced. For many object categories, the true image prior is absolutely not uniform over pose, and forcing uniformity may trade one bias for another. The authors should discuss that more honestly.

The qualitative results are pretty good. The reductions in repeated facial/body structures in the multi-view renders (Fig. 5 and Fig. 6) are noticeable, not just cherry-picked eye candy. On the other hand, the evaluation is still a bit weak by current standards. I would have liked either a stronger geometry consistency metric or at least more cross-method comparisons with recent pose-aware SDS variants. Some of the baselines feel dated.

Overall I lean positive. The paper has a real conceptual contribution, and unlike many text-to-3D submissions, it is not just incremental prompting tricks. But the theory-to-practice bridge is shakier than advertised, and the empirical section should be tougher on the method. Borderline accept.

I found this paper interesting, and in spirit I’m sympathetic to it. The community has spent a lot of time treating rationales as if “better task accuracy” automatically means “better explanations,” and I agree with the premise here that these are different things. The paper is also refreshingly focused on smaller models, which I appreciate. After reading it twice, though, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as clean or as substantial as the framing suggests.

The core idea—conditioning generation on multiple reward signals and then distilling back into a smaller model—is reasonable. It’s not wildly novel, to be honest, but it is a sensible combination of existing ingredients. I think the most compelling part of the paper is actually the empirical pattern in Tables 2 and 3: the model seems to improve not just answer accuracy but also several rationale-related metrics at once, which is harder than it sounds. Figure 3 was also helpful in showing the tradeoffs across rewards, though I wish the authors had been more candid about the degree of tuning involved.

My main issue is that the paper leans heavily on notions like “plausibility,” “consistency,” and “helpfulness,” but the operationalization feels a bit slippery. On p. 5–6 (around the reward definitions), I kept asking myself whether these rewards are measuring genuine rationale quality or just stylistic conformity to what annotators/models already prefer. I might be wrong, but some of this looks like reward hacking with nicer language. The distinction between “faithful” and “plausible” is mentioned in the abstract and intro, but the actual experiments mostly support plausibility/preference, not faithfulness. That gap matters. A lot.

I was also confused by Eq. (4) and the surrounding discussion on reward aggregation. Is the weighting fixed globally, tuned per dataset, or adapted during training? The text suggests one thing, the appendix another. If these coefficients were tuned on validation human preference, that would weaken the claim of generality quite a bit.

The human evaluation is a plus, but I wanted more detail. On p. 8 and Table 4, the preference gains are nice, but I couldn’t tell how often raters saw factually wrong but fluent rationales and whether this inflated preference. In my experience, human judges over-reward coherence.

Overall: promising paper, decent empirical contribution, but the claims about rationale “quality” are a bit ahead of the evidence. I’d lean weak accept if the conference values practical LM alignment/control work, but I’m not enthusiastic.

I found the paper interesting, and in parts quite elegant, but I also had a somewhat frustrating reading experience. The main result — that for deep linear MSE regression, hard-wiring invariance and data augmentation induce the same critical points, while regularization introduces extra saddles but converges along a continuous path — is clean and, I think, worth knowing. In particular, the claim around the equivalence of critical points for the augmented and hard-wired objectives (I’m referring to the theorem stated around p. 4–5, and the discussion near Eq. (6)/(7)) is the kind of structural statement that could actually clarify a lot of hand-wavy empirical folklore.

That said, the paper feels narrower than it first appears. The title says “loss landscape” in invariant deep linear neural networks, which sounds broad, but the actual setting is very specific: linear maps, MSE, rank-constrained factorizations, and fairly idealized group averaging. That’s fine, but I wanted more honesty about how much of the conclusion depends on linearity. After reading the proofs around the critical point characterization (I think this is in Section 3, around Proposition/Theorem on p. 6–7), my sense is that almost everything depends on spectral structure and exact factorization geometry. So the broader motivation about modern invariant deep learning felt a bit oversold.

I did like the result that all non-optimal critical points are saddles. This is a strong and useful property, and the authors seem to prove it carefully. Figure 1 helped, though honestly I’m not sure it added much beyond intuition. Table 1 (if I understood it correctly) was useful as a summary, but I wish it had been more explicit about assumptions, especially whether the equivalence requires exact invariance of the data distribution versus only the target map. I might be wrong here, but I found the setup around the group action and the projection to the invariant subspace a little too compressed.

Another issue is novelty. There is already a fairly mature literature on benign landscapes for deep linear nets and on equivalences induced by augmentation/group averaging. The contribution here is the comparison among three mechanisms in one framework, which is nice, but I’m not convinced it is a major conceptual leap. It’s more of a careful synthesis plus one or two clean new observations.

Overall I lean positive, though not strongly. I think the math is probably correct and the paper is technically solid. But the scope is limited, and the writing could do a better job separating what is genuinely new from what is inherited from standard deep linear network geometry. If accepted, this would be as a neat theory paper, not a broad statement about invariant deep learning in general.

I read this with some interest because the TRPO/PPO lineage is very familiar territory, and honestly the field has a lot of “slightly different clipping / penalty” papers that don’t amount to much. This one is somewhat better than that, but I’m still not fully convinced.

The main idea—replace the KL-flavored trust-region story with a TV-based surrogate and derive a simple first-order update—does make intuitive sense. I think the discussion around the probability ratio being more tightly controlled than PPO’s clipping is the most compelling part of the paper. In particular, the derivation around Eq. (6)–(9) (assuming I tracked the notation correctly) is cleaner than I expected, and the link back to monotonic improvement arguments is a reasonable attempt to recover some of TRPO’s theoretical flavor without actually doing constrained second-order optimization. That said, I found the theory a bit slippery. On page 4, around the theorem statement and the assumptions below it, the practical meaning of the TV bound is not very clear. I might be wrong, but it seems like the guarantee still depends on quantities that are not directly controlled in the actual implementation. If so, the claimed middle ground between TRPO and PPO is more rhetorical than real.

The empirical section is decent but not decisive. Table 1 and Figure 3 suggest SPO beats PPO on several benchmarks, but the margins are not huge, and I really wanted stronger baselines. Where is PPO with tuned KL penalty? Or adaptive clipping? Or even a well-implemented TRPO on at least a subset of tasks? Beating a vanilla PPO setup in 2026 is just not enough. Also, on the “large, complex network architectures” claim from the abstract: the evidence for that felt thin. The results on page 7 are suggestive, sure, but not enough for such a broad claim.

I also found the presentation uneven. Figure 2 is helpful. Figure 4, less so. Some notation changes midstream, and after reading page 5 twice I was still unsure how the TV term is estimated in minibatch training versus how it appears in the theory. That gap matters.

Overall, I think this is a credible paper, not nonsense, but it overclaims. If accepted, it should be on the basis of a neat and plausible objective, not because it has definitively “combined the strengths” of TRPO and PPO. Right now I’d lean weak reject, maybe weak accept if the empirical comparisons are stronger in the rebuttal.

I read this paper with interest because memory scheduling for MCU-class deployment is a real problem, and a lot of prior work in this space either cheats a bit by accepting terrible latency or only works on toy sequential graphs. The core idea here — fine-grained operator partitioning plus schedule search — is actually pretty reasonable, and I think the paper is addressing an important systems problem. The claimed gains are also nontrivial. Table 2 and Table 3, if I understood them correctly, show pretty substantial peak memory reductions over prior methods, and unlike some earlier papers, the latency hit does not seem catastrophic.

That said, I found the paper harder to trust than I wanted. The ACG representation (around p.4–5, especially Fig. 3 and Eq. (4)) is probably the technical heart of the work, but the exposition is not very clean. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure what is encoded in the graph versus what is deferred to the search procedure. The distinction between “axis connection” and ordinary dependency structure felt under-motivated. Maybe this is just a writing issue, but for a systems paper, if the abstraction is new, it really needs to be nailed down.

I also think the evaluation is somewhat selective. The abstract claims “various popular networks,” but the experiments seem concentrated on a limited set of CNN-like architectures. I might be wrong, but I did not see convincing evidence that this handles genuinely messy topologies beyond the examples in Fig. 1. If the claim is that MoteS can optimize “complex structures,” then I would want to see stronger cases: residual-heavy models, multi-branch modules, maybe even transformers or at least something less regular. Otherwise this feels a bit oversold.

Another issue is comparison fairness. On p.7, the latency overhead discussion is too brief. Fine-grained partitioning often introduces extra kernel launches / fragmented execution / buffer management overhead in practice, especially on tiny runtimes. The paper says “nearly no latency overhead,” but I wanted more low-level measurements or at least a breakdown. In my experience, these overheads are easy to hide in aggregate numbers.

Still, I think there is something real here. The bottleneck-guided search is sensible, and the memory reductions are strong enough that I wouldn’t dismiss the contribution. But the paper needs tighter technical explanation and a more convincing evaluation before I’d be fully comfortable. Right now I’m leaning borderline positive, mostly because the problem matters and the approach seems more practical than a lot of prior memory-saving work, but I’m not fully sold.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more compelling than I expected from the title. The core claim — that effective context length is limited by a left-skewed relative-position distribution induced during pretraining/post-training — feels plausible, and the proposed fix is refreshingly simple. I’ve read a lot of “long-context” papers that are basically eval-heavy prompt engineering with a new name; this one at least has a concrete mechanism and a testable hypothesis.

The analysis around the positional skew was the part I liked most, especially the empirical distributions in Fig. 2 and the discussion around why later positions are undertrained. That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper nails causality. After reading the middle sections twice, I still think there is some slippage between “these positions occur less often” and “this is the main reason effective context collapses.” There are other well-known issues here: optimization dynamics, instruction tuning over short sequences, retrieval failure due to attention dilution, etc. The ablations around post-training vs pretraining contributions helped, but not enough for me to conclude the story is complete.

The method itself (STRING) is clever in a slightly hacky way, which I mean as a compliment. Shifting well-trained RoPE positions to overwrite weak ones at inference is the kind of idea that sounds wrong for five minutes and then annoyingly works. Table 2 and Table 3 are strong. The gains on RULER/InfiniteBench are large enough that they’re hard to dismiss as noise, and the fact that this works on models as large as Llama 3.1 70B and Qwen2 72B matters. If those numbers hold, practitioners will definitely try this.

I did have some confusion on the exact implementation details. Eq. (6) and the surrounding text (I think on p. 5) could be clearer about how the shift amount is selected across tasks and whether there is any benchmark-specific tuning. If there is, that weakens the “training-free” story somewhat. Also, Fig. 4 looked promising, but I wanted more failure analysis: where does STRING hurt? Needle-style retrieval is one thing; realistic multi-document reasoning is another.

One more concern: the comparisons to commercial models in the abstract feel a bit too triumphalist. Different APIs, prompting, and undocumented system behavior make those comparisons messy. I would tone that down.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper. Not airtight theoretically, but novel, useful, and backed by unusually strong empirical results. I lean accept.

I had mixed feelings about this paper. The core idea — using Holographic Reduced Representation to induce a more decomposed linguistic representation inside an LM — is interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing given how many papers just scale a standard encoder and call it insight. I’ve read a fair amount of older distributed representation work, including Plate-style HRR and some tensor/product-key variants, so I was curious to see whether this would amount to something more than nostalgia. I’m not fully convinced, but I do think there is something here.

What I found most compelling is the attempt to move beyond “structureless” embeddings, especially the chunk-level construction described around Eq. (3)–(5). The paper’s claim that the learned factors loosely separate syntactic and semantic roles is plausible, and Figure 2 does suggest some nontrivial clustering. Table 2 also points in the right direction. That said, “crude linguistic roles” is doing a lot of work in this paper, maybe too much. After reading the analysis section twice, I still don’t know how robust this decomposition really is, versus being an artifact of the probing setup or the chosen visualization. The discussion on page 5 (around lines 180–205, if I’m reading the numbering right) felt hand-wavy.

A bigger issue for me is the experimental framing. The baselines seem weak. If the goal is to show that HRR gives a better inductive bias for decomposed representations, then comparing mainly to fairly standard word-level/chunk-level models is not enough. I kept wanting a stronger disentanglement-oriented baseline, or at least an ablation where the binding/unbinding operations are replaced by similarly parameterized but unstructured composition. Without that, it’s hard to attribute the effect to HRR per se rather than just extra machinery. Also, Figure 1 is not very helpful; it gives the high-level architecture but omits details that matter for interpretation.

I also found the writing uneven. Some parts are crisp, then suddenly important implementation details are missing. For example, I’m not entirely sure how sensitive the results are to dimensionality and chunking decisions; page 4 hints at this but doesn’t really answer it. In my experience, these structured representation papers often hinge on exactly those choices.

Overall, I think the paper is intellectually interesting, but the empirical case is not strong enough yet for a top conference. I’d lean borderline reject. If the authors tightened the analysis and added stronger controls, I could imagine being more enthusiastic.

I went into this a bit skeptical because “v3” papers in this space are often just small architectural edits plus a lot of benchmark tables, and to be honest this paper is not entirely free of that feeling. Still, after reading it twice, I think the core idea is reasonable and probably useful in practice.

The main change—simplifying the fusion block by combining local, global, and input features in a cleaner way—does make intuitive sense. In MobileViTv1, the fusion stage was always a little awkward, and I agree with the authors’ claim around p.3–4 that it creates a scaling bottleneck and a harder optimization problem than necessary. Figure 2 helped, although I found the exact distinction between the old and new fusion paths a bit too visually compressed. I had to cross-reference Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) a few times to make sure I understood what was being added versus concatenated. Maybe that’s on me, but I suspect other readers will also stumble there.

The empirical results are solid overall. Table 1 and Table 3 show consistent gains on ImageNet and downstream tasks, and I appreciate that the paper compares against both MobileViTv1 and v2 rather than pretending v2 does not exist. That said, I’m not fully convinced the story is as clean as the paper wants it to be. Some of the gains are modest, and because multiple design choices differ across versions, it is hard to isolate how much comes specifically from the fusion block versus training recipe/details. The ablation around the fusion design (I think this is Table 2) is directionally helpful, but I wanted more. For example, what is the compute/memory effect at inference time on actual mobile hardware, not just FLOPs? This matters a lot for a paper making “mobile-friendly” claims.

I also found the writing uneven. There are several places where the grammar is rough and the argument skips steps. On p.5, lines around the discussion of why the new block “simplifies learning,” the explanation felt more asserted than demonstrated. In my experience, if optimization difficulty is a central claim, I want either training curves, gradient/statistics analysis, or at least a more careful mechanistic argument.

So: useful paper, probably incrementally publishable, but not exciting. I think the method is practical and the results are credible, yet the novelty is modest and the paper could be sharper in both analysis and presentation. Weak accept, assuming the bar for this venue includes strong empirical systems/modeling papers and not just major conceptual leaps.

I found the paper interesting, and I do think it is trying to solve a real issue that gets glossed over in a lot of “graph imputation” work. The point about low-variance feature channels being effectively ignored by diffusion-style imputers is, in my experience, plausible and not something many papers discuss explicitly. The synthetic-feature idea is also simple in a good way. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as principled as the presentation suggests.

The core mechanism around pre-diffusion and identifying low-variance channels (I think this is introduced around Sec. 3, with the main update in Eq. (6) or nearby) is a bit murky. I could follow the high-level intuition, but I had trouble understanding why generating a synthetic feature for one randomly chosen node per low-variance channel is the right intervention, rather than, say, using a learned channel-wise prior or a global feature anchor. The randomness especially bothers me. Is the method stable across seeds? I didn’t see enough discussion of variance over runs. If Table 2 is averaged over multiple seeds, that should be stated much more clearly.

I also think the empirical claims are somewhat overstated. The results in the classification setting are good, yes, but on some datasets the margins in Table 1/Table 3 are not huge, and I’m not sure they justify the “state-of-the-art” language in the abstract. There are also a lot of moving parts: pre-diffusion, channel selection, synthetic node initialization, joint diffusion with observed features. I wanted a cleaner ablation. Figure 2 helped intuition a little, but not enough. For example, what happens if synthetic features are added to all channels? Or to the top-k lowest variance channels? Or to multiple nodes instead of one? These are obvious ablations and I was surprised they were not all there.

Another issue is realism of missingness. Unless I missed it, the experiments mostly use random masking. That’s standard, but also kind of lazy at this point. Real partially observed graph features are often not MCAR. In practice they are correlated with node type, degree, time, etc. A method built around low-variance channels might behave very differently there.

Still, I think there is a kernel of a good paper here. The idea is novel enough for me, and the results are generally solid. But the paper needs more justification for the synthetic-feature construction and better robustness analysis. Right now I’m leaning weak accept / borderline, mostly because the contribution is interesting, but I’m not entirely sure the method is as robust or broadly motivated as the paper claims.
