I read this paper with some interest because the gap between what EF-type methods seem to do in practice and what we can actually prove under non-standard smoothness assumptions is real, and honestly a bit annoying. So I appreciate the direction here. The main claim — convergence of normalized error feedback methods under generalized smoothness, in distributed stochastic/nonconvex settings without the usual crutches — is potentially quite meaningful.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as clean or as strong as it presents itself. The technical statements around the generalized smoothness model are interesting, but I found the setup a bit slippery on first read and, after reading parts of Sections 2–3 twice, still not completely transparent. In particular, the exact role of the normalization in the proofs versus in the algorithmic intuition is not explained very well. Equation (6) and the discussion right after it seem to be doing a lot of hidden work. I think the authors should be more explicit about what fails for standard EF21 under the same assumptions and why normalization specifically fixes it, beyond “larger allowable stepsizes.” Right now that part feels more asserted than illuminated.

The assumptions are better than a lot of recent distributed generalized-smoothness papers, yes. I did notice the repeated emphasis that no data heterogeneity condition is needed, which is good, but I’m not entirely sure the practical stochastic assumptions are truly mild either. I might be wrong, but the constants in the rate bounds look quite pessimistic, and the stepsize-independence claim in the abstract should be stated more carefully. On p. 5–6 (around Theorem 1 / 2), the stepsize may not depend on unknown problem parameters in one sense, but it certainly depends on algorithmic quantities and boundedness surrogates that are not obviously easier to know in practice.

Empirically, the paper is decent but not amazing. Figure 1 and Table 1 support the story, and I liked that they included polynomial objectives in addition to standard ML tasks. Still, the deep learning evidence is fairly thin. One ResNet-20 experiment is not enough for the breadth of the claims, especially since these methods are motivated by DNN training. I also would have liked a direct ablation on normalization itself and maybe a sensitivity plot for the larger stepsizes they keep advertising.

Overall, I think there is a real contribution here, and the paper is closer to accept than reject for me, but it needs sharper exposition and a more convincing empirical section. The theory seems nontrivial and probably sound, though I confess I did not verify every lemma in the appendix. My score would be mildly positive.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because the “DG does not beat ERM” story has been told many times already, but I do think this paper has a reasonably fresh angle. Looking at DG through label-noise robustness is interesting, and at least to me the synthetic results make the intuition fairly concrete. I especially liked the setup around the finite-sample effect of noisy labels amplifying reliance on spurious features; that part around the main theoretical development (I think Sec. 3, around Eq. (4)–(7)?) was one of the more compelling parts of the paper.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper lands as strongly as it wants to. The central narrative seems to be: DG methods are implicitly more robust to label noise, this can help in synthetic settings, but on real benchmarks it still does not really beat ERM because maybe the ERM failure mode is weak in practice. That is plausible, yes, but also a bit deflating. After reading this twice, I’m left wondering whether the paper is mainly diagnosing a limited corner case rather than explaining the broader empirical puzzle. The abstract is actually honest about this, which I appreciate, but the contribution then feels narrower than the framing suggests.

I also found some of the theoretical assumptions a little too tidy. I might be wrong here, but the finite-sample analysis seems heavily tied to a stylized spurious-correlation model with specific noise assumptions, and I’m not sure how much survives outside that regime. The paper should be clearer about what exactly is algorithm-specific versus what is an artifact of the simplified model. On page 5 (near the proposition following Eq. (8)?), I had trouble seeing whether the robustness claim really distinguishes DG objectives in a principled way or just reflects a regularization effect that other baselines could also enjoy.

The experimental section is mixed for me. The synthetic figures (Fig. 2/3, if I’m remembering correctly) are clean and support the story. The real-data tables are less exciting. Table 1 basically confirms the now-standard result that ERM is very hard to beat, and while that negative result is still useful, I wanted more careful probing: e.g., controlled noise injection on DomainBed benchmarks, sensitivity to noise rate, and whether all DG methods benefit equally or some families do. As written, the empirical story feels a bit undercooked.

Overall, I think this is a thoughtful paper with one genuinely interesting insight, but the practical takeaway is limited and the theory-to-practice bridge is not fully there. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values careful empirical diagnosis, otherwise weak reject.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more practically motivated than a lot of robustness submissions. The physical-world angle in the abstract is compelling, and the idea of coupling adversarial training with a meta-update for universal perturbations does make intuitive sense. In particular, the core issue they highlight — that universal perturbations become stale as the model changes during training — is real, and I’ve seen similar problems in prior adversarial training work. So at a high level, I buy the premise.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I still think the presentation is not as clear as it needs to be. The update rule around Eq. (3) / Eq. (4) (assuming I’m tracking the notation correctly) is written in a way that makes MAT sound almost simpler than it is. I’m not entirely sure whether the meta-learned perturbation bank is actually optimizing for transfer across nearby model iterates, or if this is just a heuristic online update with a meta-learning label attached. There is a fine line there, and I don’t think the paper fully justifies the terminology. In my experience, robustness papers often overuse “meta” when they really mean “bi-level-ish training trick.”

The empirical results are promising, especially the patch attack robustness numbers in Table 1 and the traffic-light detection results later on (I think Table 3 / Fig. 5). But I wanted more stress testing. For example, how sensitive is MAT to the number of maintained perturbations, their initialization, and the ratio of clean/adversarial batches? The paper claims “little extra computation,” but the wall-clock and memory overhead discussion on page 6 is too light. Relative FLOPs are not enough; actual training-time multipliers matter here.

I also found the comparison set a bit selective. The paper compares mostly against standard adversarial training baselines and some universal-attack defenses, but I would have liked stronger baselines with larger perturbation pools or simply more frequent regeneration of universal patches. Maybe those are too expensive, fine, but then say that clearly and quantify it.

One thing I did like: the paper is not just CIFAR robustness theater. The traffic-light detection experiment gives the work more credibility. Still, I’m unsure how much of the gain comes from the specific attack/defense setup being well matched. The adaptive attack evaluation needs to be stronger; otherwise these robustness claims feel a little optimistic.

Overall, I think the idea is solid and probably worth acceptance if the bar is novelty plus practical relevance. But the paper currently undersells the methodological subtlety and oversells the evaluation a bit. I’m leaning weak accept / borderline.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thoughtful than the usual “yet another SAM variant” submission. The main idea — that the original SAM min-max game is not really aligned with the 0-1 objective we care about, and should instead be reformulated as a bilevel problem with different surrogates for the inner and outer players — is conceptually clean. I especially liked the discussion around the generalization-gap bound on p.2–3; the motivation is better grounded than many optimization papers in this area. The distinction between using an upper bound for the minimizing player and a lower bound for the maximizing player makes intuitive sense, and I think the paper explains that fairly well, at least at a high level.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some reservations. The theoretical story is plausible, but I’m not entirely sure how much is genuinely new versus a reframing of existing surrogate-risk arguments. The key claim seems to rest on the tighter relation to 0-1 loss, but the actual optimization procedure in Eq. (6)/(7) still depends heavily on specific surrogate choices, and I found the practical consequences of those choices under-discussed. Why this lower bound in particular? Are the gains from the bilevel structure, or from picking a more favorable surrogate pair? The paper hints at this, but never really isolates it.

The empirical section is solid but not knockout-level. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent improvements, yes, but mostly modest ones. In my experience with SAM-style methods, small gains can disappear with enough tuning, and I wanted more evidence that the comparisons are equally optimized. I might be wrong, but the baselines feel a bit under-stressed here. Also, Figure 3 is suggestive, though I wasn’t sure it added much beyond the tables. The computational overhead claim (“similar complexity”) also deserves a more careful wall-clock analysis; counting backward passes is not always enough in practice.

One thing I found confusing: the derivation around the bilevel approximation on p.4, around Eq. (8), moves pretty quickly, and the assumptions needed for the approximation/gradient estimation are easy to miss. I would strongly encourage the authors to slow down there.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, maybe borderline depending on venue competitiveness. The core idea is meaningful and better motivated than average, but the empirical margins are not huge, and some of the theoretical novelty feels a little overstated. Still, I’d lean weak accept because this is a real attempt to rethink SAM rather than just tweak it.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea—explicitly separating structural node representations from positional ones and letting both evolve through the network—is sensible, and honestly overdue. A lot of recent graph PE work just dumps Laplacian eigenvectors into the input and hopes for the best. Here, the authors are at least trying to treat position as a first-class learnable object rather than static decoration. I liked that.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper isolates what is actually new versus what is a fairly natural architectural tweak. The LSPE formulation around Eq. (4)–(7) is clear enough mechanically, but conceptually it feels incremental. The claim of “decoupling structural and positional representations” sounds stronger than what is really happening: the two streams are still tightly entangled through message passing and the readout, so “decoupling” may be overselling it a bit. Maybe “separate channels” would be more accurate.

The experimental section has some good signs. Table 2 and Table 3 show consistent gains on molecular benchmarks, and I do believe the method helps in practice. But the reporting is a little too celebratory for my taste. Saying improvements range “up to 64.14%” in the abstract is not very informative when some of those relative gains likely come from weak baselines or low absolute margins. I would much rather see a sober discussion of absolute error reduction and variance. On page 6, the ablations are useful, but I wanted more direct comparison against stronger PE baselines beyond Laplacian sign-flip handling and simple concatenation. In 2021-ish graph representation learning, that bar is not especially high.

I was also confused by Figure 2. Maybe this is on me, but the distinction between the structural stream and positional stream is visually clean while the actual update dependencies are not. I had to go back to the equations. Some notation is also slippery around the positional initialization on page 4, especially regarding eigenvector multiplicities and sign ambiguity. The paper mentions these issues, but I’m not sure it fully resolves them.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the acceptance bar if the venue values practical architectural improvements. It is not a conceptual breakthrough. But in my experience, simple changes that work across sparse GNNs and Transformer-like models do matter. My score would be in the weak accept range, with the caveat that the claims should be toned down and the empirical analysis made more rigorous.

I like the basic idea here, and honestly I’m a bit surprised this did not exist earlier in a more standardized form. A benchmark for HW-aware NAS is useful, and probably overdue. In that sense the paper is addressing a real pain point. I’ve reviewed a lot of NAS papers over the years, and the hardware side is often the least reproducible part by far. People use different latency proxies, different compilers, different measurement setups, and then compare search algorithms as if those details don’t matter. So the motivation in the abstract, and later around p.2–3, definitely resonates with me.

That said, after reading this twice, I think the paper is more valuable as an infrastructure/data release than as a research contribution with deep technical novelty. That is not necessarily bad, but the paper sometimes oversells the “first public dataset” angle without fully convincing me that the benchmark design choices won’t quickly become limiting. In particular, restricting to NAS-Bench-201 and FBNet is understandable, but also quite narrow given where the field is going. I found myself wondering how future-proof this is. If the benchmark only really supports a couple of old-ish search spaces, then the impact may be mostly historical.

The measurement/estimation setup is the core of the paper, so I wanted more detail there. On the pages describing the six hardware platforms (I think around p.4–5), some details are present, but not enough for me to judge fairness and noise. For measured latency/energy, what exactly is the variance across runs? Are the numbers batch-size dependent? Are data transfer and warm-up included? For the FPGA/ASIC side, the paper seems to mix measured and estimated quantities; I might be wrong, but this makes cross-device comparison less clean than the presentation suggests. Figure 3 and Table 2 are interesting, but I wanted error bars or at least some discussion of uncertainty. Hardware numbers are messy in practice.

I did appreciate the analysis sections. The cross-device ranking inconsistency is important and, in my experience, exactly the kind of thing NAS papers tend to ignore. But some of the “insights” felt a bit obvious once you’ve spent time with deployment papers. Also, the user-case/demo part near the end reads somewhat like a workshop paper tutorial.

Overall: useful benchmark, likely impactful for practitioners, but somewhat limited novelty and not fully rigorous in how the hardware data is characterized. I’m positive on release value, less positive on the paper as a top-tier research contribution.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea—modeling a population of adversarial examples with a Gaussian and then optimizing the mean in a black-box setting—is actually pretty clean. On first read I thought “okay, this is just NES with a slightly different story,” but after reading the method section again (around Eq. (5)–(8), if I’m tracking correctly), I do think the distributional view is more than cosmetic. It gives a principled way to smooth the search space, and that probably is why the attack seems to work well against gradient-masked defenses.

The empirical results are undeniably strong. Table 1 / Table 2 (depending on the version; the defense comparison table near the end) is the main selling point: 100% success on six defenses and >90% on four more is hard to ignore. Figure 2 was also useful for intuition, though I wish the visual examples were larger; it’s hard to judge “imperceptibility” from tiny crops. In general, the paper’s message is clear: many published defenses are not robust, and black-box attacks can be much stronger than people assume. I agree with that message.

That said, I have several concerns. First, the novelty is a little borderline. In my experience, whenever I see Gaussian search distributions and score-function / evolution-strategy style updates in black-box optimization, I immediately ask what is genuinely new beyond the application and tuning. The paper gestures at “adversarial population” as motivation, but I’m not entirely sure the experiments really validate that population model, as opposed to simply showing that randomized gradient-free optimization works well. A small ablation on the variance parameter, or comparison to other search distributions, would have helped a lot. I kept looking for that and didn’t really find it.

Second, the query budget discussion felt too light. On p. 6-ish, where the attack results are reported, success rate is emphasized much more than number of queries. For black-box attacks, that matters. A lot. Some defenses can be broken eventually by almost anything if the budget is large enough. I might be wrong, but I don’t think the paper is fully transparent here.

Also, several of the defended models are exactly the sort that later turned out to rely heavily on obfuscated gradients. So while the results are strong, the “universal” claim in the title feels a bit overcooked. Universal across a set of weak defenses is not the same thing.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with practical importance, but I’m less convinced by the conceptual novelty than the authors seem to be. I’d lean weak accept if the bar is empirical impact, and weak reject if the bar is methodological originality.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than most of the recent brain-encoding submissions I’ve seen. The core idea — replacing the usual subject-specific encoder with a voxel-centric model that uses learned voxel embeddings and cross-attention into image features — is pretty appealing. Figure 1 sells the intuition well, and I think the framing around a “crowd of brains” is memorable without being too gimmicky. In my experience, this area badly needs methods that can pool data across subjects without collapsing into trivial alignment tricks, so I was predisposed to like this.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what exactly is universal here versus merely jointly trained. The method section around the voxel embeddings and cross-attention (Eq. 2/3, around p. 4) is a bit slippery. Are voxel embeddings completely free parameters per voxel, or is there any structural constraint tying them to anatomy, coordinates, ROI identity, scanner, etc.? If they are entirely free, then the model may just be learning a giant lookup table with a powerful visual backbone attached. That can still work! But then the “functional role naturally emerges” claim in the abstract and later discussion feels too strong. I wanted a much more serious analysis of whether embeddings align across subjects or datasets in any meaningful sense.

The empirical results are promising but not fully convincing. Table 1 and Table 2 show improvements, but the baselines feel a little underspecified, and I’m not entirely sure the comparison is fair in terms of parameter count and pretraining. The transfer results are probably the strongest part of the paper, especially the few-shot setup (I think this is in Sec. 4.3, around p. 6-7), but even there I wanted variance bars or at least clearer reporting across random seeds. fMRI results can swing a lot. Also, mixing 3T and 7T is interesting, but I kept asking myself whether scanner-specific nuisance factors are being absorbed into voxel IDs rather than solved in any principled way.

I also thought the interpretability claims were oversold. The visualizations in the later figures (Fig. 5 or 6 — I may be misremembering) are suggestive, not definitive. They look like post hoc attention stories, and I’m generally skeptical of attention-as-explanation.

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The paper has a good chance of influencing follow-up work, and I’d rather see this at the conference than another incremental encoding benchmark. My recommendation is weak accept / borderline accept, but I’d urge the authors to tone down the universality and neuroscience interpretation claims, and be much sharper about what is actually shared across brains.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly more ambitious than most synthetic-data papers I’ve seen lately. The central idea — replacing seed tasks / seed examples with a hand-curated or semi-automatic taxonomy and then generating “from scratch” via syllabus/class-session/key-concept expansion — is appealing. In spirit it feels like taking the Self-Instruct line of work and trying to remove the hidden dependence on existing benchmark/task distributions. I like that goal a lot.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the “almost from scratch” claim. The taxonomy itself is doing a huge amount of work here, and from the abstract (and I assume the main method section around the taxonomy construction), it sounds semi-automatically built with LLM assistance. So the method does not really eliminate prior structure; it just moves the inductive bias into a curriculum graph. That may still be a very good idea! But the framing felt a bit too strong to me.

What I most wanted, and only partially got, was a clearer accounting of where the gains come from. Is it the taxonomy granularity? The syllabus generation stage? The subject coverage? Or just scale? If Table 2/3 are the main empirical results, then I think the paper needs better ablations isolating each stage of the pipeline. Right now the overall recipe seems to work, but the causal story is fuzzy. In my experience, these synthetic pipelines are quite sensitive to prompting details, filtering, and deduplication, and I did not see enough detail to make this fully reproducible. I may have missed it, but I was also looking for contamination controls, especially on academic exams and coding.

I did like the breadth of evaluation. The claim that performance improves across math, code, logical reasoning, and generic instruction following without task-specific data is notable if the setup is clean. Figure 1 (assuming this is the taxonomy/syllabus overview) is intuitive, though a bit high-level. I wanted one concrete walkthrough example from discipline → subject → syllabus session → final instruction/response pair. That would make the method feel less hand-wavy.

One other concern: quality versus coverage. Broad coverage is great, but synthetic data often gets bland, repetitive, or subtly wrong. The paper should discuss failure modes more candidly. Some generated “knowledge” in niche disciplines is probably dubious. That matters.

Overall, I think this is a promising paper with a genuinely novel angle, but the presentation oversells the “scratch” aspect and underspecifies several implementation/evaluation details that are essential for confidence. I’m leaning positive, but not by a lot.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “memorization in foundation models” submissions. The core question—what memorization even means in CLIP-style multimodal contrastive training—is nontrivial, and I appreciated that the authors did not just port over a supervised definition and call it a day. The CLIPMem formulation around Eq. (3)–(5) is, I think, the main contribution here. After reading that section twice, I think I understand what they are trying to isolate, though I’m still not fully convinced the definition separates memorization from ordinary hard-example fitting as cleanly as the paper claims on p. 4. In particular, the distinction between “mis-captioned” examples and merely semantically rare examples felt blurry.

The empirical section is generally solid. Figure 2 and Table 1 are the most convincing parts for me: the result that CLIP sits somewhere between supervised and self-supervised behavior matches intuition, but it’s still useful to see it quantified. The observation that the text encoder appears to drive memorization more than the image encoder (Fig. 4, if I’m reading correctly) is also plausible and somewhat novel. In my experience with web-scale image-text data, the text side is almost always where the weird artifacts and shortcuts live, so this resonates.

That said, I had several frustrations. First, the mitigation story is a bit too neat. The claim in the abstract and later on p. 7–8 that one can reduce memorization and improve utility is potentially important, but the gains in Table 3 look modest, and I’m not sure they survive broader evaluation. Are these improvements consistent across seeds? Across backbones? The paper feels underpowered there. Also, I wanted a stronger discussion of whether the proposed interventions are really reducing memorization, versus just denoising captions and thereby helping optimization. Those are related, but not identical.

I was also confused by some presentation details. The setup around line 186–205 is dense, and the notation gets overloaded. A few definitions are introduced in a way that assumes the reader is already on board. Figure 3 could be clearer too; I stared at it longer than I should have.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper asks a real question, proposes a reasonably principled metric, and provides evidence that is more than anecdotal. But I do think some claims are a bit oversold, especially around mitigation. Borderline accept for me.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than flashy. The main contribution is pretty clear: a multilingual code retrieval benchmark without requiring paired non-English query/code data, plus a training recipe that composes English↔code supervision with NL↔English translation data. In spirit this is simple, but simple is not bad. In fact, I think this is exactly the kind of thing the community should be testing more systematically.

What I liked most is the dataset/benchmark side. The M²CRB benchmark (described around pp. 3–4, if I’m reading the pagination correctly) fills a real gap. Most code retrieval papers still quietly assume English, which is pretty unrealistic. The coverage across Spanish, Portuguese, German, and French, and across Python/Java/JavaScript, is a sensible starting point. Table 1 and the dataset statistics were helpful, although I wish they were a bit more explicit about per-language quality differences. The human verification step is mentioned, but somewhat briefly; after reading this twice I still don’t have a great sense of annotation guidelines or inter-annotator agreement. “Human verification” can mean many things.

The core hypothesis is plausible and the experiments mostly support it. I appreciated that the paper does not oversell this as some grand new theory. It’s basically compositional alignment. That said, I was left wondering how much of the gain is really “cross-lingual transfer for code retrieval” versus just better multilingual text representations plus a strong English-code retriever. Figure 2 (the training setup) helps, but the causal story remains a little muddy. I might be wrong, but I think stronger ablations are needed here: for example, what happens if the English↔other-language data is domain-mismatched or low quality? And how sensitive is the method to the amount of English-code data? I expected a scaling plot and didn’t see one.

Some parts are also underexplained. The automated GitHub filtering pipeline is important, but on p. 4 it felt a bit hand-wavy. Filtering mined code/text pairs is notoriously brittle; in my experience this is where many benchmarks quietly fall apart. I would have liked concrete examples of rejected/accepted pairs, maybe in an appendix, and more discussion of leakage or near-duplicates. Table 3 shows good results, but I’m not fully convinced the benchmark is hard in the right way.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical value, even if the technical novelty is moderate. My leaning is positive, but not enthusiastic. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to improve the dataset documentation and tighten the ablations, because right now the benchmark contribution is stronger than the scientific claim.

I found this paper interesting but also pretty frustrating to read. The biological question is important, and I do think there is real potential in using unpaired translation models to compare pre/post learning neural population activity. In that sense the paper is aiming at something worthwhile. But after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the method is either well justified for this setting or evaluated rigorously enough to support the claims.

The core idea—CycleGAN-style mapping between pre-learning and post-learning activity distributions—is introduced around Sec. 3, with the cycle-consistency and adversarial losses in Eqs. (1)–(4). Fine. But the paper slides too quickly from “we can map distributions” to “this reveals mechanisms of learning.” That is a huge leap. Cycle-consistent GANs are notoriously underconstrained; they can produce plausible-looking translations without preserving the semantics one actually cares about. In this application that matters a lot. I kept looking for a stronger argument that the learned mapping corresponds to neuronally meaningful change rather than just matching marginal statistics. The synthetic experiment (I think this is around Fig. 2 / Table 1) is a start, but it felt toyish and too aligned with the model assumptions. I might be wrong, but I don’t think it establishes identifiability in any useful sense.

I was also confused by the “pre-sorting” procedure for neurons, discussed on p. 5-ish. The paper claims this lets convolutional networks exploit spatial structure, but the ordering seems partly engineered. If so, how much of the performance gain comes from the GAN versus this hand-crafted representation? An ablation is really needed here. Right now Table 2 reports improvements, but it’s not cleanly disentangled.

The interpretability section was, frankly, weak. The use of saliency/visual explanation methods on calcium traces (Fig. 4?) is trendy but not especially convincing. In my experience, these explanations are fragile even in standard vision models, and here the paper treats them as biological insight with very little skepticism. That made me uneasy.

There are also some presentation issues. The writing is awkward in several places, and I had trouble following the exact train/test split across animals/days (lines around the experimental setup need to be much clearer). Were mappings learned per animal, pooled, or cross-animal? This really matters. Also, evaluation on both fluorescence and inferred spikes is sensible, but inferred spikes are themselves model-dependent, so I would not oversell that as independent validation.

Overall, I like the ambition, but the current paper feels more like a promising exploratory workshop paper than a solid conference paper. The method may work empirically, but the evidence here is not yet strong enough for the biological or methodological claims.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more ambitious than most submissions in this area. The main claim — that comparison oracles can essentially match zeroth-order function-value complexity in dimension dependence — is clean and potentially important. I’ve seen a lot of derivative-free / bandit optimization papers over the years, and this one is trying to unify several regimes in a fairly elegant way.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced the presentation does justice to the results. The technical core seems to be around converting pairwise comparisons into useful directional information, but the way this is motivated in the early part of the paper felt too compressed. In particular, the transition from the oracle model to the gradient-estimation-style arguments around Eq. (3)–(5) (I may be slightly off on numbering) is not very intuitive. I think the authors know the area too well and skip steps that non-experts will need.

I did like the convex results. The two different query bounds, roughly \~O(n/epsilon) and \~O(n^2), are interesting, and Table 1 is helpful in situating them. But I also found Table 1 a little too “best-case marketing.” It emphasizes matching in n dependence, which is true and worth saying, but hides a lot in the polylogs and smoothness/regularity assumptions. The slogan in the title is catchy, maybe too catchy. “All you need” is doing a lot of work here. In practice, constants and oracle noise matter enormously for comparisons, especially if the intended motivation includes human preference data.

The nonconvex part is probably the strongest from a novelty standpoint. The \~O(n/epsilon^2) rate for first-order stationarity is nice, and the saddle-escaping result is also nontrivial. Still, I was confused by the second-order guarantees around the theorem in the later pages (I think p. 9–10). Are the Hessian-Lipschitz assumptions exactly the standard ones, or slightly stronger because of the comparison oracle simulation step? I might be wrong, but it felt like the paper was borrowing intuitions from function-value access and not always making explicit what is lost in the reduction.

The experiments, if I’m being blunt, are not doing much for me. For a paper with such a broad claim, the empirical section felt like an afterthought. A simple synthetic validation is fine, but I wanted at least one setting where comparisons are genuinely natural and values are unavailable/noisy.

Overall, I think this is a solid theory paper with meaningful results, but the exposition and empirical support need work. I lean positive because the core contribution seems real.

I found this paper interesting, and I also found parts of it a bit slippery. The core idea — adding a time-reversal consistency regularizer to a latent GraphODE for multi-agent dynamics — is intuitive and, frankly, more broadly applicable than the now-standard “hard physics prior” papers that overcommit to exact conservation laws. I liked the motivation in the intro, especially the point that Hamiltonian-style constraints are often too rigid for damped systems. That part rang true to me.

The method itself seems fairly simple: enforce agreement between forward and backward rollouts in latent space (Eq. 6/7, if I followed correctly), and this is probably the paper’s main selling point. I mean that in a good way. In my experience, simple regularizers with the right inductive bias often beat more elaborate mechanistic constructions. Figure 2 helped, and Table 1/Table 2 suggest the gains are real across several systems, not just one cherry-picked benchmark. The triple-pendulum result is particularly nice because those systems are nasty. An 11.5% MSE gain is not trivial.

That said, I’m not entirely convinced by the way the paper talks about “time-reversal symmetry” for non-conservative and even irreversible systems. This is where I started raising my eyebrows. On p.3 the wording seems stronger than what the actual regularizer justifies. A consistency penalty between forward/backward predictions is not the same thing as the underlying dynamics being time-reversal symmetric in the physics sense. Later, in the theory section around Eq. 9–12, the argument that the regularizer suppresses higher-order Taylor terms is plausible, but it felt a bit hand-wavy to me. I might be wrong, but I wanted a cleaner statement of assumptions. What exactly is being minimized, under which solver, and how does this survive discretization error? Right now it reads a little like post-hoc justification.

I also found the irreversible-system claim surprisingly bold. If the method helps there, great, but then the framing should maybe shift from “imposing symmetry” to “using bidirectional consistency as a stabilizer.” Those are not the same. Figure 4 seems to support robustness, but I wanted a more direct ablation on noise and solver step size. Also, after reading twice, I still wasn’t fully sure whether the backward model shares all parameters with the forward one, or whether there is any asymmetry hidden in implementation details.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a good empirical story and a neat idea, but the theoretical framing overshoots a bit. I lean positive because the method is useful and the experiments are reasonably convincing, though I’d want the claims toned down and some of the analysis clarified before final acceptance.

I found the paper interesting, and in some sense quite elegant, but I’m not fully convinced by the empirical case as written. The core idea—building the Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition directly into a GP prior for vector fields—is nice, and honestly this is the sort of thing I tend to like: it bakes in structure without going all the way to a rigid mechanistic model. The connection to interpretability is also more substantive than in many papers that throw that word around too casually. On p. 2–3, where the authors motivate the curl-free / divergence-free split, I think the framing is clear and scientifically meaningful.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I still found parts of the construction harder to parse than they should be. In particular, the jump around Eq. (4)–(6) is a bit abrupt. I think the authors are defining separate GP priors over scalar/vector potentials and then inducing the field components through differential operators, but the notation becomes dense and the assumptions needed for identifiability are not discussed carefully enough. I might be wrong, but it seems like there is some gauge freedom or non-uniqueness lurking here, especially in bounded domains; if that is handled, it should be stated explicitly. Figure 1 helps conceptually, but the derivation itself could be much cleaner.

The experimental section is solid but not fully persuasive. Table 1 shows improvements, yes, but the baselines feel somewhat underpowered relative to the claims. If the point is that symmetry-aware inductive bias helps, then comparisons to more modern neural ODE / physics-informed baselines would strengthen the case. As it stands, beating generic GP models and a few standard methods is encouraging but not enough for me to conclude broad practical superiority. Also, the energy evolution results (I think this is in Fig. 3 / Table 2) are appealing, but I wanted more detail on how sensitive these estimates are to kernel choice and hyperparameter learning. In my experience with structured GPs, these things can be pretty brittle.

I did appreciate that the paper goes beyond raw prediction and actually inspects the learned components. That’s a real plus. But some claims are a bit too sweeping. “Interpretability” here is partial, not automatic; a decomposition into physically suggestive pieces is not the same as recovering true mechanisms.

Overall, I lean positive on novelty and taste, but I’m somewhere in the middle on acceptance. The idea is good. The presentation and validation need tightening.

I had to read this twice, and I’m still not fully convinced by the paper’s main claim. The topic is interesting — negative curvature detection is indeed a meaningful subroutine in non-convex optimization, and I appreciate that the paper is trying to isolate that primitive rather than oversell a full end-to-end quantum optimizer. That said, the presentation feels slippery in exactly the places where it needs to be most careful.

The main theorem around the query complexity (I think this is the result stated around p. 3 and then formalized later near Eq. (8) or so) gives an O(polylog(d) ε^{-1}) complexity for preparing a state aligned with the negative curvature direction. Fine, but the comparison to classical methods in the abstract already raised a red flag for me. The paper repeatedly contrasts state preparation/query complexity with classical runtime, and then calls this an “exponential speedup.” In my experience reviewing quantum optimization papers, this is almost always where the story gets inflated. If the output is a quantum state, that is not the same object as a classical vector direction used by an optimizer. The readout section is therefore absolutely central, but I found it much less convincing than the state-preparation part.

More specifically, the classical read-out claim (I believe Table 1 and the discussion on p. 6–7) seems to hide fairly strong assumptions about access models and output precision. I might be wrong, but it looks like the algorithm assumes a rather favorable oracle for Hessian-vector or block-encoding access. If so, the “existing counterparts” being compared against are not really on equal footing. Also, if one needs to recover a d-dimensional direction classically, then some dependence on d should reappear in a serious way unless the task is weakened. The paper gestures at this, but I didn’t find the resolution satisfactory.

I also found the exposition rough. Figure 1 is helpful at a high level, but several proof steps are too compressed. The transition around the negative-eigenvalue filtering argument (near Eq. (12)–(15), if I tracked it correctly) is especially hard to parse. There are also places where ε seems to play multiple roles: eigenvalue threshold, optimization accuracy, and readout precision. That should be disentangled much more clearly.

Overall, I think there may be a technically solid kernel here, but the current version oversells the algorithmic implications and understates the output-model issue. Right now I lean skeptical. If this were positioned more modestly as a quantum subroutine under strong access assumptions, I’d be more positive.

I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The basic intuition is sensible, and honestly I appreciate that the authors are trying to make “video jigsaw” actually workable rather than just proposing an impossible pretext task and hoping for the best. The motivation on p.2 is clear enough: full 3D jigsaw is combinatorially nasty, so constrain the shuffling and replace exact solving with surrogate objectives. In spirit, that’s a good idea.

That said, I found the paper harder to pin down than it should be. After reading Sec. 3 twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure why the particular constrained cuboid construction is the right one, as opposed to many other ways of injecting continuity cues. Eq. (3) and the discussion around the four surrogate tasks make the method sound a bit engineered. Maybe that is unavoidable, but I wanted a cleaner conceptual story. Figure 2 helps, although I think the visualization is doing more work than the text here. Also, I might be wrong, but the distinction between “local continuity” and “global continuity” felt somewhat asserted rather than really demonstrated.

The empirical section is pretty solid overall. The downstream results in Table 1 and Table 2 are competitive, and I do believe the method is better than a lot of older pretext-task-style SSL for video. But “state-of-the-art” is a slippery claim here. The comparisons seem selective. On p.6–7, I was looking for stronger baselines from more recent contrastive / predictive video SSL papers, or at least a clearer explanation of why they are omitted. In 2024/2025, beating older permutation-based methods is not enough. This matters because the paper’s contribution is mostly methodological, not theoretical.

I did like the ablations in Table 3; they suggest the constraints actually matter, which is important. Still, some details are missing. For example, how sensitive is performance to the exact cuboid size / shuffle budget? The appendix may cover this, but in the main paper I wanted more than one narrow setting. And I found it a bit suspicious that the surrogate tasks all help in a nicely complementary way—possible, yes, but I would like variance over runs.

Overall, I think this is a decent paper with a real idea, but not a slam dunk. My main hesitation is that the method feels somewhat handcrafted and the empirical evidence, while promising, does not fully convince me that this is the right pretext task rather than just one workable one. Weak accept / borderline for me.

I had to read this twice, partly because the problem setup is interesting and partly because the presentation is pretty dense. The paper tackles meta inverse constrained RL, i.e., learning both rewards and constraints from few demos by leveraging related tasks. That is a worthwhile problem. In my experience, the combination of IRL + constraints + meta-learning is underexplored, so at a high level I liked the ambition here.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the current version. The main theoretical claim is the bilevel formulation with convergence to an \epsilon-stationary point in O(1/\epsilon^2), and a generalization bound to new tasks. On paper this sounds strong, but I found the assumptions doing a lot of work. Around the theorem statements (I think pp. 5–7), the smoothness / bounded gradient / Lipschitz-type assumptions on both upper and lower objectives seem fairly standard for bilevel optimization, but I’m not sure they map cleanly to the actual inverse constrained RL setting. In particular, once the lower level is itself tied to constraint inference from demonstrations, the practical meaning of these assumptions gets fuzzy. I might be wrong, but the theory felt somewhat “ported in” from generic bilevel optimization rather than emerging naturally from the MICRL model.

I was also confused by the modeling choice that the upper level learns the reward meta-prior and the lower level learns the constraint meta-prior. Why this asymmetry? Why not joint meta-learning, or the reverse? The motivation around Eq. (3)–(5) was not persuasive to me. Figure 1 gives the pipeline, but it does not really explain why this decomposition is the right one. This is important because the whole algorithm depends on it.

The experimental section is okay but not especially convincing. Table 1 and Table 2 show improvements in few-demo adaptation, but the baselines feel limited. I would have expected stronger comparisons to meta-IRL variants and also ablations removing either the reward prior or constraint prior. Without that, it’s hard to tell where the gains come from. Also, the environments look fairly small-scale; for a paper making theory-plus-generalization claims, I wanted at least one more realistic setting. The discussion around Fig. 3 is a bit hand-wavy.

So overall: interesting topic, some technical effort clearly went in, but I found the paper overclaims relative to what is actually demonstrated. If the authors tightened the motivation, clarified the assumptions, and significantly improved the empirical validation, I’d be much more positive. Right now I’m leaning borderline reject.

I found the core idea interesting, and honestly a bit unusual compared to the now-standard detect/reject pipelines. The paper’s main claim — that one can locally reshape the decision geometry so gradient-based attacks get funneled into “trapdoors” and then flagged via a Trapclass — is novel enough that I paid attention. Figure 1 helps, and I think the intuition on p.2–3 is reasonably clear. Still, after reading the method section twice, I’m not fully convinced the paper has nailed down what is being guaranteed versus what is just observed empirically.

A lot seems to hinge on the statement in the abstract (and again around p.4, near Eq. (3)–(5), if I understood correctly) that low-budget attacks “necessarily” end up in trapdoors. That is a very strong word. I did not see a formal argument establishing necessity except under what appear to be fairly idealized assumptions about local manifold structure and attack optimization. Maybe I missed it, but the theoretical part felt more like geometric motivation than theorem-level support. In my experience with adversarial defense papers, this distinction matters a lot, because the gap between “gradient attacks tend to go here” and “adaptive attacks are forced to go here” is where many defenses collapse.

The empirical section is promising but also a bit too favorable in a way that made me uneasy. Table 2 and Table 3 report very high detection rates across six attacks, but I wanted a much stronger adaptive evaluation. Are the attacks optimized jointly against the classifier and the detector/trapdoor criterion, or are they mostly standard attacks run on the defended model? The text around p.6 suggests some adaptation, but not enough detail. I’m not entirely sure how BPDA/EOT-style considerations apply here, especially if the detector uses neighborhood structure or absence of trapdoors. A truly adaptive attacker would likely target “non-trapclass but still near-manifold” regions if such regions exist. The paper says these are hard to find; maybe. But “hard” is not the same as demonstrated impossible.

I also found the OOD argument a little hand-wavy. The claim that high-budget attacks escaping trapdoors end up far from the data manifold, and can then be detected by absence of nearby trapdoors, sounds nice, but I wanted quantitative evidence beyond aggregate AUROC-style summaries. Some visualization in representation space (maybe like Fig. 4, but more systematic) would help.

That said, I do think this is more creative than the average defense paper, and the clean accuracy tradeoff seems surprisingly mild. If the authors can strengthen the adaptive attack section and tighten the theoretical claims, I’d be much more positive. Right now I’m interested but skeptical. My score would be around weak reject / borderline.

I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea — treating HOI denoising through a diffusion-style framework with a contact-centric representation — is actually pretty sensible, and in my experience this is exactly the sort of problem where plain regression models fall apart once the test-time corruption shifts even a little. I like the motivation in the abstract, and the claims about generalization are important. That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as novel or as cleanly formulated as it wants to be.

The representation seems to be doing a lot of the heavy lifting. The GeneOH parameterization (I think this is introduced around Sec. 3, with the formal definition near Eq. (3) or (4)?) is probably the most valuable part of the paper. The contact-centric view makes intuitive sense for HOI, and the qualitative examples in the figures (I’m referring to the reconstruction/denoising visuals, I think Fig. 2 and Fig. 4) do suggest that the model preserves object-relative plausibility better than baselines. But I found the exposition vague in places. What exactly is retained and what is discarded by this representation? On page 5, the wording around “informatively parameterizes the HOI process” felt more like sales language than technical clarification.

I also have some skepticism about the “domain-generalizable denoising scheme.” The whitening/noise-space alignment idea is reasonable, but it sounds suspiciously close to saying: we train on one corruption family, then diffuse arbitrary corruptions until they look like the training noise. Maybe that works empirically, fine, but I wanted a sharper argument for why this should preserve the recoverable signal rather than wash it out. Eq. (7)–(9) give the mechanics, not really the intuition. I might be missing something.

The experiments are strong overall. Four benchmarks is good, and Table 1/Table 2 seem to show consistent gains. Still, I wanted more ablations. In particular: how much comes from GeneOH versus the diffusion strategy? The paper gestures at this, but not enough. Also, are the baselines truly tuned for cross-domain denoising, or are they just generic methods dropped in somewhat unfairly? Hard to tell. The downstream application section is a nice touch, though a bit rushed.

So my overall take is positive but not enthusiastic. There is a real idea here, and the problem matters. But the paper needs a clearer technical story and a more convincing unpacking of where the gains actually come from. Right now it feels like a strong empirical paper wrapped around a method section that is trying a little too hard to sound deeper than it is.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because “scaling laws for graphs” gets claimed a lot more often than it is actually demonstrated, and after reading the paper twice I still think the title/abstract overstate what is shown. This is not really a scaling-law paper in the sense people use for language or vision; it is a pretrain-on-more-graphs-and-use-the model-as-an-augmentor paper. That may still be interesting, but I think the framing should be toned down.

That said, I do think the core idea is reasonable. Using a discrete diffusion model as a universal graph structure augmentor, then guiding generation for downstream tasks, is a nice angle. Figure 1 helps, and the plug-and-play story is appealing. In my experience, graph pretraining papers often collapse once you move off a narrow benchmark family, so I appreciate the attempt to cover multiple domains. The empirical section has breadth. Table 1 and Table 2 suggest fairly consistent gains, which is better than the usual cherry-picked “wins on 3/10 datasets” situation.

Still, I found several things confusing. The paper keeps saying “cross-domain scaling,” but I could not find a clean characterization of what domains are included and how heterogeneous they really are. On page 4, around the paragraph introducing the pretraining corpus, the description is too vague. Are these molecular graphs, citation graphs, knowledge graphs, TU-style graph classification datasets, all mixed together? If so, what is the node/edge vocabulary alignment? This matters a lot. Equation (3) also goes by too quickly: the guided generation objective seems central, but the conditioning signal and its interaction with the pretrained diffusion prior are not explained carefully enough. I might be wrong, but I suspect a lot of the benefit comes from fairly standard task-aware augmentation rather than anything uniquely “scaled.”

I also wanted stronger ablations. The paper needs a plot of performance versus number of pretraining graphs, not just final comparisons. Otherwise the main claim is under-supported. If that is in Figure 3, it is too limited; I expected something much more systematic. Likewise, comparisons against simpler augmentors are not strong enough. A well-tuned edge perturbation or motif-based augmentation baseline can be surprisingly hard to beat.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, but the paper is trying to sell a bigger story than the evidence currently supports. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the setup and provide more convincing scaling analysis.

I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The core idea — a shifting buffer / “phonological loop” that is reused for attention, output generation, and state update — is interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing compared to the usual ever-more-complicated seq2seq TTS architectures. I appreciate the attempt to simplify. Figure 1 helped, although I had to go back to it after reading Section 2 because the role of the buffer is not immediately intuitive from the text alone. After reading the model description twice, I think I understand the mechanism, but I’m still not fully convinced the paper explains it cleanly.

The strongest part for me is the voice fitting angle. The claim that a speaker embedding can be fitted from a few unconstrained samples, without aligned phonemes or linguistic features, is pretty compelling if it really works as advertised. In my experience reviewing TTS/voice conversion papers, this is exactly where many methods quietly rely on curation or supervision they downplay. Here the setup seems more realistic. I also liked the priming idea for controlling variability; it’s simple and kind of elegant.

That said, the empirical section felt weaker than it needed to be. Table 2 and the listening results are suggestive, but not decisive. A paper making “in the wild” claims needs stronger evidence about robustness: noisy samples, channel mismatch, duration of adaptation audio, outright failure cases. I kept looking for this and didn’t really find it. The examples around the fitting procedure (I think discussed around p. 5–6) are promising, but they read more like demos than a careful evaluation. Also, the comparisons are a bit slippery. I might be wrong, but some baselines seem either older or not tuned for the same adaptation setting, which makes the simplicity argument look better than it may actually be.

I was also confused by the training objective around Eq. (7)/(8) and how much teacher forcing the model needs. The paper emphasizes architectural simplicity, but there are several practical choices that seem important and are not discussed enough. Attention stability, in particular, is something I would expect more ablation on. Figure 2 looks nice, but a few successful alignments do not tell us much.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I like that the authors release code/models. But the paper oversells the breadth of the claims relative to the evidence. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values novelty and practical impact over exhaustive validation.

I found the paper interesting and, at a high level, quite timely. There is a real problem here: privacy policies are unreadable, and interactive assistance is plausibly more useful than static summarization. The user study results in the abstract are also pretty striking. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the empirical story is as solid as the paper wants it to be.

The strongest part for me is the idea of moving from “ask a question about a policy” to a more guided, agent-like interaction. The examples in Fig. 3 (if I understood the setup correctly) make this fairly concrete, and I think the design intuition is good. In my experience reviewing privacy/NLP work, many papers stop at extraction or QA benchmarks and never show what an actual user-facing workflow would look like. This paper at least tries.

Where I struggled is the evaluation methodology. On pp. 6–7, the paper reports large gains on Data Practice Identification / Choice Identification / Summarization / Privacy QA, but I had trouble understanding what exactly the baselines were and whether they were tuned comparably. Some of the comparisons felt a bit unfairly stacked against “traditional models.” If Eq. (2) is the core scoring objective for the agent’s recommendation/ranking step, then I think it needs much more justification; right now it reads like a fairly ad hoc combination of signals. Also, Table 2 reports strong benchmark improvements, but I wanted confidence intervals or at least some significance testing. Maybe I missed it.

The user study is potentially impactful, but also the part I trust least. A 2.6/3 vs. 1.8/3 comprehension score (Table 4?) is a big effect, almost suspiciously big, especially alongside the huge reduction in perceived difficulty. I’m not saying it’s impossible, just that the task design matters enormously. Were users in the control condition artificially handicapped by having to read raw legal text with no tooling at all? If so, the comparison is not very informative. A simpler non-LLM interactive UI would be a much better baseline. I also wanted more detail on recruitment, exclusions, and whether participants were actually making privacy decisions or just answering researcher-authored questions. Those are not the same thing.

Another concern: the paper repeatedly uses language like “expert system” and “more informed consent,” which I think oversells things. LLMs hallucinate. Privacy policies are often ambiguous on purpose. I’m not entirely sure the paper engages seriously enough with failure modes, especially misleading but fluent explanations. The discussion on p. 9 touches this, but briefly.

Overall, I like the direction, and I think there is a real paper here, but in its current form it feels under-controlled and somewhat overclaimed. My leaning is weak reject, though I could imagine being persuaded with clearer baselines and a more careful framing.

I have somewhat mixed feelings about this paper. I like the motivation, and in fact I’m pretty sympathetic to it. In deep RL, a lot of codebases are either too “productionized” to modify easily or too brittle to trust, so a compact framework with clean baselines is genuinely useful. I also appreciated the discussion around different research goals; the taxonomy around reproducibility / compactness / accessibility (around p. 2–3, if I’m reading it right) felt more thoughtful than the usual “here is our software” pitch.

That said, as a conference paper, I’m not fully convinced there is enough here technically. The core contribution is a framework, plus a framing of what kinds of research such a framework supports. That can be valuable, yes, but large parts of the paper read more like a systems/demo paper or an extended software release note than a research contribution. I don’t mean that dismissively — I’ve built on these kinds of libraries myself — but the bar should then be very clear evidence that Dopamine enables something meaningfully different.

The empirical section did not quite get me there. Table 1 and the Atari results are fine, but mostly confirm that the implementations are sane. Figure 1 is helpful for understanding the design philosophy, although I found the text around it a bit self-congratulatory. I also wasn’t sure how to interpret the comparisons to prior frameworks: are these apples-to-apples in terms of tuning, evaluation protocol, preprocessing, etc.? In RL those details matter a lot, and I wanted more specificity. Maybe I missed it, but after reading this twice I still found the exact benchmarking protocol under-described.

I did like the emphasis on small code footprint and readability. The agent descriptions and equations are standard, but that’s probably the point. In my experience, many researchers — especially students — would benefit from a codebase that is opinionated and minimal rather than endlessly configurable. The discussion around “compact and reliable” on p. 4 resonated with me.

Still, I’m not entirely sure the taxonomy section is deep enough. It gestures at heterogeneity in RL research, but doesn’t really develop a rigorous framework for evaluating software against those goals. So I was left with: useful artifact, sensible philosophy, somewhat thin paper.

If this venue is open to infrastructure/resource papers, I can see an argument for acceptance. If the expectation is a stronger novel technical contribution, I would lean borderline to reject.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea — learning a conditional EBM as a synthetic likelihood for SBI and then doing posterior inference with MCMC — is reasonable, and in some sense overdue. There has been a lot of work around NLE/NPE/ratio methods, and EBMs are an obvious thing to try, especially if the authors want to avoid some of the brittleness of flow parameterizations. So at a high level, I buy the motivation.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper fully earns the novelty claims. The abstract says the combination of flexible EBMs with KL minimization is “unique,” which feels overstated. By page 3, around the discussion of prior synthetic likelihood approaches, the positioning is a little selective. I would have liked a more careful comparison to existing unnormalized likelihood learning / classifier-based density ratio estimation papers in SBI and adjacent literatures. Maybe I’m missing something, but the distinction between AUNLE and prior conditional EBM training recipes did not feel as sharp as the authors suggest.

I did like the tilting trick in AUNLE (I think this is introduced around Eq. (7)–(9)?), and Figure 1 helps. But after reading that section twice, I’m still not entirely sure I understand when the resulting MCMC target is actually well-behaved. The paper says this enables “efficient MCMC techniques,” but efficiency depends heavily on geometry and normalization constants; in my experience, this is exactly where EBM papers hand-wave. The discussion on page 5 is too breezy. Likewise SUNLE’s “doubly intractable” construction is clever, but also computationally scary. Table 2 reports simulation efficiency, but I wanted actual wall-clock and sampler-mixing diagnostics, not just simulation counts. These methods can look great if one only counts simulator calls.

Empirically, the results are solid but not spectacular. On the benchmark tasks, the methods seem competitive, and the pyloric network experiment is a good choice. Still, I found Figure 3 and Table 1 a bit cherry-picked in presentation. Where are the failure cases? EBMs are notorious to train, and I simply don’t believe all hyperparameter tuning was painless. The appendix may cover this, but the main paper should be more candid.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, maybe borderline very good, but not a slam dunk. The ideas are promising and the experiments are competent. I just wanted more honesty about the computational tradeoffs and a less aggressive novelty pitch.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than most work in this area. The problem setup is important: offline, partially observed, no simulator, and still trying to say something interpretable about a human decision policy. That combination is genuinely hard, and I appreciate that the authors are not just doing post-hoc saliency and calling it “understanding.” The core idea of jointly modeling belief updates and the belief-to-action mapping is, in my view, the right direction.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails the identifiability story. The claim that the method can disentangle a biased belief-update process from a suboptimal decision rule seems quite strong. Around the model section (I think pp. 4–6, especially the discussion around Eq. 3 and Eq. 5), it feels like there are many degrees of freedom that could explain the same trajectories. Maybe the Bayesian priors regularize this enough in practice, but that is not the same as a principled identifiability argument. I might be missing something, but the paper gestures at interpretability more than it really secures it.

I also found Figure 2 helpful conceptually, but the real-data section is weaker than the framing suggests. The Alzheimer’s diagnosis application is compelling, but Table 1 and the surrounding text do not really establish that the learned components correspond to meaningful clinical behavior rather than just a flexible latent-variable model with nice labels attached. In my experience, this is exactly where these papers overclaim. If you say “auditing” and “quantifying bias,” I want a much more careful validation story. Some expert annotation, some known subgroup effect, some sanity check beyond predictive fit. Otherwise it feels a bit too neat.

The writing is mostly clear, though there are places where notation becomes heavy and the distinction between latent state, belief state, and observed history gets slippery. I had to go back a few times. Also, I’m not entirely sure the comparison baselines are strong enough. If the point is interpretability under partial observability, then comparing mainly to generic black-box imitation models is not sufficient; simpler structured baselines would be informative.

Overall, I think this is a serious paper with a good idea, but the empirical evidence for the central interpretability claims is not yet where it needs to be for a top venue. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the rebuttal can clarify the identifiability assumptions and give a more convincing read of the clinical case study.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more ambitious than most sequential recommendation submissions. The core idea—explicitly conditioning a generative recommender on user preferences expressed in natural language—makes intuitive sense, and I do think the benchmark contribution may end up being more important than the model itself. The “preference steering” and “sentiment following” settings are a nice way to stress-test whether these systems are actually personalized or just good next-item predictors. In my experience, a lot of recent generative recsys papers implicitly claim this kind of controllability without really measuring it.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates “preference discerning” from just adding another textual conditioning signal. After reading Sec. 3 twice, I still wasn’t sure what is fundamentally new in the paradigm beyond using LLM-generated preference summaries from reviews and then training/evaluating on those. Eq. (4) on p. 5 makes the formulation look fairly standard conditional generation. So the novelty seems to hinge on the benchmark/task definition and on Mender’s specific multimodal fusion design, not really on a new learning principle. That’s okay, but the paper oversells this a bit.

I also have some concerns about the benchmark construction. The preferences are generated by LLMs from user reviews (abstract; I assume detailed in Sec. 4 / appendix), which means the supervision is only as good as the summarizer/extractor. I might be wrong, but this creates a circularity risk: the model is judged on alignment to synthetic preference text that may reflect the prompting style or biases of the LLM rather than genuine user intent. I wanted a stronger human validation of these preference annotations. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains, but I don’t know how robust they are to different LLMs/prompts.

Mender itself seems solid. Figure 3 is helpful, though a little busy. The gains in Table 4 are meaningful, especially in the controllability-oriented settings, and I appreciated that baselines are reasonably strong. Still, I would have liked more ablations isolating which part matters most: multimodal item features, preference encoder, or training objective. The paper gestures at this, but not enough for me.

Overall, I lean positive because the problem is timely and the benchmark exposes a real weakness in current methods. But the conceptual framing is somewhat inflated, and the evaluation depends heavily on LLM-derived preference labels. Borderline accept for me.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — learning branching/search heuristics for automated reasoning with RL — is appealing, and honestly overdue. People have been talking about replacing hand-engineered SAT/QBF heuristics with learned policies for years, and most prior attempts either don’t scale or are too toy-ish to matter. This paper at least tries to engage with real backtracking solvers and nontrivial quantified Boolean formulas, which I appreciate.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the empirical story. The claim in the abstract about “up to 10x” speedup sounds impressive, but when I looked at the results tables (I assume Tables 1 and 2; the formatting made this a little hard to parse), it seemed like the gains were concentrated on a subset of “challenging” instances, while on others the learned heuristic was roughly comparable or sometimes worse. That’s not fatal, but the presentation leans a bit too hard on the best-case outcome. In my experience with solver papers, robustness across distributions matters more than a few dramatic wins.

I did like the representation discussion in the middle of the paper — I think around p. 4–5 — where the authors explain how they encode formulas in a way that is permutation-invariant and scalable. That is probably the most valuable technical contribution here. Still, I wanted more precision. Equation (3) seems to define the policy/value objective, but I couldn’t tell how much of the eventual performance comes from the architecture versus the RL setup versus simply expensive offline data generation. Relatedly, the training protocol is underexplained. How many solver runs per benchmark family? How sensitive is this to the reward shaping? I might have missed it, but I didn’t see a convincing ablation.

Figure 2 was also less informative than it should have been. A runtime cactus plot or scatter against the handwritten heuristic would have helped a lot more than aggregate summaries. Right now it’s hard to judge whether the method is broadly better or just occasionally lucky.

One other concern: generalization. The paper gestures at solving large formulas (100k+ variables), but I’m not entirely sure the experiments really establish out-of-distribution scaling, as opposed to interpolation within a narrow benchmark distribution. This is a common problem in learned reasoning papers, and I don’t think this one fully escapes it.

Overall, I think this is promising work, and more serious than many “ML for reasoning” submissions I’ve seen. But the paper oversells the maturity of the approach. I’m leaning weak accept if the bar is novelty plus potential impact, and weak reject if the bar is rigorous empirical validation.

I read this paper with some interest because long-horizon sequence modeling for recommendation is a real problem in practice, and the paper is clearly trying to address the right bottleneck. The core idea — using the target item as a trigger and iteratively scanning the sequence with a memory vector — is intuitive, and I can see why this might work better than a plain target-attention model when the sequence is very long. In particular, the discussion around linear complexity versus quadratic self-attention is reasonable, and the motivation on p.1–2 is mostly well aligned with what people in industry actually care about.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the technical novelty. After reading Sec. 3 twice, the proposed iterative memory update feels like a fairly incremental combination of target attention, memory networks, and repeated refinement. Eq. (4)–(7) define the update, but I found it hard to isolate what is genuinely new beyond “do attention-like aggregation multiple times with a recurrent memory state.” Maybe I’m being overly harsh here, but the paper somewhat oversells the “O(1) signal traveling path” point. Yes, in some abstract graph sense the path is short, but the model still processes the whole sequence repeatedly across iterations, so the practical optimization difficulty is not obviously comparable to standard self-attention. The complexity discussion around p.4 is too clean and, honestly, a bit marketing-like.

The experiments are decent but not airtight. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains, sometimes nontrivial ones, but I wanted more careful controls. If IMN uses multiple iterations, then compute budget and parameter count should be normalized more explicitly against the baselines. I might have missed it, but I did not see a convincing apples-to-apples comparison with stronger efficient-attention or linear-attention style baselines. Given the paper’s framing, that omission matters. Also, Figure 3 is suggestive, but not really diagnostic. An ablation on the number of iterations is there, but I wanted more analysis of failure modes: when does the memory actually help, and when does it just behave like repeated smoothing?

Another issue is clarity. Parts of Sec. 3.2 are written in a compressed way, and the distinction between target-sequence dependency modeling and intra-sequence dependency modeling is asserted more than demonstrated. I also found some wording imprecise. For example, the claim that target attention “cannot model intra-sequence dependencies” is true in the most direct sense, but in recommender architectures downstream nonlinearities often blur that clean separation.

Overall, I think the paper is solid and probably useful, but I’m somewhere in the weak accept / borderline zone rather than enthusiastic accept. The empirical results are promising, and the method is practical. Still, the novelty is modest and some claims are stronger than the evidence supports.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — using gradients w.r.t. parameters as features for transfer / representation learning — is not new in spirit, but the particular way the paper combines gradient and activation information in a linear model is clever. I think the nicest part is actually the derivation around the local linearization of the pretrained network (around Eq. 4–6, if I’m reading the notation correctly). That gives the method at least some conceptual backbone beyond “here is another feature map that works.”

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the presentation is muddled in places. The main algorithmic claim is that training/inference can be done “without computing the actual gradients,” but the exact computational shortcut was not immediately clear to me from Sec. 3.2. I eventually understood the factorization argument, but it took more work than it should have. Figure 2 helps, though I wish it were more explicit about tensor dimensions. On page 5, the jump from the gradient-feature definition to the efficient implementation feels hand-wavy. Maybe this is obvious to people who work on Fisher kernels / tangent kernels all day, but not everyone will.

I also have some concerns about novelty. In my experience, this sits somewhere between gradient embeddings, Fisher vectors, and first-order network linearization. The paper does cite some of this, but I think it undersells how close the connection is. The “theoretical insights” are okay, but not especially deep. I’m not entirely sure the local linear approximation result is saying much more than a first-order Taylor expansion plus a particular parameterization choice. That doesn’t make it useless, just less groundbreaking than the paper suggests.

The experiments are decent overall. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, and I appreciate that multiple backbone architectures are included. Still, I wanted stronger baselines. Where are more modern adaptation methods, or at least a more careful parameter-budget comparison? Some of the gains look modest, and I suspect part of the improvement comes from feature dimensionality / implicit ensembling effects rather than the proposed principle itself.
