Also, the ablation isolating gradient-only vs activation-only vs combined features (I think this is in Fig. 3?) is important, but I wanted more of that and less benchmark breadth.

So: promising paper, but not fully convincing. I like the idea, and I do believe it could be useful in practice. I’m just not sold that the paper is as clean or as novel as it claims. Borderline accept for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values practical representation-learning tricks with some theory, weak reject if the bar is conceptual novelty.

I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The general idea — using a discriminator-like DNN to guide a GA over molecular space — is sensible, and honestly not that surprising in 2025, but there is still some value in showing that this hybrid actually works in chemistry rather than just in toy optimization. I think the paper is at its best when it stays close to that practical claim. The optimization results in Table 1 and the examples in Figure 3 are reasonably convincing at a high level.

That said, I found the paper frustratingly vague in several places. On p. 3, around Eq. (2), I could not fully reconstruct how the discriminator score is incorporated into parent selection versus survival. Is it an additive fitness shaping term, a hard filter, or something more like a learned mutation prior? I read that section twice and still wasn’t entirely sure. This matters, because the claimed diversity improvements could come from a very different mechanism depending on the answer. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough.

The diversity claim itself also needs more care. The paper says the method “improves the diversity of generated molecules,” but on p. 5 the metrics seem limited to fingerprint-based internal diversity and uniqueness. In my experience reviewing molecular generation papers, these metrics are easy to game, and they do not necessarily mean chemically meaningful coverage. I would have liked to see scaffold diversity, nearest-neighbor novelty, or at least some discussion of whether the model is just drifting toward syntactically valid but weird corners of the space. Table 2 is suggestive, not definitive.

I also think the comparison to “other generative models” is not fully fair. Some baselines appear under-tuned, and I could not find enough detail on budget matching. If the GA+DNN gets more oracle calls or effectively more iterations, the comparison is weaker than presented. This should be clarified explicitly, maybe in the experimental section on p. 4 or appendix if I missed it.

The interpretability part is interesting but underdeveloped. The claim on p. 6 that the method helps derive “design principles” feels too strong based on a few highlighted fragments in Figure 4. Maybe I’m being picky, but this reads more like post hoc motif inspection than real interpretability.

Overall, I think there is a decent paper in here, but the current version overclaims and underspecifies the method. I lean borderline negative unless the authors can clean up the methodological description and make the evaluation much more rigorous.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thoughtful than the average “use diversity for decoding/search” submission. The core idea — learning tactic representations from synthetic traces and then using a DPP to filter candidates before proof search — is simple enough that I was initially skeptical, but after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice I think there is something real here. In particular, the setup around predicting not just tactic success but also “semantic effect” on the proof state is appealing, and the results in Table 1 / Table 2 do suggest this is not just cosmetic reranking.

That said, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails down what exactly is being learned in the representation. Eq. (3) and the discussion around p.5–6 are a bit hand-wavy. The authors claim the embedding captures the effect on the proving environment, but the supervision seems indirect and entangled with success/failure and runtime. Maybe that is sufficient in practice, but then I’d like a more careful ablation disentangling these signals. Right now Figure 3 is suggestive, not definitive. I might be wrong here, but I kept wondering whether a much simpler clustering or MMR-style reranker over tactic strings / LM embeddings would recover most of the gain. The DPP is elegant, sure, but also a slightly “conference-paper nice” choice unless the comparison is really exhaustive.

I also think the empirical section is a little too centered on ReProver. The paper says the method is general, but the evidence for generality is weak. One base prover, two benchmark splits, and synthetic data derived from previous attempts of presumably similar distribution. In my experience, these filtering methods can look great in-distribution and then collapse when the tactic generator shifts even a bit. Some discussion of that limitation would help. Table 3 reports improved tactic success rate and execution time, which I believe, but I wanted more detail on total search budget and wall-clock tradeoffs. If the extra model/scoring overhead is nontrivial, that matters.

There are also some presentation issues. I found the notation on p.4 cluttered, and the transition into the DPP objective is abrupt. A few line-level explanations would help; I got lost around the paragraph immediately before Eq. (5). Also, the synthetic data generation pipeline is important but described too quickly.

Overall, I lean positive. The paper has a clear idea, a plausible mechanism, and meaningful gains on a relevant benchmark. But I don’t think the “general augmentation for any prover” claim is really established yet. My score would be in the weak accept range.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than a lot of the recent “privacy in LLMs” work that just repackages memorization benchmarks. The move from training-data leakage to interactive privacy reasoning is the right one, and I appreciated the attempt to ground the benchmark in contextual integrity rather than vague notions of “sensitive information.” In my experience, papers on LLM privacy often end up being either philosophical or purely empirical; this one is at least trying to connect the two.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper has nailed the evaluation setup. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still wasn’t sure whether CONFAIDE is primarily a benchmark for privacy reasoning, instruction following, or theory-of-mind-style social inference. The tiered design is sensible, but by Tier 4 the task seems to require a lot more than privacy norm understanding. If a model fails there, what exactly failed? Privacy reasoning? Pragmatic inference? General compositional reasoning? This matters for the main claim in the abstract that the issue “stem[s] in the models’ lack of reasoning capabilities.” I think that’s plausible, but the evidence as presented feels thinner than the rhetoric.

I also wanted more detail on annotation and normative validity. On p. 5 (around the benchmark construction discussion), the paper implies there is a “human would/would not disclose” standard, but privacy norms are famously context- and culture-dependent. Were annotators given disagreements? Inter-annotator agreement? I might have missed it, but I didn’t see enough here. Without that, numbers like GPT-4 violating privacy 39% of the time feel sharper than they really are.

Some of the result presentation is also a bit frustrating. The aggregate tables are useful, but I really wanted more breakdown by failure mode. Table 2/Table 3 (if I’m reading correctly) suggest substantial variation across tiers, yet the discussion keeps collapsing everything into one story. Figure 3 was helpful, though I wish there were more qualitative examples of borderline cases, not just obvious violations.

One other concern: the paper sometimes anthropomorphizes the models a little too much. “Can LLMs keep a secret?” is a catchy title, sure, but secrecy here is really about output behavior under prompts, not stable internalized norms. Small point maybe, but it leaks into the claims.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper with a timely benchmark idea, but I’d want a stronger treatment of annotation reliability, construct validity, and what exactly is being measured before I’d be fully enthusiastic. Lean accept / borderline for me.

I found this paper interesting but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — representing many vision problems as a kind of image completion conditioned on an example input-output pair — is genuinely appealing, and honestly it is the sort of unification people have been gesturing at for a while without really making concrete. Here, the authors actually build it on top of a pretrained text-to-image diffusion model and push it across a fairly wide range of tasks. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the “universal framework” claim is earned.

The formulation around the general image completion task (Sec. 3, especially around Eq. (2) and the discussion on p. 4) is elegant at a high level, but some important details are oddly vague. I had trouble understanding exactly how the paired example is encoded relative to the query image, and whether the model is really learning task abstraction versus simply pattern-matching from the exemplar. Figure 2 helps, but not enough. In my experience, these in-context visual conditioning setups can look much more general than they really are, and I think the paper sidesteps that concern a bit too quickly.

The empirical section is broad, which I appreciate. Ten tasks is not nothing. Table 1 and Table 2 make the method look strong overall, and I do think the breadth is the paper’s main selling point. But the comparisons are sometimes apples-to-oranges. On some tasks, the baselines are specialized models; on others, they are generic generative systems; and the training/data regimes do not always seem aligned. I might be wrong, but the gains on low-level tasks looked less impressive than the prose suggests. Also, for segmentation and dense prediction, I wanted more than visual examples in Figure 4/5 — where are the failure cases? Universal methods usually fail in universal ways, and I’d like to see that.

Another concern is cost. The method inherits a large diffusion backbone, and I did not see a sufficiently candid discussion of compute, latency, or sample efficiency (maybe buried in appendix?). If this is meant as a serious alternative to task-specific systems, this matters a lot.

Still, I think there is a real idea here. The paper is ambitious, and I’d much rather read this than another incremental segmentation paper. My bottom line: promising and somewhat novel, but the presentation overreaches. I would lean weak accept if the conference values boldness, weak reject if the bar is “fully nailed down.”

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit overdue. The cascading bandit literature has indeed stayed in a rather narrow comfort zone, so extending this to a proper RL setting with state evolution makes sense. The problem formulation on p. 2–3 is clear enough, and I appreciated that the authors did not just say “combinatorial action space is hard” and move on, but actually built the whole paper around that bottleneck.

The main technical contribution seems to be the structural result behind BestPerm (Algorithm 1, p. 5), and this is the part I read twice. If I understand correctly, the key observation is encoded in Eq. (4) and then used in Lemma 3 / Theorem 1 to reduce what looks like an exponential search over ordered lists to something tractable. That is the right idea, and in my experience these kinds of ordering/oracle arguments are where papers in this area either become elegant or collapse. Here it mostly works. “Mostly” because I still found some steps too compressed. In particular, the transition from the Bellman-style objective to the sortable score in the proof around p. 6 was not as transparent as it should be. I might be wrong, but I suspect many readers will have to reconstruct part of the argument themselves.

On the theory side, the regret/sample-complexity guarantees for CascadingVI and CascadingBPI are nice on paper. Table 1 is useful. That said, the presentation leans heavily on “near-optimal” without always making the comparison class precise enough. Near-optimal relative to what lower bound exactly? The discussion around lines 210–230 felt a bit hand-wavy. Also, some assumptions are doing a lot of work. The click model is still the standard first-attractive-item cascade, and while that is fine as a starting point, the paper sometimes oversells generality.

The experimental section is okay but not amazing. Figure 2 and Figure 3 do show runtime/sample benefits over naïve adaptations, but the baselines are a little weak. Comparing against “straightforward adaptations” of generic RL algorithms is not terribly convincing, because of course those will struggle with combinatorial actions. I would have liked at least one stronger combinatorial RL baseline, or even an ablation isolating how much BestPerm alone buys you. Right now the empirical story is supportive but not definitive.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a real idea in it. Not flawless, and the writing could be sharper in the core proofs, but the problem is meaningful and the algorithmic insight is nontrivial. I’m leaning accept.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly more interesting than I expected from the title. The core claim — that sparsity can simultaneously reduce AT cost and improve robust generalization — is plausible, and the empirical trends in Tables 1–3 are pretty compelling at first glance. In particular, the CIFAR-100 results with ResNet-18 (I think this is Table 2?) are strong enough that I paused. Robust overfitting under AT is real, and in my experience many “fixes” either help robustness a bit while making training even more painful, or they are very benchmark-specific. Here the FLOPs savings are substantial, not cosmetic.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what exactly is driving the gains. The static sparsity method seems to lean heavily on early lottery-ticket-style subnetwork identification, but I’m not convinced the paper disentangles “sparsity as regularization” from “early stopping / reduced capacity / altered optimization trajectory.” Around the method section (pages 3–4, near the equations defining the sparse mask updates; maybe Eq. (5)–(7)?), the exposition gets a bit slippery. The dynamic method especially feels under-justified theoretically. It works, apparently, but why should adaptive rewiring during adversarial training improve robust generalization specifically, rather than just acting as another noisy optimizer? I might be missing something, but the intuition is not crisp.

I also wanted a more careful accounting of fairness in compute comparisons. The paper emphasizes training FLOPs reduction, but sparse training implementations are notoriously tricky: theoretical FLOPs are not wall-clock speedups. If the claim is “more efficient training,” I would expect at least one real runtime/memory table, not just FLOPs. Figure 3 (if I’m remembering correctly) shows the robust overfitting curves, and those look nice, but I would have liked variance bars or multiple seeds throughout. Some of the gains are not huge, and AT results can move around annoyingly from seed to seed.

Another concern: the combination with existing regularizers is nice, but it also muddies the contribution. Are we learning that sparsity is the key ingredient, or just that “add one more regularizer and tune carefully” helps? The ablations on sparsity ratio and pruning schedule are useful, though I think the paper should say more about failure cases. Does this still help at extreme sparsity? On Tiny-ImageNet the picture seemed less clean.

Overall, I think the paper has real merit and is above the bar empirically, but the mechanism is underexplained and some evaluation choices feel a bit too convenient. Tentative accept from me, though not an enthusiastic one.

I found the paper interesting, but also somewhat underdeveloped in ways that matter for a top venue. The basic idea — learning soft matches between input tokens and a set of semantically meaningful concept vectors, then amplifying errors on highlighted spans — is intuitively appealing, especially for low-resource settings where inductive bias really matters. I can see why this might work. And I do appreciate that the authors are trying to get interpretability without bolting on a post-hoc explanation method.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the contribution is as novel as the paper claims. The mechanism in Eq. (3) on page 4 looks quite close in spirit to attention over prototype-like concept embeddings, and the “error boosting” term introduced around lines 168–182 is basically a reweighting scheme on annotated tokens. Maybe I’m missing a subtlety, but the paper does not do enough to distinguish itself from prior prototype networks / rationale-supervised classifiers / token-level loss weighting papers. The related work section on page 2 feels too light for this. In my experience, if you want to claim interpretability + low-resource gains, you need to be much sharper about what is actually new.

I also had trouble evaluating the empirical story. Table 1 shows improvements, yes, but the benchmark suite is pretty narrow, and the low-resource protocol is not described with enough care. Are the few-shot splits fixed? Averaged over how many seeds? The variance matters a lot here, and I only saw limited reporting (Table 2, maybe, but it’s incomplete). Figure 3 is suggestive, but a couple of qualitative examples do not establish interpretability. Honestly, the highlighted-token supervision may be doing most of the work, which is fine, but then the comparison should include stronger rationale-based baselines. Right now the ablation on page 6 is not enough to isolate whether soft matching itself helps, or whether any token upweighting would give similar gains.

I did like the simplicity of the method. That’s a real plus. And I think the paper is readable overall, though some notation becomes messy around Eq. (5). But I’m left with too many open questions: annotation cost, robustness to noisy highlights, sensitivity to the boosting factor, and whether the learned “concepts” are actually stable across runs.

So, borderline leaning reject for me. There is a decent idea here, but the paper in its current form feels more like a solid workshop paper than a fully convincing conference submission.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more compelling than I expected from the title. The main claim — that under a fixed parameter budget, splitting capacity across independently trained random-feature ridge models is suboptimal and that \(K=1\) wins when ridge is tuned optimally — is clean and important. This is one of those results that sounds “obvious” in hindsight, but actually pinning down the regime and assumptions matters. I also appreciated that the authors did not stop at the theorem and tried to connect it to modern deep nets on CIFAR-10 and C4.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper always says as much as it thinks it says. The random-feature theory seems solid on its own terms, but the bridge to practical neural ensembles feels weaker. On p. 3–4 (around the main setup and theorem statement), the assumptions are pretty specific: independent models, partitioned trainable parameters, optimally tuned ridge, and effectively no interaction among ensemble members except averaging. Fine. But then the experimental framing later starts sounding broader, almost like “ensembles are not worth it” in general, which is just too strong. In practice people ensemble for robustness, optimization variance, calibration, etc., not only average test MSE under perfect tuning. I think the paper knows this, but the writing occasionally oversells.

I liked the scaling-law discussion, especially the dependence on kernel/task eigenspectra. Figure 1 (if I read it right) helps, though I had to go back and forth with Eq. (6)/(7) a couple times because the notation around effective dimension and budget allocation is not especially reader-friendly. Maybe that’s on me, but after reading this twice I still found parts of the derivation compressed. A bit more intuition would help. Table 1 and the deep-net experiments are directionally supportive, but I’m not entirely sure they isolate the claimed mechanism. For example, on the transformer/C4 side, how sensitive are the conclusions to optimization budget and early stopping? Equal parameter count is not equal training compute, and for ensembles that matters a lot.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a real theorem and a useful negative message. I just want the claims calibrated more carefully. My leaning is positive, but not enthusiastically so. If accepted, I’d strongly encourage the authors to tone down the generality of the practical conclusions and make the assumptions impossible to miss.

I had to read this twice, partly because the writing is pretty rough, and partly because the method itself mixes several ideas at once (anchor-sample affinity, anchor-anchor self-expression, permutation-based alignment, and direct binary clustering). I think I understand the intended contribution, but the paper does not make it easy.

At a high level, the idea of modeling anchor-anchor relations in addition to anchor-sample relations is reasonable, and honestly overdue in this line of anchor-graph MVC papers. The motivation around cross-view cluster-order inconsistency is also valid. On p.2–3 the authors argue that misaligned anchors “confuse the graph structure”; that part made sense to me, at least intuitively. I also liked the attempt to avoid the usual embedding + k-means/spectral post-processing pipeline. In principle, directly optimizing for discrete assignments could reduce instability.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the actual technical development. Eq. (6)–(9) introduces the self-expression/topology learning part, but the connection between the anchor-anchor coefficient matrix and the final anchor-sample graph is not explained clearly enough. Figure 2 helps a bit, though even there the data flow is somewhat hand-wavy. I kept asking myself: is this genuinely learning extra structure, or just adding another smoothing/regularization term with a more elaborate story? I might be wrong, but the novelty feels incremental relative to prior anchor-based MVC and permutation alignment work.

The permutation mechanism is probably the core claim, yet I found it under-specified. Around p.5, lines 168–190, the optimization over permutation matrices seems expensive and nontrivial; the paper then moves quickly to an alternating scheme without much discussion of convergence beyond a generic statement. In my experience, these alignment variables are exactly where methods become brittle. I would really want an ablation showing what happens when alignment is imperfect, or when views have very different cluster granularity.

The experiments are okay but not especially persuasive. Table 2 shows gains, but mostly modest ones, and there is the usual issue that baselines may not have been tuned equally hard. I also missed runtime/scalability numbers, which matter a lot for anchor methods. Figure 4’s parameter sensitivity is appreciated, though I wish the paper discussed why performance is so flat in some regimes and sharply unstable in others.

Overall, I see a potentially useful idea here, but the presentation and empirical validation are not yet at top-conference level. My leaning is weak reject. If the authors can clean up the derivation, better isolate what the dual-level affinity actually buys, and give a more convincing treatment of the alignment optimization, I’d be more positive.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, kind of refreshing. There are a lot of “new benchmark” papers right now that feel incremental, but this one is at least asking a real question: can VLMs infer plausible causes/effects from images rather than just describe them. I buy the motivation in the abstract and early intro, especially the wet floor example. In my experience, this is exactly where current multimodal systems often fall apart despite looking impressive on standard VQA-style tasks.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails the benchmark design. The core idea—premise image plus two hypothesis images, choose the more plausible one and explain why—is good. But with only 350 triplets, I worry a lot about variance and about over-interpreting the results. On p. 4, where the authors describe the six reasoning categories, I wanted a much more careful breakdown of category balance and ambiguity. Are “cultural” and “social” actually separable in practice here? Some examples in Fig. 2 looked more like commonsense scene understanding than true abductive inference. Maybe that’s fine, but then the claim should be toned down.

The data creation pipeline also gave me mixed feelings. The two-stage process on p. 5 (write textual descriptions, then generate images with T2I, then manually filter) is sensible, but it also introduces a pretty artificial distribution. I’m not entirely sure whether poor model performance reflects failure at abductive reasoning or sensitivity to generated-image artifacts / style inconsistencies. Table 2 shows models near random, which is striking, but I would have liked at least one control with fully human-collected photographs or a subset audited for image-generation cues. Otherwise this may be benchmarking “reasoning over synthetic compositions” more than visual abduction.

I did appreciate the human evaluation component. The explanation scoring is a nice touch, and the human-model gap in Table 3 is meaningful. But the explanation rubric itself felt underspecified; the paper mentions plausibility and justification quality, yet I couldn’t find enough annotation detail to judge reproducibility. If Eq. (1) is the final aggregate score, it’s too lightweight for something doing a lot of argumentative work.

Overall I lean positive because the task is worthwhile and the empirical result is provocative. But the benchmark is small, somewhat synthetic, and maybe a bit too eager in its conclusions. I’d probably support acceptance if the venue wants bold benchmark papers, though not without reservations.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantive than a lot of the recent “PATE for LLMs” submissions I’ve seen. The core point — that in diverse-output settings the disagreement among teachers is not just noise but actually the signal we want to preserve — is a good one, and it is stated clearly already in the abstract and then more formally around the definition of “preserving diversity” on p. 3. In my experience, this is exactly the place where a lot of PATE-style work gets hand-wavy. Here the authors at least try to pin it down.

The main technical idea, as I understand it after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, is to aggregate distributions rather than hard labels, while claiming “no privacy penalty” for diversity preservation. That claim is strong, maybe a bit too strong. I think the intuition is plausible, and the theorem around the hot aggregation mechanism (I believe Theorem 4.1, with the privacy argument following near Eq. (5) or (6)) seems directionally correct, but I was not fully convinced by how much is hidden in the neighboring assumptions. I might be wrong here, but the jump from preserving the response distribution to saying this comes “for free” in privacy terms felt under-explained. The proof sketch is terse. I would really like a more explicit comparison to standard GNMax/cold PATE accounting under identical teacher distributions.

The empirical section is promising but also the weakest part for me. Table 1 shows the big gain claim (“order of magnitude”), but I had to work to understand what exactly is being compared. Is the baseline tuned equally hard? Figure 2 is suggestive, but I wanted ablations on number of teachers, entropy of teacher outputs, and API noise/temperature sensitivity. Right now the experiments feel a bit narrow for the breadth of the paper’s claims. If this is supposed to be a plug-in replacement, show it in more than one setting.

I did appreciate the practical angle that the method only needs API access to proprietary models (mentioned around p. 2 and again later). That matters. A lot. It makes the work more relevant than a purely white-box construction.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a real idea, but it is slightly oversold and somewhat under-validated. I’m leaning positive because the conceptual contribution is clean and useful, and because the paper addresses a real failure mode in prior PATE adaptations. Still, I’d want the camera-ready to tighten the theorem exposition and expand the empirical comparisons.

I read this fairly carefully, and I think the paper is tackling a real gap. A lot of speech-driven face animation work does indeed wash out person-specific style, especially in head motion, and the idea of separating expression personalization from pose personalization is sensible. The paper is also generally readable. Figure 1 gives the pitch clearly, and I appreciated that the method is not just “fine-tune everything harder,” which is where a surprising number of these papers end up.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the technical novelty once I got past the framing. The MoLoRA idea on p.4–5 feels like a fairly incremental adaptation of low-rank fine-tuning ideas into this domain. Maybe that is enough if the empirical story is really strong, but I don’t think the paper quite gets there. Equation (6) and the surrounding text describe the mixture mechanism, but I found it under-specified: how exactly are the experts selected/gated at inference, and what prevents collapse to a small subset? I may have missed it, but after reading this twice I still wasn’t sure. Similarly, the pose adapter section on p.5 is interesting in spirit, but the “semantic-aware pose style matrix” reads a bit hand-wavy. The retrieval process around Eq. (9) needs more detail. Is this nearest-neighbor over a learned codebook? Soft retrieval? How sensitive is it to the discretization granularity?

The experimental section is a mixed bag for me. The qualitative results in Fig. 4 and the video would probably matter a lot here, because these problems are very hard to judge from static frames. Table 1 looks good, but I’m always wary when “vividness” and “style preservation” are claimed mostly through proxy metrics. The user study helps, but I would have liked more information about the protocol and statistical significance; Table 3 is a little too compact. Also, the 10-second reference claim is central, yet I didn’t see a convincing analysis of how performance degrades from 5s to 10s to 30s. That ablation seems essential. Same for the number of LoRA experts.

One thing that bothered me more than it should have: the comparisons do not feel completely airtight. Some baselines are fine-tuning based, yes, but are they all given the same personalization budget and same reference duration? On p.7 the wording is a bit slippery. If not, the gains are harder to interpret.

Overall, I think this is promising and probably useful in practice, but the paper currently feels stronger as an engineering system than as a clean research contribution. I’m leaning borderline positive, mainly because the problem matters and the method seems to work, but I do think the presentation and ablations need tightening.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing, because it pushes back on a premise that a lot of the unsupervised style transfer literature has sort of taken for granted: namely, that you need attribute-invariant latent representations. The central claim — that disentanglement is neither necessary nor reliably achieved in practice — rang true to me. I’ve seen similar failure modes in prior work on controllable generation, even when adversarial objectives are supposed to “remove” style information. So the premise is good.

The argument around this is laid out reasonably clearly in the first half of the paper, especially around the discussion of adversarial invariance and the move to back-translation. I think the empirical evidence that the “fully entangled” model works better is probably the most convincing part of the submission. Table 2 and Table 3 (assuming I’m reading the right ones; the formatting was a bit dense) seem to show fairly consistent gains on transfer accuracy and overall generation quality, especially on the harder multi-sentence setting. That said, I had to read the experimental section twice to understand exactly which components differ between the compared systems. The pooling operator in latent space is presented as giving a controllable trade-off between content preservation and attribute change, but I did not think this was explained crisply enough. I’m still not entirely sure, after reading Eq. (whatever the pooling definition is — I think around page 4), why this specific operator is the right one, or how sensitive the method is to that choice.

A bigger issue for me is evaluation. This paper makes fairly broad claims about “better generations,” but the automatic metrics here are the usual shaky ones for style transfer. If the human evaluation is in Figure 2 / Table 4, it needs more detail. How many annotators? What exact instructions? Were they blind to system identity? In my experience, small changes in annotation protocol can completely flip the conclusions in this area. Also, for a paper about multiple attributes, I wanted more analysis of attribute interactions: does changing sentiment while preserving gender behave differently from changing product type while preserving sentiment? This feels underexplored.

I also think the novelty is somewhat incremental at the modeling level. Back-translation plus attribute conditioning is not exactly a shocking recipe. The contribution is more “this simpler thing works better than the ideology of disentanglement,” which is still valuable, but let’s be honest, it is not a major architectural leap.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper is thoughtful, the claims are relevant, and the experiments seem broader than many papers in this space. But I do wish the paper were sharper about what is genuinely new versus what is just a sensible recombination, and the evaluation section needs to be more rigorous.

I thought this was an interesting paper, and in spirit I’m quite sympathetic to it. The basic idea—compute embeddings for rare words from auxiliary information rather than either giving up and using UNK or relying on huge external pretraining—is sensible and, honestly, something many people have wanted for a while. After reading the paper twice, I think the contribution is real, but the presentation made it harder than necessary to pin down exactly what is new versus what is an engineering combination of existing ingredients.

The core method is described reasonably clearly around Eq. (3)–(6), but I found myself flipping back and forth to understand what is actually available at test time. On p. 3, the distinction between “surface-form” and “context” information is there, but the training/inference protocol is a bit slippery. Are both always present? Figure 1 helps, but not enough. I might be wrong, but it seems some of the gains depend on having fairly informative auxiliary contexts, which makes the setting less general than the abstract suggests.

The empirical section is encouraging but also where I have the most reservations. The reading comprehension and SNLI/RTE-style results in Table 2 and Table 3 are positive, yes, but the margins are not huge, and I was missing stronger ablations. In particular, how much comes from subword information alone? How much from context alone? There is some version of this, but not in the systematic way I wanted. Also, comparing mainly to “train from scratch,” “pretrained,” and “UNK” baselines feels a little thin. By the time this kind of paper is submitted, I would expect stronger comparisons to morphology-aware or character-CNN/LSTM baselines that also address rare words. Maybe they are included briefly, but if so, they are not emphasized enough.

I did like the language modeling experiment more than I expected. It gets at a real practical issue. Still, Table 4 left me unsure whether the improvements are statistically robust or just dataset-specific noise. No variance, no confidence intervals. That’s a recurring issue in NLP papers from this era, but it still matters.

My overall take: good idea, plausible method, useful empirical gains, but the paper slightly oversells novelty and underdoes the comparisons. I lean positive because the problem is important and the approach seems broadly applicable, but I’m not entirely convinced the experimental case is as airtight as it should be for a top venue.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more thought-provoking than I expected from the title. The core claim — that LLMs can in-context learn several distinct tasks simultaneously in one forward pass — is unusual but not obviously crazy, and the empirical results seem to suggest something real is happening rather than a prompt-formatting trick. I especially liked the scaling trend (I think this is around Table 2 / Fig. 4?) showing larger models handling more parallel tasks and producing better calibrated outputs. That part felt like the most solid contribution to me.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper has pinned down “task superposition” as a distinct phenomenon rather than a somewhat broad umbrella over compositional prompting / multitask conditioning / token-level mixture behavior. The terminology is catchy, but I’m not sure the conceptual boundaries are sharp enough. On p. 3–4, where the setup is introduced, I wanted a much cleaner formal definition of what counts as “simultaneous” task execution. Is the model genuinely representing multiple latent task vectors in parallel, or is it just conditionally routing based on local context segments and output formatting? Those are not the same thing.

The theoretical section also left me mixed. The transformer expressivity argument (around the equations in the middle of the paper — Eq. 2 or Eq. 3, if I remember correctly) shows plausibility, not really explanation. That’s fine, but the prose sometimes overclaims. “Well within the expressive power” is a low bar; many things are. I would have preferred a tighter bridge from the theorem to the actual measured behavior in the studied models.

I did appreciate the interpretability angle, especially the discussion of composing task vectors internally (Fig. 5 maybe?). But I found the evidence suggestive rather than decisive. In my experience, these representation analyses are very sensitive to probe choice and prompt template details, and I didn’t see enough robustness checks. Maybe I missed them.

One more concern: I’m not entirely sure the baselines are strong enough. A simple concern is whether some of the gains come from repeated task markers or regularized output structure rather than genuine parallel ICL. Ablations on delimiter choice, task ordering, and conflict between tasks would help a lot. If those are already in the appendix, they should be surfaced more prominently.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a memorable idea, but it currently feels a bit ahead of its evidence. I’m positive on the empirical observation itself; I’m more skeptical about the mechanistic interpretation and framing. Still, this is the kind of paper people will talk about, and probably argue about, which is usually a good sign.

I had a somewhat mixed reaction to this paper. The general problem—conversational assistance for subjective search, especially image or asset search—is interesting and absolutely relevant. In practice, these systems are hard to evaluate and even harder to train because, as the authors note, there is rarely a nice supervised conversational dataset lying around. So the idea of using a stochastic virtual user to bootstrap an RL agent is sensible, and not new-new, but still useful if done carefully.

That said, I found the paper fairly underdeveloped. After reading it twice, I still wasn’t fully convinced what the real technical contribution is beyond “use A3C with context + simulated users.” The virtual user model seems to be doing a lot of work here, but from the description (especially around the simulator construction in the middle sections; I think around pp. 4–5), it is hard to judge whether it is realistic enough to support the conclusions. If the simulator is biased toward the action patterns the proposed agent exploits, then higher reward in simulation does not mean much. This is the big issue for me.

I also thought the experimental section was thin. The comparison is mainly against Q-learning, which feels like a weak baseline in 2020s RL-for-dialog terms. Why not compare to a supervised ranking/dialog policy, a contextual bandit, or at least a stronger deep RL baseline? Table 1 and the reward curves (I assume Fig. 3/4) show improvement, but the setup seems almost designed to make A3C look good. I might be wrong, but I did not see convincing ablations on the context-preserving architecture itself. Is the gain coming from A3C, from recurrence/context, or from reward shaping? This is not disentangled.

There were also clarity issues. Equation definitions are a bit rushed, and some notation is introduced without enough grounding. I found the state/action design interesting, but the paper needs more concrete examples of interactions. A sample dialogue trace would have helped a lot. Right now it feels abstract.

I do think the application is meaningful, and in industry settings this kind of bootstrapping with simulators is common. So I’m not dismissing the direction. But as a conference paper, I wanted either stronger empirical validation (ideally some human or offline log-based evaluation) or a more novel algorithmic idea. As written, it feels incremental and somewhat fragile in its evaluation story.

My leaning is below acceptance.

I read this paper with some interest because SCI reconstruction is a space I’ve followed for a while, and the basic idea here — injecting “high-quality knowledge” into an unfolding model via a two-phase training recipe — is not crazy at all. In fact, it’s one of the more sensible directions in this area, where a lot of papers just make the backbone bigger and call it a day. The results in Table 1 and Table 2 are clearly strong, at least on the standard benchmarks, and Fig. 1/2 make the method look coherent.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the novelty claim as written. After reading the method section twice, the actual contribution seems to be a fairly standard deep unfolding pipeline plus a knowledge-assist branch/teacher-style prior (Sec. 3.2–3.4), with degradation-aware conditioning. Useful, yes. But conceptually it feels incremental relative to recent SCI works that combine physics-based iterations with learned priors and transformer denoisers. Eq. (6)–(10) are fine, but I kept asking myself: what is the irreducible new idea here beyond “pretrain on cleaner/high-quality data, then transfer that guidance into the reconstruction network”? Maybe I’m underselling it, but the paper does not articulate this sharply enough.

I also found the “degradation-aware” part underexplained. On p. 5 around the training description, it sounds like degradation information is implicitly learned rather than explicitly modeled. If so, the title oversells it a bit. What exactly is the degradation representation? How robust is it to mismatch between synthetic and real degradations? The real-data results are visually better in Fig. 7, yes, but still a bit cherry-picked. I would have liked more failure cases.

Another concern is ablation quality. Table 3 helps, but it is not enough. The two-phase training strategy is central, yet there is little analysis of sensitivity: what happens if the “knowledge” source is weaker, out-of-domain, or imperfect? In my experience these teacher/prior-transfer schemes can collapse when the clean prior is not truly aligned with test conditions. Also, complexity is not discussed honestly enough. The model appears heavy, and I could not find a clear accounting of training cost versus prior SCI transformers.

Overall, I think the paper is solid empirically and probably acceptable at a workshop or a mid-tier venue. For a top conference, I’m on the fence. The gains are real, but the methodological jump feels modest, and some key details are glossed over. My leaning is weak reject, though I could be persuaded otherwise if the authors clarify the degradation modeling and strengthen the ablations.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve followed the disentanglement literature since the early beta-VAE / FactorVAE wave, and honestly the field has needed something a bit less heuristic for a while. The paper is ambitious: it tries to connect the group-theoretic notion of disentanglement to actual unsupervised VAE training, and then proposes “Groupified VAEs” as a practical route. I appreciate that ambition. But I also found the paper uneven, and at times much more suggestive than convincing.

The theoretical part is the most interesting piece. The discussion around the group-based definition and the role of the n-th dihedral group is novel, at least relative to the usual total-correlation story. I think the authors are right, broadly speaking, that minimizing TC alone is not enough, and the motivation in the abstract and early sections is aligned with the Locatello et al. impossibility results. That said, after reading the theorem section twice, I still found the exact scope of the “three sufficient conditions” somewhat slippery. On p. 4–5 (around the theorem statements), the assumptions on the data-generating group action seem very strong, and I’m not entirely sure whether they are realistic outside toy settings. The paper says this provides “an option” for the missing inductive bias, which is fair, but in practice this feels less like a general framework and more like a fairly specific structural assumption. Equation references around the group decomposition (I think Eq. 6–8) could also be explained more concretely. Right now it reads a bit like mathematics placed adjacent to the learning problem rather than fully integrated with it.

The empirical section is large in model count (1800 models is nontrivial), but the evidence is still a bit frustrating. The main claim is “better mean performance with smaller variances,” which is good, but I wanted to see stronger statistical reporting in the tables and clearer effect sizes. Table 1 / Table 2 (if I’m tracking correctly) show improvements, but often modest ones. Figure 3 was helpful, though I wish the visualizations made the alleged group structure more explicit. Also, since disentanglement metrics are notoriously unstable and often misleading, I would have liked more discussion of metric dependence. In my experience, papers in this area can look better than they are if the metric happens to align with the inductive bias.

One thing that bothered me: the writing is pretty hard to parse. There are many sentences where the claim is technically important but buried in awkward phrasing. This is fixable, but it matters here because the contribution is conceptual/theoretical.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it is more substantive than another “add a regularizer to VAE” paper. But I’m not yet convinced the theory-to-practice bridge is solid enough, or that the assumptions are broad enough, for a top conference acceptance in its current form. Borderline, leaning weak reject.

I had to read this twice to understand what the actual claim is. As I see it, the paper is not really “proving” something about deep learning representations in any concrete finite-sample sense, but rather showing that under a fairly strong concentration assumption, the Gram matrix of features extracted from GAN-generated data asymptotically behaves like that of a Gaussian mixture. That is a much narrower and more conditional statement than the title suggests, and I think the title overreaches quite a bit.

The random matrix part seems technically competent, or at least consistent with a line of work that has been around for a while. Eq. (2) / the concentration assumption is doing almost all the heavy lifting. My issue is that this assumption is exactly where the interesting deep learning question gets hidden. On p. 3–4 (around the discussion introducing concentrated random vectors), the paper seems to move very quickly from “DL representations of GAN data” to “vectors in a class for which known RMT results apply.” But why should features from BigGAN images passed through standard representation nets satisfy the needed independence / concentration structure in a meaningful way? I’m not entirely sure the empirical section really checks this, as opposed to showing that some downstream statistics look vaguely compatible.

I also found the use of “fully described by their first two moments” too strong. For “a wide range of standard classifiers” maybe asymptotic performance depends only on low-order statistics under the model, but that is not the same thing. Table 1 and Fig. 2 (if I’m reading them correctly) show reasonable agreement for some architectures, but not enough to justify the broad wording in the abstract. There is a difference between “well approximated in these experiments” and “fully described.”

The experimental validation feels thin. BigGAN is a natural testbed, sure, but one generative family is not enough if the paper wants to say something about GAN-data in general. I would also have liked comparisons with real data features, not only generated data, and some quantitative test of Gaussian-mixture adequacy beyond classifier behavior. Right now the empirical part reads a bit like a sanity check attached to a theory paper.

That said, I do think there is an interesting idea here. In my experience, connecting modern feature representations to classical high-dimensional asymptotics can be quite valuable, and the paper is aiming in that direction. But the framing is too sweeping and the assumptions are not interrogated enough. My leaning is below the bar unless the authors significantly tone down the claims and clarify precisely what is proved versus hypothesized.

I read this paper with interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of the recent “codec LM for TTS” submissions, and this one is at least trying to address a real bottleneck rather than just scaling a decoder and reporting MOS. The core idea — using probabilistic RVQ to shorten token sequences and then predicting multiple tokens jointly — is sensible, and in parts of the paper (especially around Fig. 1 and the discussion near Eq. (3)-(5), if I’m reading the notation right) the motivation is quite clear. I do think the paper has a real contribution here. The reduction in sequence length is not just a convenience; for these models it changes the whole feasibility envelope.

That said, I found the presentation uneven. On p. 3-4, the explanation of the probabilistic residual vector quantization is more compressed than it should be. I had to reread the paragraph around Eq. (2) twice to understand what exactly is sampled, what is deterministic at training vs inference, and how this differs from prior RVQ variants used in neural codecs. Maybe this is obvious to authors in this subcommunity, but I don’t think it is obvious to a broader ML audience. Similarly, the claim that the method “eliminates the need for cascaded modeling” felt a bit overstated. It removes one kind of cascade, sure, but there is still structured dependence across quantization levels/tokens implicitly baked into the codec and decoding process. So I would tone that down.

The empirical section is decent but not fully convincing. Table 1 and Table 2 show competitive results, but I was missing stronger ablations isolating where the gains come from: shorter sequence length, multi-token prediction, codec quality, or just model/data scale. The pretraining analysis is interesting — actually one of the more useful parts of the paper in my opinion — but it also raises the question of whether the proposed architecture is the main story or whether pretrained LM choice dominates. I might be wrong, but the latter seems plausible from the numbers. Also, speaker similarity results look good, yet I wanted more failure cases or qualitative discussion. Zero-shot TTS always breaks somewhere.

One more issue: inference speed comparisons need more care. On p. 6 the setup is not detailed enough; these numbers are notoriously fragile to batching, hardware, and decoding strategy. I wouldn’t want readers to over-interpret them.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper with a practical idea and mostly solid results, but it needs clearer technical exposition and a more surgical ablation story. Borderline positive from me.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than flashy. The core idea—automatically expanding visual hallucination test cases from existing ones—is pretty natural in hindsight, but I have not seen it done this systematically for MLLMs. The combination of question/answer negation with image perturbations is a sensible recipe, and the paper does a decent job motivating why manual VH curation does not scale. I also appreciated that the authors actually use the expanded data both for evaluation and for fine-tuning, rather than stopping at benchmark construction.

That said, I have mixed feelings about how solid the method really is. The biggest issue for me is validity of the generated test cases. On p.4–5, where VHExpansion is described, the perturbation pipeline sounds straightforward, but I kept wondering how often the negated question/answer pair is truly semantically valid after image modification. Figure 2 gives the intuition, but it is a bit cherry-picked. After reading this twice, I still was not fully convinced that the automated expansions preserve the intended visual semantics at scale. If the generated “negative” example is ambiguous or unnatural, then symmetric accuracy is measuring robustness to artifacts, not just hallucination. I think the paper needs a stronger human validation study here, ideally with inter-annotator agreement and not just a few examples.

I did like the metric discussion. The argument around symmetric accuracy is one of the stronger parts of the paper. The proposition/theorem around unbiasedness (I think this is around Section 4, with the formal statement near Eq. 5 or 6) is reasonable, and the intuition is clear. Still, the framing is a little overstated. “Unbiased” is only with respect to a random-guessing baseline under the stated assumptions; in practice MLLMs are not random guessers, and answer priors interact with instruction tuning in messy ways. So yes, symmetric accuracy is probably better than plain accuracy for paired cases, but I would tone down the grand claims.

The experimental section is useful but somewhat uneven. Table 2 / Table 3 (if I’m remembering correctly) show different model rankings under accuracy vs symmetric accuracy, which is interesting. But I wanted more ablations. Which component matters most: negation, common image perturbation, or adversarial perturbation? Right now the method is bundled together. Also, the fine-tuning result is promising, but I worry it partly reflects distribution matching—the model improves because the train and test sets share the same synthetic transformation style.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper, but not quite airtight. I lean positive because the problem matters and the paper contributes a practical tool plus a better metric, but I would want the camera-ready to tighten the validation story and moderate some claims.

I read this paper with some interest because structural inference from dynamical observations is a problem I’ve seen come up repeatedly, and the active learning angle is potentially useful. The paper is aiming at an important problem. That said, I came away fairly unconvinced, mostly because the technical story feels underspecified and the experimental claims are stronger than what the evidence really supports.

The core idea, as I understand it from Sec. 3 and especially around Eq. (4)–(7), is to use active learning to query informative structural labels and combine this with “inter-scope” and “out-of-scope” message learning. I might be wrong, but I found the terminology pretty opaque. Even after reading the method section twice, I was not fully sure what exactly distinguishes the two message pipelines in operational terms. Fig. 2 is supposed to clarify the architecture, but it remains high level; I wanted a more concrete algorithm box with inputs/outputs and the query/update cycle spelled out. Right now, an important amount of the contribution feels like naming.

A related issue is novelty. There is a large literature on graph structure learning from trajectories, neural relational inference, and active querying for graph recovery. The paper cites some of this, but I don’t think it clearly positions itself against the closest prior work. The claim in the abstract that the approach works in both supervised and unsupervised settings is also too broad. The “unsupervised” formulation on p. 6 (around lines 210–235) still seems to rely on fairly strong inductive bias and proxy objectives; that is not the same thing, in my view.

The experiments are decent in breadth, and I appreciated the inclusion of real-world networks. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains, but they are not always large, and I was surprised by how little discussion there is of annotation/query budget versus performance. If active learning is the point, I want to see label efficiency curves prominently, not just endpoint numbers. Fig. 4 hints at this, but it is not analyzed carefully. Also, some baselines seem under-tuned or outdated. In my experience, these comparisons can swing a lot with stronger graph inference baselines.

One more thing: scalability is asserted repeatedly for large dynamical systems, but I could not find convincing runtime or memory analysis beyond brief remarks near the end of Sec. 4. For a paper making large-system claims, that’s a real omission.

Overall, I think the paper has a potentially interesting direction, but in its current form it feels premature. Too many conceptual pieces are vague, and the empirical case does not yet fully justify the claims. I’d lean weak reject.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea — ask an LLM to generate a progress estimator rather than a full reward, then build count-based intrinsic rewards on top of that — is actually pretty clever, and in some sense much more reasonable than the recent “let the LLM write the whole reward function and pray” line of work. I think that framing is the main contribution here, and it’s a good one.

The empirical results on Bi-DexHands are impressive on the face of it. If I’m reading Table 1 correctly, the claimed ~20x reduction in reward-function samples versus prior LLM-based reward search is substantial. Figure 3 also helps make the case that progress+counts is doing something qualitatively different from just using the raw progress function as reward. That said, I found myself repeatedly wondering how much of the gain is really from “LLM-generated progress functions” versus simply injecting a lot of task structure into the state abstraction. In other words, once the progress function exists, the rest looks like a fairly standard discretization/counting pipeline. That’s not a dealbreaker, but the paper occasionally overclaims novelty.

I also found the methodology a bit under-explained in places. Around the description of the two-step pipeline (I think this is Sec. 3, around Eq. 2/3), it was not totally clear to me how robust the generated progress functions are to partial observability, noisy simulator state, or task variants. Maybe I missed it, but I wanted concrete examples of failure cases. Some of the generated functions shown in the examples look almost like hand-designed shaping terms in disguise. After reading this twice, I’m still not entirely sure where the line is between “automated reward design” and “LLM-assisted manual decomposition.”

Another issue is evaluation scope. Bi-DexHands is hard, yes, but it is also a fairly specific benchmark family. The paper leans heavily on “broad domain knowledge” in the abstract, yet I didn’t see evidence that this transfers beyond dexterous manipulation. Even one non-manipulation domain would have helped. Otherwise the claims feel a bit too broad.

On clarity: generally decent, but some of the writing is a little salesy. I’d tone down “state-of-the-art” language unless the comparisons are truly apples-to-apples in compute and environment setup. The ablations in Table 2 are useful, though I wish they included a stronger non-LLM baseline for constructing progress features.

Overall, I think this is interesting and probably above the bar, but not a slam dunk for me. The idea is better than the paper’s presentation, oddly enough. My score would be cautiously positive.

I’m a bit torn on this paper. I like the ambition: revisiting a crowded area and trying to unify a bunch of traffic forecasting ideas under a PINN lens is, at least in principle, useful. There are too many papers in this space that just stack modules and report tiny gains on METR-LA/PEMS-BAY, so a conceptual paper is not unwelcome. And I do think the discussion around symmetry and boundary conditions has some intuition behind it. The point on p.3–4 that many deep models are “spatially symmetric in the backbone” except for node embeddings is interesting, and the analogy to PDEs with symmetric operators but asymmetric boundary conditions is the most original part of the submission.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m not convinced the paper really delivers on its central claim. A lot of the argument is suggestive rather than rigorous. Eq. (2) and the surrounding discussion seem to formalize the symmetry notion, but I found the definitions too loose to support the later conclusions. In particular, the jump from “node embeddings break permutation symmetry” to “therefore they encode boundary conditions” (around p.5, lines ~180–210) feels more like an interpretive metaphor than an actual result. Maybe that’s intended, but then the paper should be much clearer that this is a perspective piece and not a theoretical characterization.

I also struggled with the adaptive adjacency / graph rewiring section. Figure 2 is helpful at a high level, but the equivalence being claimed is not really established. In my experience these adaptive matrices often absorb all kinds of dataset-specific bias, optimization artifacts, and even missing normalization choices; calling this “rewiring” is fine informally, but the paper occasionally overstates it. Table 1 is also odd: it reads more like a taxonomy than evidence. That’s okay, but then I wanted either a more systematic survey protocol or at least one concrete case study where the PINN interpretation leads to a nontrivial prediction or design recommendation.

Honestly, the paper is more essay than ML paper. That is not automatically bad, but top venues usually need either sharper theory, stronger empirical backing, or a genuinely new synthesis. I’m not entirely sure about my score because I did find parts thought-provoking, especially the boundary-condition angle, but right now it feels under-substantiated and a bit hand-wavy. My leaning is negative, though not strongly so.

I found the paper interesting and pretty timely. The problem is real: in practice, a lot of people are building on API-based LLMs and have essentially zero visibility into when behavior changes. I’ve complained about this myself. So I was positively inclined going in. The online change-point framing is also sensible, and I appreciated that the paper is not just another offline benchmark exercise.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical core. The main statistic based on entropy/Gini of the response distribution sounds neat, but in the actual black-box setting the “distribution” is only indirectly observed through repeated prompting / sampled outputs, and I think the assumptions around this are doing a lot of hidden work. Around Eq. (3)-(5) (if I’m reading correctly), the derivation feels cleaner than the actual deployment scenario warrants. In particular, I could not tell how sensitive the method is to ordinary nonstationarity from decoding randomness, diurnal load effects, hidden system prompt changes, etc. The paper says it controls false alarm rate, but Table 2 and the discussion around p. 6 seem mostly empirical rather than giving a convincing operational guarantee.

I did like the adaptive prompt selection idea more than I expected. The UCB formulation is reasonable, and Figure 2 makes the intuition clear. But I also think this is where the paper is a bit too optimistic. If prompts are adaptively chosen based on previous outcomes, then the data stream is no longer i.i.d. in any useful sense, and I’m not entirely sure the CUSUM calibration still means what the authors want it to mean. Maybe I’m missing a standard argument here, but I wanted a much more explicit treatment.

The real-world API experiments are the most exciting part and also the most frustrating. Detecting announced updates is good. Claiming “strong evidence” of unreported changes (p. 8, near Figure 4) is provocative, but honestly this needs more caution. It could be backend routing, safety filter updates, rate-limit effects, or just sampling drift. I’m not saying the claim is false, just under-justified.

Overall, I think there is a good paper hiding in here, but the current version overclaims relative to what is established. I’d lean borderline reject, maybe weak reject, mainly because the methodology/guarantees are not yet as solid as the application demands. If the authors tighten the statistical story and are more careful with the API-change claims, I’d be much more enthusiastic.

I found this paper interesting, and in a crowded “uncertainty for GNNs” space it at least feels like it is trying to say something nontrivial rather than just porting a Bayesian MLP trick to graphs and calling it a day. The main idea — adapting stochastic data centering via graph anchoring and arguing that partially stochastic GNNs are enough — is, I think, the most compelling part of the submission. In particular, the claim around pretrained models is practically meaningful. If that result really holds broadly, people will use it.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the evidence as presented. After reading the method section twice, I still found the exact role of the anchors somewhat slippery. Eq. (4) and the discussion around it (page 5, roughly mid-page) suggest the anchoring induces functional diversity in the posterior samples, but the paper sometimes talks as if this is a proxy for epistemic uncertainty and other times as if it is a mechanism for approximating it. Those are not the same thing. I might be wrong, but the exposition blurs that distinction. Figure 2 helps a bit, though I wish it were less schematic and more diagnostic.

The experimental section is fairly extensive, and I appreciated that the authors did not stop at ECE under IID conditions. The concept shift and graph-size shift evaluations are useful. Table 2 and Table 3 look strong overall. Still, I wanted more clarity on baselines. Some of the comparisons seem slightly too favorable. For example, if the point is that post-hoc calibration is insufficient under shift, then the strongest versions of temperature scaling / ensemble calibration should really be included everywhere, not only in some settings. Also, on page 8, the discussion of OOD detection feels a bit hand-wavy: AUROC improvements are there, yes, but I could not tell whether these are due to better uncertainty or just better raw predictive features.

I also think the paper undersells possible failure modes. Anchoring strategies for graphs can easily inject structural bias; in my experience with graph classification benchmarks, small implementation choices here matter a lot. An ablation isolating anchor construction (especially versus random or degree-based anchors) would strengthen the paper considerably. I was looking for that in Figure 4 / Appendix tables and didn’t really find it.

Overall, I lean positive because the idea is novel enough and the empirical scope is decent, but this is not a slam dunk for me. The paper would benefit from sharper conceptual framing and tougher baselines. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to clean up the method explanation and be more honest about what exactly is being estimated.

I found the paper interesting, and I do think the problem is timely. There is a real gap between “LLMs can sort of reason over graph descriptions” and “LLMs are actually competitive graph models,” so I was inclined to like this. The two-block hierarchical design is also intuitively sensible: local aggregation first, then some global reasoning. That part, at least at a high level, is easy to buy.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by either the novelty claim or the empirical story. The architecture in Sec. 3 feels like a fairly natural decomposition rather than a fundamentally new model class. Eq. (2) and Eq. (4) mostly instantiate local neighborhood encoding followed by higher-level token interaction, and I kept wondering how different this really is from prior hierarchical transformers / graph-text hybrids. The related work on p. 3–4 gestures at this, but I think it undersells how much overlap there is. Maybe I’m being too harsh, but “new state-of-the-art” plus “significant advancement” felt oversold relative to the actual conceptual delta.

The evaluation is broad, yes, but uneven. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains, though in several cases they are not large, and I could not tell whether prompt tuning / verbalization effort was matched fairly across baselines. This matters a lot here. The robustness-to-prompting result (I think Fig. 4?) is nice, but I wanted much more detail on how the prompt variants were constructed; otherwise it’s hard to know if this is real robustness or just robustness to mildly paraphrased templates. In my experience reviewing LLM-for-graphs papers, this is where a lot of the apparent gains hide.

I was also unconvinced by the interpretability claims. Attention maps are not explanations, or at least not by default, and the discussion around this on p. 7 is too casual. The use of “established explainers” helps a bit, but the paper never really validates whether the highlighted substructures correspond to ground-truth reasoning paths. Without that, “interpretable” feels aspirational.

One smaller but important point: I found the computational story vague. The hierarchical design sounds expensive, but I didn’t see a clear accounting of training/inference cost versus the GNN baselines and the plain LLM baselines. If this is on p. 8 in the implementation details, it needs to be surfaced more clearly.

Overall, I think there is a decent paper in here, but right now I see solid engineering and decent benchmarking more than a clear scientific advance. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject.

I went into this paper somewhat sympathetic to the premise, because in my experience active learning for 3D detection has indeed been oddly underwhelming compared to the 2D case, and people often gloss over the annotation-cost mismatch. So I appreciated that the paper starts from a negative empirical observation rather than pretending uncertainty sampling should magically work. The motivation around point-cloud acquisition versus box-level labeling cost is, I think, basically correct, and the framing on p.2–3 is one of the better parts of the paper.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical story. The method itself — combining label conciseness, feature representativeness, and geometric balance in CRB — is sensible enough, but it also feels like a fairly heuristic pipeline dressed up with stronger theoretical claims than it can really support. The generalization argument around aligning marginal distributions and minimizing an upper bound (Sec. 4, especially around Eq. (7)–(10)) reads a bit hand-wavy to me. I might be wrong, but several assumptions seem to be doing a lot of work, and I did not see a clear connection between the actual greedy selection procedure and the stated bound. This is one of those cases where the theorem is not false exactly, but not very informative either.

The experiments are solid overall. I liked that the authors evaluate both SECOND and PV-RCNN, and on both KITTI and Waymo. Table 1 and Table 2 are useful, and the gains over uncertainty/diversity baselines seem real. Figure 1 is also effective in motivating the annotation-cost imbalance. But some details are frustratingly underexplained. For example, the computational overhead of the hierarchical filtering is not clearly quantified; if this has to be run repeatedly over a large unlabeled pool, that matters. Also, the claim in the abstract about reaching fully supervised performance with 1% boxes / 8% point clouds is striking, maybe too striking, and I wanted a more careful discussion of variance across runs. I did not see enough error bars or standard deviations. In active learning, results can swing a lot depending on initialization.

I also found the ablation section weaker than it should be. The paper says the three criteria work jointly, but the interaction effects are not really dissected. Table 4 helps, but I wanted more than leave-one-out. Does geometric balance matter mainly on Waymo? Is conciseness just selecting sparse scenes? That part is still fuzzy.

Overall, I think the paper has a good empirical point and a practically relevant method, but the theory is oversold and some experimental details need tightening. Borderline accept from me, mostly on empirical relevance rather than conceptual novelty.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit uneven. The core idea — using random matrix theory as a “zero-information” baseline for transformer weights — is sensible, and honestly cleaner than a lot of recent interpretability papers that overclaim from messy probes. I liked the basic empirical observation in Fig. 1 / Fig. 2 (assuming I’m reading the ordering right) that random initializations follow the RMT prediction closely while trained models systematically depart from it. That part is convincing. The paper is at its best when it stays descriptive and careful.

What I’m less convinced by is the jump from “spectral deviations exist” to “we can locate learned information” in a meaningful mechanistic sense. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still think the term “locate” is doing a lot of work here. Yes, certain submatrices/layer types deviate more strongly, and yes the activation covariance comparison helps, but this is still fairly indirect. In my experience, correlations between weight spectra and function can be surprisingly slippery. The discussion around Eq. (4) and the surrounding text (around p. 5, if I remember correctly) felt a bit too confident given the evidence.

I did appreciate the cross-architecture consistency claim for BERT and Llama. That matters. Table 1 is useful, and the layer-type trends seem real rather than cherry-picked. But I wanted more controls. For example, are these deviations stable across seeds, checkpoints during pretraining, or different tokenizer/vocab sizes? Maybe this is in the appendix and I missed it, but it feels important. Also, the activation covariance comparison is suggestive, not definitive. I’m not entirely sure the paper separates architectural priors from learned structure as cleanly as it claims.

The finetuning/alignment part is probably the most provocative claim, and also the shakiest. The statement in the abstract about small singular values being “crucial” seems too strong based on the evidence presented in the later experiments (I think this was around Fig. 6 or Table 3). It may well be true, and I’m sympathetic to the intuition, but the causal story is underdeveloped. There are many reasons truncating small singular values could hurt besides “alignment information lives there.”

Overall, I think this is a solid and somewhat novel empirical paper, but not yet a fully convincing interpretability contribution. I’d lean weak accept if the venue values spectral analysis/diagnostics, weak reject otherwise. The ideas are good; the claims need tightening.

I found the core idea interesting and, honestly, pretty intuitive in a good way: if humans use emphasis to guide reading, why shouldn’t we give LLMs an analogous mechanism? The paper is easy to grasp at a high level, and I appreciate that PASTA is post-hoc/inference-time only. In practice, that matters a lot more than many papers in this area seem to realize.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m less convinced by the technical depth than by the framing. The method, as described around Eq. (3) and the discussion on selective head intervention (I think this is on p.4–5), feels like a fairly direct attention reweighting heuristic plus a head selection procedure. That’s not necessarily bad, but the paper sometimes writes as if it has uncovered a more fundamental mechanism than it really has. I would have liked a more sober positioning against prior activation steering / inference-time control work. The related work section felt a bit thin to me.

The empirical results are the strongest part. Table 1 and Table 2 are pretty striking, especially the gains on instruction following for LLaMA-7B. Figure 3 was also helpful in showing that only a small number of heads are modified. Still, I have questions. First, how brittle is this to the exact emphasis span? I might have missed it, but I didn’t see a convincing analysis of over-marking or slightly wrong marking. In real use, users won’t annotate perfectly. Second, I’m not entirely sure the paper disentangles “better attention to the marked span” from “distribution shift caused by an intervention that effectively sharpens behavior.” The causal claim is stronger than the evidence.

I also found the evaluation setup a bit too favorable. On the knowledge integration tasks (around p.6, maybe Table 3), the model is explicitly told what to focus on, and then the method helps it focus there. Yes, that is the point — but it makes the benchmark almost tailor-made for the method. I wanted at least one harder setting where the emphasis is partial, noisy, or competing.

A smaller issue: the head selection criterion was underexplained. Why these heads, exactly? Are they stable across prompts/tasks/models? The appendix may address some of this, but in the main paper it felt hand-wavy.
