Overall, I think this is a solid and useful paper, though not quite as conceptually novel as it presents itself. I lean positive because the method is simple, practical, and the gains seem real, but I do think the paper oversells the mechanism and underserves the robustness story.

I have mixed feelings about this paper. The core idea is good, and honestly overdue. Too much code evaluation work is still stuck in exact match / BLEU-ish nonsense, so I appreciate the push toward execution-based evaluation across multiple tasks and languages. A benchmark that tries to unify generation, translation, retrieval, and understanding at document level is potentially very useful, and the scale here is undeniably large. Figure 1 and Table 1 help sell that story well.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as clean as it wants to be. The biggest issue for me is that there are really two contributions jammed together: the dataset/benchmark itself, and the splitting/selection methodology with the “geometric mean and graph-theoretic principle” (I think this is around Sec. 4, with Eq. 3/4?). The latter is presented as novel, but I found it under-motivated and oddly hard to parse. Maybe I’m missing something, but the paper never quite demonstrates that this split construction materially changes scientific conclusions versus simpler stratified or problem-level splits. A benchmark paper should be very crisp about why this complexity is necessary.

I also worry about contamination and duplication. With 25M examples from 7.5K unique problems, the number sounds huge, but the true semantic diversity may be much smaller than the headline suggests. On p. 5–6 (dataset construction), I wanted a much more explicit discussion of near-duplicate solutions, templated contest problems, and leakage across languages/tasks. “Execution-level parallelism” sounds nice, but in practice it may make memorization easier, not harder. This is especially relevant when evaluating commercial LLMs whose training data is unknown.

The execution engine is interesting, and in my experience this is the part that usually breaks in multilingual code benchmarks. So I do give the authors credit for actually building ExecEval. But I wanted more failure analysis. How often do executions fail due to environment issues, flaky tests, timeouts, nondeterminism, etc.? Some of this may be in the appendix, but it should be more prominent. Table 6/7 report results, but not enough diagnostic breakdowns.

The empirical section is serviceable, though a bit benchmark-report-ish. Lots of numbers, less insight. I’m not entirely sure the retrieval task belongs in the same framing as the executable tasks, and its evaluation felt less integrated.

Overall, I think this will probably be useful to the community, but as a paper I found it somewhat overclaimed and under-analyzed. Leaning positive, but not enthusiastically so.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit frustrating in equal measure. The topic is important. A lot of Bayesian deep learning papers still make claims about “the posterior” while only looking at tiny models or toy datasets, so I appreciate the attempt here to actually inspect posterior structure for modern vision models at scale. The checkpoint release is also genuinely useful if it is maintained well. In my experience, artifacts like this can outlast the paper.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper has a sharp enough technical contribution beyond a fairly broad empirical survey. The abstract promises “one of the first large-scale explorations,” and that does seem directionally true, but much of the paper felt like a collection of observations rather than a coherent set of claims with strong evidence. For example, the discussion around scaling symmetries versus L2 regularization is potentially the most interesting part, but I thought it was under-theorized. The argument near Eq. (4)/(5) (assuming I followed correctly) gestures at why scaling-equivalent solutions interact oddly with Gaussian priors, but the paper stops short of really pinning down what this means for the actual posterior geometry. I wanted a cleaner statement here.

I also struggled with the methodology in the posterior “quality” analysis. The results in Table 2 and the uncertainty plots in Fig. 3 suggest some connection between better posterior approximations and improved OOD/calibration behavior, but this is not exactly a new idea, and I wasn’t sure the experiments isolate that claim cleanly. There are too many moving parts: architecture, training recipe, approximate inference family, symmetry handling. I might be wrong, but I suspect some of the gains are just due to better optimization / ensembling effects rather than “posterior fidelity” per se.

The visualization section is nice, though a little hand-wavy. Fig. 5 is suggestive, but I wanted more than suggestive. Also, some writing was harder to parse than it should be; I marked several places around p.6–7 where the narrative jumps from permutation symmetry to mode connectivity to uncertainty without enough signposting.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper tackles a real problem and assembles a substantial empirical study. But I’m not enthusiastic. I would want the final version to sharpen the central claims, be more careful about causal interpretations, and explain much more clearly what is genuinely new versus what is a well-executed large-scale confirmation of things many of us already suspected.

I read this paper with some interest because multimodal ICL is still oddly underexplored compared to the amount of hype around it, and in principle this is a useful direction. The core idea — a lightweight prependable module for multimodal in-context tuning — is sensible, and I do think the paper is aiming at a real problem. In particular, the claim that unified multimodal models like OFA/Unival/LLaVA do not naturally perform strong few-shot in-context adaptation matches what I’ve seen myself. The cross-model applicability is probably the most appealing part of the submission.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper in its current form. A lot of the empirical story feels stronger in the abstract than in the actual presentation. On p. 4–5, where the method is introduced, I had to reread the description of the “expandable context window” a couple of times. Eq. (2) and the surrounding text are not very clear about what exactly is learned versus frozen across backbones, and how the modality-specific representations are aligned before entering the M$^2$IXT module. Maybe I’m missing something, but this is important: if the gain comes partly from additional learned projection capacity, then the “lightweight” narrative needs more nuance.

I also found the comparisons a bit slippery. Table 1 and Table 2 report strong gains, yes, but the baselines are not always normalized in a way I would trust. For example, are all methods using the same number of in-context exemplars, same prompt construction, same decoding settings? The paper says “mixed-tasks strategy” on p. 6, but the training data composition is underdescribed. 50K multimodal data sounds impressively small, but small relative to what distribution? Curated from the target tasks? Held-out task families? This matters a lot for the few-shot adaptation claim. In my experience, these setups can quietly become task-specialized tuning under another name.

Figure 3 is nice qualitatively, though a bit cherry-picked. I would have liked failure cases. Also, the SOTA claim feels too broad. Some of the compared systems are not equally recent or equally large-context capable, and the paper sometimes mixes “parameter efficiency” with “performance leadership” in a way that is marketing-ish, frankly.

Still, I think there is a decent paper in here. The idea is practical, and the gains on OFA/LLaVA are nontrivial. But I need cleaner ablations: number/order of context examples, effect of modality combinations, frozen-vs-tuned backbone components, and robustness to context permutation. Without that, I’m left thinking the method probably works, but not yet understanding why. Weak accept / borderline for me.

I had a somewhat mixed reaction to this paper. The high-level idea—using an auxiliary classifier together with adversarial domain training to enlarge effective source-domain diversity—sounds reasonable, and in the abstract it is positioned as if this is a meaningful extension of standard adversarial DG. In principle, I like this direction. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the contribution is either conceptually crisp or empirically nailed down.

The main issue for me is clarity. Around the method section (especially the part around Eq. (3)–(6)), the paper seems to conflate at least three goals: class discrimination, domain confusion, and source-domain “diversification.” I think the auxiliary classifier is supposed to create harder or more varied decision boundaries, but the mechanism is not explained in a way that makes this precise. On p. 4, the text claims the model learns “condition-invariant” features, but I’m not entirely sure what that means here—class-conditional invariance? domain-invariant conditional on class? Those are not the same thing, and in my experience this distinction matters a lot in DG papers.

I also found Figure 2 less helpful than it should be. The architecture diagram looks standard, but it does not really show where the proposed novelty enters beyond adding another head. If that’s basically the method, then the novelty is fairly incremental. Maybe I’m being a bit harsh, but this literature is crowded, and “we add an auxiliary classifier” is not enough unless the paper really demonstrates why this changes the optimization dynamics in a nontrivial way.

The experimental section is promising but incomplete. Table 1 shows gains over prior methods, but I wanted stronger ablations. What happens if you remove the auxiliary classifier but keep the rest identical? What if you vary its weight in the total loss? The paper mentions optimized batch normalization in the title, but I honestly expected more direct analysis of BN behavior—e.g., source-specific statistics, mixing strategy, test-time handling. As written, BN feels under-motivated relative to the title. Also, some gains look modest, and I could not tell whether variance across seeds was reported consistently (Table 2 maybe, but not everywhere).

There are also writing issues. A few claims are overstated, especially in the abstract and conclusion. “Surpassing contemporaneous state-of-the-art” is a strong statement; the evidence here is not broad enough for that.

Overall, I think there is a kernel of a useful idea here, but the paper needs sharper technical exposition and more convincing ablations before I would be comfortable accepting it. Right now it reads more like an incremental workshop paper than a strong conference submission.

I like this paper, and I also found it a little frustrating in the way many benchmark papers are frustrating: the core idea is very good, clearly timely, and probably useful to the community, but some of the methodological details feel less nailed down than they should be for something that will likely become a standard evaluation.

The basic premise — evaluating models on real GitHub issues paired with actual PRs — is compelling. In my experience, this is much closer to what people actually want from “coding agents” than HumanEval-style function completion. The examples around multi-file edits and repository-level reasoning make that point well. I thought Figure 1 (the task setup) was effective, and Table 1 gives a nice sense of the spread across repositories, although I would have liked even more detail on issue types/difficulty. Are these mostly bug fixes? API updates? tests? refactors? The benchmark risks overrepresenting one flavor of maintenance work.

The most important contribution here is not the 1.96% number, which is eye-catching but kind of ephemeral, but the dataset construction and execution-based evaluation harness. That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still have concerns about contamination and benchmark validity. The paper says issues are drawn from public GitHub histories, and I’m not entirely sure the contamination story is handled convincingly. If a frontier model has seen the issue text, the patch, or nearby commits during training, then low scores are reassuring in one sense, but it still muddies the interpretation. I might be wrong, but this deserved a more serious treatment than it gets.

I also found the evaluation protocol slightly brittle. Passing the associated tests is reasonable, but only associated tests. On p. 5/6 (around the description of the grading harness), I wanted more discussion of under-specification and false positives. A model can overfit to tests; conversely, real fixes may fail because of environment/setup quirks. The paper acknowledges some of this, but briefly. The claim that this measures “real-world software engineering” is directionally true, though maybe a bit grandiose.

The SWE-Llama part felt weaker to me than the benchmark itself. The fine-tuning recipe and analysis are serviceable, but not especially novel, and I honestly would not weight that heavily.

Overall, I think this is a meaningful benchmark paper and likely to have impact. I’m positive on acceptance, though not unreservedly. The community needs harder, more realistic software engineering evaluations, and this is one of the more credible attempts I’ve seen. But I do think the authors should tighten the discussion around contamination, task distribution, and what exactly is being measured.

I found the paper interesting but also somewhat uneven. The core idea — using an LLM to decompose a complex instruction and a 3D grounding model to identify targets/obstacles, then feeding this into a motion generator — is sensible, and honestly kind of inevitable given where the field is going. In that sense the paper is timely. The examples in Fig. 1 and the longer qualitative sequences later in the paper do make the task setting clear: this is not just “walk to chair,” it is multi-stage embodied behavior in cluttered scenes. I appreciate that ambition.

That said, after reading this twice, I am still not fully convinced the technical contribution is as strong as the framing suggests. A lot of the method seems to be a pipeline of existing components: LLM for task decomposition, visual grounding for object localization, then a motion model with spatial controls. I might be wrong, but the novelty feels more in integration than in new modeling. That can still be publishable, but then the evaluation has to be rock solid, and here I had concerns.

The biggest issue for me is the new “complex motion” benchmark. As described around the experimental section (I think p. 6–7), this evaluation set is automatically generated from possible virtual human behaviors in indoor scenes. This is convenient, sure, but also potentially very brittle and biased toward the decomposition assumptions of the proposed method. I really wanted a stronger discussion of how realistic these generated tasks are, and whether baselines were fairly adapted. Table 2/Table 3 look favorable to GCML, but since the benchmark is synthetic, I am not sure how much to trust the margins. In my experience, these automatically constructed embodied benchmarks often reward the system that shares the benchmark’s ontology.

I also found the exact role of the LLM underspecified. The prompt design, failure rate, and sensitivity are not discussed enough. Eq. (4) and the surrounding text are a bit too abstract; I could not reconstruct what supervision the subtask planner actually receives. Likewise, the transition stitching between subtasks seems important for motion naturalness, but the paper moves past it quickly. Fig. 4 looks okay visually, but I wanted more quantitative evidence that the composed long motions are temporally coherent rather than just locally plausible.

On the positive side, I do think the paper identifies a real gap in HSI. Current systems absolutely struggle with “mop around clutter, then move to another area” type instructions. The HUMANISE results are decent, and the generalization angle is worthwhile. Still, I am leaning borderline to weak reject because the paper overclaims novelty and the evaluation setup does not fully convince me. With a stronger ablation on decomposition/grounding quality and a more credible benchmark or human study, I’d feel differently.

I ended up fairly mixed on this paper. The high-level idea—using base skill priors to stabilize / structure language-conditioned imitation learning under messy, unstructured data—is sensible, and honestly pretty aligned with where a lot of the field has been drifting. I also appreciate that the authors actually try a sim-to-real zero-shot evaluation instead of stopping at CALVIN. That said, after reading the paper twice, I still found the methodological novelty somewhat blurry.

The main claim seems to be that the policy benefits from a decomposition into reusable base skills plus language conditioning, but on p. 3–4 the distinction from prior hierarchical or skill-conditioned IL papers is not very crisply made. Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) look like a standard latent prior regularization setup with language as context; I might be missing something, but I did not see a compelling reason this is fundamentally different from existing skill-prior or option-style imitation frameworks. The paper repeatedly says “under unstructured data,” but I wanted a much clearer operational definition. Is “unstructured” referring to mixed-quality demonstrations, lack of task segmentation, environment variation, or all of the above? Right now it reads a bit like a catch-all phrase.

The empirical section is more convincing, especially Table 1 and Table 2. The CALVIN gains are large, and if the reported 2.5x over HULC in the zero-shot multi-environment setup holds under comparable training data and model capacity, that is definitely notable. But this is also where I got uneasy. The fairness of the comparison is not discussed carefully enough. On p. 6, the training recipe seems substantially different from HULC’s, and I could not tell whether the baselines were re-run under the same data preprocessing and augmentation pipeline. In my experience, CALVIN numbers move a lot with these details. Figure 4 is nice qualitatively, but I would have preferred failure case analysis instead of more success screenshots.

The real-world result is interesting but also thin. Ten tasks is better than nothing, but the sample size is small, and Table 3 really needs variance / confidence intervals. A “30% improvement” sounds impressive until one realizes the absolute success rates may still be modest. Also, sim-to-real without adaptation is hard, so I don’t want to dismiss it outright.

Overall, I think this is promising work, but the paper currently oversells novelty and underspecifies evaluation details. I’m leaning weak accept if the conference rewards strong empirical gains, but weak reject if the bar is methodological clarity.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantive than a lot of recent “causal discovery under shift” submissions that are mostly repackaging invariance ideas. The core idea of using a score that explicitly aggregates evidence across multiple domains is sensible, and the paper does a decent job motivating why naive pooling or running a single-domain method separately can fail. I especially appreciated the discussion around heterogeneous data in the intro and the toy example around Fig. 1 (if I’m reading it correctly, this is the example showing spurious dependence induced by mixing domains). That part was clear.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I still think the paper is harder to parse than it should be. The definition of the MDSS score around Eq. (4)–(6) is central, but the assumptions under which it is valid are a bit scattered. I kept flipping between the theorem statement and the earlier setup. In particular, the asymptotic skeleton recovery guarantee (I think this is Theorem 1 on p. 5?) seems to depend on fairly strong conditions on how shifts occur across domains. Maybe I’m wrong, but the paper at times sounds more general than the theory actually supports. The phrase “heterogeneous data” is broad; the analysis appears to cover a narrower class of interventions / mechanism changes.

I also wasn’t fully convinced by the directional identifiability claims. The paper says MDSS can identify more directions than single-domain methods by exploiting shifts, which is plausible, but I wanted a sharper characterization of exactly when this happens. The discussion near p. 6, around the proposition after Eq. (8), felt somewhat hand-wavy. In my experience, these claims can be surprisingly brittle.

The experiments are solid but not spectacular. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, though in several settings the margins are modest. I would have liked more stress tests where the domain shifts are weak, misspecified, or only affect a tiny subset of variables. Also, the real-data section is a bit too quick; Fig. 4 looks nice, but it’s hard to assess whether the recovered directions are genuinely meaningful or just plausible-looking.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with real technical content, but it needs cleaner exposition and a more honest framing of the assumptions. I lean positive because the problem matters and the approach is not trivial. Still, I’m not entirely sure the current version nails the theory-to-practice gap.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more insightful than a lot of the recent “mechanistic interpretability on toy tasks” submissions, though it also has some of the same limitations. The main claim — that in small transformers trained on addition, the digitwise sum and the carry computation separate cleanly across layers/modules — is plausible and mostly well supported. I liked the analysis around the 2-layer encoder on pp. 3–5, especially the decomposition into “same-position addition” in layer 1 and carry routing in layer 2 attention followed by carry application in the layer-2 MLP. Figure 2 and Figure 3 were helpful. Equation (4) also made the story more concrete, though I had to read that section twice to understand exactly what was being projected and what was just an interpretive shorthand.

The paper is at its best when it is specific. The neuron identification procedure (I think this is in Sec. 4, around p. 6?) is neat, and I appreciated that the authors seem to really isolate functional units rather than just wave at activation correlations. In my experience, many papers in this area overclaim from saliency-style evidence; this one is better than that. Table 1 and the ablations around hyperparameter variation also help.

That said, I’m not entirely convinced by the broader framing. “Modular fashion” is doing a lot of work here. The circuits look modular under this training setup and tokenization, yes, but it is not obvious to me how robust this is to distribution shift, different positional encodings, different data formatting, or even different random seeds. The authors say it occurs “across a range of hyperparameters,” but the evidence felt narrower than the abstract suggests. I might be wrong, but the 3-layer results (p. 7?) felt more like “something similar happens” than a crisp characterization.

The Alpaca 7B part is also pretty weak. Suggestive evidence is the right phrase, but then maybe it should be de-emphasized. Right now it reads a bit like an attempt to buy relevance. Figure 6 was intriguing, but I did not find it strong enough to support much beyond anecdotal resemblance.

Overall I lean positive. This is a solid mechanistic case study with some genuinely nice analysis, even if the generalization claims should be toned down. I’d accept if the bar is “careful empirical interpretability on a canonical task,” but I would push the authors to be more modest in the claims, especially around larger models.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — replacing the usual “domain-invariant representation” story with a latent causal/content variable whose marginal can shift while \(p(y \mid z_c)\) stays fixed — is actually a nice reframing of MSDA. In my experience, a lot of domain adaptation papers quietly assume some version of label shift not happening, and this paper at least tries to say clearly what is assumed and why. I appreciated that.

That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the identifiability story. The claim around identifying \(z_c\) up to scaling using source labels plus nonlinear ICA conditions is intriguing, but the exact bridge from the generative assumptions to the learning objective feels too quick. Eq. (4) and the discussion around Proposition/Theorem on p. 4–5 (sorry, I may be slightly off on pagination) seem to rely on conditions that are quite strong and somewhat hidden in the prose. In particular, I’m not entirely sure how realistic the auxiliary-variable assumptions from nonlinear ICA are in the MSDA setting. Domains themselves are being used as side information, I think, but then the relation between domain index, style, and content shift needs to be much more carefully unpacked.

I also found Figure 2 conceptually helpful, but the empirical section is less convincing than the theory section wants it to be. Table 1 shows gains, yes, but not huge ones, and on some datasets the margins look modest enough that I would want stronger ablations. For example, what happens if the latent covariate shift assumption is mildly violated? Or if \(z_s\) carries some label information? This seems essential, because in real data “style” is rarely cleanly non-predictive. The simulation results are directionally supportive, but simulations built from the assumed model are never that persuasive.

Another thing: the paper repeatedly says it learns the invariant label distribution conditional on latent content rather than invariant representations, but in practice the model still learns a representation. This may sound pedantic, but I think the distinction is overstated.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it is more thoughtful than many MSDA submissions. But the theory-to-method connection is not yet airtight, and the experimental validation feels a bit thin for the strength of the claims. I lean borderline reject, though I could be persuaded if others found the identifiability argument more solid than I did.

I found the paper interesting at a high level, but I also came away pretty unconvinced by the framing and, honestly, by the evidence. The core idea — that models should answer “closed” questions more deterministically and “open” questions with more diversity — is sensible, almost obvious. But I’m not sure the paper really pins down what “question awareness” is in a rigorous enough way to support the claims being made.

After reading it twice, I still struggled with the definition around p.2–3. The notion seems to mix at least three things: answer entropy, stylistic variation, and factual correctness. Those are not the same. A factual question can have one correct answer but many valid phrasings; an open-ended question can still benefit from structure and not just “higher temperature.” I think the paper slides over this distinction. Eq. (3) and the QAT mechanism are basically a heuristic for adaptive decoding, which is fine, but the conceptual packaging is stronger than what is actually delivered.

The experiments also left me with questions. In Table 2 and Table 3, the gains are there, but they are fairly modest, and I could not tell whether they come from better matching the evaluation metric rather than genuinely improved behavior. Figure 4 is suggestive, but anecdotal. I really wanted a cleaner human evaluation here. If the central claim is that responses are “too casual” or “too boring,” that is exactly the sort of thing automatic metrics are weak at capturing. The paper says QAT removes manual temperature tuning, but in practice it seems to replace one tuning problem with another learned/engineered mapping from question features to decoding settings. Maybe I’m being unfair, but that was my reaction.

I was also confused by the dataset construction details around p.5 (the split between open-ended and non-open-ended categories). Some examples looked debatable to me. In my experience reviewing generation papers, once the taxonomy is fuzzy, the whole evaluation becomes shaky. The line of argument would be much stronger if the authors showed robustness across multiple annotations or even measured annotator agreement.

That said, I do think the paper is asking a good question. The writing is generally clear, and the practical instinct behind QAT is reasonable. I just don’t think the current version has the empirical depth or conceptual sharpness for a top-tier venue. Right now it feels more like a promising workshop paper than a fully convincing conference paper.

I found this paper moderately interesting, but also somewhat frustrating to read. The core idea — recasting K-means in a manifold-based form and then adding an \(\ell_{2,p}\)-norm maximization term to encourage balanced partitions — is not unreasonable, and I can see why the authors think it addresses two very standard pain points of vanilla K-means: nonlinear structure and sensitivity to initialization. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the contribution is as substantial as the paper claims.

The main technical development around the objective (I think this is around Eq. (8)–(12), depending on the version) feels like a combination of ingredients that are each fairly familiar: graph/manifold regularization, discrete cluster indicator learning, and a balance-promoting norm penalty. The paper repeatedly says the method “does not require the calculation of the cluster center,” but in my view that is more a reformulation than a conceptual breakthrough. Kernel K-means, spectral clustering, and graph-based clustering papers have been exploiting similar perspectives for a long time. I would have liked a much more careful positioning relative to that literature. Right now the novelty feels incremental.

I was also confused by the theoretical claim about \(\ell_{2,p}\)-norm maximization ensuring class balance. The argument around this (roughly the proposition/theorem in the middle of the paper, near p. 5–6) is suggestive, but not fully persuasive. It seems to rely on assumptions that may be too strong or too idealized for practical clustering. I might be wrong here, but I do not think “balance” in the sense induced by the row norms of the indicator matrix necessarily corresponds to semantically meaningful clustering. Sometimes balanced clusters are exactly the wrong inductive bias.

The experiments are okay, not great. Table 2 and Table 3 show improvements, but the baselines feel selective. Where are stronger graph clustering or kernel clustering baselines? Also, there is a lot of emphasis on “multiple databases,” yet the datasets look fairly standard and mostly small/medium-scale. Figure 3 is visually helpful, though I wish the authors had included sensitivity to \(p\) and the graph construction choices more systematically. In my experience, manifold methods can be annoyingly brittle to nearest-neighbor graph parameters, and the paper downplays this.

Presentation-wise, the writing is uneven. Some notation is introduced too quickly, and I found the optimization section hard to follow. There are also several grammar issues throughout pages 3–7 that made the paper feel less polished than it should be.

Overall, I lean negative. There is some value here, and I can imagine this being useful in a more mature form, but for a top ML venue I don’t think the novelty, clarity, and empirical depth are quite there.

I found the paper interesting in intent, but honestly fairly underdeveloped as a conference submission. The problem is important: open-world classification is more realistic than the standard closed-set setup, and trying to go beyond rejection into actual discovery of unseen classes is worthwhile. I’ve seen a number of papers on open set recognition / novelty detection / class discovery, and this one sits somewhere in between, but I’m not convinced the paper is technically mature enough.

The core idea, as I understand it from Sec. 3 (especially around Eq. (3)–(6)), is to jointly train an open classifier and a pairwise same/different model on seen classes, then use the learned pairwise score as a distance for clustering rejected examples. That is sensible. In fact, it’s almost the obvious baseline I would try first. I don’t mean that as praise or criticism necessarily, but the novelty is limited unless the authors can really show why this formulation is better than more standard metric learning or deep clustering pipelines. Right now I don’t think they do.

A lot of the paper felt hand-wavy to me. On page 4, where the pairwise classification branch is introduced, I kept looking for a clearer justification of why “same vs different on seen classes” should transfer to fine-grained structure among unseen classes. This assumption might hold, but it is not trivial. Figure 2 gives the architecture, but not much intuition. Also, after reading Table 2 twice, I still wasn’t sure whether the gains are coming from the joint training, from the rejection stage, or simply from having a stronger feature extractor than the baselines. The ablation is too thin. If there is an ablation in Table 3, it is not enough.

I was also confused by the evaluation protocol. On page 5/6 the paper switches between open classification accuracy and clustering metrics on rejected samples, but the dependency between the two is crucial: bad rejection can poison clustering. I might be wrong, but I don’t think the paper cleanly disentangles these effects. It would help to report oracle rejection, or oracle number of clusters, or both. Otherwise it is hard to know what is being solved.

There are also some presentation issues. Eq. (7) appears suddenly without enough explanation of hyperparameters, and the experimental section reads a bit too much like “we outperform prior work” without enough detail. Some baselines also seem dated.

Overall, I like the problem and I think there is a seed of a good paper here, but in its current form I’m not convinced. My leaning is weak reject.

I found the core idea interesting, and honestly a bit more elegant than a lot of recent “CNN + attention” mashups. The paper’s main claim — that one can recast a pre-trained convolutional layer into a functionally equivalent self-attention-style layer and then fine-tune efficiently — is appealing, and the construction around Eq. (3) / Eq. (4) is, at least on paper, clever. I also liked the framing on p.2 that this is not just another hybrid architecture, but a way to initialize one from an existing CNN without paying the full optimization cost of training attention from scratch. That part feels practically relevant.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the empirical story. The “only 50 epochs of fine-tuning” line in the abstract sounds strong, but it is a bit slippery. Compared to what exactly? Table 1 reports gains over the original CNN, yes, but I wanted a cleaner baseline where the same hybrid architecture is trained for 50 epochs from standard initialization, and also one where the total compute budget is matched more carefully. I might have missed it, but the comparisons on p.5–6 don’t really settle whether the benefit comes from the proposed recasting specifically, or simply from starting from a good CNN and adding capacity.

I also found the robustness claims somewhat overstated. The results in Table 3 are promising, but robustness is a big word. Are these corruption benchmarks only? Adversarial? Distribution shift? The text around lines 210–230 gestures at “substantially improved robustness,” but in my experience this usually requires more nuance than is given here.

Figure 2 is helpful, though Figure 4 felt a bit like “representation analysis because reviewers expect it.” Maybe that’s unfair, but the insights there seemed qualitative and not especially deep. Also, I was confused by the exact computational story: the abstract motivates avoiding the attention bottleneck at high resolution, yet the method still ends up with self-attention layers. So the savings are in optimization time, not inference/runtime scaling, right? This should be stated more plainly.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it is more principled than many hybrid papers I’ve seen. But the paper oversells the robustness/general insight angle and underspecifies the baseline/compute comparisons. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the experimental claims can be tightened.

I had to read this paper twice to be sure I understood what the claimed contribution actually is. My current understanding is that the authors propose an exact transformation of a standard ResNet block into a residual-free form by “reserving and merging” features, with the main motivation being better online inference due to avoiding multi-branch execution. The idea is interesting, and I do think the paper is trying to address a real systems issue that often gets hand-waved in architecture papers. In practice, branchy topologies are annoying. That part resonated with me.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by either the novelty or the empirical case as presented. The core construction around the RM operation (Sec. 3, especially around Eq. (5)–(8)) is clever, but I found the exposition surprisingly hard to follow for something that is supposed to be an “equivalent” transformation. Figure 3 helped a bit, but even after that I was still unsure what exactly is being materialized at inference and what the memory/computation overhead is before the final merge. The paper keeps saying “remove residual connection,” but in some sense the identity information is still being explicitly carried forward, just not as an additive branch. So the wording feels a little too strong, maybe even slightly misleading.

I also think the comparison to RepVGG is not entirely fair. The abstract says RepVGG “can not transform ResNet to VGG equivalently” because of the nonlinearities, which is basically true, but the paper then leans pretty heavily on that contrast without really discussing other structural re-parameterization work beyond the obvious baseline. On page 6, Table 2, the accuracy-speed trade-off numbers look decent, but I would have liked much more detail on the inference setup. What hardware? What batch size? Is the speedup from better kernel fusion, less synchronization, lower memory access, or just implementation specifics? In my experience, these deployment claims can flip depending on device.

The pruning angle (Table 4) is promising, maybe actually the most interesting part of the paper, but it feels underdeveloped. Why exactly does the RM form help high-ratio pruning beyond making the graph simpler? I think the authors have an intuition here, but it is not made sharp.

Overall, I think there is a neat idea in here, and I appreciate that the authors are targeting exact functional equivalence rather than retraining tricks. But the paper currently overclaims and underexplains. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the camera-ready-level clarifications were already available somehow.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The main idea—casting one-step distillation as learning the solution map of the probability flow ODE using a PINN-like objective—is actually pretty neat, and I do think there is some novelty here. In particular, the connection they make around p.3–4 between diffusion distillation and physics-informed residual minimization is conceptually appealing. Eq. (6) and the training objective around Eq. (8) seem to be the core contribution, and at a high level I buy the motivation.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is either as general or as practically clean as the paper suggests. The claim in the abstract about “without additional methodology-specific hyperparameters” feels overstated. The discretization number is clearly a crucial knob, and from Table 2 / the discussion around p.6 it seems performance is fairly sensitive to it. Maybe the authors mean no extra loss-balancing hyperparameters, which is fair, but the current wording sounds stronger than the evidence.

The empirical section is decent but not fully persuasive. The CIFAR-10 single-step FID of 3.92 is good, yes, but this is now a crowded space and the paper really needs tighter comparisons against recent consistency / adversarial / progressive distillation baselines under matched teacher, architecture, and compute. I might have missed it, but I could not tell whether all baselines use the exact same pretrained diffusion teacher and same data augmentation/training budget. That matters a lot. Figure 3 looks nice qualitatively, though honestly these image grids all look similar nowadays.

I also struggled with the theory section. The stability statement on p.5 (I assume around Theorem 1) reads more like a PINN-inspired plausibility argument than a sharp result about the actual distillation procedure used in practice. The assumptions seem strong, and I’m not entirely sure how they map to the neural parameterization or finite optimization error. In other words: mathematically respectable, maybe, but not yet illuminating.

One more thing: the paper would benefit from clearer exposition. Several notation jumps between the diffusion ODE, the student mapping, and the residual loss were harder than they needed to be. Lines around the derivation after Eq. (7) especially felt compressed.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I appreciate papers that try to unify areas instead of just stacking tricks. But I’m not yet convinced by the experimental rigor or the strength of the theoretical claims. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the camera-ready version substantially clarifies comparisons and assumptions.

I found the paper interesting in intent, but ultimately a bit underwhelming as a research contribution. The basic idea — avoid expensive multimodal alignment by converting each modality to text and then letting an LLM do the fusion/summarization — is sensible, and honestly fairly intuitive. My first reaction after reading the abstract was “yes, of course someone should benchmark this.” So I do appreciate the practical angle. In my experience, a lot of multimodal video papers hand-wave the compute story, and this submission at least tries to quantify it.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper gets beyond a reasonable engineering baseline. After reading the method section twice, I still think the core novelty is limited. The plug-and-play framework seems to amount to: run modality-specific foundation models independently, collect their textual outputs, concatenate/prompt them into a language model, and use few-shot instruction adaptation rather than end-to-end tuning. That may be useful, but I’m not sure it is enough for a top conference unless the empirical study is really airtight. Here it isn’t.

A lot hinges on the compute/performance tradeoff, but I found the reporting somewhat slippery. On p. 5–6 (around the main experimental setup), the paper compares “computational costs” across methods, but I could not tell whether these are pretraining-equivalent costs, adaptation-time costs, or full pipeline costs including running the upstream captioning/ASR/vision models. Those distinctions matter a lot. Table 2 is suggestive, but not rigorous enough. If the plug-and-play method pushes cost into frozen upstream models, that is still cost.

I also had concerns about the evaluation. The domain shift section is probably the most valuable part of the paper, but even there the conclusions feel stronger than the evidence. I might be wrong, but the datasets seem too few and too similar to support broad claims about “generalizability.” Also, video summarization metrics are notoriously noisy. If Figure 3 is supposed to show robustness under limited data, I wanted variance bars, multiple random seeds, and some human evaluation. Without that, small differences are hard to take seriously.

There are also places where the writing is vague. The exact prompting strategy and few-shot selection procedure are not described with enough precision for reproduction (I noticed this especially in the method details near Eq. 1 / the adaptation description). And I’m not entirely sure whether the baseline tuning methods were all comparably optimized; some baselines look weaker than what I’ve seen in recent workshop and industry reports.

So: useful practical benchmark, decent negative/positive lessons, but not a strong enough methodological advance, and the experimental evidence is a bit thin for the scale of the claims. Borderline to reject for me, leaning reject.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thoughtful than the average offline-to-online RL submission. The core framing — that in OtO the real issue is not just “debiasing” offline regularization but using the tiny online budget intelligently for exploration — felt right to me. In my experience, a lot of recent OtO papers inherit the offline RL mindset too literally, so I appreciated the authors being willing to drop policy constraints altogether and ask a different question.

The empirical section is fairly strong. The results in Fig. 2 and Fig. 4 (especially on Walker and Humanoid) are convincing at a high level: PTGOOD seems to reach strong returns much faster than the adapted exploration baselines. Table 1 also gives the impression that this is not just a tiny effect. I also liked that the paper does some work adapting “standard” online exploration methods to the OtO setting rather than comparing only against weak baselines. That said, I’m not entirely sure the comparison is as clean as it should be. After reading the method section twice, I still had some confusion about whether PTGOOD benefits from extra modeling capacity / extra learned components beyond what the baselines get. Eq. (6) and the discussion around the conditional entropy objective (page 5, around lines 180–210 I think) are a bit too compressed. I understand the intuition, but I’m less convinced the optimization target is justified in the exact form used here.

I also worry the paper overclaims on “planning to go out of distribution.” In several plots it looks more like reward-guided optimistic exploration with a learned information bonus, which is fine, but the title and some of the prose oversell the conceptual novelty a little. Maybe that’s harsh, but that was my reaction. I would have liked a clearer ablation on the individual pieces: what happens if you remove the CEB-inspired term, or keep only the high-reward targeting part? Fig. 3 gestures in this direction, but not enough.

Another issue is scope. The paper is all MuJoCo-style continuous control. That is standard, yes, but for a paper making fairly broad claims about OtO exploration, I wanted at least some evidence outside this regime. I might be wrong, but some of these gains could be very tied to smooth dynamics and decent value generalization.

Overall I lean positive. The idea is sensible, the experiments are stronger than average, and the paper is asking an important question. But the method exposition needs sharpening, and the novelty should be stated a bit more modestly.

I think this paper is tackling a real gap. Most LVLM hallucination benchmarks do implicitly assume the question is answerable, and in practice a lot of failures happen exactly when the model should just say “I can’t tell from the image.” So the premise of TUBench is good, and honestly overdue. I also appreciated that the benchmark is not just natural images; the four domains listed in the abstract (code screenshots, geometry, tables, natural images) make this feel broader than the usual VQA repackaging. In my experience, multimodal benchmarks often claim diversity but are still 80% COCO-style object questions. This seems more thoughtful.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m not fully convinced the paper has nailed the distinction between “unanswerable because information is missing” and “unanswerable because the annotation or question construction is overly adversarial / unnatural.” The ten construction strategies are central here, but the paper needs to be much more concrete about them. Around the benchmark construction section (I think this is around pp. 3–5), I kept wanting more examples per strategy, and clearer failure boundaries. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough. Some of the examples read like reasonable abstention tests; others feel closer to gotchas.

I also found the evaluation protocol a bit underdeveloped. The main metric is essentially answerability classification accuracy, but for a benchmark about trustworthiness, that feels too thin. The manual analysis later in the paper is actually more interesting than the leaderboard. The claim that Gemini-1.5-Pro gets correct answer+explanation only 41% of the time is useful, and the hallucination breakdown is maybe the most important result in the paper. I wish this had been elevated into the main evaluation design rather than left as post hoc analysis. Table 4 (if I’m recalling correctly) was more informative than Table 2.

Another concern: I’m not entirely sure about contamination / prompt sensitivity. For closed models especially, small wording changes can swing abstention behavior a lot. Did the authors test paraphrases of the same unanswerable question? If not, some of the ranking differences among top models may be noise. Similarly, I wanted a stronger human validation story: inter-annotator agreement, domain expert review for code/geometry/table items, etc. Without that, “high-quality” is asserted more than demonstrated.

Overall, I like the direction and I think the benchmark could become useful. But right now it feels somewhat benchmark-first, methodology-second. I’d lean weak accept if the dataset quality checks are indeed strong in the appendix; otherwise weak reject. The idea is solid, the execution is promising, but I’m not yet fully sold.

I found the paper interesting, and a bit unusual in a good way. Most defenses against backdoors intervene at training time or inspect model behavior post hoc; this paper instead tries to “repair” the dataset itself via class-conditional generative modeling in a pretrained SSL feature space. That is a clean idea, and I think the relabelling angle is more novel than the density-estimation part per se. The intuition on p.2–3, that backdoor examples either stay near their source class in representation space or get displaced in a structured way, is plausible and mostly borne out by Fig. 1 and Fig. 2.

That said, I had to read Sec. 3 twice. The method is conceptually simple, but the presentation is oddly slippery. Eq. (2) / the scoring rule for deciding whether to relabel or just flag as anomalous is not explained as crisply as it should be. I’m still not entirely sure how sensitive the procedure is to the specific generative model family chosen for each class, and whether the gains come from “generative modeling” or simply from having a good SSL encoder plus nearest-class density thresholding. Table 1 looks good, but I wanted an ablation where the generative model is replaced by a much simpler baseline in the same latent space. Without that, the central claim feels a little overstated.

I also worry about scope. The abstract says “recent backdoor attacks,” and yes, on the benchmarks shown the method reduces ASR a lot while preserving clean accuracy. But the attacks seem fairly standard and mostly single-target/single-trigger. In my experience, defenses that look strong there often crack under adaptive attacks that optimize to remain in-distribution in the SSL feature space. The discussion around this on p.6 is too brief. I might be wrong, but I think an adaptive attacker could poison examples so that the class-conditional density under the wrong label is not obviously low. Relatedly, the paper leans heavily on the feature extractor being semantically stable; that assumption is doing a lot of work.

Still, I think there is real value here. The “treat trigger as augmentation after restoring original labels” idea is clever, maybe the best part of the paper. Fig. 3 and Table 2 suggest this is not just filtering, which I appreciated. The empirical section is decent, though I would have liked confidence intervals and some reporting of how many samples were relabelled incorrectly.

Overall, I lean positive, but not strongly. Good idea, somewhat under-validated, and the writing could be sharper. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to better delimit the threat model and include stronger latent-space baselines.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than a lot of the recent “commonsense generation” submissions that mostly just bolt retrieval onto a seq2seq model and call it reasoning. The central idea — first imagine a scene knowledge graph, then verbalize conditioned on that graph — is intuitive, and I do think it addresses a real failure mode of current generators. The examples around Figure 1 and the qualitative cases later in the paper (I think page 6/7) make the motivation pretty clear: plain T5/BART-style models often produce locally fluent nonsense, while the proposed model at least tries to organize entities and relations before decoding.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some confusion about what exactly is learned versus injected from the curated resources. The paper emphasizes “generalizable reasoning about unseen concept compositions,” but much of the gain may simply come from richer structured supervision. That is not necessarily bad, but the framing oversells the reasoning angle a bit. I would have liked a cleaner decomposition: how much comes from the SKG intermediate representation itself, how much from the harmonized external resources, and how much from extra annotation effort? Table 2 hints at this, but not enough. A stronger ablation would remove or corrupt parts of the SKG supervision and test robustness. Right now the paper gives me the feeling of a carefully engineered pipeline rather than a fundamentally new reasoning mechanism.

I also wasn’t fully convinced by the evaluation. The automatic metrics in the main generation tables (Table 3/4, if I’m remembering correctly) are standard but pretty weak for this claim. Human evaluation is more relevant here, and those results are promising, but the protocol details are a bit thin. On page 8, the criteria for “common sense” judgments seemed underspecified. In my experience, annotators conflate plausibility, specificity, and fluency unless the instructions are very carefully designed.

One more issue: the graph generation component is treated almost as a latent explanatory object, but I’m not sure the paper demonstrates that the generated SKGs are genuinely interpretable beyond cherry-picked examples. I might be wrong, but Eq. (3)/(4) and the training objective read like a multitask setup with constrained decoding, not really explicit compositional inference.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper tackles an important problem and the method is sensible and reasonably well executed. But I’m not as convinced as the authors seem to be that this is a major step toward commonsense reasoning, as opposed to better structured conditioning. Borderline accept.

I read this paper with some interest because multi-character image animation is genuinely underexplored, and most prior work I’ve seen basically falls apart once there is occlusion plus a nontrivial background. The paper’s main idea — adding optical-flow-based background guidance, depth-order guidance, and a reference pose map to “enhance implicit decoupling” — is reasonable, and in a few of the qualitative results it does seem to help. Figure 1 and the later comparisons (I think around Fig. 5/6) do show cleaner separation between foreground characters than what I’d expect from a vanilla pose-guided diffusion/generative baseline. The new benchmark is also potentially useful. We need more datasets in this space.

That said, I found the paper somewhat frustrating to read. The central claim is “implicit decoupling,” but after reading Sections 3.1–3.3 twice, I’m still not fully convinced this is more than a collection of auxiliary inputs that bias the model toward easier decomposition. That may be fine! But the wording oversells the conceptual novelty. In particular, the optical flow guider on page 4 is described as letting the model decouple “background constants” and “background momentum,” and then zeroing momentum at inference yields a stable background. I might be wrong, but this sounds more like a heuristic operational interpretation than something the model is explicitly constrained to learn. Eq. (3)–(5) did not really clarify this for me.

The depth order guider is probably the most convincing part. Multi-person occlusion is hard, and encoding order information is sensible. But then I wanted stronger ablations. Table 2 shows gains from the full model, yes, but I was missing a more careful breakdown: depth order only on heavily occluded subsets, optical flow only on dynamic-background subsets, etc. Otherwise it’s hard to tell which module is doing the real work. Also, on the benchmark side, 4,000 frames is useful but still not large; the fairness claim felt a bit strong.

I also worry about dependence on preprocessing quality. If the flow or depth/order estimates are noisy, does performance collapse? The examples shown are relatively clean. Some discussion around failure cases (maybe the qualitative appendix, if any) would help. Right now the paper feels a bit curated.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here and the application setting matters. But the technical story is not as tight as it should be, and the empirical validation needs to be more surgical. I’m leaning borderline positive, though not enthusiastically.

I found this paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating to read. The core idea—giving a non-asymptotic regret guarantee for reward modeling from pairwise comparisons with deep nets—is timely, and I do think there is value in trying to move beyond the very stylized parametric analyses that dominate this area. In particular, the architecture-dependent bound in Theorem 3.1 (if I’m reading the numbering correctly; around p. 5–6) is potentially useful, because a lot of theory papers wave their hands about “expressive function classes” and never say anything concrete about the network. So on that front, I give the authors some credit.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not entirely sure what the main technical novelty is relative to existing empirical process / nonparametric classification analyses for pairwise comparison models. The reduction from regret to estimation error under a margin condition felt quite standard to me. The margin-type assumption introduced around p. 4 is sensible, yes, but also pretty strong, and the paper occasionally oversells it as an explanation for RLHF’s empirical success. I think that claim is too sweeping. Human annotators are noisy, inconsistent, context-dependent, often not “clear” in the sense required here. In my experience reading the RLHF literature, data quality matters, of course, but this theorem does not really “substantiate” practical efficiency in the broad way the abstract suggests.

I also had trouble parsing the setup. Equation (2) and the definition of the optimal action/policy around lines 120–140 seemed under-specified: is the action set finite, compact, or arbitrary measurable? This matters for how I interpret the regret. Figure 1 didn’t help much; honestly it felt decorative. Table 1 is nicer, but I would have liked a much sharper comparison to prior theory papers—what exactly is improved, and under what assumptions? Right now the related work section (p. 2) is too quick.

One thing I did appreciate is the estimator-agnostic message of the sharper bound under the margin condition (Theorem 4.2, I think, around p. 7). If true in the stated generality, that is neat. But then the paper should be much clearer about which parts depend on the deep network approximation result and which parts don’t. I kept flipping back and forth.

Overall, I lean borderline negative. There is some real substance here, but the presentation is murky, the assumptions are stronger than the paper admits, and the connection to practical RLHF is overstated. With clearer positioning and a more careful discussion of what is genuinely new, I’d be more positive.

I read this paper with interest because task-agnostic distillation for pretrained transformers is genuinely useful, and in my experience it is also where a lot of papers become hand-wavy very quickly. This one is better than that, but I still have some reservations.

The core idea — initialize from the teacher and then iteratively prune neurons while distilling — is intuitive and, honestly, kind of obvious in retrospect. I don’t mean that as a dismissal; sometimes the good ideas are exactly like that. What matters is whether the paper really shows that the “homotopic” path is the reason it works, rather than just a reasonably careful pruning + continued pretraining recipe. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I’m still not fully convinced the mechanism is isolated. Eq. (6) and the surrounding discussion on the discrepancy objective are plausible, but the paper leans a bit too hard on the narrative that smaller teacher-student prediction gaps “ensure” effective transfer. Ensure is too strong. I think “may facilitate” would be more accurate.

I liked Figure 2, which makes the procedure easy to follow, and Table 2 is probably the most important result in the paper. The gains over standard task-agnostic distillation baselines seem real. That said, I found the comparisons slightly selective. On page 6, the baselines are mostly classic KD variants; I would have liked stronger recent compression baselines, especially methods that also exploit structured pruning or progressive shrinking. Maybe I missed one, but the empirical positioning felt a little narrow.

A more concrete issue: the iterative pruning schedule is under-motivated. The choice of pruning ratio per stage, and how sensitive results are to the number of stages, seems central to the whole method. Table 4 helps, but not enough. If the method only works with a carefully tuned schedule, that matters a lot in practice. I also wasn’t entirely sure from page 5 whether the student’s architecture after pruning is hardware-friendly in the sense people usually mean; pruning width is better than unstructured sparsity, yes, but deployment claims should be backed by actual latency numbers, not just parameter/FLOP counts.

Still, I think the paper has merit. The writing is mostly clear, and the empirical improvements look meaningful. My overall feeling is positive but not enthusiastic: this is a solid idea with decent execution, though the novelty is somewhat incremental and the causal story is oversold. I would lean weak accept if the conference values practical model compression work; otherwise borderline.

I came away fairly skeptical of this paper, although I do think the underlying motivation is reasonable. The authors are trying to answer a real question — why NNs lag on heterogeneous tabular data, and whether one can build something more “universal.” That said, after reading the paper twice, I still don’t think the central claim is really supported, and in places the presentation is much fuzzier than it should be for a top conference submission.

The biggest issue is conceptual. The paper repeatedly markets TabNN as a “universal neural network solution,” but the actual method seems heavily powered by GBDT-derived structure/feature interactions (see the model construction discussion around p.3–4, especially Eq. (3) and the paragraph below it). At that point I honestly started wondering whether this is really a neural model with some tree-inspired inductive bias, or a tree pipeline wrapped in NN terminology. I’m not saying hybridization is bad — actually, in my experience these cross-paradigm methods can work very well — but the paper oversells the novelty and the generality. “Universal” is a very strong word. The experiments do not justify it.

I also found the empirical section underwhelming. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains on several datasets, but the baseline selection feels dated and incomplete. Where are stronger tabular DL baselines? Depending on the paper’s date, I would at least expect careful comparison to modern tabular architectures, not just generic MLP-style models and standard GBDT variants. Figure 2 is supposed to illustrate robustness, I think, but the variance/error bars are either missing or too hard to interpret. For tabular benchmarks, tiny differences often disappear with different splits or hyperparameter budgets. That matters a lot here.

Another problem is clarity. I found the description around Fig. 1 and the complexity argument on p.5 somewhat hand-wavy. The paper says it “reduces model complexity,” but I’m not entirely sure compared to what, exactly. Parameter count? Search space? Sample complexity? Computational cost? These are not interchangeable. The training procedure is also not described with enough precision for reproduction. I might be wrong, but several implementation choices seem buried or omitted.

There is something potentially useful here: using GBDT to induce feature combinations for a downstream NN is sensible, and probably practical. But as written, this feels more like an engineering recipe than a clean methodological advance. My overall take is borderline-to-negative. I would need a much more careful ablation study, stronger baselines, and a less inflated framing to be convinced.

I found the core idea interesting, but I’m not convinced the paper really nails either the conceptual contribution or the empirical case. The main claim — that decision-boundary exploration for attribution is “consistent with” transferable adversarial attacks — is intriguing, and honestly that connection is not crazy. In fact, when I first read the abstract I thought this might be a neat bridge between two fairly separate literatures. But after reading the method section twice, I still think the paper overstates what is actually shown.

The presentation is part of the problem. Around Sec. 3 (especially the discussion near Eq. (6)–(9)), the argument shifts between “exploring model parameters,” “exploring decision boundaries,” and “frequency-based perturbation of inputs” in a way that feels slippery. These are not the same thing. If the method is fundamentally an input perturbation scheme inspired by transfer attacks, then say that plainly. Calling it “model parameter exploration” seems a bit grandiose unless parameters are actually varied or sampled in a principled way. I might be missing something, but Fig. 2 did not fully clarify this for me.

On the empirical side, the paper has breadth, which I appreciate. There are many baselines and several datasets/models. Table 1 and Table 2 look strong at first glance, and the method seems competitive on insertion/deletion-style metrics and localization. But I have the usual concern: these metrics are not that reliable anymore, and the paper does not do enough to address their known failure modes. In my experience, if you want to claim better “explainability,” you need at least one more convincing sanity check. The model/randomization tests are either missing or too light (I may have overlooked them). Also, some visualizations in Fig. 4/5 look sharper, yes, but sharper is not always better. A few maps honestly look a bit edge-detector-ish.

I also wanted a clearer ablation. The frequency component seems important, but the paper doesn’t isolate whether the gains come from transfer-style perturbation, frequency manipulation, or just more aggressive smoothing/aggregation. The claim in the abstract that this is the “first time” such consistency is presented also feels risky. I would be careful there; there has been a lot of cross-pollination between adversarial sensitivity and attribution already.

Overall, I think there is a kernel of a good idea here, and the paper is not bad. But right now it feels more like a clever engineering combination than a clean scientific contribution. Weak accept at best, more likely borderline reject from me unless the rebuttal clears up the method/mechanism story substantially.

I read this paper with some interest because the “decouple perception from reasoning” idea is intuitively appealing, and honestly a lot of current LVLM work still muddles these two things together. The paper’s core proposal, ProReason, is to alternate proactive visual information gathering with reasoning, and to let an external LLM handle more of the “wisdom” part. I think this is a sensible direction, and in practice probably useful.

That said, I’m less convinced this is as novel or as cleanly justified as the paper suggests. After reading Section 3 twice, I still found the exact mechanism a bit slippery. The multi-run proactive perception loop seems close in spirit to iterative prompting / self-ask style decomposition, except here the queried object is the image rather than just the text. That is a nice reframing, but I’m not sure it rises to the level of a substantially new framework. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text (page 5, around the algorithm description) are not very precise about what terminates the loop and how “necessary and sufficient visual descriptions” are operationalized. Is this learned, heuristic, or prompt-defined? This matters.

I also had some concerns about the empirical story. The reported gains on MMMU are strong, especially the up to 15% with LLM assistance, but Table 2 and Table 3 make me wonder how much of the improvement is coming from better reasoning versus simply using a much stronger external language model. In other words, is ProReason the key contribution, or is the result mainly “plug GPT-4-like reasoning into a weaker LVLM and performance goes up”? That’s not uninteresting, but it should be framed more honestly. Figure 1 is conceptually clear, though a bit marketing-ish.

I did appreciate the breadth of benchmarks, and I think the paper is strongest when it documents failure modes of passive visual description approaches. Some of the examples on page 7 were convincing. Still, I wanted more ablations on cost and latency. Multi-run perception sounds expensive, and for real systems this is not a side issue. I might have missed it, but I did not see a serious accounting of inference-time overhead.

Overall, I think the paper has a good practical idea but is somewhat overclaimed. Borderline accept for me if the venue values system-oriented improvements; otherwise weak reject on novelty/clarity grounds.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit frustrating in equal measure. The core idea — modeling approximate Q-learning / SARSA with ε-greedy via a differential inclusion rather than a standard ODE — is, in my view, the right conceptual move. People have been hand-waving around the discontinuity from greedy improvement for years, and the paper is trying to formalize exactly that. I appreciated the motivation in the introduction, especially the discussion around policy oscillation and convergence to bad attractors. That part felt real, not just theorem-chasing.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper delivers on the “demystifying” claim. The main DI formulation (around Eq. (6)–(8), if I’m tracking correctly) is plausible, but the assumptions under which the stochastic iterates shadow the inclusion are scattered and, frankly, under-explained. I had to flip back and forth between the setup on p. 4 and the technical conditions on p. 7. Maybe this is just me, but I think many readers will struggle to understand what is actually new versus what is imported from standard stochastic approximation / set-valued dynamical systems results.

The examples are helpful. Figure 2 and Figure 3 do illustrate chattering and multiple attractors in a way that matches practitioners’ folklore. But they also feel a bit too curated. I would have liked at least one experiment where the DI analysis makes a genuinely non-obvious prediction that is then verified numerically, rather than mostly post-hoc explanation. Table 1 (assuming I’m reading the numbering right) was less useful to me than the phase portraits; the paper is strongest when it visualizes the geometry.

I also think the exposition around “worst policy convergence” is somewhat overstated. Yes, the phenomenon exists, but the paper occasionally slides from “can happen” to “is structurally explained” without really quantifying how generic it is. In my experience reviewing RL theory, this kind of language tends to age badly.

Still, there is real substance here. The technical framing is novel enough for a top venue, and I do think the paper will be of interest to people who care about the old Sutton-style approximation pathologies. My main reservation is clarity and whether the theoretical statements are as sharp as the narrative suggests. I’m leaning positive, but not by a lot.

I found the core idea reasonable and, honestly, fairly intuitive: don’t just randomize simulator parameters blindly, but maintain a set of environment configurations that induce diverse rollouts/failure modes, and train against those. In robotics RL this is a sensible direction, and I’ve seen adjacent ideas work well in practice. The paper is readable overall, but I came away a bit unconvinced that the contribution is as substantial as the authors claim.

The main mechanism around the “kernel-based summarization” is described around Eq. (3)–(5) and the algorithm box on p. 4. After reading this twice, I still think the presentation is too compressed. What exactly is being summarized—the trajectories, the environment parameter vectors, or some learned embedding of outcomes? The abstract says “configurations that lead to diverse behaviors,” which is the right intuition, but in the method section this gets slippery. Figure 2 helps a little, but not enough. I would have liked one concrete worked example with, say, 10 sampled environments and how the subset is selected.

I also worry that the empirical story is narrower than the framing. The paper repeatedly talks about sim-to-real and “real robots,” but unless I missed something, the experiments in Section 5 are fully in simulation. That’s not fatal, but then the claims should be toned down. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains in the harder settings, yes, but in the simpler cases the method mostly matches standard domain randomization, while presumably adding bookkeeping/hyperparameters/kernel choices. In my experience, these selection-based methods can be quite brittle to feature design, and I did not see enough ablation on the kernel, summary size, or refresh frequency. The appendix may have some of this, but it really belongs in the main paper.

Another issue: I’m not entirely sure the baseline setup is strong enough. On p. 6, lines around the training protocol, the comparison seems to be against uniform domain randomization. Why not compare to curriculum-style randomization or adversarial environment selection? Those are much closer conceptually. Without that, the paper risks beating a strawman.

That said, I do think there is signal here. The diverse failure mode argument is plausible, and Figure 4 is actually quite convincing qualitatively. If the authors can clarify the method and strengthen the experimental comparisons, this could become a solid paper. Right now I’m leaning borderline reject—not because the idea is bad, but because the evidence and exposition don’t quite meet top-conference bar.

I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating in places. The high-level idea—editing hidden representations by separating “language-agnostic” and “language-specific” subspaces—is genuinely appealing, and honestly more novel than the usual “just do more multilingual SFT” story. I especially liked the motivation in the first couple of pages. The authors are going after a real problem, and in my experience the off-target degradation / forgetting issue in multilingual adaptation is absolutely real, even if papers often underplay it.

That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as principled as the paper wants it to sound. Eq. (3)–(6) gives the impression of a clean geometric decomposition, but I could not tell whether this “language-agnostic vs language-specific” split is actually identifiable or just an operational definition induced by the chosen projections. Maybe I missed it, but around p.5 the text moves a little too fast from empirical observation to method. Figure 2 is intuitive, yes, but also a bit cartoonish relative to the actual claim.

The empirical results are good overall. Table 2 and Table 3 show fairly consistent gains, and I appreciate that the paper includes both an English-centric base model and multilingual ones. That makes the story more credible. The compute-efficiency angle is also useful, not just marketing. Still, I wanted more detail on variance/significance. Some of the gains are not huge, and without error bars or multiple seeds I’m not sure how robust these are. Table 4 in particular looks nice, but I don’t know if the comparisons are fully fair in terms of data volume and optimization budget.

My bigger concern is evaluation breadth. The paper repeatedly claims preservation of “central language capabilities,” but the evidence for this seems a bit narrow. If I’m reading p.7 correctly, this is mostly benchmark retention, not a broader check for instruction-following quality, safety behavior, or generation fluency. I might be wrong, but these hidden-space interventions can have odd side effects. I’ve seen this in related representation-editing work.

I also found the terminology slightly slippery. “Central language” is understandable, but it risks encoding a very English-default framing, especially since the method is supposed to be generally multilingual. Not fatal, just awkward.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a neat core idea, but the mechanistic claims are stronger than the evidence. I’d lean weak accept if the bar is novelty plus decent empirical support; weak reject if the venue is expecting a really airtight explanation of why the method works.

I found the core idea pretty intuitive and, honestly, somewhat appealing: use a running estimate of “hardness” to decide which examples deserve continued attention, instead of repeatedly spending compute on examples the model already handles well. In spirit this is not wildly new—there is a long line of work on curriculum/self-paced learning, forgetting events, online example mining, etc.—but I do think the particular emphasis on temporal stability of hardness is a reasonable angle.
