Figure 1 (if I’m reading it correctly) and the discussion around the early/late correlation of DIH are probably the most convincing parts of the paper.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails either the theory or the empirical story. The theoretical section around the multi-set function formulation and the approximation bound (I think this is around Eq. 6 onward) feels quite detached from what the actual algorithm is doing in practice. The assumptions needed seem strong, and I’m not entirely sure they correspond to modern DNN training dynamics in any meaningful sense. It reads a bit like “we wanted a theorem,” and the theorem is not useless, but I doubt it explains the observed gains. I would have preferred a more modest positioning there.

On the empirical side, the claim that DIH “does not require additional computation” is a little slippery. Yes, you avoid extra forward passes on skipped samples, but you are also making decisions based on stale hardness estimates because DIH is only updated for selected samples. This is actually a major algorithmic choice, and I didn’t see enough ablation on how harmful/helpful that staleness is. Table 2 and whatever ablation table covers selection ratio / warmup period should have gone deeper. In my experience, methods like this can look great with a carefully tuned keep-rate and then degrade badly elsewhere.

I also wanted more clarity on what “instantaneous hardness” exactly is in each experiment. Loss? Margin? Misclassification indicator? I think this is defined, but the presentation is a bit muddy around the method section. The paper would benefit from being more concrete earlier.

The results do seem promising, especially if the gains in efficiency and early convergence are robust across architectures. But I’m not yet sure the comparisons to baselines are fully fair—particularly if other curriculum methods were not equally tuned. Some plots looked nice, though.

Overall: interesting paper, decent empirical idea, but currently a bit overclaimed. I lean positive on novelty of the practical heuristic, weaker on theory, and medium confidence overall.

I found this paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating. The basic idea—doing actual statistical inference for RL quantities rather than just point estimation—is worthwhile, and honestly underexplored relative to how huge the RL literature has become. In that sense the paper is aimed at a real gap. I also appreciated that the authors try to connect asymptotic variance formulas to exploration design, instead of stopping at a purely asymptotic theory result. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the whole story hangs together as cleanly as the abstract suggests.

The technical core seems to be the asymptotic normality of the Q-value estimators and the closed-form variance expression (around Eq. (4)–(7), if I’m reading the notation correctly). I think this is potentially useful, but the presentation is quite dense and occasionally slippery. For instance, on p. 4 the assumptions behind the large-sample approximation are stated in a very compressed way, and I had to infer what is fixed and what is growing. Are we in a tabular finite-state finite-action setting throughout? I think yes, but then some of the claims later, especially around constructing confidence regions for optimal value functions on p. 5, read more broadly than the theory really supports. I might be wrong, but the jump from inference on Q under a given policy to inference on the optimal value seems nontrivial because of the max operator, and I did not see enough discussion of possible nonregularity issues.

I also found the exploration proposal a bit under-motivated. The policy based on “relative discrepancies among the Q estimates” (Sec. 4, and I think Figure 2 illustrates this) is intuitively sensible, but it feels like a heuristic piggybacking on the variance analysis rather than something with a sharp optimality guarantee. That’s okay, but then the empirical section should really carry more weight. Right now Table 1/Table 2 show gains over benchmarks, but the set of baselines is not especially convincing. In particular, I was surprised not to see stronger uncertainty-aware exploration baselines. In my experience, papers in this area can look much better than they are if compared only to simplistic methods.

There is definitely something here. I like the direction, and parts of the asymptotic analysis may be publishable on their own. But the paper currently overclaims a bit, and the empirical validation does not fully back the methodological ambition. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the camera-ready version substantially clarifies assumptions, tempers claims, and strengthens experiments.

I found the core idea pretty intuitive and, honestly, somewhat overdue: not all activations deserve the same precision, so learn to spend high precision only where it matters. In that sense the paper is easy to like. After reading it twice, I think the main contribution is less the quantization formulation itself and more the fact that the gating seems trainable end-to-end and apparently works across both CNNs and RNNs. Figure 1 communicates the intuition well. Table 1 and Table 3 are also reasonably convincing at first glance, especially the ImageNet comparison where the claimed 2.4× compute reduction versus prior prediction-based methods is nontrivial.

That said, I have a few reservations, and some are pretty important. First, the paper leans heavily on “compute reduction,” but the actual hardware story is a bit slippery. Around the method section (I think page 4, near Eq. 3–5), the gating mechanism itself introduces overhead, and I’m not convinced the accounting fully captures this in a realistic accelerator or even on commodity hardware. Is the reported cost just MAC-equivalent cost under assumed bit scaling? If so, fine, but then the claims should be toned down. In my experience, these precision-adaptive methods often look much better on paper than in deployment because of control-flow and memory overheads.

I was also left wanting more ablations. The fraction of high-precision features seems central, but the sensitivity analysis is thin. Figure 4 hints at the tradeoff, but I would have liked a more systematic sweep, maybe per layer or per stage in the CNN. Likewise, for the LSTM/PTB result, the 1.2% perplexity improvement over 8-bit quantization is interesting, but PTB is a small and somewhat tired benchmark. I’m not entirely sure this says much about modern language models.

Another issue is novelty. The paper distinguishes itself from prior prediction-based quantization, but conceptually it still lives in the same neighborhood: estimate importance, allocate precision accordingly. I think the authors do enough experimentally to justify publication discussion, but I would not oversell the conceptual leap.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar if the conference values practical efficiency work. But it needs a clearer statement of what is genuinely new, and much more honesty about the hardware assumptions. Right now the empirical results are promising, but the paper occasionally reads like it wants credit for deployment realism that it hasn’t really earned.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more relevant than a lot of “safe RL” submissions that only solve the easy version of transfer. The core idea — robustifying the source policy so that safety survives shifts in the target dynamics — makes sense, and I appreciate that the paper is trying to address the actual failure mode that happens in deployment rather than assuming away changes in safety-related dynamics. In that sense, the motivation in the abstract and early setup is strong.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the main guarantee. The theorem in Section 4 (I think around p. 5–6) leans very heavily on what the authors call a “mild assumption,” but I don’t think it is mild in practice. Maybe I’m being unfair, but any assumption that effectively requires the target environment shift to stay inside the uncertainty set used during robustification is doing a lot of work. If that is the case, then the guarantee is somewhat circular: safety transfers because the target is assumed to be captured by the robustness model. That’s not useless, but it should be stated more plainly.

I also found the exposition uneven. Equation (3) and the discussion around it were harder to parse than they needed to be, and Figure 1 gives the high-level story but not enough detail about what exactly is being robustified (transition kernel? constraint dynamics? both?). Later, Table 1 suggests consistent gains in safety violation rate, which is good, but I wanted more stress tests. The empirical section feels a bit narrow. If the claim is “robust transfer,” then two or three benchmark shifts are not really enough, at least in my experience reviewing RL robustness papers. I would have liked to see stronger out-of-distribution changes, and ideally some ablation on how conservative the robustification becomes. Does reward collapse? The paper hints no, but the evidence is thin.

There is still value here. I think the problem formulation is timely, and the paper is more serious than many safe transfer papers I’ve read recently. But the novelty is a little incremental relative to robust CMDP / domain-randomization-style ideas, just packaged for transfer. The authors need to do a better job on positioning and on clarifying what is genuinely new.

Overall I’m somewhere in the middle. Promising idea, decent theory, but the assumptions and experiments currently undersell the paper and also make me skeptical. I might be wrong, but I don’t yet see this as a top-tier acceptance without revision.

I read this paper with interest because KV-cache pruning/eviction has become a bit crowded lately, and a lot of papers really do boil down to “keep the tokens with large attention.” This submission is trying to put that on a more principled footing via output perturbation, and I do think that angle is potentially worthwhile. The core observation around Eq. (5)–(8) (if I’m reading the derivation correctly) — that the effect of dropping cache entries depends not just on attention weights but also on the value vectors and downstream projection parameters — is not shocking, but it is useful to state formally. In that sense the paper is better motivated than many systems-ish KV cache papers I’ve seen.

That said, I’m not entirely convinced the technical novelty matches the way the paper is pitched. The “first formal investigation” claim in the abstract feels overstated. There has already been a fair amount of work analyzing cache importance beyond raw attention, even if under different language. I might be wrong here, but the paper should calibrate this claim more carefully. Also, after reading Section 3 twice, I still found the optimization target somewhat slippery. The worst-case perturbation criterion sounds nice, but the actual selection rule introduced around Algorithm 1 and the approximation used to make it tractable felt like a fairly heuristic scoring function in the end. That is not fatal, but then the paper should be more candid about where the theory stops and the engineering begins.

The experiments are solid in breadth: 16 LongBench datasets is a reasonable spread, and Table 2 / Table 3 do suggest consistent gains when plugged into existing eviction schemes. The “up to 34%” additional savings is eye-catching, though as usual the average-case story matters more than the best case. I wish Figure 4 had clearer error bars or variance reporting; some of the margins look small enough that I’m not sure how stable they are across prompts/seeds. Also, I found the setup details a little thin on the actual runtime overhead. If your scoring depends on value states and parameter matrices, what is the practical latency cost at decode time? Page 6 hints that it is lightweight, but I wanted harder numbers.

One more issue: the paper keeps saying “maintaining the same generation quality,” but the results look more like “similar on average.” That wording should be toned down.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably above the acceptance bar, but not a clear standout. The main idea is sensible and empirically useful, but the theoretical framing is a bit stronger than what is actually delivered, and some practical details need tightening.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core idea — directly optimizing a sparse linear code through a differentiable factor-graph/BP surrogate — is interesting, and honestly it is the kind of thing people have been circling around for a while without quite formulating cleanly. So I do think there is novelty here, especially in the “complete graph tensor representation” described around Sec. 3 and Alg. 1. That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is as solid as the abstract claims.

My biggest issue is that the empirical story feels overstated. The abstract says “outperform… by orders of magnitude,” but when I looked at Fig. 4 and Table 2, the gains seemed highly dependent on block length and channel setting, and I could not tell whether the baselines were truly competitive. In particular, comparing against “popular codes” is not enough if those codes were not designed for the same short-length regime and same decoding budget. I might be wrong, but I think the paper really needs BCH / polar / modern short-blocklength LDPC baselines under matched BP or near-matched complexity. Right now the comparison feels a bit too convenient.

I also found the optimization details under-specified. Around Eq. (7)–(10), the paper moves from a continuous relaxation to a finite-field code construction, but the projection/rounding step is exactly where these methods often break, and here it is described too quickly. The line search in Sec. 4 sounded promising, but I could not reconstruct what objective is actually guaranteed to improve, if any. Is this optimizing BER proxy? syndrome consistency? a differentiable approximation to BLER? This matters. The text around page 6, lines 12–25, was especially confusing to me.

There is also a conceptual tension the paper does not fully confront: optimizing the factor graph “for BP” can absolutely produce structures that are decoder-specific and fragile. In my experience with learned decoders and code design papers, this often leads to gains that disappear under iteration mismatch, quantization, or slight channel shift. The robustness experiment in Fig. 5 is a start, but it is too limited. I wanted to see sensitivity to number of BP iterations, LLR clipping, and maybe a different channel from training.

I don’t want to sound overly negative. The paper is ambitious, and parts of it are genuinely exciting. But the current version feels more like a promising workshop paper than a top-conference one. If the claims were toned down and the experimental section were much more rigorous, I’d be more enthusiastic. As it stands, I lean weak reject.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of recent Video-LLM systems work. The core idea—persisting video KV caches and retrieving only relevant ones at question time—is sensible, and I can imagine this being useful in real deployments. In that sense, the paper is solving a real systems bottleneck rather than inventing yet another prompting trick. I appreciated the framing in the abstract and early method section that the goal is “training-free” integration with existing Video-LLMs. That part is appealing.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the technical novelty is very high. A lot of the method feels like a fairly direct combination of sliding-window attention, KV cache offloading, and retrieval over cached representations. Useful, yes. But conceptually new? I’m not entirely sure. The paper would benefit from being more upfront about what is borrowed versus what is actually new here. Right now it sometimes reads as if the combination itself is the contribution, which may be fine for a systems paper, but then the experimental evidence needs to be especially strong.

I also found some details under-specified. The retrieval mechanism is central, but the description (around the method section; I think this was near Eq. 3/4) felt too high-level. If the retriever can be “external” or based on Video-LLM parameters, then what exactly is used in each experiment? Are these frozen embeddings? Text-frame similarity? Hidden-state pooling? This matters a lot because the claimed efficiency/accuracy tradeoff depends on it. Figure 2 gives the pipeline at a high level, but it glosses over the actual indexing/granularity of the KV chunks. Page 5 (around the implementation discussion) also raised questions for me about RAM/disk transfer overheads: in practice, is the GPU reload latency hidden, or is it just shifted elsewhere?

The evaluation seems decent but not yet airtight. I wanted more ablations on retrieval recall versus QA accuracy, and especially failure cases. Table 2/3 (if I’m remembering the numbering correctly) show efficiency gains, but I would like to see end-to-end latency under realistic multi-query workloads, not just isolated components. Also, comparisons to stronger long-video baselines would help. In my experience, these systems papers can look great until you test them under messy access patterns.

Overall, I think this is promising and practically relevant, but the paper currently overclaims a bit on novelty and needs clearer methodological detail plus more convincing end-to-end evaluation. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the venue values practical systems contributions, otherwise weak reject.

I went into this paper somewhat positively because SPD networks are a niche but important area, and honestly a lot of architecture design there is still hand-crafted and pretty ad hoc. So the basic motivation made sense to me. I also appreciate that the authors are not just applying off-the-shelf DARTS language to a new domain, but trying to define what an “SPD cell” should even mean. Figure 2 and the discussion around the search space (I think around pp. 3–4) were helpful, and Table 1 gives at least some sense that the searched models are competitive and lighter.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as solid as it wants to be. My main issue is that the technical novelty feels narrower than the framing suggests. The core recipe is still supernet + continuous relaxation + differentiable architecture parameters. The domain-specific part is the search space, which is not trivial, but the paper sometimes oversells this as a fundamentally new NAS problem. I think it is more accurate to say this is a specialized NAS instantiation on SPD manifold networks. That’s still publishable maybe, but the distinction matters.

I also found the exposition pretty hard to follow in places. The formulation around the relaxed search space and the SPD constraints (Eq. 6–8, if I tracked correctly) is not explained carefully enough. I might be wrong, but it was not obvious to me that every mixed operation during supernet training preserves the SPD property without hidden caveats. This is crucial, not a side detail. If there is a theorem, it should be stated more cleanly; if not, that is a problem. On p. 5, the transition from discrete cell design to continuous optimization felt too fast.

The empirical section is decent but not fully convincing. There are several datasets/tasks, which I liked, but I wanted stronger ablations. For example, how much gain comes from the search space itself versus the differentiable search procedure? Also, comparison to “state-of-the-art NAS algorithms” in Table 3 is a bit slippery unless those methods are adapted carefully to the SPD setting with matched budgets. Search cost, variance across runs, and sensitivity to initialization all matter a lot in NAS. I did not see enough of that. Figure 4 looked nice, but visualizing discovered cells is not the same as understanding why they work.

Overall, I think the paper has a good idea and some promising results, but right now it feels under-justified mathematically and somewhat under-ablated experimentally. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject. If the authors tighten the SPD-preservation argument and make the comparisons more airtight, I’d be much more enthusiastic.

I found the paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating to read. The core idea—turning conditional moment restrictions into unconditional ones via importance weighting—is appealing and, at least at a high level, fairly natural. I’ve seen adjacent tricks in semiparametrics and off-policy evaluation, so the contribution here seems to be making this work cleanly for causal model learning under CMRs, plus the accompanying error analysis. That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper pins down what is genuinely new versus what is a repackaging of existing density-ratio / moment-restriction machinery.

The setup on p.2–3 is reasonable, but I think the exposition becomes slippery around Eq. (4) and the transition to the weighted unconditional restriction. The derivation is probably correct, but it is written in a way that hides the assumptions doing the real work. In particular, the overlap/support conditions seem crucial, yet they are not emphasized enough early on. I might be wrong, but it looks like the entire practical viability of the method hinges on estimating a conditional density ratio that could be very unstable in exactly the regimes where causal problems are hard. The theory later acknowledges this, but a bit too politely.

I did like the generality of allowing both parametric and nonparametric structural functions. The bound in Theorem 1 (p.5) is useful in spirit, though I found the statement cluttered and the proof sketch a bit hand-wavy. The dependence on the density ratio estimation error is unsurprising; what I wanted to know is whether this creates a meaningful advantage over directly solving the conditional moment problem with existing kernel/GMM-style methods. Table 1 and Figure 2 suggest decent empirical behavior, but the baselines felt weak. Why not compare to more serious CMR solvers, or at least stronger neural IV / adversarial GMM variants? As is, the experiments don’t really settle much.

There are also some writing issues. Figure 1 is too schematic to help. The notation changes around line 188 confused me. And honestly the paper could use a pass by a native English speaker; several claims are harder to parse than they need to be.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I can imagine this being useful, but the current version feels under-benchmarked and somewhat overclaims novelty. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can better clarify assumptions, sharpen the comparison to prior work, and strengthen the empirical section.

I read this paper with a fair amount of interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of “let’s remove the RNN” papers over the last few years, and most of them are either incremental or they quietly give up a lot in accuracy. This one is better than that. The basic idea in QANet — stacking depthwise separable convolutions with self-attention in the encoder — is actually pretty sensible for reading comprehension, and the empirical results are strong enough that I take the paper seriously.

What I liked most is that the paper is not just claiming speed in a vague way. Table 2 is useful, and the training/inference speedups are large enough to matter in practice, not just in a cherry-picked setting. I also think Figure 1 makes the architecture fairly easy to parse. After reading it twice, the overall model is straightforward, which is a compliment. The encoder block design on p. 4–5 is clean, and Eq. (2) / the contextual attention part is standard enough that the novelty is really in the encoder choice and how well it works.

That said, I found some parts frustratingly under-explained. The data augmentation via backtranslation is a huge part of the headline number in the abstract, but the method description later in the paper feels a bit thin relative to how much the result depends on it. I’m not entirely sure how robust that gain is, or whether it would hold under a stronger baseline with comparable augmentation. Table 5 helps, but I wanted more detail about filtering, noise, and failure modes. In my experience, synthetic QA data can help a lot or completely destabilize training depending on small choices.

I also have some reservations about how much of the gain is architectural versus engineering. The model uses several tricks at once: separable convs, self-attention, layer dropout/stochastic depth, augmentation, etc. The ablations in Table 4 are decent, but I still think the paper could do a better job isolating what is essential. For example, how much does self-attention buy over a stronger pure-convolution baseline at matched parameter count and wall-clock budget? I might have missed it, but I didn’t see that comparison made as directly as I wanted.

Still, the main claim — you can replace recurrent encoders in RC with conv + self-attention and get similar or better accuracy with much better speed — is convincing. That is a meaningful result, and frankly the field was overdue for it. I’d lean accept. Not because every experimental question is perfectly answered, but because this is one of the more practically relevant architecture papers I’ve seen in this area.

I went into this somewhat skeptical because “LLM generates robot tasks” papers can be pretty fluffy, but this is actually more substantial than I expected. The core idea — using an LLM not just to vary scenes but to generate task programs and curricula — is interesting and, frankly, timely. I also appreciate that the paper goes beyond toy examples and claims a 10x benchmark expansion to 100+ tasks. If that benchmark is actually usable by others, that alone could make the paper impactful.

That said, I had to read parts of the method section twice. The distinction between the goal-directed and exploratory modes is intuitive at a high level, but the actual pipeline was not always crisp to me. Around the method description (I think page 4–5), it was unclear where human filtering ends and autonomous generation begins. This matters a lot. If there is substantial manual verification or curation hidden in the loop, then the practical contribution is smaller than the framing suggests. Figure 2 helps, but I still wanted a more explicit accounting of failure rates: how many generated tasks are invalid, redundant, physically nonsensical, or just trivial? Table 1 gives scale, but not enough texture.

I also found the evaluation a bit uneven. The code-generation benchmark is useful, but I’m not entirely convinced it predicts downstream policy utility. In my experience, code correctness for simulator tasks is brittle in weird ways, and “passes syntax / compiles / runs” is far from “induces meaningful skill acquisition.” The real-world transfer result is the most exciting claim in the paper, especially the +25% over baselines, but I wanted stronger baselines and more detail on variance. Was this over 3 seeds? 5? The paper seemed a bit too eager to celebrate this number without fully stress-testing it. Also, if this is on a small set of long-horizon tasks, the result may be less general than the abstract implies.

Another thing: the paper sometimes attributes too much “grounding” ability to GPT-4. I might be wrong, but what’s really carrying the system may be the simulator API structure and the constrained program templates, not deep semantic grounding per se. That’s still valuable! But the rhetoric should be toned down.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably accept. The idea is strong, the engineering appears serious, and the benchmark expansion is potentially very useful. I’m not fully sold on all the causal claims, and I think the paper occasionally oversells autonomy, but there is enough here that I’d rather see it in the conference than not.

I found this paper ambitious, and in parts genuinely exciting, but also a bit over-claimed relative to what is actually demonstrated. The core idea — combining continuous flow matching for geometry with a discrete Markov bridge for atom/bond types — makes sense for this problem, and I do think the modeling direction is better aligned with 3D drug design than yet another autoregressive graph generator. Figure 1 gives a decent high-level picture, though I had to go back to Sections 3.1 and 3.2 twice to understand exactly how the continuous and discrete components are coupled during training versus only at sampling time. Equation (4) and the transition around Eq. (6) were especially dense; I’m still not fully sure whether the factorization assumption is principled or just convenient.

The empirical section is strong on breadth, maybe too strong. There are many claims: SOTA generation quality, OOD detection, preference alignment, and protein side-chain co-design. In my experience, when a paper tries to win on four fronts, one or two of them end up under-validated, and that happened here. The main generation results in Table 1 and Table 2 are good, yes, but I would have liked clearer significance or variance reporting. Some of the improvements are not huge, and with these datasets small setup differences matter a lot. Also, the comparison set feels selective. I might be wrong, but I expected stronger baselines around recent diffusion/flow hybrids for structure-based design.

The uncertainty/OOD part felt the least convincing. The paper says the uncertainty estimate “is able to detect out-of-distribution samples,” but from the discussion around Figure 4 and the corresponding appendix results, this looks more like a correlation story than a robust detection benchmark. I wanted AUROC/AUPRC against explicit OOD splits, not just qualitative separation. This is fixable, but right now it reads stronger than the evidence supports.

I did like the preference alignment idea in Section 4 more than I expected. It’s actually practical, and the fact that it can be applied to both the flow and bridge components is nice. But then again, I found the objective under-motivated. The text near page 8 (around Eq. 11 if I noted correctly) jumps quickly from “desirable metric values” to optimization, without really confronting reward hacking or mode collapse. Some generated examples in Figure 5 look suspiciously optimized for the reported metrics rather than chemically plausible — maybe unfair, but that was my reaction.

The side-chain extension is cool and probably the most novel part conceptually. Still, it feels bolted on in the last part of the paper. I wanted more ablation there: does joint modeling actually improve ligand quality, or is it mainly recovering protein flexibility metrics?

Overall, I lean positive on novelty and relevance, but I’m not fully sold on the completeness of the validation. Borderline accept for me, mostly because the method is interesting and timely, not because every claim landed.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantial than the average theory submission in this area. The main idea—using distributed computing lower bounds to derive limitations for message-passing GNNs—is clever, and I think the paper is onto something important. In particular, the negative results around depth/width tradeoffs feel nontrivial and potentially quite influential if they hold up. I also appreciated that the paper does not stop at a toy separation but tries to cover decision, optimization, and estimation problems. That gives the work some breadth.

That said, after reading it twice, I still think the presentation is harder than it needs to be. The setup around the Turing universality result in the first part (around p. 3–5) is a bit slippery. The theorem is technically impressive, sure, but I’m not entirely sure what practical or conceptual role it plays relative to the lower bounds, except maybe as a “yes, in principle these models are very powerful” contrast. If that is the point, I would have liked a more direct discussion. As written, the paper spends a lot of energy on universality and then pivots to impossibility, and the narrative connection is weaker than it should be.

The lower-bound technique itself is the strongest part. I thought the discussion around the simulation of local distributed algorithms (I think this is around Sec. 4, maybe Theorem 4.1 / 4.2 depending on numbering) was the core contribution. But some assumptions need to be stated more plainly. The dependence on node attributes and on the expressiveness of the layer update functions matters a lot, and I found myself flipping back to earlier definitions. Eq. (2) and the formal GNNmp model are doing a lot of work here; I would strongly suggest the authors make explicit which lower bounds survive under common practical instantiations like MPNNs with sum aggregation + MLPs.

I also wasn’t fully convinced by the framing of “simple” tasks being impossible without polynomial depth×width. Maybe I’m being unfair, but some of the tasks in the later tables (Table 1?) are simple only from one angle; from a locality/distributed-computing perspective they are exactly the sort of thing one expects to be hard. The paper should be more careful not to oversell.

Still, I think this is a good paper. The technical content seems real, the angle is fresh, and in my experience very few GNN theory papers manage to say something both general and nontrivial. My main ask is clarity: tighten the assumptions, improve the exposition, and better explain what is genuinely new versus imported from classical lower bounds. Assuming no hidden gap in the reductions, I lean positive.

I found this paper interesting but also somewhat uneven. The core idea — taking object-centric representations learned from static frames and then distilling explicit dynamics variables such as velocity into those slots — is sensible, and honestly a direction the community should have explored more. Too many object-centric papers stop at “nice segmentation masks” and never really test whether the representation supports physical reasoning. So I appreciate the motivation here.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as novel or as cleanly justified as the paper suggests. The main mechanism around dynamics distillation (Eq. 3–5, if I’m reading correctly) feels like a fairly natural temporal extension of slot/object-centric encoders plus relation modeling. The relation module especially looked standard to me; I didn’t see much beyond the usual pairwise interaction setup. Maybe I’m being harsh, but the paper sometimes presents fairly incremental design choices as if they are conceptual breakthroughs.

I did like the empirical scope. Evaluating on both video reasoning and video prediction is the right thing to do, and Table 1 / Table 2 show meaningful gains over prior object-centric baselines. The physical reasoning results are probably the strongest part of the paper. Figure 4 was also helpful qualitatively; you can actually see that the learned decomposition is more stable across time than some previous methods. In my experience, temporal consistency is where many slot-based models quietly fail, so this matters.

Still, the paper is often frustratingly vague where it should be precise. On p.4–5, the description of how “static representations” are converted into explicit dynamics is not very transparent. Are these dynamics supervised in any way, or entirely latent and only constrained through reconstruction/reasoning losses? I think it is the latter, but the wording is slippery. Also, the claim that the model “distillates explicit object dynamics” is too strong unless there is stronger evidence that the variables correspond to physically meaningful quantities rather than just useful hidden states. Some ablation around identifiability or correlation with true velocity would help a lot.

I also wish the paper did more to separate the contribution of the dynamics module from simply using more temporal context. The ablations in Table 3 are a start, but not enough. A stronger sequential baseline with comparable capacity seems necessary. Right now, I’m not entirely sure whether the gains come from “explicit dynamics” per se or just better temporal modeling.

Overall, I think the paper has merit and the results are solid, but the presentation overclaims and the novelty is moderate. I’m leaning weak accept / borderline, mostly because the task setup is meaningful and the empirical improvements look real, even if the conceptual contribution is less sharp than advertised.

I went into this paper somewhat positively because robustness of sEMG recognition under realistic noise is actually an important problem, and too many papers in this area report clean-condition results that do not survive contact with the real world. The paper’s central claim — that emphasizing short-term structure via the proposed STET helps both classification and regression under noisy conditions — is plausible, and in parts the empirical section is genuinely interesting. I liked the breadth of evaluation on the large public dataset, especially the inclusion of single-finger, multi-finger, wrist, and rest gestures. Figure 1 also does a decent job motivating why local temporal perturbations matter, although I found the caption a bit vague.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper is not as clear or as convincing as it needs to be. The method description around Eq. (6)–(9) is hard to follow. I understand there is a short-term enhancement module inserted before/within the Transformer blocks, but the exact inductive bias is not crisply isolated. Is this mostly a local temporal convolutional prior? A windowed attention variant? Some kind of feature reweighting? The paper gestures at all three. Figure 3 should have made this obvious, but honestly it didn’t. I might be missing something, but if the contribution is “short-term enhancement,” then the ablation in Table 4 should more explicitly separate short temporal context from model capacity and preprocessing.

The experimental story is also a bit too favorable in a way that made me suspicious. The claim in the abstract that the impact of noise is reduced by “more than 20%” sounds strong, but on page 7 the exact definition of this reduction is not stated carefully enough. Relative reduction in error? Absolute drop in performance under corruption? Averaged over noise types? These are not interchangeable. I also wanted more detail on how noise is injected. The discussion around page 6 / Table 2 suggests several common noise scenarios, but I could not tell whether these are physiologically realistic contamination processes or generic signal perturbations chosen for convenience. In my experience with biosignal work, this matters a lot.

There are also presentation issues. The writing is rough throughout (“3D Interactive Scene” in the abstract is odd, and there are many grammatical errors). That alone is not fatal, but here it contributes to ambiguity. I do think there is a potentially useful idea here, and the regression results are a nice touch, but the paper currently overclaims relative to what is rigorously demonstrated. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can substantially clarify the method and make the noise protocol much more defensible.

I read this paper with some interest because finite-horizon policy optimization is one of those topics that everyone says is “classical” and then, somehow, the actual optimization theory is much thinner than for discounted infinite-horizon settings. The main idea here — training softmax policy parameters backward in time, in a way that mirrors dynamic programming — is sensible and, honestly, a bit overdue. I think the paper’s core contribution is the convergence comparison between simultaneous policy gradient and this “dynamical policy gradient” (DPG), especially in the sampled-gradient setting. That part feels potentially useful.

That said, I had a somewhat mixed reaction. The high-level message is clear from the abstract and early setup, but the technical presentation is harder to follow than it should be. Around the main results (I’m referring in particular to the theorem statements in the middle sections, around the exact-gradient and stochastic-gradient analyses), I found myself flipping back repeatedly to the notation. There are just too many value-like quantities and time-indexed constants introduced without enough intuition. Maybe this is inevitable, but after reading this twice I still found the proof flow a bit opaque. Equation numbering is used heavily, but the surrounding explanations are sparse. In particular, the role of the softmax geometry in the convergence constants is not explained very cleanly; I might be wrong, but some of the dependence looked more like a proof artifact than a real phenomenon.

I did like the comparison between simultaneous and backward training. If I understood Table 1 correctly, the dependence on horizon is substantially improved for the dynamic variant, and that is exactly the kind of result one would hope for. But I wanted a sharper discussion of whether this is just “better constants” or a genuinely different scaling regime. The paper hints at the latter, but does not quite sell it. Also, the sampled-gradient section seems important, yet the assumptions there felt a bit sanitized. Are these unbiased gradient estimators realistic in the finite-horizon RL settings motivating the paper (e.g., RLHF as mentioned in the abstract)? I’m skeptical.

Another issue: the paper is very theory-only, which is okay in principle, but then the writing has to do more work. A tiny tabular experiment would have helped, even just to illustrate the backward-training effect. Figure-wise, I kept expecting at least a schematic of simultaneous vs backward updates; unless I missed it, there wasn’t one, and I think that hurts accessibility.

Overall, I think there is a real contribution here, and it is more interesting than many incremental policy-gradient theory papers I’ve seen. But the presentation is dense and the practical implications are overstated a bit. My leaning is borderline positive if the conference values finite-horizon RL theory, otherwise borderline reject.

I had mixed feelings about this paper. The high-level idea in SAP — learn a local score over state-action regions, aggregate it to estimate trajectory quality, and combine with a learned dynamics model for zero-shot planning — is interesting, and I do think the problem is important. In particular, the attempt to use arbitrary-quality offline interaction rather than expert data is appealing. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is either as general or as well-justified as the authors claim.

The core decomposition around the score function (Eq. 3, if I’m reading the notation correctly) is intuitive, but the paper slides past a lot of assumptions. Why should local regional scores aggregate into something meaningful for long-horizon behavior, especially in tasks with delayed effects or strong action dependencies? The text around p.4–5 gestures at “task-agnostic semantics and dynamics priors,” but this feels more like a slogan than a demonstrated property. I kept waiting for either a theorem or at least a failure-mode discussion. Figure 2 gives the pipeline, but it is too cartoonish to answer the real question, which is where the generalization is actually coming from.

The experimental section is broad, yes, but oddly unsatisfying. Table 1 and Table 2 show gains over baselines, sometimes substantial, but I’m not sure the comparisons are entirely fair. Several baselines seem much weaker than what I would expect in 2025 for offline RL / model-based planning. Maybe I missed it, but I didn’t see enough tuning detail to know whether the baselines were put on equal footing. Also, in the robotics results (I think p.7, around Fig. 4), the paper emphasizes zero-shot transfer, but the train-test task relationship is not clearly characterized. How novel are the unseen tasks really? This matters a lot.

Another issue: the claim that sparse rewards are enough is a bit overstated. The method still uses reward-labeled interactions to shape the score, and the amount/distribution of reward signal across tasks seems crucial. I would have liked an ablation on reward sparsity, data quality, and especially the aggregation operator. Right now SAP feels like a bundle of design choices with limited disentanglement. The ablations in Table 3 help, but not enough.

I do think there is something promising here. The paper is not bad. But in its current form, it overclaims and under-explains. My leaning is weak reject: interesting direction, decent empirical effort, but the conceptual contribution is still too murky for a top conference.

I read this with some interest because I’ve followed the “RL as sequence modeling” line pretty closely, and I’ve also reviewed a lot of MCTS papers over the years. The basic idea here — using a Transformer to guide/evaluate search while MCTS handles lookahead over previously seen states — is sensible, and for SameGame it’s not crazy at all. In fact, I’m a little surprised more people haven’t tried exactly this hybridization in a cleaner offline-RL framing.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper as written. My main issue is that the novelty feels narrower than the presentation suggests. By page 3, around the actor-critic description, the method sounded to me less like a fundamentally new offline RL framework and more like a search-policy/value hybrid with a Transformer function approximator. That is not necessarily bad, but the framing is oversold. I also found the offline RL terminology somewhat slippery. SameGame is a deterministic puzzle domain, and although one can cast it as offline RL, this is pretty far from the standard setting people in offline RL will have in mind. I think the paper would be stronger if it just admitted that and positioned itself more honestly.

There are also some technical details that I had to reread. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text on page 5 were confusing to me: it was not clear whether the Transformer is predicting action priors only, state values only, or both depending on search depth. Maybe I’m missing something, but the interaction between the critic estimate and the rollout/evaluation budget is underspecified. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough. I also wanted a cleaner statement of what “previously-explored states” means in the offline setting. Is the tree restricted to dataset support, or can the search branch into model-implied but unseen states? This matters a lot.

The empirical section is promising but a bit thin. Table 1 shows gains, yes, but I really wanted stronger ablations: number of node evaluations, effect of Transformer size, and what happens if you replace the Transformer with a simpler learned heuristic. Right now the claim that the method achieves “more efficient sampling” is not fully nailed down. Efficiency relative to what exactly? Wall-clock? node expansions? training data? I’m not entirely sure. Also, benchmarking only on SameGame makes the paper feel narrow. Good result, but one domain.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I liked parts of it. But in its current form I see this as borderline: interesting hybrid method, weak positioning, and not enough evidence for the broader claims. My inclination is weak reject unless the camera-ready version substantially clarifies the method and adds stronger ablations/generalization evidence.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit overdue as a topic. MCES is one of those things everyone in RL “knows” from Sutton & Barto, but the convergence story is murky, and the paper is right to focus on the original algorithm rather than the Tsitsiklis-style equal-rate modification. So I was positively inclined going in.

The main result, as I understand it, is an almost sure convergence proof for original MCES on what the authors call Optimal Policy Feed-Forward MDPs. The proof strategy is the most appealing part of the paper. The inductive argument laid out around the main theorem (I think this is in Section 4, around Theorem 1 / pages 5–7) is much cleaner than I expected. In particular, reducing the argument to repeated applications of the strong law rather than heavy stochastic approximation machinery is elegant. I can imagine this being pedagogically useful too, not just technically correct.

That said, I’m less convinced by the scope and by how the class of MDPs is motivated. The definition of “states are not revisited within any episode when using an optimal policy” sounds simple, but after reading it twice I still wasn’t entirely sure what is ruled in/out when there are multiple optimal policies, or when suboptimal policies can revisit states a lot. The abstract claims this includes “all deterministic environments,” which struck me as too broad unless there is some episodicity assumption hiding somewhere. A deterministic MDP can certainly have cycles. Maybe the intended meaning is deterministic episodic environments with no revisitation under some optimal policy? If so, this needs to be stated much more carefully. I would strongly suggest tightening the statement around page 2 where the examples are introduced.

I also thought the paper could do a better job situating itself relative to Bertsekas & Tsitsiklis (1996) and Tsitsiklis (2002). The contrast is there, but a sharper explanation of exactly which obstacle in the classical counterexample is avoided by the feed-forward assumption would help. Right now the theorem is clear-ish, but the intuition is not as clear as it should be.

I might be wrong, but I also wanted at least one concrete worked example or toy MDP illustration of the induction over decision stages. A figure or even a small table would help. For a theory paper this is not mandatory, but here it would materially improve readability.

Overall: solid technical contribution, narrower than the abstract initially suggests, but worthwhile. I lean accept if the claims are toned down and the exposition is tightened.

I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than most “physics-informed but structure-agnostic” submissions in this area. The core claim — that FINDE can both discover and preserve first integrals without assuming the geometric structure in advance — is important if true. In particular, the combination of a learned invariant with a projection/discrete-gradient style correction seems sensible. I liked the motivation in the intro, and Fig. 1 helped clarify the high-level idea.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates “finding” an invariant from “imposing” one. Around Eq. (6)–(9), the learned quantity feels underconstrained: what exactly prevents the network from converging to trivial or nearly constant functions that are easy to preserve but not scientifically meaningful? I think the discussion near p. 4 is trying to address this, but it’s pretty hand-wavy. The examples show quantities “consistent with” known first integrals, but that phrase is doing a lot of work. Are these identifiable up to affine transforms only? Monotone transforms? Products with constants? This matters a lot for the discovery claim.

I also had some concerns about the empirical section. Table 1 and Table 2 show better long-horizon prediction than baselines, which is good, but I wanted stronger ablations. How much comes from projection alone versus the discrete gradient component versus the particular parameterization of the invariant network? Right now the method is a bundle. In my experience, these corrections can stabilize trajectories almost regardless of whether the learned conserved quantity is “right.” Fig. 3 looks nice, but I would have liked error bars or multiple seeds; the current plots look very single-run-ish. Maybe I missed it.

Another issue is scope. The systems considered are fairly standard benchmark dynamical systems. That’s fine, but the paper sometimes overstates generality. I’m not entirely sure how this would behave with noisy, partially observed, or stiff systems, which is where “unknown structure” actually becomes painful. The claim in the abstract that it works “in a unified manner” felt too strong given the evidence.

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The idea is better than the presentation. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to clarify identifiability, add stronger ablations, and be more precise about what “finding” an invariant means. My leaning is weak accept / borderline, mostly because the problem is important and the approach seems promising, but I’m not yet fully sold by the current experimental support.

I found the core idea interesting and, honestly, more sensible than a lot of “compression” papers that just stack heuristics and hope for the best. The paper’s main observation — that layer activations live in a lower-dimensional subspace even when the weights themselves are not low-rank — is plausible, and the derivation of the data-aware factorization around Eq. (4)–(7) is probably the strongest part of the submission. I also appreciated that the method does not require retraining. In practice that matters a lot more than authors sometimes admit.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m not fully convinced by either the novelty claim or the experimental story. The method is presented as “provably optimal,” but the optimality is with respect to a fairly specific objective defined on the empirical representation covariance (Sec. 3, around page 4). That is not the same as saying it is the best compression in any broader deployment sense. I think the wording oversells this. It is a good closed-form projection-based approximation, yes. “Provably optimal” in the abstract feels a bit too strong.

I also struggled with the exposition in the self-attention case. The extension described on page 5 is compact to the point of being hard to verify. Are Q, K, V all compressed with separate subspaces estimated from their own inputs, or is there sharing? Figure 2 hints at one thing, but the text seems to suggest another. I might be wrong, but I think this needs to be clarified because the practical cost depends on it.

The empirical section is decent but not as complete as it should be for a top conference. Table 2 and Table 3 report task accuracy and speedup, but there is very little detail on the actual hardware/software setup. In compression papers, “1.92x faster” can mean many things. Batch size? Sequence length? CPU vs GPU? Kernel fusion? Without that, I have a hard time interpreting the latency numbers. Also, MRPC is tiny and noisy; emphasizing a 1.5% drop there is not that informative. I would have liked to see stronger ablations on rank selection, sensitivity to the calibration data size, and layerwise compression behavior. Figure 3 is suggestive, but it stops short of explaining when the method fails.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it is cleaner than average. But the paper is a bit too eager in its claims and a bit too thin experimentally. Borderline for me; I lean weak reject unless the camera-ready version would substantially tighten the presentation and add more convincing evaluation.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than most fairness papers I’ve seen lately. The core idea—doing sensitivity analysis for causal fairness under unobserved confounding—is important, and I do think the paper is aiming at a real gap. In practice, people absolutely make fairness claims from observational data while quietly assuming away hidden confounding, so the problem setup resonates.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as clear or as airtight as it should be for a top venue. The framing around “different sources of observed confounding” versus “unobserved confounding” was oddly hard to parse early on (around pp. 2–4). I think the notation in the formal setup, especially around the fairness estimands and the sensitivity parameterization, becomes overloaded quickly. Equation (3) and the discussion right after it seemed to be doing a lot of work, but I had to backtrack several times to understand what exactly is being bounded and under which assumptions. Maybe this is just me, but the exposition needs tightening.

I did like the theoretical direction. The bounds are potentially useful, and I appreciated that the authors are not pretending to identify causal fairness exactly when hidden confounding exists. That’s the right instinct. But I’m not entirely sure the guarantees are as practically meaningful as the paper suggests. A lot seems to depend on the choice/range of the sensitivity model, and I wanted a much more grounded discussion of how a practitioner should set that parameter. Without that, “worst-case guarantees” can become a bit rhetorical. Figure 2 helped intuitively, but I wanted something more operational, maybe tied to domain knowledge or benchmarked against known confounding strengths.

The neural method is reasonable, though not especially exciting algorithmically. To be blunt, the conceptual contribution is much stronger than the model itself. I also worry the experimental section oversells things. The prison-sentencing case study is timely, but these applications are exactly where assumptions should be scrutinized hardest, and I found the treatment somewhat breezy. Table 1 and the real-data results later on show patterns, yes, but I could not tell whether the gains are robust or partly an artifact of modeling choices. More ablations would help. In particular, how sensitive are the fairness bounds and learned predictors to misspecification of the sensitivity parameter or network architecture?

Overall, I think this is a serious paper with a worthwhile problem and some novel ingredients. But for me, the current version is not yet fully persuasive: strong idea, uneven exposition, and the empirical validation doesn’t completely close the loop. I’m leaning weak accept / borderline, mostly on significance.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more thought-provoking than I expected from the title. The core question—whether a model that behaves correctly can also verbally explain the rule it is using—is important, and I like that the authors try to make it testable with a synthetic benchmark rather than just philosophical discussion. The ArticulateRules setup is clean, and Figure 1 / the examples early in the paper do a good job making the task intuitive. In particular, I appreciated the attempt to separate classification competence from explanation competence. That distinction is often hand-waved in this area.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m not fully convinced the paper gets all the way to “LLMs struggle to explain themselves” rather than “LLMs struggle to reverse-engineer and verbalize latent task rules from behavioral evidence.” Those are related, but not identical. The models here are not necessarily explaining an internal process; they may just be inferring the task specification from demonstrations and then paraphrasing it. The paper gestures at this caveat, but I think it is more central than the framing suggests. Around the discussion on p. 3–4, I wanted a much sharper statement of what exactly is being measured.

I also had questions about the evaluation of free-form explanations. The abstract says the rules provide ground-truth explanations, but natural language equivalence is messy. On the pages describing scoring (I think around the methodology section, with the exact-match / rule-matching procedure), I wasn’t entirely sure how robust this is to valid paraphrases, partial explanations, or explanations that are behaviorally equivalent but phrased differently. If Table 2 is the main articulation result, I would really like to see more error analysis there. A few examples are not enough.

The GPT-3 vs GPT-4 jump is striking, maybe too striking. My immediate reaction was contamination or prior exposure to similar instruction-following data, though I might be wrong. The finetuning result is also interesting, but I wanted more detail on training setup and variance across seeds; otherwise “completely fails on 7/10 rules” sounds dramatic but maybe brittle.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper with a neat benchmark, and I can imagine it being useful to others. But the claim needs tightening, and the evaluation story needs more care. Right now I’m positive on the dataset contribution, somewhat skeptical on the interpretation, and not totally sold on the broader conclusion.

I read this paper with some interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of stereo papers over the years, and every few cycles someone argues that “deeper is not better” for correspondence. Sometimes that is true. Here, the core claim is that a very shallow feature extractor plus ASPP gives better structural detail for stereo than a standard deeper backbone. I think the intuition is reasonable, and in fact fairly plausible for low-level matching. But I also found the paper a bit overstated relative to what is actually demonstrated.

The main idea seems to be concentrated in Sec. 3, especially the architecture around Fig. 2 and the discussion near Eq. (3)–(5). A three-layer primary extractor, followed by ASPP and a fusion block, is simple and easy to understand. That simplicity is honestly a plus. I also appreciated that the authors try to plug the module into an existing stereo pipeline rather than inventing an entirely new system. In practice that is often the right way to test such a claim.

That said, I’m not entirely convinced by the “state-of-the-art” framing in the abstract. Table 2 (if I’m reading it correctly) seems to compare mostly against a limited set of baselines, and I didn’t see a serious comparison to the strongest contemporary stereo methods under matched training settings. Maybe I missed one appendix detail, but after reading this twice, the experimental protocol still felt under-specified. For example, on the KITTI 2015 result: was the gain due to the feature extractor itself, or due to changed training, regularization, or cost-volume settings? The paper says the feature extraction part was replaced, but the isolation is not as clean as it should be.

I also found the receptive field argument slightly hand-wavy. Yes, dilated convolutions enlarge receptive field, but they also can introduce gridding artifacts; stereo is particularly sensitive to local consistency. I expected a more direct analysis here, maybe some error breakdown in reflective/thin-structure regions, not just aggregate numbers. Fig. 4 qualitatively looks okay, but it is not very diagnostic.

The writing is serviceable but rough in places. Some claims around p. 5–6 are stronger than the evidence supports. Still, there is a decent paper hiding here: the module is lightweight, the idea is not crazy, and the parameter reduction is meaningful. I just think the empirical case needs to be tighter and the novelty is somewhat incremental. My leaning is borderline reject, though I could be persuaded if the authors clarified the comparisons and added stronger ablations.

I read this paper with interest because the “clean-label” angle is actually important, and in my experience a lot of backdoor papers quietly assume a pretty unrealistic data-poisoning capability. Here the authors are explicitly trying to remove that crutch. I think that is the main contribution, and it is a meaningful one.

The basic idea, as introduced around p. 2–3, is clever: use adversarial perturbations to pull selected source-class training points toward the target representation, then optionally use GAN blending so the poisons still look semantically correct to a human. Figure 1 communicates the setup reasonably well, although I had to read the caption twice to understand exactly which images are poisoned and which are trigger-bearing test examples. The attack itself is conceptually simple, which I mean as a compliment.

That said, I found the presentation uneven. Equation (1) is central, but the notation is a bit underexplained; the feature collision objective is intuitive if you already know the adversarial examples literature, but I’m not sure a broader ML audience will immediately follow all the constraints and assumptions. Also, the threat model on p. 3 is stronger than the paper sometimes admits. The attacker seems to need access to the victim architecture or at least a very similar surrogate, plus enough control to insert carefully optimized poisons. Yes, the labels remain correct, but this is not exactly a “weak” attacker.

The empirical results are interesting, especially the transfer-learning setting in Table 1 / Table 2, where the attack appears much more effective. Honestly, this makes sense: fixed feature extractors are much easier to manipulate with feature collision poisons. The end-to-end setting is less convincing. If I’m reading Figure 5 correctly, success rates drop a lot unless the trigger is fairly conspicuous or the poisoning budget increases. That doesn’t kill the paper, but it does narrow the practical impact. I also wanted more discussion of detectability beyond “looks fine to humans.” Human inspection is a very low bar. What about nearest-neighbor checks, influence functions, spectral signatures—well, some of those may postdate this exact framing, but still, even simple embedding outlier tests would have been useful.

Overall I think the paper is novel and worth attention. I’m somewhat positive on acceptance because the clean-label formulation is a real step forward, even if the attack is not universally strong. But I do wish the paper were a bit more honest about where it really works best: transfer learning, limited defenses, and fairly tailored optimization.

I had to read this twice to be sure I understood what the paper is actually claiming, and I’m still not entirely sure I do. The core idea—learn hybrid discrete + continuous latent codes by maximizing mutual information between x and z, with a decoder trained to approximate p(x|z)—is interesting, and I do think there is a real gap in the literature around principled joint discrete/continuous unsupervised representations. In that sense, the paper is aiming at something worthwhile. But the presentation made the contribution feel more abstract than it needed to be.

What bothered me most is that the “theoretically valid and principled framework” language in the abstract feels stronger than what is actually established. Around the derivation of the objective (I think this is in the section introducing the decomposition, roughly around Eq. 4–7), the paper seems to lean heavily on variational approximations and factorization assumptions. That’s fine, but then the conclusions about disentanglement and tradeoffs are more suggestive than proven. I might be wrong here, but I did not see a convincing argument that maximizing the proposed MI surrogate leads to disentanglement in any meaningful sense beyond the usual hope that bottlenecks/factorization help. A lot of papers make this leap, and I’m skeptical every time.

I also found the role of the decoder a bit confusing. The paper positions itself against the VAE perspective, but in practice there is still a learned conditional model and a variational flavor to the training. So the novelty seems to be more in the objective framing than in the actual learning machinery. That’s not necessarily bad, but the paper should be more candid about this. Right now it reads a little like “not a VAE,” while much of the machinery is adjacent.

The empirical section is decent but not especially convincing. The figures/tables showing learned discrete and continuous factors (I assume the main qualitative figure is early in the experiments section, maybe Fig. 2 or 3) are suggestive, but I wanted stronger baselines and ablations. In particular, what happens if one removes the discrete part, or fixes the MI weights differently? Table results without a serious comparison to strong hybrid-latent baselines make it hard to know whether this is progress or just another objective that sort of works on standard toy-ish benchmarks. In my experience reviewing disentanglement papers, this is where many submissions overclaim.

Overall, I think there is an interesting idea here, but the paper currently oversells the theory and undersupports the empirical case. Borderline for me, leaning reject unless the authors can sharpen the claims and clarify exactly what is genuinely new.

I found the paper interesting in intent, but pretty uneven in execution. The basic idea — “let an LLM talk to an embedding” — is intuitively appealing, and I can imagine this being useful for analysis workflows. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the authors have really shown a principled method rather than a collection of prompting demos.

The setup on p.3–4 is a bit too hand-wavy for my taste. The “embedding injection” mechanism is described as if it is the key technical contribution, but I had trouble understanding exactly what is learned, what is frozen, and what information is actually accessible to the language model. Eq. (1) and the surrounding text seem to suggest a projection into the LLM token space, but the implementation details are thin, and the distinction between direct decoding versus retrieval-like conditioning remains blurry. I might be wrong, but this matters a lot, because otherwise the method risks being just a sophisticated prompting interface over metadata.

The examples are sometimes compelling, especially the user preference/recommender case. Figure 3 gives the kind of qualitative result that makes you think “okay, maybe there is something here.” But the evaluation is weak. Table 2 reports what looks like human preference or judged interpretability, yet I could not find a convincing protocol for annotator blinding or inter-rater agreement. That’s a serious omission for a paper whose core claim is improved interpretability. Also, on the CAV section (around p.5), I was expecting comparison to standard concept bottleneck / TCAV-style analyses beyond a few anecdotes. Instead, the paper mostly shows that the model can produce plausible-sounding textual descriptions. In my experience, that is exactly where LLMs are dangerous: they are very good at sounding semantically aligned while being only loosely grounded.

I also think the novelty is overstated. There has been a lot of recent work on probing embeddings with generative models, natural-language explanations of latent features, and even recommender profile verbalization. The related work section mentions some of this, but too briefly and somewhat selectively.

That said, I do like the ambition. If the authors tightened the method section, added more rigorous faithfulness evaluations, and included failure cases (please!), I’d be much more positive. Right now I’m leaning borderline reject. The paper has a neat premise, but it feels premature for a top ML venue.

I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more serious than a lot of recent “verification for LLMs” submissions that are really only about toy classifiers with a transformer-ish wrapper. The core idea — using a non-convex set representation to preserve dependencies across attention computations instead of immediately collapsing everything into a loose convex relaxation — makes sense to me, and I think the motivation on p. 2 is well stated. Figure 1 helped. The contrast between independent head-wise relaxation and the proposed dependency-preserving propagation is intuitive there, even if the notation later gets fairly dense.

That said, I struggled with the presentation in the middle sections. I had to read the derivation around Eq. (6)–(9) twice, and I’m still not entirely sure I understand what is guaranteed versus what is approximated heuristically. The paper says the method is “formally sound,” but the tunable precision parameter (introduced around p. 5, near Alg. 1) seems to trade exactness for tractability in a way that deserves a much sharper statement. Is soundness preserved for all settings of that parameter, or only in the limit / under some partition assumptions? I think the paper believes the former, but it is not stated crisply enough.

I also wanted more specificity about the threat model. The abstract talks broadly about adversarial attacks on LLMs, but the experiments (Table 1 and Table 2) seem much narrower — if I understood correctly, this is token-embedding perturbation robustness for relatively small transformer models, not anything close to full modern LLM verification. That’s fine! But then the title feels a bit overclaimed. “Towards” gives some cover, but still, readers will infer more than is actually delivered.

On the empirical side, the results are promising but limited. The runtime/precision tradeoff in Fig. 3 is probably the most important result in the paper, and I wish it were discussed more concretely. In my experience with verification papers, a method lives or dies on whether the improved tightness is worth the computational pain. Here it seems maybe yes for small models, maybe no beyond that. I might be wrong, but the scaling discussion on p. 8 felt evasive.

Overall, I think this is a worthwhile paper with a real technical contribution, and it is more novel than many submissions in this area. But the claims should be tightened, and the formal guarantee statement needs to be much cleaner. Borderline accept for me, leaning positive if the authors can clarify the soundness/computation tradeoff.

I found the paper interesting in intent, but overall I was not convinced by either the technical novelty or the empirical support. The core idea—decode a foreground/attention map from fMRI and then use it to guide image reconstruction—is reasonable, and honestly pretty intuitive. But after reading the paper twice, I still think the actual contribution is somewhat incremental relative to the recent fMRI-to-image reconstruction literature that already uses intermediate semantic or spatial guidance signals.

A lot hinges on the notion of “F-attention,” and here I think the paper is underspecified. On p. 3, where the authors define foreground attention, it seems to be operationalized more or less as a foreground mask/saliency map, but the distinction is blurry. I’m not entirely sure what neuroscientific claim is being made beyond “foreground matters.” If this is just a learned foreground prior, that is fine, but then the language about human visual attention feels overstated. Figure 2 gives the pipeline clearly enough, but it also made me wonder whether the gains come from attention decoding per se, or simply from giving the reconstruction network an easier auxiliary target.

The use of self-attention for fMRI decoding also did not strike me as especially novel. The argument on p. 4 about sulci/gyri and nonlocal voxel relationships is sensible, but many transformer-style or nonlocal approaches have already made essentially this point. I would have liked a stronger architectural justification, maybe with ablations on local vs global receptive fields. Table 2 has some ablations, but they are too limited, and I could not tell whether the improvements are statistically meaningful. Some of the reported gains look small.

The Loop-Enc-Dec training strategy is also hard to assess. Equation (8) and the surrounding text were not very clear to me; I may be wrong, but it seems like a cycle/consistency-style training loss dressed up under a new name. If so, the novelty is again limited. More importantly, the paper does not really disentangle the effects of F-attention guidance from the loop training. Figure 5 shows visually better reconstructions in some cases, yes, but the examples are cherry-picked and not especially compelling by current standards.

I also had concerns about evaluation. The paper leans heavily on reconstruction visuals and a few similarity metrics, but there is little discussion of subject-wise variability, robustness, or generalization. In my experience reviewing this area, that is exactly where methods often fall apart.

So, my feeling is: decent idea, somewhat plausible method, but the paper is not yet at top-conference level. The framing overclaims, and the experiments do not quite close the loop. I would lean reject.

I found the paper reasonably interesting, but I also had a fairly strong “haven’t we seen a lot of this already?” reaction while reading it. The core idea — learn embeddings for supervision sources and aggregate them with context-dependent attention — is sensible, and for sequence labeling it is probably useful in practice. But I’m not fully convinced the novelty is as high as the paper claims. After reading Section 3 twice, CONNET feels like a fairly natural combination of source modeling + attention over weak labels, rather than a genuinely new conceptual contribution.

The framing across crowd annotations and cross-domain transfer is nice, though. I do appreciate papers that try to unify these settings instead of treating them as separate literatures. Figure 1 helps, and Table 2/Table 3 suggest the method is consistently competitive. In particular, the gains in the crowd setting seem meaningful, not just noise. That said, I wanted a clearer sense of when the method actually helps. Right now the paper mostly says “attention learns to weight sources contextually,” but that can explain almost any result after the fact.

A few things bothered me. On p.4 around Eq. (3)-(5), the supervision source representation is introduced a bit too quickly. I’m not entirely sure whether this representation is learned globally per annotator/source or token-conditioned through the encoder states; the text seems to suggest both. If I’m misunderstanding, that probably means the exposition needs work. Similarly, the training objective on p.5 is written cleanly, but I had trouble seeing what prevents the model from simply over-trusting the strongest source and collapsing the consensus mechanism. Is there any entropy regularization or diversity effect here? I might have missed it.

The experimental section is decent but not airtight. I would have liked stronger baselines from the label model / crowd aggregation literature, not just standard neural sequence taggers with weak-supervision variants. Also, the ablation in Table 4 is too thin. If contextual attention is the main claim, then show non-contextual source weighting, token-independent weighting, and maybe even a simple majority-vote-plus-BiLSTM baseline more carefully. Right now the empirical story is suggestive, not definitive.

Overall, I think the paper is solid and potentially useful, but a bit oversold. My leaning is weak accept / borderline, mostly on practical value rather than originality. If accepted, I’d strongly encourage the authors to clarify the model description and sharpen the ablations.

I had mixed reactions to this paper. The core motivation is reasonable: KL-based VI in BNNs does have the usual mode-seeking/pathology issues, and using a bounded symmetric divergence is not a crazy direction at all.
