In fact, when I first read the abstract I thought this might be a nice, clean contribution. But after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced the technical story is as solid as the authors seem to think.

The main issue for me is that the paper keeps saying JS “overcomes” limitations of KL because it is bounded and symmetric, but the actual optimization problem introduced in Section 3 (especially around Eq. (8)–(11), if I’m following correctly) no longer looks like straightforward JS-VI. It becomes a constrained surrogate objective with auxiliary terms, and at that point I found myself asking: are we really optimizing a meaningful divergence between the approximate posterior and the true posterior, or are we just introducing a regularized objective that is inspired by JS? I might be wrong, but the gap between the conceptual claim and the actual trainable loss is not resolved clearly enough. The discussion on page 5 around the “generalization” of the KL loss also felt a bit hand-wavy. Saying the proposed losses generalize KL is a strong statement; I did not see a precise theorem with assumptions that fully justifies it.

I also struggled with the exposition. Figure 1 is too high-level to help, while the derivation around the constrained formulation is dense and skips steps. There are several places where notation changes or is overloaded. By page 6 I was honestly a bit lost. In my experience, if a method is genuinely simple and useful, the paper should make it easier to see what is going on.

The experiments are decent but not decisive. CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 with added noise and the histopathology setting are sensible choices, and Table 1/Table 2 do show gains. Still, I wanted stronger baselines. Comparing mostly against KL-VI BNNs and deterministic models is not enough in 2025. Where are modern uncertainty baselines, e.g. deep ensembles, Laplace variants, or even more competitive variational objectives? Also, I did not see enough evidence that the observed gains come specifically from the JS formulation rather than tuning, constraints, or optimization details. Ablations are pretty limited.

So overall: interesting idea, and I do believe there is something here, but right now the paper overclaims and under-explains. I lean negative. If the authors tightened the theory, clarified the exact objective, and ran stronger empirical comparisons, I’d be much more receptive.

I went into this paper somewhat skeptical because “prompt learning” has become a catch-all idea lately, and in non-language domains it is often more branding than substance. After reading this twice, I think this paper is actually doing something reasonably sensible, but I’m still not fully convinced by the empirical story.

The core idea—adapting a pretrained spatio-temporal forecaster with a lightweight prompt module rather than full finetuning—is intuitive and, honestly, overdue for this area. The method description around Eq. (3)–(6) is fairly easy to follow, and Fig. 2 helped. I appreciated that the prompt is not presented as magic; it is basically a small conditioning mechanism over spatial-temporal representations. That said, I found the exact insertion point of the prompt network into the backbone a bit under-specified on first pass. On page 5, around the paragraph after Eq. (7), I had to infer some implementation details that really should be explicit. Maybe I missed it, but I’m still unsure whether prompts are injected at every block or only once at the encoder input.

The experimental section is solid in breadth. Table 2 and Table 3 suggest consistent gains across datasets/tasks, and I do think the parameter-efficiency angle is a real contribution here. In my experience reviewing traffic forecasting papers, many “efficient adaptation” methods quietly sacrifice stability, but the results here look fairly robust. Still, the baselines are a bit uneven. If the claim is about adaptation to distribution shift, then stronger test-time adaptation / domain generalization baselines should really be included. Comparing mostly against standard pretrained or finetuned variants is not enough. Also, on page 7 the discussion of unseen distributions feels too vague relative to how strong the claims are in the abstract.

I also wasn’t crazy about the writing. There’s a lot of inflated language (“successfully adapt,” “superiority,” etc.) that the paper doesn’t quite earn. And some of the motivation section reads like generic spatio-temporal ML boilerplate. Minor thing, but Fig. 3 could be much clearer.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and it is refreshingly simple. Simple is good. But the paper oversells, and the evaluation does not fully pin down why PromptST works versus just adding a small trainable adapter. I lean positive, though weakly, because the method is practical and likely useful to the community.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than the abstract first suggests. The core idea — comparing noisy neural trajectories via an OT distance between Gaussian processes — is timely, and I buy the motivation. A lot of representation-similarity work really does quietly assume static, deterministic responses, which is not how biological data behave in practice. The examples in motor cortex and diffusion models are also a nice pairing; unusual, but it works better than I expected.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper has nailed the methodological story. The derivation around the GP-based distance (Sec. 3, especially around Eq. 4–7) is mathematically plausible, but the presentation is too compressed. I think the authors are relying on readers to fill in several gaps. In particular, I was unsure what exactly is identifiable from finite noisy trajectories versus what is imposed by the GP prior/covariance choice. This matters a lot, because if the metric is heavily shaped by the kernel assumptions, then the “geometry” being compared is partly authored by the modeler. Maybe I missed it, but I wanted a much more explicit sensitivity analysis here.

Figure 2 makes the qualitative failure mode of existing metrics clear, and I liked that part. But the empirical sections are more mixed. The motor system application (I think this is around pp. 6–7, with Table 1) is suggestive, though the biological interpretation feels a bit overstated. Distances change, yes, but I didn’t come away believing the metric isolates something uniquely dynamical rather than just second-order temporal statistics. Those are not the same thing. On the diffusion-model side, the results are fun, but also somewhat opportunistic. I’m not against that — I actually like papers that try odd cross-domain applications — but here it reads a little like “look, it also works on generative models,” without a strong scientific question.

I also found the computational story underdeveloped. OT between stochastic processes sounds expensive; the runtime/scaling discussion near the end is too hand-wavy. For a methods paper, that’s a real issue.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, maybe very good, but not yet fully convincing at top-conference level. The problem is important and the proposed direction is promising. Still, the paper needs sharper exposition, more ablations on kernel/model assumptions, and a clearer account of what the metric is actually sensitive to. Right now I’m leaning weak accept / borderline, mostly on novelty and relevance rather than airtight execution.

I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “yet another sparse attention for long context” submissions. The main idea — adapting both the sparsity pattern and the budget per head/per input during prefilling — makes intuitive sense, and the paper does a decent job motivating why fixed patterns are too rigid. I especially appreciated the discussion around query diversity on p.3–4, and Fig. 2 helps. In my experience, many papers in this area hide behind hardware numbers, but here there is at least an attempt to connect the mechanism to attention behavior.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as principled as the paper wants it to sound. The use of Jensen-Shannon divergence for switching between query-specific and predefined patterns (around Eq. 4, if I’m tracking correctly) is plausible, but the justification feels a bit post hoc. Why JSD specifically? Why should that be the right signal for routing between sparse regimes? I might be wrong, but it reads more like an empirical heuristic than a real modeling contribution. The cumulative-attention thresholding part is also somewhat underexplained. On p.5, the threshold criterion seems critical, yet I couldn’t tell how sensitive the whole method is to that hyperparameter, and Table 3 does not really settle this.

The empirical results are good, maybe very good, but I wanted more clarity on what exactly is being compared fairly. In Table 1 and Table 2, are all methods run with matched kernel implementations and similar engineering effort? This matters a lot for prefill speed claims. I’ve reviewed enough long-context systems papers to know that “method” improvements and “CUDA love” improvements get mixed together all the time. Also, the gains on accuracy are promising, but I would have liked more breakdown by task type and prompt structure; the average numbers are a little too clean.

One thing I did like: the head-wise adaptivity is not just cosmetic. Fig. 4 suggests different heads really do settle into different budgets, which is believable and useful. But then the paper should go further and analyze failure cases. When does FlexPrefill choose the wrong pattern? Does it hurt on retrieval-heavy prompts? Needle-in-a-haystack style setups would be relevant here.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a real idea, but the presentation oversells the novelty a bit, and some of the central design choices still feel heuristic. Weak accept from me.

I read this paper with some interest because there has been a flood of “medical Mamba” papers recently, and frankly many of them feel interchangeable. This one at least tries to do something specific: use uncertainty to define the scan order rather than just inheriting the usual raster or directional scans. I think that basic idea is reasonable and, at least on first pass, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent SSM-for-segmentation work.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the way the paper motivates and validates the method. The core claim is that channel uncertainty gives a better pixel ordering for the Mamba blocks, but after reading Section 3 twice I still found the mechanism a bit murky. On p. 4, around Eq. (3)–(5), it seems uncertainty is computed from channel statistics and then used to induce both “sequential” and “skip” scans. But the exact mapping from uncertainty map to 1D token order is not explained cleanly enough. Figure 2 helps a little, but not enough. I kept asking myself: is this genuinely a data-dependent scan per image, or more like a soft routing prior? The distinction matters.

I also think the paper overstates the novelty. Reordering tokens/pixels based on saliency/uncertainty is not conceptually far from prior adaptive scanning / sorting / importance-based traversal ideas. The Mamba angle is new-ish, sure, but only to a degree. The four learnable fusion parameters (p. 5) felt especially lightweight; I’m not sure this deserves the emphasis it gets.

The empirical results are decent. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, though mostly not huge ones. I appreciated the diversity of datasets. Still, I wanted more ablations. The current ablation in Table 3 is too coarse. For example: what happens if uncertainty is random but with the same marginal distribution? What if one uses entropy from the segmentation logits instead of the proposed channel uncertainty? Also, the cosine consistency loss (Eq. (9)) is introduced as important, but its actual contribution looks modest and somewhat unstable across datasets. I might be wrong, but I suspect the gains are coming mostly from the scan redesign, not the consistency term.

Another issue is computational overhead. The abstract leans on Mamba efficiency, but I could not find a really transparent runtime/memory comparison on p. 7–8. If scan order is data-adaptive, there is usually a hidden cost. This should be quantified more honestly.

Overall, I think the paper has a real idea, and in a weaker year I might lean positive. But for a top ML conference, this feels a bit under-justified and under-analyzed. My recommendation is borderline to weak reject.

I read this paper with some interest because parameter-efficient tuning for multimodal models is a space that is getting crowded, and honestly a lot of submissions in this area feel like minor adapter reshufflings. This one is somewhat better than that, but I’m still not fully convinced by the presentation or by how strong the claimed novelty really is.

The core idea—injecting an audio-visual adapter into a frozen ViT so that audio can be handled “without prior audio pre-training”—is interesting. Figure 2 helped, although I had to go back to it after reading Sec. 3.2 because the text around Eq. (4)-(6) is more opaque than it needs to be. In particular, the way the audio tokens are projected and then exchanged with visual cues inside each transformer block is described a bit too abstractly. I think the method is probably simpler than the writing makes it sound. Page 4, around the discussion of the “modality-specific” and “modality-shared” branches, was especially confusing to me. Maybe I’m missing something, but I was not sure what exactly is shared across layers versus just shared across modalities.

That said, the empirical coverage is pretty solid. It is rare to see AVEL, AVQA, retrieval, and captioning all in one paper with a mostly unified mechanism. Table 1 and Table 2 are the most convincing parts of the paper for me: the method seems consistently competitive while training a small fraction of parameters. I do appreciate that they report parameter counts clearly. In my experience, many PEFT papers hide the true overhead in task heads or modality encoders; here it seems more transparent, though I would still like FLOPs and inference latency somewhere. “Parameter-efficient” is not the whole story.

I also think the comparison set is a little selective. On page 6, the baselines are mostly full fine-tuning, adapters, and prompt-style methods, but I was expecting a stronger discussion of whether the gain comes from the specific AV exchange mechanism or simply from giving audio its own learned entry point into a strong frozen backbone. The ablations in Table 4 go part of the way, but not enough. For example, what happens with a much simpler late-fusion adapter of matched parameter budget? I might be wrong, but I don’t think that question is fully answered.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper tackles a real problem and has broader experimental validation than average. But the novelty is incremental, and the exposition needs work. If accepted, I would strongly encourage the authors to simplify Sec. 3 and be much clearer about what is actually new relative to existing multimodal adapters.

I went into this paper fairly positively because, at a high level, the premise is sensible: if you want an LLM to reason over a 3D scene, then giving it only object coordinates and not explicit relations is leaving performance on the table. That part I buy. And in fact Figure 1 makes the motivation pretty clearly. But after reading the paper twice, I’m less convinced by how much is actually new here versus a fairly straightforward “scene graph + encoder + LLM adapter” recipe.

The core idea, as I understand it from Sec. 3 and especially the formulation around Eq. (2)–(5), is to construct a semantic graph over scene objects, encode nodes/edges, and feed the learned representation to the LLM for grounding / QA / captioning. Fine. Reasonable. But the paper is surprisingly fuzzy on some implementation choices that matter a lot. For example, on page 4, around the graph construction description, I could not tell whether the relations are predicted once from annotations, heuristically derived from geometry, or learned jointly. This is important because if the graph uses strong supervision or dataset-specific relation labels, then the comparison to “coordinate-only” baselines is not entirely apples-to-apples. I might be wrong, but I think this is only partially clarified in the appendix.

The experimental section is broad, which I appreciate. Running on ScanRefer, RIORefer, Multi3DRefer, ScanQA, Sqa3D, and Scan2Cap is nontrivial. Table 1/Table 2 suggest consistent gains, and I do think the cross-task evaluation is the strongest part of the submission. Still, the margins are mostly modest. In a few cases they are the kind of improvements that could easily come from prompt engineering, better object proposals, or larger backbones. I kept looking for a really clean ablation isolating “semantic relations” from everything else. The ablations in Table 3 help, but not enough. I wanted to see a version with identical token budget / identical encoder capacity, differing only in whether relation edges are present. Without that, the main claim feels a bit under-proven.

I also found the writing uneven. Page 5, the transition from graph encoder to LLM conditioning is rushed, and Figure 3 is more schematic than explanatory. Some notation is introduced late. Minor issue, but it adds friction.

Overall, I think the paper is competent and probably useful, but I’m not excited. The idea is intuitive rather than surprising, and the evidence, while positive, doesn’t fully nail causality. Borderline for me; leaning weak accept if the venue values solid empirical integration papers, weak reject if the bar is stronger novelty.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea — moving from single-task restoration to a more general speech restoration setting — is absolutely worthwhile. In practice, real recordings are rarely “just denoising” or “just dereverberation,” so I’m sympathetic to the motivation. And using a vocoder-based synthesis stage instead of directly predicting waveform or spectrogram feels sensible. I’ve seen similar intuitions pay off in speech enhancement / bandwidth extension work, especially when the degradations are ugly and interacting.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper really nails the “general” claim. After reading it twice, my impression is that this is still basically a model trained on a fixed menu of degradations (noise, reverb, low-resolution, clipping) with mixtures sampled from known procedures, rather than a genuinely open-ended restoration framework. That may be fine, but then the framing should be toned down. On p. 2 the paper emphasizes “general speech restoration” pretty strongly, and I think that oversells what is actually demonstrated.

The empirical section is somewhat uneven. The MOS gains reported in the abstract are nice, but I really wanted more objective evidence broken down by distortion type and, especially, combinations of distortions. If I’m reading Table 2 correctly, the average improves, but it is hard to tell where the gain is actually coming from. Is the vocoder mostly helping super-resolution? Or clipping? Or just making outputs sound smoother and therefore better in MOS even when content fidelity is not improved? This matters. Figure 3 is suggestive but mostly qualitative.

I also found the method description a little thin. The ResUNet + vocoder pipeline is clear at a high level, but some training details are buried or missing. On p. 4 around the loss definition (I think Eq. 5/6 area), I wasn’t entirely sure how the analysis-stage target is chosen under multiple simultaneous degradations, and whether the vocoder is frozen or jointly optimized for all experiments. These details can change the story a lot.

Another concern: comparison baselines seem weaker than they should be. If the claim is that the proposed system beats SSR methods because they are single-task, then okay, but I would still expect stronger multi-condition or cascaded baselines. In my experience, a decent cascade of denoising + dereverb + bandwidth extension can be annoyingly competitive. I don’t think the paper rules that out.

Overall, I like the direction and I do think the paper has a real idea in it. But right now it feels more like a promising system paper than a fully convincing conference paper. I’m leaning weak reject, though not by a huge margin. With clearer claims, stronger baselines, and more careful ablations, I’d view it much more favorably.

I read this paper with some interest because clustered FL under mixed heterogeneity is a real problem, and in practice most papers quietly assume one kind of shift at a time. I do appreciate that the authors are at least trying to frame feature shift, label shift, and concept shift jointly rather than inventing yet another method for one clean synthetic setting. That part felt timely.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper is as solid as it wants to be. The central “clustering principle” is presented as the key insight, but after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still found it somewhat slippery. On p.4, around the discussion leading into Eq. (6), the paper argues that good client clustering should reflect optimization compatibility under diverse shifts rather than raw model similarity. Fine. But then the actual objective seems to rely on proxies whose relationship to those shifts is not really established theoretically. I might be missing something, but the leap from the principle to the bi-level formulation felt more asserted than derived.

The method itself is reasonable enough. The bi-level setup and robust clustering objective are not crazy, and I can believe they help. But the novelty seems incremental. The paper sometimes writes as if existing clustered FL methods fundamentally “fail” under mixed shifts; I think this is overstated. There is a difference between “not designed for” and “fail.” Also, the optimization description is pretty dense. Algorithm 1 is helpful, but the notation around the cluster assignment/update steps is harder than it needs to be. Eq. (9) and the paragraph below it on p.5 were especially confusing.

The experiments are the strongest part, though even there I have reservations. The results in Table 2 and Table 3 look good, and Figure 3 does suggest FedRC is more stable across heterogeneous settings. Still, I wanted a cleaner disentangling study. If the whole pitch is “diverse simultaneous shifts,” then please show more systematically what happens when only two shift types co-occur, or when one dominates. Right now the ablations are not enough to tell me why FedRC works. Is it the robust objective? the bi-level optimization? the specific clustering metric? Hard to say. I also did not see much discussion of computational overhead or communication cost, which matters a lot in FL and is oddly underplayed here.

Overall, I think the paper has a worthwhile problem statement and decent empirical performance, but the conceptual contribution is fuzzier than advertised. I’m leaning borderline positive if the conference values practical empirical advances, but from a top-tier ML conference bar, I’m not fully sold yet.

I found the premise interesting, and honestly somewhat refreshing: a lot of robustness papers still act as if robustness means “PGD robustness,” full stop. So I appreciate that this paper tries to put adversarial and natural perturbations into the same experimental frame. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced the methodology is solid enough to support the headline claim in the abstract, especially the part that natural perturbations improve adversarial robustness “in contrast” to adversarial training.

My main issue is with what exactly is being standardized across perturbation types. The paper says this repeatedly (around p. 2 and again near the experimental setup on p. 4), but I never felt the comparison was actually principled. Adversarial perturbations are worst-case and input-dependent; elastic, occlusion, and wave perturbations are not. Matching them by some norm budget or visual severity is not really enough. I might be wrong, but the paper seems to slide over this distinction and then interprets accuracy numbers as if they are directly comparable. That is a pretty big conceptual leap.

I was also confused by the training/evaluation protocol. In Table 1 and Table 2 (if I’m reading them correctly), the gains from natural-perturbation training on adversarial examples are modest, while the drop from adversarial training on natural corruptions is larger. But then the abstract states a much stronger conclusion than I think the evidence supports. Were the adversarial attacks strong enough? I did not see enough detail on attack settings, number of PGD steps, restarts, or whether hyperparameters were tuned per model. In my experience, “natural training helps adversarial robustness” often disappears once the attack is made stronger. This paper really needs a much more careful robustness evaluation to rule out gradient masking or just weak attacks.

Figure 3 was also a bit odd. The visualization is nice, but it is doing a lot of rhetorical work without much quantitative backup. And Eq. (2) / the perturbation parameterization section felt under-motivated. Why these three natural perturbations? Why not blur, noise, weather, or standard corruption benchmarks? The selection feels arbitrary.

I do think there is a potentially good workshop paper here, or maybe a short empirical study, but in its current form it feels undercooked. The central claim is too strong for the evidence presented, and the evaluation details are not at the standard I’d expect for a top ML venue. My leaning is reject.

I found this paper interesting, but also a bit narrower than it initially sounds. The title/abstract suggest broad “insights” into EoS, while the actual results are for a fairly stylized setting: shallow ReLU, squared loss, orthogonal inputs, and full-batch GD. That is not a dealbreaker in itself — honestly, a lot of the better theory papers isolate one clean regime and really analyze it — but I do think the framing should be more careful.

The technical core seems to be the characterization of the sharpening phase and then the EoS regime via the top Hessian eigenvalues. I liked the fine-grained perspective, especially around the transition point. The discussion around the evolution of the leading eigenvalue on p. 4–6 is, in my view, the most valuable part of the paper. Equation (3) and the claims that follow give a reasonably crisp story for why GD can drift into instability rather than simply diverge. I also appreciated Figure 2; it conveys the qualitative behavior pretty clearly. In my experience, many EoS papers wave hands around “oscillations” without really pinning down the mechanism, and this paper at least tries to do that.

That said, I struggled with the novelty claim. After reading this twice, I still think the main contribution is more of a careful specialization/refinement of existing EoS analyses than a major conceptual jump. The assumptions are doing a lot of work. Orthogonal inputs are especially strong, and I’m not entirely sure the manuscript is sufficiently honest about how much they simplify the Hessian structure. The proof strategy around Theorem 4.1 (p. 7?) felt technically competent but somewhat tailored to the setting. I might be wrong, but I don’t see a clear path from these arguments to anything close to realistic network/data regimes.

I was also confused by the empirical section. Table 1 and Figure 4 are presented as validation, but the experiments mostly mirror the theory setup. That is fine, but then the paper should say “sanity check” rather than “empirical support” in a broader sense. I would have liked at least one experiment outside the orthogonal-data model to test robustness of the predictions. Otherwise it feels a bit self-contained.

Another small thing: some notation was hard to follow around lines 210–240, where the decomposition of the dynamics changes perspective from parameter space to activation patterns. Maybe this is just me reading too quickly, but I had to backtrack.

Overall, I think this is a solid theory paper with some real insight, but not quite as broad or as surprising as the framing suggests. Weak accept / borderline for me, depending on the conference bar and how much it values clean but narrow analyses of optimization dynamics.

I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more substantive than the usual “stable neural ODE for chaos” submission, but I’m not fully convinced by the presentation or by the scope of the empirical evidence.

The main idea, as I understand it after reading the method section twice, is to jointly learn the dynamics and an energy/Lyapunov-like function, then impose algebraic dissipativity conditions via a projection layer so that trajectories remain bounded. That is a real contribution if the guarantees are stated correctly. The control-theoretic framing is sensible, and I appreciated that the paper is not just claiming “better long-term statistics” while quietly ignoring blow-up cases. In my experience, a lot of papers in this area do exactly that.

That said, the paper is harder to parse than it should be. Around the main derivation (I think Sec. 3, especially the equations around the dissipativity condition—Eq. (6) to (9)?), I was not entirely sure what is assumed versus what is learned. Is the energy-like function required to be radially unbounded by construction, or is that only approximately true in practice? This matters a lot for the boundedness claim. The ReLU projection layer is clever, but also feels a little ad hoc; I would have liked a clearer statement of when the projection is active and whether it damages local accuracy. Figure 2 helped somewhat, but the text around it was too compressed.

The experiments are decent but not exhaustive. The Lorenz-96 and truncated KS examples are reasonable choices, and Table 1/2 suggest improved long-horizon behavior and fewer invalid rollouts. Good. But chaotic forecasting papers live and die by ablations, and I wanted more. How much comes from the architecture versus simply regularizing the vector field norm? What if one learns the same model without the projection but with the same parameter count? Also, the attractor characterization claim feels overstated. An “outer estimate” of an invariant set is useful, yes, but from the figures (I’m thinking of the attractor plots later in the paper, maybe Fig. 4/5), it is still pretty qualitative.

I also worry about scalability. The examples are low/moderate dimensional by modern standards. For high-dimensional PDE surrogates, can the learned energy function and projection still be trained stably? Maybe this is future work, but the paper occasionally sounds more general than the evidence supports.

Overall, I think there is a solid idea here and some real technical value, but the paper needs clearer assumptions, better ablations, and a more careful framing of the guarantees. Borderline accept for me, leaning weak accept if the claims are toned down.

I found the paper interesting and, at least at a high level, timely. The NTK literature has covered a lot of feedforward and convolutional ground, and I agree with the authors that recurrent models are an obvious missing piece. The main idea here — defining an RNTK that naturally handles variable-length sequences — is appealing, and in practice probably more useful than yet another static-data kernel paper.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the technical story is as clean as the paper suggests. The derivation around the recurrent limit is dense, and I think some steps are hand-waved a bit too much. In particular, around Eq. (6)–(9) and the discussion on p. 4, I had trouble tracking exactly which parameters are scaled with width and which expectations are taken conditionally on previous hidden states. Maybe this is standard to people steeped in GP/NTK proofs, but I had to reconstruct parts of the argument myself. I might be wrong, but the assumptions needed for the convergence seem stronger than the prose lets on.

I did like the discussion of how different time steps are weighted. Figure 2 is one of the more informative parts of the paper, and the observations about initialization variance / nonlinearity affecting temporal emphasis are useful. This is where the paper feels less like “just another kernelization exercise” and more like it is saying something about recurrent inductive bias. On the other hand, I wanted a sharper takeaway. Table 1 and the large experimental section show many wins, yes, but the comparisons are a bit uneven. Some baselines feel dated or under-tuned. If you are going to claim “significant performance gains” in the abstract, I really want to see stronger sequence-specific baselines, not mainly generic kernels plus standard NTKs. In my experience, kernel papers can look great on many UCI-style tasks if the baseline setup is not extremely careful.

I’m also unsure about the practical message. Computing kernels on long sequences is not cheap, and I did not see a serious discussion of runtime/memory tradeoffs beyond passing remarks (maybe around p. 7?). Since the paper is partly pitched to practitioners, this omission matters.

Overall, I think there is real novelty here, and I learned something. But the presentation is rough in places, and the empirical case, while broad, is not as airtight as the paper claims. Borderline accept for me, leaning weak accept if the bar is novelty/theory, weaker if the bar is practical impact.
